# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Difference Across a Liquid Droplet (Δp = 2T/R)

**Physical Setup:** Consider a spherical liquid droplet of radius $R$ with surface tension $T$. The droplet is in equilibrium with its vapour, and the pressure inside ($p_i$) is greater than outside ($p_o$) by an amount $\Delta p$.

**Step 1 — Force balance on a hemispherical element.**

Cut the droplet into two equal hemispheres by an imaginary diametrical plane. The right hemisphere is held in place by:
- The pressure difference $\Delta p = p_i - p_o$ acting on the projected circular area $\pi R^2$ (pushing the hemispheres apart).
- The surface tension $T$ acting around the circumference of the great circle ($= 2 \pi R$), pulling the two hemispheres together.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Force due to pressure difference (pushing outward)} &= \Delta p \cdot \pi R^{2} \\
\text{Force due to surface tension (pulling inward)} &= T \cdot 2\pi R
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — At equilibrium**, these two forces must balance each other:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta p \cdot \pi R^{2} &= T \cdot 2\pi R
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solving for the pressure difference $\Delta p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta p \cdot \pi R^{2} &= 2\pi R T \\
\Delta p \cdot R^{2} &= 2 R T \\
\Delta p &= \frac{2T}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$\boxed{\;\Delta p \;=\; \frac{2T}{R}\;}\quad\text{(inside a liquid droplet, single surface)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pressure Difference Across a Soap Bubble (Δp = 4T/R)

A **soap bubble** differs from a liquid droplet in that the liquid film is *hollow inside* and has *two free surfaces* (inner surface and outer surface). Both surfaces contribute equally to the tensile force.

**Step 1 — Force balance on a hemispherical element of the soap film.**

The right hemisphere is held in place by:
- Pressure difference $\Delta p$ acting on projected area $\pi R^{2}$.
- Surface tension acting on **both** inner and outer circumferences, each of length $2\pi R$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Force pushing hemispheres apart} &= \Delta p \cdot \pi R^{2} \\
\text{Force pulling hemispheres together} &= T \cdot 2\pi R \;+\; T \cdot 2\pi R \;=\; 4\pi R T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Equating the two forces at equilibrium:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta p \cdot \pi R^{2} &= 4 \pi R T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solving for $\Delta p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta p \cdot \pi R^{2} &= 4\pi R T \\
\Delta p \cdot R &= 4 T \\
\Delta p &= \frac{4T}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$\boxed{\;\Delta p \;=\; \frac{4T}{R}\;}\quad\text{(inside a soap bubble, two surfaces — KTU favourite 14-mark question)}$$

**Conversion Logic Summary:**

| Quantity | Droplet | Soap Bubble | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of free surfaces | 1 | 2 | Bubble has inner + outer film |
| Effective tensile force | $2\pi R T$ | $4\pi R T$ | $T \times$ (perimeter $\times$ number of surfaces) |
| Final $\Delta p$ | $\dfrac{2T}{R}$ | $\dfrac{4T}{R}$ | Doubled in the soap bubble case |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capillary Rise (h = 2T cos θ / ρgr)

**Physical Setup:** A glass capillary tube of internal radius $r$ is dipped into a liquid of density $\rho$ and surface tension $T$. The liquid wets the glass, forming a concave meniscus, and rises to a height $h$ above the free surface.

**Step 1 — Identify the upward force due to surface tension.**

The meniscus is concave with contact angle $\theta$ (measured inside the liquid). The vertical component of the surface tension force, acting around the wetted perimeter of length $2\pi r$, pulls the column upward.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{upward}} &= T \cdot 2\pi r \cdot \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the downward force due to gravity.**

The weight of the cylindrical liquid column of height $h$ and radius $r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \text{Volume} \times \rho \times g \\
W &= \pi r^{2} h \cdot \rho \cdot g
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — At equilibrium, the upward force equals the downward weight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T \cdot 2\pi r \cdot \cos\theta &= \pi r^{2} h \rho g
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the capillary rise $h$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\pi r T \cos\theta &= \pi r^{2} h \rho g \\
2T \cos\theta &= r h \rho g \\
h &= \frac{2 T \cos\theta}{\rho g r}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$\boxed{\;h \;=\; \frac{2 T \cos\theta}{\rho \, g \, r}\;}\quad\text{(Jurin's Law — KTU board favourite for 7-mark questions)}$$

**Analytical Insights:**

- $h \propto \dfrac{1}{r}$ — *smaller tubes cause higher capillary rise* (capillary action is strong in very fine tubes).
- $h \propto T$ — liquids with higher surface tension (e.g., water) show more pronounced capillarity.
- $h \propto \cos\theta$ — when $\theta > 90°$, $\cos\theta < 0$ and $h$ becomes *negative* → this represents **capillary depression** (e.g., mercury in glass).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Work Done in Splitting a Droplet into Smaller Droplets

A frequent KTU Part B 14-mark question. We have a large liquid drop of radius $R$, surface tension $T$. It splits into $n$ smaller droplets, each of radius $r$.

**Step 1 — Initial surface area and surface energy.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{initial}} &= 4\pi R^{2} \\
E_{\text{initial}} &= T \cdot 4\pi R^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Final surface area and surface energy.**

Volume is conserved, so:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3} &= n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; r = R \, n^{-1/3} \\
A_{\text{final}} &= n \cdot 4\pi r^{2} = 4\pi n \cdot R^{2} n^{-2/3} = 4\pi R^{2} \, n^{1/3} \\
E_{\text{final}} &= T \cdot 4\pi R^{2} \, n^{1/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Work done = Increase in surface energy.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= E_{\text{final}} - E_{\text{initial}} \\
W &= 4\pi R^{2} T \, n^{1/3} - 4\pi R^{2} T \\
W &= 4\pi R^{2} T \,(n^{1/3} - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for surface tension $T$ in terms of $W$ (often the asked quantity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{W}{4\pi R^{2} (n^{1/3} - 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$\boxed{\;T \;=\; \frac{W}{4\pi R^{2}\,(n^{1/3} - 1)}\;}$$
> Equivalently, if the new radius is $r$: $\;T = \dfrac{W}{4\pi r^{2}\,(n^{2/3} - 1)}$

This is the standard KTU framing — students should memorise the volume-conservation identity $r = R n^{-1/3}$ and the area-scaling identity $A_{\text{final}} = 4\pi R^{2} n^{1/3}$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Molecular Force Balance at a Liquid Surface

This diagram maps the *molecular origin* of surface tension. A bulk molecule is pulled equally in all directions, while a surface molecule has a net inward attraction — the very reason surface tension exists.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Liquid Bulk Molecule] --> B1[Forces from 12 neighbour molecules]
    B1 --> C1[Net force is ZERO balanced attraction]
    A2[Liquid Surface Molecule] --> B2[Forces from below and sideways only]
    B2 --> C2[Net INWARD force no upward pull from above]
    C2 --> D1[Surface molecules compressed tighter than bulk]
    D1 --> E1[Residual tensile stress along surface]
    E1 --> F1[This tensile stress IS surface tension T]
    E1 --> G1[Work needed to bring a molecule to surface stored as potential energy]
    G1 --> H1[Equivalent units: N per m AND J per m squared]
    F1 --> I1[(Engineering consequence Liquid surface behaves like a stretched membrane)]
    I1 --> J1[Spherical droplets, capillary rise, pressure difference]
    I1 --> J2[Soap bubbles and emulsions are stable]
    I1 --> J3[Lubrication, wetting, surface cleaning]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Cue:** Whenever a question mentions *"explain the molecular theory of surface tension"*, this is the only diagram you need. It visually captures the entire reasoning chain from intermolecular forces to engineering applications.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Capillary Action Functional Architecture

This is a block-level flow chart tracing the equilibrium balance in a capillary tube — perfectly suited for KTU diagram questions and viva-voce.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Capillary tube dipped in liquid] --> P2[Contact angle THETA forms at three phase line]
    P2 --> P3[Adhesion vs cohesion decided]
    P3 --> P4{Which is larger}
    P4 -- Adhesion greater --> P5[THETA less than 90 DEG concave meniscus]
    P4 -- Cohesion greater --> P6[THETA greater than 90 DEG convex meniscus]
    P5 --> Q1[Surface tension pulls column UPWARD force = 2 pi r T cos THETA]
    P6 --> Q2[Surface tension pulls column DOWNWARD same magnitude]
    Q1 --> R1[Weight of liquid column opposes = pi r squared h rho g]
    Q2 --> R2[Weight deficit makes column DROP below free surface]
    R1 --> S1[Equilibrium gives Jurin law h = 2 T cos THETA divided by rho g r]
    R2 --> S2[Same equation with h NEGATIVE]
    S1 --> T1[Smaller tube radius r gives LARGER h]
    S2 --> T2[Mercury in glass falls capillary depression]
    S1 --> U1[(Engineering implications water rises in soil cracks ink rises in pen nib oil wicks in wicks)]
    S2 --> U2[(Engineering implications mercury thermometers read correctly mercury does not wet glass)]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Droplet vs Soap Bubble Pressure Decision Tree

A clean decision-flow diagram showing the *reason* for the factor-of-two difference in $\Delta p$ formulas.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[Curved liquid interface under study] --> K2{How many free surfaces}
    K2 -- One free surface --> K3[Example liquid droplet in air]
    K2 -- Two free surfaces --> K4[Example soap bubble in air]
    K3 --> L1[Equate Delta p times pi R squared to 2 pi R T]
    L1 --> M1[Result Delta p equals 2 T divided by R]
    K4 --> L2[Equate Delta p times pi R squared to 4 pi R T]
    L2 --> M2[Result Delta p equals 4 T divided by R]
    M1 --> N1[Half the pressure jump of a soap bubble of same radius]
    M2 --> N2[Doubly efficient at compressing inner gas]
    M2 --> O1[(Memorisation tip KTU board exam)]
    O1 --> P1[Drop uses 2 T over R, bubble uses 4 T over R, jet uses 1 T over R]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Note the third case — a **liquid jet** (cylindrical surface) has only one surface but a different curvature, giving $\Delta p = T/R$. This is rarely asked, but appears in viva. Use this diagram if the question is a 14-marker asking you to *"derive and compare"* all three cases.
