# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU ESE 2024 Scheme — PCAUT302):**
> - **Part A (Answer 4 out of 5):** 3 marks each × 4 = 12 marks
> - **Part B (Module Choice 14 marks):** Choose ONE of two, internal choice per module.
> - Typical weightage of *Surface Tension* in the entire paper: **14–20 marks** if a full Part B is set on it, or 6–9 marks if integrated with other Module 1 topics (fluid properties, capillarity combined).

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PCAUT302]`
**Define surface tension. State its SI units and dimensions. Show that surface tension can be expressed in N/m or J/m².**

**Model Answer:**

> Surface tension is the tensile force per unit length acting tangentially on an imaginary line drawn on a free liquid surface, perpendicular to the line. It arises from the imbalance of cohesive intermolecular forces at the free surface.
>
> $$\text{SI units: N/m} \quad \text{Dimensions: } M^{1} L^{0} T^{-2}$$
>
> **Proof of N/m ≡ J/m² equivalence:**
> Work done $W$ in increasing surface area by $\Delta A$ is $W = T \cdot \Delta A$. So $T = W / \Delta A$, with units of (J)/(m²) = N·m / m² = **N/m**.

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCAUT302]`
**Distinguish between cohesion and adhesion. How do their relative magnitudes determine the meniscus shape of a liquid in a glass tube?**

**Model Answer:**

> - **Cohesion:** Attraction between molecules of the *same* liquid (e.g., water–water).
> - **Adhesion:** Attraction between molecules of *different* substances (e.g., water–glass).
> - When **adhesion > cohesion** → liquid wets the tube wall → **concave meniscus** and capillary **rise** (e.g., water in clean glass).
> - When **cohesion > adhesion** → liquid does not wet the tube wall → **convex meniscus** and capillary **depression** (e.g., mercury in glass).
> - The contact angle $\theta$ quantifies this: $\theta < 90°$ for wetting, $\theta > 90°$ for non-wetting.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PCAUT302 — Module 1]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **pressure difference across a liquid droplet** and across a **soap bubble**. Clearly state the difference and physical reason.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A soap bubble of radius 12 mm is formed inside water, with surface tension 0.072 N/m. Calculate: (i) the gauge pressure inside the bubble, and (ii) the work done in doubling the bubble's radius (assume isothermal process, isothermal work = $\Delta p \cdot \Delta V$ for the gas).

---

#### Solution to A(a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the physical setup — a spherical droplet in equilibrium with its vapour. The pressure inside exceeds the outside by $\Delta p$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Free-body diagram of a hemispherical element, identifying:
- Outward force from $\Delta p$ on projected area $\pi R^2$
- Inward force from surface tension on perimeter $2\pi R$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Equate and solve:
- Droplet (1 surface): $\Delta p \cdot \pi R^2 = T \cdot 2\pi R \;\Rightarrow\; \Delta p = 2T/R$
- Soap bubble (2 surfaces): $\Delta p \cdot \pi R^2 = T \cdot 2 \cdot 2\pi R \;\Rightarrow\; \Delta p = 4T/R$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** State the physical reason — a soap bubble has two air–liquid interfaces, both contributing to the tensile pull. Conclude that the pressure inside a soap bubble is *twice* that of a droplet of equal radius.

---

#### Solution to A(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $R = 12 \text{ mm} = 0.012 \text{ m}$
- $T = 0.072 \text{ N/m}$
- Soap bubble has **two surfaces**.

**Part (i) — Gauge pressure inside the bubble (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta p &= \frac{4T}{R} \\
\Delta p &= \frac{4 \times 0.072}{0.012} \\
\Delta p &= \frac{0.288}{0.012} \\
\Delta p &= 24 \;\text{N/m}^{2} \;=\; 24 \;\text{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — Work done in doubling the radius (4 marks):**

New radius $R' = 2R = 0.024$ m. Work done against surface tension equals the increase in surface energy.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= T \cdot \Delta A \;=\; T \cdot \big(A_{\text{new}} - A_{\text{old}}\big) \\
A_{\text{old}} &= 2 \times 4\pi R^{2} = 8\pi (0.012)^{2} = 8\pi (1.44 \times 10^{-4}) \\
A_{\text{new}} &= 8\pi (0.024)^{2} = 8\pi (5.76 \times 10^{-4}) \\
W &= 0.072 \times 8\pi \times \big[(0.024)^{2} - (0.012)^{2}\big] \\
W &= 0.072 \times 8\pi \times \big[5.76 \times 10^{-4} - 1.44 \times 10^{-4}\big] \\
W &= 0.072 \times 8\pi \times 4.32 \times 10^{-4} \\
W &= 0.072 \times 1.0857 \times 10^{-2} \\
W &\approx 7.82 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\Delta p = 24$ Pa ; $W \approx 7.82 \times 10^{-4}$ J

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCAUT302 — Module 1]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** State and derive **Jurin's Law of Capillarity**. Discuss how the contact angle modifies the rise of a wetting liquid.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A clean glass capillary of internal diameter 0.8 mm is dipped in water at 20 °C. Find: (i) the capillary rise, and (ii) the capillary rise if the tube is made of material with contact angle 60°. Given: $T = 0.0728$ N/m, $\rho_{\text{water}} = 998$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

---

#### Solution to B(a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State Jurin's Law: the height of capillary rise in a tube of radius $r$ is given by $h = \dfrac{2T \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Free-body of the liquid column of height $h$ in a tube of radius $r$, contact angle $\theta$. Show the forces: $F_{\text{up}} = T \cdot 2\pi r \cos\theta$ and $F_{\text{down}} = \pi r^{2} h \rho g$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Equate and solve for $h$:
$2\pi r T \cos\theta = \pi r^{2} h \rho g \;\Rightarrow\; h = \dfrac{2T \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Discussion of $\theta$:
- $\theta = 0$ (perfect wetting) → $h = 2T/(\rho g r)$, maximum rise.
- $\theta = 90°$ → $h = 0$ (no rise).
- $\theta > 90°$ → $\cos\theta < 0$ → $h$ negative → capillary *depression* (mercury in glass).

---

#### Solution to B(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 0.8$ mm → $r = 0.4$ mm $= 0.4 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $T = 0.0728$ N/m
- $\rho = 998$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Part (i) — Capillary rise for clean glass (assume $\theta \approx 0$, $\cos\theta = 1$) — 3 marks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{2T \cos\theta}{\rho g r} \\
h &= \frac{2 \times 0.0728 \times 1}{998 \times 9.81 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-3}} \\
h &= \frac{0.1456}{3.916} \\
h &= 0.0372 \;\text{m} \\
h &= 37.2 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — Capillary rise for $\theta = 60°$ (so $\cos 60° = 0.5$) — 4 marks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h' &= \frac{2T \cos 60°}{\rho g r} \\
h' &= h \times \cos 60° \\
h' &= 37.2 \times 0.5 \\
h' &= 18.6 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** (i) $h = 37.2$ mm (clean glass) ; (ii) $h' = 18.6$ mm ($\theta = 60°$)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways KTU students lose marks on Surface Tension questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the factor of 2 between droplet and soap bubble.** A soap bubble has *two* surfaces. Writing $\Delta p = 2T/R$ for a soap bubble costs 3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to include the contact angle in the capillary rise formula.** Writing $h = 2T / \rho g r$ without the $\cos\theta$ is the most common error. The examiner will deduct 1 mark per instance.
> 3. **Sign mistakes in capillary depression.** Mercury gives a *negative* $h$ (depression, not rise). Students often report a positive value, which is physically wrong.
> 4. **Wrong factor of $\pi$ when computing meniscus force.** The surface tension acts on the *perimeter* $2\pi r$, not the cross-sectional area $\pi r^2$. This swap loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **In splitting-droplet problems, not conserving volume first.** Always write $\frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3} = n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}$ *before* computing energies. Skipping this step loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Surface Tension (Module 1)**

- ☐ **Surface tension** = force per unit length (N/m) **or** energy per unit area (J/m²). **Same physical quantity, two units.**
- ☐ **SI Units:** N/m. **Dimensions:** $M L^{0} T^{-2}$.
- ☐ **Cause:** imbalance of cohesive forces at the free liquid surface; surface molecules are pulled inward, creating a tensile "skin".
- ☐ **Pressure inside a liquid droplet:** $\Delta p = \dfrac{2T}{R}$ — **one free surface**.
- ☐ **Pressure inside a soap bubble:** $\Delta p = \dfrac{4T}{R}$ — **two free surfaces**.
- ☐ **Pressure inside a liquid jet (cylindrical):** $\Delta p = \dfrac{T}{R}$.
- ☐ **Work done in changing surface area:** $W = T \cdot \Delta A$.
- ☐ **Splitting a drop of radius $R$ into $n$ drops of radius $r$:** $r = R n^{-1/3}$, $W = 4\pi R^{2} T (n^{1/3} - 1)$.
- ☐ **Capillary rise (Jurin's Law):** $h = \dfrac{2T \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$.
- ☐ **Capillary rise ∝ 1/r** — smaller tubes produce *higher* rise.
- ☐ **Adhesion > cohesion** → concave meniscus, $\theta < 90°$, wetting, capillary rise (water on glass).
- ☐ **Cohesion > adhesion** → convex meniscus, $\theta > 90°$, non-wetting, capillary depression (mercury on glass).
- ☐ **Contact angle θ** is measured *inside* the liquid, between the liquid surface and the solid.
- ☐ **Standard surface tension of water at 20 °C:** $T = 0.0728$ N/m.
- ☐ **Practical Applications:** soil moisture migration, ink pen design, lung surfactants, lubrication, soap bubble stability, paint wetting, detergent action.
- ☐ **Engineering pitfall to avoid:** always state clearly whether the question is about a droplet, a soap bubble, or a jet — the formula changes accordingly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bulk Modulus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bulk Modulus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bulk Modulus (K or β):** It is defined as the ratio of the change in hydrostatic pressure (or compressive stress) applied on a fluid to the corresponding **volumetric strain** (relative change in volume). It quantifies a fluid's inherent resistance to uniform compression under hydrostatic loading.

Mathematically, the Bulk Modulus is expressed as:

$$K = - \frac{dP}{\left(\dfrac{dV}{V}\right)}$$

The **negative sign** is incorporated to ensure that the value of $K$ remains positive, because an increase in pressure ($+dP$) always results in a decrease in volume ($-dV$). 

The **Coefficient of Compressibility** ($\beta_c$) is the reciprocal of the Bulk Modulus:

$$\beta_c = \frac{1}{K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values at 20°C and Atmospheric Pressure:**
> - **Water:** $K_{water} \approx 2.1 \times 10^{9} \, \text{N/m}^2 \,\, (2.1 \, \text{GPa})$
> - **Air (Isothermal):** $K_{air} \approx 1.01 \times 10^{5} \, \text{N/m}^2$
> - **Mercury:** $K_{Hg} \approx 2.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{N/m}^2$
> - **Oil (typical):** $K_{oil} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{9} \, \text{N/m}^2$

**Unit:** $\text{N/m}^2$ or Pascal (Pa). The dimensional formula is $[M^1 L^{-1} T^{-2}]$.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you have a **sealed syringe filled with water** and another **identical syringe filled with air**. If you press the plunger of both with the same force:

- The **water syringe** will barely move — the water resists compression aggressively. This fluid has a **HIGH Bulk Modulus**.
- The **air syringe** will compress easily — the air yields to pressure. This fluid has a **LOW Bulk Modulus**.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Summary:** A fluid with a high Bulk Modulus is "stiff" and almost incompressible (like water or mercury). A fluid with a low Bulk Modulus is "spongy" and easily compressible (like gases). For most engineering hydraulic calculations, liquids are often assumed to be **incompressible** because their Bulk Modulus is extremely large compared to gases.

**Real-World Analogy — The Diving Tank:**
A scuba diver's air tank is filled with highly compressed air. The reason it requires such thick, reinforced steel walls is because air at high pressure has a measurable, finite Bulk Modulus and the molecules resist being pushed closer together. Liquids, on the other hand, are already so tightly packed that further compression is geometrically constrained at the molecular level — making their Bulk Modulus about **20,000 times** that of atmospheric air.

---

### 1.3 KTU 2024 Syllabus Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This topic is a **High-Yield Module 1 Concept** that directly underpins:
> - Velocity of sound in fluids (Celerity)
> - Water hammer / Surge analysis in pipes
> - Hydraulic accumulator design
> - Compressible flow in turbo-machinery
> - Pump and turbine dynamic response

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Hydrostatic Compression Setup**
Consider a fluid element of initial volume $V$ confined within a container. A uniform hydrostatic pressure $P$ is applied on all its faces.

**Step 2: Resulting Volumetric Deformation**
The fluid element experiences a uniform reduction in volume. Let the new volume be $V - dV$. The relative change in volume (volumetric strain) is:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{dV}{V}$$

**Step 3: Definition Re-Statement**
By Newton's experimental observations generalized for fluids, the applied stress and the resulting strain are linearly related for small deformations (Hooke's Law for fluids):

$$P = K \cdot \varepsilon_v$$

This rearranges to our master equation:

$$K = - \frac{dP}{\left(\dfrac{dV}{V}\right)}$$

**Step 4: Differentiation of Mass Conservation**
Since mass is conserved during compression (closed system, no leakage):
$$\rho V = \text{constant} = m$$
Differentiating both sides:
$$V \cdot d\rho + \rho \cdot dV = 0$$
$$\frac{dV}{V} = - \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

**Step 5: Alternative Form Using Density**
Substituting the relation from Step 4 back into the master equation:

$$K = \frac{dP}{\left(\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}\right)} = \rho \cdot \frac{dP}{d\rho}$$

**Step 6: The Acoustic / Sonic Velocity Link**
For adiabatic (isentropic, no heat transfer) conditions applicable to rapid sound wave propagation:

$$c^2 = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_s$$

Substituting this into our density-based form:

$$\boxed{K = \rho \cdot c^2}$$

This is one of the **most frequently tested relations** in KTU exams.

---

### 2.2 Types of Bulk Modulus

> [!NOTE]
> The numerical value of $K$ depends on the thermodynamic condition of compression:

| Type | Condition | Governing Equation | Applicable For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Isothermal Bulk Modulus ($K_T$)** | Constant Temperature ($T = \text{const}$) | $K_T = -V \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_T$ | Slow processes, gases in isothermal containment |
| **Adiabatic / Isentropic Bulk Modulus ($K_s$)** | Constant Entropy ($s = \text{const}$) | $K_s = -V \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_s$ | Rapid processes, sound wave propagation, water hammer |
| **Secant Bulk Modulus** | Between two finite pressure states | $K = \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{(V_1 - V_2)/V_1}$ | Engineering design over pressure ranges |

> [!WARNING]
> **For Liquids:** $K_T \approx K_s$ — the difference is negligible (< 0.1%). Most KTU problems assume **$K_s$** for liquids unless specified otherwise.
> **For Gases:** $K_s = \gamma \cdot K_T$ where $\gamma$ is the specific heat ratio (e.g., $\gamma = 1.4$ for air).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $K = - \dfrac{dP}{(dV/V)}$ | Master definition | All problems |
| 2 | $K = \rho \cdot \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}$ | Density-based form | When $\rho$ variation is given |
| 3 | $K = \rho \cdot c^2$ | Acoustic velocity form | Sound, water hammer |
| 4 | $\beta_c = \dfrac{1}{K}$ | Coefficient of compressibility | Reciprocal conversions |
| 5 | $K_s = \gamma \cdot K_T$ | Gas relation | Air, steam problems |
| 6 | $K = K_0 + n \cdot P$ | Pressure-dependent (liquids) | High-pressure hydraulic design |
| 7 | $\dfrac{dV}{V} = - \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} = - \dfrac{dP}{K}$ | Volumetric strain | Strain calculations |
| 8 | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}}$ | Velocity of sound | Acoustic calculations |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic Press & Accumulator Sizing:** The stiffness of hydraulic oil determines energy storage capacity. A higher $K$ means more compact accumulator design.
- **Water Hammer Analysis in Penstocks:** Sudden valve closure generates pressure surges of magnitude $\Delta P = \rho \cdot c \cdot \Delta V$. The celerity $c$ directly depends on $K$.
- **Dam Hydraulic Design:** Pressure variations at depth must account for slight fluid compressibility over long penstock columns.
- **Submarine Hull Design:** Understanding water compressibility at depth (where $P$ can exceed $50 \, \text{MPa}$) is critical.
- **Calibration of Pressure Transducers:** Many high-precision transducers use the known bulk modulus of a reference fluid.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Bulk Modulus from First Principles (Mass + Compressibility)

**Given:** A fluid element of mass $m$, initial volume $V_1$, density $\rho_1$. Under pressure increase $dP$, volume becomes $V_2 = V_1 - dV$, density becomes $\rho_2 = \rho_1 + d\rho$.

**Step 1: Mass conservation** (no fluid enters or leaves the element):
$$m = \rho_1 V_1 = \rho_2 V_2$$

**Step 2: Express mass after compression:**
$$m = (\rho_1 + d\rho)(V_1 - dV)$$

**Step 3: Expand the product:**
$$m = \rho_1 V_1 - \rho_1 \, dV + V_1 \, d\rho - d\rho \cdot dV$$

**Step 4: Since $m = \rho_1 V_1$, cancel the leading term:**
$$0 = - \rho_1 \, dV + V_1 \, d\rho - d\rho \cdot dV$$

**Step 5: Neglect the second-order infinitesimal $d\rho \cdot dV$:**
$$\rho_1 \, dV = V_1 \, d\rho$$

**Step 6: Rearrange to get the volumetric strain-density relation:**
$$\frac{dV}{V_1} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho_1}$$

Wait — careful with sign convention. Since $dV$ is a *decrease*, the strain is:
$$\frac{dV}{V_1} = - \frac{d\rho}{\rho_1}$$

**Step 7: Substituting into the definition of Bulk Modulus:**
$$K = - \frac{dP}{(dV/V_1)} = - \frac{dP}{(- d\rho/\rho_1)} = \frac{dP}{(d\rho/\rho_1)} = \rho_1 \frac{dP}{d\rho}$$

**Result:**
$$\boxed{K = \rho \, \frac{dP}{d\rho}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relation Between Bulk Modulus and Velocity of Sound

**Given:** A plane acoustic wave propagates through a fluid at celerity $c$. The process is adiabatic (rapid oscillations).

**Step 1: Continuity equation for a fluid element:**
$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho \, \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$

**Step 2: Euler's momentum equation (inviscid, no body force, one-dimensional):**
$$\rho \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = - \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3: Linearize for small perturbations** about a base state (denoted by subscript 0):
$$\rho = \rho_0 + \rho', \quad u = 0 + u', \quad P = P_0 + P'$$
where primed quantities are infinitesimally small.

**Step 4: Linearized continuity:**
$$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \frac{\partial u'}{\partial x} = 0$$

**Step 5: Linearized momentum:**
$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial u'}{\partial t} = - \frac{\partial P'}{\partial x}$$

**Step 6: Combine the two equations** — differentiate continuity with respect to $t$, momentum with respect to $x$, then substitute:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} = \frac{\partial^2 P'}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 7: Apply the adiabatic equation of state** (sound wave assumption):
$$P' = c^2 \rho' \quad \text{where} \quad c^2 = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_s$$

**Step 8: Substitute into the wave equation:**
$$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial x^2}$$

This is the classical **1D wave equation**, with solution $\rho' = f(x - ct)$, confirming $c$ is the wave speed.

**Step 9: Recall from Derivation 1 that for adiabatic process:**
$$K_s = \rho_0 \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_s = \rho_0 \cdot c^2$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{\rho}} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad K_s = \rho \cdot c^2}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Standard 3-Mark KTU Type)

**Problem:** A hydraulic fluid occupying $0.05 \, \text{m}^3$ is subjected to a pressure rise of $1.4 \times 10^6 \, \text{N/m}^2$. The volume decreases by $3.0 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^3$. Calculate:
(a) The Bulk Modulus of the fluid.
(b) The Coefficient of Compressibility.
(c) The percentage change in density.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Bulk Modulus**

Given data:
- Initial volume: $V_1 = 0.05 \, \text{m}^3$
- Pressure increase: $dP = 1.4 \times 10^6 \, \text{N/m}^2$
- Volume decrease: $dV = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^3$

Volumetric strain:
$$\frac{dV}{V_1} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{-5}}{0.05} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

Bulk Modulus:
$$K = - \frac{dP}{(dV/V_1)} = \frac{1.4 \times 10^6}{6.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.333 \times 10^{9} \, \text{N/m}^2$$

**Part (b): Coefficient of Compressibility**

$$\beta_c = \frac{1}{K} = \frac{1}{2.333 \times 10^{9}} = 4.286 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{m}^2/\text{N}$$

**Part (c): Percentage Change in Density**

Using $\dfrac{dV}{V} = - \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}$:

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} = - \frac{dV}{V} = - 6.0 \times 10^{-4} = -0.0006$$

Percentage change:
$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} \times 100 = -0.06\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A pressure rise of $1.4 \, \text{MPa}$ caused only a **0.06%** density change — confirming why liquids are treated as **incompressible** in nearly all routine hydraulic engineering.

---

### 3.4 Advanced Numerical (Celerity Application)

**Problem:** Water in a steel penstock of diameter $D = 0.5 \, \text{m}$ and wall thickness $e = 12 \, \text{mm}$ is suddenly stopped. Given: $K_{water} = 2.1 \times 10^9 \, \text{N/m}^2$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $E_{steel} = 2.0 \times 10^{11} \, \text{N/m}^2$.

**Find:** (a) Velocity of sound in water considering pipe elasticity, (b) Pressure rise if flow velocity was $2 \, \text{m/s}$ before closure.

**Part (a): Effective Bulk Modulus including pipe expansion**

The pipe wall expansion modifies the effective bulk modulus:

$$\frac{1}{K_{eff}} = \frac{1}{K_{water}} + \frac{D}{E_{steel} \cdot e}$$

$$\frac{1}{K_{eff}} = \frac{1}{2.1 \times 10^9} + \frac{0.5}{(2.0 \times 10^{11})(0.012)}$$

$$\frac{1}{K_{eff}} = 4.762 \times 10^{-10} + 2.083 \times 10^{-10} = 6.845 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$K_{eff} = 1.461 \times 10^9 \, \text{N/m}^2$$

Celerity:
$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K_{eff}}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.461 \times 10^9}{1000}} = 1208.7 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Part (b): Joukowsky Pressure Rise**

$$\Delta P = \rho \cdot c \cdot \Delta V = 1000 \times 1208.7 \times 2 = 2.417 \times 10^6 \, \text{N/m}^2 \approx 2.42 \, \text{MPa}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Note:** Without the pipe elasticity correction, the celerity would be $\sqrt{2.1 \times 10^9/1000} \approx 1449 \, \text{m/s}$, giving $\Delta P = 2.9 \, \text{MPa}$. Real pipe compliance **reduces surge pressure by ~16%** — a critical design insight.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Map: Bulk Modulus in Fluid Mechanics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID ELEMENT] --> B[Apply Hydrostatic Pressure dP]
    B --> C[Volume Changes: V to V-dV]
    C --> D[Volumetric Strain dV over V]
    D --> E[Compute K = -dP / dV over V]
    E --> F{Process Type?}
    F -->|Slow, Isothermal| G[Isothermal K sub T]
    F -->|Rapid, Adiabatic| H[Adiabatic K sub s]
    G --> I[Used for Gases in Steady State]
    H --> J[Used for Water Hammer and Sound]
    I --> K[Engineering Applications]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Hydraulic Accumulator Sizing]
    K --> M[Pipe Surge Analysis]
    K --> N[Pump and Turbine Design]
    K --> O[Pressure Calibrator Design]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Bulk Modulus in Hydraulic Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input: Pressure Change dP] --> B1[Bulk Modulus K of Fluid]
    A2[Input: Initial Volume V] --> B1
    A3[Input: Fluid Density rho] --> C1[Acoustic Celerity c]
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Wave Speed Equation c = sqrt K over rho]
    B1 --> E1[Volumetric Strain Calculator]
    A2 --> E1
    A1 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Output: dV over V]
    C1 --> G1[Water Hammer Module]
    A1 --> G1
    A4[Input: Pipe Diameter D] --> H1[Pipe Elasticity Module]
    A5[Input: Wall Thickness e] --> H1
    A6[Input: Young Modulus E] --> H1
    H1 --> G1
    G1 --> I1[Output: Surge Pressure deltaP]
    F1 --> J1[Compressibility Coefficient beta]
    B1 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[Output: 1 over K]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Schematic: Liquid vs Gas Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Apply Pressure dP] --> B{Fluid State}
    B -->|Liquid| C[Strong Intermolecular Bonds]
    B -->|Gas| D[Weak Intermolecular Bonds]
    C --> E[Volume barely changes]
    D --> F[Volume reduces significantly]
    E --> G[High K approx 2 GPa]
    F --> H[Low K approx 0.1 MPa]
    G --> I[Treated as INCOMPRESSIBLE in most flows]
    H --> J[Treated as COMPRESSIBLE]
    I --> K[Use K for surge and celerity only]
    J --> L[Use ideal gas law with K = gamma P]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Q1. Define Bulk Modulus of a fluid. State its SI unit and the value for water at standard conditions.**

**Model Answer:**

The Bulk Modulus ($K$) of a fluid is defined as the ratio of the increase in hydrostatic pressure (stress) to the corresponding volumetric strain (relative decrease in volume) of the fluid element. Mathematically:

$$K = - \frac{dP}{(dV/V)}$$

- **SI Unit:** $\text{N/m}^2$ or Pascal (Pa)
- **Dimensional Formula:** $[M^1 L^{-1} T^{-2}]$
- **Value for water at 20°C:** $K \approx 2.1 \times 10^9 \, \text{N/m}^2$ (or $2.1 \, \text{GPa}$)

> **Mark Allocation (3 marks):** Definition (1.5 marks) + Unit & value (1 mark) + Equation (0.5 mark)

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]**

**Q2. Differentiate between isothermal and adiabatic bulk modulus. For which type of process is the adiabatic bulk modulus relevant in water hammer analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Isothermal Bulk Modulus ($K_T$) | Adiabatic Bulk Modulus ($K_s$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Condition** | Temperature constant ($T = \text{const}$) | Entropy constant ($s = \text{const}$) |
| **Heat Transfer** | Allowed (slow process) | No heat transfer (rapid process) |
| **Relation** | $K_T = -V \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_T$ | $K_s = -V \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_s$ |
| **Gas Relation** | $K_s = \gamma \cdot K_T$ | $K_s = \gamma \cdot K_T$ |
| **Application** | Slow compression, gas storage | Rapid wave propagation |

For water hammer analysis, valve closure occurs in **fractions of a second**, hence the compression is effectively **adiabatic** (no time for heat exchange). Therefore, **$K_s$** is used.

> **Mark Allocation (3 marks):** Table/definitions (2 marks) + Application justification (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 1]**

#### ⭐ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the relationship $K = \rho \cdot c^2$ between the bulk modulus of a fluid and the velocity of sound propagation in it, clearly stating the assumptions involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A liquid of bulk modulus $1.96 \times 10^9 \, \text{N/m}^2$ and density $850 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ flows at $1.8 \, \text{m/s}$ in a $300 \, \text{mm}$ diameter, $8 \, \text{mm}$ thick steel pipe. Calculate:
  (i) The velocity of sound (celerity) in the liquid considering pipe elasticity. Given $E_{steel} = 2.0 \times 10^{11} \, \text{N/m}^2$.
  (ii) The pressure surge generated by sudden valve closure.
  (iii) The percentage change in fluid density under the surge. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]:**

**Assumption 1:** The fluid obeys the continuity equation (mass is conserved).
**Assumption 2:** The fluid is inviscid and obeys Euler's equation of motion.
**Assumption 3:** The process is **adiabatic** (no heat transfer), making $K = K_s$.
**Assumption 4:** Acoustic perturbations are infinitesimally small (linearization valid).

**Step 1:** Linearized 1-D continuity:
$$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \frac{\partial u'}{\partial x} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Linearized 1-D momentum (Euler's equation):
$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial u'}{\partial t} = - \frac{\partial P'}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate continuity w.r.t. $t$ and momentum w.r.t. $x$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} = \rho_0 \frac{\partial^2 u'}{\partial x \, \partial t} \quad \text{and} \quad \rho_0 \frac{\partial^2 u'}{\partial x \, \partial t} = \frac{\partial^2 P'}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 4:** Eliminate cross derivatives:
$$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} = \frac{\partial^2 P'}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 5:** For adiabatic sound propagation, $P' = c^2 \rho'$:
$$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 6:** Recognizing this as a wave equation, the wave speed is $c$. By definition:
$$c^2 = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_s$$

**Step 7:** Recall the density-based bulk modulus identity (from Derivation 1):
$$K_s = \rho_0 \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_s$$

**Step 8:** Substitute Step 6 into Step 7:
$$\boxed{K_s = \rho_0 \cdot c^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{\rho_0}}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]:**

**Given:** $K_{liq} = 1.96 \times 10^9 \, \text{N/m}^2$, $\rho = 850 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $V = 1.8 \, \text{m/s}$, $D = 0.3 \, \text{m}$, $e = 0.008 \, \text{m}$, $E = 2.0 \times 10^{11} \, \text{N/m}^2$.

**(i) Effective Bulk Modulus (with pipe elasticity):**

$$\frac{1}{K_{eff}} = \frac{1}{K_{liq}} + \frac{D}{E \cdot e}$$

$$\frac{1}{K_{eff}} = \frac{1}{1.96 \times 10^9} + \frac{0.3}{(2.0 \times 10^{11})(0.008)}$$

$$\frac{1}{K_{eff}} = 5.102 \times 10^{-10} + 1.875 \times 10^{-10} = 6.977 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$K_{eff} = 1.4333 \times 10^9 \, \text{N/m}^2$$

Celerity:
$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K_{eff}}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.4333 \times 10^9}{850}} = \sqrt{1.6862 \times 10^6} = 1298.5 \, \text{m/s}$$

**(ii) Surge Pressure (Joukowsky Equation):**

$$\Delta P = \rho \cdot c \cdot \Delta V = 850 \times 1298.5 \times 1.8 = 1.9867 \times 10^6 \, \text{N/m}^2 \approx 1.99 \, \text{MPa}$$

**(iii) Percentage Change in Density:**

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} = \frac{dP}{K_{eff}} = \frac{1.9867 \times 10^6}{1.4333 \times 10^9} = 1.386 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\text{Percentage change} = 0.1386\%$$

> **Mark Allocation:** (i) 2.5 marks, (ii) 2.5 marks, (iii) 2 marks.

---

#### ⭐ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**(a)** Explain the concept of Bulk Modulus and Coefficient of Compressibility. Derive an expression for the relationship between bulk modulus and density for a compressible fluid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic oil in a closed cylinder is compressed from an initial volume of $2.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3$ to $1.998 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3$ under a pressure increase of $3.5 \, \text{MPa}$. Calculate:
  (i) The bulk modulus of the oil.
  (ii) The coefficient of compressibility.
  (iii) The velocity of sound through this oil, given $\rho = 920 \, \text{kg/m}^3$.
  (iv) Comment on whether the incompressibility assumption is justified. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Theory + Derivation [7 marks]:**

**Definitions [2 marks]:**
- **Bulk Modulus ($K$):** The ratio of hydrostatic pressure increase to the volumetric strain produced.
- **Coefficient of Compressibility ($\beta_c$):** The reciprocal of bulk modulus, representing fractional volume change per unit pressure.

$$\beta_c = \frac{1}{K} = - \frac{(dV/V)}{dP}$$

**Derivation [5 marks]:**

**Step 1:** Consider a fluid element with initial volume $V$ and density $\rho$. Mass is $m = \rho V$.

**Step 2:** Apply pressure increase $dP$ → volume becomes $V - dV$, density becomes $\rho + d\rho$.

**Step 3:** Mass conservation:
$$m = \rho V = (\rho + d\rho)(V - dV)$$

**Step 4:** Expanding and neglecting the second-order term $(d\rho)(dV)$:
$$0 = V \, d\rho - \rho \, dV \quad \Rightarrow \quad V \, d\rho = \rho \, dV$$

**Step 5:** Hence:
$$\frac{dV}{V} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} \quad \text{(with proper sign)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dV}{V} = - \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute into bulk modulus definition:
$$K = - \frac{dP}{(dV/V)} = \frac{dP}{(d\rho/\rho)} = \rho \cdot \frac{dP}{d\rho}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]:**

**Given:** $V_1 = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3$, $V_2 = 1.998 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3$, $dP = 3.5 \times 10^6 \, \text{N/m}^2$, $\rho = 920 \, \text{kg/m}^3$.

**Change in volume:**
$$dV = V_1 - V_2 = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} - 1.998 \times 10^{-3} = 2.0 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^3$$

**(i) Bulk Modulus:**

$$\frac{dV}{V_1} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-6}}{2.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$K = \frac{dP}{(dV/V_1)} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^6}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.5 \times 10^{9} \, \text{N/m}^2 = 3.5 \, \text{GPa}$$

**(ii) Coefficient of Compressibility:**

$$\beta_c = \frac{1}{K} = \frac{1}{3.5 \times 10^9} = 2.857 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{m}^2/\text{N}$$

**(iii) Velocity of Sound:**

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.5 \times 10^9}{920}} = \sqrt{3.804 \times 10^6} = 1950.5 \, \text{m/s}$$

**(iv) Justification of Incompressibility Assumption:**

$$\frac{dV}{V} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1\%$$

A volume change of only **0.1%** for a pressure change of $3.5 \, \text{MPa}$ confirms the oil is **highly incompressible**. The incompressibility assumption is **justified** for steady-state analysis, but should be **relaxed for transient events** like valve closure (water hammer) where surge pressures amplify local density changes.

> **Mark Allocation:** (i) 1.5 marks, (ii) 1.5 marks, (iii) 2 marks, (iv) 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
>
> 1. **Missing the negative sign** in the bulk modulus definition. $K$ must always be **positive**; the negative sign cancels the negative $dV$ sign.
> 2. **Confusing isothermal vs adiabatic** for gases. If a problem says "sound" or "rapid" or "water hammer", use $K_s = \gamma K_T$. If it says "slow" or "isothermal", use $K_T$.
> 3. **Forgetting pipe elasticity correction** in water hammer problems. Real penstocks expand, and the **effective bulk modulus is lower** than the pure fluid value — affecting the celerity.
> 4. **Mixing units:** Pressure must be in Pa (not bar or MPa without conversion). $1 \, \text{MPa} = 10^6 \, \text{Pa}$. Markers deduct 0.5 marks per unit mismatch.
> 5. **Not specifying adiabatic/isothermal** in the final answer for gases — always state which $K$ you have computed.
> 6. **Missing the assumption statement** in derivations: 1 mark is reserved in KTU for explicitly listing assumptions in any derivation question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — BULK MODULUS**

- 🔑 **Master Definition:** $K = - \dfrac{dP}{(dV/V)}$ — Bulk Modulus is the ratio of pressure rise to volumetric strain.
- 🔑 **Sign Convention:** Always positive. Negative sign in the formula balances the negative $dV$.
- 🔑 **Units & Dimensions:** SI unit is $\text{N/m}^2$ (Pa). Dimensions $[M L^{-1} T^{-2}]$.
- 🔑 **Reciprocal Relation:** Coefficient of compressibility $\beta_c = 1/K$.
- 🔑 **Density Form:** $K = \rho \cdot \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}$ — derived from mass conservation.
- 🔑 **Acoustic Form:** $K_s = \rho c^2$ — used for sound waves and water hammer (Joukowsky).
- 🔑 **Celerity Equation:** $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}}$ — for liquids, $c \approx 1400 \, \text{m/s}$; for air, $c \approx 340 \, \text{m/s}$.
- 🔑 **Gas Relation:** $K_s = \gamma \cdot K_T$ — for air $\gamma = 1.4$.
- 🔑 **Pressure-Dependent Liquid Form:** $K = K_0 + nP$ (where $n \approx 7$ for water).
- 🔑 **Water Hammer Surge Pressure:** $\Delta P = \rho \cdot c \cdot \Delta V$ — derived from celerity.
- 🔑 **Effective Bulk Modulus with Pipe Elasticity:** $\dfrac{1}{K_{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{K_{fluid}} + \dfrac{D}{E \cdot e}$
- 🔑 **Typical Water Value:** $K_{water} \approx 2.1 \times 10^9 \, \text{N/m}^2$ — memorize this.
- 🔑 **Typical Air Value:** $K_{air} \approx 1.01 \times 10^5 \, \text{N/m}^2$ — about 20,000× smaller than water.
- 🔑 **Incompressibility Justification:** For liquids, $\Delta V/V < 0.1\%$ in normal engineering pressure ranges, validating the incompressibility assumption in Bernoulli and continuity equations.
- 🔑 **Volumetric Strain–Density Strain Link:** $\dfrac{dV}{V} = - \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}$ — a corner stone for any derivation question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compressibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Compressibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Compressibility** of a fluid is defined as the fractional decrease in volume (or equivalently, the fractional increase in density) per unit increase in pressure, under isothermal conditions. It is mathematically expressed as the reciprocal of the **Bulk Modulus of Elasticity (K)**.

$$\beta = \frac{1}{K} = -\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T = \frac{1}{\rho}\left(\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

Where:
- $\beta$ = Compressibility (Pa⁻¹ or m²/N)
- **K** = Bulk Modulus of Elasticity (Pa or N/m²)
- $V$ = Volume of the fluid element
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid
- $P$ = Applied pressure
- $T$ = Temperature (held constant for isothermal process)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For Board Examinations, remember that compressibility and bulk modulus are NOT independent properties — they are exact reciprocals of one another. Many students lose marks by mixing up the sign convention in the partial derivative.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you have a fully sealed, air-tight plastic water bottle. If you squeeze it gently, the volume inside decreases ever so slightly, and the density of the water increases. The *ease* with which you can squeeze it (i.e., how much it gives way per unit pressure) is the **compressibility**. Liquids like water are **very stiff** — they barely change volume even under enormous pressure, so their compressibility is extremely small (and their bulk modulus is very high).

Now imagine the same experiment with a marshmallow or a sponge filled with air. Air collapses easily — its compressibility is huge (bulk modulus is small). Gases are therefore highly compressible, while liquids are nearly **incompressible** under typical engineering conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (used in Hydraulic Machines):** For liquids (especially water), we often assume incompressibility when flow velocities are less than ~100 m/s. For gases, compressibility cannot be ignored above Mach 0.3.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The bulk modulus of some common fluids (highlighted for KTU Board problems):

| Fluid | Bulk Modulus K (Pa) | Compressibility β (Pa⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|
| Water (20°C) | **2.2 × 10⁹** | **4.5 × 10⁻¹⁰** |
| Mercury | 2.8 × 10¹⁰ | 3.5 × 10⁻¹¹ |
| Air (atmos. cond.) | 1.01 × 10⁵ | 9.9 × 10⁻⁶ |
| Oil (typical) | 1.5 × 10⁹ | 6.7 × 10⁻¹⁰ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Trick:** Notice that the bulk modulus of water is roughly **20,000 times** that of air. This is why liquids are treated as incompressible in most hydraulic machine problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

The phenomenon of compressibility can be decomposed into the following structured logical flow:

- **Step 1 — Pressure Application:** An external pressure $dP$ is applied uniformly on a fluid element of volume $V$.
- **Step 2 — Volume Response:** The fluid resists compression by an internal restoring force. As a result, its volume decreases by an amount $dV$ (negative sign in the definition).
- **Step 3 — Density Response:** Because mass is conserved but volume shrinks, the density increases by $d\rho$ (positive sign).
- **Step 4 — Mathematical Quantification:** The ratio of the fractional volume change to the applied pressure gives compressibility $\beta$. Its reciprocal $K$ measures fluid stiffness.
- **Step 5 — Process Classification:**
  - **Isothermal Bulk Modulus ($K_T$):** Process occurs at constant temperature (typical for slow processes, liquids).
  - **Adiabatic Bulk Modulus ($K_S$):** Process occurs at constant entropy (typical for fast processes like sound waves, gases).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\beta = -\dfrac{1}{V}\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial P}$ | Compressibility definition | Pa⁻¹ |
| 2 | $K = -\dfrac{V \, dP}{dV} = \rho \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}$ | Bulk Modulus of Elasticity | Pa |
| 3 | $K_T = p$ (ideal gas, isothermal) | Isothermal K for ideal gas | Pa |
| 4 | $K_S = \gamma p$ (ideal gas, adiabatic) | Adiabatic K for ideal gas | Pa |
| 5 | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}}$ | Speed of sound in fluid | m/s |
| 6 | $M = \dfrac{V}{c}$ | Mach number (compressibility indicator) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V \, \Delta P}{K}$ | Volume change under pressure | m³ |
| 8 | $\Delta \rho = \dfrac{\rho \, \Delta P}{K}$ | Density change under pressure | kg/m³ |
| 9 | $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$ | Specific heat ratio (air = 1.4) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** The ratio $K_S / K_T = \gamma$ is a frequent two-mark short-answer question in KTU boards. Memorize it!

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic Machines (Pumps & Turbines):** Cavitation prediction requires accurate bulk modulus to compute pressure wave speeds.
- **Water Hammer Analysis:** Sudden valve closure in a pipeline generates shock waves whose speed depends on $\sqrt{K/\rho}$.
- **Aerospace & Gas Dynamics:** Mach number classification (subsonic, sonic, supersonic) is built entirely on compressibility.
- **Hydraulic Accumulators:** Compressed gas (nitrogen) pre-charge stores energy — directly a compressibility application.
- **Submarine & Deep-Sea Engineering:** At 10 km ocean depth, pressure is ~1000 atm; water density increases by ~5% due to compressibility.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Starting from Compressibility Definition

**Starting Point:** The formal definition of compressibility is given as:

$$\beta = -\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)$$

**Step 1:** Multiply both sides by $-V$:

$$-\beta V = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Cross-multiply to obtain bulk modulus expression:

$$K = \frac{1}{\beta} = -V \frac{dP}{dV}$$

> **Logic note:** The negative sign ensures that $K$ is positive (an increase in pressure causes a *decrease* in volume).

**Step 3:** Convert the volume form into density form using mass conservation:
Since mass $m = \rho V$ is constant during compression, differentiate:

$$\frac{dm}{dt} = 0 \implies d(\rho V) = 0 \implies V \, d\rho + \rho \, dV = 0$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $dV$:

$$dV = -\frac{V}{\rho} d\rho$$

**Step 5:** Substitute this $dV$ back into the bulk modulus equation:

$$K = -V \frac{dP}{dV} = -V \frac{dP}{-\dfrac{V}{\rho} d\rho} = \rho \frac{dP}{d\rho}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{K = \rho \frac{dP}{d\rho}}$$

> **Interpretation:** This is the most board-friendly form — bulk modulus equals density times the slope of the pressure-density curve.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bulk Modulus of an Ideal Gas (Isothermal Process)

For an ideal gas at constant temperature:

$$P V = m R T = \text{constant}$$

**Step 1:** Differentiate both sides with respect to $P$:

$$\frac{d}{dP}(PV) = 0$$

$$V + P \frac{dV}{dP} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange:

$$P \frac{dV}{dP} = -V$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the bulk modulus definition $K = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV}$:

$$K_T = -V \cdot \frac{P}{-V} = P$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{K_T = P}$$

> **Logic note:** At standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa), $K_T$ for air equals exactly 101.325 kPa — this matches the table in Section 1.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bulk Modulus of an Ideal Gas (Adiabatic Process)

For an adiabatic (isentropic) process in an ideal gas:

$$P V^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 1:** Differentiate both sides:

$$V^{\gamma} + P \gamma V^{\gamma - 1} \frac{dV}{dP} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange:

$$P \gamma V^{\gamma - 1} \frac{dV}{dP} = -V^{\gamma}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the powers of $V$:

$$P \gamma \frac{dV}{dP} = -V$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\dfrac{dV}{dP}$:

$$\frac{dV}{dP} = -\frac{V}{\gamma P}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back into the bulk modulus formula:

$$K_S = -V \cdot \frac{dP}{dV} = -V \cdot \frac{-\gamma P}{V} = \gamma P$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{K_S = \gamma P}$$

> **Logic note:** For air, $\gamma = 1.4$, so $K_S = 1.4 \times P$. Since $K_S > K_T$, gas is *stiffer* during rapid compression than slow compression — this is why sound travels faster than the rate of slow pressure diffusion.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Speed of Sound in a Fluid

Newton's original derivation begins from the bulk modulus definition. Consider a pressure disturbance $dP$ propagating through a fluid at speed $c$.

**Step 1:** The pressure disturbance causes a density change:

$$d\rho = \frac{\rho \, dP}{K}$$

(from Section 3.1 result)

**Step 2:** Using the continuity equation for a 1-D wave, the relationship between velocity $V$, density change, and wave speed is:

$$dP = \rho c \, dV = \rho c^2 \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

(after substituting $dV = c \, d\rho / \rho$ from continuity)

**Step 3:** Equate both expressions for $dP$:

$$\rho c^2 \frac{d\rho}{\rho} = \frac{K}{\rho} d\rho$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $d\rho$ on both sides and multiply by $\rho$:

$$\rho c^2 = K$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $c$:

$$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}}}$$

**Step 6 (KTU Special Case):** For air at standard conditions ($K = 1.4 \times 101325 = 1.418 \times 10^5$ Pa, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³):

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{1.418 \times 10^5}{1.225}} \approx 340 \, \text{m/s}$$

This matches the experimental value — confirming the derivation.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison

| Process | Governing Equation | Bulk Modulus K | Speed of Sound c |
|---|---|---|---|
| Liquid (isothermal) | Empirical K value | ~2.2 × 10⁹ Pa (water) | ~1480 m/s |
| Gas, Isothermal | PV = const | $P$ | $\sqrt{P/\rho}$ |
| Gas, Adiabatic | PV$^\gamma$ = const | $\gamma P$ | $\sqrt{\gamma P / \rho}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Compressibility Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Fluid Element Under Compression"] --> B["Apply External Pressure dP"]
    B --> C["Volume Decreases by dV"]
    B --> D["Density Increases by drho"]
    C --> E["Fractional Change dV / V"]
    D --> F["Fractional Change drho / rho"]
    E --> G["Compressibility beta = minus 1 over V dV dP"]
    F --> H["Bulk Modulus K = rho dP drho"]
    G --> I["Reciprocal Relationship beta times K equals 1"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Process Type"]
    J --> K1["Isothermal K equals P"]
    J --> K2["Adiabatic K equals gamma P"]
    K1 --> L["Speed of Sound c equals sqrt K over rho"]
    K2 --> L
    L --> M["Mach Number M equals V over c"]
    M --> N["Flow Classification"]
    N --> O1["Incompressible M less than 0.3"]
    N --> O2["Compressible M greater than 0.3"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Application in Hydraulic Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Pump / Compressor Unit"] --> V1["Control Valve"]
    V1 --> L["Pipeline Conduit"]
    L --> V2["Downstream Valve"]
    V2 --> T["Turbine / Load"]
    L -.->|"Sudden Closure"| S["Water Hammer Shock"]
    S -->|"Uses c = sqrt K over rho"| W["Wave Speed Computation"]
    W --> R["Pressure Surge Calculation"]
    R --> EM["Engineered Mitigation: Surge Tanks, Accumulators"]
    EM -.->|"Feedback"| P
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The dashed lines indicate the *feedback / analysis loop*. In a real KTU hydraulic-machine numerical, you would be asked to compute the surge pressure $\Delta P = \rho c \Delta V$ at point S after a sudden valve closure at V2.

### 4.3 State Diagram — Isothermal vs Adiabatic Comparison

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Undisturbed
    Undisturbed --> SlowCompression: Isothermal
    Undisturbed --> RapidCompression: Adiabatic
    SlowCompression --> K_T: K equals P
    RapidCompression --> K_S: K equals gamma P
    K_T --> SoundSpeedLow: c equals sqrt P over rho
    K_S --> SoundSpeedHigh: c equals sqrt gamma P over rho
    SoundSpeedLow --> [*]
    SoundSpeedHigh --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
Define the term **Bulk Modulus of Elasticity** and state its SI unit. How is it related to compressibility?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Bulk Modulus of Elasticity ($K$) is defined as the ratio of the increase in pressure to the fractional decrease in volume of a fluid element. Mathematically,

$$K = -\frac{dP}{dV/V} = -V \frac{dP}{dV}$$

Its SI unit is **N/m²** or **Pascal (Pa)**.  
Bulk modulus is the exact reciprocal of compressibility: $K = 1/\beta$, where $\beta$ has units of Pa⁻¹.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**  
Distinguish between **isothermal** and **adiabatic** bulk moduli for an ideal gas. Give the expressions.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Process | Condition | Expression |
|---|---|---|
| Isothermal | Temperature constant | $K_T = P$ |
| Adiabatic | Entropy constant | $K_S = \gamma P$ |

The ratio is $K_S / K_T = \gamma$, the specific heat ratio. For air ($\gamma = 1.4$), adiabatic bulk modulus is 40% larger than isothermal.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** Each Module in the End Semester Examination carries 14 marks with **internal choice**. Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B (not both). Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **bulk modulus of an ideal gas undergoing an isothermal process**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** The speed of sound in water is measured as **1480 m/s**. Given the density of water as **1000 kg/m³**, determine the **bulk modulus of water**. State two practical situations where this property is significant. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

For an isothermal process in an ideal gas:

$$P V = \text{constant}$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $P$:

$$V + P \frac{dV}{dP} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dV}{dP} = -\frac{V}{P}$$

Substituting into the definition $K = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV}$:

$$K_T = -V \cdot \frac{P}{-V} = P$$

$$\boxed{K_T = P}$$

> **Mark Allocation:** Equation statement — 2 marks, differentiation — 3 marks, final substitution — 2 marks.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $c = 1480$ m/s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³.

Using the speed of sound relation:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$c^2 = \frac{K}{\rho}$$

Solving for $K$:

$$K = \rho \, c^2 = 1000 \times (1480)^2$$

Computing:

$$(1480)^2 = 2{,}190{,}400$$

$$K = 1000 \times 2.19 \times 10^6 = 2.19 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{K \approx 2.19 \, \text{GPa}}$$

**Two practical situations (2 of these, 1 mark each):**
1. **Water hammer analysis** in pipelines — sudden valve closures generate pressure surges whose magnitude depends on $\sqrt{K/\rho}$.
2. **Cavitation prediction** in pumps and turbines — local pressure drops are compared against vapor pressure using bulk modulus.
3. **Design of hydraulic accumulators** — gas pre-charge calculations rely on accurate $K$.
4. **Sonar / ultrasonic flow metering** — sound propagation in seawater depends on $K$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **speed of sound in a fluid** in terms of its bulk modulus and density. Explain why the speed of sound in air is higher under adiabatic conditions than isothermal conditions. (7 Marks)

**(b)** For air at standard atmospheric conditions ($\gamma = 1.4$, $P = 101.325$ kPa, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³), calculate the **Mach number** of an aircraft flying at **250 m/s**. Comment on the compressibility effect. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

Starting from the bulk modulus formula:

$$K = \rho \frac{dP}{d\rho}$$

For a small pressure disturbance $dP$ traveling at speed $c$, the continuity equation gives:

$$d\rho = \frac{\rho \, dV}{c}$$

Substituting back:

$$dP = K \frac{d\rho}{\rho} = K \frac{dV}{c}$$

Also, from the momentum equation for a sound wave:

$$dP = \rho c \, dV$$

Equating both expressions:

$$K \frac{dV}{c} = \rho c \, dV$$

Cancelling $dV$ and multiplying by $c$:

$$K = \rho c^2$$

$$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}}}$$

**Why adiabatic > isothermal (3 Marks):**  
For sound, compression occurs so rapidly that there is no time for heat exchange — the process is **adiabatic**. Hence $K_S = \gamma P$ instead of $K_T = P$. Since $\gamma > 1$, we have $K_S > K_T$, and therefore $c_S > c_T$. Numerically, $c_S / c_T = \sqrt{\gamma} = \sqrt{1.4} \approx 1.183$, a difference of 18.3%.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $V = 250$ m/s, $\gamma = 1.4$, $P = 101.325$ kPa, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.

**Step 1:** Compute adiabatic bulk modulus:

$$K_S = \gamma P = 1.4 \times 101.325 \times 10^3 = 1.41855 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2:** Compute speed of sound:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K_S}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.41855 \times 10^5}{1.225}}$$

$$c = \sqrt{1.158 \times 10^5} = 340.3 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Compute Mach number:

$$M = \frac{V}{c} = \frac{250}{340.3} \approx 0.735$$

$$\boxed{M \approx 0.735}$$

**Comment on compressibility (2 Marks):**  
Since $M = 0.735 > 0.3$, the flow is in the **compressible regime (high subsonic)**. Density variation along the flow cannot be neglected, and one must apply compressible flow equations (e.g., Prandtl-Glauert correction) for accurate aerodynamic analysis.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing negative sign:** Many students write $K = V(dP/dV)$ without the minus sign, making $K$ negative. Always include the minus.
> 2. **Wrong formula for adiabatic process:** Writing $K = \gamma^2 P$ instead of $K = \gamma P$ costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using isothermal K for sound problems:** Sound propagation is *always* adiabatic — using $K_T$ gives wrong numerical value (~290 m/s instead of ~340 m/s).
> 4. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert kPa to Pa in the bulk modulus calculation is the single most common 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Confusing Mach classification:** $M < 0.3$ is incompressible, NOT $M < 1$. Subsonic flow can still be compressible.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Compressibility $\beta$** = fractional volume decrease per unit pressure (Pa⁻¹).
- **Bulk Modulus $K$** = fractional volume decrease per unit pressure, reciprocal of $\beta$ (Pa).
- **Isothermal:** $K_T = P$ | **Adiabatic:** $K_S = \gamma P$ | Ratio: $K_S / K_T = \gamma$.
- **Speed of sound:** $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ — always use $K_S$ for gases, since sound is a rapid (adiabatic) process.
- **Water:** $K \approx 2.2$ GPa → $c \approx 1480$ m/s. **Air:** $K \approx 1.42 \times 10^5$ Pa → $c \approx 340$ m/s.
- **Mach number:** $M = V/c$. Compressibility effects become significant for $M > 0.3$.
- **Engineering applications:** water hammer, cavitation, hydraulic accumulators, sonar, aerospace.
- **Sign convention:** always use the minus sign in $K = -V(dP/dV)$ to keep $K > 0$.
- **Air specific heat ratio:** $\gamma = 1.4$ (memorize this constant for KTU exams).
- **Rule of thumb:** Liquids → incompressible (unless extremely high pressure); Gases → compressible (unless $M < 0.3$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Velocity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Velocity in Fluid Mechanics

In fluid mechanics, **velocity** is defined as the **vector quantity that specifies the rate of change of position of a fluid particle with respect to time**, including both its magnitude (speed) and its direction of motion at a given instant and point in space.

Mathematically, for a fluid particle moving along a path, the instantaneous velocity vector $\vec{V}$ is expressed as:

$$\vec{V} = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta \vec{s}}{\Delta t} = \frac{d\vec{s}}{dt}$$

In a Cartesian coordinate system, the velocity vector has three orthogonal components:

$$\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$$

where:
- $u = \dfrac{dx}{dt}$ → velocity component along the **x-axis**
- $v = \dfrac{dy}{dt}$ → velocity component along the **y-axis**
- $w = \dfrac{dz}{dt}$ → velocity component along the **z-axis**

The **magnitude of velocity (speed)** is:

$$\vert \vec{V} \vert = \sqrt{u^2 + v^2 + w^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Velocity is treated as a *field variable* in fluid mechanics. Unlike rigid-body mechanics, velocity is defined **at every point in a flow domain**, not just at the center of mass of a body.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The River Current**
>
> Imagine standing on a bridge over a wide river. The water surface does not move as a single block — instead, water at different locations moves at different speeds and directions.
>
> - Right at the **center of the river**, the water rushes fastest (high magnitude).
> - Near the **river banks**, the water drags along the stones and slows down.
> - A **leaf floating** in the middle moves faster than one near the edge.
>
> If we could attach a tiny GPS tracker to every water particle and record its position every second, the **velocity** is exactly how fast and in what direction that GPS dot moves between two instants.
>
> - **Speed** = how fast (e.g., 2 m/s).
> - **Direction** = the heading (e.g., downstream, slightly tilted).
> - **Velocity** = both put together as a single arrow.

So when KTU problems say "velocity at point P", they are asking you to find **both the magnitude and the direction** of motion at that exact point in the fluid.

## 1.3 Classification of Velocity

### (a) Based on Variation with Position

| Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Uniform velocity** | Magnitude and direction are **same at every point** in the flow field | Flow through a constant-area straight pipe (ideal case) |
| **Non-uniform velocity** | Magnitude or direction (or both) **change from point to point** | Flow through a converging nozzle |

### (b) Based on Variation with Time

| Type | Definition | Mathematical Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **Steady (time-independent)** | Velocity at a point does **not change with time** | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0$ |
| **Unsteady (time-dependent)** | Velocity at a point **changes with time** | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} \neq 0$ |

### (c) Based on Dimensionality

| Type | Velocity Components | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **One-dimensional (1-D)** | Only one non-zero component | Flow in a straight pipe |
| **Two-dimensional (2-D)** | Two non-zero components | Flow past a long cylinder |
| **Three-dimensional (3-D)** | All three components non-zero | Flow around an aircraft wing tip |

### (d) Specialised Velocities in Pipe/Channel Flow

| Velocity Type | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Local / Point velocity** | $u$ | Velocity at a single point in the cross-section |
| **Average / Mean velocity** | $V$ | Velocity uniformly distributed across the cross-section that would give the same discharge |
| **Maximum velocity** | $u_{max}$ | Highest velocity, typically at the centre for laminar flow |

The relationship between average and local velocity:

$$V = \frac{1}{A} \int_A u \, dA$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constant:** The **kinematic viscosity** $\nu$ (with SI units of **m²/s**) appears in many velocity-related relations (e.g., Reynolds number, Navier–Stokes). Its value for water at 20 °C is approximately **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Velocity Field — Concept and Notation

In fluid mechanics, instead of tracking individual particles, we adopt the **Eulerian approach**, where velocity is treated as a **field function** of space and time:

$$\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$$

This means: *"At any spatial coordinate $(x, y, z)$ and at any instant $t$, the fluid has a velocity $\vec{V}$."*

In component form:

$$u = u(x, y, z, t), \quad v = v(x, y, z, t), \quad w = w(x, y, z, t)$$

## 2.2 Streamlines — Visualising the Velocity Field

A **streamline** is an imaginary curve drawn through the flow field such that the **velocity vector at every point on the curve is tangent to the curve** at that instant.

The differential equation of a streamline in 2-D is:

$$\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}$$

**Why streamlines matter (engineering utility):**
- They visualize flow patterns around aircraft wings, ships, turbine blades.
- They never cross each other (in steady flow), because at any crossing the velocity would have two different directions simultaneously.
- They are used as the basis of **stream function** $\psi$ in 2-D flow analysis.

## 2.3 Pathline, Streakline, and Timeline — The Three Fundamental Flow Visualisations

| Concept | Definition | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Pathline** | The actual trajectory followed by a **single fluid particle** over time | Photograph with long exposure of a marked particle |
| **Streakline** | The locus of all particles that have **previously passed through a given point** | Continuous injection of dye at a fixed point |
| **Timeline** | A set of fluid particles that **occupied a line in space at some earlier instant** | A line of bubbles released simultaneously |

> [!NOTE]
> **For STEADY flow, all three (pathline, streakline, timeline) are identical to the streamline.** This is a frequently tested KTU conceptual point.

## 2.4 Acceleration Derived from the Velocity Field

A fluid particle does not have a fixed identity in space — it moves. To find the acceleration of the particle, we apply the **material derivative** (also called *substantial* or *total* derivative):

$$\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \left(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla\right) \vec{V}$$

In expanded component form:

$$a_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$

This decomposition has a deep physical meaning:

| Term | Name | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$ | **Local acceleration** | Unsteadiness — velocity changes at a fixed point |
| $\left(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla\right)\vec{V}$ | **Convective acceleration** | The particle moves into a region where velocity is different |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the flow is **steady**, local acceleration is zero. Yet the particle can still accelerate because it moves into regions of different velocity — that is the convective term. This is a classic KTU trap question!

## 2.5 Continuity Equation — Velocity-Based Mass Conservation

For an **incompressible fluid** (constant density $\rho$), conservation of mass reduces to the **continuity equation** in differential form:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$

In vector notation:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$

## 2.6 Stream Function and Velocity Potential — Elegant 2-D Tools

For **2-D, incompressible flow**, two scalar functions fully describe the velocity field:

### Velocity Potential $\phi$ (for irrotational flow)

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \qquad v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$$

### Stream Function $\psi$ (always exists for incompressible 2-D flow)

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The stream function automatically satisfies continuity, while the velocity potential automatically satisfies irrotationality ($\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$). Both reduce the problem from three unknowns ($u, v, p$) to one unknown.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Velocity

| # | Formula | Meaning / Where Used | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$ | Velocity vector in 3-D | m/s |
| 2 | $\vert \vec{V} \vert = \sqrt{u^2 + v^2 + w^2}$ | Speed (magnitude of velocity) | m/s |
| 3 | $u = dx/dt$ | Velocity component as time derivative | m/s |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$ | Streamline equation | dimensionless |
| 5 | $a_x = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z}$ | x-component of acceleration (material derivative) | m/s² |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$ | Continuity (incompressible, differential) | 1/s |
| 7 | $u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \; v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ | Stream function definition | m/s |
| 8 | $u = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \; v = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$ | Velocity potential definition | m/s |
| 9 | $V = \dfrac{1}{A}\int_A u \, dA$ | Average velocity across section | m/s |
| 10 | $\dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ | Material (total) acceleration | m/s² |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Material (Total) Acceleration

### Starting Premise
A fluid particle moves through space. At time $t$, its position is $(x, y, z)$. At time $t + dt$, it has moved to $(x + dx, y + dy, z + dz)$. Its velocity, which is a function of both position and time, has changed from $\vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$ to $\vec{V}(x + dx, y + dy, z + dz, t + dt)$.

### Step 1 — Total Differential of the Velocity Component $u$

We write the change in $u$ using a multivariable Taylor expansion:

$$du = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}dt + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}dz$$

### Step 2 — Divide Throughout by $dt$

$$\frac{du}{dt} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\frac{dz}{dt}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Kinematic Relations

By the chain rule applied to the moving particle:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = u, \quad \frac{dy}{dt} = v, \quad \frac{dz}{dt} = w$$

### Step 4 — Final Expression for the x-Component of Acceleration

$$a_x = \frac{du}{dt} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$

### Step 5 — Repeat by Symmetry for y and z Components

$$\begin{aligned}
a_y &= \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} \\[6pt]
a_z &= \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial w}{\partial z}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Compact Vector Form

Using the del-operator $\nabla = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\hat{i} + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}\hat{j} + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}\hat{k}$:

$$\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \underbrace{\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}}_{\text{Local}} + \underbrace{(\vec{V}\cdot \nabla)\vec{V}}_{\text{Convective}}$$

**Physical interpretation of each step:**
- Step 1 captures the fact that velocity changes because *time passes* and because *position shifts*.
- Step 2 normalises by the time interval to get an acceleration.
- Step 3 replaces the position rates with velocity components (this is the trick that couples kinematics to fluid mechanics).
- Step 4–6 compactly encode the result.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Continuity Equation for an Incompressible Fluid

### Step 1 — Control Volume Setup
Consider an elementary rectangular parallelepiped control volume with edges $dx$, $dy$, $dz$ centred at $(x, y, z)$.

### Step 2 — Mass Flow In (x-direction)
Mass entering the left face (at $x - dx/2$) per unit time:

$$\rho \cdot u \big|_{x - dx/2} \cdot dy \cdot dz$$

### Step 3 — Mass Flow Out (x-direction)
Mass leaving the right face (at $x + dx/2$) per unit time:

$$\rho \cdot u \big|_{x + dx/2} \cdot dy \cdot dz$$

### Step 4 — Net x-Direction Mass Flux Out
Subtracting and using Taylor expansion:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Net out (x)} &= \rho\left[u\big|_{x+dx/2} - u\big|_{x-dx/2}\right] dy \, dz \\[4pt]
&= \rho \cdot \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\, dx \, dy \, dz
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Sum Contributions From All Three Directions

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total net efflux} &= \rho\left[\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}\right] dx\,dy\,dz
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Apply Conservation of Mass
For incompressible flow, the net mass efflux from a fixed control volume must be zero (mass cannot accumulate or vanish inside the volume):

$$\rho\left[\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}\right] = 0$$

Since $\rho$ is constant for an incompressible fluid:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0\;}$$

Or in compact form:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$

This is the **continuity equation** for incompressible flow. It is purely a *velocity-based* constraint, expressing that velocity field lines diverge at zero rate.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Stream Function in 2-D

For an incompressible 2-D flow ($w = 0$), the continuity equation reduces to:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

### Step 1 — Introduce an Unknown Scalar $\psi(x, y)$
Let us postulate the existence of a scalar function $\psi$ such that:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute into Continuity

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x\,\partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x\,\partial y} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

The two terms cancel identically, satisfying continuity **for any smooth $\psi$**. Hence, defining $\psi$ this way is always valid.

### Step 3 — Volumetric Flow Rate Between Two Streamlines
The volume flow rate $q$ between any two streamlines $\psi_1$ and $\psi_2$ is constant:

$$q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$$

This is because the difference in $\psi$ values represents the volumetric flux per unit depth crossing any curve joining them — a key result exploited in designing flow channels and weirs.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Velocity Potential for Irrotational Flow

A flow is **irrotational** when the curl of velocity vanishes:

$$\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$$

In 2-D, this gives:

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$$

### Step 1 — Introduce a Scalar $\phi$
Define a scalar potential $\phi$ such that:

$$\vec{V} = \nabla \phi \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x},\;\; v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute into Irrotationality Condition

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x\,\partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y\,\partial x} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

This is automatically satisfied for any continuously differentiable $\phi$. Combining with continuity gives **Laplace's equation**:

$$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = 0$$

This is the foundation of classical potential-flow theory used in aerodynamics, hydrodynamics, and ground-water flow.

---

## 3.5 Worked Analytical Example — Finding Stream Function

**Problem:** A 2-D incompressible velocity field is given by $u = 3x + 2y$ and $v = 2x - 3y$. Determine whether a stream function exists, and if so, find $\psi$.

### Step 1 — Check Continuity

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial (3x + 2y)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (2x - 3y)}{\partial y} = 3 + (-3) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

Continuity is satisfied. Therefore, a stream function exists.

### Step 2 — Integrate Definition of $u$

From $u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}$:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = 3x + 2y$$

Integrating with respect to $y$:

$$\psi = 3xy + y^2 + f(x)$$

where $f(x)$ is an arbitrary function of $x$ alone.

### Step 3 — Use the Definition of $v$ to Find $f(x)$

$$v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -(3y + f'(x)) = 2x - 3y$$

Equating:

$$-3y - f'(x) = 2x - 3y \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; f'(x) = -2x$$

Integrating:

$$f(x) = -x^2 + C$$

### Step 4 — Final Stream Function

$$\boxed{\;\psi(x,y) = 3xy + y^2 - x^2 + C\;}$$

The constant $C$ has no physical meaning — only differences in $\psi$ matter.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Velocity Field Conceptual Map
