<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define velocity potential function. State Laplace equation.**

**Model Answer:**

A scalar function $\phi(x, y, z, t)$ whose gradient equals the velocity vector is called the **velocity potential function**. For irrotational flow:

$$\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$$

If the fluid is also incompressible, substituting into continuity $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ yields:

$$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

This is **Laplace's equation**, governing potential (irrotational) flow.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between streamline, pathline and streakline. When are they identical?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Streamline | Pathline | Streakline |
|---|---|---|---|
| Definition | Curve tangent to velocity field at an instant | Trajectory of a single particle | Locus of all particles that passed through a fixed point |
| Time concept | Instantaneous | Over a duration | Over a duration |
| For steady flow | Equal to pathline | Equal to streamline | Equal to streamline |

> **Identical in steady flow** — all three definitions collapse onto the same curve. In **unsteady flow** they generally differ.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the **continuity equation** for a 3-D incompressible flow in Cartesian coordinates. Explain its physical significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The velocity field in a flow is given by $\vec{V} = (3x + 2y)\hat{i} + (2x - 3y)\hat{j}$ m/s. Check whether the flow is **(i)** continuous, **(ii)** irrotational. Find the stream function if it exists. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### (a) Model Solution — Continuity Equation Derivation

**Step 1 — Control Volume:** Consider an elementary fluid element of dimensions $dx$, $dy$, $dz$ fixed in space with centre at $(x, y, z)$.

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate entering through the left face (at $x - dx/2$):**

$$\dot{m}_{in,x} = \rho \, u\big|_{x-dx/2} \, dy \, dz$$

**Step 3 — Mass flow rate leaving through the right face (at $x + dx/2$):**

$$\dot{m}_{out,x} = \rho \, u\big|_{x+dx/2} \, dy \, dz$$

**Step 4 — Net efflux in x-direction (using Taylor series):**

$$\Delta \dot{m}_x = \rho \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\, dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 5 — Repeat for y and z directions:**

$$\Delta \dot{m}_y = \rho \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\, dx\, dy\, dz$$
$$\Delta \dot{m}_z = \rho \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}\, dx\, dy\, dz$$

**Step 6 — Total net efflux must equal rate of change of mass inside the element:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho\left[\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}\right] dx\,dy\,dz = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\, dx\,dy\,dz
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — For incompressible flow** ($\rho = $ const, hence $\partial \rho / \partial t = 0$):

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0\;}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- It expresses that the **net volume flux out of any infinitesimal fluid element is zero** for incompressible flow.
- Mathematically equivalent to $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ — flow is **divergence-free** or **solenoidal**.
- Streamlines never begin or end inside the fluid; they either form closed loops or extend to infinity.

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Velocity Field Analysis

**Given:** $u = 3x + 2y$, $v = 2x - 3y$ (2-D, $w = 0$)

**(i) Check continuity:**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial(3x+2y)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(2x-3y)}{\partial y} = 3 + (-3) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

The flow is continuous (and hence a stream function exists).

**(ii) Check irrotationality:**

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial(2x-3y)}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial(3x+2y)}{\partial y} = 2 - 2 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

The flow is **irrotational**; hence a velocity potential also exists.

**Stream function:** Using $u = \partial \psi / \partial y$:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = 3x + 2y \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \psi = 3xy + y^2 + f(x)$$

Differentiating with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = 3y + f'(x)$$

From the definition $v = -\partial \psi / \partial x$:

$$2x - 3y = -(3y + f'(x)) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; f'(x) = -2x \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; f(x) = -x^2 + C$$

$$\boxed{\;\psi(x, y) = 3xy + y^2 - x^2 + C\;}$$

The arbitrary constant $C$ is dropped for physical relevance.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Define the **material derivative**. Derive expressions for the components of acceleration of a fluid particle in Cartesian coordinates, clearly identifying the local and convective parts. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fluid flow has velocity components $u = x^2 + y^2$, $v = 2xy$. Determine the acceleration of a fluid particle at the point $(1, 2, 0)$ at time $t = 0$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### (a) Model Solution — Material Derivative Derivation

**Definition:** The material derivative is the rate of change of a fluid property (such as velocity) **following a moving fluid particle**, as opposed to at a fixed point.

$$\frac{D}{Dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial}{\partial z}$$

**Derivation:**
A particle at $(x, y, z)$ at time $t$ moves to $(x + dx, y + dy, z + dz)$ at time $t + dt$. The change in $u$ is:

$$du = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}dt + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}dz$$

Dividing by $dt$ and using $dx/dt = u$, $dy/dt = v$, $dz/dt = w$:

$$a_x = \frac{Du}{Dt} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$

Similarly:

$$\begin{aligned}
a_y &= \frac{Dv}{Dt} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} \\[6pt]
a_z &= \frac{Dw}{Dt} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial w}{\partial z}
\end{aligned}$$

**Identification of terms:**
- $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t}, \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial t}, \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial t}$ → **Local acceleration** (zero for steady flow).
- $u\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z}$ → **Convective acceleration** (non-zero even in steady flow if velocity varies in space).

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Acceleration of a Fluid Particle

**Given:** $u = x^2 + y^2$, $v = 2xy$, $w = 0$ (also implicit steady flow since there is no $t$).

**Note:** Because the field is independent of time, local accelerations are zero.

**Compute x-acceleration:**

$$a_x = u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = (x^2+y^2)(2x) + (2xy)(2y) = 2x^3 + 2xy^2 + 4xy^2 = 2x^3 + 6xy^2$$

**Compute y-acceleration:**

$$a_y = u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = (x^2+y^2)(2y) + (2xy)(2x) = 2x^2y + 2y^3 + 4x^2y = 6x^2y + 2y^3$$

**Evaluate at $(x, y) = (1, 2)$:**

$$a_x = 2(1)^3 + 6(1)(2)^2 = 2 + 24 = 26 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$a_y = 6(1)^2(2) + 2(2)^3 = 12 + 16 = 28 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$\boxed{\;\vec{a} = 26\hat{i} + 28\hat{j} \;\text{m/s}^2\;}$$

**Magnitude:**

$$\vert \vec{a} \vert = \sqrt{26^2 + 28^2} = \sqrt{676 + 784} = \sqrt{1460} \approx 38.21 \text{ m/s}^2$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse partial derivative $\partial / \partial t$ with total derivative $D/Dt$.** Many students compute only the local term and miss the convective terms — this is the single biggest source of lost marks (typically 4 marks out of 7) on material derivative problems.
> 2. **Always explicitly state which terms vanish** for steady or uniform flow conditions; silent omissions are penalised.
> 3. **Sign error in the stream function definition** ($v = +\partial \psi/\partial x$ vs $-\partial \psi/\partial x$): double-check the textbook convention your module follows.
> 4. **For 3-D continuity,** writing only $\partial u/\partial x = 0$ instead of the full sum is incomplete — full marks require all three partial derivatives.
> 5. **Don't forget units** — they carry 0.5–1 mark in many KTU answer scripts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Velocity is a vector** — it always carries both magnitude (speed) and direction.
- In Cartesian form: $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$.
- **Speed (magnitude):** $\vert \vec{V} \vert = \sqrt{u^2 + v^2 + w^2}$.
- The velocity field $\vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$ is the foundation of the **Eulerian** description of fluid motion.
- **Material derivative** = local + convective acceleration — distinguishes fluid mechanics from rigid-body kinematics.
- For **steady flow** ($\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$), particle acceleration can still be non-zero due to convective effects.
- For **incompressible flow**, the **continuity equation** is $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$.
- **Stream function $\psi$** exists for any 2-D incompressible flow; $u = \partial \psi / \partial y$, $v = -\partial \psi / \partial x$.
- **Velocity potential $\phi$** exists only for **irrotational** flow; $u = \partial \phi / \partial x$, $v = \partial \phi / \partial y$.
- Streamlines, pathlines, streaklines are **identical in steady flow** but **differ in unsteady flow**.
- **Streamline equation** in 2-D: $dx/u = dy/v$.
- **Average velocity** across a section: $V = \dfrac{1}{A}\int_A u \, dA$.
- Standard SI unit of velocity = **m/s** (or **m·s⁻¹**); of acceleration = **m/s²**.
- Master the boundary between **local** and **convective** terms — it is the heart of every KTU Module 1 velocity problem.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rate of Shear Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Rate of Shear Strain # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 🔬 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Rate of Shear Strain** (also called the **Shear Rate** or **Velocity Gradient**) is defined as the **time rate of change of angular deformation** of a fluid element subjected to a shear stress. Mathematically, for a fluid element undergoing shear deformation: $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{d\gamma}{dt}$$ where: - $\dot{\gamma}$ = Rate of shear strain (in $\text{s}^{-1}$) - $\gamma$ = Shear strain (dimensionless) - $t$ = Time (in $\text{s}$) For a **Newtonian fluid** with a linear velocity profile, the rate of shear strain simplifies to the velocity gradient: $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{du}{dy}$$ where: - $u$ = Velocity of fluid layer in the $x$-direction (in $\text{m/s}$) - $y$ = Distance measured perpendicular to the direction of flow (in $\text{m}$) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Rate of Shear Strain is **NOT** a property of the fluid — it is a **kinematic quantity** that describes the *motion* of the fluid. It becomes the link between the kinematic study of fluid motion and the dynamic study of fluid forces (viscous stresses). --- ## 🍯 Conceptual Analogy: The Honey on Toast Imagine spreading a thick layer of **honey** on a slice of bread using a knife: - The knife (top surface) moves faster than the bread (bottom surface, stationary). - The honey layer **continuously deforms** as long as the knife slides. - The *faster* you spread, the *greater* the rate at which the honey layers slide past each other. In this analogy: | Real Object | Fluid Mechanics Equivalent | |-------------|----------------------------| | Knife velocity | Fluid layer velocity ($u$) | | Thickness of honey layer | Distance between layers ($dy$) | | How fast layers slide | **Rate of Shear Strain** ($\dot{\gamma}$) | | Stickiness of honey | Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) | | Force needed to pull the knife | Shear Stress ($\tau$) | The **unit** of rate of shear strain is the **reciprocal of time**, i.e., $\text{s}^{-1}$ or **1/second** (or sometimes written as $\text{seconds}^{-1}$). > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** *Shear strain* ($\gamma$) is the *total angular deformation* (dimensionless). *Rate of shear strain* ($\dot{\gamma}$) is the *speed at which this deformation happens* (in $\text{s}^{-1}$). Don't confuse the two in your KTU exam! --- ## 🌊 Why is Rate of Shear Strain Important? In any real fluid flow, layers of fluid move at different velocities (this is the **no-slip condition** at boundaries). The *measurement* of how quickly these layers deform is the rate of shear strain. It is the **fundamental input** to Newton's Law of Viscosity: $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ So if you know the rate of shear strain, you can directly compute the **viscous shear stress** in the fluid — which is essential for calculating friction losses in pipes, power required by pumps, and torque in viscometers. # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🧠 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Rate of Shear Strain ### Step-by-Step Conceptual Breakdown 1. **Fluid Element Selection:** Consider a tiny rectangular fluid element of dimensions $dx \times dy$ inside a flowing fluid. 2. **Shear Action:** The top face (at $y + dy$) moves with velocity $u + du$, while the bottom face (at $y$) moves with velocity $u$. 3. **Deformation:** This velocity difference causes the originally rectangular element to deform into a **parallelogram** over time $dt$. 4. **Angular Change:** The bottom-right corner angle changes from $\pi/2$ to $(\pi/2 - d\theta)$. The change in angle is $d\theta$ — this is the **shear strain increment**. 5. **Differentiation with Time:** Dividing $d\theta$ by $dt$ gives the rate at which angular deformation is happening — the **Rate of Shear Strain**. ### The Three Different Forms of the Same Concept In KTU exams, you may see rate of shear strain expressed in three equivalent ways: | Form | Expression | When Used | |------|-----------|-----------| | Angular form | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{d}{dt}(d\theta)$ | Derivation context | | Velocity gradient form | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | General flow problems | | Cylindrical form | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{2\pi N r}{h}$ | Rotational viscometer problems | where for the cylindrical form: - $N$ = Rotational speed in **revolutions per second (rev/s)** - $r$ = Radius of the rotating cylinder in $\text{m}$ - $h$ = Gap thickness between cylinders in $\text{m}$ --- ## 📊 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exams) > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize this table — at least **2 out of 6 KTU questions in Module 1** typically test these formulas directly. | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | Engineering Use | |---|-------------|-------------------------|-------|-----------------| | 1 | Shear Strain | $\gamma = \dfrac{dx}{dy}$ | Dimensionless | Measures angular deformation | | 2 | Rate of Shear Strain (General) | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{d\gamma}{dt}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | Time rate of deformation | | 3 | Velocity Gradient Form | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | Most common in flow problems | | 4 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | $\text{Pa}$ or $\text{N/m}^2$ | Links stress to velocity gradient | | 5 | Combined Form | $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Used in viscometry | | 6 | Rotational Viscometer Form | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{2\pi N r}{h}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | For concentric cylinder viscometer | | 7 | Kinematic Viscosity Relation | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | Often confused — remember it is **NOT** rate of shear strain! | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **kinematic viscosity** ($\nu$, in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$) with **rate of shear strain** ($\dot{\gamma}$, in $\text{s}^{-1}$). They have *similar-looking* units ($\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ vs $\text{s}^{-1}$), but they represent *completely different physical quantities*. Kinematic viscosity is a **fluid property**; rate of shear strain is a **flow property**. --- ## 🏗️ Real-World Engineering Applications - **🛢️ Petroleum Industry:** Calculating pressure drops when pumping crude oil through pipelines. Crude oil has high viscosity, so even small velocity gradients create significant shear stress. - **🩸 Biomedical Engineering:** Blood flow analysis in arteries. Blood is a non-Newtonian fluid, but the *concept* of rate of shear strain is still used to model its behavior. - **🍫 Food Industry:** Chocolate, ketchup, and paint quality control. The "spreadability" of these materials is directly tied to how they respond to a given rate of shear strain. - **⚙️ Lubrication Engineering:** Designing bearing lubricants. The shear rate in journal bearings can exceed $10^6 \text{ s}^{-1}$. - **🌊 Hydraulic Machines:** Computing the power required to rotate an impeller in a viscous fluid — the shear rate at the blade surface determines the viscous losses. # 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 🔍 Derivation 1: Rate of Shear Strain from First Principles ### Setup: Fluid Element Under Shear Consider an infinitesimal fluid element of size $dx \times dy$ embedded in a flowing fluid at time $t = 0$. The bottom face $AB$ lies along the $x$-axis at height $y = 0$, and the top face $CD$ lies at height $y = dy$. After a small time interval $dt$ elapses, the fluid element has been deformed by the flow. The top face $CD$ has moved horizontally by a distance $dx$ relative to the bottom face $AB$, because the fluid at height $y = dy$ is moving with velocity $u + du$ while the fluid at $y = 0$ is moving with velocity $u$. ### Visualizing the Deformation - The originally vertical side $BD$ tilts and becomes the line $BD'$. - The originally right-angle corner at $D$ is now an angle $\frac{\pi}{2} - d\theta$. - The change in the angle $d\theta$ is the **incremental shear strain**. ### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation The incremental shear strain (the small angular change $d\theta$) is defined as: $$d\gamma = \tan(d\theta) \approx d\theta \quad \text{(for very small angles)}$$ **Step 1: Express the geometry of the deformation** From the geometry of the deformed fluid element, the tangent of the tilt angle $d\theta$ is the ratio of the horizontal shift to the vertical height: $$\tan(d\theta) = \frac{\text{horizontal shift}}{\text{vertical height}} = \frac{dx}{dy}$$ **Step 2: Apply the small-angle approximation** Since $d\theta$ is infinitesimally small (the deformation is happening over an infinitesimal time step $dt$), we can safely use $\tan(d\theta) \approx d\theta$: $$d\theta = \frac{dx}{dy}$$ **Step 3: Recognize that the shear strain equals this angular change** By definition, the shear strain $\gamma$ for this small deformation equals $d\theta$: $$\gamma = d\theta = \frac{dx}{dy}$$ **Step 4: Differentiate both sides with respect to time** To get the rate of shear strain, we differentiate the shear strain with respect to time: $$\frac{d\gamma}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{dx}{dy}\right)$$ **Step 5: Swap the order of differentiation** Since $dy$ is the fixed geometric height of the fluid element (it does not change with time for an incompressible fluid element in simple shear), the derivative with respect to $t$ can be moved inside: $$\frac{d\gamma}{dt} = \frac{1}{dy}\cdot\frac{d(dx)}{dt}$$ **Step 6: Recognize $\dfrac{d(dx)}{dt}$ as velocity** The time derivative of displacement $dx$ is by definition the velocity $u$ of the fluid layer: $$\frac{d(dx)}{dt} = u$$ **Step 7: Substitute back and simplify** Substituting $u$ back into the equation, we get: $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{d\gamma}{dt} = \frac{1}{dy}\cdot u = \frac{u}{dy}$$ But wait — we must remember that $u$ is the *incremental* velocity $du$ corresponding to the height $dy$. So we should be more precise: $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{du}{dy}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{\dot{\gamma} = \frac{du}{dy} = \text{Rate of Shear Strain}}$$ This confirms that the **rate of shear strain is exactly the velocity gradient** in the direction perpendicular to the flow. --- ## 🔍 Derivation 2: Rate of Shear Strain in a Rotational Viscometer ### Setup: Concentric Cylinder Viscometer A typical **rotational viscometer** consists of two concentric cylinders. The outer cylinder is stationary, the inner cylinder rotates at $N$ revolutions per second. The fluid is trapped in the gap of thickness $h$ between the two cylinders. ### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation **Step 1: Calculate the linear velocity of the inner cylinder surface** A point on the surface of the inner cylinder at radius $r$ moves in a circular path. The linear (tangential) velocity is: $$u = 2\pi r N$$ where $N$ is in rev/s and the factor $2\pi$ converts revolutions to radians. **Step 2: Identify the velocity gradient across the gap** The velocity at the inner cylinder surface is $u = 2\pi r N$, and the velocity at the outer cylinder surface is $0$ (stationary). The gap thickness is $h$. For a thin gap ($h \ll r$), the velocity profile across the gap is approximately linear. The velocity gradient is therefore: $$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta y} = \frac{2\pi r N - 0}{h} = \frac{2\pi r N}{h}$$ **Step 3: Identify this gradient as the rate of shear strain** By the result of Derivation 1, $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{du}{dy}$. Substituting: $$\boxed{\dot{\gamma} = \frac{2\pi r N}{h}}$$ **Final Result:** This is the rate of shear strain experienced by the fluid trapped in the viscometer gap. Once you know $\dot{\gamma}$, the shear stress follows directly from $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$, and the viscosity $\mu$ can be computed from the measured torque. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** In a KTU numerical, if you are given the viscosity $\mu$ and the dimensions of the viscometer, you can calculate the **torque required** to rotate the inner cylinder using: > $$T = \tau \cdot A \cdot r = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma} \cdot (2\pi r L) \cdot r = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{h}$$ > This is a frequently tested KTU problem. --- ## 📋 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Form | Flow Geometry | Velocity Profile $u(y)$ | Rate of Shear Strain $\dot{\gamma}$ | |---------------|------------------------|-------------------------------------| | Flow between two parallel plates | $u = \dfrac{U \cdot y}{h}$ | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{U}{h}$ | | Hagen-Poiseuille flow (pipe) | $u = u_{max}\left(1 - \dfrac{r^2}{R^2}\right)$ | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{2 u_{max} r}{R^2}$ (at wall: $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{2 u_{max}}{R}$) | | Couette flow (concentric cyl.) | $u = \dfrac{U \cdot (R_o - r)}{h}$ | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{U}{h}$ | | Rotational viscometer | $u = 2\pi r N$ | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{2\pi r N}{h}$ | # 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 📊 Diagram 1: Fluid Element Deformation (Conceptual Flow Block) This diagram illustrates the *cause-and-effect* chain that produces the rate of shear strain in a fluid element. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[FLUID ELEMENT AT REST] -->|Shear stress tau applied| B[FLUID ELEMENT DEFORMS] B -->|Top layer moves by dx in time dt| C[ANGULAR CHANGE dtheta OCCURS] C -->|dtheta equals dx over dy| D[SHEAR STRAIN gamma equals dx over dy] D -->|Differentiate with respect to time| E[RATE OF SHEAR STRAIN equals du over dy] E -->|Multiply by viscosity mu| F[SHEAR STRESS tau equals mu times du over dy] F -->|This is Newton Law of Viscosity| G[FLUID MOTION FULLY CHARACTERIZED] style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000 style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000 style C fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000 style D fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,color:#000 style E fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,color:#000 style F fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000 style G fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000 ``` --- ## 📊 Diagram 2: Relationship Architecture (Inputs → Concept → Outputs) This is a **block-level functional architecture flow** showing how Rate of Shear Strain sits at the heart of fluid mechanics analysis. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUTS[INPUT QUANTITIES] inputA[VELOCITY u
in m per s] inputB[DISTANCE y
in m] inputC[ROTATION N
in rev per s] inputD[RADIUS r
in m] inputE[GAP h
in m] end subgraph CORE[RATE OF SHEAR STRAIN CORE] core1[VELOCITY GRADIENT
du over dy] core2[ANGULAR DERIVATIVE
d gamma over dt] core3[ROTATIONAL FORM
2 pi r N over h] end subgraph OUTPUTS[ENGINEERING OUTPUTS] outA[SHEAR STRESS
tau equals mu dot gamma] outB[VISCOSITY MEASUREMENT
from viscometer] outC[FRICTION LOSS
in pipe flow] outD[POWER REQUIRED
by pump or mixer] end inputA --> core1 inputB --> core1 inputC --> core3 inputD --> core3 inputE --> core3 core1 --> core2 core3 --> core2 core2 --> outA core2 --> outB core2 --> outC core2 --> outD style INPUTS fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000 style CORE fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825,color:#000 style OUTPUTS fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style core1 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#F9A825,color:#000 style core2 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#F9A825,color:#000 style core3 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#F9A825,color:#000 ``` --- ## 📊 Diagram 3: Velocity Profile & Shear Rate Visualization (Block Map) This shows how the rate of shear strain varies across different velocity profiles. ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[LINEAR VELOCITY PROFILE
Couette Flow Between Plates] -->|Constant gradient| Q1[CONSTANT SHEAR RATE
dot gamma equals U over h] P2[PARABOLIC VELOCITY PROFILE
Hagen Poiseuille Pipe Flow] -->|Variable gradient| Q2[ZERO AT CENTERLINE
MAXIMUM AT WALL
dot gamma max equals 2 u avg over R] P3[ROTATIONAL VELOCITY FIELD
Concentric Cylinder Viscometer] -->|Tangential motion| Q3[SHEAR RATE FROM ROTATION
dot gamma equals 2 pi r N over h] style P1 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000 style P2 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000 style P3 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000 style Q1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style Q2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style Q3 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read these diagrams in exams:** When a KTU question shows you a velocity profile diagram and asks for the *rate of shear strain at a particular point*, your strategy is to: > 1. Identify the velocity equation $u(y)$ or $u(r)$ from the given flow type. > 2. Differentiate it with respect to the perpendicular coordinate. > 3. Substitute the specific point where the shear rate is requested. > 4. Compute the numerical value with units $\text{s}^{-1}$. # 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]` **Define the term "Rate of Shear Strain". What are its SI units? How is it related to the velocity gradient in a fluid flow?** #### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks): **Definition (1 Mark):** The rate of shear strain is defined as the **time rate of change of angular deformation** of a fluid element subjected to a shear stress. It is denoted by $\dot{\gamma}$ (gamma-dot) or $du/dy$. **SI Units (1 Mark):** The SI unit of rate of shear strain is the **reciprocal of time**, i.e., $\text{s}^{-1}$ (or $\text{seconds}^{-1}$). **Relation to Velocity Gradient (1 Mark):** For a fluid element undergoing simple shear flow, the rate of shear strain is numerically equal to the **velocity gradient** perpendicular to the direction of flow: $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{du}{dy}$$ where $u$ is the fluid velocity in the flow direction and $y$ is the perpendicular distance. --- ### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]` **Distinguish between shear strain and rate of shear strain. Why is rate of shear strain considered a kinematic quantity and not a fluid property?** #### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks): **Distinction (2 Marks):** | Aspect | Shear Strain ($\gamma$) | Rate of Shear Strain ($\dot{\gamma}$) | |--------|-------------------------|--------------------------------------| | Definition | Total angular deformation of fluid element | Time rate of change of shear strain | | Mathematical form | $\gamma = dx / dy$ | $\dot{\gamma} = d\gamma / dt = du / dy$ | | Units | Dimensionless | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | | Nature | Geometric (static deformation) | Kinematic (motion-related) | **Why it is not a fluid property (1 Mark):** Rate of shear strain depends on the **flow conditions** (velocity distribution, boundary geometry), not on the fluid itself. Different fluids in the same flow will have the *same* rate of shear strain, but *different* shear stresses (because of different viscosities). Hence it is a **flow property** (kinematic quantity), not an inherent fluid property like viscosity or density. --- ## 📝 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** as two completely independent alternatives. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]` **A fluid flows between two large parallel plates separated by a distance of $10 \text{ mm}$. The lower plate is stationary and the upper plate moves with a velocity of $0.3 \text{ m/s}$. The fluid between the plates has a dynamic viscosity of $0.1 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ and a density of $900 \text{ kg/m}^3$.** #### **(a) Determine the rate of shear strain and the shear stress in the fluid. (7 Marks)** #### **(b) If the velocity profile is given by $u = U(y/h)$, verify that the rate of shear strain is constant throughout the gap. Also find the kinematic viscosity of the fluid. (7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution: ##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Identify the given data** - Plate separation: $h = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.01 \text{ m}$ - Upper plate velocity: $U = 0.3 \text{ m/s}$ - Dynamic viscosity: $\mu = 0.1 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ - Density: $\rho = 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **Step 2: Calculate the rate of shear strain** For Couette flow between parallel plates with a linear velocity profile, the rate of shear strain equals the velocity gradient: $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h}$$ Substituting the values: $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{0.3 \text{ m/s}}{0.01 \text{ m}} = 30 \text{ s}^{-1}$$ **Step 3: Calculate the shear stress using Newton's Law of Viscosity** $$\tau = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma} = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$ Substituting: $$\tau = 0.1 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s} \times 30 \text{ s}^{-1} = 3 \text{ Pa}$$ **Results:** - **Rate of Shear Strain:** $\dot{\gamma} = 30 \text{ s}^{-1}$ - **Shear Stress:** $\tau = 3 \text{ Pa}$ --- ##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Verify that the rate of shear strain is constant** Given velocity profile: $$u = U \cdot \frac{y}{h}$$ Differentiating with respect to $y$: $$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h}$$ This expression has **no dependence on $y$** — the rate of shear strain is **constant** throughout the gap. ✅ **Step 2: Find the kinematic viscosity** By definition, the kinematic viscosity is: $$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$ Substituting: $$\nu = \frac{0.1 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}}{900 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 1.111 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$ **Results:** - Rate of shear strain is constant: $\dot{\gamma} = 30 \text{ s}^{-1}$ (independent of $y$) - **Kinematic Viscosity:** $\nu = 1.111 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]` **A rotational viscometer consists of two concentric cylinders. The inner cylinder has a radius of $50 \text{ mm}$ and rotates at $120 \text{ rpm}$. The outer cylinder is stationary, and the gap between the cylinders is $2 \text{ mm}$. The fluid in the gap has a dynamic viscosity of $0.05 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$.** #### **(a) Derive an expression for the rate of shear strain in a rotational viscometer and calculate its value. (7 Marks)** #### **(b) Calculate the shear stress acting on the fluid and the torque required to rotate the inner cylinder, assuming the length of the cylinder is $100 \text{ mm}$. (7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution: ##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Convert the rotational speed to SI units** $$N = 120 \text{ rpm} = \frac{120}{60} \text{ rev/s} = 2 \text{ rev/s}$$ **Step 2: Identify the remaining data** - Inner cylinder radius: $r = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$ - Gap thickness: $h = 2 \text{ mm} = 0.002 \text{ m}$ - Dynamic viscosity: $\mu = 0.05 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ **Step 3: Derive the expression for rate of shear strain** The tangential velocity of the inner cylinder surface is: $$u = 2\pi r N$$ The outer cylinder is stationary, so the velocity difference across the gap is $\Delta u = 2\pi r N$. Assuming a thin gap (linear profile), the velocity gradient is: $$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{2\pi r N}{h}$$ By definition, the rate of shear strain is: $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{2\pi r N}{h}$$ **Step 4: Calculate the numerical value** $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{2\pi \times 0.05 \text{ m} \times 2 \text{ rev/s}}{0.002 \text{ m}}$$ $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{0.6283}{0.002} = 314.16 \text{ s}^{-1}$$ **Result:** $\dot{\gamma} = 314.16 \text{ s}^{-1}$ --- ##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Calculate the shear stress** $$\tau = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma} = 0.05 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s} \times 314.16 \text{ s}^{-1} = 15.708 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 2: Identify the surface area of the inner cylinder** $$A = 2\pi r L = 2\pi \times 0.05 \text{ m} \times 0.1 \text{ m} = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3: Calculate the force on the cylinder surface** $$F = \tau \cdot A = 15.708 \text{ Pa} \times 0.03142 \text{ m}^2 = 0.4935 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4: Calculate the torque** The torque is the force multiplied by the moment arm (the radius): $$T = F \times r = 0.4935 \text{ N} \times 0.05 \text{ m} = 0.02468 \text{ N}\cdot\text{m}$$ **Step 5: Express in a cleaner form using the combined formula** $$T = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{h}$$ Substituting: $$T = \frac{4\pi^2 \times 0.05 \times 2 \times (0.05)^3 \times 0.1}{0.002}$$ $$T = \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.05 \times 2 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.1}{0.002}$$ $$T = \frac{4.9348 \times 10^{-4}}{0.002} = 0.02468 \text{ N}\cdot\text{m}$$ **Results:** - **Shear Stress:** $\tau = 15.708 \text{ Pa}$ - **Torque Required:** $T = 0.02468 \text{ N}\cdot\text{m}$ --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Rate of Shear Strain Problems:** > > 1. **Unit Conversion Error (–2 marks):** The biggest trap! In rotational viscometer problems, students forget to convert **rpm to rev/s** by dividing by 60. Always check: $N (\text{rev/s}) = N (\text{rpm}) / 60$. > > 2. **Confusing Shear Strain with Rate of Shear Strain (–2 marks):** Shear strain is dimensionless; rate of shear strain is in $\text{s}^{-1}$. Examiners explicitly look for correct units in the final answer. > > 3. **Forgetting the Small-Angle Approximation (–1 mark):** In derivations, students sometimes write $d\theta = \sin(d\theta)$ or $d\theta = \tan(d\theta)$ without justifying why. Always mention **"for small angles, $\tan(d\theta) \approx d\theta$"**. > > 4. **Mixing Up $\mu$ and $\nu$ (–1 mark):** Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ has units $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. Kinematic viscosity $\nu$ has units $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. They are related by $\nu = \mu / \rho$, but they are NOT the same. > > 5. **Missing the Linear-Profile Assumption (–1 mark):** In a rotational viscometer, the derivation assumes $h \ll r$ (thin gap). Without this, the velocity profile is NOT linear and the formula $\dot{\gamma} = 2\pi r N / h$ is invalid. > > 6. **No Final Units (–0.5 mark):** KTU strictly deducts marks for missing or wrong units. Always end with explicit $\text{s}^{-1}$ or $\text{Pa}$. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this list — these are the high-frequency KTU exam points:** - ⭐ **Definition:** Rate of Shear Strain = time rate of angular deformation of a fluid element. - ⭐ **Symbol:** $\dot{\gamma}$ (gamma-dot) or sometimes $G$. - ⭐ **SI Unit:** $\text{s}^{-1}$ (reciprocal of time, also called **1/second**). - ⭐ **Velocity Gradient Form:** $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{du}{dy}$ — this is the most-used form in KTU problems. - ⭐ **Newton's Law Tie-in:** $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ — rate of shear strain is the *bridge* between viscosity and shear stress. - ⭐ **Viscometer Form:** $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{2\pi r N}{h}$ — use this for concentric cylinder viscometer problems. - ⭐ **Always convert rpm to rev/s:** Multiply by $\dfrac{1}{60}$ or divide by $60$. - ⭐ **Shear Strain vs Rate:** Shear strain ($\gamma$) is *dimensionless*; rate of shear strain ($\dot{\gamma}$) is in $\text{s}^{-1}$. - ⭐ **Kinematic vs Dynamic Viscosity:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ has units $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, NOT the same as $\dot{\gamma}$. - ⭐ **Kinematic Quantity:** Rate of shear strain describes *motion*, not the fluid itself — it is a *flow property*, not a *fluid property*. - ⭐ **Pipe Flow Application:** At the pipe wall, the maximum rate of shear strain is $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{2 u_{avg}}{R} = \dfrac{8 U}{D}$ (for Hagen-Poiseuille flow). - ⭐ **Engineering Use:** Rate of shear strain is critical in *viscometry*, *lubrication*, *blood flow modeling*, and *pipeline design*. - ⭐ **Sign Convention:** A positive $\dot{\gamma}$ means the top layer moves faster than the bottom — this is the standard convention in KTU problems. --- > **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Rate of Shear Strain** ✅ > *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | Outcome-Based Education Aligned | Bloom's Taxonomy Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze*

topics.title: Newton's Law of Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Newton's Law of Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's Law of Viscosity (Statement):**
> *"The shear stress $(\tau)$ developed in a real flowing fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient) $\dfrac{du}{dy}$, and the constant of proportionality is called the **dynamic viscosity** $(\mu)$ of the fluid."*

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress developed in the fluid (in $\text{N/m}^2$ or Pascal)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity of the fluid (in $\text{N·s/m}^2$ or $\text{Pa·s}$)
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to the direction of flow (in $\text{s}^{-1}$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Newton's Postulate:** A fluid that obeys Newton's Law of Viscosity is termed a **Newtonian Fluid**. All fluids that do not obey this linear relationship are classified as **Non-Newtonian Fluids**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy (The "Honey on a Spoon" Picture)

Imagine you have a thick drop of **honey on a flat plate** and you slide a thin, light paper across the top of the honey. 

- The paper moves. The plate is stationary. 
- The thin layer of honey *in direct contact* with the moving paper sticks to it and moves at the paper's speed.
- The thin layer of honey *in direct contact* with the stationary plate doesn't move at all.
- In between, the honey "drags" itself — each infinitesimal layer pulls the layer beneath it forward, but at a slightly slower speed.

This "layer-upon-layer dragging" is called **laminar shearing**. The internal molecular friction that causes this dragging is what we call **viscosity**. The faster the paper moves, the greater the drag, and the higher the shear stress the fluid exerts in resistance.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick memory hook:** Water = low viscosity (flows easily, low internal friction). Honey/glycerine = high viscosity (flows sluggishly, high internal friction). Air = extremely low viscosity compared to liquids.

## 1.3 Why This Concept Matters in Engineering

- Designing **pipelines** for water, oil, or chemical transport.
- Selecting **lubricating oils** in engines and gearboxes.
- Calculating **frictional losses (head loss)** in pumps, turbines, and hydraulic systems.
- Predicting **boundary layer behavior** over aircraft wings, ship hulls, and turbine blades.
- Formulating **Reynolds Number**, a dimensionless quantity that classifies flow as laminar, transitional, or turbulent.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value / Standard |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C** | $\mu_{water} \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$ |
| **Dynamic viscosity of air at 20 °C** | $\mu_{air} \approx 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$ |
| **Standard SI unit of dynamic viscosity** | $\text{N·s/m}^2$ or $\text{Pa·s}$ |
| **CGS unit (poise)** | $1 \text{ Poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$ |
| **Centipoise** | $1 \text{ cP} = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$ (used commonly in industry) |
| **Dimension of $\mu$** | $\text{M L}^{-1} \text{ T}^{-1}$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Newton's Law

1. **Select an elemental fluid layer:** Consider a fluid element sandwiched between two parallel layers, one at $y$ moving with velocity $u$ and another at $y+dy$ moving with velocity $u+du$.
2. **Compute the velocity gradient:** The change in velocity per unit perpendicular distance is $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ (with units $\text{s}^{-1}$).
3. **Apply the proportionality:** The internal friction (shear stress) that resists relative motion between layers is mathematically proportional to this velocity gradient.
4. **Introduce the constant of proportionality:** This constant $\mu$ is a **fluid property**, depending on temperature, pressure (very weakly for liquids), and the fluid's chemical composition.
5. **Write the governing equation:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ — this is Newton's Law of Viscosity in its canonical 1-D form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "du/dy" and not "dy/du"?** Because $y$ is measured *perpendicular* to the flow direction. The shear stress is a function of how *fast velocity changes across the depth*, not the other way around.

## 2.2 Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids — The Big Picture

| Fluid Type | $\tau$ vs. $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ Behavior | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Newtonian** | Linear, passes through origin | Water, air, gasoline, alcohol, glycerine (at constant $T$) |
| **Non-Newtonian (Pseudo-plastic / Shear-thinning)** | Curved — viscosity decreases with shear rate | Blood, ketchup, paint, paper pulp |
| **Non-Newtonian (Dilatant / Shear-thickening)** | Curved — viscosity increases with shear rate | Starch suspension, wet sand, mud |
| **Bingham Plastic** | Linear but needs yield stress to begin flowing | Toothpaste, drilling mud, mayonnaise |
| **Thixotropic** | Time-dependent (viscosity drops over time at constant shear) | Printing ink, honey |
| **Rheopectic** | Time-dependent (viscosity rises over time at constant shear) | Gypsum paste, some lubricants |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024, students **must** recognize the rheological classification from a $\tau$ vs. $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ graph. Memorize the shapes: **straight line through origin = Newtonian**.

## 2.3 Dynamic vs. Kinematic Viscosity

The **kinematic viscosity** $(\nu)$ is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density:

$$\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$$

- **Unit:** $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ (SI) or **Stoke** (CGS); $1 \text{ Stoke} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Centistoke (cSt):** $1 \text{ cSt} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ (commonly used in industry)
- **Dimension:** $\text{L}^2 \text{ T}^{-1}$

> [!TIP]
> **Why "kinematic"?** Because $\nu$ has dimensions of $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ — the same as quantities that describe motion (diffusion, conduction). Hence it is *kinematic* in nature, with no force or mass terms.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | SI Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity (1-D) | $\text{N/m}^2$ |
| 2 | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{du/dy}$ | Dynamic viscosity (definition) | $\text{Pa·s}$ |
| 3 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity (definition) | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 4 | $\tau_{max} = \mu \cdot \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)_{max}$ | Max shear stress at wall | $\text{N/m}^2$ |
| 5 | $\dfrac{du}{dy} = \dfrac{\Delta u}{\Delta y}$ | Approximate velocity gradient (finite difference) | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds Number (links to viscosity) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $P = \mu A \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Viscous force on area $A$ | $\text{N}$ |
| 8 | $\tau = \rho \nu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Alternate form using $\nu$ and $\rho$ | $\text{N/m}^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on units of $\mu$:** $\text{N·s/m}^2$ is identical to $\text{Pa·s}$ and identical to $\text{kg/(m·s)}$. Memorize all three forms — KTU examiners often test the unit conversion skill.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic machinery:** Bearing design, hydraulic oil selection — viscosity dictates film thickness.
- **Civil engineering:** Determining head loss in pipe networks (Hagen-Poiseuille flow).
- **Aerospace:** Atmospheric boundary layer over wings — air viscosity is critical.
- **Chemical engineering:** Reactor mixing, slurry transport, polymer processing.
- **Automobile engineering:** Engine oil grade (SAE 10W-30, etc.) selection is purely a viscosity-temperature problem.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Newton's Law of Viscosity (From First Principles)

### Step 1: Define the geometry

Consider a fluid element bounded by two parallel layers. Let the lower layer be at height $y$ with velocity $u$, and the upper layer at $y + dy$ with velocity $u + du$.

### Step 2: Express the shear strain rate

The **shear strain** $(\gamma)$ in the fluid at time $t$ is given by the angular deformation:

$$\gamma = \tan \theta \approx \theta = \dfrac{dx}{dy}$$

for infinitesimally small angles.

### Step 3: Compute the rate of shear strain

Differentiating with respect to time $t$:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{d\gamma}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt} \left(\dfrac{dx}{dy}\right)$$

Since $y$ does not change with time (stationary plate), the order of differentiation can be swapped:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{d}{dy}\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right) = \dfrac{du}{dy}$$

### Step 4: Apply Newton's Postulate

Newton postulated that the shear stress is linearly proportional to the rate of shear strain:

$$\tau \propto \dot{\gamma} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \tau \propto \dfrac{du}{dy}$$

### Step 5: Introduce the proportionality constant

Let the constant be $\mu$ (the dynamic viscosity). Then:

$$\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$$

This is the final form of Newton's Law of Viscosity. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Relationship Between Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity

**Given:**
- Dynamic viscosity: $\mu$ (in $\text{Pa·s}$)
- Density: $\rho$ (in $\text{kg/m}^3$)

**Definition (per KTU syllabus):**

$$\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Dimensional check:**

$$\text{Dim}(\nu) = \dfrac{\text{M L}^{-1} \text{T}^{-1}}{\text{M L}^{-3}} = \text{L}^2 \text{T}^{-1}$$

This matches the dimension of $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, confirming correctness. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU 2024 Pattern)

### Example 1: Computing Shear Stress

A fluid of dynamic viscosity $0.9 \text{ Pa·s}$ flows between two parallel plates. The bottom plate is fixed; the top plate moves at $1.2 \text{ m/s}$. The plate separation is $5 \text{ mm}$. Assuming a linear velocity profile, find:
- (a) The velocity gradient.
- (b) The shear stress on the top plate.
- (c) The shear force per unit area exerted by the fluid on the bottom plate.

**Solution:**

**(a) Velocity gradient:**

$$\dfrac{du}{dy} = \dfrac{\Delta u}{\Delta y} = \dfrac{1.2 - 0}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{1.2}{0.005} = 240 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**(b) Shear stress on the top plate:**

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy} = 0.9 \times 240 = 216 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**(c) Shear stress on the bottom plate:**

By Newton's third law, the fluid exerts an equal and opposite shear stress on the bottom plate:

$$\tau_{bottom} = 216 \text{ N/m}^2 \text{ (opposite direction)}$$

### Example 2: Computing Kinematic Viscosity

A lubricating oil has dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.082 \text{ Pa·s}$ and density $\rho = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Determine the kinematic viscosity in both SI and centistoke units.

**Solution:**

$$\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho} = \dfrac{0.082}{880} = 9.318 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

Convert to centistokes:

$$\nu = 9.318 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^{6} \text{ cSt} = 93.18 \text{ cSt}$$

### Example 3: Viscous Force on a Plate

A flat plate of area $0.4 \text{ m}^2$ is dragged through oil ($\mu = 0.45 \text{ Pa·s}$) at a velocity of $0.5 \text{ m/s}$. The oil film thickness is $3 \text{ mm}$, and the velocity profile is linear. Find the viscous force resisting the motion.

**Solution:**

Velocity gradient:

$$\dfrac{du}{dy} = \dfrac{0.5}{3 \times 10^{-3}} = 166.67 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Shear stress:

$$\tau = 0.45 \times 166.67 = 75 \text{ N/m}^2$$

Viscous force on the plate:

$$F = \tau \times A = 75 \times 0.4 = 30 \text{ N}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In numericals, **always show units throughout the calculation**. Marks are explicitly allocated in the answer key for unit consistency, even if the final answer is numerically correct.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Fluids at a Glance

| Property | Water (20 °C) | Air (20 °C) | Glycerine (20 °C) | Engine Oil (SAE 30) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mu$ (Pa·s) | $1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ | $1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ | $1.49$ | $\approx 0.29$ |
| $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $998$ | $1.204$ | $1260$ | $\approx 890$ |
| $\nu$ (m²/s) | $1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ | $1.50 \times 10^{-5}$ | $1.18 \times 10^{-3}$ | $\approx 3.26 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| Newtonian? | Yes | Yes | Yes (approx.) | Yes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Newtonian Shear Mechanism

The following block diagram illustrates the logical sequence of how Newton's Law of Viscosity arises from physical observation to mathematical formulation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Observation: Layered fluid motion] --> B[Identify two adjacent layers at y and y plus dy]
    B --> C[Measure velocities u and u plus du]
    C --> D[Compute Velocity Gradient du over dy]
    D --> E[Newton's Postulate: tau proportional to du over dy]
    E --> F[Introduce proportionality constant mu]
    F --> G[Tau equals mu times du over dy]
    G --> H[Classify Fluid]
    H --> I{Linear relationship?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Newtonian Fluid: Water, Air, Glycerine]
    I -- No --> K[Non-Newtonian Fluid: Blood, Ketchup, Paint]
    J --> L[Compute kinematic viscosity nu equals mu over rho]
    K --> M[Sub-classify into Pseudo-plastic, Dilatant, Bingham, Thixotropic, Rheopectic]
    L --> N[Apply to Engineering: Pipe flow, Bearings, Lubricants, Aerodynamics]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Typical Parallel-Plate Shear Apparatus

The block diagram below shows the conceptual layout of a typical Couette-type viscometer used to experimentally validate Newton's Law.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Fixed Lower Plate at velocity zero] -->|Contact layer drags| FL1[Fluid Layer 1: Velocity = 0]
    FL1 -->|Drag transmission| FL2[Fluid Layer 2: Velocity = small]
    FL2 -->|Drag transmission| FL3[Fluid Layer 3: Velocity = medium]
    FL3 -->|Drag transmission| FL4[Fluid Layer 4: Velocity = large]
    FL4 -->|Contact layer drags| P2[Moving Upper Plate at velocity U]
    P2 -->|Force F applied| S[Sensor measures F]
    S --> C[Compute tau equals F over A]
    C --> M[Compute mu equals tau divided by du over dy]
    M --> R[Result: Viscosity mu of fluid]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Velocity Profile (Linear, for Newtonian Fluid)

A schematic representation of the linear velocity profile between two plates:

```
   y (m) ↑
         |
   y+dy ─|───────────────► U = 1.2 m/s   (Moving plate)
         |  \           /
         |    \       /  ← Linear velocity profile
         |      \   /
         |        \/
       y ─|───────●────────────► u = 0      (Fixed plate)
         |__________________________→ x (m)
              5 mm gap
```

| Component | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Plate separation | $5 \text{ mm}$ |
| Top plate velocity | $1.2 \text{ m/s}$ |
| Bottom plate velocity | $0 \text{ m/s}$ |
| Velocity profile shape | **Linear** (Newtonian fluid) |
| Velocity gradient $du/dy$ | $240 \text{ s}^{-1}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** When a $\tau$ vs. $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ graph is provided in the question paper, the **slope of the straight line equals $\mu$**. The steeper the slope, the higher the viscosity.

## 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart: Selecting the Right Viscosity Type for a Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given a Fluid Mechanics Problem] --> Q1{Question involves pipe flow, friction, or boundary layer?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is temperature variation significant?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use dynamic viscosity mu with temperature correction]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[Use constant mu at reference temperature]
    Q1 -- No --> Q5{Is the problem about flow similarity or dimensional analysis?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Use kinematic viscosity nu equals mu over rho]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7[Check if force or stress is asked]
    Q7 -- Force or stress --> Q3
    Q7 -- Diffusive or similarity --> Q6
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"State Newton's Law of Viscosity. Mention its mathematical form and define each term."**

**Model Answer:**

> Newton's Law of Viscosity states that the shear stress $(\tau)$ developed in a real flowing fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain, i.e., the velocity gradient $\dfrac{du}{dy}$.
>
> The mathematical form is:
> $$\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$$
>
> where:
> - $\tau$ = shear stress ($\text{N/m}^2$ or Pa)
> - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity ($\text{N·s/m}^2$ or Pa·s)
> - $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = velocity gradient perpendicular to flow direction ($\text{s}^{-1}$)

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their units and dimensions."**

**Model Answer:**

> | Property | Dynamic Viscosity $(\mu)$ | Kinematic Viscosity $(\nu)$ |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Definition | Internal friction per unit velocity gradient | Ratio of dynamic viscosity to density |
> | Formula | $\mu = \tau / (du/dy)$ | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ |
> | SI Unit | $\text{N·s/m}^2$ or $\text{Pa·s}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
> | Dimension | $\text{M L}^{-1} \text{ T}^{-1}$ | $\text{L}^2 \text{ T}^{-1}$ |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (Choice A) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive Newton's Law of Viscosity from the concept of shear strain and shear strain rate. Explain the significance of dynamic viscosity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plate of area $0.5 \text{ m}^2$ is dragged through a $4 \text{ mm}$ thick oil film at a velocity of $0.75 \text{ m/s}$. The dynamic viscosity of the oil is $0.85 \text{ Pa·s}$. Assuming a linear velocity profile, calculate:
- (i) The shear stress exerted by the fluid on the plate.
- (ii) The viscous force resisting the motion.
- (iii) The kinematic viscosity if the oil density is $920 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

Consider two adjacent fluid layers. The lower layer is at position $y$ moving with velocity $u$, and the upper layer is at $y + dy$ moving with velocity $u + du$.

The shear strain is the angular deformation:
$$\gamma = \tan \theta = \dfrac{dx}{dy}$$

Rate of shear strain:
$$\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{d\gamma}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt}\left(\dfrac{dx}{dy}\right) = \dfrac{du}{dy}$$

By Newton's postulate:
$$\tau \propto \dot{\gamma} \Rightarrow \tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$$

The constant $\mu$ is the **dynamic viscosity**, which represents the fluid's internal resistance to deformation. It is a thermodynamic property depending on temperature, pressure (slightly), and chemical composition.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Velocity gradient:**

$$\dfrac{du}{dy} = \dfrac{0.75}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 187.5 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Shear stress:
$$\tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy} = 0.85 \times 187.5 = 159.375 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**(ii) Viscous force:**

$$F = \tau \times A = 159.375 \times 0.5 = 79.6875 \text{ N} \approx 79.69 \text{ N}$$

**(iii) Kinematic viscosity:**

$$\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho} = \dfrac{0.85}{920} = 9.239 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

In centistokes: $\nu = 923.9 \text{ cSt}$

---

### Question B1 (Choice B) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the concept of Newton's Law of Viscosity. Classify fluids as Newtonian and Non-Newtonian with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The velocity profile for a fluid flow over a flat surface is given by $u(y) = 2y^2 - y$ (where $u$ is in m/s and $y$ is in metres). The dynamic viscosity of the fluid is $1.5 \text{ Pa·s}$. Determine the shear stress at $y = 0.3 \text{ m}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept with Sketch:**

A flat plate is placed at the bottom; another parallel plate is placed at distance $dy$ above it. The lower plate is stationary; the upper plate is pulled with velocity $U$. A linear velocity profile is established. The shear stress at the wall is:

$$\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$$

**Classification of fluids:**

- **Newtonian fluids** obey $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ strictly. The $\tau$ vs. $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ plot is a straight line through the origin. Examples: water, air, gasoline, glycerine.
- **Non-Newtonian fluids** do not obey this linear relationship. Examples: blood (pseudo-plastic), starch suspension (dilatant), toothpaste (Bingham plastic).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $u(y) = 2y^2 - y$ and $\mu = 1.5 \text{ Pa·s}$.

Velocity gradient:
$$\dfrac{du}{dy} = \dfrac{d}{dy}(2y^2 - y) = 4y - 1$$

At $y = 0.3 \text{ m}$:
$$\dfrac{du}{dy}\bigg|_{y=0.3} = 4(0.3) - 1 = 1.2 - 1 = 0.2 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Shear stress:
$$\tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy} = 1.5 \times 0.2 = 0.3 \text{ N/m}^2$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing $du/dy$ with $dy/du$:** Always remember $y$ is perpendicular to the flow. A reversed gradient gives a *negative* shear stress and full mark deduction.
> 2. **Mixing up units:** $\text{N·s/m}^2$ vs. $\text{N/m}^2$ — losing 1 mark for unit inconsistency is extremely common.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert mm to m:** A $5 \text{ mm}$ gap must be written as $0.005 \text{ m}$ in the denominator. KTU examiners specifically check unit conversion steps.
> 4. **Linear velocity profile assumption:** Always state this assumption explicitly in numericals, especially in problems involving parallel plates. If not stated, marks are lost.
> 5. **Sign convention in velocity profiles:** For $u(y) = 2y^2 - y$, the velocity may be negative near the wall — interpret correctly by substituting $y$ first, then differentiating.
> 6. **Not drawing the schematic in derivations:** Marks are reserved for a labelled diagram in the 7-mark derivation question. Always include a clear sketch.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Newton's Law of Viscosity: KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Master List**

- **Core equation:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ — the most heavily weighted formula in this topic.
- **Definition of $\mu$:** Resistance of a fluid to deformation; the proportionality constant between shear stress and velocity gradient.
- **Units of $\mu$:** $\text{Pa·s}$, $\text{N·s/m}^2$, or $\text{kg/(m·s)}$ — all three are equivalent and interchangeable.
- **Kinematic viscosity:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$, units $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ or **cSt** ($1 \text{ cSt} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$).
- **Newtonian fluid definition:** A fluid whose $\tau$ vs. $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ graph is a *straight line passing through the origin*.
- **Non-Newtonian sub-categories:** Pseudo-plastic, Dilatant, Bingham Plastic, Thixotropic, Rheopectic — know one example for each.
- **Typical values to memorize:**
  - Water at 20 °C: $\mu \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$
  - Air at 20 °C: $\mu \approx 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$
  - Glycerine: $\mu \approx 1.49 \text{ Pa·s}$
- **Slope interpretation:** On a $\tau$ vs. $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ graph, the **slope of the line = $\mu$**.
- **Sign convention:** Shear stress on a faster-moving layer is opposite in direction to that on a slower-moving layer.
- **Boundary condition assumption:** In parallel-plate problems, always assume a *linear* velocity profile unless otherwise specified.
- **Common numerical pattern:** Plate separation in **mm → convert to m** before substituting into $du/dy$.
- **Engineering applications:** Pipe flow (Hagen-Poiseuille), bearing design, Reynolds Number classification, atmospheric boundary layer, lubrication theory.
- **Dimension recall:** $\text{Dim}(\mu) = \text{M L}^{-1} \text{ T}^{-1}$ ; $\text{Dim}(\nu) = \text{L}^2 \text{ T}^{-1}$ — frequently tested.
- **Cross-link:** This concept directly feeds into **Reynolds Number** in Module 2 and **laminar flow analysis (Hagen-Poiseuille)** in Module 3.
- **Key examiners' red flags:** Wrong unit conversion, missing sketch in derivations, ignoring linearity assumption, mixing dynamic and kinematic viscosity formulas.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **fluid** is a substance that continuously deforms (flows) under the action of an applied shear stress. The internal resistance offered by a fluid to this deformation is quantified by its **dynamic viscosity (μ)**.

A **Newtonian fluid** is a fluid in which the dynamic viscosity (μ) remains **constant and independent of the rate of shear deformation (du/dy)**. The shear stress at any point is directly proportional to the velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) at that point. Mathematically, it obeys **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

A **Non-Newtonian fluid** is a fluid in which the relationship between shear stress and rate of shear deformation is **non-linear**, and the dynamic viscosity is a function of the shear rate (or shear history). Therefore, the apparent viscosity (μₐ) is **not a constant** but varies with the kinematic conditions of the flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):** Students must be able to (a) classify fluids based on their rheological behaviour, (b) identify the governing constitutive equation for each class, and (c) compute apparent viscosity for a given shear rate. The two standard fluids referenced in the KTU syllabus are: **Water (Newtonian)** and **Blood / Drilling Mud / Paints (Non-Newtonian)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine you have two jars of liquid on a table: one filled with **water** and the other with **honey**.

- If you tilt both jars, the **water** flows out smoothly and the rate of flow does not change no matter how steeply you tilt it. The "thickness" of water feels the same always. This is a **Newtonian fluid**.
- The **honey** on the other hand, when you start tilting, barely moves (very thick), but as it begins to flow, it suddenly gets thinner and pours easily. Its "thickness" changes with the effort you apply. This is **Non-Newtonian** behaviour.

A simpler analogy: think of **ketchup** stuck in a bottle. It does not move when the bottle is upright (high viscosity at rest), but once you shake or tap the bottle, it suddenly flows freely (low viscosity under stress). This is the **Bingham plastic** behaviour — it needs a minimum stress (the "yield stress") before it will flow at all.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - **Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C:** μ ≈ **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** (or N·s/m²)
> - **Kinematic viscosity of water at 20 °C:** ν ≈ **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
> - **Dynamic viscosity of air at 20 °C:** μ ≈ **1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s**
> - **Standard gravity:** g = **9.81 m/s²**

### 1.3 Engineering Relevance

Understanding Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian behaviour is critical in:

- **Petroleum engineering:** drilling muds are designed as Bingham plastics to carry rock cuttings.
- **Biomedical engineering:** blood is a shear-thinning (pseudo-plastic) fluid — its viscosity drops in narrow capillaries.
- **Food and cosmetic industry:** chocolate, toothpaste, and lotions are shear-thinning for easy application.
- **Hydraulic machinery:** pump performance curves assume Newtonian behaviour; non-Newtonian fluids require modified pump selection.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Foundation

For a fluid element flowing between two parallel plates, the bottom plate stationary and the top plate moving with velocity **U**, the velocity distribution is linear (for the simple case). The shear stress τ is defined as the tangential force per unit area required to maintain the motion:

- **Step 1 — Force and Area:** Force F acts on a fluid layer of area A → shear stress τ = F/A.
- **Step 2 — Rate of deformation:** The fluid layer experiences a velocity gradient du/dy (perpendicular to flow direction).
- **Step 3 — Newton's Postulate:** Newton proposed that the shear stress is proportional to the rate of shear strain:

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

- **Step 4 — Interpretation:** The proportionality constant μ (mu) is the **dynamic viscosity**, with SI units of **Pa·s** or **N·s/m²** or **kg/(m·s)**.

### 2.2 Classification of Non-Newtonian Fluids (Rheological Behaviour)

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires a clear understanding of the following five categories. Each category has a distinct **τ vs (du/dy)** curve.

#### 2.2.1 Bingham Plastic Fluids
- Behaviour: Behaves like a solid until the applied stress exceeds the **yield stress (τy)**. After yielding, it flows with constant viscosity μp.
- Examples: **Toothpaste, drilling mud, sewage sludge, mayonnaise**.
- Constitutive equation:

$$\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \frac{du}{dy} \quad \text{for} \quad \tau > \tau_y$$

- If τ ≤ τy, the fluid does not flow (rigid body motion).

#### 2.2.2 Pseudo-Plastic (Shear-Thinning) Fluids
- Behaviour: Apparent viscosity **decreases** as shear rate increases. The τ vs (du/dy) curve is concave-down.
- Examples: **Blood, ketchup, paint, polymer solutions, milk**.
- Constitutive equation (Ostwald–de Waele power-law model):

$$\tau = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^n \quad \text{with} \quad 0 < n < 1$$

- Here, **K** is the flow consistency index, and **n** is the flow behaviour index.

#### 2.2.3 Dilatant (Shear-Thickening) Fluids
- Behaviour: Apparent viscosity **increases** as shear rate increases. The τ vs (du/dy) curve is concave-up.
- Examples: **Cornstarch slurry in water, wet sand, sugar in water**.
- Constitutive equation (power-law model):

$$\tau = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^n \quad \text{with} \quad n > 1$$

#### 2.2.4 Rheopectic Fluids
- Behaviour: Apparent viscosity **increases with time** under constant shear rate. Time-dependent and shear-thickening.
- Examples: **Printer ink, certain gypsum pastes**.
- Rarely examined numerically in KTU exams; conceptual question only.

#### 2.2.5 Thixotropic Fluids
- Behaviour: Apparent viscosity **decreases with time** under constant shear rate, but recovers when the shear is removed. Time-dependent and shear-thinning.
- Examples: **Honey, gels, crude oil, bentonite clay**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Fluid Type | Constitutive Equation | Apparent Viscosity μₐ | Key Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newtonian | $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$ | $\mu_a = \mu = \text{const}$ | μ (dynamic viscosity) |
| 2 | Bingham Plastic | $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \frac{du}{dy}$ | $\mu_a = \mu_p + \frac{\tau_y}{(du/dy)}$ | τy (yield stress) |
| 3 | Pseudo-Plastic | $\tau = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^n$ | $\mu_a = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^{n-1}$ | n < 1, K = consistency |
| 4 | Dilatant | $\tau = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^n$ | $\mu_a = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^{n-1}$ | n > 1, K = consistency |
| 5 | Rheopectic | Time-dependent, n > 1 | increases with time | Time-dependent |
| 6 | Thixotropic | Time-dependent, n < 1 | decreases with time | Time-dependent |

| # | Conversion Formula | Equation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kinematic viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | m²/s |
| 2 | Apparent viscosity (general) | $\mu_a = \tau / (du/dy)$ | Pa·s |
| 3 | Specific weight | $\gamma = \rho g$ | N/m³ |
| 4 | Shear strain rate | $\dot{\gamma} = du/dy$ | s⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The power-law model is the most commonly tested formulation in KTU university exams because it compactly captures both pseudo-plastic (n < 1) and dilatant (n > 1) behaviour using just two parameters: K and n.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Classification

- **Hydraulic design of pipes carrying crude oil:** The Haaland–Darcy–Weisbach equation requires a representative viscosity; for non-Newtonian crude oil, the **apparent viscosity** computed from K and n is used.
- **Selection of centrifugal pumps:** Pumps are rated for Newtonian water; Non-Newtonian fluids demand derating the pump (lower flow / higher head).
- **Biomedical devices:** Syringes and catheters carrying blood use the Carreau model (advanced form of power-law) for accurate pressure-drop predictions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Apparent Viscosity from the Power-Law Model

The **Ostwald–de Waele power-law** is the most general non-Newtonian model tested at KTU. We will derive the apparent viscosity expression step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Write the constitutive (rheological) equation:**

$$\tau = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^n$$

This is the starting point. The shear stress τ is related to the velocity gradient du/dy raised to the power n, with K as a constant of proportionality.

**Step 2 — Recall the definition of apparent viscosity:**

For any fluid (Newtonian or non-Newtonian), the apparent viscosity is defined as the ratio of shear stress to the rate of shear strain:

$$\mu_a = \frac{\tau}{(du/dy)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the power-law expression for τ into the apparent viscosity definition:**

$$\mu_a = \frac{K \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)^n}{\left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)}$$

The numerator contains the velocity gradient raised to power n, and the denominator contains it raised to power 1.

**Step 4 — Apply the law of exponents (aᵐ / aⁿ = aᵐ⁻ⁿ):**

$$\mu_a = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^{n-1}$$

This is the final form. Apparent viscosity now explicitly depends on the local shear rate (du/dy).

**Step 5 — Interpret the limiting cases:**

- If n = 1, then (n − 1) = 0, and (du/dy)⁰ = 1 → μₐ = K. This means the fluid behaves as **Newtonian** with viscosity equal to K. Therefore, **K has units of Pa·s** (same as dynamic viscosity) and represents the Newtonian limit.
- If n < 1 (pseudo-plastic): exponent (n − 1) is negative, so as (du/dy) increases, (du/dy)ⁿ⁻¹ decreases → **apparent viscosity drops with shear rate** (shear-thinning).
- If n > 1 (dilatant): exponent (n − 1) is positive, so as (du/dy) increases, (du/dy)ⁿ⁻¹ increases → **apparent viscosity rises with shear rate** (shear-thickening).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **K vs. μ Distinction:** For Newtonian fluids, K = μ. For non-Newtonian fluids, K is **not** a viscosity — it is the consistency index, with units of **Pa·sⁿ**. The actual viscosity varies with shear rate.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bingham Plastic Apparent Viscosity

**Step 1 — Write the Bingham plastic equation:**

$$\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the apparent viscosity definition μₐ = τ / (du/dy):**

$$\mu_a = \frac{\tau_y + \mu_p \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)}$$

**Step 3 — Split the fraction into two terms:**

$$\mu_a = \frac{\tau_y}{(du/dy)} + \mu_p$$

**Step 4 — Interpret each term:**

- The first term, $\tau_y / (du/dy)$, is the **shear-rate-dependent** portion. As (du/dy) increases, this term decreases, mimicking shear-thinning.
- The second term, μp, is the **plastic viscosity** (constant for a given Bingham plastic fluid).
- As (du/dy) → ∞, μₐ → μp (the fluid approaches a Newtonian plateau at very high shear rates).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relation Between Kinematic and Dynamic Viscosity

**Step 1 — Definitions:**

$$\mu = \text{dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)}, \quad \rho = \text{mass density (kg/m³)}, \quad \nu = \text{kinematic viscosity (m²/s)}$$

**Step 2 — Empirical definition of kinematic viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Step 3 — Dimensional verification:**

$$\left[\nu\right] = \frac{\left[\text{Pa·s}\right]}{\left[\text{kg/m³}\right]} = \frac{\left[\text{kg/(m·s)}\right]}{\left[\text{kg/m³}\right]} = \frac{\text{m²}}{\text{s}} = \text{m²/s}$$

This confirms that ν has units of m²/s, exactly as required.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model Problem)

**Problem:** A pseudo-plastic fluid is described by the power-law model with K = **10 Pa·sⁿ** and n = **0.4**. Calculate the apparent viscosity when the shear rate is (a) 10 s⁻¹ and (b) 1000 s⁻¹. Comment on the result.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — du/dy = 10 s⁻¹:**

$$\mu_a = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^{n-1} = 10 \times (10)^{0.4 - 1}$$

$$\mu_a = 10 \times (10)^{-0.6}$$

Computing (10)⁻⁰·⁶ using log₁₀: log(10⁻⁰·⁶) = −0.6, so 10⁻⁰·⁶ = 10⁻⁰·⁶ ≈ 0.2512.

$$\mu_a = 10 \times 0.2512 = 2.512 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Part (b) — du/dy = 1000 s⁻¹:**

$$\mu_a = 10 \times (1000)^{-0.6}$$

$$\mu_a = 10 \times (10^3)^{-0.6} = 10 \times 10^{-1.8}$$

Computing 10⁻¹·⁸ ≈ 0.01585.

$$\mu_a = 10 \times 0.01585 = 0.1585 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Comment:** The apparent viscosity dropped from **2.512 Pa·s** at 10 s⁻¹ to **0.1585 Pa·s** at 1000 s⁻¹ (almost a 16-fold reduction). This confirms the **shear-thinning** behaviour characteristic of pseudo-plastic fluids.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Newtonian | Pseudo-Plastic | Dilatant | Bingham Plastic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| n | = 1 | 0 < n < 1 | n > 1 | n = 1 (with yield stress) |
| K vs μ | K = μ | K > μ (apparent) | K > μ (apparent) | μp = constant slope |
| μₐ trend | Constant | Decreases | Increases | Decreases → μp |
| Shear stress at du/dy = 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 (no flow) or jump to τy |
| Curve shape | Straight line | Concave down | Concave up | Straight line (offset by τy) |
| Real example | Water, air | Blood, paint | Wet sand slurry | Toothpaste, mud |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Rheological Classification of Fluids

```mermaid

    nodeA[Newtonian Fluids]:::newt

    nodeP[Viscosity drops with time]:::timedepdesc

```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Shear Stress Response

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInput[Applied Shear Rate du by dy]:::in
    nodeLogic{Rheological Model}:::logic
    nodeNewton[Newtonian Branch]:::newt
    nodeNonNewton[Non-Newtonian Branch]:::nonnewt
    nodeYield[Yield Stress Test]:::logic
    nodeBinghamFlow[Plastic Flow]:::bin
    nodeNoFlow[No Flow Region]:::noresp
    nodePowerLaw[Power Law Evaluation]:::bin
    nodeOutput[Shear Stress tau]:::out
    nodeViscosity[Apparent Viscosity mu a]:::out

    nodeInput --> nodeLogic
    nodeLogic --> nodeNewton
    nodeLogic --> nodeNonNewton
    nodeNonNewton --> nodeYield
    nodeYield --> nodeBinghamFlow
    nodeYield --> nodeNoFlow
    nodeNonNewton --> nodePowerLaw
    nodeNewton --> nodeOutput
    nodeBinghamFlow --> nodeOutput
    nodePowerLaw --> nodeOutput
    nodeOutput --> nodeViscosity

    classDef in fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#78350f,color:#000
```
