```mermaid
    classDef nonnewt fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    classDef bin fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#312e81,color:#000
    classDef noresp fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#f3f4f6,stroke:#374151,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Shear Stress vs Shear Rate Curves (Textual Schematic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation Guide:** When the KTU exam asks you to "sketch the rheological diagram", draw the following in a single τ vs (du/dy) plot:
>
> - **Line A (Newtonian):** A straight line through the origin with slope = μ.
> - **Curve B (Pseudo-plastic):** A concave-down curve from the origin (no yield stress, but slope decreases).
> - **Curve C (Dilatant):** A concave-up curve from the origin (slope increases).
> - **Line D (Bingham plastic):** A horizontal "no-flow" segment from τ = 0 to τ = τy along the τ-axis, then a straight line with slope = μp.
>
> **Newtonian fluid (water) is the reference baseline** — it is the only fluid where the relationship is a single straight line through the origin.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a Newtonian fluid. Give two examples and state the constitutive equation governing its behaviour.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Newtonian fluid is one in which the shear stress is directly proportional to the rate of shear deformation (velocity gradient), and the constant of proportionality — the **dynamic viscosity (μ)** — is independent of the shear rate. **Newton's law of viscosity** gives the constitutive equation:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Examples (any two):** Water, air, glycerin, kerosene, light oils, alcohol.

**Awarding Marks (Valuator's Note):** 1 mark for the correct definition, 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for the two examples. Do not accept "blood" or "paint" as Newtonian examples.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish between a pseudo-plastic fluid and a dilatant fluid. Write the power-law equation applicable to both and indicate the range of the flow behaviour index (n) for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Both pseudo-plastic and dilatant fluids obey the **Ostwald–de Waele power-law model**:

$$\tau = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^n$$

where **K** is the flow consistency index and **n** is the flow behaviour index.

- **Pseudo-plastic fluid (shear-thinning):** Apparent viscosity **decreases** with increasing shear rate. The flow behaviour index satisfies **0 < n < 1**. Examples: blood, paint, polymer solutions.
- **Dilatant fluid (shear-thickening):** Apparent viscosity **increases** with increasing shear rate. The flow behaviour index satisfies **n > 1**. Examples: wet sand, cornstarch–water slurry.

**Awarding Marks:** 1 mark for the common power-law equation, 1 mark for the correct range of n for pseudo-plastic, 1 mark for the correct range and example of dilatant.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the rheological classification of fluids with neat sketches of shear stress vs shear rate diagrams. Mention the constitutive equation and at least one real-world example for each class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A power-law fluid flows in a circular pipe. The consistency index **K = 2.5 Pa·sⁿ** and flow behaviour index **n = 0.5**. Determine the apparent viscosity and shear stress at the wall when the shear rate at the wall is **800 s⁻¹**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A:**

**(a) Part — Rheological Classification (7 marks):**

A fluid's resistance to deformation under shear is called its **rheological behaviour**. Fluids are classified into the following categories:

**1. Newtonian Fluid:**
- Constitutive equation: $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ where μ is constant.
- The τ vs (du/dy) plot is a **straight line through the origin** with slope = μ.
- Examples: water, air, glycerin.

**2. Bingham Plastic Fluid:**
- Constitutive equation: $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dfrac{du}{dy}$ for $\tau > \tau_y$.
- The plot shows a **horizontal dead zone from 0 to τy along the τ-axis** (no flow), and a straight line of slope μp beyond τy.
- Examples: toothpaste, drilling mud, sewage sludge.

**3. Pseudo-Plastic (Shear-Thinning) Fluid:**
- Constitutive equation: $\tau = K \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)^n$ with $0 < n < 1$.
- The plot is a **concave-down curve** starting from the origin (no yield stress).
- Examples: blood, paint, polymer melts.

**4. Dilatant (Shear-Thickening) Fluid:**
- Constitutive equation: $\tau = K \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)^n$ with $n > 1$.
- The plot is a **concave-up curve** starting from the origin.
- Examples: wet sand, cornstarch slurry, sugar in water.

**5. Thixotropic Fluid:**
- Time-dependent: viscosity **decreases** with time under constant shear.
- Example: honey, gels, bentonite clay.

**6. Rheopectic Fluid:**
- Time-dependent: viscosity **increases** with time under constant shear.
- Example: printer ink, certain gypsum pastes.

**(Full-mark sketch required in exam paper; the textual description above should be supplemented with axes τ (y-axis) vs du/dy (x-axis) and the four curves mentioned.)**

**Awarding Marks (7 marks):** 1 mark for the introduction, 1.5 marks each for Newtonian + Bingham, 1.5 marks each for Pseudo-plastic + Dilatant, 0.5 marks for time-dependent classes combined.

---

**(b) Part — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- K = 2.5 Pa·sⁿ
- n = 0.5
- Shear rate at wall: $\dot{\gamma}_w = (du/dy)_w = 800 \text{ s}^{-1}$

**Step 1 — Compute the apparent viscosity using the derived formula:**

$$\mu_a = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^{n-1}$$

Substituting n − 1 = 0.5 − 1 = −0.5:

$$\mu_a = 2.5 \times (800)^{-0.5}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate (800)⁻⁰·⁵:**

$$(800)^{0.5} = \sqrt{800} = 28.284$$

$$(800)^{-0.5} = \frac{1}{28.284} = 0.03536$$

**Step 3 — Compute μₐ:**

$$\mu_a = 2.5 \times 0.03536 = 0.0884 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the shear stress at the wall:**

$$\tau_w = \mu_a \times \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)_w = 0.0884 \times 800$$

$$\tau_w = 70.72 \text{ Pa}$$

**Alternative cross-check using direct power-law formula:**

$$\tau_w = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^n = 2.5 \times (800)^{0.5} = 2.5 \times 28.284 = 70.71 \text{ Pa}$$

(The small rounding difference of 0.01 Pa is acceptable; both answers match to within 0.02%.)

**Final Answer:** $\mu_a = 0.0884 \text{ Pa·s}$ and $\tau_w = 70.72 \text{ Pa}$.

**Awarding Marks (7 marks):** 1 mark for correct formula selection, 2 marks for evaluating (du/dy)ⁿ⁻¹, 1 mark for μₐ value, 1 mark for the shear stress formula, 2 marks for the final shear stress value with correct units.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for apparent viscosity of a fluid obeying the **Ostwald–de Waele power-law model**. Hence show that for n = 1 the fluid behaves as a Newtonian fluid and identify the physical significance of the consistency index K. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Bingham plastic fluid has a yield stress **τy = 12 Pa** and a plastic viscosity **μp = 0.05 Pa·s**. Compute: (i) the shear stress when the velocity gradient is **150 s⁻¹**; (ii) the apparent viscosity at the same shear rate. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B:**

**(a) Part — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — State the power-law (Ostwald–de Waele) constitutive equation:**

$$\tau = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^n$$

where τ is the shear stress, K is the flow consistency index, n is the flow behaviour index, and du/dy is the velocity gradient (shear rate).

**Step 2 — Definition of apparent viscosity:**

The apparent viscosity of any fluid is defined as the ratio of shear stress to the velocity gradient:

$$\mu_a = \frac{\tau}{(du/dy)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitution of the power-law expression for τ:**

$$\mu_a = \frac{K \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)^n}{\left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)} = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^{n-1}$$

(after cancelling one factor of du/dy using the law of exponents).

**Step 4 — Newtonian limit (n = 1):**

Substituting n = 1:

$$\mu_a = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^{1-1} = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^{0} = K \times 1 = K$$

The apparent viscosity becomes a **constant** equal to K, and is **independent** of the shear rate. This is precisely the definition of a **Newtonian fluid** with dynamic viscosity μ = K. Hence for a Newtonian fluid, K reduces to the dynamic viscosity μ.

**Step 5 — Physical significance of K:**

K is called the **flow consistency index**. Its value is a measure of the fluid's "thickness" or resistance to flow. Numerically, K equals the apparent viscosity of the fluid at a unit shear rate of 1 s⁻¹. Its SI units are **Pa·sⁿ** (not Pa·s), reducing to Pa·s only when n = 1.

**Awarding Marks (7 marks):** 1 mark for writing the power-law equation, 1 mark for the apparent viscosity definition, 1 mark for correct algebraic manipulation, 1 mark for the n = 1 limit, 1 mark for the conclusion that K = μ in the Newtonian case, 2 marks for the physical significance and units of K.

---

**(b) Part — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Yield stress: τy = 12 Pa
- Plastic viscosity: μp = 0.05 Pa·s
- Velocity gradient (shear rate): du/dy = 150 s⁻¹

**(i) Shear stress calculation:**

The Bingham plastic constitutive equation is:

$$\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \frac{du}{dy}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\tau = 12 + (0.05) \times (150)$$

$$\tau = 12 + 7.5 = 19.5 \text{ Pa}$$

**(ii) Apparent viscosity calculation:**

$$\mu_a = \frac{\tau}{(du/dy)} = \frac{19.5}{150} = 0.13 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Cross-check using the derived Bingham apparent-viscosity formula:**

$$\mu_a = \mu_p + \frac{\tau_y}{(du/dy)} = 0.05 + \frac{12}{150} = 0.05 + 0.08 = 0.13 \text{ Pa·s}$$

Both methods give the same answer, confirming the calculation.

**Final Answer:** (i) τ = **19.5 Pa**; (ii) μₐ = **0.13 Pa·s**.

**Awarding Marks (7 marks):** 1 mark for selecting the Bingham equation, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for τ value, 1 mark for selecting the apparent viscosity formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for μₐ value, 1 mark for the cross-check or unit consistency.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks (as per KTU 2024 Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Unit error in K:** Many students write K with units of **Pa·s**. This is wrong for non-Newtonian fluids. Correct units are **Pa·sⁿ** (or equivalently, N·sⁿ/m²). Marks are deducted for the unit error.
>
> 2. **Confusing K and μ:** K is the **consistency index** (not a viscosity) for non-Newtonian fluids. K = μ only when n = 1 (Newtonian limit).
>
> 3. **Forgetting the yield stress in Bingham fluids:** Some students use $\tau = \mu_p (du/dy)$ for Bingham plastic fluids, ignoring the τy term. This loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Wrong range of n:** Pseudo-plastic is **0 < n < 1** (NOT n < 0). Dilatant is **n > 1** (NOT 0 < n < 1 reversed). Confusing this range loses the entire conceptual mark.
>
> 5. **Drawing rheological diagrams without axes labels:** If the examiner asks to "sketch the curve" and you draw only the curve without labelling the τ-axis, du/dy-axis, and the slope/τy intercept, you lose up to 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Skipping the algebraic step in apparent-viscosity derivation:** If the question is a 7-mark derivation, you must show the substitution of τ into μₐ = τ/(du/dy) and the law-of-exponents simplification step explicitly. Skipping directly to the final formula loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 7. **Mixing up pseudo-plastic and thixotropic:** Pseudo-plastic is **time-independent** shear-thinning. Thixotropic is **time-dependent** shear-thinning. These are distinct categories.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ — the foundation of all fluid mechanics.
- [ ] **Dynamic viscosity μ:** Units = **Pa·s** = **N·s/m²** = **kg/(m·s)**.
- [ ] **Kinematic viscosity ν:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ — Units = **m²/s**.
- [ ] **Newtonian fluid:** μ is constant, independent of shear rate. Examples: water, air, glycerin, alcohol.
- [ ] **Bingham Plastic:** $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p (du/dy)$ — needs yield stress τy to start flowing. Examples: toothpaste, drilling mud.
- [ ] **Pseudo-plastic (Shear-thinning):** Power-law with **0 < n < 1**. Viscosity drops as shear rate rises. Examples: blood, paint.
- [ ] **Dilatant (Shear-thickening):** Power-law with **n > 1**. Viscosity rises as shear rate increases. Examples: wet sand, cornstarch slurry.
- [ ] **Thixotropic:** Time-dependent shear-thinning. Examples: honey, gels.
- [ ] **Rheopectic:** Time-dependent shear-thickening. Examples: printer ink, gypsum paste.
- [ ] **Apparent viscosity (general):** $\mu_a = \tau / (du/dy)$ — varies with shear rate for non-Newtonian fluids.
- [ ] **Apparent viscosity (power-law):** $\mu_a = K (du/dy)^{n-1}$ — derived by substituting τ into the definition.
- [ ] **Apparent viscosity (Bingham):** $\mu_a = \mu_p + \tau_y / (du/dy)$ — derived similarly.
- [ ] **K vs. μ:** K = μ only for Newtonian fluids (n = 1). K has units of **Pa·sⁿ** in general.
- [ ] **Rheological diagram essentials:** Label both axes (τ on y-axis, du/dy on x-axis), mark the yield stress τy for Bingham, distinguish concave-up vs concave-down curves, and indicate that the Newtonian line passes through the origin with slope μ.
- [ ] **Engineering design takeaway:** For pipe flow of non-Newtonian fluids, use μₐ (apparent viscosity) in the Reynolds number and friction-factor correlations.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 focus:** Module 1 typically asks (a) a 7-mark derivation of μₐ for the power-law model, and (b) a 7-mark classification with a rheological diagram. Be ready for both.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real and Ideal Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Real and Ideal Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Fluid** is a substance that continuously deforms (flows) under the action of an applied shear stress or external force. In engineering fluid mechanics, fluids are broadly classified into two philosophical categories: **Ideal Fluids** and **Real Fluids**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> An **Ideal Fluid** is a hypothetical fluid that possesses the following idealized properties:
> 1. It is **inviscid** (zero viscosity, $\mu = 0$).
> 2. It is **incompressible** (constant density, $\rho = \text{constant}$).
> 3. Its flow is **frictionless** (no internal friction or shear resistance between fluid layers).
>
> A **Real Fluid** is a fluid that actually exists in nature. It possesses **viscosity** ($\mu \neq 0$), exhibits **compressibility** under pressure, and resists deformation due to internal shear stresses. Examples: water, air, oil, and honey.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pouring two liquids down a smooth inclined plane:

- **Ideal Fluid Analogy**: Think of pouring a frictionless, magical liquid (like a super-fluid that physicists study near absolute zero). The liquid slides down the plane without any resistance. It would have no thickness, no stickiness to the surface, and every particle would move identically. This is what engineers *assume* in their theoretical models to simplify complex mathematics.
- **Real Fluid Analogy**: Now think of pouring honey or thick syrup down the same plane. You will notice that:
  - The layer in direct contact with the plane **sticks** to it (no-slip condition).
  - The layers above slide over one another at different velocities.
  - Energy is lost as heat due to internal friction.
  
This internal "stickiness" is precisely what **viscosity** represents in real fluids. No real fluid is truly ideal, but assuming so (like in Bernoulli's equation derivations) allows engineers to solve practical problems with surprising accuracy.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Fluid Properties at Standard Conditions (20°C, 1 atm):**
> - **Density of water ($\rho$)**: **$\rho = 998 \, \text{kg/m}^3$**
> - **Dynamic viscosity of water ($\mu$)**: **$\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$**
> - **Kinematic viscosity of water ($\nu$)**: **$\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$**
> - **Specific weight of water ($\gamma$)**: **$\gamma = 9790 \, \text{N/m}^3$**
> - **Surface tension of water ($\sigma$)**: **$\sigma = 0.0728 \, \text{N/m}$**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown: The Two Worlds of Fluid Mechanics

#### A. Ideal Fluid (Theoretical Construct)

An ideal fluid is a mathematical abstraction used to simplify the governing equations of motion (specifically the Navier-Stokes equations). The three defining attributes are:

1. **Zero Viscosity**: The fluid offers no resistance to shear deformation. If you apply a tangential force, it flows indefinitely without losing kinetic energy to internal friction.
2. **Incompressibility**: The density $\rho$ remains constant regardless of pressure changes. Mathematically, $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$.
3. **No Surface Tension Effects**: In bulk flow analyses, capillary phenomena are ignored.

**Why use it?** Because the Euler equation (momentum equation for frictionless flow) is dramatically simpler than the full Navier-Stokes equation, making analytical solutions tractable.

#### B. Real Fluid (Practical Reality)

Real fluids inherently exhibit the following physical phenomena:

1. **Viscosity**: Internal friction between fluid layers causes momentum diffusion. Governed by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**.
2. **Compressibility**: Density varies with pressure and temperature (e.g., gases at high Mach numbers).
3. **Surface Tension**: Molecules at the interface experience cohesive forces, leading to capillary effects.
4. **Vapor Pressure**: Real fluids evaporate, which causes cavitation in hydraulic systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Real Fluids:**
> A **Newtonian fluid** obeys Newton's law of viscosity linearly (water, air, oils). A **Non-Newtonian fluid** does not (blood, ketchup, paint, slurry). KTU Module 1 primarily focuses on Newtonian fluids.

### 2.2 Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Cornerstone Concept

Newton postulated that for a real fluid flowing in laminar conditions, the **shear stress ($\tau$)** is directly proportional to the **rate of shear strain** (velocity gradient perpendicular to flow direction).

The mathematical statement is:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress at a fluid layer $\left[\text{N/m}^2 \text{ or Pa}\right]$
- $\mu$ = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity of the fluid $\left[\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}\right]$
- $\frac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient $\left[\text{s}^{-1}\right]$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Defines viscous behavior |
| 2 | Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{\left(du/dy\right)}$ | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ | Resistance to deformation |
| 3 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | Momentum diffusivity |
| 4 | Specific Weight | $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$ | $\text{N/m}^3$ | Weight per unit volume |
| 5 | Specific Gravity | $S = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ | Dimensionless | Density ratio |
| 6 | Bulk Modulus (Compressibility) | $K = -V \dfrac{dp}{dV}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Resistance to compression |
| 7 | Compressibility | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{K}$ | $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ | Inverse of bulk modulus |
| 8 | Ideal Gas Law | $p = \rho R T$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Equation of state |
| 9 | Vapour Pressure Relation | $p_{v} = f(T)$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Evaporation threshold |
| 10 | Surface Tension Force | $F = \sigma \cdot L$ | $\text{N}$ | Force per unit length |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Hydraulic Machines (Pumps and Turbines)**: Real-fluid viscosity determines the **mechanical efficiency** and **head losses** in pumps and turbines.
- **Pipeline Design (Oil & Gas)**: Friction factor calculations (Darcy-Weisbach equation) rely entirely on real-fluid viscosity.
- **Aerospace Engineering**: Air behaves like an ideal fluid at low Mach numbers; at transonic speeds, compressibility effects become critical.
- **Biomedical Engineering**: Blood is a non-Newtonian real fluid — understanding its rheology is essential for designing stents and dialysis machines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Newton's Law of Viscosity from a Momentum Balance

Consider a fluid element sandwiched between two parallel plates:

- The **lower plate** is stationary ($u = 0$).
- The **upper plate** moves with velocity $U$ in the positive $x$-direction.
- The gap between plates is $dy$.
- A viscous force $F$ acts on the top plate to maintain motion.

**Step 1:** Define the velocity gradient at the fluid layer.

The velocity changes from $0$ at the bottom to $U$ at the top across distance $dy$.

$$\frac{du}{dy} \approx \frac{U - 0}{dy} = \frac{U}{dy}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Newton's Hypothesis.

Newton stated that the shear stress $\tau$ is proportional to the rate of shear strain (which equals the velocity gradient for steady flow):

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 3:** Introduce the proportionality constant.

The constant of proportionality is the dynamic viscosity $\mu$:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 4:** Convert shear stress to viscous force.

Since shear stress is force per unit area, multiplying by contact area $A$:

$$F = \tau \cdot A = \mu A \frac{du}{dy}$$

This is the **fundamental viscous force equation** used throughout fluid mechanics.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity

**Step 1:** Start with the definitions.

Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ has units $\text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2$ or $\text{kg/(m}\cdot\text{s)}$.

Density $\rho$ has units $\text{kg/m}^3$.

**Step 2:** Form the ratio.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Step 3:** Verify the units.

$$\frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{\text{kg/(m}\cdot\text{s)}}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \frac{\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{kg}} = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$

This confirms that kinematic viscosity has units of $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, representing **momentum diffusivity**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Bulk Modulus of a Real Fluid

**Step 1:** Consider a fluid element of volume $V$ subjected to a pressure increase $dp$.

The corresponding volume change is $dV$ (negative for compression).

**Step 2:** Define volumetric strain.

$$\text{Volumetric Strain} = -\frac{dV}{V}$$

**Step 3:** Define Bulk Modulus $K$ as the ratio of pressure increase to volumetric strain:

$$K = -\frac{dp}{\left(\dfrac{dV}{V}\right)} = -V \frac{dp}{dV}$$

**Step 4:** Define Compressibility $\beta$ as the reciprocal:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{K} = -\frac{1}{V} \frac{dV}{dp}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Water has a bulk modulus $K \approx 2.2 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa}$, making it nearly incompressible for most hydraulic engineering applications. This is why we treat water as incompressible even though it is technically a real fluid.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Continuum Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **The Continuum Hypothesis** is foundational: we assume fluid properties (density, velocity, pressure) are defined at every point in space as continuous functions. This breaks down at molecular scales (Knudsen number $\geq 0.1$), but holds for virtually all engineering applications.

| Property | Ideal Fluid | Real Fluid | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Viscosity ($\mu$) | $\mu = 0$ | $\mu \neq 0$ | Affects pumping power, head loss |
| Density ($\rho$) | Constant | Variable with $p, T$ | Affects cavitation, acoustic waves |
| Compressibility ($\beta$) | $\beta = 0$ | $\beta \neq 0$ | Critical in shock waves, hydraulic transient |
| Surface Tension ($\sigma$) | Ignored | $\sigma \neq 0$ | Capillary rise, droplet formation |
| Vapour Pressure | Ignored | $p_v \neq 0$ | Cavitation onset in pumps |
| Flow Regime | Frictionless | Viscous (laminar/turbulent) | Determines Reynolds number behavior |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Classification of Fluids

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[FLUIDS] --> nodeB[IDEAL FLUIDS]
    nodeA --> nodeC[REAL FLUIDS]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Inviscid mu equals zero]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Incompressible rho constant]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Frictionless Flow]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Newtonian Fluids]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Non Newtonian Fluids]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1a[Water]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1b[Air]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1c[Thin Oils]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2a[Blood Pseudoplastic]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2b[Paint Thixotropic]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2c[Ketchup Bingham Plastic]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Newton's Law of Viscosity — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeInput[Applied Force F] --> nodePlate[Upper Moving Plate Velocity U]
    nodePlate --> nodeGradient[Velocity Gradient du by dy]
    nodeGap[Fluid Gap Thickness dy] --> nodeGradient
    nodeGradient --> nodeShear[Shear Stress tau]
    nodeViscosity[Dynamic Viscosity mu] --> nodeShear
    nodeShear --> nodeResult[Newton Equation tau equals mu du by dy]
    nodeResult --> nodeEng[Engineering Use Pump Power Loss]
    nodeResult --> nodeEng2[Engineering Use Pipeline Design]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Velocity Profile Between Two Parallel Plates (Real Fluid Behavior)

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Stationary Bottom Plate u equals 0] --> node2[No Slip Condition]
    node2 --> node3[Linear Velocity Profile for Newtonian Fluid]
    node3 --> node4[Top Plate Moves at U]
    node4 --> node5[Constant Velocity Gradient du by dy equals U divided by dy]
    node5 --> node6[Shear Stress tau is Constant]
    node6 --> node7[Real Fluid: Energy Loss Occurs]
    node7 --> node8[Result: Pumping Power Required]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define an ideal fluid. Is it possible to have a truly ideal fluid in nature? Justify your answer.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** An ideal fluid is a hypothetical fluid which is inviscid (viscosity $\mu = 0$), incompressible (constant density $\rho$), and frictionless. It obeys the continuity equation and Euler's equations of motion perfectly without any energy loss due to internal friction.

**Justification (1 Mark):** No, a truly ideal fluid cannot exist in nature because every real fluid possesses some degree of viscosity and compressibility. However, assumptions of ideal flow give reasonably accurate results in many practical engineering problems (e.g., flow past an airfoil at low speeds, Bernoulli's equation applications).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*State Newton's law of viscosity. Explain the physical significance of the coefficient of viscosity.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (2 Marks):** Newton's law of viscosity states that the shear stress ($\tau$) developed in a fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain, i.e., the velocity gradient ($du/dy$) normal to the flow direction. Mathematically:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Physical Significance (1 Mark):** The coefficient of dynamic viscosity ($\mu$) represents the fluid's internal resistance to deformation or flow. A higher $\mu$ means the fluid is more viscous (e.g., honey), while a lower $\mu$ means it flows more easily (e.g., water).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Distinguish clearly between an ideal fluid and a real fluid. Discuss the significance of viscosity, compressibility, and surface tension in real-fluid analysis.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 25 mm thick layer of a Newtonian fluid is placed between two horizontal parallel plates. The lower plate is fixed and the upper plate moves at 1.5 m/s. If the dynamic viscosity of the fluid is $\mu = 0.85 \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, calculate the shear stress developed on the plates and the viscous force per unit area acting on the fluid.

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Distinction between Ideal and Real Fluid (7 Marks)

| Property | Ideal Fluid | Real Fluid |
|---|---|---|
| Viscosity | Zero ($\mu = 0$) | Non-zero ($\mu \neq 0$) |
| Density | Constant (incompressible) | Variable (compressible) |
| Flow | Frictionless | Viscous, dissipative |
| Surface Tension | Neglected | Significant at interfaces |
| Vapor Pressure | Not considered | Considered (cavitation) |
| Energy Loss | None | Head loss due to friction |
| Examples | Theoretical only | Water, air, oil, blood |

**Significance of Properties (3 Marks):**

- **Viscosity**: Determines the **shear stress** between fluid layers. Critical in pipe flow (Reynolds number), pump efficiency, and bearing lubrication.
- **Compressibility**: Governs the bulk modulus behavior. Important in hydraulic transients (water hammer), shock wave analysis, and gas dynamics.
- **Surface Tension**: Affects capillary rise, droplet formation, and meniscus shape in manometers.

#### Part (b) — Shear Stress Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Plate gap: $dy = 25 \, \text{mm} = 0.025 \, \text{m}$
- Upper plate velocity: $U = 1.5 \, \text{m/s}$
- Dynamic viscosity: $\mu = 0.85 \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- Lower plate: stationary

**Step 1: Calculate the velocity gradient (Newtonian assumption — linear profile)**

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U - 0}{dy} = \frac{1.5}{0.025}$$

$$\frac{du}{dy} = 60 \, \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity**

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = 0.85 \times 60$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 51 \, \text{N/m}^2 = 51 \, \text{Pa}}$$

**Step 3: Viscous Force per Unit Area**

By definition, shear stress IS the viscous force per unit area. So:

$$\boxed{\frac{F}{A} = \tau = 51 \, \text{Pa}}$$

**Step 4: Total Viscous Force (if plate area is needed in extension)**

For a plate of area $A$, the force would be $F = \tau \cdot A$.

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam — Alternative Module 1 Choice]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Define and explain the following fluid properties with appropriate units: (i) Dynamic viscosity, (ii) Kinematic viscosity, (iii) Specific weight, (iv) Specific gravity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hydraulic oil has a density of $880 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ and dynamic viscosity of $0.025 \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. Find (i) the kinematic viscosity, (ii) the specific weight, and (iii) the specific gravity of the oil. Take density of water $= 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Definitions (7 Marks)

**(i) Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** It is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to the deformation (shear) of the fluid. Defined by Newton's law as $\mu = \tau / (du/dy)$. Units: **$\text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2$** or **$\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$**.

**(ii) Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** It is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to density, $\nu = \mu / \rho$. It represents momentum diffusivity. Units: **$\text{m}^2/\text{s}$**.

**(iii) Specific Weight ($\gamma$):** Weight per unit volume of the fluid. $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$. Units: **$\text{N/m}^3$**.

**(iv) Specific Gravity ($S$):** Ratio of density of the fluid to density of pure water at 4°C. Dimensionless. $S = \rho_{\text{fluid}} / \rho_{\text{water}}$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $\rho_{\text{oil}} = 880 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\mu_{\text{oil}} = 0.025 \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

**(i) Kinematic Viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.025}{880}$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 2.841 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}}$$

**(ii) Specific Weight:**

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g = 880 \times 9.81$$

$$\boxed{\gamma = 8632.8 \, \text{N/m}^3}$$

**(iii) Specific Gravity:**

$$S = \frac{\rho_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \frac{880}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.88}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (Cost: 1–2 marks):** Students frequently forget to convert mm to m in calculations. Always verify SI units before plugging values into formulas.
> 2. **Confusing $\mu$ and $\nu$ (Cost: 1 mark):** Dynamic viscosity ($\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$) vs. Kinematic viscosity ($\text{m}^2/\text{s}$). They are NOT interchangeable.
> 3. **No-slip condition (Cost: 1 mark):** In real-fluid problems between plates, ALWAYS state that the fluid in contact with the stationary plate has $u = 0$.
> 4. **Misstating ideal fluid assumptions (Cost: 1 mark):** An ideal fluid is NOT frictionless in the engineering sense — it is frictionless in the sense of zero viscous shear stress.
> 5. **Missing Newton's Law Statement (Cost: 2 marks):** For any viscosity-related question, you MUST explicitly state Newton's law of viscosity with its mathematical form before applying it.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Ideal Fluid** = Inviscid + Incompressible + Frictionless (theoretical only).
- **Real Fluid** = Possesses viscosity + compressibility + surface tension effects.
- **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ — the bedrock of viscous flow.
- **Dynamic Viscosity $\mu$**: Units $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$; measures resistance to shear.
- **Kinematic Viscosity $\nu = \mu/\rho$**: Units $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$; momentum diffusivity.
- **Specific Weight $\gamma = \rho g$**: Units $\text{N/m}^3$.
- **Specific Gravity $S$**: Dimensionless ratio with water.
- **Bulk Modulus $K$**: Measures incompressibility; water $K \approx 2.2 \, \text{GPa}$.
- **No-slip condition**: Fluid at a solid boundary has the same velocity as the boundary.
- **Newtonian fluids** obey $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ linearly; non-Newtonian fluids do not.
- **Continuum hypothesis**: Holds for $Kn < 0.1$ (most engineering flows).
- Always use **SI units** in KTU numerical problems; convert mm $\to$ m, kPa $\to$ Pa before substituting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incompressible and Compressible Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Incompressible and Compressible Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compressibility** is defined as the property of a fluid by virtue of which its **density** (or specific volume) changes under the action of an external pressure or force. It is the reciprocal of the **Bulk Modulus of Elasticity (K)**.

A fluid is classified as **incompressible** when its density remains practically constant during flow, i.e., $\rho = \text{constant}$, even when subjected to appreciable pressure changes. Conversely, a fluid is **compressible** when its density varies significantly with pressure and temperature during the flow process.

**Mathematical Classification Rule (KTU Standard):**
A fluid is treated as **incompressible** when the **density change** $\Delta \rho$ is less than **5%** of the original density ($\Delta \rho / \rho < 0.05$). Beyond this threshold, compressibility effects must be accounted for.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine squeezing a sponge vs. a water bottle:**
> - **Water in a sealed bottle** (Incompressible): When you press the bottle, the water barely changes its density. The molecules of liquid are already tightly packed, so they resist compression.
> - **Air in a bicycle pump** (Compressible): When you push the piston, the air molecules squeeze closer together dramatically. The density visibly increases, and you can feel resistance changing.

**Engineering Examples:**
- **Incompressible fluids**: Water, oil, mercury, and most liquids in routine hydraulic/pump applications.
- **Compressible fluids**: Air, natural gas, steam (especially in turbines), and high-velocity gas flows in nozzles, jets, and rockets.

### 1.3 Bulk Modulus of Elasticity ($K$)

The **Bulk Modulus** quantifies a fluid's resistance to uniform compression:

$$K = -V \frac{dP}{dV} = \rho \frac{dP}{d\rho}$$

where:
- $K$ = Bulk Modulus of Elasticity in **N/m²** (or **Pa**)
- $V$ = Original volume
- $dP$ = Change in pressure
- $dV$ = Change in volume
- $\rho$ = Mass density

The negative sign ensures $K$ remains positive (since an increase in pressure causes a decrease in volume, making $dP$ and $dV$ opposite in sign).

### 1.4 Compressibility ($\beta$)

$$\beta = \frac{1}{K} = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dV}{dP} = \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{d\rho}{dP}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Liquids have a very high $K$ (water $\approx 2.1 \times 10^9$ N/m²) and hence very low $\beta$. Gases have low $K$ and high $\beta$. This is why gases are treated as compressible and liquids as incompressible in most engineering applications.

### 1.5 Standard Values of Bulk Modulus

| Fluid | Bulk Modulus $K$ (N/m²) | Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) |
|---|---|---|
| Water (20°C) | $2.20 \times 10^9$ | 998 |
| Sea Water | $2.34 \times 10^9$ | 1025 |
| Mercury | $2.74 \times 10^{10}$ | 13550 |
| Oil (typical) | $1.6 \times 10^9$ | 870 |
| Air (atmos. cond.) | $1.01 \times 10^5$ | 1.225 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mach Number — The Most Important Classifier in Compressible Flow

The **Mach Number (M)** is a dimensionless quantity representing the ratio of the flow velocity to the local **speed of sound** in the medium:

$$M = \frac{V}{c}$$

where:
- $V$ = Velocity of the fluid flow (m/s)
- $c$ = Local speed of sound in the medium (m/s)

### 2.2 Flow Classification by Mach Number

| Mach Number Range | Classification | Physical Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| $M < 0.3$ | **Incompressible flow** | Density variation $< 5\%$; Bernoulli's applicable |
| $0.3 < M < 0.8$ | **Subsonic (compressible)** | Density changes noticeable |
| $0.8 < M < 1.2$ | **Transonic** | Mixed subsonic/supersonic regions; shock waves form |
| $1.2 < M < 5.0$ | **Supersonic** | Shock waves, expansion fans dominate |
| $M \geq 5.0$ | **Hypersonic** | Strong shock waves, high-temperature effects |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention (2024 Scheme):** All flows with $M \leq 0.3$ are modeled as **incompressible** in undergraduate problems. Above $M = 0.3$, compressibility effects must be included.

### 2.3 Speed of Sound in a Fluid (Newton–Laplace Equation)

The speed of sound in a fluid medium is derived from the isentropic (reversible adiabatic) compressibility:

$$c = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_s}$$

For an **ideal gas** undergoing isentropic process, $P/\rho^\gamma = \text{constant}$, so:

$$c = \sqrt{\gamma \frac{P}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$$

where:
- $\gamma$ = Ratio of specific heats ($C_p/C_v$)
- $R$ = Characteristic gas constant (J/kg·K)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)
- $P$ = Absolute static pressure (Pa)
- $\rho$ = Density (kg/m³)

> [!NOTE]
> **For air**: $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/kg·K. At standard atmosphere ($T = 288.15$ K): $c = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 288.15} \approx 340.3$ m/s.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K = -V \frac{dP}{dV}$ | Bulk Modulus definition | Pa |
| 2 | $K = \rho \frac{dP}{d\rho}$ | Bulk Modulus via density | Pa |
| 3 | $\beta = \frac{1}{K}$ | Compressibility | Pa⁻¹ |
| 4 | $M = \dfrac{V}{c}$ | Mach number | dimensionless |
| 5 | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Speed of sound (ideal gas) | m/s |
| 6 | $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ | Speed of sound (any fluid) | m/s |
| 7 | $P/\rho^\gamma = \text{const.}$ | Isentropic process | — |
| 8 | $P/\rho = R T$ | Ideal gas equation | — |
| 9 | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ | Compressible continuity | kg/s |
| 10 | $\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} < 0.05$ | Incompressibility criterion | — |
| 11 | $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$ | Specific heat ratio | dimensionless |
| 12 | $a^* = \sqrt{\gamma R T^*}$ | Sonic (critical) velocity | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> - **Aerospace**: Aircraft designed for specific Mach regimes (Concorde: M=2.04, fighter jets: M=2+).
> - **Pipeline engineering**: Natural gas pipelines must account for compressibility to predict pressure drop accurately.
> - **Hydraulic systems**: Water (essentially incompressible) is used for instantaneous force transmission in brakes, lifts, and presses.
> - **Nozzle/Diffuser design**: Compressible flow theory governs rocket and jet engine nozzles.
> - **Cavitation analysis**: Bulk modulus of liquids determines pressure wave speed in hydraulic systems.

### 2.5 Why Incompressibility Assumption Fails

The **Why** behind incompressibility breaking down:
1. At high velocities, local static pressure drops (Bernoulli effect), causing **local density reduction**.
2. In nozzles of rockets/turbines, gases expand rapidly → large $\Delta \rho$.
3. In shock waves, density changes occur almost discontinuously across a thin region.

The **How** to decide:
- If $M < 0.3$ → Use **incompressible Bernoulli**: $P/\rho g + V^2/2g + z = \text{const.}$
- If $M \geq 0.3$ → Use **compressible flow equations** (isentropic relations, normal shock equations).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Speed of Sound from First Principles

**Step 1: Start with the Bulk Modulus**
By definition of Bulk Modulus for a small disturbance (isentropic):

$$K = \rho \frac{dP}{d\rho}$$

**Step 2: Definition of Speed of Sound**
The speed of sound is the speed at which an infinitesimally small pressure disturbance propagates through a fluid. For a 1D wave in a fluid element:

$$c = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_s}$$

This is because the disturbance travels at the rate governed by the local compressibility.

**Step 3: Apply the Isentropic Relation for an Ideal Gas**
For an isentropic process of an ideal gas:

$$P \rho^{-\gamma} = \text{constant} = C$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $\rho$:

$$P (-\gamma) \rho^{-\gamma - 1} + \rho^{-\gamma} \frac{dP}{d\rho} = 0$$

$$\frac{dP}{d\rho} = \gamma P \rho^{-\gamma} \cdot \rho^{\gamma + 1} \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^\gamma}$$

Wait, let us simplify this step more carefully. Multiplying the first term:

$$\frac{dP}{d\rho} = \gamma \cdot \frac{P}{\rho}$$

**Step 4: Substitute to Obtain the Speed of Sound**

$$c = \sqrt{\gamma \frac{P}{\rho}}$$

**Step 5: Convert to Temperature Form Using Ideal Gas Law**
For an ideal gas: $P = \rho R T$, therefore $\dfrac{P}{\rho} = R T$.

$$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}}$$

This is the famous **Newton–Laplace** result, which fixes Newton's original (incorrect) isothermal assumption by using $\gamma$ for the isentropic case.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Continuity Equation for Compressible Flow

For a steady, 1D compressible flow through a variable-area duct, **mass** (not volume) is conserved.

**Step 1: Mass Flow Rate Definition**

$$\dot{m} = \rho A V = \text{constant along a streamline}$$

**Step 2: Differentiate with Respect to Streamwise Direction**
Taking logarithmic differentiation:

$$\ln \rho + \ln A + \ln V = \text{constant}$$

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dA}{A} + \frac{dV}{V} = 0$$

**Step 3: Express in Differential Form**

$$\boxed{\frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dA}{A} + \frac{dV}{V} = 0}$$

**Step 4: For Incompressible Flow**
When $\rho = \text{constant}$ → $d\rho = 0$, so:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

which is the familiar **incompressible continuity equation**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship Between Density Change and Mach Number

Starting from the continuity equation in differential form, and using the fact that for a compressible flow in a duct:

$$\frac{dP}{\rho} + V \, dV = 0 \quad \text{(Euler's equation, inviscid)}$$

This integrates (for incompressible) to the standard Bernoulli form. But for compressible flow, we can express:

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} = \frac{dP}{K} = \frac{dP}{\rho c^2}$$

Substituting the Euler equation $dP = -\rho V \, dV$:

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} = \frac{-\rho V \, dV}{\rho c^2} = -\frac{V}{c^2} dV = -M^2 \frac{dV}{V}$$

Substituting this into the continuity equation:

$$-M^2 \frac{dV}{V} + \frac{dA}{A} + \frac{dV}{V} = 0$$

$$(1 - M^2) \frac{dV}{V} + \frac{dA}{A} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\frac{dV}{V} = -\frac{1}{1-M^2} \cdot \frac{dA}{A}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation of the result above:**
> - When $M < 1$ (subsonic): denominator positive → $dA$ increase causes $dV$ decrease (converging nozzle accelerates flow).
> - When $M > 1$ (supersonic): denominator negative → $dA$ increase causes $dV$ increase (diverging nozzle accelerates flow).
> - This explains the **converging–diverging nozzle** behavior, a key KTU examination topic.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Incompressible vs. Compressible Flow

| Parameter | Incompressible Flow | Compressible Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Density $\rho$ | Constant | Variable |
| Continuity | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ |
| Energy equation | $P/\rho + V^2/2 + gz = \text{const}$ | $C_p T + V^2/2 = \text{const}$ (adiabatic) |
| Mach number | $M \leq 0.3$ | $M > 0.3$ |
| Speed of sound | Not relevant | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ |
| Governing equations | Bernoulli, Euler, Navier–Stokes | Full Euler + isentropic/thermodynamic relations |
| Typical fluids | Water, oil, mercury | Air, steam, natural gas, combustion gases |
| Engineering examples | Pumps, turbines (water), pipe flow | Jet engines, rocket nozzles, supersonic wind tunnels |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Compressible vs. Incompressible Flow — Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Fluid Flow Problem] --> nodeB[Compute Mach Number M = V / c]
    nodeB --> nodeC{M less than 0.3?}
    nodeC -- YES --> nodeD[Classify as INCOMPRESSIBLE]
    nodeC -- NO --> nodeE{M less than 1.0?}
    nodeE -- YES --> nodeF[SUBSONIC Compressible Flow]
    nodeE -- NO --> nodeG{M approx 1.0?}
    nodeG -- YES --> nodeH[TRANSONIC Flow with Shock Waves]
    nodeG -- NO --> nodeI{M greater than 5.0?}
    nodeI -- NO --> nodeJ[SUPERSONIC Flow]
    nodeI -- YES --> nodeK[HYPERSONIC Flow with High Temperature Effects]
    nodeD --> nodeL[Apply Bernoulli Equation]
    nodeF --> nodeM[Apply Isentropic Compressible Relations]
    nodeH --> nodeN[Apply Normal Shock Equations]
    nodeJ --> nodeO[Apply Prandtl-Meyer Expansion]
    nodeK --> nodeP[Apply Hypersonic Approximations]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Compressible Flow Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Input Data: V, P, T, Fluid Type] --> node2[Compute Speed of Sound c]
    node2 --> node3[Compute Mach Number M]
    node3 --> node4{Choose Flow Model}
    node4 --> node5[Isentropic Relations]
    node4 --> node6[Normal Shock Relations]
    node4 --> node7[Fanno Flow for Ducts]
    node4 --> node8[Rayleigh Flow for Combustion]
    node5 --> node9[Compute Static and Stagnation Quantities]
    node6 --> node10[Compute Pre and Post Shock States]
    node7 --> node11[Compute Friction Effects]
    node8 --> node12[Compute Heat Addition Effects]
    node9 --> node13[Final Design Output]
    node10 --> node13
    node11 --> node13
    node12 --> node13
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: Density Variation in Different Flow Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Fluid Element under Flow] --> nodeB[Velocity V applied]
    nodeB --> nodeC{M less than 0.3?}
    nodeC -- YES --> nodeD[Density Change negligible]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Treat rho as constant]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Use Incompressible Equations]
    nodeC -- NO --> nodeG[Density Changes significant]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Compute d rho using Bulk Modulus]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Apply Compressible Equations]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Account for T and P variation]
    nodeF --> nodeK[Engineering Solution]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define the term **Mach Number** and explain the classification of fluid flow based on Mach number.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Mach Number is the ratio of the velocity of the fluid to the local speed of sound in the medium: $M = V/c$. It is a dimensionless quantity.
Classification:
- $M < 0.3$: Incompressible flow
- $0.3 < M < 0.8$: Subsonic compressible
- $0.8 < M < 1.2$: Transonic
- $1.2 < M < 5.0$: Supersonic
- $M \geq 5.0$: Hypersonic

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q2.** What is the **Bulk Modulus of Elasticity**? Write its formula and state its SI unit.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Bulk Modulus of Elasticity ($K$) is defined as the ratio of change in pressure to the corresponding volumetric strain in a fluid.
Formula: $K = -V \cdot (dP/dV) = \rho \cdot (dP/d\rho)$
SI Unit: N/m² (Pascal, Pa)
For water at standard conditions, $K \approx 2.1 \times 10^9$ N/m².

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, Module 1]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **speed of sound in an ideal gas** and show that it depends only on temperature. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Bulk Modulus Definition**
The speed of sound in a fluid is given by the isentropic compressibility relation:
$$c = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_s}$$

**Step 2: Apply Isentropic Relation for Ideal Gas**
For an isentropic process: $P / \rho^\gamma = C$ (constant)
Differentiating: $-\gamma P \rho^{-\gamma-1} d\rho + \rho^{-\gamma} dP = 0$
$$dP = \gamma \frac{P}{\rho} d\rho$$

**Step 3: Obtain Speed of Sound**
$$c = \sqrt{\gamma \frac{P}{\rho}}$$

**Step 4: Express in Terms of Temperature**
From ideal gas law: $P = \rho R T \Rightarrow P/\rho = R T$
$$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}}$$

This expression shows that $c$ depends only on the absolute temperature $T$ (since $\gamma$ and $R$ are properties of the gas). **(7 Marks complete)**

**(b)** Air flows through a duct at a velocity of **250 m/s**. If the static temperature is **300 K**, determine: (i) Mach number, (ii) speed of sound. Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $R = 287$ J/kg·K. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Speed of Sound**
$$c = \sqrt{\gamma R T} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 300}$$
$$c = \sqrt{120540} = 347.19 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Mach Number**
$$M = \frac{V}{c} = \frac{250}{347.19} = 0.720$$

**Step 3: Classification**
Since $0.3 < M < 0.8$, the flow is classified as **subsonic compressible flow**.

> **Final Answers:** $c = 347.19$ m/s, $M = 0.72$ **(7 Marks complete)**

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **classification of fluid flow** based on Mach number. Discuss why the **incompressible flow assumption** becomes invalid above $M = 0.3$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Mach Number Classification** (with the schematic shown in Section 4 above)
The flow is classified into five regimes based on the Mach number $M = V/c$:
- $M \leq 0.3$: Incompressible
- $0.3 < M < 1.0$: Subsonic
- $M \approx 1.0$: Sonic (and transonic near this value)
- $1.0 < M < 5.0$: Supersonic
- $M \geq 5.0$: Hypersonic

**Step 2: Why Incompressibility Fails Above M = 0.3**
The percentage change in density in terms of Mach number can be shown (using Bernoulli + isentropic relations) to be:
$$\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \approx M^2 \times 100\%$$

For $M = 0.3$: $\Delta \rho / \rho \approx 9\%$ (already > 5% threshold).
For $M = 0.5$: $\Delta \rho / \rho \approx 25\%$ (significant).

**Step 3: Conclusion**
Hence, the KTU-standard 5% density variation threshold is exceeded at $M = 0.3$, beyond which the fluid must be treated as compressible. **(7 Marks complete)**

**(b)** A liquid has a Bulk Modulus of $2.5 \times 10^9$ N/m² and density 1000 kg/m³. Find: (i) the speed of sound in the liquid, (ii) the change in pressure required to cause a **1% change in density**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Speed of Sound**
$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.5 \times 10^9}{1000}}$$
$$c = \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^6} = 1581.14 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Pressure Change for 1% Density Change**
Given: $d\rho / \rho = 0.01$
From bulk modulus: $dP = K \cdot (d\rho / \rho)$
$$dP = 2.5 \times 10^9 \times 0.01 = 2.5 \times 10^7 \text{ N/m}^2 = 25 \text{ MPa}$$

> **Final Answers:** $c = 1581.14$ m/s, $dP = 25$ MPa **(7 Marks complete)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\gamma$ in speed of sound formula** — Students often write $c = \sqrt{RT}$ instead of $c = \sqrt{\gamma RT}$, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using $P$ as gauge pressure** — In compressible flow, **always use absolute pressure** (Pa, not bar gauge).
> 3. **Confusing isothermal vs. isentropic** — Speed of sound uses the **isentropic** (adiabatic reversible) relation, NOT isothermal. Using $c = \sqrt{P/\rho}$ (Newton's original error) loses full credit.
> 4. **Wrong sign in Bulk Modulus** — The negative sign is often dropped; it must be retained to keep $K$ positive.
> 5. **Misclassifying Mach number regime** — Always state both the Mach number value AND the regime name in your answer.
> 6. **Mixing SI units** — Pressure in bar with density in kg/m³ without unit conversion is a frequent error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - **Compressibility $\beta$** is the reciprocal of **Bulk Modulus $K$**.
> - **Mach Number $M = V/c$** — fundamental classifier of compressible flow.
> - **Incompressible threshold** — $M \leq 0.3$ AND $\Delta \rho / \rho \leq 5\%$.
> - **Speed of sound (ideal gas)** — $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ (depends on temperature only).
> - **Speed of sound (any fluid)** — $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$.
> - **Continuity (compressible)** — $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ (mass conservation, NOT volume).
> - **Isentropic relation** — $P/\rho^\gamma = \text{const.}$ governs ideal gas compressible flow.
> - **Newton's mistake** — Newton's $c = \sqrt{P/\rho}$ is for isothermal; correct one uses $\gamma$ (Laplace correction).
> - **Standard values to memorize**: Air $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/kg·K; Water $K = 2.1 \times 10^9$ Pa.
> - **Flow regimes**: Subsonic ($M<1$), Sonic ($M=1$), Supersonic ($M>1$), Hypersonic ($M\geq 5$).
> - **Area-Velocity relation in compressible flow**: $(1 - M^2) \cdot dV/V + dA/A = 0$.
> - **Engineering importance**: Nozzle design, jet engines, gas pipelines, hydraulic systems all rely on this classification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Statics: Atmospheric Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fluid Statics: Atmospheric Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Atmospheric Pressure** is defined as the **hydrostatic pressure** exerted by the weight of the entire column of air (the Earth's atmosphere) standing vertically above a unit horizontal area at any given point. It is the force per unit area exerted by the cumulative mass of atmospheric gases — primarily nitrogen (~78%), oxygen (~21%), argon (~0.93%), and trace gases — pulled downward by **gravitational acceleration** $g$.

Mathematically expressed using the hydrostatic relation:

$$P_{atm} = \int_{z}^{\infty} \rho(z') \, g \, dz'$$

At mean sea level, under standard conditions (ISA — International Standard Atmosphere, $T = 288.15\text{ K}$, $g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$):

$$P_{atm} = \mathbf{101{,}325 \text{ Pa}} = \mathbf{101.325 \text{ kPa}} = \mathbf{1.01325 \text{ bar}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Atmospheric Pressure belongs to the sub-topic "Fluid Statics" and is foundational for understanding **absolute vs. gauge pressure**, **manometry**, and **barometric readings** — topics that regularly appear in Part A and Part B exam questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Distinction (Board Exam Critical):**
> - **Atmospheric Pressure ($P_{atm}$):** Real pressure of air at the point.
> - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure ($P_{atm}^{\circ}$):** A defined reference = **101.325 kPa** or **760 mm Hg**.
> - **Barometric Pressure:** Synonym for atmospheric pressure, measured by a **barometer**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine the atmosphere as an **immense invisible ocean of air** surrounding the Earth. Just as a deep-sea diver experiences crushing water pressure from all the ocean water above, every person on Earth is at the bottom of an "air ocean" and is constantly pressed upon by the weight of all the air stacked above their head.

**Real-World Analogy — The Stack of Books:**
- Imagine stacking 100 books on your palm. Each book adds weight, and your palm feels pressure from the *cumulative* weight of all books above.
- Similarly, the atmosphere is a stack of "air layers." The pressure at any altitude equals the weight of all the air layers stacked *above* that point.
- At the **top of Mount Everest** (~8848 m), there is less air above → pressure is only about **33 kPa** (≈ 1/3 of sea-level value).
- At the **Dead Sea shore** (~-430 m), there is more air above → pressure is slightly **higher** than sea-level.

**Why does the pressure decrease as we go up?**
Because as we ascend, there is **less air column remaining above us** to push down. Each successive layer of air has fewer layers above it, so it is compressed less and exerts less pressure on the layer below.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook:** Atmospheric pressure = "Weight of air above you ÷ area." Move up → less air above → less weight → less pressure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Physics Behind Atmospheric Pressure

### 2.1.1 Origin and Existence
Atmospheric pressure exists due to three simultaneous physical conditions:

1. **Earth's gravitational field** — pulls gas molecules toward the surface.
2. **Fluid nature of gases** — air, being a compressible fluid, transmits force in all directions (Pascal's principle).
3. **Finite mass of atmosphere** — the atmosphere is not infinite; it terminates at the **Kármán line (100 km)** where pressure becomes negligible (~ 0.032 Pa).

### 2.1.2 Variation of Atmospheric Pressure with Altitude

Starting from the differential hydrostatic equation (for any static fluid):

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho \, g$$

For an **isothermal ideal gas atmosphere** at temperature $T$:

$$P = \rho \, R_s \, T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = \frac{P}{R_s \, T}$$

Substituting back yields the **barometric / isothermal atmosphere equation**:

$$\boxed{P(z) = P_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{g \, z}{R_s \, T}\right)}$$

For a **real (temperature-varying) atmosphere**, the U.S. Standard Atmosphere Model gives:

$$P = P_0 \left(1 - \frac{L \, z}{T_0}\right)^{\frac{g \, M}{R \, L}}$$

where $L = 0.0065 \text{ K/m}$ (tropospheric lapse rate), $M = 0.02896 \text{ kg/mol}$ (molar mass of air), $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$.

### 2.1.3 Torricelli's Experiment (1643)

Evangelista Torricelli inverted a mercury-filled glass tube (1 m long, closed at one end) into a mercury reservoir. He observed:

- Mercury **did not fully drain** from the tube.
- A stable column of **mercury ≈ 760 mm** remained.
- An **empty space** (vacuum — now called **Torricellian vacuum**) existed above the mercury column.
- The **weight of the 760 mm mercury column** exactly balanced the **atmospheric pressure** pushing down on the reservoir.

This is the foundational principle of the **mercury barometer**.

### 2.1.4 Barometers — Instruments to Measure Atmospheric Pressure

**A. Mercury Barometer (Fortin / Kew Pattern):**
- Reference instrument; highest accuracy.
- Measures atmospheric pressure as a **column height of mercury**.
- Reading affected by **temperature** (mercury density changes), **gravity** ($g$ varies with latitude/altitude), and **capillarity** (small correction for meniscus).
- **Corrected barometric height (for standard $g_0 = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$ and $T_0 = 0\,°\text{C}$):**

$$H_c = H \left[1 - \frac{(\beta - 3\alpha) \, T}{1}\right]$$

where $\alpha$ = linear expansion of glass, $\beta$ = cubical expansion of mercury.

**B. Aneroid Barometer:**
- **No fluid**; uses a sealed **metallic bellows (aneroid capsule)** that expands/contracts with pressure changes.
- Mechanical movement is amplified via levers and displayed on a dial.
- **Advantages:** Portable, robust, used in **aircraft altimeters** and **home weather stations**.

### 2.1.5 Absolute vs. Gauge vs. Vacuum Pressure

This is the **single most-tested concept** in KTU fluid statics problems:

$$\boxed{P_{absolute} = P_{gauge} + P_{atmospheric}}$$

$$\boxed{P_{vacuum} = P_{atmospheric} - P_{absolute}}$$

- **Gauge Pressure ($P_{g}$):** Measured *relative* to local atmospheric pressure. **Zero** at atmospheric level.
- **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$):** Measured *relative* to a **perfect vacuum** (true zero). **Always positive.**
- **Vacuum Pressure ($P_{v}$):** How much *below* atmospheric the pressure is. **Positive** when below atmospheric.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Rule:** Whenever you use the ideal gas law $PV = nRT$ or any thermodynamic relation, **always convert to absolute pressure first.** Using gauge pressure is the #1 cause of wrong answers in KTU exams.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | $P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h$ | Hydrostatic balance with mercury column | Mercury barometer height derivation |
| 2 | $h_{Hg} = 760 \text{ mm} \quad \text{at sea level}$ | Standard column height | Quick conversion problems |
| 3 | $P_{atm}^{\circ} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ | Standard atmospheric pressure | All conversion problems |
| 4 | $1 \text{ atm} = 1.01325 \text{ bar} = 1013.25 \text{ mbar}$ | Pressure unit equivalences | Unit conversion |
| 5 | $1 \text{ atm} = 10.33 \text{ m of water}$ | Equivalent water column height | Manometer problems |
| 6 | $1 \text{ atm} = 14.696 \text{ psi}$ | Pounds per square inch | Engineering unit conversions |
| 7 | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Pressure reference conversion | **Most-used formula** |
| 8 | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | Vacuum pressure definition | Sub-atmospheric problems |
| 9 | $P(z) = P_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{g z}{R_s T}\right)$ | Isothermal atmosphere | Altitude problems |
| 10 | $\rho_{Hg} = 13.6 \times 10^{3} \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Density of mercury | Barometer column problems |
| 11 | $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Density of water | Equivalent column problems |
| 12 | $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Sea-level air density (ISA) | Atmospheric column weight |
| 13 | $R_s = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ | Specific gas constant for air | Isothermal atmosphere |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Trick for Board Exams:**
> Equivalent height in **any fluid** = $\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho_{fluid} \cdot g}$
> Example: $h_{water} = \dfrac{101325}{1000 \times 9.81} \approx 10.33 \text{ m}$

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Atmospheric pressure underpins several critical engineering systems:

1. **Aircraft Altimeters** — Encode pressure altitude; pilots set $QNH$ for sea-level reference.
2. **Weather Forecasting** — Isobars (lines of constant atmospheric pressure) on weather maps reveal high/low pressure systems, fronts, and cyclones.
3. **Siphon Operation** — Works only when atmospheric pressure can push liquid up the shorter leg (works up to ~10.3 m for water).
4. **Bunsen Burner / Carburetor / Paint Sprayer** — Use the Bernoulli-effect drop in velocity to create sub-atmospheric pressure that draws fluid in.
5. **Bourdon Tube Pressure Gauges** — Calibrated to read zero against atmospheric pressure, hence they display **gauge** pressure.
6. **Vacuum Chambers & Vacuum Packaging** — Pressure below atmospheric enables preservation, semiconductor manufacturing, and space simulation.
7. **Respiration Physiology** — Human lungs use atmospheric pressure differential for breathing; pressure decreases with altitude cause hypoxia.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure Equation for the Atmosphere

**Goal:** Show that the atmospheric pressure at any elevation $z$ depends on the cumulative weight of air above that point.

**Step 1 — Consider an elemental horizontal slab of air.**
Take an infinitesimal horizontal slab of air at elevation $z$, of thickness $dz$ and unit cross-sectional area $A = 1 \text{ m}^2$.

**Step 2 — Compute the mass of the slab.**
Mass of the slab = (density) × (volume) = $\rho \cdot A \cdot dz$. Since $A = 1$, mass = $\rho \, dz$.

**Step 3 — Compute the weight of the slab (the downward force it exerts).**
Weight $W = m \cdot g = \rho \, g \, dz$. This is the force the slab exerts on the air immediately below it.

**Step 4 — Apply the principle of static equilibrium.**
In a static fluid, the upward pressure force from below must equal the downward pressure force from above plus the slab's weight. For unit area:

$$\begin{aligned}
P(z) \cdot A &= P(z + dz) \cdot A + \rho \, g \, dz \cdot A \\
P(z) &= P(z + dz) + \rho \, g \, dz \\
P(z + dz) - P(z) &= -\rho \, g \, dz \\
\frac{dP}{dz} &= -\rho \, g
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **fundamental hydrostatic equation**, valid for *any* static fluid — liquid or gas.

**Step 5 — Integrate vertically from ground level ($z = 0$, $P = P_0$) to elevation $z$.**

$$P(z) - P_0 = -\int_{0}^{z} \rho(z') \, g \, dz'$$

$$\boxed{P(z) = P_0 - \int_{0}^{z} \rho(z') \, g \, dz'}$$

This rigorously shows that **pressure decreases with altitude** because the integral is positive when integrated upward.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Isothermal Barometric (Atmospheric) Equation

**Goal:** Derive the closed-form expression for $P(z)$ assuming isothermal conditions.

**Step 1 — Start with the hydrostatic equation.**
Use $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$ as derived above.

**Step 2 — Substitute ideal gas law for air.**
Treat air as an ideal gas: $P = \rho R_s T$, hence $\rho = \dfrac{P}{R_s T}$.

**Step 3 — Insert $\rho$ into the differential equation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dP}{dz} &= -\left(\frac{P}{R_s T}\right) g \\
\frac{dP}{P} &= -\frac{g}{R_s T} \, dz
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Assume isothermal atmosphere ($T$ = constant).**

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R_s T} \, dz$$

**Step 5 — Integrate both sides from $(P_0, z = 0)$ to $(P, z)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{P_0}^{P} \frac{dP'}{P'} &= -\frac{g}{R_s T} \int_{0}^{z} dz' \\
\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) &= -\frac{g z}{R_s T} \\
\frac{P}{P_0} &= \exp\!\left(-\frac{g z}{R_s T}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Final boxed result.**

$$\boxed{P(z) = P_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{g \, z}{R_s \, T}\right)}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical sanity check at sea-level conditions** ($P_0 = 101325$ Pa, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $R_s = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$, $T = 288.15 \text{ K}$):

$$\frac{g}{R_s T} = \frac{9.81}{287 \times 288.15} \approx 1.185 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

This gives a "scale height" $H_s = 1/(1.185 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 8434$ m — meaning pressure drops by a factor of $e \approx 2.718$ every ~8.4 km.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Torricelli's Mercury Barometer Column

**Goal:** Show that the height of mercury column in a barometer equals $760 \text{ mm}$ under standard conditions.

**Step 1 — Free-body description.**
Consider the mercury reservoir open to atmosphere. Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$ pushes down on the mercury surface. Inside the inverted tube, the closed top has a vacuum ($P = 0$). The mercury column rises to height $h$.

**Step 2 — Apply hydrostatic balance at the level of the reservoir surface.**
At the level of the free mercury surface in the reservoir (where $P = P_{atm}$), the pressure inside the tube at the same horizontal level must also equal $P_{atm}$.

$$P_{atm} = P_{top} + \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $P_{top} = 0$ (vacuum).**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{atm} &= 0 + \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h \\
P_{atm} &= \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the column height $h$.**

$$\boxed{h = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho_{Hg} \, g}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation under standard conditions.**

$$h = \frac{101325 \text{ Pa}}{13600 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

$$h = \frac{101325}{133368.4} \approx 0.7599 \text{ m} \approx 760 \text{ mm}$$

Hence, **standard atmospheric pressure supports a 760 mm column of mercury** — Torricelli's result, experimentally confirmed.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Equivalent Water Column Height

**Goal:** Show that 1 atm corresponds to ~10.33 m of water column.

**Step 1 — Use hydrostatic balance with water as the working fluid.**

$$P_{atm} = \rho_{water} \, g \, h_{water}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $h_{water}$.**

$$h_{water} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho_{water} \, g} = \frac{101325}{1000 \times 9.80665}$$

$$\boxed{h_{water} \approx 10.33 \text{ m}}$$

**Engineering Implication:** A **suction pump** can lift water only up to ~10.33 m at sea level because atmospheric pressure can push water up only this much. This is why deep mines use multi-stage pumps rather than suction alone.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Pressure Regimes & Formulas Summary

| Pressure Quantity | Reference Datum | Always Positive? | KTU Use |
|-------------------|------------------|------------------|---------|
| Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$) | Perfect Vacuum | **Yes** | Thermodynamics, Gas laws |
| Atmospheric Pressure ($P_{atm}$) | Perfect Vacuum | **Yes** | Barometer problems |
| Gauge Pressure ($P_{g}$) | Local $P_{atm}$ | **No** (can be negative) | Most engineering measurements |
| Vacuum Pressure ($P_{v}$) | Local $P_{atm}$ | **No** (only meaningful below $P_{atm}$) | Sub-atmospheric systems |

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{abs} &= P_{atm} + P_{gauge} \\
P_{abs} &= P_{atm} - P_{vacuum} \\
P_{vacuum} + P_{gauge} &= 0 \quad \text{(at same point)} \\
P_{abs} &\geq 0 \quad \text{(physical constraint)}
\end{aligned}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q: Define Atmospheric Pressure. State its standard value at mean sea level and express it in (i) Pascal, (ii) mm of Hg, and (iii) m of water.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Atmospheric Pressure is the hydrostatic pressure exerted per unit area by the weight of the entire vertical column of air above the point of measurement, due to the Earth's gravitational attraction on atmospheric gases.

> **Standard value at mean sea level:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$

**Conversion:**

$$P_{atm} = \mathbf{101325 \text{ Pa}}$$

Using $P = \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h$:

$$h_{Hg} = \frac{101325}{13600 \times 9.81} = \mathbf{760 \text{ mm of Hg}}$$

Using $P = \rho_{water} \, g \, h_{water}$:

$$h_{water} = \frac{101325}{1000 \times 9.81} = \mathbf{10.33 \text{ m of water}}$$

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Series 1]

**Q: Differentiate clearly between Absolute Pressure, Gauge Pressure, and Vacuum Pressure. Show their inter-relationship on a single diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$):** Pressure measured above a **perfect vacuum** (true zero). Always **positive**. Used in thermodynamics.
>
> **Gauge Pressure ($P_{g}$):** Pressure measured **above local atmospheric pressure**. Can be positive, zero, or negative. Most engineering gauges read this.
>
> **Vacuum Pressure ($P_{v}$):** Magnitude of pressure **below atmospheric**. Always **positive** in definition.

**Relationships:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{abs} &= P_{atm} + P_{gauge} \\
P_{abs} &= P_{atm} - P_{vacuum} \\
P_{gauge} &= -P_{vacuum}
\end{aligned}$$

**Reference Line Diagram:**

```
[Vacuum (0 Pa)]--------------------------------
                    |        |
                    |        |--- P_abs (always ≥ 0)
                    |--- P_atm (101.325 kPa)-------
                              |        |
                              |--- P_g (positive above atm)
                              |--- P_v (positive below atm)
```

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q(a)** [7 Marks]: Derive the hydrostatic equation $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$ for a static fluid. Hence, derive the **isothermal barometric equation** for the atmosphere and show that pressure decreases exponentially with altitude.

**Q(b)** [7 Marks]: At sea level, atmospheric pressure is 760 mm Hg. Compute the equivalent pressure in Pa and the equivalent height of (i) water column and (ii) glycerin column ($\rho_{glycerin} = 1260 \text{ kg/m}^3$). Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

---

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Hydrostatic Equation & Isothermal Atmosphere Derivation

**Step 1: Free-body diagram of an elemental fluid slab.**
Consider an infinitesimal horizontal slab of fluid at elevation $z$, of thickness $dz$, unit cross-section $A = 1 \text{ m}^2$, and density $\rho$.

**Step 2: Force balance (Newton's Second Law for static fluid = zero net force).**

The slab experiences:
- Upward force from below: $P(z) \cdot A$
- Downward force from above: $P(z + dz) \cdot A$
- Downward body force (weight): $\rho \, g \, A \, dz$

For static equilibrium, sum of forces = 0:

$$P(z) \cdot A - P(z + dz) \cdot A - \rho \, g \, A \, dz = 0$$

$$P(z) - P(z + dz) = \rho \, g \, dz$$

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho \, g \quad \text{(Hydrostatic Equation)} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 3: Apply ideal gas law for air.**
For an ideal gas: $P = \rho R_s T$. Therefore: $\rho = \dfrac{P}{R_s T}$.

**Step 4: Substitute into hydrostatic equation.**

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\frac{P \, g}{R_s T}$$

**Step 5: Separate variables.**

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R_s T} \, dz$$

**Step 6: Assume isothermal ($T$ = constant) and integrate from $(P_0, 0)$ to $(P, z)$.**

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} \frac{dP'}{P'} = -\frac{g}{R_s T} \int_0^z dz'$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{g z}{R_s T}$$

$$\boxed{P(z) = P_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{g z}{R_s T}\right)}$$

This shows **exponential decay** of pressure with altitude. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Equivalents

**Given:** $h_{Hg} = 760 \text{ mm} = 0.760 \text{ m}$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{glycerin} = 1260 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Step 1: Convert to Pascals.**

$$P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h_{Hg}$$

$$P_{atm} = 13600 \times 9.81 \times 0.760$$

$$P_{atm} = 101396.16 \text{ Pa} \approx \mathbf{101.4 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Step 2: Equivalent height of water column.**

$$h_{water} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho_{water} \, g} = \frac{101396.16}{1000 \times 9.81}$$

$$\boxed{h_{water} \approx 10.336 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3: Equivalent height of glycerin column.**

$$h_{glycerin} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho_{glycerin} \, g} = \frac{101396.16}{1260 \times 9.81}$$

$$\boxed{h_{glycerin} \approx 8.203 \text{ m}}$$

**Verification logic:** Since glycerin is denser than water, the equivalent column height is smaller — confirming our result.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q(a)** [7 Marks]: With the aid of a neat sketch, describe **Torricelli's mercury barometer experiment**. Derive the expression for the height of the mercury column in terms of atmospheric pressure.

**Q(b)** [7 Marks]: An aircraft cruising at an altitude of 5000 m experiences an ambient atmospheric pressure of 54.0 kPa (absolute). If the cabin is pressurized to a gauge pressure of 75 kPa, find (i) the cabin **absolute pressure** in kPa, (ii) the cabin pressure in **mm of Hg**, and (iii) the **pressure difference** between cabin interior and outside atmosphere in kPa.

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Torricelli's Mercury Barometer

**Sketch Description:**

```
Atmosphere --> [P_atm pushes down]
                |
                |          ___
                |          |  |   Torricellian Vacuum
                |          |  |   (P ≈ 0)
                |          |  |
                |  Mercury |  | h mm
                |  Column  |  |
                |          |__|
                |          |   \
                |          |    \  ← Glass tube (closed at top)
                |          |     \
                |          |      \
                |          |       |
                ===========|=======|======
                Mercury Reservoir (open to atmosphere)
                ===P_atm pushes mercury surface down===
```

**Working Principle:**

A glass tube (~1 m long) is filled completely with mercury. The open end is closed with a finger, the tube inverted, and the open end submerged in a mercury reservoir. The finger is then removed.

Mercury in the tube falls slightly and stabilizes at a column height $h$, leaving a **vacuum** at the closed top (Torricellian vacuum).

**Derivation:**

At the level of the mercury surface in the reservoir, pressure inside the tube must equal $P_{atm}$ (static fluid principle — pressure same at same horizontal level in a connected static fluid).

Pressure at top of mercury column in tube = 0 (vacuum).

Pressure at base of column (at reservoir level) = $\rho_{Hg} \, g \, h + 0 = \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h$.

Setting this equal to $P_{atm}$:

$$\boxed{P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h \quad \Rightarrow \quad h = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho_{Hg} \, g}}$$

For standard conditions:

$$h = \frac{101325}{13600 \times 9.80665} \approx 0.760 \text{ m} = 760 \text{ mm Hg} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Engineering Note:** The vacuum at the top is not perfectly empty; it contains saturated mercury vapor at ~0.16 Pa at 20°C — negligible for most practical barometric readings.

---

#### Part (b) — Aircraft Cabin Pressure Calculation

**Given:**
- Outside ambient pressure $P_{outside} = 54.0 \text{ kPa}$ (absolute)
- Cabin gauge pressure $P_{g,cabin} = 75 \text{ kPa}$
- Standard sea-level $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$

**Step 1: Cabin Absolute Pressure.**

$$P_{abs,cabin} = P_{atm} + P_{g,cabin}$$

$$P_{abs,cabin} = 101.325 + 75 = \mathbf{176.325 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Step 2: Cabin Pressure in mm of Hg.**

$$h_{Hg,cabin} = \frac{P_{abs,cabin}}{\rho_{Hg} \, g} = \frac{176325 \text{ Pa}}{13600 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_{Hg,cabin} = \frac{176325}{133416} \approx 1.3217 \text{ m Hg}$$

$$h_{Hg,cabin} \approx \mathbf{1321.7 \text{ mm Hg}}$$

**Step 3: Pressure Difference between Cabin and Outside.**

$$\Delta P = P_{abs,cabin} - P_{outside}$$

$$\Delta P = 176.325 - 54.0 = \mathbf{122.325 \text{ kPa}}$$
