This corresponds to a pressure differential of **1.223 bar** — high enough to stress the fuselage, which is why aircraft fuselages are designed as **pressure vessels** with **bleed valves** to maintain a safe differential (~ 75 kPa gauge cabin pressure is standard).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert gauge → absolute:** When a problem gives "gauge pressure = 75 kPa" and asks for absolute pressure, students often write 75 kPa as the answer. Always add $P_{atm}$.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency in $P = \rho g h$:** Mixing cm and m — must convert **all lengths to meters** before plugging in.
> 3. **Using $\rho_{water} = 1000$ instead of $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³** in mercury barometer problems.
> 4. **Confusing $P_{vacuum}$ with $P_{absolute}$:** They are *different* concepts. $P_{vac}$ = how far below $P_{atm}$ you are.
> 5. **Wrong reference for absolute pressure:** Some students think absolute = gauge. The reference is the **vacuum**, not the atmosphere.
> 6. **Skipping $g$ in barometric calculations:** Forgetting the $g$ factor loses full marks — examiners expect the dimensional consistency.
> 7. **Not stating standard values explicitly:** In exam answers, always write "$P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa" or "$h_{Hg} = 760$ mm" before plugging in.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Atmospheric Pressure**

**Core Definitions**
- ☐ Atmospheric Pressure = weight of air column above unit area.
- ☐ Standard $P_{atm}$ = **101.325 kPa = 760 mm Hg = 10.33 m water = 1.01325 bar = 14.696 psi**.
- ☐ Torricellian vacuum = near-perfect vacuum above mercury column in barometer.

**Key Equations (Must Memorize)**
- ☐ Hydrostatic: $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$.
- ☐ Mercury column: $P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h$.
- ☐ Isothermal atmosphere: $P(z) = P_0 \, \exp(-gz / R_s T)$.
- ☐ Absolute ↔ Gauge: $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$.
- ☐ Absolute ↔ Vacuum: $P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vacuum}$.
- ☐ Equivalent fluid column: $h_f = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho_f \, g}$.

**Physical Constants to Memorize**
- ☐ $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{air,sea-level} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- ☐ $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (use $9.80665$ if specified).
- ☐ $R_s = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ for air.
- ☐ Atmospheric scale height ≈ 8.4 km.

**Engineering Applications (Mention in Theory Answers)**
- ☐ Mercury barometer — most accurate; reference instrument.
- ☐ Aneroid barometer — portable; used in altimeters.
- ☐ Bourdon gauge — reads gauge pressure.
- ☐ Altimeter — encodes atmospheric pressure to indicate altitude.
- ☐ Siphon / suction pump — limited to ~10.33 m water lift.

**Most Frequently Asked Concepts**
- ☐ Derivation of hydrostatic equation.
- ☐ Torricelli's experiment explanation.
- ☐ Absolute vs. gauge vs. vacuum pressure conversion.
- ☐ Equivalent column height in different fluids.
- ☐ Numerical pressure conversion problems.

**One-Line Mnemonic**
> **"AGV — Always Gauge Vacuum-paired; Absolute = Gauge + Vacuum-free."**
> (i.e., $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge} = P_{atm} - P_{vacuum}$)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gauge Pressure and Absolute Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Gauge Pressure and Absolute Pressure # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In fluid statics and dynamics, **pressure** is defined as the normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area on a surface. However, the numerical value of pressure depends critically on the **reference datum (zero point)** chosen for measurement. This gives rise to two fundamentally distinct pressure scales used universally in engineering practice: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Absolute Pressure (P_abs)** is the total pressure measured relative to a **perfect vacuum** (absolute zero pressure). It is always a **positive** quantity and represents the *true* thermodynamic pressure of the fluid. > > $$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gauge Pressure (P_gauge)** is the pressure measured relative to the **local atmospheric pressure** as the datum. It may be **positive** (pressure above atmospheric) or **negative** (pressure below atmospheric, also termed *vacuum pressure*). > > $$P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Vacuum Pressure (P_vac)** is the magnitude by which absolute pressure falls *below* atmospheric pressure. It is always a positive number, expressed as: > $$P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$$ ## 1.2 Real-World Conceptual Analogy Imagine you are standing inside a swimming pool. The water pushes on your eardrums. Now think about how we describe this push: - **Absolute Pressure Analogy** 🏊‍♂️: Think of pressure like **elevation measured from sea level**. Sea level is zero, and everything is referenced from there. The *total* depth of water above you (water pressure + atmospheric air column above the pool) is like absolute pressure — it counts *everything* from a true zero (vacuum in space). - **Gauge Pressure Analogy** 🌡️: Think of gauge pressure like **temperature measured in Celsius**. The reference is not absolute zero but a convenient everyday reference (the atmosphere). Just as +30°C means 30 degrees above the freezing point of water, a gauge pressure of +30 kPa means 30 kPa above atmospheric pressure. - **Vacuum Pressure Analogy** 🏔️: When you climb a mountain, atmospheric pressure drops. The "shortfall" from standard atmospheric pressure is analogous to vacuum pressure — how much the local pressure *lacks* compared to atmospheric. > [!TIP] > **Engineer's Quick Mental Check**: A standard tire pressure gauge reads **zero** when the tire is flat and you disconnect it — this is because it reads **gauge pressure**, and the inside of the flat tire is at atmospheric pressure (same as outside). ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Reference Values The following standard reference values are **critical for KTU numerical problems**: | Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value | |----------|--------|----------------| | Standard Atmospheric Pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** | | Standard Atmospheric Pressure (bar) | $P_{atm}$ | **1.01325 bar** | | Standard Atmospheric Pressure (SI) | $P_{atm}$ | **101,325 N/m²** | | Mercury column height | $h_{Hg}$ | **760 mm of Hg** | | Water column height | $h_{w}$ | **10.33 m of water** | | Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | | Density of mercury | $\rho_{Hg}$ | **13,600 kg/m³** | | Density of water | $\rho_{w}$ | **1000 kg/m³** | # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Two-Reference System The Earth's atmosphere exerts pressure on every surface exposed to it. This atmospheric pressure **varies** with altitude, weather, and temperature. Therefore, an instrument that measures pressure using atmospheric pressure as its zero point will read differently at the beach versus at hill stations. ### 🔹 Logic Step 1: Why Two References? - **Thermodynamic & Scientific calculations** (gas laws, compressible flow, vapor pressure) **require absolute values** because zero must represent a true absence of molecular motion (vacuum). - **Industrial & field measurements** use gauge pressure because most process equipment (pipes, tanks, boilers) operates in an environment already at atmospheric pressure. Engineers care about the *difference* between inside and outside pressures — that is what stresses the vessel walls. ### 🔹 Logic Step 2: The Sign Convention | Condition | Physical Meaning | Mathematical Relation | |-----------|------------------|----------------------| | $P_{abs} > P_{atm}$ | Pressure **above** atmospheric (compressed gas, hydraulic line) | $P_{gauge} = +ve$ | | $P_{abs} = P_{atm}$ | Pressure **equal** to atmospheric | $P_{gauge} = 0$ | | $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$ | Pressure **below** atmospheric (vacuum, suction line) | $P_{gauge} = -ve$ | | $P_{abs} = 0$ | Perfect vacuum (theoretically) | $P_{gauge} = -P_{atm}$ | ### 🔹 Logic Step 3: Practical Instruments & Their Readings | Instrument | Type of Reading | Typical Use | |------------|----------------|-------------| | **Bourdon Gauge** | Gauge Pressure | Boiler pressure, tire pressure | | **Manometer (U-tube)** | Gauge Pressure (differential) | Laboratory, calibration | | **Barometer** | Atmospheric Pressure | Weather forecasting, altimeters | | **Dead-weight Tester** | Absolute or Gauge (calibrated) | Primary standard for calibration | | **Piezoelectric Transducer** | Absolute Pressure | Engine cylinder pressure, aerospace | | **Aneroid Barometer** | Atmospheric (Absolute) | Altimetry in aircraft | ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates every formula you must memorize for the KTU University Examination on this topic. **Memorize all entries in bold without fail.** | # | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---------|-------------|------| | 1 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | Absolute from gauge | Pa, kPa, bar | | 2 | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ | Gauge from absolute | Pa, kPa, bar | | 3 | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | Vacuum pressure (positive) | Pa, kPa, bar | | 4 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$ | Absolute from vacuum | Pa, kPa, bar | | 5 | $P_{gauge} = -P_{vac}$ | Gauge equals negative of vacuum | Pa, kPa, bar | | 6 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure from column | Pa | | 7 | $P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg}$ | Atmospheric pressure via barometer | Pa | | 8 | $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ | Standard conversion | — | | 9 | $1 \text{ bar} = 100 \text{ kPa}$ | Engineering conversion | — | | 10 | $1 \text{ psi} = 6.895 \text{ kPa}$ | Imperial conversion | — | ### Critical Numerical Conversion Chain $$ \begin{aligned} 1 \text{ atm} &= 101.325 \text{ kPa} \\ &= 1.01325 \text{ bar} \\ &= 14.696 \text{ psi} \\ &= 760 \text{ mm Hg} \\ &= 10.33 \text{ m of water} \\ &= 1.01325 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Understanding the gauge-vs-absolute distinction is not merely academic — it directly impacts: 1. **Aerospace Engineering**: Altimeters in aircraft use **absolute pressure** (since atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude, this directly correlates to height). A pilot reading 30.00 inches Hg is at approximately sea level. 2. **HVAC & Refrigeration**: Suction-side refrigerant pressures are **below atmospheric**, so they are read as *vacuum* (or negative gauge). Misreading this as positive gauge pressure can damage compressors. 3. **Hydraulic & Pneumatic Systems**: Hydraulic presses are typically rated by **gauge pressure** because the system operates in atmospheric air. A 10 MPa hydraulic system means the fluid is 10 MPa above atmospheric, not 10 MPa absolute. 4. **Medical Devices**: Blood pressure is universally measured as **gauge pressure** (mmHg) — sphygmomanometers are calibrated against atmospheric pressure. 5. **Boiler Safety**: Steam boilers operate at high gauge pressures. The safety valves are set by gauge pressure, but thermodynamic calculations require absolute pressure. # 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I: Fundamental Relationship Between Absolute, Gauge, and Atmospheric Pressure ### Statement To prove that **$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$** using a control-volume and reference-frame argument. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Define the Reference Frames** Let us define a point P inside a fluid container. Two reference frames exist: - **Reference Frame A (Vacuum Reference)**: A perfect vacuum has $P = 0$. All molecular motion ceases (theoretical). - **Reference Frame B (Atmospheric Reference)**: Local atmospheric pressure is set to $P = 0$ (gauge zero). **Step 2: Express Pressure in Each Frame** In **Reference Frame A** (absolute), the pressure at point P is some unknown total value $P_{abs}$. In **Reference Frame B** (gauge), the same physical pressure is read as $P_{gauge}$. **Step 3: Quantify the Offset Between Frames** The two reference frames are separated by a constant offset equal to the atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$. This is because atmospheric pressure is itself a real physical pressure acting on all exposed surfaces, and it must be added to the gauge reading to recover the true absolute value. **Step 4: Establish the Coordinate Transformation** Since the absolute frame reads from vacuum (zero) and the gauge frame reads from $P_{atm}$, the gauge pressure is the difference: $$ P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm} $$ **Step 5: Solve for Absolute Pressure** Rearranging: $$ P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge} \quad \blacksquare $$ **Step 6: Validate by Boundary Condition Check** | Test Case | $P_{abs}$ | $P_{atm}$ | Predicted $P_{gauge}$ | Physical Interpretation | |-----------|-----------|-----------|----------------------|--------------------------| | Perfect vacuum | 0 | 101.325 kPa | −101.325 kPa | Gauge reads maximum negative (full vacuum) | | Tire at atmospheric | 101.325 kPa | 101.325 kPa | 0 | Gauge reads zero ✓ | | Tire pumped to 200 kPa | 200 kPa | 101.325 kPa | +98.675 kPa | Gauge reads positive inflation ✓ | | Suction line | 50 kPa | 101.325 kPa | −51.325 kPa | Gauge reads negative; vacuum = +51.325 kPa ✓ | --- ## 3.2 Derivation II: Atmospheric Pressure from Barometer Column Height ### Statement Derive the standard atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg}$ and verify it equals **101.325 kPa**. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Set Up the Physical System** A mercury barometer consists of an evacuated glass tube (closed at top, open at bottom) inverted into a mercury reservoir exposed to atmosphere. Atmospheric pressure pushes mercury up the tube to a height $h_{Hg}$ where the mercury column's weight balances the atmospheric push. **Step 2: Apply Hydrostatic Equilibrium** At the level of the mercury reservoir surface (just outside the tube), the pressure must be equal on both sides of the fluid (Pascal's principle): - **Outside the tube** (in the open reservoir): $P = P_{atm}$ - **Inside the tube** (at the same horizontal level): $P = P_{vacuum} + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg}$ **Step 3: Substitute Vacuum Pressure** The space above mercury inside the tube is a near-perfect vacuum, so $P_{vacuum} \approx 0$: $$ P_{inside} = 0 + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg} $$ **Step 4: Equate and Solve** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{atm} &= \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg} \\ &= 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \times 0.760 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Compute Numerically** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{atm} &= 13{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.760 \\ &= 13{,}600 \times 7.4556 \\ &= 101{,}396.16 \text{ N/m}^2 \\ &\approx 101.325 \text{ kPa} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ (The slight discrepancy is due to rounding of $g = 9.81$ m/s² instead of the standard $g = 9.80665$ m/s².) --- ## 3.3 Derivation III: Conversion Between Pressure Units ### Statement Convert a pressure of **250 kPa (absolute)** to (a) bar, (b) psi, (c) m of water column, (d) mm of Hg. ### Solution **Step (a): kPa to bar** $$ P = 250 \text{ kPa} \times \frac{1 \text{ bar}}{100 \text{ kPa}} = 2.5 \text{ bar} $$ **Step (b): kPa to psi** $$ P = 250 \text{ kPa} \times \frac{1 \text{ psi}}{6.895 \text{ kPa}} = 36.26 \text{ psi} $$ **Step (c): kPa to m of water** Using $P = \rho_w g h$: $$ h_w = \frac{P}{\rho_w g} = \frac{250{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81} = 25.48 \text{ m of water} $$ **Step (d): kPa to mm of Hg** $$ h_{Hg} = \frac{P}{\rho_{Hg} g} = \frac{250{,}000}{13{,}600 \times 9.81} = 1.874 \text{ m} = 1874 \text{ mm of Hg} $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix This is a decision-flow guide for solving KTU numerical problems on this topic. Memorize this algorithm. > [!NOTE] > **MASTER ALGORITHM FOR GAUGE/ABSOLUTE PROBLEMS** > > **STEP 1 → IDENTIFY** the type of pressure given (absolute or gauge) from the problem statement keywords. > > **STEP 2 → CONVERT** all pressures to a **single consistent unit** (preferably kPa or Pa) before any arithmetic. > > **STEP 3 → APPLY** the governing relation based on the unknown: > - To find $P_{abs}$ → use $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ > - To find $P_{gauge}$ → use $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ > - To find $P_{vac}$ → use $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ (only when $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$) > > **STEP 4 → REPORT** the final answer with proper units and sign convention (positive for gauge above atm, negative for vacuum). ### Worked Example (For Internalization) **Problem**: A hydraulic press operates at a gauge pressure of 8.5 MPa. The local barometric reading is 740 mm Hg. Find the absolute pressure in (a) MPa, (b) kPa, (c) bar. **Solution using the algorithm**: **Step 1**: Given $P_{gauge} = 8.5$ MPa = 8500 kPa. Barometer reads 740 mm Hg, which is *less than* standard 760 mm Hg, so local $P_{atm}$ is proportionally lower. **Step 2**: Convert barometer to kPa: $$ P_{atm,local} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.740 = 98{,}717 \text{ Pa} = 98.717 \text{ kPa} $$ **Step 3**: Apply $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$: $$ P_{abs} = 98.717 + 8500 = 8598.717 \text{ kPa} \approx 8.599 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 4 (c)**: Convert to bar: $$ P_{abs} = 8598.717 \text{ kPa} \times \frac{1 \text{ bar}}{100 \text{ kPa}} = 85.987 \text{ bar} \approx 86.0 \text{ bar} $$ # 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Pressure Scale Block Architecture (Mermaid) The following diagram illustrates the **complete pressure reference hierarchy** and the relationship between vacuum, atmospheric, gauge, and absolute pressure domains. ```mermaid flowchart TB A["PERFECT VACUUM ZONE
P_abs equals 0
P_gauge equals minus P_atm"] B["VACUUM / SUCTION ZONE
0 less than P_abs less than P_atm
P_gauge is negative
P_vac is positive"] C["ATMOSPHERIC REFERENCE LINE
P_abs equals P_atm
P_gauge equals 0
P_vac equals 0"] D["GAUGE POSITIVE ZONE
P_abs greater than P_atm
P_gauge is positive
P_vac is undefined"] E["STANDARD ATMOSPHERIC VALUE
P_atm equals 101.325 kPa"] F["TOOLS USED
Dead Weight Tester
Vacuum Gauge
Barometer"] G["TOOLS USED
Bourdon Gauge
Manometer
Pressure Transducer"] A --> B B --> C C --> D E -.->|defines zero of gauge| C F -.-> B G -.-> D ``` ## 4.2 Pressure Type Comparison Flowchart (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Pressure Reading
Obtained from Instrument"] Q1{"Does instrument
reference vacuum?"} Q2{"Does instrument
reference atmosphere?"} P2["ABSOLUTE PRESSURE
P_abs"] P3["GAUGE PRESSURE
P_gauge"] P4["VACUUM PRESSURE
P_vac"] P5["CALCULATE
P_abs equals P_atm plus P_gauge"] P6["CALCULATE
P_abs equals P_atm minus P_vac"] P1 --> Q1 Q1 -->|YES| P2 Q1 -->|NO| Q2 Q2 -->|YES and greater than atm| P3 Q2 -->|YES and less than atm| P4 P3 --> P5 P4 --> P6 ``` ## 4.3 Measurement Instrument Schematic Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB M1["MEASUREMENT INSTRUMENTS"] N1["Bourdon Gauge
Reads Gauge Pressure
Range: minus 1 to plus 700 bar"] N2["Diaphragm Gauge
Reads Gauge or Differential
Range: 0 to 100 bar"] N3["Piezoelectric Sensor
Reads Absolute or Gauge
High Frequency Response"] N4["Dead Weight Tester
Primary Calibration Standard
Reads Absolute"] N5["U-tube Manometer
Reads Differential or Gauge
Reference: atmospheric"] N6["Barometer
Reads Atmospheric Pressure
Used as Reference Datum"] M1 --> N1 M1 --> N2 M1 --> N3 M1 --> N4 M1 --> N5 M1 --> N6 ``` ## 4.4 Manometer Concept Schematic (Mermaid) A U-tube manometer is the most common instrument for measuring gauge pressure in laboratory settings. ```mermaid flowchart TB S1["Pressure Vessel
P_unknown"] S2["U-tube Manometer"] S3["Mercury Column
Density rho_Hg"] S4["Difference in Heights
h_left minus h_right"] S5["Atmospheric Opening
P_atm"] S6["Apply Hydrostatic Equilibrium
P_unknown plus rho_1 g h_1
equals P_atm plus rho_Hg g h"] S7["Solve for P_unknown
P_unknown equals P_atm plus rho_Hg g h"] S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S5 --> S2 S2 --> S6 S6 --> S7 ``` # 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define absolute pressure, gauge pressure, and vacuum pressure. Establish the relation between them with a neat sketch.** **Model Answer (3 Marks)**: **Definition 1 (1 mark)**: **Absolute pressure** is the pressure measured with reference to a **perfect vacuum** (absolute zero pressure) as datum. It is always positive and represents the true pressure of the fluid. **Definition 2 (1 mark)**: **Gauge pressure** is the pressure measured with reference to **local atmospheric pressure** as datum. It may be positive (above atmospheric) or negative (below atmospheric). **Definition 3 (0.5 mark)**: **Vacuum pressure** is the pressure below atmospheric pressure, measured as the deficit from atmospheric. It is always expressed as a positive value. **Relation (0.5 mark)**: $$ P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs} $$ (Neat sketch showing the three scales — vacuum, atmospheric, and absolute — must accompany.) --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **A pressure gauge reads 50 kPa at a location where the atmospheric pressure is 98 kPa. What is the absolute pressure? If the gauge is relocated to a place where atmospheric pressure is 105 kPa, what will it read?** **Model Answer (3 Marks)**: **Given**: $P_{gauge} = 50$ kPa, $P_{atm,1} = 98$ kPa **Step 1 (1.5 marks)**: Absolute pressure at first location: $$ P_{abs} = P_{atm,1} + P_{gauge} = 98 + 50 = 148 \text{ kPa} $$ **Step 2 (1.5 marks)**: When relocated, the absolute pressure inside the system does not change (closed system, sealed gas). So: $$ P_{abs} = 148 \text{ kPa} \text{ (unchanged)} $$ New gauge reading: $$ P_{gauge,2} = P_{abs} - P_{atm,2} = 148 - 105 = 43 \text{ kPa} $$ **Final Answer**: Absolute pressure = **148 kPa**; New gauge reading = **43 kPa**. --- --- ## 📝 PART B QUESTIONS — 14 Marks (ESE Module Internal Choice) --- ### **🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]` **Question**: A hydraulic cylinder operates with a fluid at a gauge pressure of **12 MPa**. The local atmospheric pressure is **0.97 bar** and the gravitational acceleration is **9.81 m/s²**. **(a)** [7 Marks] Calculate the absolute pressure of the hydraulic fluid in (i) MPa, (ii) kPa, (iii) psi, and (iv) meters of water column. **(b)** [7 Marks] If the system develops a leak and the pressure drops to a vacuum gauge reading of **40 kPa**, determine the new absolute pressure in kPa and verify whether the fluid is in a sub-atmospheric state. --- ### **Model Solution — Question A** #### **Part (a) — Solution** [7 Marks] **Given Data**: - $P_{gauge} = 12$ MPa $= 12{,}000$ kPa - $P_{atm} = 0.97$ bar $= 97$ kPa - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 1 (1 mark): Apply absolute pressure formula** $$ P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge} = 97 + 12{,}000 = 12{,}097 \text{ kPa} $$ **Step 2 (1 mark): Express in MPa** $$ P_{abs} = \frac{12{,}097}{1000} = 12.097 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 3 (1 mark): Express in kPa** $$ P_{abs} = 12{,}097 \text{ kPa} \quad \text{(already computed)} $$ **Step 4 (2 marks): Express in psi** Using conversion $1 \text{ psi} = 6.895 \text{ kPa}$: $$ P_{abs} = \frac{12{,}097}{6.895} = 1754.46 \text{ psi} $$ **Step 5 (2 marks): Express in meters of water column** Using $P = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h_w$: $$ h_w = \frac{P_{abs}}{\rho_w \cdot g} = \frac{12{,}097 \times 1000}{1000 \times 9.81} = \frac{12{,}097{,}000}{9810} $$ $$ h_w = 1233.13 \text{ m of water} $$ **Final Answers for Part (a)**: - (i) $P_{abs} = 12.097$ MPa - (ii) $P_{abs} = 12{,}097$ kPa - (iii) $P_{abs} = 1754.46$ psi - (iv) $h_w = 1233.13$ m of water column ✓ --- #### **Part (b) — Solution** [7 Marks] **Given Data**: - Vacuum gauge reading: $P_{vac} = 40$ kPa - $P_{atm} = 97$ kPa **Step 1 (2 marks): Recall vacuum pressure relation** When a gauge indicates **vacuum**, the absolute pressure is given by: $$ P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac} $$ **Step 2 (2 marks): Substitute and solve** $$ P_{abs} = 97 - 40 = 57 \text{ kPa} $$ **Step 3 (2 marks): Verify sub-atmospheric condition** Compare $P_{abs}$ with $P_{atm}$: $$ P_{abs} = 57 \text{ kPa} < P_{atm} = 97 \text{ kPa} $$ Since $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$, the fluid pressure is indeed **sub-atmospheric**. The pressure deficit (vacuum) is 40 kPa, which matches the gauge reading. ✓ **Step 4 (1 mark): Final verification** Equivalent gauge reading: $$ P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm} = 57 - 97 = -40 \text{ kPa} $$ The negative sign confirms the vacuum state with magnitude 40 kPa, consistent with the given data. ✓ **Final Answer for Part (b)**: $P_{abs} = 57$ kPa; **Yes, the fluid is in a sub-atmospheric (vacuum) state.** ✓ --- ### **🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **Question**: A closed tank contains a gas at an absolute pressure of **275 kPa**. A Bourdon gauge attached to the tank reads the local atmospheric pressure as **752 mm Hg**. **(a)** [7 Marks] Determine (i) the gauge pressure of the gas in kPa, (ii) the gauge pressure in bar, and (iii) whether the gas is at a higher or lower pressure than the atmosphere. **(b)** [7 Marks] If the same gas is transferred to a rigid container at a high-altitude location where the barometric pressure is **600 mm Hg**, what will be the new gauge pressure (in kPa) and new absolute pressure (in bar) of the gas? State clearly any assumption made. --- ### **Model Solution — Question B** #### **Part (a) — Solution** [7 Marks] **Given Data**: - $P_{abs} = 275$ kPa - Barometer reading: $h_{Hg} = 752$ mm $= 0.752$ m **Step 1 (2 marks): Calculate atmospheric pressure in kPa** $$ P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.752 $$ $$ P_{atm} = 100{,}325.5 \text{ Pa} \approx 100.33 \text{ kPa} $$ **Step 2 (2 marks): Calculate gauge pressure in kPa** $$ P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm} = 275 - 100.33 = 174.67 \text{ kPa} $$ **Step 3 (1.5 marks): Convert gauge pressure to bar** $$ P_{gauge} = 174.67 \text{ kPa} \times \frac{1 \text{ bar}}{100 \text{ kPa}} = 1.7467 \text{ bar} $$ **Step 4 (1.5 marks): Identify higher or lower** Since $P_{abs} = 275 \text{ kPa} > P_{atm} = 100.33 \text{ kPa}$, and $P_{gauge} = +174.67$ kPa (positive), the gas is at a **HIGHER** pressure than the atmosphere. ✓ **Final Answers for Part (a)**: - (i) $P_{gauge} = 174.67$ kPa - (ii) $P_{gauge} = 1.7467$ bar - (iii) Gas pressure is **higher** than atmospheric ✓ --- #### **Part (b) — Solution** [7 Marks] **Given Data**: - New barometer reading: $h_{Hg,2} = 600$ mm $= 0.600$ m - **Assumption (1 mark)**: The gas is transferred **isothermally** and the **amount of gas and volume remain constant**, so the absolute pressure of the gas stays at 275 kPa (assuming negligible temperature/altitude effects on the gas itself). **Step 1 (2 marks): Calculate new atmospheric pressure** $$ P_{atm,2} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg,2} = 13{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.600 $$ $$ P_{atm,2} = 80{,}049.6 \text{ Pa} \approx 80.05 \text{ kPa} $$ **Step 2 (2 marks): Calculate new gauge pressure** $$ P_{gauge,2} = P_{abs} - P_{atm,2} = 275 - 80.05 = 194.95 \text{ kPa} $$ **Step 3 (2 marks): Convert absolute pressure to bar** $$ P_{abs} = 275 \text{ kPa} = 2.75 \text{ bar} $$ (Note: Absolute pressure remains **unchanged** at 275 kPa by our assumption.) **Final Answers for Part (b)**: - New gauge pressure: $P_{gauge,2} = 194.95$ kPa - New absolute pressure: $P_{abs} = 2.75$ bar - Observation: At higher altitude, the **gauge reading increases** even though absolute pressure is unchanged — this is because the reference (atmospheric) has dropped. ✓ --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > **🔴 Pitfall 1 (Most Common — 2 to 3 marks lost)**: Students often **add atmospheric pressure twice** when a barometer reading is given. If a problem says "the barometer reads 750 mm Hg," you must FIRST convert that barometer reading to kPa using $\rho g h$, and THEN apply $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$. Do not assume $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa when a specific barometer reading is provided. > > **🔴 Pitfall 2 (1 to 2 marks lost)**: **Forgetting the unit conversion** of $g = 9.81$ m/s². When computing $\rho g h$, all units MUST be SI: $\rho$ in kg/m³, $g$ in m/s², $h$ in meters. Final answer will be in Pa. > > **🔴 Pitfall 3 (1 to 2 marks lost)**: **Confusing vacuum with negative absolute pressure**. Absolute pressure is *never negative* — even a perfect vacuum gives $P_{abs} = 0$, not negative. Vacuum pressure is a *positive* magnitude indicating shortfall from atmospheric. > > **🔴 Pitfall 4 (1 mark lost)**: Failing to **state the sign convention** explicitly in numerical answers. Always write $P_{gauge} = +50$ kPa or $P_{gauge} = -50$ kPa (vacuum) — do not just write the magnitude. > > **🔴 Pitfall 5 (1 mark lost)**: Using $1 \text{ bar} = 101.325$ kPa instead of $1 \text{ bar} = 100$ kPa. Engineering usage defines 1 bar = 100 kPa exactly (the "bar" is a metric but non-SI unit). Use the 100 kPa conversion unless the problem specifically requests otherwise. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Gauge & Absolute Pressure** > > ✅ **1.** Absolute pressure references **vacuum**; Gauge pressure references **atmosphere**; Vacuum pressure references how far below atmosphere the absolute pressure is. > > ✅ **2.** Master formula: $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$. Derive all others from this one equation. > > ✅ **3.** Vacuum pressure formula: $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ — always positive magnitude. > > ✅ **4.** Standard atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa = 1.01325 bar = 760 mm Hg = 10.33 m water. > > ✅ **5.** Atmospheric pressure from barometer: $P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg}$ — must convert mm Hg to meters before calculation. > > ✅ **6.** Memorize conversions: 1 bar = 100 kPa; 1 psi = 6.895 kPa; 1 atm = 101.325 kPa. > > ✅ **7.** Bourdon gauge reads **gauge pressure**; Barometer reads **atmospheric**; Dead-weight tester reads **absolute**. > > ✅ **8.** When a problem gives a barometer reading, **always compute $P_{atm}$ from it** using $\rho g h$ — do NOT use 101.325 kPa unless the barometer reads 760 mm Hg. > > ✅ **9.** Sign convention: $+P_{gauge}$ = above atmospheric; $-P_{gauge}$ = below atmospheric (vacuum state). > > ✅ **10.** Absolute pressure can never be negative (minimum is 0 = vacuum). Gauge pressure ranges from $-P_{atm}$ to theoretically $+\infty$. > > ✅ **11.** At higher altitudes, atmospheric pressure **decreases**, so gauge pressure of a sealed gas **appears to increase** even though absolute pressure is unchanged. > > ✅ **12.** Tire gauges, hydraulic systems, and boilers use **gauge pressure**; Thermodynamics, gas laws, and altitude measurements use **absolute pressure**. > > ✅ **13.** For manometer problems in subsequent modules, the column height is ALWAYS in gauge pressure form: $\Delta P = \rho g \Delta h$. > > ✅ **14.** In KTU exams, **always show the unit conversion chain explicitly** — partial credit is heavily dependent on showing your conversion steps.

topics.title: Pressure at a Point in a Fluid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pressure at a Point in a Fluid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pressure at a Point in a Fluid (Definition):**
> The pressure at a point in a fluid is defined as the **normal compressive force per unit area** exerted by the fluid on an infinitesimally small surface surrounding that point, as the area approaches zero in the limit.

Mathematically, if a small elemental area $\Delta A$ experiences a normal compressive force $\Delta F$, then the pressure $P$ at that point is:

$$P = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A} = \frac{dF}{dA}$$

The **SI unit** of pressure is the **Pascal (Pa)**, which equals **1 N/m²**. Other common units include **bar**, **atm**, **psi**, and the CGS unit **dyne/cm²**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction — Pressure vs. Force:**
> - **Force** is a vector quantity (has direction).
> - **Pressure** is a **scalar** quantity at a point. Although physically it arises from force, at any point in a static fluid, the pressure is the **same in all directions** (this is proven by Pascal's Law).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **standing in a swimming pool**. The deeper you go, the more your ears hurt. Why? Because the **weight of the water column above you** is pressing down on every square centimeter of your body. This is pressure at a point!

Now think of a **sharp needle vs. a flat plate** of the same weight. The needle punctures skin easily because the same force is concentrated on a **tiny area**, producing enormous pressure. The flat plate doesn't hurt because the force spreads over a **large area**, producing tiny pressure.

So:
- **Pressure = Force spread over an Area**
- **Smaller area → Higher pressure** (for the same force)
- **Deeper depth → More fluid above → Higher pressure**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Atmosphere (Atmospheric Pressure):**
> $P_{atm} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$
> This is the pressure exerted by the Earth's atmosphere at sea level due to the weight of the air column above us.

## 1.3 Types of Pressure (KTU Must-Know)

| Type | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| **Atmospheric Pressure** | $P_{atm}$ | Pressure exerted by the Earth's atmosphere |
| **Absolute Pressure** | $P_{abs}$ | Pressure measured from **perfect vacuum (zero)** as reference |
| **Gauge Pressure** | $P_{gauge}$ | Pressure measured from **atmospheric pressure** as reference |
| **Vacuum Pressure** | $P_{vac}$ | Pressure **below** atmospheric pressure (negative gauge) |

The fundamental relationship is:

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$

$$P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pascal's Law — The Foundation of Fluid Pressure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pascal's Law (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> *"The pressure applied to a confined fluid at rest is transmitted undiminished and equally in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the surfaces with which it is in contact."*

**Why is this true?**
- Fluid molecules are free to move (unlike solids).
- If pressure were higher in one direction than another at a point, the fluid would accelerate in the direction of lower pressure.
- Since the fluid is in **static equilibrium**, the net force on any infinitesimal fluid element must be zero.
- Therefore, pressure at a point must be **isotropic** (same in all directions).

**Engineering Applications of Pascal's Law:**
1. **Hydraulic Jack / Hydraulic Lift** — Used in car workshops, elevators, braking systems
2. **Hydraulic Press** — For forging, stamping, and compacting
3. **Hydraulic Brakes** — In automobiles and aircraft

## 2.2 Hydrostatic Pressure Equation — Variation with Depth

Consider a small fluid element at depth $h$ below the free surface. The pressure at depth $h$ is determined by the weight of the fluid column above it.

For an **incompressible fluid** (density $\rho$ = constant):

$$\boxed{P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h}$$

For a **compressible fluid**, pressure variation is **exponential** (not linear). The general form:

$$P = P_0 \cdot e^{\frac{-g \cdot (z_0 - z)}{R \cdot T}}$$

(For KTU 2024 B.Tech, focus on the incompressible case unless otherwise stated.)

## 2.3 Hydrostatic Paradox

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrostatic Paradox:**
> The pressure at the bottom of a container depends **only on the height of the fluid column** and the **fluid density**, NOT on the **shape or volume** of the container.

This is why vessels with very different shapes (a wide tank vs. a narrow tube vs. an irregular vase) all have the **same pressure at the same depth** if they are filled with the same fluid to the same level.

**Why?** Because the vertical force balance depends only on the weight of the **vertical column** directly above the point, not on the total mass of fluid.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are HIGH-PRIORITY for KTU University Exams, Lab Tests, and Assignments.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{dF}{dA}$ | Definition of pressure at a point | Basic definition |
| 2 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | Relation between pressures | Conversion problems |
| 3 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Pressure variation |
| 4 | $P = \gamma h$ | Hydrostatic pressure using specific weight $\gamma = \rho g$ | Same as #3 |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z} = -\rho g$ | Fundamental equation of fluid statics (vertical direction) | Derivation |
| 6 | $P_A - P_B = \rho g (z_B - z_A)$ | Pressure difference between two points in static fluid | Multi-point problems |
| 7 | $P_1 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \rho g z_2$ | Hydrostatic Manometer Equation | Manometer problems |
| 8 | $P_2 - P_1 = \rho g (z_1 - z_2)$ | Alternative manometer form | Manometer problems |
| 9 | $h = \dfrac{P - P_{atm}}{\rho g}$ | Gauge height in piezometer | Pressure measurement |
| 10 | $P = P_{atm} + \rho_m g h_m - \rho_f g h_f$ | U-tube manometer equation | Differential manometer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Specific Weights (for KTU Problems):**
> - Water: $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3 = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$
> - Mercury (Hg): $\gamma_{Hg} = 133.1 \text{ kN/m}^3$
> - Air: $\gamma_{air} \approx 11.8 \text{ N/m}^3$ (at $15^\circ C$)
> - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (standard gravity)

## 2.5 Pressure Measurement Devices (KTU Module 1)

### A. Piezometer
- A simple vertical glass tube open to atmosphere.
- Measures **gauge pressure** at the point of connection.
- $P_A = \rho g h$, where $h$ is the height of fluid column in the tube.
- **Limitation:** Cannot measure high pressures (tube becomes impractically tall) or vacuum.

### B. U-Tube Manometer
- A U-shaped tube containing a heavier manometric fluid (usually **mercury** for water systems).
- One end connects to the point of measurement; the other is open to atmosphere.
- **Use:** Measures high pressures and can detect vacuum.

### C. Inverted U-Tube Manometer
- Uses a **lighter fluid** (like oil or air) instead of mercury.
- Used for measuring pressure differences in **water pipes** or systems where contamination by mercury must be avoided.

### D. Differential Manometer
- Measures the **pressure difference** between two points in a system.
- Common types: **U-tube differential manometer**, **Inverted U-tube differential manometer**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pressure measurement is critical in:
- **Civil Engineering:** Dam design, water supply networks, sewer systems
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Boiler design, hydraulic presses, hydraulic brakes
- **Aerospace:** Altitude measurement, cabin pressurization
- **Chemical Engineering:** Reactor design, pipeline flow monitoring
- **Biomedical:** Blood pressure measurement (sphygmomanometer)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure Equation (Variation of Pressure with Depth)

### Setup
Consider a static fluid in a container. Take a small fluid element of:
- Cross-sectional area $= dA$
- Height $= dz$ (in vertical direction)
- Density $= \rho$
- Located at depth $h$ below the free surface

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify forces on the fluid element**

The forces acting on the element in the vertical direction are:
- Pressure force from **above** (pushing down): $F_1 = P \cdot dA$
- Pressure force from **below** (pushing up): $F_2 = (P + dP) \cdot dA$
- Weight of the element (pulling down): $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot dz$

> Where $dP$ is the increase in pressure over the small height $dz$.

**Step 2 — Apply Static Equilibrium Condition**

Since the fluid is in **static equilibrium**, the net vertical force = 0:

$$F_2 - F_1 - W = 0$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$(P + dP) \cdot dA - P \cdot dA - \rho \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot dz = 0$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the equation**

Expand the first term:

$$P \cdot dA + dP \cdot dA - P \cdot dA - \rho \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot dz = 0$$

The $P \cdot dA$ terms **cancel out**:

$$dP \cdot dA - \rho \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot dz = 0$$

Factor out $dA$:

$$dA \cdot (dP - \rho \cdot g \cdot dz) = 0$$

Since $dA \neq 0$, we have:

$$dP = \rho \cdot g \cdot dz$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over the fluid depth**

Let the free surface be at $z_0$ where pressure is $P_0$ (usually $P_0 = P_{atm}$).
At depth $h$ (so $z = -h$ from free surface), pressure is $P$:

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} dP = \int_{0}^{-h} \rho \cdot g \cdot dz$$

Performing the integration:

$$P - P_0 = \rho \cdot g \cdot (0 - (-h)) \cdot (-1) = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

Wait — let me redo this with care. If we measure $z$ as **positive downward**:

$$dP = \rho \cdot g \cdot dz$$

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} dP = \int_{0}^{h} \rho \cdot g \cdot dz$$

$$P - P_0 = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 5 — Final Result**

$$\boxed{P = P_0 + \rho g h}$$

This is the **fundamental equation of fluid statics** — also called the **Hydrostatic Pressure Equation**.

**Interpretation:**
- $P_0$ = pressure at the free surface (atmospheric for open tanks)
- $\rho g h$ = pressure due to the weight of the fluid column of height $h$
- For a **gauge pressure** measurement, $P_0 = 0$, so $P_{gauge} = \rho g h$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — U-Tube Manometer Equation (Pressure at a Point in a Tank)

### Setup
Consider a closed tank containing a gas at pressure $P_A$ (above a liquid). A U-tube manometer is connected, with the right limb open to atmosphere. The manometric fluid is **mercury** ($\rho_m$), and the fluid in the tank is a lighter fluid (e.g., water, $\rho_f$). The mercury level difference is $h$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the manometer equation by traversing the U-tube**

Starting from point $A$ in the tank, move through the connecting fluid and mercury to the open end.

Adding pressures when going **down**, subtracting when going **up**:

$$P_A + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1 - \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h_2 = P_{atm}$$

Where:
- $h_1$ = distance from point $A$ down to the top of the mercury column
- $h_2$ = vertical height difference of mercury between the two limbs

**Step 2 — Solve for $P_A$**

$$P_A = P_{atm} + \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h_2 - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1$$

**Step 3 — For the special case where the fluid in the tank is the same as above the mercury (e.g., air above water in tank):**

Often $P_{atm} = 0$ (gauge), and $h_1$ is small, simplifying to:

$$P_A = h_2 \left( \rho_m - \rho_f \right) \cdot g \quad \text{or} \quad P_A = g \left( \rho_m h_2 - \rho_f h_1 \right)$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Differential Manometer (Pressure Difference Between Two Points)

### Setup
Two pipes A and B carry a fluid of density $\rho_f$. A U-tube differential manometer with mercury ($\rho_m$) is connected between them. The mercury level difference is $h$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Start at point A and traverse to point B**

$$P_A + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1 - \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_2 = P_B$$

Where:
- $h_1$ = vertical distance from A down to the top of mercury in the left limb
- $h_2$ = vertical distance from top of mercury in right limb up to B
- $h$ = mercury level difference

**Step 2 — Solve for pressure difference**

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_2 - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1$$

**Step 3 — For the common KTU case where $h_1 = h_2$ (same elevation of pipes), or in the standard configuration:**

$$P_A - P_B = h \left( \rho_m - \rho_f \right) \cdot g$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = \left( \rho_m - \rho_f \right) \cdot g \cdot h}$$

This is the **most frequently tested manometer formula** in KTU exams.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Problem Types in KTU

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Formula |
|--------------|-------|------|-------------|
| Pressure at depth | Depth $h$, fluid type | $P$ | $P = \rho g h$ |
| Absolute vs Gauge | $P_{gauge}$ | $P_{abs}$ | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ |
| Simple manometer | Heights of fluids | $P_A$ | Manometer equation |
| Differential manometer | Two pipe pressures | $P_A - P_B$ | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ |
| Pressure on submerged surface | Area $A$, depth $h$ | Force $F$ | $F = \rho g h_c A$ ($h_c$ = depth of centroid) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Conceptual Flow of Pressure Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["FLUID IN STATIC EQUILIBRIUM"]
    nodeB["Apply Newton's 2nd Law for Equilibrium"]
    nodeC["Pascal's Law: Isotropic Pressure"]
    nodeD["Hydrostatic Equation: P = P0 + rho*g*h"]
    nodeE["Manometer Equation: Pressure Balance"]
    nodeF["Pressure Measurement Devices"]
    nodeG["Engineering Applications"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pressure Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputP["PRESSURE SOURCE Point A in fluid"]
    dev1["PIEZOMETER Tube for low pressure"]
    dev2["U-TUBE MANOMETER Mercury for high pressure"]
    dev3["INVERTED U-TUBE For light fluids"]
    dev4["DIFFERENTIAL MANOMETER Pressure difference"]
    output["READING Height h converted to P"]

    inputP --> dev1
    inputP --> dev2
    inputP --> dev3
    inputP --> dev4
    dev1 --> output
    dev2 --> output
    dev3 --> output
    dev4 --> output
```

## 4.3 Schematic — U-Tube Manometer Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    tankA["TANK CONTAINING FLUID Pressure P_A at point A"]
    h1desc["Vertical distance h1 down to mercury surface in left limb"]
    hgdesc["Mercury level difference h"]
    h2desc["Distance from mercury to point B"]
    atm["OPEN TO ATMOSPHERE Pressure P_atm"]

    tankA -- "Down h1" --> hgdesc
    hgdesc -- "Up to atmosphere" --> atm
    atm -- "Path" --> h2desc
    h2desc -- "Connection to point B in tank" --> tankA
```

## 4.4 Diagram — Hydrostatic Pressure Variation with Depth

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s0["FREE SURFACE Pressure = P_atm"]
    s1["Depth z1 Pressure = P_atm + rho*g*z1"]
    s2["Depth z2 Pressure = P_atm + rho*g*z2"]
    s3["Depth z3 Pressure = P_atm + rho*g*z3"]

    s0 --> s1
    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Pressure increases **linearly** with depth in an incompressible fluid. The slope of the pressure-vs-depth line is $\rho g$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Define pressure at a point in a fluid. What are its SI units?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Pressure at a point in a fluid is defined as the **normal compressive force per unit area** exerted by the fluid on an infinitesimally small surface surrounding the point, as the area approaches zero.
> 
> Mathematically: $P = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \dfrac{\Delta F}{\Delta A} = \dfrac{dF}{dA}$
> 
> The **SI unit** of pressure is **Pascal (Pa)**, where $1 \text{ Pa} = 1 \text{ N/m}^2$.
> 
> *(Marks breakdown: 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark SI unit)*

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`** **State and explain Pascal's Law. Mention any two of its practical applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Pascal's Law:** "The pressure applied to an enclosed fluid at rest is transmitted undiminished and equally in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the surfaces in contact with it."
> 
> **Explanation:** Since fluid particles are free to move, any external pressure applied at one point is transmitted uniformly to every point in the fluid. This is the principle behind all hydraulic systems.
> 
> **Applications:**
> 1. **Hydraulic Jack / Lift** — used for lifting heavy vehicles.
> 2. **Hydraulic Brakes** — used in automobiles for transmitting brake force.
> 
> *(Marks breakdown: 1 mark statement, 1 mark explanation, 1 mark for two applications)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) — Full Solution Provided

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**a)** Derive the hydrostatic pressure equation $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ for an incompressible fluid at rest. **[7 Marks]**

**b)** A U-tube manometer containing mercury is used to measure the pressure of water in a pipe. The mercury level in the limb connected to the pipe is 200 mm **below** the pipe centerline, while the mercury level in the open limb is 100 mm **below** the pipe centerline. The specific gravity of mercury is 13.6. Calculate the pressure in the pipe. Take atmospheric pressure as 101.325 kPa. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

Consider a fluid element of cross-section $dA$ and height $dz$ at depth $z$ below the free surface, in static equilibrium.

**Forces on the element (vertical direction):**
- Force from above (downward): $F_1 = P \cdot dA$
- Force from below (upward): $F_2 = (P + dP) \cdot dA$
- Weight (downward): $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot dz$

**Equilibrium condition:**

$$(P + dP) \cdot dA - P \cdot dA - \rho \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot dz = 0$$

Simplifying:

$$dP = \rho \cdot g \cdot dz$$

**Integrating from free surface to depth $h$:**

$$P = P_0 + \rho g h \quad \text{(QED)}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given:**
- Specific gravity of mercury: $S_{Hg} = 13.6$
- Height of water column in connected limb: $h_w = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- Mercury level difference: $h_{Hg} = 200 - 100 = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Manometer Equation (traversing from pipe to atmosphere):**

$$P_{pipe} + \rho_w g (0.2) - \rho_{Hg} g (0.1) = P_{atm}$$

Calculate $\rho_{Hg} = 13.6 \times 1000 = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

Substituting:

$$P_{pipe} + 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.2 - 13600 \times 9.81 \times 0.1 = 101.325 \times 10^3$$

$$P_{pipe} + 1962 - 13341.6 = 101325$$

$$P_{pipe} - 11379.6 = 101325$$

$$P_{pipe} = 112704.6 \text{ Pa} = 112.7 \text{ kPa (absolute)}$$

**Gauge pressure:** $P_{gauge} = 112.7 - 101.325 = 11.38 \text{ kPa}$

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative for Internal Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - Jun 2023]`**

**a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a U-tube differential manometer. Derive the expression for the pressure difference $P_A - P_B$ in terms of mercury level difference $h$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**b)** Two pressure points A and B in a water pipeline are connected to a U-tube differential manometer containing mercury. The mercury level in the limb near A is 150 mm **below** A, and in the limb near B is 250 mm **below** B. The point B is 1 m **above** point A. If the manometer reads a mercury difference of 200 mm, find the pressure difference $(P_A - P_B)$. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Working Principle:** A U-tube differential manometer has two limbs connected to two pressure points A and B. The manometric fluid (mercury) sits at the bottom of the U. The pressure difference $P_A - P_B$ causes a height difference $h$ in the mercury columns, which is measured.

**Derivation:**

Starting at A, traversing down through water, across mercury, and up to B:

$$P_A + \rho_w g (h_1) - \rho_{Hg} g (h) - \rho_w g (h_2) = P_B$$

If A and B are at the same elevation ($h_1 = h_2$):

$$P_A - P_B = h(\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) g$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given:**
- $h_1 = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$ (water column above mercury in A limb)
- $h_2 = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$ (water column above mercury in B limb, but B is 1 m above A, so effective height adjustment is needed)
- $h = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$ (mercury difference)
- $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Note:** Since B is 1 m above A, the actual water column in the B-limb is: $h_2 + 1 = 1.25$ m (with respect to A's elevation).

**Manometer equation:**

$$P_A + \rho_w g (0.15) - \rho_{Hg} g (0.2) - \rho_w g (0.25) = P_B$$

Wait, careful — the correct traversal (treating A and B's elevations properly) gives:

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_{Hg} g h - \rho_w g (h_2 - h_1 + \Delta z)$$

Where $\Delta z = z_B - z_A = +1$ m.

$$P_A - P_B = 13600 \times 9.81 \times 0.2 - 1000 \times 9.81 \times (0.25 - 0.15 + 1)$$

$$= 26683.2 - 1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.1$$

$$= 26683.2 - 10791 = 15892.2 \text{ Pa} \approx 15.89 \text{ kPa}$$

**$P_A - P_B \approx 15.89 \text{ kPa}$**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Converting all lengths to **meters** and pressures to **Pa** before substituting. Mixing mm and m is a top reason for losing 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Sign Errors in Manometer Traversal:** When traversing the manometer, students often add pressure when going up and subtract when going down — but it is the **opposite** (going down adds pressure due to weight of fluid column).
> 3. **Forgetting Atmospheric Pressure:** In absolute pressure problems, don't forget to add $P_{atm}$. In gauge pressure problems, atmospheric pressure is taken as zero.
> 4. **Using Density of Water Instead of Mercury:** In manometer formulas, the **mercury** density ($13.6 \times 1000$) is used for the heavy fluid, not water.
> 5. **Hydrostatic Paradox Misconception:** Pressure depends only on **vertical depth**, not the **shape** of the container.
> 6. **Skipping Diagrams in 14-mark Questions:** Always draw a labeled manometer/tank diagram in part (a) — KTU examiners allocate **at least 2 marks** for neat sketches.
> 7. **Forgetting to State Assumptions:** Always mention: fluid is static, incompressible, temperature is constant.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Pressure at a Point in a Fluid**

- [x] **Definition:** Pressure at a point = Normal force per unit area ($P = dF/dA$); SI unit = Pascal (Pa = N/m²).
- [x] **Pressure is a Scalar** at a point in a static fluid (same in all directions).
- [x] **Pascal's Law:** Pressure transmitted equally in all directions throughout an enclosed static fluid.
- [x] **Hydrostatic Equation:** $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ — pressure increases **linearly** with depth.
- [x] **Hydrostatic Paradox:** Pressure at the bottom depends only on **vertical depth**, not on container shape.
- [x] **Three Types of Pressure:** $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$; $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$.
- [x] **Manometer Formulas:**
    - Simple: $P_A = P_{atm} + \rho_m g h - \rho_f g h_1$
    - Differential: $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$
- [x] **Specific Gravity of Mercury = 13.6** — this is a KTU-favorite numerical value.
- [x] **Standard values:** $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$.
- [x] **Pressure Measurement Devices:** Piezometer, U-tube manometer, Inverted U-tube manometer, Differential manometer.
- [x] **Engineering Applications:** Hydraulic jack, hydraulic press, hydraulic brakes, dam design, blood pressure measurement.

> **🎯 KTU Module 1 Pressure Mastery Tip:** Practice at least 5 manometer problems with **different configurations** (open/closed tanks, differential setups, inverted U-tube) — they constitute 50%+ of Part B questions in Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pascal's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pascal's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Pascal's Law** (also known as the **Principle of Transmission of Fluid Pressure**) is a fundamental principle in fluid statics, originally formulated by the French mathematician, physicist, and philosopher **Blaise Pascal** in **1653**. The law states:

> *"Pressure applied to a confined, incompressible fluid at rest is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicularly to the surfaces of the containing vessel."*

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Fluid Mechanics and Hydraulic Machines*, this law is the cornerstone of all hydraulic machinery analysis, forming the theoretical foundation for devices such as **hydraulic presses**, **hydraulic lifts**, **hydraulic jacks**, **hydraulic brakes**, and **hydraulic actuators**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Pascal's Law is explicitly listed under *Fluid Statics* and is a mandatory precursor to studying **Hydraulic Machines** (covered later in the module and in subsequent modules such as Module 2 and Module 4). Students are expected to derive the law mathematically and apply it to solve numerical problems on hydraulic press efficiency, force multiplication, and pressure transmission.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Squeezed Water Balloon" Intuition

Imagine a completely sealed **water-filled balloon** with multiple nozzles pointing in different directions. Now, suppose you squeeze one side of the balloon with your hand. What happens?

- Water does not compress — it has nowhere to go except **outward through every nozzle equally**.
- The force you apply on one side is felt **instantly and with the same intensity** at every other point inside the balloon.
- Each nozzle shoots water with the same pressure, regardless of the direction the nozzle faces.

This is precisely what Pascal's Law describes:

| Real-World Element | Pascal's Law Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Sealed water balloon | Confined incompressible fluid |
| Your squeezing hand | External applied force ($\small F$) |
| Water squirting from nozzles | Pressure ($\small P$) transmitted in all directions |
| Equal water pressure at every nozzle | Equal pressure at every point in the fluid |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Incompressible" Matters:** Pascal's Law strictly applies to **incompressible fluids** (liquids like water, oil, hydraulic fluid) — NOT to gases. Gases compress, so the pressure distribution is not uniform. This is why hydraulic systems always use **oil or water**, never air.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $\small P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar} = 14.696 \text{ psi}$
- **Density of Water (at 4°C):** $\small \rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Acceleration due to Gravity:** $\small g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Bulk Modulus of Water:** $\small K \approx 2.2 \text{ GPa}$ (very high, confirming near-incompressibility)
- **SI Unit of Pressure:** $\small \text{Pascal (Pa)} = \text{N/m}^2$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

Let us dissect Pascal's Law into its operational components:

**Step 1 — The Confined Fluid Condition**
The fluid must be **enclosed** within a rigid boundary. An open cup of water does not satisfy this condition because the fluid surface is exposed to the atmosphere. Hydraulic systems always use a **closed container** with pistons, cylinders, or sealed chambers.

**Step 2 — The Incompressibility Assumption**
The fluid must not significantly change its density when pressure is applied. For liquids like hydraulic oil or water, the density change is negligible (typically less than **0.5%** even at high pressures of 100 MPa). This is verified by a very high **Bulk Modulus ($\small K$)**.

**Step 3 — The Static Condition**
The fluid must be **at rest** (no flow, no velocity gradients). Pascal's Law is a **fluid statics** principle. When fluid is moving, additional factors like Bernoulli's equation and viscous losses come into play.

**Step 4 — Pressure Transmission Mechanism**
When an external force $\small F_1$ is applied to a piston of cross-sectional area $\small A_1$, the pressure generated is:

$$\small P = \frac{F_1}{A_1}$$

This pressure is transmitted **instantaneously** (the speed of sound in water is approximately **1480 m/s**, so transmission is effectively immediate) to every other point in the fluid, including a second piston of area $\small A_2$.

**Step 5 — Force Amplification**
At the second piston, the transmitted pressure $\small P$ acts on area $\small A_2$, producing an output force $\small F_2$:

$$\small F_2 = P \times A_2 = \frac{F_1}{A_1} \times A_2$$

Because $\small A_2 \gg A_1$ in typical hydraulic systems, $\small F_2 \gg F_1$, which is the principle of **mechanical advantage** through fluid pressure.

**Step 6 — Perpendicularity of Pressure**
The pressure always acts **normal (perpendicular)** to any surface it contacts. This is a property of fluid pressure in general (not unique to Pascal's Law), but it ensures that pistons move along their axis without lateral leakage forces.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure Definition | $\small P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Force per unit area | $\small \text{Pa}$ or $\small \text{N/m}^2$ |
| Pascal's Transmission | $\small P_1 = P_2$ | Pressure equality throughout fluid | $\small \text{Pa}$ |
| Hydraulic Press Force | $\small F_2 = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | Output force on larger piston | $\small \text{N}$ |
| Hydraulic Press Pressure | $\small P_1 = \dfrac{F_1}{A_1} = \dfrac{F_2}{A_2}$ | Pressure identity (input = output) | $\small \text{Pa}$ |
| Mechanical Advantage | $\small MA = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = \dfrac{D_2^2}{D_1^2}$ | Ratio of output force to input force | Dimensionless |
| Volume Conservation | $\small A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$ | Displacement ratio (smaller piston moves more) | $\small \text{m}^3$ |
| Work/Energy Balance | $\small F_1 \cdot x_1 = F_2 \cdot x_2$ | Input work = Output work (ideal case) | $\small \text{J}$ |
| Pressure–Depth Relation | $\small P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure from fluid column | $\small \text{Pa}$ |
| Gauge to Absolute | $\small P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Convert gauge to absolute pressure | $\small \text{Pa}$ |
| Force Ratio (diameters) | $\small \dfrac{F_2}{F_1} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$ | Force amplification using piston diameters | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Exam Tip:** When a problem states "two pistons of diameters $\small D_1$ and $\small D_2$", always convert to **area** first: $\small A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$. Using diameter squared directly is correct only when the $\pi/4$ factor cancels out on both sides of the equation.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pascal's Law is the foundational principle behind an entire industry: **Fluid Power Engineering**. Key applications include:

- **Hydraulic Press:** Used in forging, stamping, compression molding, and baling (compacting scrap metal or cotton).
- **Hydraulic Lift / Jack:** Used in automobile service stations and aircraft maintenance to lift multi-ton vehicles.
- **Hydraulic Brakes:** The brake pedal pushes a small piston, generating pressure that clamps brake pads against rotors in all four wheels with amplified force.
- **Hydraulic Steering Systems:** Used in heavy trucks and earth-moving equipment to reduce driver effort.
- **Hydraulic Dampers / Shock Absorbers:** Use Pascal's principle to convert kinetic energy into heat via fluid flow through orifices.
- **Injection Molding Machines:** Use hydraulic pressure to inject molten plastic into molds with high precision.
- **Earth-Moving Equipment:** Excavators, bulldozers, and backhoes use hydraulic actuators controlled by Pascal's Law.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The *force* is amplified, but the *energy* is conserved. The smaller piston must travel a **larger distance** to displace the same volume of fluid, which moves the larger piston a **smaller distance**. This is a direct consequence of the **Law of Conservation of Energy** and the **incompressibility** of the fluid ($\small A_1 x_1 = A_2 x_2$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Pressure Equality in a Confined Fluid

**Statement to Prove:** If pressure $\small P_1$ is applied at point 1 in a confined incompressible fluid, then the pressure $\small P_2$ at any other point 2 in the fluid is equal to $\small P_1$.

**Setup:** Consider a small cylindrical fluid element of cross-sectional area $\small dA$ at an arbitrary orientation inside the fluid. Let the element make an angle $\small \theta$ with the horizontal.

**Step 1 — Force Balance on the Cylindrical Element**

The fluid element is in static equilibrium (no acceleration), so the **sum of all forces** acting on it must be zero.

Let the pressure at the two ends of the cylindrical element be $\small P_1$ and $\small P_2$. The forces acting along the axis of the cylinder are:

- Force at end 1: $\small F_1 = P_1 \cdot dA$ (acting inward)
- Force at end 2: $\small F_2 = P_2 \cdot dA$ (acting outward)

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's First Law (Equilibrium Condition)**

For static equilibrium, the net force along the axis must vanish:

$$\small F_1 - F_2 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Pressure–Force Relations**

Substituting $\small F_1 = P_1 \cdot dA$ and $\small F_2 = P_2 \cdot dA$:

$$\small P_1 \cdot dA - P_2 \cdot dA = 0$$

**Step 4 — Factor Out the Common Term $\small dA$**

Since $\small dA \neq 0$ (the element has non-zero cross-section):

$$\small dA (P_1 - P_2) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Pressure Relation**

Dividing both sides by $\small dA$:

$$\small P_1 - P_2 = 0$$

**Step 6 — Final Result**

$$\small \boxed{P_1 = P_2}$$

**Interpretation:** The pressure is identical at both ends of the fluid element, regardless of the orientation of the element. Since the element was arbitrarily chosen, the pressure must be the same at **every point** in the fluid. **Pascal's Law is proved.** $\small \blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Force Amplification in a Hydraulic Press

**Setup:** Consider a hydraulic press with two connected cylinders containing an incompressible fluid. The smaller piston (input) has cross-sectional area $\small A_1$ and the larger piston (output) has cross-sectional area $\small A_2$. An input force $\small F_1$ is applied on piston 1.

**Step 1 — Calculate the Pressure Generated at Piston 1**

The pressure exerted on the fluid by the input piston is force divided by area:

$$\small P_1 = \frac{F_1}{A_1}$$

This pressure is transmitted throughout the fluid (by Pascal's Law, as derived above).

**Step 2 — Apply Pascal's Law at Piston 2**

By Pascal's Law, the pressure at piston 2 equals the pressure at piston 1:

$$\small P_2 = P_1$$

**Step 3 — Express the Output Force in Terms of Input Force**

The output force on piston 2 is:

$$\small F_2 = P_2 \cdot A_2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\small P_2 = P_1$**

$$\small F_2 = P_1 \cdot A_2 = \frac{F_1}{A_1} \cdot A_2$$

**Step 5 — Final Result — The Hydraulic Press Equation**

$$\small \boxed{F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}}$$

This is the **governing equation** of every hydraulic press, lift, and jack ever built.

**Step 6 — Express in Terms of Piston Diameters**

Since $\small A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$, the area ratio becomes:

$$\small \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{\dfrac{\pi D_2^2}{4}}{\dfrac{\pi D_1^2}{4}} = \frac{D_2^2}{D_1^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\small \boxed{F_2 = F_1 \cdot \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Displacement Ratio (Conservation of Volume)

**Setup:** When piston 1 is pushed down by a distance $\small x_1$, it displaces a volume of fluid $\small V_1$. This volume must be transmitted to piston 2, pushing it up by a distance $\small x_2$ with volume $\small V_2$. By the **incompressibility assumption**, $\small V_1 = V_2$.

**Step 1 — Volume Displaced by Piston 1**

$$\small V_1 = A_1 \cdot x_1$$

**Step 2 — Volume Received by Piston 2**

$$\small V_2 = A_2 \cdot x_2$$

**Step 3 — Apply Volume Conservation**

$$\small V_1 = V_2 \implies A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Displacement Ratio**

$$\small \boxed{\frac{x_1}{x_2} = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{D_2^2}{D_1^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Verify Energy Conservation**

Work input (force times distance on piston 1):

$$\small W_{in} = F_1 \cdot x_1$$

Work output (force times distance on piston 2):

$$\small W_{out} = F_2 \cdot x_2$$

Substitute $\small F_2 = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ and $\small x_2 = x_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}$:

$$\small W_{out} = \left(F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}\right) \cdot \left(x_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right) = F_1 \cdot x_1 = W_{in}$$

$$\small \boxed{W_{out} = W_{in} \quad \text{(Ideal, lossless case)}}$$

This confirms that **no energy is created or destroyed** in an ideal hydraulic system — force is amplified at the cost of distance, in perfect accordance with the **Law of Conservation of Energy**.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

**Problem:** A hydraulic press has a small piston of diameter $\small 5 \text{ cm}$ and a large piston of diameter $\small 50 \text{ cm}$. A force of $\small 200 \text{ N}$ is applied on the small piston. Find:
(a) The pressure transmitted through the fluid.
(b) The output force on the large piston.
(c) The distance the large piston moves if the small piston moves $\small 20 \text{ cm}$.

**Given Data:**
- $\small D_1 = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- $\small D_2 = 50 \text{ cm} = 0.50 \text{ m}$
- $\small F_1 = 200 \text{ N}$
- $\small x_1 = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Compute Cross-Sectional Areas**

$$\small A_1 = \frac{\pi D_1^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$\small A_2 = \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.50)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.25}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-1} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Part (a): Pressure Transmitted**

$$\small P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{200}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\small \boxed{P \approx 101,859 \text{ Pa} \approx 101.86 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Step 3 — Part (b): Output Force**

$$\small F_2 = P \times A_2 = 101,859 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-1}$$

Alternatively, using the diameter-squared ratio:

$$\small F_2 = F_1 \cdot \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 = 200 \times \left(\frac{50}{5}\right)^2 = 200 \times 100$$

$$\small \boxed{F_2 = 20,000 \text{ N} = 20 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 4 — Part (c): Displacement of Large Piston**

Using volume conservation:

$$\small A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$$

$$\small x_2 = \frac{A_1 \cdot x_1}{A_2} = \frac{1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.20}{1.9635 \times 10^{-1}}$$

$$\small \boxed{x_2 = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 2 \text{ mm}}$$

**Verification (Energy Balance):**

$$\small W_{in} = F_1 \cdot x_1 = 200 \times 0.20 = 40 \text{ J}$$

$$\small W_{out} = F_2 \cdot x_2 = 20{,}000 \times 0.002 = 40 \text{ J} \checkmark$$

**Physical Insight:** A modest **200 N** input force (roughly the weight of a 20 kg object) produces a **20,000 N** output force (about 2 tonnes). This is a **100× mechanical advantage**. However, the operator must push the small piston through **20 cm** to raise the large piston by only **2 mm** — a classic force-versus-distance trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hydraulic Press Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Operator Input Force F1] --> B[Small Piston Area A1]
    B --> C[Pressure P equals F1 over A1]
    C --> D[Incompressible Hydraulic Fluid in Sealed Chamber]
    D --> E[Pressure P transmitted undiminished in all directions]
    E --> F[Large Piston Area A2]
    F --> G[Output Force F2 equals P times A2]
    G --> H[Mechanical Advantage MA equals A2 over A1]
    H --> I[Load Lifted or Work Performed]
    J[Conservation of Volume A1 times x1 equals A2 times x2] --> F
    K[Energy Balance W in equals W out] --> I
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pascal's Law Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Fluid is Confined] --> P2[Step 2: Fluid is Incompressible]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Fluid is at Rest]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: External Force F1 Applied on Piston]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Pressure P1 equals F1 over A1 Generated]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Pressure P1 equals P2 at All Points]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Pressure Acts Perpendicular to All Surfaces]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Output Force F2 equals P2 times A2]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Force Amplified When A2 greater than A1]
    P9 --> P10[Result: Hydraulic Work Performed]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Applications Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Pascal Law: Pressure Transmission Principle] --> App1[Hydraulic Press]
    Root --> App2[Hydraulic Lift and Jack]
    Root --> App3[Hydraulic Brake System]
    Root --> App4[Hydraulic Steering]
    Root --> App5[Injection Molding]
    Root --> App6[Shock Absorbers]

    App1 --> Sub1[Forging and Stamping]
    App1 --> Sub2[Compression Molding]
    App2 --> Sub3[Automobile Service Stations]
    App2 --> Sub4[Aircraft Maintenance]
    App3 --> Sub5[Automotive Disc Brakes]
    App3 --> Sub6[Drum Brake Actuation]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**State Pascal's Law. Mention any two practical applications of this law.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Pascal's Law states that *"the pressure applied to a confined, incompressible fluid at rest is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicularly to the surfaces of the containing vessel."*

**Two Practical Applications:**

1. **Hydraulic Press / Hydraulic Lift:** Used in automobile garages to lift vehicles for servicing. A small input force on a small piston produces a large output force on a larger piston, enabling heavy loads to be raised with minimal effort.

2. **Hydraulic Brake System:** Used in automobiles. When the brake pedal is pressed, a small force is applied to a master cylinder piston, generating pressure that is transmitted through brake fluid to actuate larger pistons (slave cylinders) at each wheel, applying the brake pads with amplified force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** 1.5 marks for the precise statement of the law, 0.75 marks for each application (1.5 marks for two applications).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**A hydraulic press has a small piston of area $\small 0.01 \text{ m}^2$ and a large piston of area $\small 1 \text{ m}^2$. If a force of $\small 50 \text{ N}$ is applied on the small piston, calculate the force on the large piston.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- $\small A_1 = 0.01 \text{ m}^2$
- $\small A_2 = 1 \text{ m}^2$
- $\small F_1 = 50 \text{ N}$

**Step 1 — Calculate Pressure at Small Piston**

$$\small P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{50}{0.01} = 5000 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Pascal's Law**

Pressure at large piston $\small =$ Pressure at small piston $\small = 5000 \text{ Pa}$.

**Step 3 — Calculate Force on Large Piston**

$$\small F_2 = P \times A_2 = 5000 \times 1 = 5000 \text{ N}$$

$$\small \boxed{F_2 = 5000 \text{ N} = 5 \text{ kN}}$$

**Mechanical Advantage** $\small = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = \dfrac{1}{0.01} = 100$. A 100-fold force amplification is achieved.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** State and prove Pascal's Law. Explain with a neat sketch the working of a hydraulic press. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic lift is used to raise a car of mass **1500 kg** for servicing. The diameter of the larger piston is **40 cm**, and the diameter of the smaller piston is **8 cm**. Determine: **(i)** the force that must be applied on the smaller piston to lift the car, and **(ii)** the pressure developed in the hydraulic fluid. Take $\small g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Statement, Proof, and Working of Hydraulic Press

**Statement of Pascal's Law:** Pressure applied to a confined, incompressible fluid at rest is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicularly to the surfaces of the containing vessel.

**Proof:**
Consider a small cylindrical fluid element of cross-sectional area $\small dA$ inside a confined fluid at rest. Let the pressure at one end be $\small P_1$ and at the other end be $\small P_2$. The forces on the two ends are $\small F_1 = P_1 \cdot dA$ and $\small F_2 = P_2 \cdot dA$. For static equilibrium:

$$\small F_1 - F_2 = 0 \implies P_1 \cdot dA - P_2 \cdot dA = 0 \implies P_1 = P_2$$

Since the element was arbitrarily chosen, the pressure is the same at all points in the fluid. **Hence proved.**

**Working of Hydraulic Press (with verbal sketch description):**

A hydraulic press consists of two cylinders of different cross-sectional areas $\small A_1$ (smaller) and $\small A_2$ (larger), connected at the base and filled with an incompressible fluid (usually oil). A small piston fits in cylinder 1, and a large piston fits in cylinder 2. A force $\small F_1$ applied on the small piston generates pressure $\small P = F_1 / A_1$, which is transmitted throughout the fluid to the large piston. The output force is $\small F_2 = P \times A_2 = F_1 \times (A_2 / A_1)$. Since $\small A_2 \gg A_1$, a small input force produces a large output force capable of compressing, lifting, or forging heavy objects.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given Data:**
- Mass of car: $\small m = 1500 \text{ kg}$
- Diameter of larger piston: $\small D_2 = 40 \text{ cm} = 0.40 \text{ m}$
- Diameter of smaller piston: $\small D_1 = 8 \text{ cm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$
- $\small g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Calculate the Weight of the Car (Load to be Lifted)**

$$\small W = m \times g = 1500 \times 9.81 = 14{,}715 \text{ N}$$

This weight must be supported by the output force on the large piston, so $\small F_2 = W = 14{,}715 \text{ N}$.

**Step 2 — Calculate Cross-Sectional Areas**

$$\small A_1 = \frac{\pi D_1^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.08)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$\small A_2 = \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.40)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.16}{4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-1} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Required Input Force $\small F_1$**

Using Pascal's Law:

$$\small P_1 = P_2 \implies \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

$$\small F_1 = F_2 \times \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 14{,}715 \times \frac{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-1}}$$

$$\small F_1 = 14{,}715 \times 0.04 = 588.6 \text{ N}$$

$$\small \boxed{F_1 = 588.6 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Pressure in the Fluid**

$$\small P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{588.6}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.171 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\small \boxed{P \approx 117.1 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Verification:**

$$\small \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{1.2566 \times 10^{-1}}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}} = 25$$

Mechanical Advantage = 25. A 588.6 N input produces $\small 588.6 \times 25 = 14{,}715 \text{ N}$ output. ✓

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 — Alternative Choice]**
