**(a)** Derive the expression for the mechanical advantage of a hydraulic press. Show that the work input equals the work output in an ideal (frictionless) hydraulic system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a hydraulic system, a force of **500 N** is applied to the small piston of diameter **10 cm**. If the large piston has a diameter of **50 cm**, determine: **(i)** the pressure intensity in the fluid, **(ii)** the load that can be lifted by the large piston, and **(iii)** the distance moved by the large piston when the small piston moves through **15 cm**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Mechanical Advantage and Work Equality

**Derivation of Mechanical Advantage:**

Consider a hydraulic press with a small piston of area $\small A_1$ and a large piston of area $\small A_2$. A force $\small F_1$ applied to piston 1 creates pressure:

$$\small P = \frac{F_1}{A_1}$$

By Pascal's Law, this pressure is transmitted unchanged to piston 2, producing force $\small F_2$:

$$\small F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = \frac{F_1 \cdot A_2}{A_1}$$

**Mechanical Advantage:**

$$\small \boxed{MA = \frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{A_2}{A_1}}$$

In terms of diameters: $\small MA = \dfrac{D_2^2}{D_1^2}$.

**Work Equality Proof:**

By volume conservation (incompressibility):

$$\small A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2 \implies x_2 = \frac{A_1 \cdot x_1}{A_2}$$

Work input:

$$\small W_{in} = F_1 \cdot x_1$$

Work output:

$$\small W_{out} = F_2 \cdot x_2 = \left(\frac{F_1 \cdot A_2}{A_1}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{A_1 \cdot x_1}{A_2}\right) = F_1 \cdot x_1$$

$$\small \boxed{W_{in} = W_{out} = F_1 \cdot x_1}$$

In an ideal frictionless system, the hydraulic press conserves energy perfectly — it amplifies force at the cost of distance.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given Data:**
- $\small F_1 = 500 \text{ N}$
- $\small D_1 = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$
- $\small D_2 = 50 \text{ cm} = 0.50 \text{ m}$
- $\small x_1 = 15 \text{ cm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Calculate Areas**

$$\small A_1 = \frac{\pi (0.10)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$\small A_2 = \frac{\pi (0.50)^2}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-1} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Part (i): Pressure Intensity**

$$\small P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{500}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.366 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\small \boxed{P \approx 63.66 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Step 3 — Part (ii): Load on Large Piston**

$$\small F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = 6.366 \times 10^{4} \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-1} = 12{,}500 \text{ N}$$

$$\small \boxed{F_2 = 12{,}500 \text{ N} = 12.5 \text{ kN}}$$

Alternatively: $\small F_2 = F_1 \times \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 = 500 \times (5)^2 = 500 \times 25 = 12{,}500 \text{ N}$. ✓

**Step 4 — Part (iii): Distance Moved by Large Piston**

Using volume conservation:

$$\small A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$$

$$\small x_2 = \frac{A_1 \cdot x_1}{A_2} = \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.15}{1.9635 \times 10^{-1}}$$

$$\small x_2 = \frac{1.1781 \times 10^{-3}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-1}} = 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\small \boxed{x_2 = 6.0 \text{ mm}}$$

**Verification (Energy Balance):**

$$\small W_{in} = 500 \times 0.15 = 75 \text{ J}$$

$$\small W_{out} = 12{,}500 \times 0.006 = 75 \text{ J} \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert cm to m:** Many students lose 1-2 marks by leaving lengths in cm. SI units are mandatory. If you write $\small A_1 = \dfrac{\pi (10)^2}{4} = 78.54 \text{ cm}^2$, the numerical answer will be **off by a factor of 10,000** because area is in $\small \text{cm}^2$ but force is in **newtons** (which uses $\small \text{m}^2$).
>
> 2. **Using diameter directly instead of area:** Writing $\small F_2 = F_1 \times \dfrac{D_2}{D_1}$ (without squaring) is a **critical error**. The correct formula is $\small F_2 = F_1 \times \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$. The square appears because **area scales as diameter squared**.
>
> 3. **Confusing gauge and absolute pressure:** If the problem gives "pressure" without specifying, in KTU problems it usually means **gauge pressure**. Only add atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa) if the question explicitly says "absolute pressure".
>
> 4. **Not showing the volume conservation step:** When asked about piston displacement, you **must** show $\small A_1 x_1 = A_2 x_2$ explicitly. Skipping this step costs 2-3 marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the sketch in theory questions:** A "neat sketch" of the hydraulic press is worth **1-2 marks** in KTU board exams. Always include at least a labeled box diagram showing both pistons, the fluid, and the area/force labels.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Pascal's Law (1653):** Pressure applied to a confined, **incompressible**, static fluid is transmitted **equally in all directions** and acts **perpendicular** to containing surfaces.
- **Three Pre-requisites:** (1) Confined fluid, (2) Incompressibility, (3) Static condition.
- **Core Equation:** $\small P_1 = P_2$ at all points in the fluid.
- **Hydraulic Press Force Equation:** $\small F_2 = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = F_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$
- **Volume Conservation:** $\small A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$ (incompressibility assumption).
- **Work Conservation:** $\small F_1 \cdot x_1 = F_2 \cdot x_2$ (energy is conserved, not created).
- **Mechanical Advantage:** $\small MA = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = \dfrac{D_2^2}{D_1^2}$ (dimensionless ratio).
- **Pressure Units:** $\small 1 \text{ Pa} = 1 \text{ N/m}^2$; $\small 1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$; $\small 1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$.
- **Key Applications:** Hydraulic press, lift, jack, brake, steering, injection molding, shock absorbers, earth-moving equipment.
- **Trade-off Principle:** Force is **amplified**, but distance (and therefore speed) is **reduced** — this is the **conservation of energy** in action.
- **Bulk Modulus of Water:** $\small K \approx 2.2 \text{ GPa}$ (confirms incompressibility assumption).
- **Formula Conversion Tip:** Always convert cm → m **before** computing areas to avoid SI unit mismatch errors.
- **Diameter vs. Area Rule:** When the problem uses diameters, the force ratio is **diameter-squared**, not diameter.
- **Real Systems vs. Ideal:** In real systems, there are losses due to **friction**, **leakage**, and **fluid compressibility**, so $\small W_{out} < W_{in}$. Efficiency $\small \eta = W_{out} / W_{in} < 1$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Pressure - Piezometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Pressure - Piezometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Piezometer** is the simplest form of manometer used for the direct measurement of **gauge pressure** of a fluid contained in a vessel or pipe. It consists of a transparent (glass) vertical tube, one end of which is connected to the point in the fluid system where the pressure is to be measured, while the other (upper) end is left **open to the atmosphere**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> A piezometer is a device used to measure pressure at a point in a fluid by observing the height to which a column of the same fluid rises in a vertical tube open to the atmosphere, connected directly to the point of measurement.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are drinking a milkshake through a long, transparent straw. If you hold the straw perfectly vertical and place your thumb off the top, the milkshake stays suspended at a certain height inside the straw. Why does it not fall? Because the pressure you create inside the straw (lower than atmospheric) is balancing the weight of the milkshake column.

**The piezometer works on this exact same principle!**

- The fluid pressure inside a pipe "pushes" the liquid up a vertical glass tube.
- The fluid rises until the **downward weight of the column** exactly equals the **upward pressure force** at the base.
- The height of the column becomes the direct visual indicator of the pressure.

Think of it like this: the piezometer is essentially a "fluid ruler" — pressure is read off directly in terms of **millimeters or meters of fluid column**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Values Used in Piezometer Calculations:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Density of water (at 4°C): **ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Density of mercury (at 0°C): **ρ_mercury = 13600 kg/m³**
> - Specific weight of water: **γ_water = 9810 N/m³**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: **P_atm = 101.325 kPa ≈ 10.33 m of water column**
> - Pressure units frequently used: **Pa (N/m²)**, **kPa**, **bar**, **m of fluid**, **mm of Hg**

### 1.4 Classification of Pressure

Before diving deep, it is essential to understand the **three types of pressure** that a piezometer deals with:

| Type of Pressure | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Absolute Pressure** | P_abs | Pressure measured from perfect vacuum (zero reference) |
| **Atmospheric Pressure** | P_atm | Pressure exerted by the earth's atmosphere |
| **Gauge Pressure** | P_gauge | Pressure measured above atmospheric (positive) |
| **Vacuum Pressure** | P_vacuum | Pressure measured below atmospheric (negative) |

The fundamental relationships are:

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vacuum}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **piezometer can ONLY measure gauge pressure**. It cannot measure vacuum pressure (because the fluid would have to be pulled *downward* in the tube, which requires a different setup) and it does not directly give absolute pressure (atmospheric pressure must be added separately).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of a Piezometer

The structural components of a typical piezometer are:

- **Reservoir / Pipe (A):** The closed or open container in which the fluid is stored and whose pressure is to be measured.
- **Connecting Nozzle / Ferrule:** A small, threaded opening on the side of the reservoir through which the piezometer tube is attached. This is positioned perpendicular to the wall to avoid measurement errors due to velocity effects.
- **Vertical Glass Tube (B–C):** A transparent, calibrated (graduated) tube typically 5 mm to 15 mm in diameter. It must be perfectly vertical for accurate readings.
- **Free Liquid Surface (C):** The upper end of the fluid column, which is open to the atmosphere. The height 'h' is measured from the centerline of the pipe (point A) up to this free surface.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Step by Step Logic

1. The piezometer tube is fixed vertically and connected to the pipe/reservoir at point A.
2. The fluid in the pipe exerts a pressure $P_A$ at the connection point.
3. This pressure pushes the same fluid upward into the vertical glass tube.
4. The fluid rises against gravity until equilibrium is reached.
5. At equilibrium, the weight of the fluid column in the tube (acting downward) exactly balances the pressure $P_A$ (acting upward at the base).
6. The height 'h' of the fluid column is read from the graduated scale.
7. The gauge pressure at point A is then computed using the **hydrostatic pressure equation**.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Equation

Consider a small fluid element at the base of the column (point A). Two forces act on it:
- **Pressure Force (Upward):** $P_A \times A$ (where A is cross-sectional area of the tube)
- **Weight Force (Downward):** $\rho \times g \times h \times A$ (mass × gravity, where mass = ρ × A × h)

At equilibrium:

$$P_A \times A = \rho g h A$$

The 'A' cancels out, giving the elegant result:

$$P_A = \rho g h$$

This means **the pressure depends only on the height of the column, NOT on the cross-sectional area or shape of the tube** — this is known as the **hydrostatic paradox**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - Piezometers are used in **dam inspection** to measure water pressure at the base of the dam.
> - In **boilers and pressure vessels**, piezometer taps are provided for low-pressure local measurements.
> - In **civil engineering lab experiments** on Bernoulli's apparatus, piezometers are used to measure static pressure heads at different points along a flow channel.
> - **Limitation:** They cannot measure high pressures (tube would need to be impractically tall) and cannot be used for gases (gas columns are too light to give visible heights).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{gauge} = \rho g h$ | Gauge pressure from piezometer height | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $P_{gauge} = \gamma h$ | Using specific weight γ = ρg | N/m² |
| 3 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + \rho g h$ | Absolute pressure from piezometer | Pa |
| 4 | $h = \dfrac{P_{gauge}}{\rho g}$ | Height of fluid column for given pressure | m |
| 5 | $P_1 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \rho g z_2$ | Hydrostatic law (used to derive piezometer equation) | Pa |
| 6 | $\rho_{Hg} = 13.6 \times \rho_{water}$ | Standard mercury-to-water density ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_{atm} = 10.33 \, m \text{ of water} = 760 \, mm \text{ of Hg}$ | Standard atmospheric pressure | varies |
| 8 | $P_{abs} \geq 0$ | Physical constraint (absolute pressure cannot be negative) | Pa |

### 2.5 Limitations of a Piezometer (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams)

1. It can only measure **gauge pressure**, not vacuum.
2. It is **inadequate for gases** (the height of gas column would be impracticably large).
3. It requires **sufficient height** for high-pressure fluids.
4. It is **fragile** (glass tube can break easily).
5. Readings may be affected by **capillary rise** if the tube is very narrow (typically < 5 mm diameter).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Piezometer Equation

We will rigorously derive $P_A = \rho g h$ from first principles using Newton's laws of statics.

**Setup:** Consider a piezometer tube attached to a pipe at point A. The fluid rises to a height 'h' above point A in the vertical glass tube. The free surface of the fluid in the tube is exposed to atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$.

**Step 1: Apply the basic hydrostatic principle**

The pressure at any point in a static fluid depends only on the depth below the free surface. The fundamental equation is:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$$

where 'z' is the vertical elevation (positive upward).

**Step 2: Integrate between two points**

Integrating from the free surface (point C) where the pressure is $P_{atm}$ and elevation is $z_C = h$, to the connection point A where the pressure is $P_A$ and elevation is $z_A = 0$:

$$\int_{P_{atm}}^{P_A} dP = -\int_{h}^{0} \rho g \, dz$$

**Step 3: Perform the integration**

$$P_A - P_{atm} = -\rho g \, (0 - h) = \rho g h$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to get the gauge pressure**

$$P_A - P_{atm} = \rho g h$$

Since gauge pressure is defined as pressure *above* atmospheric:

$$P_{gauge} = P_A - P_{atm} = \rho g h$$

**Step 5: Alternative derivation via force balance**

Consider the fluid column of height 'h' and cross-sectional area 'A' inside the piezometer tube. Two vertical forces act on this column:

- **Upward force** at the base (point A): $F_{up} = P_A \cdot A$
- **Downward force** (weight of column + atmospheric pressure on top): $F_{down} = (P_{atm} \cdot A) + (W)$ where $W = \rho \cdot V \cdot g = \rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g$

For static equilibrium, $\Sigma F_{vertical} = 0$:

$$P_A \cdot A - P_{atm} \cdot A - \rho A h g = 0$$

Dividing the entire equation by A:

$$P_A - P_{atm} = \rho g h$$

Hence:

$$P_{gauge} = P_A - P_{atm} = \rho g h$$

**Step 6: Express in terms of specific weight**

Since specific weight $\gamma = \rho g$, we can compactly write:

$$P_{gauge} = \gamma h$$

**Final Piezometer Equation:**

$$\boxed{P_{gauge} = \rho g h = \gamma h}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

#### Example 1: Basic Pressure Calculation (Direct Application)

**Problem:** A piezometer is connected to a water pipe. The water rises to a height of 2.5 m in the piezometer tube. Calculate the gauge pressure at the connection point. Take ρ = 1000 kg/m³ and g = 9.81 m/s².

**Solution:**

$$\text{Given: } h = 2.5 \, m, \quad \rho = 1000 \, kg/m^3, \quad g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$$

$$\text{To find: } P_{gauge}$$

Apply the piezometer equation:

$$P_{gauge} = \rho g h$$

Substitute the values:

$$P_{gauge} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2.5$$

$$P_{gauge} = 24525 \, Pa$$

$$P_{gauge} = 24.525 \, kPa$$

**Answer:** The gauge pressure at the connection point is **24.525 kPa**.

#### Example 2: Calculation of Absolute Pressure

**Problem:** If the atmospheric pressure at the location is 101.3 kPa, determine the absolute pressure in the pipe for Example 1.

**Solution:**

Using the relationship:

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$

Substitute:

$$P_{abs} = 101300 + 24525$$

$$P_{abs} = 125825 \, Pa = 125.825 \, kPa$$

**Answer:** The absolute pressure is **125.825 kPa**.

#### Example 3: Height Calculation (Inverse Problem)

**Problem:** A pipeline carries oil of specific gravity 0.85. A piezometer attached to it shows a height of 1.8 m. Calculate the gauge pressure and express it in N/m².

**Solution:**

$$\text{Given: } SG = 0.85, \quad h = 1.8 \, m, \quad \rho_{water} = 1000 \, kg/m^3$$

Density of oil:

$$\rho_{oil} = SG \times \rho_{water} = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \, kg/m^3$$

Gauge pressure:

$$P_{gauge} = \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h = 850 \times 9.81 \times 1.8$$

$$P_{gauge} = 15004.83 \, Pa \approx 15.005 \, kPa$$

**Answer:** The gauge pressure of oil is **15.005 kPa (or 15004.83 N/m²)**.

#### Example 4: Multi-fluid Analysis at Depth

**Problem:** A tank contains mercury (SG = 13.6) at the bottom with a 0.5 m layer, then water (SG = 1.0) of 1.0 m height above it. A piezometer open to atmosphere is connected at the bottom. Find the height to which water will rise in the piezometer tube (the piezometer tube is filled with water from the tank connection point to the free surface).

**Solution:**

Pressure at the connection point A (gauge):

$$P_A = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg} + \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h_{water}$$

$$P_A = (13.6 \times 1000)(9.81)(0.5) + (1000)(9.81)(1.0)$$

$$P_A = 66708 + 9810 = 76518 \, Pa$$

If the piezometer tube contains only water rising to height h_w:

$$\rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h_w = P_A$$

$$h_w = \dfrac{76518}{1000 \times 9.81} = 7.8 \, m$$

**Answer:** Water rises to a height of **7.8 m** in the piezometer.

#### Example 5: Comparison of Pressure Heads in Different Fluids

**Problem:** A pressure of 50 kPa (gauge) is to be measured. What would be the height of: (a) water column, (b) mercury column required in a piezometer?

**Solution:**

$$P = 50000 \, Pa$$

(a) Water column height:

$$h_w = \dfrac{P}{\rho_w \cdot g} = \dfrac{50000}{1000 \times 9.81} = 5.097 \, m$$

(b) Mercury column height:

$$h_{Hg} = \dfrac{P}{\rho_{Hg} \cdot g} = \dfrac{50000}{13600 \times 9.81} = 0.375 \, m = 375 \, mm$$

**Answer:** Water column = **5.097 m**; Mercury column = **375 mm**. This is why mercury is used in manometers — it gives shorter, more practical column heights.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Piezometer Functional Architecture

The following diagram illustrates the **functional flow architecture** of pressure measurement using a piezometer system, showing how the components interact to produce a readable pressure value.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reservoir or Pipe A] --> B[Pressure Point A at Centerline]
    B --> C[Connecting Nozzle Ferrule]
    C --> D[Vertical Glass Tube Section B to C]
    D --> E[Liquid Column of Height h]
    E --> F[Free Liquid Surface C open to atmosphere]
    F --> G[Atmospheric Pressure P_atm acts on free surface]
    E --> H[Hydrostatic Equilibrium Condition]
    H --> I[Force Balance: P_a x A equals rho g h x A]
    I --> J[Simplified Equation: P_gauge equals rho g h]
    J --> K[Read Height h from Graduated Scale]
    K --> L[Substitute rho and g values]
    L --> M[Final Pressure Value in Pascals]
    M --> N[Add P_atm to get Absolute Pressure]
    N --> O[Output: Pressure Report in kPa or N per m squared]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Diagram — Measurement Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Identify measurement point on pipe] --> P2[Step 2: Drill and tap perpendicular to flow]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Connect vertical glass tube]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Allow fluid to rise to equilibrium]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Read height h from scale]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Apply formula P equals rho g h]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Record and report pressure]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Types of Pressure Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V[Vacuum Zero Reference] --> AB[Absolute Pressure Scale]
    AT[Atmospheric Pressure P_atm] --> AB
    AB --> GA[Region 1: Positive Gauge Pressure]
    AT --> VA[Region 2: Vacuum Pressure negative gauge]
    GA --> PZ1[Piezometer CAN measure this region]
    VA --> PZ2[Piezometer CANNOT measure this region]
    PZ1 --> OUT1[Output: h positive upward]
    PZ2 --> OUT2[Output: Requires inverted tube or different manometer]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description of the Piezometer Setup

A typical piezometer installation consists of:

- **Horizontal Pipe (at the bottom):** Carries the fluid under pressure.
- **Vertical Glass Tube (rising upward):** Transparent, graduated in mm.
- **Free Surface at Top:** Exposed to atmospheric pressure.
- **Height 'h':** Measured from the centerline of the pipe (point A) to the free surface of the liquid in the tube.
- **The fluid in the tube is the SAME as the fluid in the pipe** (this is a defining feature of a piezometer).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define a piezometer. State its two main limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

A piezometer is a simple device used for measuring the gauge pressure of a fluid at a point. It consists of a vertical glass tube, open at the top to the atmosphere, and connected at the bottom to the vessel or pipe containing the fluid whose pressure is to be measured. The fluid rises in the tube to a height 'h' such that the weight of the column balances the pressure at the connection point. The gauge pressure is given by $P = \rho g h$.

**Limitations:**
1. It can measure **only gauge pressure**, not vacuum pressure or absolute pressure directly.
2. It is **not suitable for measuring gas pressures** because gas columns are extremely light and would require impractically tall tubes.
3. It is **fragile** and cannot be used for high pressures due to height limitations.

---

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**A piezometer shows a water column of 3.2 m. Find the gauge pressure in kPa. Take ρ = 1000 kg/m³ and g = 9.81 m/s².**

**Model Answer:**

$$P_{gauge} = \rho g h = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 3.2$$

$$P_{gauge} = 31392 \, Pa = 31.392 \, kPa$$

**Answer: 31.392 kPa**

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the fundamental equation for pressure measurement using a piezometer, clearly stating the assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A pipe carrying oil of specific gravity 0.85 has a piezometer tube attached. The oil rises to a height of 4.2 m in the tube. Calculate: (i) the gauge pressure, (ii) the absolute pressure at that point. Take atmospheric pressure = 100 kPa.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The fluid is in static equilibrium (no acceleration).
2. The fluid is incompressible (constant density).
3. The fluid is a single homogeneous substance (no layered fluids in the tube).
4. The piezometer tube is perfectly vertical.
5. Temperature effects on density are negligible.

**Derivation:**

Let point A be the point on the pipe where pressure is to be measured, and point C be the free surface of the liquid in the piezometer tube. Let the vertical distance between A and C be 'h'.

Apply the hydrostatic equation:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$$

Integrate from A (where pressure is $P_A$ and elevation is 0) to C (where pressure is $P_{atm}$ and elevation is h):

$$\int_{P_A}^{P_{atm}} dP = -\int_0^h \rho g \, dz$$

$$P_{atm} - P_A = -\rho g h$$

$$P_A - P_{atm} = \rho g h$$

Since $P_{gauge} = P_A - P_{atm}$:

$$\boxed{P_{gauge} = \rho g h}$$

This is the fundamental piezometer equation.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** SG = 0.85, h = 4.2 m, P_atm = 100 kPa = 100,000 Pa

**Step 1:** Calculate density of oil:

$$\rho_{oil} = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \, kg/m^3$$

**Step 2:** Calculate gauge pressure:

$$P_{gauge} = \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h = 850 \times 9.81 \times 4.2$$

$$P_{gauge} = 35025.42 \, Pa \approx 35.025 \, kPa$$

**Step 3:** Calculate absolute pressure:

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge} = 100 + 35.025 = 135.025 \, kPa$$

**Final Answers:** (i) Gauge pressure = **35.025 kPa**, (ii) Absolute pressure = **135.025 kPa**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) - Alternative Choice:** [KTU University Exam - June 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a piezometer. Mention any four limitations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pressure of 70 kPa (gauge) is to be measured using a piezometer. Find: (i) the height of the water column required, (ii) the height of the mercury column required. Take ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³ and ρ_Hg = 13600 kg/m³.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Construction:** (Refer to schematic in Section 4)
A piezometer consists of a vertical glass tube of small bore (typically 5 to 15 mm) which is open at the top to the atmosphere. The lower end is connected to the pipe or vessel containing the fluid under measurement, through a small ferrule fitted perpendicular to the wall. The tube is graduated in mm for direct height reading.

**Working:**
The fluid pressure at the connection point pushes the same fluid up the vertical tube. The fluid rises until equilibrium is established. The height 'h' of the fluid column above the centerline of the pipe is a direct measure of the gauge pressure at that point, given by $P = \rho g h$.

**Limitations (any four):**
1. Measures only gauge pressure, not vacuum.
2. Unsuitable for gas pressure measurement.
3. Impractical for high pressures (tube becomes too tall).
4. Fragile glass construction.
5. Capillary effect errors in narrow tubes.
6. Cannot be used where the fluid is highly viscous or contains suspended solids.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** P = 70 kPa = 70,000 Pa

**(i) Height of water column:**

$$h_w = \dfrac{P}{\rho_w \cdot g} = \dfrac{70000}{1000 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_w = 7.1356 \, m \approx 7.14 \, m$$

**(ii) Height of mercury column:**

$$h_{Hg} = \dfrac{P}{\rho_{Hg} \cdot g} = \dfrac{70000}{13600 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_{Hg} = 0.5246 \, m = 524.6 \, mm$$

**Final Answers:** (i) Water column = **7.14 m**, (ii) Mercury column = **524.6 mm**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Many students use g = 9.8 instead of 9.81, or forget to convert kPa to Pa. This costs **1 mark per occurrence**.
> 2. **Confusing gauge and absolute pressure:** The piezometer measures **gauge pressure only**. If the question asks for absolute pressure, you MUST add atmospheric pressure. Failure to do so = **2 marks lost**.
> 3. **Forgetting the specific gravity factor:** When the fluid is NOT water, you must calculate $\rho_{fluid} = SG \times 1000$ first. Using ρ = 1000 for oil = **3 marks lost**.
> 4. **Sign convention error:** Never report a negative gauge pressure from a piezometer — a negative value means vacuum, and the piezometer cannot measure it. State this limitation clearly.
> 5. **Wrong atmospheric pressure value:** Use 101.325 kPa (standard) or as given in the problem. Using 10.33 m of water as a height (without proper conversion) is a frequent error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Points — Must Remember for KTU Exam:**
> - A piezometer is the **simplest pressure measuring device** — a vertical open tube.
> - The fundamental equation is: **$P_{gauge} = \rho g h = \gamma h$**
> - The fluid in the piezometer is **SAME as the fluid in the pipe** (defining feature).
> - Piezometer measures **GAUGE pressure only** (not absolute, not vacuum).
> - The pressure is **independent of tube cross-section** (hydrostatic paradox).
> - For absolute pressure, add: $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + \rho g h$
> - Density of water = **1000 kg/m³**, mercury = **13600 kg/m³**, SG_Hg = **13.6**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: **101.325 kPa ≈ 10.33 m of water ≈ 760 mm of Hg**
> - **Piezometer cannot be used for gases** (column height would be impractically large).
> - For high pressures, mercury is preferred (shorter column height).
> - Always use **g = 9.81 m/s²** unless specified otherwise.
> - Capillary effect: avoid tube diameter < **5 mm** to prevent measurement errors.
> - The tube must be installed **perpendicular to the flow** to avoid velocity pressure artifacts.
> - Pressure head 'h' is measured from the **centerline of the pipe**, not the pipe wall.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Manometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Manometer** is a device used for the precise measurement of fluid pressure (static, gauge, or differential) by balancing the column of fluid under test against the column of a known reference fluid (manometric fluid) under gravitational equilibrium.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, a manometer is fundamentally a **U-tube (or modified U-tube) hydrostatic instrument** that operates on the principle of **Pascal's Law of Transmission of Fluid Pressure** and the **Hydrostatic Pressure Equation**:

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h = \gamma \cdot h$$

where:
- $P$ = Pressure exerted by the fluid column (in Pa or N/m²)
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (in kg/m³)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $h$ = Height of the fluid column (in m)
- $\gamma$ = Specific weight of the fluid (in N/m³)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Manometers fall under the **"Fluid Statics"** domain of Module 1. Every problem-solving question will test your ability to write the **manometric equation** correctly by tracing pressure changes along a continuous fluid path, **adding** pressure when moving *downward* and **subtracting** when moving *upward*.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine two children sitting on a **seesaw**. The seesaw balances only when the products of weight and distance on both sides are equal. Similarly, a manometer is like a fluid seesaw — pressure at one point (say, inside a pipe) tries to push the fluid *down* on one side, while atmospheric pressure pushes *up* on the other side. The fluid finds its equilibrium height. By **measuring the height difference**, we can **decode the unknown pressure** — exactly like reading how far one child is heavier than the other from the tilt angle of the seesaw.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Context:** Manometers are indispensable in **boiler pressure measurement**, **blood pressure apparatus (sphygmomanometer)**, **gas pipeline pressure monitoring**, and **aerospace altitude measurement**. They are preferred over mechanical gauges in applications requiring **high accuracy and zero electrical dependence**.

### 1.3 Classification of Manometers

Manometers are broadly classified into two categories:

**A. Simple Manometers** — measure pressure at a single point.
1. **Piezometer**
2. **U-Tube Manometer**
3. **Single Column Manometer (Inclined Tube)**

**B. Differential Manometers** — measure the pressure difference between two points.
1. **U-Tube Differential Manometer**
2. **Inverted U-Tube Differential Manometer**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

Every manometer works on **TWO** golden hydrostatic laws:

**Law 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure Variation:**
$$dP = -\rho \cdot g \cdot dz \quad \text{(pressure increases with depth)}$$

**Law 2 — Pascal's Law:**
Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted **undiminished** in all directions.

### 2.2 Type 1: Piezometer (Simplest Manometer)

A **piezometer** is a vertical glass tube open to atmosphere, connected directly to the vessel/pipe whose pressure is to be measured.

**Working Principle:** The fluid rises in the tube until the hydrostatic head of the risen fluid equals the pressure at the connection point.

**Pressure Equation:**
$$P_A = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

where $h$ is the height of the fluid column in the piezometer above the centerline.

**Limitations:**
- Cannot measure **negative (vacuum) pressures**
- Cannot be used for fluids that are **highly viscous** or **opaque** (e.g., oils with sediment)
- Impractical for **high pressures** (requires impractically tall tubes)

### 2.3 Type 2: U-Tube Manometer

A **U-shaped tube** containing a heavy immiscible **manometric fluid** (commonly Mercury, $\rho_{Hg} = 13,600$ kg/m³) is connected between the point of measurement and a reference.

**Working Principle:** When the pressure at point A ($P_A$) is greater than the reference, the manometric fluid is depressed on the A-side and rises on the reference side. The difference in mercury levels gives the pressure difference.

**Standard Pressure Equation:**
$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h$$

For gases (where $\rho_{gas} \ll \rho_{Hg}$, so $\rho_f$ is often neglected):
$$P_A - P_B \approx \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$

### 2.4 Type 3: Differential U-Tube Manometer

Used to find the **pressure difference between two points A and B** in a system. Both limbs are connected to the points under measurement.

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (z_A - z_B)$$

For horizontal pipes ($z_A = z_B$):
$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h$$

### 2.5 Type 4: Inverted U-Tube Manometer

Inverted type uses a **lighter fluid** than the flowing fluid (typically air or oil), making it suitable for measuring pressure differences in **liquids like water** where $\rho_m < \rho_f$.

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_f - \rho_m) \cdot g \cdot h$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 95% of KTU numerical problems on Manometers.**

| Sl. No. | Manometer Type | Governing Pressure Equation | Conditions / Use Case |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Piezometer** | $P_A = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Vertical open tube; measures gauge pressure |
| 2 | **Simple U-Tube** (gas) | $P_A - P_{atm} = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$ | $\rho_{gas} \ll \rho_m$, so $\rho_f$ neglected |
| 3 | **Simple U-Tube** (liquid) | $P_A = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot x + \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$ | Fluid is liquid with density $\rho_f$ above mercury |
| 4 | **Differential U-Tube** | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h$ | Horizontal pipe; $z_A = z_B$ |
| 5 | **Differential U-Tube** (general) | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (z_A - z_B)$ | Inclined/vertical pipe configuration |
| 6 | **Inverted U-Tube** | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_f - \rho_m) \cdot g \cdot h$ | Lighter manometric fluid than flowing liquid |
| 7 | **Inclined Single Column** | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot L \cdot \sin\theta$ | $\theta$ is angle of inclination; $L$ is length along slope |
| 8 | **Micromanometer** | $P_A - P_B = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h \left(1 + \frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$ | High-precision low-pressure measurement |

**Critical Notation Convention:**
- $\rho_m$ = density of manometric (heavy) fluid
- $\rho_f$ = density of flowing fluid
- $h$ = difference in manometric fluid levels
- $g$ = **9.81 m/s²** (universally used in KTU)

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Manometers are critical in:
- **Hydraulic systems** — measuring pump discharge pressure
- **Aerospace** — altitude indicators (aneroid + manometric principle)
- **Medical field** — sphygmomanometer (blood pressure)
- **Petrochemical plants** — flow measurement via differential manometer across orifice plates
- **HVAC** — building pressure monitoring
- **Marine engineering** — draft measurement of ships

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: General Manometric Equation (Tracing Pressure Path)

**Statement:** Starting from point A, as we move through a continuous fluid column in a manometer, pressure changes follow the hydrostatic rule.

**Starting Equation:** Let $P_A$ be the pressure at point A (unknown). We traverse the manometer tube and arrive at a known reference (usually atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$ or another known point).

**Rule 1 — Downward movement:** Pressure **increases** by $\rho \cdot g \cdot h$
$$P_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{old}} + \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Rule 2 — Upward movement:** Pressure **decreases** by $\rho \cdot g \cdot h$
$$P_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{old}} - \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Rule 3 — Across the same horizontal level in the same continuous fluid:** Pressure **remains constant**.

### 3.2 Worked Derivation: U-Tube Manometer with Two Liquids

**Problem Setup:** A pipe carrying oil (specific gravity = 0.8) is connected to a U-tube manometer containing mercury. The mercury level on the pipe side is **100 mm** below the pipe centerline, and on the open side is **150 mm** above the same reference. Find the gauge pressure at the pipe centerline.

**Given Data:**
- $SG_{oil} = 0.8 \Rightarrow \rho_{oil} = 800$ kg/m³
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13,600$ kg/m³
- $h_1$ (oil column above mercury on pipe side) = 0.1 m
- $h_2$ (mercury level difference) = 0.15 m

**Step 1 — Identify starting and ending points:**
Start at pipe center A ($P_A$ = unknown), end at open end ($P_{atm}$ = 0 gauge).

**Step 2 — Apply pressure tracing from A to atmosphere:**

Tracing from A downward through oil (pressure increases):
$$P_1 = P_A + \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h_1$$

Tracing from A downward by $h_1 + h_2$ to lower mercury level, then across to higher mercury level:
$$P_1 = P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_2$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions:**
$$P_A + \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h_1 = P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P_A$ (using gauge pressure, so $P_{atm} = 0$):**
$$P_A = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_2 - \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h_1$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$P_A = (13,600)(9.81)(0.15) - (800)(9.81)(0.1)$$

$$P_A = 20,012.4 - 784.8$$

$$P_A = 19,227.6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_A \approx 19.23 \text{ kPa (gauge)}}$$

### 3.3 Worked Derivation: Differential U-Tube Manometer (Inclined Pipe)

**Problem Setup:** Two points A and B in an inclined pipe (carrying water) are connected to a differential mercury manometer. The mercury level difference is **200 mm**. Vertical distance between A and B is **150 mm**, with A being higher.

**Given:**
- $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13,600$ kg/m³
- $h = 0.2$ m
- $z_A - z_B = 0.15$ m (A is higher, so $z_A > z_B$)

**Step 1 — Trace pressure from A to B through the manometer:**

Moving from A through water downward to mercury interface on A-side:
$$P_{A1} = P_A + \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot y_1$$

where $y_1$ is the depth from A to mercury interface on A-side.

Moving through mercury from low side to high side (crosses the level difference $h$):
$$P_{B1} = P_{A1} - \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$$

Moving from mercury interface on B-side upward to point B:
$$P_B = P_{B1} - \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot y_2$$

where $y_2$ is the height from mercury interface to B.

**Step 2 — Recognize geometric relationship:**
$$y_1 - y_2 = (z_A - z_B) + h = 0.15 + 0.2 = 0.35 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Combine all equations algebraically:**
$$P_B = P_A + \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot y_1 - \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot y_2$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange:**
$$P_A - P_B = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot (y_1 - y_2)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $y_1 - y_2$ expression:**
$$P_A - P_B = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot [(z_A - z_B) + h]$$

**Step 6 — Group terms:**
$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_{water}) \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot (z_A - z_B)$$

**Step 7 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$P_A - P_B = (13,600 - 1000)(9.81)(0.2) - (1000)(9.81)(0.15)$$

$$P_A - P_B = (12,600)(9.81)(0.2) - (1000)(9.81)(0.15)$$

$$P_A - P_B = 24,721.2 - 1,471.5$$

$$P_A - P_B = 23,249.7 \text{ Pa} \approx 23.25 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B \approx 23.25 \text{ kPa}}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (Which Manometer to Use?)

> [!NOTE]
> **This matrix is a high-yield KTU tool** — use it to pick the correct formula before solving.

| Situation / Given Condition | Recommended Manometer | Key Reason |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Measure pressure in a single pipe at one point, low pressure | **Piezometer** | Simplest, no manometric fluid needed |
| Gas pressure measurement, large pressure magnitude | **U-Tube with Mercury** | Mercury's high density gives compact $h$ values |
| Pressure difference between two points | **Differential U-Tube** | Directly reads $\Delta P$ |
| Flowing fluid is heavy (e.g., oil), small pressure diff | **Inverted U-Tube** | Lighter fluid (air) gives larger reading |
| Very small pressure differences required with high precision | **Micromanometer** or **Inclined** | Magnifies small $h$ via slope geometry |
| Pipe is inaccessible / vibrating / remote | **Remote-reading manometer** with transducer | Mechanical isolation |

### 3.5 Quick Sanity Check Formula

For any manometer problem, always verify by checking **units consistency**:

$$\left[\rho \text{ in kg/m}^3\right] \times \left[g \text{ in m/s}^2\right] \times \left[h \text{ in m}\right] = \left[\text{N/m}^2 = \text{Pa}\right] \checkmark$$

To convert Pa to other common units:
- $1 \text{ kPa} = 10^3 \text{ Pa}$
- $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $1 \text{ m of water column} = 9810 \text{ Pa}$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Classification & Functional Architecture of Manometers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Fluid Pressure Measurement]) --> nodeClass{Simple or Differential?}
    nodeClass -->|Simple| nodeSimple[P = gauge at ONE point]
    nodeClass -->|Differential| nodeDiff[P diff between TWO points]

    nodeSimple --> nodePZ[Piezometer]
    nodeSimple --> nodeU[U Tube Manometer]
    nodeSimple --> nodeInc[Inclined Single Column]

    nodePZ --> nodePZdesc[Vertical open tube. P equals rho g h. Used for low pressure liquids.]

    nodeU --> nodeUdesc[U tube with heavy fluid mercury. Reads rho Hg g h. Industry standard.]

    nodeInc --> nodeIncdesc[Tube inclined at angle theta. Reads rho g L sin theta. High accuracy for low P.]

    nodeDiff --> nodeDiffU[Differential U Tube]
    nodeDiff --> nodeInvU[Inverted U Tube]

    nodeDiffU --> nodeDiffUdesc[Two points A and B connected. Heavy fluid mercury in U. P_A minus P_B equals rho_m minus rho_f times g h.]

    nodeInvU --> nodeInvUdesc[Inverted geometry. Light fluid air or oil. P_A minus P_B equals rho_f minus rho_m times g h.]

    nodePZdesc -.-> nodeFinal([Output Pressure Reading])
    nodeUdesc -.-> nodeFinal
    nodeIncdesc -.-> nodeFinal
    nodeDiffUdesc -.-> nodeFinal
    nodeInvUdesc -.-> nodeFinal

    style nodeStart fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeFinal fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeClass fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Pressure Tracing Algorithm (How KTU Examiners Want You to Solve)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1([Start at Point A with P_A unknown]) --> step2[Identify endpoint and reference P]
    step2 --> step3[Choose tracing direction through manometer]
    step3 --> step4{Direction of motion?}
    step4 -->|Downward| step5[ADD rho times g times h]
    step4 -->|Upward| step6[SUBTRACT rho times g times h]
    step4 -->|Same level same fluid| step7[No pressure change]
    step5 --> step8[Apply at each fluid interface]
    step6 --> step8
    step7 --> step8
    step8 --> step9[Equate to known reference P]
    step9 --> step10[Solve algebraically for unknown P]
    step10 --> step11([Final Answer in Pa or kPa])

    style step1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style step11 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style step4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Differential Manometer Operation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PipeA as Point A in Pipe
    participant TubeA as Left Limb of U Tube
    participant Mercury as Mercury Column
    participant TubeB as Right Limb of U Tube
    participant PipeB as Point B in Pipe
    participant Reader as Observer/Examiner

    PipeA->>TubeA: High pressure pushes mercury down on A side
    Note over Mercury: Mercury level on B side rises by h
    TubeA->>TubeB: Pressure equalizes at lower mercury level
    PipeB->>TubeB: Lower pressure at B allows mercury rise
    Reader->>Reader: Measure vertical mercury difference h
    Reader->>Reader: Apply formula P_A minus P_B equals rho_Hg minus rho_f times g times h
    Reader-->>Reader: Calculate pressure difference in Pascals
```

### 4.4 Schematic Textual Diagram: U-Tube Manometer Layout (Convention)

```
        ATMOSPHERE (Reference)
              |
              |  ↑ Open limb
              |
              |   ___ 
              |  |   |
              |  |   |  ← (a) Air column above mercury
              |  |   |
              |  |   |—————————  ← Upper mercury level (Right side)
              |  |   |   *
              |  |   |   *  ← (b) Mercury column, height = h
              |  |   |   *
              |  |   |—————————  ← Lower mercury level (Left side)
              |  |   |
              |  |___|
              |  ↑
              |  Pipe carrying fluid (e.g., oil)
              |
              A (Center of pipe, unknown pressure P_A)
              
      Notation:
      ───────────────────────────────
      ρ_f = density of flowing fluid
      ρ_m = density of manometric fluid (mercury)
      h   = vertical difference in mercury levels
      ───────────────────────────────
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define a manometer and state its principle of working. Mention two limitations of a piezometer.

**Model Answer:**

A manometer is a device used to measure fluid pressure by balancing the fluid column against a known reference (typically atmospheric pressure), based on the principle of **hydrostatic equilibrium** ($P = \rho g h$) and **Pascal's Law**.

**Limitations of a piezometer:**
1. It cannot measure **negative (vacuum) gauge pressures** because fluid cannot be pulled upward indefinitely.
2. It cannot be used for **highly viscous or opaque fluids** like heavy oils, because visual observation of the fluid column becomes impossible and viscous drag may retard the rise.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q:** Distinguish between a simple U-tube manometer and a differential U-tube manometer. State one practical application of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Simple U-Tube Manometer | Differential U-Tube Manometer |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Purpose | Measures pressure at a **single point** | Measures pressure difference between **two points** |
| Connection | One end connected to vessel; other end open | Both ends connected to two separate points |
| Reading | Gives gauge pressure directly | Gives pressure drop $\Delta P$ |
| Application | Boiler gas pressure measurement | Pressure drop across orifice meter in pipe flow |

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice**

---

#### **QUESTION A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A pipe carrying oil of specific gravity **0.85** is connected to a U-tube mercury manometer. The mercury level in the limb connected to the pipe is **80 mm below** the pipe center, while the mercury level in the open limb is **120 mm above** the same reference. The manometer reads a mercury difference of **200 mm**. Determine the **gauge pressure at the pipe center** in kPa. **(7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**
- $SG_{oil} = 0.85 \Rightarrow \rho_{oil} = 850$ kg/m³
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13,600$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Oil column above mercury (pipe side): $h_1 = 0.08$ m
- Mercury level difference: $h = 0.2$ m
- Open limb mercury above reference: 0.12 m

**Step 1 — Apply hydrostatic pressure tracing from pipe center A (P_A) to atmosphere:**

Tracing from A **downward** through oil column:
$$P_{at\ mercury\ level\ A} = P_A + \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h_1$$

Tracing from mercury level A-side **upward** to open end:
$$P_{atm} = P_{at\ mercury\ level\ A} - \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2 — Combine:**
$$P_{atm} = P_A + \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h_1 - \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $P_A$ (using $P_{atm} = 0$ for gauge):**
$$P_A = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h_1$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$P_A = (13,600)(9.81)(0.2) - (850)(9.81)(0.08)$$

$$P_A = 26,683.2 - 666.9$$

$$\boxed{P_A = 26,016.3 \text{ Pa} \approx 26.02 \text{ kPa (gauge)}}$$

---

**(b)** Two pressure taps A and B in a horizontal water pipeline are **2 m apart**. A differential mercury manometer connected across A and B shows a mercury level difference of **100 mm**. Calculate the **pressure drop between A and B** in kPa, and the **head loss** in meters of water. **(7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13,600$ kg/m³
- $h = 0.1$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Horizontal pipe $\Rightarrow z_A = z_B$

**Step 1 — Apply differential manometer formula (horizontal pipe):**
$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_{water}) \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$P_A - P_B = (13,600 - 1000)(9.81)(0.1)$$

$$P_A - P_B = (12,600)(9.81)(0.1)$$

$$P_A - P_B = 12,360.6 \text{ Pa} \approx 12.36 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to head of water:**
$$h_{water} = \frac{P_A - P_B}{\rho_{water} \cdot g} = \frac{12,360.6}{1000 \times 9.81} = 1.26 \text{ m of water}$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B \approx 12.36 \text{ kPa} \quad \text{and} \quad h_L \approx 1.26 \text{ m of water}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often forget that the **water column on both sides** contributes equally to the pressure difference in a horizontal pipe differential manometer, so the water term **cancels out**. Only the mercury level difference matters. Always write the formula with $(\rho_m - \rho_f)$ — never $\rho_m$ alone when the flowing fluid is a liquid.

---

#### **QUESTION B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** A simple U-tube manometer containing mercury is connected to a gas pipeline. The mercury level in the open limb is **150 mm higher** than in the limb connected to the gas. Atmospheric pressure is **101.325 kPa**. Find the **absolute pressure** of the gas in the pipeline. Take $\rho_{Hg} = 13,600$ kg/m³. **(7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $h = 0.15$ m (open limb is HIGHER, meaning gas pressure < atmospheric)
- $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13,600$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Identify scenario:**
Since open limb mercury is **higher**, the gas pressure is **lower than atmospheric** (vacuum gauge pressure). The gas is "sucking" the mercury up.

**Step 2 — Write manometric equation (from gas to atmosphere):**
$$P_{gas} + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h = P_{atm}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $P_{gas}$:**
$$P_{gas} = P_{atm} - \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$P_{gas} = 101,325 - (13,600)(9.81)(0.15)$$

$$P_{gas} = 101,325 - 20,012.4$$

$$\boxed{P_{gas} = 81,312.6 \text{ Pa} \approx 81.31 \text{ kPa (absolute)}}$$

**Gauge Pressure:** $P_{gauge} = P_{gas} - P_{atm} = -20.01$ kPa (i.e., 20.01 kPa vacuum)

---

**(b)** An **inverted U-tube differential manometer** is used to measure the pressure drop across a horizontal pipe carrying oil of specific gravity **0.9**. The manometric fluid is air with specific weight **12 N/m³**. The difference in air column heights in the two limbs is **250 mm**. Determine the **pressure difference** between the two sections of the pipe in kPa. **(7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $SG_{oil} = 0.9 \Rightarrow \rho_{oil} = 900$ kg/m³
- $\gamma_{air} = 12$ N/m³ $\Rightarrow \rho_{air} = 12/9.81 = 1.223$ kg/m³
- $h = 0.25$ m (height difference in air columns)
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Horizontal pipe $\Rightarrow z_A = z_B$

**Step 1 — Apply inverted U-tube manometer formula:**
$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_{oil} - \rho_{air}) \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$P_A - P_B = (900 - 1.223)(9.81)(0.25)$$

$$P_A - P_B = (898.777)(9.81)(0.25)$$

$$P_A - P_B = 2,204.55 \text{ Pa} \approx 2.205 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B \approx 2.205 \text{ kPa}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** In **inverted U-tube manometers**, students frequently reverse the formula and write $(\rho_m - \rho_f)$ instead of $(\rho_f - \rho_m)$. Remember: in inverted type, the **flowing fluid is heavier** than the manometric fluid, so the difference is $(\rho_f - \rho_m)$. Always draw a free-body sketch and trace pressures before plugging numbers.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read before entering the exam hall.**

- **Core Principle:** Manometers work on **hydrostatic equilibrium**: $P = \rho g h$. No moving parts, no electrical energy required.

- **Universal Pressure Tracing Rule:**
  - Moving **downward** through a fluid of density $\rho$ by height $h$ → **add** $\rho g h$
  - Moving **upward** through a fluid of density $\rho$ by height $h$ → **subtract** $\rho g h$
  - Moving **horizontally** in the **same fluid** → **no change**

- **Sign Convention:**
  - If the manometric fluid is **higher on the open side** $\Rightarrow$ unknown pressure is **lower** than atmospheric (vacuum gauge).
  - If the manometric fluid is **higher on the unknown side** $\Rightarrow$ unknown pressure is **higher** than atmospheric (positive gauge).

- **Memorize the Big Four Formulas:**
  1. **Piezometer:** $P = \rho g h$
  2. **Simple U-tube (gas):** $P = \rho_m g h$
  3. **Differential U-tube:** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$
  4. **Inverted U-tube:** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_f - \rho_m) g h$

- **Standard Constants to Memorize:**
  - $\rho_{Hg} = 13,600$ kg/m³
  - $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³
  - $g = 9.81$ m/s²
  - $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa (or 101.3 kPa for quick calcs)

- **Common Mistake to Avoid:**
  - Do **not** confuse *absolute* and *gauge* pressures. A question asking for "gauge pressure" gives you $P_{atm} = 0$; for "absolute pressure," you must **add** $P_{atm}$ at the end.

- **Unit Conversion Shortcuts:**
  - $1 \text{ kPa} = 1000 \text{ Pa}$
  - $1 \text{ bar} = 100 \text{ kPa}$
  - $1 \text{ m of water column} = 9.81 \text{ kPa}$
  - $1 \text{ mm of Hg} = 133.32 \text{ Pa}$

- **When to Choose Which Manometer:**
  - **Gas + high pressure** → Mercury U-tube
  - **Liquid + low pressure** → Piezometer or inverted U-tube
  - **Pressure difference between two points** → Differential U-tube
  - **Very low pressure difference** → Inclined / Micromanometer

- **Always draw a free-body diagram** showing all fluid columns, fluid interfaces, and the direction of pressure tracing. KTU examiners award **2-3 marks** just for a clear diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pressure Gauges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **pressure gauge** (or pressure instrument) is a mechanical or electromechanical device engineered to measure the **intensity of fluid pressure** (gauge, absolute, vacuum, or differential) within a closed system or free atmosphere. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCAUT302, pressure gauges are classified into two broad functional families:

1. **Manometric Gauges (Hydrostatic Type):** Devices that balance the unknown fluid pressure against the weight of a column of a reference manometric fluid (typically **mercury**, **water**, or light oils). The hydrostatic equation $P = \rho g h$ forms their analytical core.
2. **Elastic/Mechanical Gauges (Elastic Deformation Type):** Devices that convert fluid pressure into measurable mechanical deformation (strain), which is then transduced into a pointer deflection or electrical signal. The **Bourdon tube gauge** is the most ubiquitous industrial example.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCAUT302, Module 1):**
> "Pressure measurement - Manometers: simple, differential, inverted, micro-manometer. Mechanical gauges: Bourdon tube, diaphragm, dead weight pressure gauge."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine **inflating a bicycle tire** by hand. You can feel the resistance in your palms — that "push-back" sensation is the air pressure. Now imagine you cannot feel the push well enough, or you need a precise number. You attach a mechanical gauge to the valve. Inside, a curved metal tube (the Bourdon tube) tries to straighten itself because of the air pressure inside, and that straightening motion moves a needle across a numbered dial.

In essence, **all pressure gauges are translators**: they take an invisible, formless force (pressure) and convert it into something a human or a computer can read — the **height of a fluid column** (manometer) or the **angular twist of a needle** (Bourdon gauge).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Gauge Pressure ($P_{gauge}$):** Measured relative to local atmospheric pressure. Zero is set at **101.325 kPa** of ambient air.
> - **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$):** Measured relative to a perfect vacuum. Always positive.
> - **Vacuum Pressure ($P_{vac}$):** Pressure below atmospheric. Always negative gauge pressure.
> - Relation: $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$

## 1.3 Standard Reference Constants (SI Units)

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101,325 Pa** | N/m² |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | m/s² |
| Density of Mercury (at 20°C) | $\rho_{Hg}$ | **13,600 kg/m³** | kg/m³ |
| Density of Water (at 4°C) | $\rho_{w}$ | **1,000 kg/m³** | kg/m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exam settings, always use **$\rho_{Hg} = 13.6 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/m}^3$** and **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$** unless explicitly stated otherwise. Using $g = 9.8$ may also be accepted.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Pressure Gauges (KTU Taxonomy)

The KTU 2024 syllabus strictly requires mastery of the following pressure measurement devices. They are categorized as follows:

### A. Hydrostatic / Manometric Pressure Gauges

A manometer is fundamentally a **U-tube (or variant) filled with a heavy reference liquid** that balances the unknown pressure against a known hydrostatic head.

- **Simple Manometer (Piezo-meter / Single Limb):** A vertical tube open to atmosphere at the top, connected to the vessel at the bottom. Measures the **gauge pressure** of the contained liquid directly.
- **U-Tube Manometer:** A U-shaped tube containing manometric fluid (mercury, water, etc.) connected to the point of measurement on one side and open to atmosphere (or another reference) on the other.
- **Differential Manometer:** A modified U-tube designed specifically to measure the **pressure difference** between two points in a system (e.g., across an orifice meter or a venturi).
- **Inverted U-Tube Manometer:** The reference fluid (typically oil or water) is **lighter** than the flowing fluid. The manometric fluid sits in the inverted upper section, used when measuring pressure in **water** systems where mercury is undesirable.
- **Micro-Manometer:** A highly sensitive instrument that uses an inclined tube or a small reservoir of large cross-section to amplify small pressure differences into large readable deflections.

### B. Elastic / Mechanical Pressure Gauges

- **Bourdon Tube Pressure Gauge:** A curved, flattened metallic tube of **elliptical cross-section**. Internal pressure tends to **straighten** the curved tube, and the tip movement is converted into rotary motion of a pointer.
- **Diaphragm Gauge:** A thin circular metal disc (corrugated or flat) deflects under pressure. The deflection is proportional to the pressure differential across the diaphragm.
- **Dead-Weight Pressure Gauge (Dead-Weight Tester):** A **primary standard** in pressure calibration. Known weights are loaded on a precision piston; the fluid pressure is generated by the piston-cylinder arrangement to balance these weights.

## 2.2 Operating Logic — Step-by-Step

### Logic Flow of a Bourdon Tube Gauge (Most KTU-Frequently Asked)
1. Fluid under pressure enters the fixed end of the curved Bourdon tube through a socket.
2. Internal pressure exerts force on the inner walls of the elliptical cross-section.
3. Because the cross-section is **elliptical, not circular**, the wall stiffness is non-uniform. The minor axis tries to expand more than the major axis.
4. The net effect is that the curved tube **tends to straighten** (uncoil).
5. The free (closed) end of the tube moves outward by a small displacement $\delta$.
6. This displacement is transmitted via a **mechanical linkage** (sector and pinion gear) to the pointer.
7. The pointer rotates across a calibrated dial, reading the pressure in **Pa, bar, psi, or kg/cm²**.

### Logic Flow of a Dead-Weight Tester
1. A precision piston of known effective area $A$ is inserted into a closely-fitted cylinder.
2. Known dead weights $W$ are loaded onto the piston.
3. Fluid (usually oil) is pumped into the cylinder below the piston, lifting the weights.
4. Equilibrium is reached when the upward fluid force exactly equals the total weight.
5. The **generated pressure** is calculated as $P = \dfrac{W}{A}$.
6. This pressure is applied to the gauge under calibration, serving as the **reference standard**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving KTU Part B numerical questions on pressure gauges.

| Device | Governing Equation | Variable Definitions | Typical Application |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Simple Manometer** | $P_A = \rho g h$ | $P_A$ = pressure at point A; $h$ = height of liquid column above A | Measuring pressure in open tanks |
| **U-Tube Manometer** | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | $\rho_m$ = manometric fluid density; $\rho_f$ = flowing fluid density; $h$ = differential column height | Measuring pressure in closed pipes |
| **Inverted U-Tube** | $P_A - P_B = h(\rho_f - \rho_o)$ | $\rho_o$ = light oil density; $\rho_f$ = flowing water density | Measuring small pressure differences in water systems |
| **Differential Manometer** | $P_1 - P_2 = h \left( \dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_f} - 1 \right) \rho_f g$ | $P_1, P_2$ = upstream and downstream pressures | Across orifice plates, venturi meters |
| **Micro-Manometer (Inclined)** | $P = \rho g L \sin \theta$ | $L$ = length along inclined tube; $\theta$ = inclination angle | Measuring very low gas pressures |
| **Bourdon Tube** | $P \propto \delta$ (Linear response in elastic limit) | $\delta$ = tip displacement of free end | Industrial pressure measurement |
| **Dead-Weight Tester** | $P = \dfrac{W}{A} = \dfrac{mg}{A}$ | $W$ = total dead weight; $A$ = piston effective area | Calibration standard |
| **Universal Pressure Relation** | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa at sea level | Converting between absolute and gauge |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** In the differential manometer formula above, $\rho_m$ refers to the **manometric fluid** (heavier, like mercury), while $\rho_f$ refers to the **flowing fluid** in the pipe (lighter, like water/oil). Mixing these up is the **#1 reason students lose 3–4 marks** in KTU Part B questions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bourdon Tube Gauges:** Standard in **steam boilers**, **hydraulic presses**, **gas cylinders**, and **air compressors** across every industry — from petroleum refineries to hospital medical gas lines.
- **Dead-Weight Testers:** Used in **national metrology institutes** (e.g., NABL-accredited labs in India) as the **gold standard** to certify industrial gauges before dispatch.
- **Inverted U-Tube Manometers:** Critical in **water treatment plants** and **HVAC chilled water systems** where contamination by mercury is unacceptable.
- **Micro-Manometers:** Used in **aerodynamic wind tunnels** and **building ventilation studies** where differential pressures are extremely small (often < 1 mm of water column).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pressure at a Point in a Static Fluid Column

**Problem Setup:** Consider a static incompressible fluid of density $\rho$ contained in a vessel. We wish to derive the pressure at a depth $h$ below the free surface.

### Step 1: Apply Newton's Second Law to a Fluid Element

Consider a vertical column of fluid of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$ extending from the free surface down to a point at depth $h$.

The forces acting on this column are:
- **Weight of the fluid column (downward):** $W = \rho g A h$
- **Pressure force at the top (downward):** $F_{top} = P_{atm} \cdot A$
- **Pressure force at the bottom (upward):** $F_{bottom} = P \cdot A$

### Step 2: Apply Static Equilibrium

Since the fluid is in static equilibrium, the net force is zero. The upward force at the bottom must balance the downward atmospheric pressure force plus the weight of the column.

$$P \cdot A = P_{atm} \cdot A + \rho g A h$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Equation

Divide both sides by the cross-sectional area $A$:

$$\boxed{P = P_{atm} + \rho g h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **fundamental hydrostatic pressure equation** from which all manometric derivations follow. It is the **most heavily tested** equation in Module 1 of PCAUT302.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: U-Tube Manometer Equation

**Problem Setup:** A pipe carrying a fluid of density $\rho_f$ has its pressure at point A measured using a U-tube manometer containing a manometric fluid of density $\rho_m$ (with $\rho_m > \rho_f$). The right limb of the manometer is open to the atmosphere. The manometric fluid shows a height difference of $h$ between the two limbs.

### Step 1: Identify Reference Levels

Let us mark the **lower meniscus** of the manometric fluid in the right limb as the **datum line (point X)**. The corresponding point in the left limb is at the same elevation (point X') and lies **inside the manometric fluid at the same horizontal level**.

Pressure at X (right limb, going down from atmosphere):
$$P_X = P_{atm} + \rho_m g \cdot 0 = P_{atm}$$
*(Because the right limb is open to atmosphere, and the right limb's fluid above point X is also manometric fluid, but for simplicity we choose X at the manometric fluid surface in the right limb. We can re-derive more rigorously below.)*

### Step 2: Rigorous Step-by-Step Pressure Tracing

Let $H$ be the vertical distance from point A to the manometric fluid interface in the left limb. Let $h$ be the vertical difference in manometric fluid levels between the two limbs.

Tracing the pressure from point A upward through the flowing fluid, then down through the manometric fluid to the datum level X in the right limb:

**Starting at A, going up by $H$ in flowing fluid:**
$$P_{interface, left} = P_A - \rho_f g H$$

**Going from the left interface down to the datum level (a distance of $h$ in manometric fluid):**
$$P_X = P_{interface, left} + \rho_m g h$$

**At the datum level X in the right limb (open to atmosphere):**
$$P_X = P_{atm}$$

### Step 3: Combine and Solve for $P_A$

Equating the two expressions for $P_X$:

$$P_A - \rho_f g H + \rho_m g h = P_{atm}$$

Rearranging for $P_A$:

$$\boxed{P_A = P_{atm} + \rho_f g H - \rho_m g h}$$

For the simplified case where $\rho_f \ll \rho_m$ (e.g., gas in pipe), the term $\rho_f g H$ is negligible, yielding the standard textbook form:

$$P_A \approx P_{atm} - \rho_m g h$$
*(if the gas pressure is below atmospheric; signs depend on orientation)*

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Differential Manometer — Pressure Drop Across a Pipe Section

**Problem Setup:** A differential U-tube manometer is connected across two points A and B in a horizontal pipe carrying water ($\rho_f = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$). The manometric fluid is mercury ($\rho_m = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$). The mercury level in the limb connected to A is **lower** by a height $h$ than in the limb connected to B. (This indicates that $P_A > P_B$.)

### Step 1: Define the Geometry

Let the centerline of the pipe be at the same elevation for both points A and B. Let the manometric fluid interface in limb A be at elevation $z_1$ and in limb B be at elevation $z_2$, with $z_1 - z_2 = h$.

### Step 2: Trace Pressure from A to the Datum

Starting at A, we move down the left limb through water to reach the manometric fluid interface:
$$P_1 = P_A + \rho_f g (z_{pipe} - z_1)$$

where $z_{pipe}$ is the elevation of the pipe centerline.

### Step 3: Cross the Mercury Column

Moving horizontally through the mercury does not change pressure. Moving from limb A up to the level $z_2$ in limb B (going up by $h$ through mercury):
$$P_2 = P_1 - \rho_m g h$$

### Step 4: Trace Pressure from B Back Up to the Datum

At limb B, the mercury interface is at $z_2$, and we move up to the pipe centerline:
$$P_2 = P_B + \rho_f g (z_{pipe} - z_2)$$

### Step 5: Equate the Two Expressions for $P_2$

$$P_A + \rho_f g (z_{pipe} - z_1) - \rho_m g h = P_B + \rho_f g (z_{pipe} - z_2)$$

The $z_{pipe}$ terms cancel. Since $z_1 - z_2 = h$, we have $-(z_1) - (-(z_2)) = -(z_1 - z_2) = -h$, so:

$$P_A - \rho_f g h - \rho_m g h = P_B$$

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_f g h + \rho_m g h$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = h g (\rho_m - \rho_f)}$$

This is the **canonical KTU differential manometer equation**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Dead-Weight Pressure Gauge — Generated Pressure

**Problem Setup:** A dead-weight pressure tester has a piston of effective cross-sectional area $A = 1/100 \text{ m}^2$ ($\frac{1}{100} \text{ m}^2 = 10 \text{ cm}^2$). A total mass of $M = 50 \text{ kg}$ (piston + weights) is loaded.

### Step 1: Identify Forces

The total downward force on the fluid is the weight of the piston + weights:
$$W = Mg = 50 \times 9.81 = 490.5 \text{ N}$$

The upward force from fluid pressure on the piston area:
$$F_{fluid} = P \cdot A$$

### Step 2: Apply Equilibrium

At balance, the fluid force exactly supports the dead weight:
$$P \cdot A = Mg$$

### Step 3: Solve for $P$

$$P = \dfrac{Mg}{A} = \dfrac{490.5}{0.01} = 49{,}050 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P = 49.05 \text{ kPa}}$$

This computed value is the **reference pressure** that the gauge under calibration must read. Any deviation indicates gauge inaccuracy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Classification Hierarchy of Pressure Gauges

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the hierarchical classification of pressure measurement devices, as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1: Pressure Gauges).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Pressure Gauges] --> B[Hydrostatic Manometers]
    A --> C[Elastic Mechanical Gauges]

    B --> B1[Simple Manometer]
    B --> B2[U-Tube Manometer]
    B --> B3[Differential Manometer]
    B --> B4[Inverted U-Tube Manometer]
    B --> B5[Micro Manometer]

    B1 --> B1A[Vertical tube open to atmosphere]
    B2 --> B2A[Mercury as manometric fluid]
    B2 --> B2B[Measures gauge pressure of pipe]
    B3 --> B3A[Measures pressure difference]
    B3 --> B3B[Used across orifice and venturi]
    B4 --> B4A[Light oil as manometric fluid]
    B4 --> B4B[Used in water systems]
    B5 --> B5A[Inclined tube type]
    B5 --> B5B[Measures very low pressures]

    C --> C1[Bourdon Tube Gauge]
    C --> C2[Diaphragm Gauge]
    C --> C3[Dead Weight Tester]

    C1 --> C1A[C-shaped elliptical tube straightens under pressure]
    C1 --> C1B[Most common industrial gauge]
    C2 --> C2A[Thin metal disc deflects under pressure]
    C3 --> C3A[Primary calibration standard]
    C3 --> C3B[Equation P equals W divided by A]

    A --> D[Modern Electronic Gauges]
    D --> D1[Strain Gauge Transducer]
    D --> D2[Piezoelectric Sensor]
    D --> D3[Capacitive Pressure Sensor]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Operating Logic of a Bourdon Tube Pressure Gauge

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Pressurized Fluid Enters Socket] --> P2[Pressure Acts on Elliptical Cross Section]
    P2 --> P3[Minor Axis Tries to Expand]
    P3 --> P4[Curved Tube Tends to Straighten]
    P4 --> P5[Free End Tip Displaces by Delta]
    P5 --> P6[Linkage Transfers Motion to Sector Gear]
    P6 --> P7[Pinion Gear Rotates Pointer]
    P7 --> P8[Pointer Reads Pressure on Calibrated Dial]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Dead Weight Tester — Calibration Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    W1[Load Known Weights on Piston] --> W2[Pump Oil into Cylinder]
    W2 --> W3[Oil Pressure Lifts Piston Plus Weights]
    W3 --> W4{Piston Floats in Equilibrium}
    W4 --> W5[Compute Reference Pressure P equals W by A]
    W5 --> W6[Connect Gauge Under Test to Same Circuit]
    W6 --> W7[Compare Gauge Reading to Reference P]
    W7 --> W8[Record Deviation as Calibration Error]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Schematic: U-Tube Manometer (Text-Based ASCII Schematic)

```
              |           |
              |    Air    |
              |           |
   ___________|___________|___________
  |           |           |          |
  |   Pipe    |  Mercury  |  Open to |
  |   Fluid   |  Column   |  Atmosphere
  |  (rho_f)  |  (rho_m)  |  (P_atm) |
  |___________|           |__________|
              |           |
              |    h      |  <-- Mercury level difference
              |___________|
        P_A            P_atm
```

**Reading the schematic:**
- Left limb: connected to the pressurized pipe at point A. Pressure inside the pipe is $P_A$.
- Right limb: open to atmosphere. Pressure at the open surface is $P_{atm}$.
- The shaded region in the middle represents the manometric fluid (mercury) with a height difference $h$ between the two limbs.
- The unshaded region above the mercury in the left limb contains the flowing fluid from the pipe (e.g., water).
- The governing equation is $P_A = P_{atm} + h g (\rho_m - \rho_f)$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions are typically **direct, definition-based, or short derivation problems**. They test **CO1 (Apply knowledge of mathematics, science, and engineering fundamentals)** and **RBT Level 2 (Understand)**.

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a Bourdon tube pressure gauge. Mention its typical industrial application."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**
A Bourdon tube is a curved metallic tube of **elliptical (or oval) cross-section**, fixed at one end (the socket) and free at the other. When the fluid under pressure enters the tube through the socket, the internal pressure exerts a force on the inner walls. Because the cross-section is non-circular, the wall stiffness along the minor axis is lower than along the major axis. The minor axis tends to expand outward, and as a result, the **curved tube tends to straighten (uncoil)**. The free end of the tube moves outward by a small displacement proportional to the applied pressure. This displacement is transmitted through a **mechanical linkage (sector and pinion)** to a pointer, which rotates across a calibrated dial to indicate the pressure.

**Industrial Application (1 Mark):**
Bourdon tube gauges are widely used in **steam boilers, hydraulic systems, gas cylinders, compressed air lines, and process industries** for measuring pressures typically up to **700 MPa** (depending on tube material and design).

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between a simple manometer and a differential manometer."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Simple Manometer | Differential Manometer |
|---------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Function** | Measures pressure at a single point relative to atmosphere. | Measures the **pressure difference** between two points in a system. |
| **Construction** | Single vertical tube open at the top. | Modified U-tube with manometric fluid between two flowing-fluid columns. |
| **Reference** | Open to atmosphere ($P_{atm}$). | Both limbs connected to two separate pressure points. |
| **Application** | Open tanks, gas pressure vessels. | Across orifices, venturis, and pipe fittings where $\Delta P$ is needed. |
| **Equation** | $P = \rho g h$ | $P_1 - P_2 = h g (\rho_m - \rho_f)$ |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B (14 Marks) questions are **structured numerical/analytical problems** that test **CO2 (Identify and formulate engineering problems)** and **RBT Levels 3–4 (Apply / Analyze)**. They **must** include internal choice — the student answers either **Question A** or **Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**A differential U-tube mercury manometer is connected across an orifice meter fitted in a horizontal pipe carrying water. The manometer reads a mercury column difference of $h = 250 \text{ mm}$. The pipe centerline is at the same elevation for both tapping points. Calculate:**

**(a) The pressure difference between the upstream and downstream tapping points in Pa and in bar. (7 Marks)**

**(b) The gauge pressure at the upstream tapping point if the absolute pressure at the downstream point is $P_2 = 120 \text{ kPa}$ (absolute). Take $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $h = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$
- $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $P_2 = 120 \text{ kPa (abs)}$

**Part (a): Pressure Difference**
