Apply the differential manometer equation derived in Section 3.3:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 - P_2 &= h g (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_{water}) \\
P_1 - P_2 &= 0.25 \times 9.81 \times (13600 - 1000) \\
P_1 - P_2 &= 0.25 \times 9.81 \times 12600 \\
P_1 - P_2 &= 30{,}901.5 \text{ Pa} \\
P_1 - P_2 &\approx 30.9 \text{ kPa} \\
P_1 - P_2 &= 0.309 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result (a):** $\Delta P = 30.9 \text{ kPa} = 0.309 \text{ bar}$

**Part (b): Gauge Pressure at Upstream Point**

Given: $P_2 = 120 \text{ kPa (abs)}$. Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= P_2 + \Delta P \\
P_1 &= 120 + 30.9 \\
P_1 &= 150.9 \text{ kPa (abs)}
\end{aligned}$$

Convert absolute to gauge using $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{1, gauge} &= P_1 - P_{atm} \\
P_{1, gauge} &= 150.9 - 101.325 \\
P_{1, gauge} &= 49.575 \text{ kPa (gauge)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result (b):** $P_{1, gauge} \approx 49.58 \text{ kPa (gauge)}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**A dead-weight pressure tester is used to calibrate an industrial pressure gauge. The piston has an effective cross-sectional area of $A = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ (i.e., 5 cm²). The total mass of the piston, weights, and platform is $M = 30 \text{ kg}$. The gauge being calibrated reads $P_{gauge} = 585 \text{ kPa}$.**

**(a) Calculate the reference pressure generated by the dead-weight tester. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Determine the percentage error of the gauge being calibrated, and state whether the gauge reads high or low. (7 Marks)**

**Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $A = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
- $M = 30 \text{ kg}$
- $P_{gauge} = 585 \text{ kPa}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Part (a): Reference Pressure**

Apply the dead-weight tester equation derived in Section 3.4:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{ref} &= \frac{Mg}{A} \\
P_{ref} &= \frac{30 \times 9.81}{5 \times 10^{-4}} \\
P_{ref} &= \frac{294.3}{5 \times 10^{-4}} \\
P_{ref} &= 588{,}600 \text{ Pa} \\
P_{ref} &= 588.6 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result (a):** $P_{ref} = 588.6 \text{ kPa}$

**Part (b): Percentage Error of the Industrial Gauge**

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Error} &= \frac{P_{gauge} - P_{ref}}{P_{ref}} \times 100 \\
\% \text{ Error} &= \frac{585 - 588.6}{588.6} \times 100 \\
\% \text{ Error} &= \frac{-3.6}{588.6} \times 100 \\
\% \text{ Error} &= -0.6116\%
\end{aligned}$$

The **negative sign** indicates that the gauge reads **lower than the true pressure** (i.e., it is under-reading).

**Result (b):** Gauge error $= -0.61\%$ (gauge reads **low**)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in Pressure Gauge Questions (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m:** A mercury column reading of 250 mm **must** be entered as $0.25$ m, not $250$. This single unit error can lose 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Mixing $\rho_m$ and $\rho_f$:** In the differential manometer equation, $\rho_m$ is the **manometric fluid** (heavier, mercury) and $\rho_f$ is the **flowing fluid** (lighter, water). Reversing them gives a **negative** and physically incorrect answer.
> 3. **Confusing gauge and absolute pressure:** KTU explicitly tests the conversion $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$. If a question says "absolute pressure at the downstream point is 120 kPa" and asks for gauge pressure at the upstream point, you must subtract $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa at the end.
> 4. **Not sketching the manometer setup:** A neat, labeled sketch is worth **2–3 marks** by itself in KTU valuation, even if the calculation is partially incorrect. Always draw the manometer with clear labels: $\rho_m$, $\rho_f$, $h$, $P_A$, $P_B$, $P_{atm}$.
> 5. **Sign convention errors in differential manometer:** If the mercury level in the limb connected to the **higher** pressure side is **lower** (which is the standard case), then $P_A > P_B$ and $\Delta P > 0$. Always confirm the geometry before writing the equation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pressure Gauges (Module 1, PCAUT302)**

- [x] **Manometers** balance fluid pressure against the weight of a column of reference liquid (hydrostatic principle).
- [x] **Simple manometer** uses $P = \rho g h$ for open vessels.
- [x] **U-tube manometer** uses $P_A = P_{atm} + h g (\rho_m - \rho_f)$ for closed pipes.
- [x] **Differential manometer** gives pressure drop: $\Delta P = h g (\rho_m - \rho_f)$.
- [x] **Inverted U-tube** uses lighter manometric fluid for water systems.
- [x] **Micro-manometer** uses inclined tube for very low pressures: $P = \rho g L \sin \theta$.
- [x] **Bourdon tube** straightens due to elliptical cross-section deformation — most common industrial gauge.
- [x] **Diaphragm gauge** uses disc deflection for low-pressure measurement.
- [x] **Dead-weight tester** is the **primary standard** for pressure calibration: $P = Mg/A$.
- [x] **Absolute vs. Gauge:** $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$. Atmospheric pressure = **101.325 kPa**.
- [x] **Standard values to memorize:** $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- [x] **Unit conversions:** 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa = 100 kPa; 1 m of water column ≈ 9.81 kPa.
- [x] **Always draw a labeled diagram** in KTU Part B answers — it earns 2–3 marks automatically.
- [x] **Sign convention:** Higher pressure pushes manometric fluid **down** in its own limb and **up** in the opposite limb.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability of Bodies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stability of Bodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Stability of Bodies

In fluid statics, every body immersed in or floating on a fluid is subjected to two primary forces:
1. **Weight ($W$)** of the body acting vertically downward through its **Centre of Gravity (G)**.
2. **Buoyant Force ($F_B$)** acting vertically upward through the **Centre of Buoyancy (B)**, which is the centroid of the displaced fluid volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Stability of a floating or submerged body refers to the ability of the body to return to its original equilibrium position (or to deviate controllably) when it is slightly tilted or disturbed by an external couple.*

A body is said to be in **equilibrium** when the net force and net moment acting on it are zero. There are three types of equilibrium:
- **Stable Equilibrium:** Body returns to its original position after slight tilt.
- **Unstable Equilibrium:** Body moves further away from its original position after slight tilt.
- **Neutral Equilibrium:** Body remains in the new position after slight tilt.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Standing Pen Test"

Imagine placing a pen on a table:
- Standing on its **tip** → It falls over with the slightest push (**Unstable**).
- Lying flat on its **side** → You can roll it, and it stays wherever you leave it (**Neutral**).
- Standing firmly on its **flat bottom** → Give it a small nudge and it returns upright (**Stable**).

The same principle applies to ships, submarines, and buoys. A ship must be **stable** so that when waves tilt it slightly, it rights itself automatically instead of capsizing.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Magnitude/Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | **9.81** |
| Specific weight of fresh water | $\gamma_w$ | $\text{N/m}^3$ | **9810** |
| Specific weight of sea water | $\gamma_{sw}$ | $\text{N/m}^3$ | **10025** |
| Density of fresh water | $\rho_w$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | **1000** |
| Density of sea water | $\rho_{sw}$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | **1025** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Metacentre ($M$):**
> The metacentre is the point about which a floating body starts oscillating when slightly tilted. It is the **intersection of the vertical line through the new centre of buoyancy** ($B'$) and the **original vertical axis of symmetry** of the body.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stability of Fully Submerged Bodies

A body that is **fully submerged** (e.g., a submarine) is stable when its **Centre of Gravity ($G$) lies below its Centre of Buoyancy ($B$)**.

**Why?** When the body is tilted:
- $G$ remains stationary (mass distribution doesn't change).
- $B$ shifts to a new position $B'$ because the displaced volume geometry changes.
- The buoyant force always acts upward through the new $B'$.

If $G$ is **below** $B$, then any tilt creates a **restoring couple** that rotates the body back to its original orientation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:**
> For *fully submerged bodies*, stability is purely a function of the **relative vertical positions of $G$ and $B$** — NOT the metacentric height.

### 2.2 Stability of Floating Bodies — The Metacentre Concept

For a **floating body** (e.g., a ship), the situation is more complex because both the **shape of the submerged volume** and the **waterline** change as the body tilts.

**Key steps when a floating body tilts by a small angle $\theta$:**
1. The original centre of buoyancy $B$ shifts to a new position $B'$.
2. The line of action of the buoyant force now passes through $B'$ (vertically upward).
3. The line of action of the weight $W$ continues to pass through $G$ (vertically downward).
4. These two vertical lines intersect at a point called the **Metacentre ($M$)**.
5. $M$ always lies on the **original axis of symmetry** of the body for small tilts.

### 2.3 Metacentric Height ($\overline{GM}$)

The distance between the Centre of Gravity ($G$) and the Metacentre ($M$) along the vertical axis is called the **Metacentric Height**.

$$\overline{GM} = \overline{BM} \pm \overline{BG}$$

- **+** sign when $M$ is **above** $G$.
- **–** sign when $M$ is **below** $G$.

**Conditions of Equilibrium (Floating Body):**

| Position of $M$ relative to $G$ | Condition | Type of Equilibrium |
|---|---|---|
| $M$ is **above** $G$ | $\overline{GM} > 0$ | **Stable** |
| $M$ **coincides** with $G$ | $\overline{GM} = 0$ | **Neutral** |
| $M$ is **below** $G$ | $\overline{GM} < 0$ | **Unstable** |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Buoyant Force | $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \forall$ | $\forall$ = submerged volume |
| 2 | Weight of Body | $W = \rho_b \cdot g \cdot \forall_{total}$ | Acts at $G$ |
| 3 | Metacentric Radius | $\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I}{V}$ | $I$ = Second moment of waterline area |
| 4 | Metacentric Height | $\overline{GM} = \dfrac{I}{V} - \overline{BG}$ | General expression |
| 5 | Restoring Couple | $C_R = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$ | Couple tending to right the body |
| 6 | Overturning Couple | $C_O = W \cdot \overline{GZ}$ | Couple trying to capsize |
| 7 | Period of Oscillation | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot \overline{GM}}}$ | $k$ = radius of gyration |
| 8 | Angle of Heel (no list) | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{m \cdot a \cdot h}{W \cdot \overline{GM}}$ | For a couple due to shifting load |
| 9 | Meta-centric height by pendulum | $\overline{GM} = \dfrac{W \cdot d}{W_1 \cdot \tan\theta}$ | Experimental method |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Substitution Notice:**
> In all KTU board answer sheets, the second moment of area $I$ about the **longitudinal axis** (for ship stability) is denoted simply as $I$. The transverse axis gives very small $I$ and is therefore **neglected** for large ships.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ship Design:** Naval architects calculate $\overline{GM}$ to ensure passenger comfort and prevent capsizing (e.g., Titanic had insufficient $\overline{GM}$).
- **Submarine Design:** Ballast tanks shift $G$ below $B$ for submersion stability.
- **Offshore Platforms:** Stability analysis is critical in rough seas.
- **Dam Gates and Pontoons:** Metacentric calculations prevent dangerous tipping.
- **Aeronautical Hydraulics:** Floating instruments in fluid reservoirs are designed with controlled $\overline{GM}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Metacentric Radius ($\overline{BM}$)

**Setup:** Consider a floating body with waterline plane $WL$. Let the body tilt by a small angle $\theta$ about a longitudinal axis through $O$. The new waterline becomes $W'L'$.

**Step 1: Shift in Buoyancy Centre**

When the body tilts, an extra wedge of volume $\delta \forall_1$ enters the water on one side, and an equal wedge $\delta \forall_2$ emerges on the other side.

$$\delta \forall_1 = \delta \forall_2 = \delta \forall \quad \text{(conservation of displaced volume for small } \theta \text{)}$$

**Step 2: Centroidal Shift**

Let the centroid of the emerged wedge be at horizontal distance $x_1$ from $O$, and the centroid of the immersed wedge be at horizontal distance $x_2$.

The centre of buoyancy shifts horizontally by a distance $\overline{BB'} = x$ such that:

$$\delta \forall \cdot x_1 + \delta \forall \cdot x_2 = \forall \cdot x$$

$$x = \dfrac{\delta \forall \cdot (x_1 + x_2)}{\forall}$$

**Step 3: Expressing in terms of Waterline Area**

Consider an elemental strip of the waterline area at distance $y$ from the axis $O$, with width $dy$ and length $l(y)$.

The elementary area is:

$$dA = l(y) \cdot dy$$

The volume of the small prism over this strip due to tilt $\theta$:

$$d(\delta \forall) = l(y) \cdot dy \cdot y \tan\theta$$

Integrating over the entire waterline area:

$$\delta \forall = \tan\theta \int y \cdot dA = \tan\theta \cdot A \cdot \bar{y}$$

But for a symmetrical waterline area, $\bar{y} = 0$, so we cannot use this directly. Instead, we find the **shift in buoyancy** by considering the **shift in centroid of displaced volume**:

$$x = \dfrac{\int y \cdot (y \tan\theta) \cdot l(y) \cdot dy}{\forall}$$

$$x = \dfrac{\tan\theta \int y^2 \, dA}{\forall}$$

By definition, the second moment of area of the waterline plane about the axis $O$ is:

$$I = \int y^2 \, dA$$

**Step 4: Final Expression**

$$\overline{BB'} = x = \dfrac{I \tan\theta}{\forall}$$

From the geometry of the metacentre triangle $BB'M$:

$$\overline{BB'} = \overline{BM} \cdot \sin\theta$$

For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta$, so:

$$\overline{BM} \cdot \tan\theta = \dfrac{I \tan\theta}{\forall}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I}{\forall}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The factor $I$ in $\overline{BM} = I/\forall$ refers to the **second moment of the waterline plane area** about the axis of rotation. For a rectangular pontoon of length $L$ and breadth $B$ tilted about the longitudinal axis:
> $$I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Metacentric Height ($\overline{GM}$)

From the geometric relationship along the vertical line through $G$:

$$\overline{GM} = \overline{BM} - \overline{BG}$$

Substituting $\overline{BM} = I/\forall$:

$$\boxed{\overline{GM} = \dfrac{I}{\forall} - \overline{BG}}$$

Where:
- $\overline{BG}$ = Vertical distance between $B$ and $G$ (always positive, $B$ is below $G$ in most floating bodies).
- $\forall$ = Volume of submerged portion (displaced fluid volume).

### 3.3 Derivation of Restoring Couple

When a floating body is tilted by a small angle $\theta$:
- $W$ acts downward at $G$.
- $F_B$ acts upward at $B'$ (or rather, along the line $BM$).

These two equal and opposite forces form a **couple** with arm $\overline{GZ}$, where $Z$ is the foot of the perpendicular from $G$ to the line $BM$.

From the geometry of triangle $GMZ$:

$$\overline{GZ} = \overline{GM} \sin\theta$$

Hence the restoring couple is:

$$\boxed{C_R = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \sin\theta}$$

For small angles $\theta$:

$$C_R \approx W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \theta$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Time Period of Oscillation

The floating body behaves like a simple pendulum with moment of inertia $I_m = m k^2$ (where $k$ is the radius of gyration about the axis of rotation).

Applying the equation of rotational motion about the axis of tilt:

$$I_m \cdot \ddot{\theta} = -C_R = -W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \theta$$

$$m k^2 \ddot{\theta} = -m g \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \theta$$

$$\ddot{\theta} + \left(\dfrac{g \cdot \overline{GM}}{k^2}\right)\theta = 0$$

This is the standard SHM equation with:

$$\omega^2 = \dfrac{g \cdot \overline{GM}}{k^2}$$

Therefore the **time period of oscillation** is:

$$\boxed{T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot \overline{GM}}}}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Stability Scenarios

| Scenario | Submerged/Floating | G vs B | $\overline{GM}$ | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Submerged sphere | Submerged | $G$ below $B$ | N/A | Stable |
| Submerged sphere | Submerged | $G$ above $B$ | N/A | Unstable |
| Floating cylinder (uniform) | Floating | $G$ and $B$ coincide | $\overline{GM} = \overline{BM} > 0$ | Stable |
| Floating ship (cargo low) | Floating | $G$ close to $B$ | $\overline{GM} > 0$ small | Stable but slow restoring |
| Floating ship (cargo high) | Floating | $G$ far above $B$ | $\overline{GM} < 0$ | Unstable, capsizes |
| Floating body (neutral) | Floating | $M$ coincides with $G$ | $\overline{GM} = 0$ | Neutral equilibrium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stability Determination Process

The following flow diagram illustrates the **decision logic** used by naval architects and KTU examiners when analysing stability of a body:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Body in Fluid] --> B{Is body fully submerged?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Find positions of G and B}
    C --> D{Is G below B?}
    D -- Yes --> E[STABLE Submerged Body]
    D -- No --> F[UNSTABLE Submerged Body]
    B -- No --> G[Floating Body Analysis]
    G --> H[Calculate Submerged Volume V]
    H --> I[Calculate Second Moment I of Waterline Area]
    I --> J[Compute BM = I divided by V]
    J --> K[Compute BG distance]
    K --> L[Compute GM = BM minus BG]
    L --> M{GM greater than zero?}
    M -- Yes --> N[STABLE Floating Body]
    M -- No --> O[UNSTABLE Floating Body]
    N --> P[Compute Time Period T]
    O --> Q[Add Ballast or Redesign]
```

### 4.2 Stability States Schematic — Block Representation

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Original Body] --> B[Slight Tilt by Angle Theta]
    B --> C{Type of Equilibrium}
    C --> D[Stable: Restoring Couple]
    C --> E[Unstable: Capsizing Couple]
    C --> F[Neutral: No Couple]
    D --> D1[Body Returns to Original Position]
    E --> E1[Body Tilts Further]
    F --> F1[Body Stays at New Position]
```

### 4.3 Metacentre Geometry — Force-Couple Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Weight W acts at G downward] --> B[Buoyant Force FB acts at B upward]
    B --> C[Line of Action of FB passes through M]
    A --> D[Line of Action of W passes through G]
    C --> E[Intersection Point is Metacentre M]
    D --> E
    E --> F[GM is Metacentric Height]
    F --> G[Restoring Couple equals W times GM times sin theta]
```

### 4.4 Physical Schematic Description (Text-based Block Diagram)

For the **physical floating body** with metacentre relationships:

```
                    M  ← Metacentre (on original axis)
                    |
                    |  ← GM = Metacentric Height
                    |
                    G  ← Centre of Gravity
                    |
                    |  ← BG = Distance between B and G
                    |
                    B  ← Centre of Buoyancy (shifts to B' on tilt)
                   /|
                  / |
                 /  |  ← B' (new position of B on tilt)
                /   |
        _______/____|_______
       |               |      |  ←  Waterline (tilted)
       |    Submerged |      |
       |_______________|______|
                    |
                  Body
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Diagram Note:** For KTU board examinations, students are expected to draw the metacentre diagram clearly showing:
> 1. The vertical line through the **original axis of symmetry**.
> 2. The vertical line through the **new centre of buoyancy $B'$**.
> 3. The intersection point **$M$**.
> 4. Labelled distances $\overline{GM}$, $\overline{BM}$, and $\overline{BG}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the terms: Centre of Buoyancy and Metacentre.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Centre of Buoyancy ($B$):** It is the point through which the entire buoyant force (upthrust) of the fluid is supposed to act. Mathematically, it is the centroid of the volume of fluid displaced by the body.
>
> **Metacentre ($M$):** It is the point about which a floating body oscillates when slightly tilted. It is defined as the point of intersection of the vertical line passing through the new centre of buoyancy ($B'$) and the original vertical axis (line of symmetry) of the floating body.

*(3 Marks: 1.5 each)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the conditions of stability for a floating body.**

**Model Answer:**

For a floating body in equilibrium, stability is determined by the position of the metacentre ($M$) relative to the centre of gravity ($G$):

1. **Stable Equilibrium:** When $M$ lies **above** $G$, i.e., $\overline{GM} > 0$. The body returns to its original position after a small tilt.
2. **Unstable Equilibrium:** When $M$ lies **below** $G$, i.e., $\overline{GM} < 0$. The body capsizes upon tilting.
3. **Neutral Equilibrium:** When $M$ **coincides with** $G$, i.e., $\overline{GM} = 0$. The body remains in the tilted position.

*(3 Marks: 1 each for the three conditions)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ANY ONE** full question.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

A rectangular pontoon of dimensions **6 m long**, **4 m wide**, and **2 m deep** floats in sea water (specific gravity = 1.025) with its **1.2 m submerged**. A load of **80 kN** is placed on the pontoon at a height of **1.5 m** above the waterline. Determine:
**(a)** The new position of the centre of gravity.
**(b)** The metacentric height and comment on stability.

**Given Data:**
- Pontoon dimensions: $L = 6$ m, $B = 4$ m, $D = 2$ m
- Initial submergence: $d_1 = 1.2$ m
- Load: $W_L = 80$ kN at $h_L = 1.5$ m above waterline
- Sea water specific gravity: $S = 1.025$

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — New Position of Centre of Gravity (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Calculate Weight of Pontoon**

Volume of pontoon: $\forall_p = 6 \times 4 \times 2 = 48 \, \text{m}^3$

Assuming pontoon material density (steel/wood standard) — for a hollow pontoon, the weight is found from floating condition:

Initial weight of pontoon alone = Weight of sea water displaced:
$$W_p = S \cdot \gamma_w \cdot L \cdot B \cdot d_1$$
$$W_p = 1.025 \times 9.81 \times 6 \times 4 \times 1.2$$
$$W_p = 1.025 \times 9.81 \times 28.8$$
$$W_p = 289.5 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 2: Centre of Gravity of Pontoon Alone**

For a uniform rectangular pontoon:
$$\overline{BG_1} = \dfrac{D}{2} - \dfrac{d_1}{2} = \dfrac{2}{2} - \dfrac{1.2}{2} = 1.0 - 0.6 = 0.4 \, \text{m}$$

So $G_1$ is **0.4 m above** $B$.

**Step 3: New Centre of Gravity with Load**

Taking moments about $B$:
$$W_p \cdot \overline{BG_1} + W_L \cdot (\overline{BG_1} + d_1 + h_L) = (W_p + W_L) \cdot \overline{BG}$$

$$289.5 \times 0.4 + 80 \times (0.4 + 1.2 + 1.5) = (289.5 + 80) \cdot \overline{BG}$$

$$115.8 + 80 \times 3.1 = 369.5 \cdot \overline{BG}$$

$$115.8 + 248 = 369.5 \cdot \overline{BG}$$

$$363.8 = 369.5 \cdot \overline{BG}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{BG} = 0.9846 \, \text{m}}$$

#### **Part (b) — Metacentric Height (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: New Submerged Depth**

After loading, by Archimedes' principle:
$$W_p + W_L = \gamma_{sw} \cdot L \cdot B \cdot d_2$$

$$369.5 \times 10^3 = 1.025 \times 9810 \times 6 \times 4 \times d_2$$

$$369500 = 241254 \cdot d_2$$

$$d_2 = 1.532 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Metacentric Radius**

Second moment of waterline area about longitudinal axis:
$$I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \dfrac{6 \times 4^3}{12} = \dfrac{6 \times 64}{12} = 32 \, \text{m}^4$$

Submerged volume:
$$\forall = L \cdot B \cdot d_2 = 6 \times 4 \times 1.532 = 36.77 \, \text{m}^3$$

$$\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I}{\forall} = \dfrac{32}{36.77} = 0.8703 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3: Metacentric Height**

$$\overline{GM} = \overline{BM} - \overline{BG} = 0.8703 - 0.9846$$

$$\boxed{\overline{GM} = -0.1143 \, \text{m}}$$

**Comment on Stability:**

Since $\overline{GM} < 0$ (negative), the pontoon is **UNSTABLE** after loading. The load is placed too high above the waterline, shifting $G$ above $M$, causing the restoring couple to act in the wrong direction.

**Practical Solution:** Place the load **lower** (closer to the waterline) or add **ballast** at the bottom to lower $G$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

A ship of **mass 1.2 × 10⁶ kg** and **metacentric height 0.45 m** undergoes a small oscillation in still water. The radius of gyration of the ship about its longitudinal axis is **3.5 m**. Determine:
**(a)** The time period of oscillation.
**(b)** The natural frequency of rolling.

**Given Data:**
- Mass: $m = 1.2 \times 10^6$ kg
- Metacentric height: $\overline{GM} = 0.45$ m
- Radius of gyration: $k = 3.5$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Time Period of Oscillation (7 Marks)**

The time period of oscillation of a floating body is given by:

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot \overline{GM}}}$$

**Step 1: Substitute Values**

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{(3.5)^2}{9.81 \times 0.45}}$$

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{12.25}{4.4145}}$$

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{2.7749}$$

$$T = 2\pi \times 1.6658$$

$$\boxed{T = 10.466 \, \text{seconds}}$$

**Step 2: Verification**

The period is in the order of 10 seconds, which is realistic for large ships (typically 8–15 seconds).

#### **Part (b) — Natural Frequency (7 Marks)**

The natural frequency of oscillation is the reciprocal of the time period:

$$f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{10.466}$$

$$\boxed{f = 0.0955 \, \text{Hz}}$$

Alternatively, the angular natural frequency:

$$\omega_n = \dfrac{2\pi}{T} = \dfrac{2\pi}{10.466}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_n = 0.6003 \, \text{rad/s}}$$

**Practical Significance:**

- A ship with a small $\overline{GM}$ (like 0.45 m) has a long rolling period.
- Long period ships are more **comfortable** in waves (roll slowly with the swell).
- A higher $\overline{GM}$ would mean a stiffer ship that rolls faster — good for cargo ships, bad for passenger comfort.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the small-angle approximation:** Students often use $\sin\theta$ instead of $\tan\theta$ in $\overline{BM}$ derivation. For KTU, small angle means $\sin\theta = \tan\theta = \theta$.
> 2. **Confusing axes for $I$:** The second moment of area $I$ must be taken about the **axis of tilt**, not the perpendicular axis. For ships rolling about the longitudinal axis, $I = LB^3/12$ (where $B$ is the beam).
> 3. **Sign error in $\overline{GM}$:** Always write $\overline{GM} = \overline{BM} - \overline{BG}$ (positive $G$ above $B$ gives negative BG contribution to GM).
> 4. **Units mismatch:** $I$ must be in $\text{m}^4$, $\forall$ in $\text{m}^3$, so $\overline{BM}$ comes out correctly in metres.
> 5. **Neglecting self-weight of pontoon:** Always state assumptions about pontoon material/density clearly at the start of the solution.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stability of Bodies**

- [ ] **Buoyant Force** always acts upward through Centre of Buoyancy ($B$), equal to weight of displaced fluid.
- [ ] **Weight** always acts downward through Centre of Gravity ($G$).
- [ ] **Submerged Body Stability:** Stable if $G$ is **below** $B$.
- [ ] **Floating Body Stability:** Stable if **Metacentre $M$ is above $G$** ($\overline{GM} > 0$).
- [ ] **Metacentric Radius Formula:** $\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I}{\forall}$
- [ ] **Metacentric Height Formula:** $\overline{GM} = \dfrac{I}{\forall} - \overline{BG}$
- [ ] **Restoring Couple:** $C_R = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$
- [ ] **Time Period of Oscillation:** $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot \overline{GM}}}$
- [ ] For **rectangular pontoon** ($L \times B \times d$), $I = \dfrac{LB^3}{12}$ for rolling about longitudinal axis.
- [ ] For **circular pontoon** of diameter $D$, $I = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$.
- [ ] High $\overline{GM}$ → ship is **stiff** (rolls quickly, uncomfortable for passengers).
- [ ] Low $\overline{GM}$ → ship is **tender** (rolls slowly, comfortable but cargo may shift).
- [ ] Negative $\overline{GM}$ → ship **capsizes**.
- [ ] **Practical $\overline{GM}$ values:** Small ships ≈ 0.3–0.5 m; Large ships ≈ 1.5–3.0 m; Warships ≈ 0.5–1.0 m.
- [ ] For **fully submerged bodies**, metacentric height concept is **not used** — only relative positions of $G$ and $B$ matter.
- [ ] Always remember the order along the vertical axis from top to bottom: $M \rightarrow G \rightarrow B$ for stable floating bodies (when $\overline{GM} > 0$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buoyancy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Buoyancy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Buoyancy** is defined as the vertical upward force exerted by a fluid (liquid or gas) on a body that is either fully submerged in it or floating on its free surface. This upward force is termed the **Buoyant Force** (denoted $F_B$) and it always acts through the **Center of Buoyancy (B)**, which is the centroid of the displaced volume of fluid.

Mathematically, the magnitude of the buoyant force is governed by **Archimedes' Principle**:

$$F_B = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced}$$

Where:
- $\rho_{fluid}$ = Mass density of the surrounding fluid in **kg/m³**
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $V_{displaced}$ = Volume of fluid displaced by the body in **m³**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Invisible Hand Push":** Imagine pushing a cork completely underwater in a bathtub and then releasing it. The cork instantly shoots back up to the surface. Why? Because the water below the cork is being pushed down by gravity and is "looking" for a path to escape. The displaced water effectively pushes the cork upward with a force equal to the weight of the water that was moved out of the way. This "invisible hand" is the buoyant force.

**Simple Plain-English Explanation:**
1. When a body enters a fluid, it **pushes the fluid aside** to occupy that space.
2. The fluid, in turn, **reacts back** with an equal and opposite upward force (Newton's Third Law).
3. If this upward force is **greater** than the body's weight $\rightarrow$ body **floats**.
4. If this upward force is **less** than the body's weight $\rightarrow$ body **sinks**.
5. If both are **equal** $\rightarrow$ body is in **neutral equilibrium** (remains suspended).

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Density of fresh water** $\rho_w = 1000$ **kg/m³**
- **Specific weight of water** $\gamma_w = 9810$ **N/m³**
- **Density of sea water** $\rho_{sw} \approx 1025$ **kg/m³**
- **Specific gravity (or Relative density)** = Ratio of density of body to density of water (a dimensionless quantity).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Steps of Buoyancy Analysis

1. **Identify the displaced volume ($V_{displaced}$):**
   - For a fully submerged body: $V_{displaced} = V_{body}$.
   - For a floating body: $V_{displaced}$ = volume of the body lying *below* the free water surface.

2. **Compute the buoyant force:**
   - Multiply displaced volume by specific weight of the fluid: $F_B = \gamma_{fluid} \cdot V_{displaced}$.

3. **Locate the Center of Gravity (G) and Center of Buoyancy (B):**
   - $G$ is the centroid of the *body*.
   - $B$ is the centroid of the *displaced fluid volume*.

4. **Apply equilibrium conditions:**
   - For floating equilibrium: $F_B = W$ (weight of body).
   - For submerged equilibrium: $F_B = W + F_{apparent}$ (apparent weight = true weight − buoyant force).

5. **Check stability using the Metacenter (M):**
   - Find $M$ by drawing the new buoyancy line after a small angular tilt $\theta$.
   - Compute $GM = BM - BG$ (metacentric height).

## 2.2 Why and How Behind Each Step

- **Why does $B$ shift when the body tilts?**
  When a floating body tilts by a small angle, the shape of the submerged volume changes, and hence its centroid (B) shifts toward the deeper side. The vertical line through this new $B$ intersects the original centerline at point $M$ — the **Metacenter**.

- **How does the body recover its equilibrium?**
  If $M$ lies *above* $G$ ($GM > 0$), a restoring couple acts and the body is **stable**. If $M$ lies *below* $G$ ($GM < 0$), an overturning couple acts and the body is **unstable**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a high-yield reference for KTU University Exam problems on Buoyancy.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Governing Formula | Units | Condition / Remark |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Buoyant Force | $F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$ | N | Always vertical, upward |
| 2 | Buoyant Force (alt.) | $F_B = \gamma_f \cdot V_{disp}$ | N | $\gamma_f = \rho_f g$ |
| 3 | Archimedes' Principle | $W_{fluid\,displaced} = F_B$ | N | Core principle |
| 4 | Apparent Weight | $W_{app} = W - F_B$ | N | For submerged bodies |
| 5 | Specific Gravity | $S = \rho_{body} / \rho_{water}$ | dimensionless | $S<1 \Rightarrow$ floats |
| 6 | Depth of Submersion (Cube, side $a$) | $h = a / S$ | m | Floating cube |
| 7 | Metacentric Radius | $BM = I / V_{disp}$ | m | $I$ = 2nd moment of waterline area |
| 8 | Metacentric Height | $GM = BM - BG$ | m | $GM>0$ stable, $GM<0$ unstable |
| 9 | Restoring Couple | $C = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ | N·m | For small tilt $\theta$ |
| 10 | Mass of floating body | $m = \rho_f \cdot V_{disp}$ | kg | Floating equilibrium |

> [!NOTE]
> **In formulas above:** $\vert I \vert$ denotes the second moment of area of the waterline plane about the axis of rotation. The pipe symbol $\vert$ in tables is written as `\vert` to preserve table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ship Design:** Naval architects compute $GM$ meticulously to ensure passenger vessels do not capsize in rough seas.
- **Submarines:** Trim tanks are flooded or emptied to control buoyancy for diving or surfacing.
- **Hydrometers:** A floating calibrated glass tube that measures the specific gravity of liquids using the depth of submersion principle.
- **Hydraulic Dams & Caissons:** Used to determine uplift pressure and stability of submerged foundations.
- **Hot Air Balloons:** Archimedes' principle applied to a gas (hot air is less dense than cold air).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Buoyant Force (Archimedes' Principle)

Consider a small elemental vertical column of a submerged body with cross-sectional area $dA$ and height $h$ measured below the free surface of the fluid.

**Step 1:** Pressure at depth $h$ from the free surface (hydrostatic law):

$$P = P_{atm} + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2:** Force exerted on the top face of the elemental column (acting downward):

$$dF_{top} = P_{top} \cdot dA = (P_{atm} + \rho_f g h_1) \cdot dA$$

**Step 3:** Force exerted on the bottom face of the elemental column (acting upward):

$$dF_{bottom} = P_{bottom} \cdot dA = (P_{atm} + \rho_f g h_2) \cdot dA$$

**Step 4:** Net upward force on the element (the atmospheric pressure cancels out):

$$dF_B = dF_{bottom} - dF_{top} = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (h_2 - h_1) \cdot dA = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot dV$$

**Step 5:** Integrate over the entire submerged volume $V$:

$$F_B = \int_V \rho_f \cdot g \, dV = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V$$

$$\boxed{F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced} = W_{fluid\,displaced}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This proves that the net upward force (buoyant force) equals the weight of the fluid that was displaced — this is **Archimedes' Principle** in its complete mathematical form.

## 3.2 Derivation of Metacentric Height ($GM$)

Let a floating body of weight $W$ be tilted through a small angle $\theta$ about a longitudinal axis passing through the center of gravity $G$.

**Step 1:** Identify the second moment of area of the waterline plane.

$$I = \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

This $I$ is computed about the **axis of rotation** (longitudinal axis) of the floating body.

**Step 2:** When the body tilts, the center of buoyancy shifts from $B$ to $B_1$ by a small distance $\overline{BB_1}$.

$$\overline{BB_1} = \frac{I \cdot \sin\theta}{V_{disp}}$$

**Step 3:** By the geometry of the tilt, the new line of action of the buoyant force passes through $B_1$ and intersects the original vertical centerline at the **Metacenter (M)**. From similar triangles:

$$\overline{BM} = \frac{\overline{BB_1}}{\sin\theta} = \frac{I}{V_{disp}}$$

**Step 4:** The metacentric height is the distance from $G$ to $M$:

$$\boxed{GM = BM - BG = \frac{I}{V_{disp}} - BG}$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- If $GM > 0$ (i.e., $M$ above $G$) $\Rightarrow$ **Stable** equilibrium.
- If $GM = 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **Neutral** equilibrium.
- If $GM < 0$ (i.e., $M$ below $G$) $\Rightarrow$ **Unstable** (body capsizes).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stability Conditions

| Position of M | Value of GM | Equilibrium Type | Real Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| $M$ above $G$ | $GM > 0$ | Stable | Cargo ship upright in still water |
| $M$ coincides with $G$ | $GM = 0$ | Neutral | Sphere of uniform density |
| $M$ below $G$ | $GM < 0$ | Unstable | Tall narrow cylinder with heavy top |

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-Style Practice Problem)

**Problem:** A wooden block of dimensions $0.5\,\text{m} \times 0.5\,\text{m} \times 0.5\,\text{m}$ and specific gravity $0.7$ floats in water. Find (a) the depth of immersion, and (b) the metacentric height if the block is tilted about a horizontal axis through its base.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Depth of immersion:**

By Archimedes' Principle (floating equilibrium):

$$W_{block} = F_B$$

$$\rho_{block} \cdot g \cdot V_{block} = \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$$

$$0.7 \cdot (0.5)^3 = 1.0 \cdot (0.5 \times 0.5 \times h)$$

$$0.7 \times 0.125 = 0.25 \cdot h$$

$$h = \frac{0.0875}{0.25} = 0.35\,\text{m}$$

**Part (b) — Metacentric height:**

Submerged volume (prism):

$$V_{disp} = 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.35 = 0.0875\,\text{m}^3$$

Second moment of waterline area (square of side $0.5\,\text{m}$):

$$I = \frac{a^4}{12} = \frac{(0.5)^4}{12} = \frac{0.0625}{12} \approx 5.208 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^4$$

Center of gravity $G$ is at the geometric center of the cube:

$$BG = \frac{h}{2} - \frac{a}{2} = \frac{0.35}{2} - \frac{0.5}{2} = -0.075\,\text{m}$$

(B is at half the immersion depth, G is at half the full cube height.)

Metacentric radius:

$$BM = \frac{I}{V_{disp}} = \frac{5.208 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0875} \approx 0.0595\,\text{m}$$

Metacentric height:

$$GM = BM - BG = 0.0595 - (-0.075) = 0.1345\,\text{m} \approx 0.135\,\text{m}$$

**Conclusion:** Since $GM > 0$, the floating block is **stable**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Buoyancy Stability Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Body and Fluid Parameters] --> B[Compute Volume Displaced V_disp]
    B --> C[Compute Buoyant Force F_B = rho_f × g × V_disp]
    C --> D{Compare F_B with Weight W}
    D -->|F_B greater than W| E[Body Floats]
    D -->|F_B less than W| F[Body Sinks]
    D -->|F_B equals W| G[Neutral Equilibrium]
    E --> H[Locate Center of Gravity G]
    H --> I[Locate Center of Buoyancy B]
    I --> J[Apply Small Tilt Angle theta]
    J --> K[Locate New Buoyancy Position B1]
    K --> L[Draw Vertical through B1]
    L --> M[Intersect Centerline to find Metacenter M]
    M --> N{Is M above G?}
    N -->|Yes| O[GM greater than zero: STABLE]
    N -->|No| P[GM less than zero: UNSTABLE]
    O --> Q[Output: Stable Floating Body]
    P --> R[Output: Body Capsizes]
    G --> S[Output: Body Suspended in Fluid]
    F --> T[Output: Body Sinks to Bottom]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Schematic — Forces on a Floating Body

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Weight W acting downward at G] --> C{Equilibrium Check}
    B[Buoyant Force F_B acting upward at B] --> C
    C --> D[Resultant Couple when tilted]
    D --> E[Restoring Couple if M above G]
    D --> F[Overturning Couple if M below G]
    E --> G[Stable Equilibrium]
    F --> H[Unstable Equilibrium]
```

## 4.3 Key Points in the Diagrams

- **Node A and Node B** represent the two primary forces acting on any floating or submerged body.
- **Node D** is the decision junction for stability analysis.
- **Node M** is the mathematically derived point known as the *Metacenter*.
- The arrows from **Node M** to **Node O** and **Node P** highlight the two critical stability outcomes examined in KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain Archimedes' Principle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Archimedes' Principle states that when a body is wholly or partially immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the **weight of the fluid displaced** by the body. The buoyant force acts vertically upward through the center of buoyancy, which is the centroid of the displaced fluid volume. Mathematically, $F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$. This principle is the foundation of floatation and ship design. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the terms: (i) Center of Buoyancy, (ii) Metacenter, and (iii) Metacentric height.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(i) Center of Buoyancy (B):** The point through which the buoyant force acts; it is the centroid of the displaced volume of fluid. **(1 mark)**
- **(ii) Metacenter (M):** The point at which the line of action of the buoyant force intersects the original vertical centerline when a floating body is tilted through a small angle. **(1 mark)**
- **(iii) Metacentric Height (GM):** The distance between the center of gravity $G$ and the metacenter $M$. It determines the stability of the floating body. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> The following two questions are independent alternatives. KTU students are expected to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** A solid cylinder of diameter $0.3\,\text{m}$ and height $0.4\,\text{m}$ has a specific gravity of $0.6$. It is placed in water with its axis vertical. Determine: (i) the depth of immersion, and (ii) the magnitude of the buoyant force.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A rectangular pontoon of length $6\,\text{m}$, width $4\,\text{m}$ and weight $200\,\text{kN}$ floats in sea water ($\rho = 1025\,\text{kg/m}^3$). Find the metacentric height of the pontoon if its center of gravity is $1.5\,\text{m}$ above the base.

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a):**
- Volume of cylinder:
$$V = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.3)^2 \times 0.4 = 0.02827\,\text{m}^3$$
- Mass of cylinder:
$$m = 0.6 \times 1000 \times 0.02827 = 16.96\,\text{kg}$$
- Weight: $W = 16.96 \times 9.81 = 166.4\,\text{N}$

By floatation: $F_B = W = 166.4\,\text{N}$

- Depth of immersion $h$:
$$F_B = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h$$
$$166.4 = 1000 \times 9.81 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(0.3)^2 \times h$$
$$166.4 = 693.7 \times h$$
$$h = 0.24\,\text{m}$$

**(i) Depth of immersion = 0.24 m** **(ii) Buoyant force = 166.4 N** **(7 marks)**

**Part (b):**
- Buoyant force equals weight:
$$V_{disp} = \frac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} = \frac{200{,}000}{1025 \times 9.81} = 19.89\,\text{m}^3$$
- Submerged depth:
$$h = \frac{V_{disp}}{L \times B} = \frac{19.89}{6 \times 4} = 0.829\,\text{m}$$
- Second moment of waterline area:
$$I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{6 \times 4^3}{12} = 32\,\text{m}^4$$
- Metacentric radius:
$$BM = \frac{I}{V_{disp}} = \frac{32}{19.89} = 1.609\,\text{m}$$
- Distance BG:
$$BG = \frac{h}{2} - 1.5 = 0.4145 - 1.5 = -1.0855\,\text{m}$$
(Negative means $B$ is below $G$.)
- Metacentric height:
$$GM = BM - BG = 1.609 - (-1.0855) = 2.694\,\text{m}$$

**Metacentric height = 2.69 m (Stable, since $GM > 0$)** **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the metacentric height of a floating body. Define all terms used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wooden log of specific gravity $0.75$ has a length of $4\,\text{m}$ and a circular cross-section of diameter $0.5\,\text{m}$. It floats in water with its axis horizontal. Find: (i) the depth of immersion, and (ii) whether the floating log is stable.

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a):**
The derivation has been covered in full in **Section 3.2** of these notes. The final result is:

$$GM = \frac{I}{V_{disp}} - BG$$

Where:
- $I$ = Second moment of area of the waterline plane about the axis of tilting
- $V_{disp}$ = Volume of fluid displaced
- $BG$ = Vertical distance between the center of buoyancy and center of gravity
- $GM > 0 \Rightarrow$ Stable, $GM < 0 \Rightarrow$ Unstable **(7 marks)**

**Part (b):**
- Weight per unit length:
$$W/L = \rho_{wood} \cdot g \cdot A = 0.75 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(0.5)^2 = 1444.4\,\text{N/m}$$

- Buoyant force per unit length:
$$F_B/L = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot A_{sub} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(0.5)^2 \cdot (\theta_c / 180)$$

where $\theta_c$ is the central angle in radians for the submerged circular segment.

For circular floating body, equating $F_B$ to $W$ (and solving numerically using segment area tables or trial):

$$\theta_c \approx 168.2^\circ$$

Therefore:
- **Depth of immersion** $h = 0.5 - 0.5 \cos(84.1^\circ) \approx 0.45\,\text{m}$
- **Distance BG** $\approx 0.04\,\text{m}$
- **Submerged volume** $V_{disp} \approx 0.561\,\text{m}^3$
- **I (waterline segment)** $\approx 0.0043\,\text{m}^4$
- **$BM$** $= 0.0043 / 0.561 \approx 0.0077\,\text{m}$
- **$GM$** $= 0.0077 - 0.04 = -0.0323\,\text{m} < 0$

**Conclusion: The floating log is UNSTABLE in this orientation.** **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention for BG:** Many students forget the sign convention. If $B$ is *below* $G$, $BG$ is positive; if $B$ is *above* $G$, $BG$ is negative. KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for not mentioning the sign explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting $g$:** Some students compute $V_{disp}$ by dividing weight by $\rho$ alone (forgetting $g$). Always remember: $V_{disp} = W / (\rho \cdot g)$.
> 3. **Axis of Rotation:** In metacentric problems, the second moment $I$ must be computed about the *axis about which the body tilts*, not about an arbitrary axis.
> 4. **Units:** Carry SI units throughout. Mixing $\text{kN}$ and $\text{N}$ in the same numerical problem is a common mistake and warrants a **half-mark** deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:**

- **Buoyant force** = weight of fluid displaced: $F_B = \rho_f g V_{disp}$.
- **Archimedes' Principle** is the foundation of all floatation problems.
- **Center of Buoyancy (B)** is the centroid of the displaced fluid volume.
- **Center of Gravity (G)** is the centroid of the body's mass.
- **Metacenter (M)** is where the tilted buoyancy line meets the original centerline.
- **Metacentric radius** $BM = I / V_{disp}$, where $I$ is the second moment of the waterline area.
- **Metacentric height** $GM = BM - BG$.
- **Stability rule:** $GM > 0 \Rightarrow$ Stable, $GM = 0 \Rightarrow$ Neutral, $GM < 0 \Rightarrow$ Unstable.
- **Floating equilibrium** requires $F_B = W$, hence mass of body = mass of displaced fluid.
- **Submerged equilibrium** for a body of density $\rho_b$ in fluid of density $\rho_f$: body sinks if $\rho_b > \rho_f$, floats if $\rho_b < \rho_f$.
- **Apparent weight** of a submerged body = True weight − Buoyant force.
- **Specific gravity (S)** = $\rho_{body} / \rho_{water}$ — a dimensionless number.
- For a **floating cube** of side $a$ and specific gravity $S$, depth of immersion $h = a/S$.
- The **restoring couple** acting on a tilted floating body is $C = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$.
- KTU 2024 papers frequently mix **numerical and analytical** parts — practice both.
- For **circular cross-section floating bodies**, use segment-area tables for submerged volume calculations.
- Always quote $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$ unless the problem specifies otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metacenter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Metacenter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **METACENTER (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> The **Metacenter (M)** of a floating body is defined as the point about which a body starts oscillating when it is given a small angular displacement (heeled/rolled) about a horizontal axis through the center of buoyancy. It is the point of intersection of:
> 1. The line of action of the **buoyancy force** in the equilibrium (vertical) position, and
> 2. The line of action of the **buoyancy force** in the tilted (heeled) position.

For very small angles of tilt (typically $\theta \leq 15°$), the metacenter is assumed to remain **fixed** relative to the body, which simplifies all stability calculations.

### Supporting Points (G, B, M, KB, KG, BM, GM)

| Symbol | Name | Meaning |
|:------:|:----:|:--------|
| **G** | Center of Gravity | Point where the weight of the body acts |
| **B** | Center of Buoyancy | Centroid of the displaced (submerged) volume of fluid |
| **M** | Metacenter | Point of intersection of vertical and tilted buoyancy lines |
| **KB** | — | Distance from keel to center of buoyancy |
| **KG** | — | Distance from keel to center of gravity |
| **KM** | — | Distance from keel to metacenter |
| **BM** | Metacentric radius | Distance from center of buoyancy to metacenter |
| **GM** | Metacentric height | Distance from center of gravity to metacenter |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spinning Top"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — The Inflated Beach Ball and the Heavy Toy**
> Imagine a **light cork** floating upright in water and a **cork with a heavy iron nail glued at its top**. If you tilt both:
> - The **light cork** rights itself instantly — it is **stable**. Its metacenter lies *above* its center of gravity.
> - The **top-heavy cork** flips over — it is **unstable**. Its metacenter lies *below* its center of gravity.
>
> **Metacenter is simply the "balance pivot point"** that the fluid creates beneath the tilted body. Whether this pivot sits above or below the body's own weight decides whether the ship stays upright or capsizes.

## 1.3 Physical Significance

> [!TIP]
> The metacenter is **NOT** a fixed point on the body — its position depends on the shape of the waterline plane and the submerged volume. Naval architects spend enormous effort designing hulls so that **GM remains positive** in all loading conditions (this is the famous **"STABLE"** marking seen on ships).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Behind Metacentric Stability

The metacenter emerges from the interaction of two forces acting on a tilted floating body:

1. **Weight $W$** acts vertically **downward** through $G$.
2. **Buoyancy $F_B$** acts vertically **upward** through the *new* center of buoyancy $B_1$.

When the body heels by a small angle $\theta$:
- The buoyancy line shifts to pass through the **new center of buoyancy** $B_1$.
- The intersection of the **old vertical buoyancy line** and the **new tilted buoyancy line** defines the **metacenter $M$**.

### Stability Criterion (The Heart of the Concept)

The position of $M$ relative to $G$ determines stability:

| Condition | Stability | Behaviour |
|:---------:|:---------:|:----------|
| $M$ is **above** $G$ (i.e., $GM > 0$) | **Stable** ⭐ | Restoring couple brings body back |
| $M$ coincides with $G$ (i.e., $GM = 0$) | **Neutral** | Body remains in tilted position |
| $M$ is **below** $G$ (i.e., $GM < 0$) | **Unstable** ⚠ | Capsizing couple overturns the body |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does M lie "above" G for stable ships?**
> When the body tilts, the buoyancy shifts toward the deeper-immersed wedge side. This creates a *horizontal separation* between $W$ and $F_B$, producing a **restoring couple** $= W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ that pulls the body upright.

## 2.2 The Core Formulas

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Cheat-Sheet (memorise these!)**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Conditions |
|:-:|:-------:|:--------|:-----------|
| 1 | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{\text{submerged}}}$ | Metacentric radius | $I$ = Second moment of waterline area about axis of tilt |
| 2 | $KM = KB + BM$ | Total height of M from keel | — |
| 3 | $GM = KM - KG$ | Metacentric height | The single most important KTU quantity |
| 4 | $GM = BM - BG$ | Alternate form | $BG = KG - KB$ |
| 5 | $I = \dfrac{\pi \, d^{4}}{64}$ | M.O.I. for **circular** waterline (e.g., buoy) | $d$ = diameter |
| 6 | $I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^{3}}{12}$ | M.O.I. for **rectangular** waterline (e.g., ship) | $L$ = length, $B$ = beam (width) |
| 7 | $W = \rho_{f} \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{sub}}$ | Buoyancy = Weight at equilibrium | Floating condition |
| 8 | Restoring couple | $C_{R} = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ | For small $\theta$, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$ |
| 9 | Overturning couple | $C_{O} = W \cdot a \cdot \sin\theta_{m}$ | $a$ = distance of W from buoy's axis |
| 10 | Time period of oscillation | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{I_{M}}{W \cdot GM}} = \dfrac{2\pi \, k_{M}}{\sqrt{g \cdot GM}}$ | Roll/heel motion |
| 11 | Angular frequency | $\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\dfrac{W \cdot GM}{I_{M}}}$ | Natural roll frequency |

> Where:
> - $V_{\text{submerged}}$ = volume of liquid displaced (in $m^{3}$)
> - $I$ = second moment of area of waterline cross-section about axis of rotation (in $m^{4}$)
> - $I_{M}$ = mass moment of inertia about metacenter $= \dfrac{W \cdot k_{M}^{2}}{g}$
> - $k_{M}$ = radius of gyration about metacenter

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Naval Architecture:** Designing ship hulls with adequate $GM$ to avoid capsizing.
- **Submarine Design:** Maintaining positive $GM$ both surfaced and submerged.
- **Floating Docks & Pontoons:** Stability under varying load distributions.
- **Aerostat / Balloons:** Metacentric height of lighter-than-air craft.
- **Offshore Platforms:** Roll stability in waves.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Expression for $BM = I / V$

### Setup
Consider a floating body in equilibrium with:
- Submerged volume $= V$
- Original center of buoyancy at $B$
- After tilting by small angle $\theta$, the new buoyancy line passes through $B_1$

### Pictorial Setup (Verbal)

When the body tilts by angle $\theta$:
- One wedge-shaped volume $V_1$ (say on the **starboard** side) gets **submerged additionally**.
- An equal wedge $V_2$ on the **port** side gets **emerged**.
- The new center of buoyancy $B_1$ shifts **horizontally** toward the submerged wedge by distance $\overline{BB_1}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Shift in Center of Buoyancy:**
By the **principle of moments**, taking moments about the original vertical axis through $B$:

The volume $V_1$ is effectively *added* at a distance $\bar{x}_1$ from $B$, and $V_2$ is *subtracted* at $\bar{x}_2$.

$$\overline{BB_1} = \frac{V_1 \cdot \bar{x}_1 + V_2 \cdot \bar{x}_2}{V}$$

**Step 2 — Symmetry of Wedges:**
For equal wedge volumes $V_1 = V_2 = v$, and by symmetry $\bar{x}_1 = \bar{x}_2 = \bar{x}$:

$$\overline{BB_1} = \frac{v \cdot \bar{x} + v \cdot \bar{x}}{V} = \frac{2 \, v \, \bar{x}}{V}$$

**Step 3 — Geometry of the Wedge:**
Each wedge has its centroid at $\dfrac{2L}{3}$ from the apex (centroid of triangle from vertex is at $\dfrac{2}{3}$ the base length). For a small tilt $\theta$, the height of the wedge $\approx \dfrac{B}{2} \tan\theta$, and the centroid of each wedge lies at horizontal distance:

$$\bar{x} \approx \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cdot \tan\theta$$

where $L$ is the length along the axis of tilt direction.

But more generally, the moment of the wedge about the original center axis gives:

$$V \cdot \overline{BB_1} = I \cdot \tan\theta$$

where $I$ is the second moment of area of the waterline plane about the axis of rotation.

**Step 4 — Geometry of the Metacenter Triangle:**
From the geometry of the tilted body, point $M$ lies on the original vertical through $B$ such that:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{\overline{BB_1}}{\overline{BM}}$$

Substituting $\overline{BB_1} = \dfrac{I \tan\theta}{V}$:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{I \tan\theta}{V \cdot \overline{BM}}$$

**Step 5 — Final Result:**
Cancelling $\tan\theta$ on both sides:

$$\boxed{BM = \frac{I}{V_{\text{submerged}}}}$$

This is the **most-asked KTU formula** on metacenter.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Time Period of Rolling (Oscillation)

### Equation of Motion for a Heeling Body

For a body rotating about the metacenter $M$ with small angular displacement $\theta$:

**Restoring couple** $= W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta \approx W \cdot GM \cdot \theta$ (for small $\theta$)

**Moment of inertia about M** $= I_M$

Applying Newton's second law for rotation:

$$I_{M} \cdot \frac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}} = - W \cdot GM \cdot \theta$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}} + \left( \frac{W \cdot GM}{I_{M}} \right) \theta = 0$$

This is the standard SHM differential equation:

$$\frac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}} + \omega_{n}^{2} \, \theta = 0 \quad ; \quad \omega_{n} = \sqrt{\frac{W \cdot GM}{I_{M}}}$$

**Time Period of Oscillation:**

$$\boxed{T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_{n}} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{I_{M}}{W \cdot GM}}}$$

Substituting $I_{M} = \dfrac{W \, k_{M}^{2}}{g}$:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{2\pi \, k_{M}}{\sqrt{g \cdot GM}}}$$

where $k_M$ is the radius of gyration about the metacenter.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Analytical Matrix of Standard Waterline Shapes

| Waterline Shape | Second Moment $I$ | $BM = I/V$ |
|:---------------:|:-----------------:|:----------:|
| Rectangle ($L \times B$) | $\dfrac{L \cdot B^{3}}{12}$ | $\dfrac{L \, B^{3}}{12 \, V}$ |
| Square ($a \times a$) | $\dfrac{a^{4}}{12}$ | $\dfrac{a^{4}}{12 \, V}$ |
| Circle (diameter $d$) | $\dfrac{\pi \, d^{4}}{64}$ | $\dfrac{\pi \, d^{4}}{64 \, V}$ |
| Ellipse (axes $a$, $b$) | $\dfrac{\pi \, a \, b^{3}}{64}$ | $\dfrac{\pi \, a \, b^{3}}{64 \, V}$ |
| Triangle (base $b$, height $h$) | $\dfrac{b \, h^{3}}{36}$ | $\dfrac{b \, h^{3}}{36 \, V}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Alert:** In 80% of KTU numerical problems, the floating body is a **rectangular pontoon or a cylindrical buoy**. Recognise $I$ instantly to save time!

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture for the Metacentre Stability System — Mapping all key interactions, definitions, and stability paths.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Floating Body in Fluid] --> B[Identify Weight W acting at G]
    A --> C[Identify Buoyancy Fb acting at B]
    B --> D[Compute KG from Keel]
    C --> E[Compute KB from Keel]
    D --> F[Compute BG = KG minus KB]
    E --> G[Compute I of Waterline about Tilt Axis]
    A --> H[Submerged Volume V sub]
    G --> I[BM = I divided by V sub]
    I --> J[KM = KB plus BM]
    J --> K[GM = KM minus KG]
    K --> L{Decision: Sign of GM}
    L -->|GM greater than zero| M[STABLE Restoring couple = W times GM times sin theta]
    L -->|GM equals zero| N[NEUTRAL No restoring couple]
    L -->|GM less than zero| O[UNSTABLE Capsizing couple = W times GM times sin theta]
    M --> P[Time Period of Roll T equals 2 pi sqrt IM by W times GM]
    N --> Q[Body stays in tilted position]
    O --> R[Body overturns Capsize]
    P --> S[Engineered Ship with Positive GM]
    Q --> T[Critical State Immediate Attention Needed]
    R --> U[Redesign Hull or Shift Cargo Lower]
```

### Block-Level Stability Decision Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[INPUT Ship Data Length Beam Draft Depth] --> node2[Compute Displaced Volume V]
    node1 --> node3[Compute Waterline MOI I]
    node2 --> node4[Compute BM using formula]
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5[Compute KB via Centre of Buoyancy]
    node4 --> node6[Compute KM using KB plus BM]
    node1 --> node7[Compute KG from Mass Distribution]
    node6 --> node8[Compute GM equals KM minus KG]
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9{Is GM greater than 0}
    node9 -->|Yes| node10[Ship is Stable Pass]
    node9 -->|No| node11[Ship Unstable FAIL Redesign]
```

### Mermaid Concept Map — Relationships Between G, B, M

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[KEEL Reference Line] -->|KG| nodeG[CENTRE OF GRAVITY G]
    nodeA -->|KB| nodeB[CENTRE OF BUOYANCY B]
    nodeA -->|KM| nodeM[METACENTRE M]
    nodeG -->|BG positive when G above B| nodeB
    nodeB -->|BM equals I over V| nodeM
    nodeG -->|GM equals KM minus KG| nodeM
    nodeM -->|Stable if GM positive| nodeS[Restoring Couple Acts]
    nodeM -->|Unstable if GM negative| nodeU[Capsizing Couple Acts]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term Metacenter. Explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The metacenter (M) of a floating body is defined as the point of intersection of the line of action of the buoyancy force on the body in its equilibrium (vertical) position and the line of action of the buoyancy force on the body after it has been given a small angular displacement.
>
> **Physical Significance (1 mark):** The metacenter's position relative to the centre of gravity (G) determines the **stability** of the floating body.
> - If **M lies above G** (i.e., $GM > 0$), the body is **stable** and a restoring couple acts to right the body.
> - If **M lies below G** (i.e., $GM < 0$), the body is **unstable** and it tends to capsize.
>
> Hence, metacentre is the **most critical reference point** used in naval architecture and ship design to ensure floating stability.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**State the formula for metacentric height and explain each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\boxed{GM = \frac{I}{V_{\text{submerged}}} - BG}$$

> Where:
> - $GM$ = Metacentric height (distance from G to M) in metres
> - $I$ = Second moment of area of the waterline plane about the axis of rotation, in $m^{4}$
> - $V_{\text{submerged}}$ = Volume of the submerged portion of the body, in $m^{3}$
> - $BG$ = Vertical distance between centre of buoyancy B and centre of gravity G, in metres
>
> **Stability Condition (½ mark):** For stable floating, $GM$ must be **positive**, i.e., $I/V > BG$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A rectangular pontoon is **6 m long**, **4 m wide**, and weighs **350 kN** in air. It floats in sea water of specific gravity **1.025**. Find the depth of immersion and the position of the centre of buoyancy.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A solid cylinder of diameter **2 m** and height **2 m** weighs **40 kN**. It floats with its axis vertical. Calculate (i) the depth of immersion, (ii) the metacentric height, and (iii) state whether the cylinder is in stable equilibrium.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Pontoon

**Step 1 — Weight and Buoyancy Equate:**
For floating, weight = buoyancy:
$$W = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{sub}$$
$$350 \times 10^{3} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times (6 \times 4 \times h)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for depth $h$:**

$$h = \frac{350 \times 10^{3}}{1025 \times 9.81 \times 24}$$

$$h = \frac{350000}{241308} = 1.451 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 1.451 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3 — Centre of Buoyancy Position:**
For a rectangular block with uniform immersion, the centre of buoyancy lies at the centroid of the submerged volume, i.e., at $h/2$ from the base (keel):

$$KB = \frac{h}{2} = \frac{1.451}{2} = 0.7255 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{KB \approx 0.726 \text{ m}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Cylinder

**Given:** Diameter $D = 2$ m, height $H = 2$ m, weight $W = 40$ kN.

**Step 1 — Depth of Immersion $h$:**
Submerged volume $= \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} \cdot h = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(2)^{2} h = \pi h$

By floating condition:
$$W = \rho_{w} \cdot g \cdot \pi \cdot h$$
$$40 \times 10^{3} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times \pi \times h$$
$$h = \frac{40000}{30819.6} = 1.298 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 1.298 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 2 — Metacentric Radius $BM$:**
For circular waterline, $I = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{64}$:

$$I = \frac{\pi \times (2)^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 16}{64} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 0.7854 \text{ m}^{4}$$

$$V_{sub} = \pi h = \pi \times 1.298 = 4.078 \text{ m}^{3}$$

$$BM = \frac{I}{V_{sub}} = \frac{0.7854}{4.078} = 0.1926 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{BM \approx 0.193 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $KG$:**
For a uniform solid cylinder, the centre of gravity lies at the geometric centre:

$$KG = \frac{H}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $KB$:**
$$KB = \frac{h}{2} = \frac{1.298}{2} = 0.649 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $GM$:**
$$KM = KB + BM = 0.649 + 0.193 = 0.842 \text{ m}$$

$$GM = KM - KG = 0.842 - 1.0 = -0.158 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{GM \approx -0.158 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 6 — Stability Conclusion:**
Since $GM < 0$, the metacenter lies **below** the centre of gravity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Sign Convention Mistakes!**
> - Always check the sign of $GM$. A **negative $GM$** means **UNSTABLE**.
> - Don't forget to subtract $KG$ from $KM$ — many students mistakenly compute $GM = KM + KG$.
> - For Part (b), many students forget that for a circular cylinder, $I = \pi D^{4}/64$, NOT $\pi r^{4}/4$.

**Conclusion:** The cylinder is in **UNSTABLE equilibrium**. When tilted, it will capsize rather than return to vertical.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A rectangular barge of length **25 m**, width **8 m**, and draft **2 m** displaces a weight of **4000 kN**. A load of **500 kN** is placed on the barge at a height of **3 m** above the waterline. Determine the new metacentric height. Comment on the stability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A buoy is in the form of a hollow cylinder of outer diameter **1.5 m**, inner diameter **1.0 m**, and height **1.8 m**. It weighs **12 kN** and is anchored to the seabed. Find the maximum height above the waterline at which the cable can be attached without tilting the buoy.

**Model Solution Outline:**

#### Part (a) — Barge with Load

**Step 1 — Original state:** For a rectangular waterline, $I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^{3}}{12}$

$$I = \frac{25 \times 8^{3}}{12} = \frac{25 \times 512}{12} = 1066.67 \text{ m}^{4}$$

**Step 2 — New displaced volume:** Total weight = $4000 + 500 = 4500$ kN

$$V_{sub} = \frac{4500 \times 10^{3}}{1000 \times 9.81} = 458.72 \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — $BM = I/V_{sub}$:**

$$BM = \frac{1066.67}{458.72} = 2.325 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — New $KG$ via composite centre of gravity formula:**

$$KG_{new} = \frac{W_{1} \cdot KG_{1} + W_{2} \cdot KG_{2}}{W_{1} + W_{2}}$$

where $W_{1} = 4000$ kN with $KG_{1} = 1.0$ m (centroid at half the draft), and $W_{2} = 500$ kN with $KG_{2} = 2 + 3 = 5$ m.

$$KG_{new} = \frac{4000 \times 1.0 + 500 \times 5.0}{4500} = \frac{6500}{4500} = 1.444 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — $KB = h/2$ for rectangular barge:** $KB = 1.0$ m (since draft changes, recompute):
$$h_{new} = \frac{V_{sub}}{L \times B} = \frac{458.72}{25 \times 8} = 2.2936 \text{ m}$$

$$KB_{new} = \frac{h_{new}}{2} = 1.147 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — $GM = BM - BG$:**

$$BG = KG - KB = 1.444 - 1.147 = 0.297 \text{ m}$$

$$GM = BM - BG = 2.325 - 0.297 = 2.028 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{GM \approx 2.03 \text{ m (Stable)}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Hollow Cylinder Buoy

**Step 1 — Submerged Volume at equilibrium:**

Buoyancy weight = weight of buoy = $12$ kN
$$V_{sub} = \frac{12000}{1000 \times 9.81} = 1.223 \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Depth of immersion $h$:** Cross-sectional area of hollow cylinder:
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^{2} - D_i^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4}(1.5^{2} - 1.0^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4}(2.25 - 1.0) = \frac{\pi}{4}(1.25) = 0.9817 \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$h = \frac{V_{sub}}{A} = \frac{1.223}{0.9817} = 1.246 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Freeboard above water:**
$$f = H - h = 1.8 - 1.246 = 0.554 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Position of G from base:**
For uniform hollow cylinder: $KG = H/2 = 0.9$ m

**Step 5 — $KB = h/2 = 0.623$ m**

**Step 6 — $I$ of circular waterline area:**
$$I = \frac{\pi D_o^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi (1.5)^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 5.0625}{64} = 0.2485 \text{ m}^{4}$$

$$BM = \frac{I}{V_{sub}} = \frac{0.2485}{1.223} = 0.2032 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — $GM$ without cable moment:**
$$KM = KB + BM = 0.623 + 0.2032 = 0.8262 \text{ m}$$
$$GM = KM - KG = 0.8262 - 0.9 = -0.0738 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8 — Stability limit at cable:** Let the cable be attached at height $y$ above the waterline, applying a horizontal pull $P$ at distance $y$ above waterline (i.e., $h + y$ from keel).

For the buoy to remain vertical (no tilt), the restoring couple must balance the overturning couple:

$$W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta + W \cdot BG \cdot \sin\theta \geq P \cdot (h + y - KG)$$

For the **maximum** $y$ at which the buoy is just about to tilt (limit of stability), we use:

$$W \cdot \overline{GM} = P \cdot y_{\max}$$

Solving in detail (this requires equating couples):
- Overturning moment due to cable pull: $C_O = P \cdot \bar{y}$
- Restoring moment of gravity-buoyancy system: $C_R = W \cdot \overline{GM}$ (in absolute terms)

The maximum height is found by:
$$\boxed{y_{\max} = \frac{W \cdot GM}{P} + KG - KB}$$

Substituting values yields the numerical answer for $y_{\max}$ in metres.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Hollow Cylinder Waterline MOI!**
> - For hollow cylinders, students often mistakenly compute $I$ using the **inner diameter**. KTU requires you to use the **OUTER diameter** because that is what is in contact with water (waterline plane).
> - Also remember that $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^{2} - D_i^{2})$ for hollow sections (affects depth $h$).
> - In Part (b), the cable attachment height is measured **from the waterline**, not from the keel!

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Metacenter $M$ = intersection of original vertical buoyancy line and tilted buoyancy line.
- ⭐ **Master Formula:** $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{\text{submerged}}}$ — used in ~90% of KTU numericals.
- ⭐ **Stability Criterion:** $GM > 0$ → Stable; $GM = 0$ → Neutral; $GM < 0$ → Unstable.
- ⭐ **Metacentric Height:** $GM = KM - KG = BM - BG = \dfrac{I}{V} - (KG - KB)$.
- ⭐ **Waterline MOI's:**
  - Rectangle ($L \times B$): $I = \dfrac{L B^{3}}{12}$
  - Circle (diameter $d$): $I = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{64}$
  - Square: $I = \dfrac{a^{4}}{12}$
- ⭐ **$BG = KG - KB$:** Always positive when $G$ is above $B$ (typical for ships).
- ⭐ **Restoring Couple:** $C_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ (the "stability engine" of a ship).
- ⭐ **Time Period of Roll:** $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{I_M}{W \cdot GM}} = \dfrac{2\pi \, k_M}{\sqrt{g \cdot GM}}$.
- ⭐ **Centroid of a triangle from vertex:** $\dfrac{2}{3}$ from the vertex — critical for wedge derivations.
- ⭐ **Units Vigilance:** $I$ in $m^4$, $V$ in $m^3$, $BM$ in $m$, $W$ in $N$ or $kN$.
- ⭐ **Typical KTU Problem Pattern:** Rectangular pontoon OR circular buoy OR hollow cylinder with cable load.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Metacenter (PCAUT302 | KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Center of Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Center of Pressure ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Center of Pressure (CP):** The point on the surface of a submerged (or partially submerged) body at which the **resultant hydrostatic pressure force** is assumed to act. It is mathematically defined as the point where the line of action of the total hydrostatic thrust intersects the immersed surface. In rigorous fluid statics terminology, if a plane surface of area $A$ is immersed in a static fluid, the pressure distribution is **non-uniform** (it varies linearly with depth). The Center of Pressure is therefore the location on that surface where the *equivalent single concentrated force* would produce the same external mechanical effect (moment) as the distributed pressure force. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction — Centroid vs. Center of Pressure** > - **Centroid (C):** The geometric center of the surface area. Determined purely by **geometry**. > - **Center of Pressure (P):** The point of application of the resultant pressure force. Determined by **geometry + fluid pressure distribution**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are pushing a **large rectangular wooden board** underwater into a swimming pool, holding it vertically. If you press it straight in, you will intuitively feel that: 1. The **top** of the board feels almost no force (because water near the surface has negligible pressure). 2. The **bottom** of the board feels a strong push (because deep water has high pressure). This pressure distribution is **triangular** — zero at the top, maximum at the bottom. Now, if a single strong friend were to push the board with the same total force, where would they have to push? 👉 **Answer:** Not in the middle of the board (which is the centroid), but **closer to the bottom** — because the lower half experiences greater pressure. This lower location where the equivalent single force effectively acts is the **Center of Pressure**. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3 = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ - **Density of water:** $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - **Specific weight of mercury:** $\gamma_{Hg} = 133.1 \text{ kN/m}^3$ - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - **Atmospheric pressure (standard):** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU board examinations, the **Center of Pressure** is exclusively analyzed for **plane surfaces** immersed in **static, incompressible fluids** under the action of **gravity**. Curved surfaces are handled via horizontal and vertical force decomposition, not via direct CP location. --- ### 1.4 Conditions Where CP and Centroid Coincide | Condition | Reason | |---|---| | Surface is **horizontal** (parallel to free surface) | Pressure is uniform across the surface ($P = \gamma h$ = constant) | | Surface is immersed in a fluid of **uniform pressure** (rare, conceptual only) | No pressure gradient → resultant passes through centroid | | Surface lies in the **horizontal plane through the free surface** | Pressure everywhere = atmospheric (gauge = 0) | In **all other cases**, the Center of Pressure lies **below** the Centroid (for surfaces with at least one point submerged below the free surface). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Pressure Variation on an Immersed Plane Consider a plane surface immersed in a liquid at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal free surface. Let us establish the reference frame: - **Free surface** = datum (pressure = 0 gauge). - Surface is inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. - The **centroid C** of the surface lies at vertical depth $\bar{h}$ below the free surface. - Consider an elemental strip at vertical depth $h$ from the free surface, located at slant distance $l$ from the line where the inclined surface (extended) meets the free surface. The relationship between slant distance and vertical depth: $$h = l \cdot \sin\theta$$ Pressure on the elemental strip: $$dP = \gamma \cdot h \cdot dA = \gamma \cdot l \cdot \sin\theta \cdot dA$$ ### 2.2 Logic Steps — Why CP is Below Centroid 1. **Pressure increases linearly with depth:** According to the hydrostatic law $P = \gamma h$, every deeper strip experiences a proportionally greater force. 2. **Greater force at greater depths:** The lower half of the surface experiences a larger cumulative force than the upper half. 3. **Moment equivalence:** For the resultant force to produce the same moment as the distributed forces, it must act closer to the region of higher force. 4. **Conclusion:** Therefore, the Center of Pressure is **always below** the Centroid (for a fully submerged or partially submerged plane surface in a homogeneous fluid). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fundamental Theorem of Center of Pressure:** *The Center of Pressure of a plane area submerged in a homogeneous fluid is the same as the Centroid of the "Pressure Prism" (force-volume diagram) constructed over the area.* ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The following formulas are the absolute high-yield items for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize the boxed formulas.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Total Hydrostatic Thrust (Force) | $F = \gamma \cdot \bar{h} \cdot A$ | N (or kN) | Acts at the Center of Pressure | | 2 | Depth of Center of Pressure (vertical) | $h^* = \dfrac{I_{xx} \cdot \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} \cdot A} + \bar{h}$ | m | Most general formula | | 3 | Distance of CP from Free Surface (along slant) | $l^* = \dfrac{I_{xx}}{A \cdot \bar{h} \cdot \sin\theta}$ | m | When datum is the free surface | | 4 | Distance of CP from centroid (along slant) | $l^* -
