\bar{l} = \dfrac{I_{xx}}{A \cdot \bar{h} \cdot \sin\theta} - \dfrac{\bar{h}}{\sin\theta}$ | m | Shows CP is below centroid | | 5 | Simplified form (depth below free surface) | $h^* = \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}} + \bar{h}$ | m | When centroid is at depth $\bar{h}$ | | 6 | For Vertical Surface ($\theta = 90°$) | $h^* = \dfrac{I_G}{A \bar{h}} + \bar{h}$ | m | $\sin^2 90° = 1$ | | 7 | For Horizontal Surface ($\theta = 0°$) | $h^* = \bar{h}$ | m | CP coincides with centroid | | 8 | Moment of Inertia of Rectangle (about centroidal axis parallel to free surface) | $I_G = \dfrac{b d^3}{12}$ | m⁴ | $b$ = width, $d$ = depth of immersed side | | 9 | Moment of Inertia of Circle | $I_G = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$ | m⁴ | $D$ = diameter | | 10 | Moment of Inertia of Triangle (base horizontal) | $I_G = \dfrac{b h^3}{36}$ | m⁴ | Apex pointing up or down | Where the symbols denote: - $\gamma$ = specific weight of fluid (N/m³) - $\bar{h}$ = vertical depth of centroid from free surface (m) - $A$ = area of submerged surface (m²) - $\theta$ = angle of the inclined surface with the horizontal free surface - $I_G$ or $I_{xx}$ = second moment of area (moment of inertia) about the centroidal axis parallel to the free surface (m⁴) ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The Center of Pressure concept is not merely an academic exercise — it is foundational in: | Engineering Field | Application | |---|---| | **Hydraulic Gates & Dams** | Determining the hinge location and resultant force on sluice gates, lock gates, tainter gates | | **Ship Design (Naval Architecture)** | Calculating the metacenter and stability of floating vessels; CP on hull surfaces | | **Submarine Design** | Locating the effective point of pressure application for ballast calculations | | **Dam Design** | Designing spillway gates, intake structures, and bulkhead walls | | **Pressure Vessels** | Locating the point of action of fluid thrust on flat end closures | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When the surface is **vertical**, $\theta = 90°$ and $\sin\theta = 1$. In this case, slant distance = vertical depth, and the formula simplifies dramatically. **Most KTU problems use vertical surfaces** — be prepared! ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the General Center of Pressure Formula **Setup:** Consider a plane surface of arbitrary shape immersed in a static, incompressible fluid at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal free surface. **Step 1 — Define the elemental strip** Take an elemental strip of area $dA$ parallel to the free surface, located at a slant distance $l$ from the line where the plane (extended) meets the free surface. **Step 2 — Express the depth of the strip** The vertical depth of the strip below the free surface is: $$h = l \sin\theta$$ **Step 3 — Pressure on the elemental strip** By the hydrostatic pressure equation: $$p = \gamma h = \gamma \cdot l \cdot \sin\theta$$ **Step 4 — Force on the elemental strip** $$dF = p \cdot dA = \gamma \cdot l \cdot \sin\theta \cdot dA$$ **Step 5 — Total force on the surface (integration over area)** $$F = \int_A dF = \int_A \gamma \cdot l \cdot \sin\theta \cdot dA$$ $$F = \gamma \sin\theta \int_A l \cdot dA$$ **Step 6 — Recognize the first moment of area** The integral $\int_A l \cdot dA$ is the **first moment of area** of the surface about the free-surface line (intersection of the plane with the free surface). By definition, the first moment of area equals $A \cdot \bar{l}$, where $\bar{l}$ is the slant distance of the centroid from the free surface intersection line. $$\int_A l \cdot dA = A \cdot \bar{l}$$ **Step 7 — Express total force in terms of centroid depth** Since $\bar{h} = \bar{l} \sin\theta$, we have $\bar{l} = \bar{h} / \sin\theta$. Substituting: $$F = \gamma \sin\theta \cdot A \cdot \frac{\bar{h}}{\sin\theta}$$ $$\boxed{F = \gamma \cdot \bar{h} \cdot A}$$ **Step 8 — Take moments about the free surface line to find CP** Let $l^*$ be the slant distance of the Center of Pressure from the free surface intersection. The moment of the resultant force about this line must equal the sum of moments of elemental forces: $$F \cdot l^* = \int_A (l \cdot dF) \cdot l = \int_A l^2 \cdot dF$$ **Step 9 — Substitute $dF$ expression** $$F \cdot l^* = \int_A l^2 \cdot (\gamma \cdot l \cdot \sin\theta) \cdot dA$$ $$F \cdot l^* = \gamma \sin\theta \int_A l^3 \cdot dA$$ Wait — let us correct this. The element $dA$ is at distance $l$ from the intersection line, and the force $dF$ acts at $l$. So the moment is: $$F \cdot l^* = \int_A l \cdot dF = \int_A l \cdot (\gamma \cdot l \cdot \sin\theta) \cdot dA = \gamma \sin\theta \int_A l^2 \, dA$$ **Step 10 — Recognize the second moment of area** The integral $\int_A l^2 \, dA$ is the **second moment of area** (moment of inertia) of the surface about the intersection line with the free surface. Denote it as $I$. $$\int_A l^2 \, dA = I$$ **Step 11 — Solve for $l^*$** $$F \cdot l^* = \gamma \sin\theta \cdot I$$ $$l^* = \frac{\gamma \sin\theta \cdot I}{F} = \frac{\gamma \sin\theta \cdot I}{\gamma \bar{h} A} = \frac{I \sin\theta}{\bar{h} A}$$ $$\boxed{l^* = \frac{I}{A \bar{l}}}$$ where $\bar{l} = \bar{h} / \sin\theta$ is the slant distance of the centroid from the free surface. **Step 12 — Apply the parallel axis theorem to express $I$ in terms of the centroidal moment of inertia** By the parallel axis theorem: $$I = I_G + A \bar{l}^2$$ where $I_G$ is the moment of inertia about the centroidal axis (parallel to the free-surface intersection line). **Step 13 — Final form of $l^*$** $$l^* = \frac{I_G + A \bar{l}^2}{A \bar{l}} = \frac{I_G}{A \bar{l}} + \bar{l}$$ $$\boxed{l^* = \bar{l} + \frac{I_G}{A \bar{l}}}$$ The second term $\dfrac{I_G}{A \bar{l}}$ represents the **distance from the centroid to the Center of Pressure** measured along the slant. ### 3.2 Alternative Form — Depth Below Free Surface Multiplying $l^*$ by $\sin\theta$ gives the vertical depth of the CP: $$h^* = l^* \sin\theta = \bar{l} \sin\theta + \frac{I_G \sin\theta}{A \bar{l}}$$ Since $\bar{h} = \bar{l} \sin\theta$: $$h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}$$ $$\boxed{h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation for Special Case — Vertical Rectangular Gate (Vertical Surface) **Setup:** A vertical rectangular gate of width $b$ and height $d$, with its **top edge** at the free surface. **Step 1 — Identify parameters** - Area: $A = b \cdot d$ - Centroid depth: $\bar{h} = d/2$ - Moment of inertia about centroidal axis: $I_G = \dfrac{b d^3}{12}$ - Angle: $\theta = 90°$ (vertical), so $\sin^2\theta = 1$ **Step 2 — Apply the general formula** $$h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute values** $$h^* = \frac{d}{2} + \frac{\left(\dfrac{b d^3}{12}\right)(1)}{(b d)\left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the fraction** $$h^* = \frac{d}{2} + \frac{b d^3 / 12}{b d^2 / 2} = \frac{d}{2} + \frac{d^3 \cdot 2}{12 \cdot d^2} = \frac{d}{2} + \frac{d}{6}$$ $$h^* = \frac{3d + d}{6} = \frac{4d}{6} = \frac{2d}{3}$$ $$\boxed{h^* = \frac{2d}{3}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this result!** For a vertical rectangular surface with its top edge at the free surface, the Center of Pressure is located at **2/3 of the depth from the top** (or equivalently, **1/3 from the bottom**). This is the single most-tested CP result in KTU exams. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of CP and Centroid for Common Surfaces | Surface Type | Centroid Depth ($\bar{h}$) | CP Depth ($h^*$) | CP below Centroid by | |---|---|---|---| | Vertical rectangle, top at free surface | $d/2$ | $2d/3$ | $d/6$ | | Vertical rectangle, top at depth $h_1$, bottom at $h_1 + d$ | $h_1 + d/2$ | $h_1 + \dfrac{d(2h_1 + d)}{2(2h_1 + d) \cdot...}$ | (varies) | | Vertical circle, top at free surface | $D/2$ | $D/2 + \dfrac{D}{12}$ | $D/12$ | | Vertical circle, center at depth $\bar{h}$ | $\bar{h}$ | $\bar{h} + \dfrac{D^2}{16\bar{h}}$ | $\dfrac{D^2}{16\bar{h}}$ | | Inclined rectangle (top at free surface) | $\dfrac{d \sin\theta}{2}$ | $\dfrac{2d \sin\theta}{3}$ | $\dfrac{d \sin\theta}{6}$ | ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style) **Problem:** A vertical rectangular gate, 2 m wide and 3 m deep, is installed with its top edge at the water surface. Find: 1. Total hydrostatic thrust on the gate 2. Depth of the Center of Pressure below the free surface **Solution:** **Given:** - Width: $b = 2$ m - Depth: $d = 3$ m - $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ - Surface vertical: $\theta = 90°$ **Part (1): Total Hydrostatic Thrust** $$F = \gamma \cdot \bar{h} \cdot A$$ $$\bar{h} = \frac{d}{2} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 \text{ m}$$ $$A = b \cdot d = 2 \times 3 = 6 \text{ m}^2$$ $$F = 9.81 \times 1.5 \times 6 = 88.29 \text{ kN}$$ $$\boxed{F = 88.29 \text{ kN}}$$ **Part (2): Depth of Center of Pressure** For a vertical rectangle with top at free surface, using the derived result: $$h^* = \frac{2d}{3} = \frac{2 \times 3}{3} = 2 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{h^* = 2 \text{ m below free surface}}$$ **Verification using general formula:** $$h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}$$ $$I_G = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times 3^3}{12} = \frac{54}{12} = 4.5 \text{ m}^4$$ $$h^* = 1.5 + \frac{4.5 \times 1}{6 \times 1.5} = 1.5 + \frac{4.5}{9} = 1.5 + 0.5 = 2 \text{ m} \checkmark$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Immersed Plane Surface — Geometric Setup (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD A1[Free Surface of Liquid
Pressure = 0 gauge]:::surface B1[Intersection Line
Plane meets free surface]:::line C1[Centroid C of Surface
Depth h-bar below surface]:::point D1[Center of Pressure P
Depth h-star below surface]:::point E1[Inclined Plane Surface
Angle theta with horizontal]:::plane F1[Elemental Strip dA
at slant distance l from line]:::element G1[Fluid Region above surface
Specific weight gamma]:::fluid A1 -->|Intersects| B1 B1 -->|Slant distance l-bar| C1 C1 -->|Distance I_G divided by A l-bar| D1 E1 -->|Contains| F1 G1 -->|Exerts pressure on| E1 classDef surface fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef line fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B7500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef point fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef plane fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef element fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fluid fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Pressure Prism — The Heart of CP Visualization ```mermaid graph LR subgraph PressurePrism direction LR PP1[Pressure = 0
at top of surface]:::zero PP2[Pressure = gamma x h
linear increase with depth]:::linear PP3[Pressure maximum
at bottom of surface]:::max PP4[Centroid C
Geometric center]:::point PP5[Center of Pressure P
Below centroid]:::point PP1 -->|Linear gradient| PP2 PP2 -->|Continues to| PP3 PP4 -->|P is located here| PP5 end classDef zero fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#000 classDef linear fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 classDef max fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000 classDef point fill:#FF9800,stroke:#E65100,color:#FFF ``` ### 4.3 Vertical Rectangular Gate — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TB Input1[Width b = 2 m]:::input Input2[Depth d = 3 m]:::input Input3[Specific Weight gamma = 9.81 kN/m-cube]:::input Input4[Angle theta = 90 deg vertical]:::input Process1[Compute Area A = b x d]:::calc Process2[Compute Centroid Depth h-bar = d / 2]:::calc Process3[Compute Moment of Inertia I_G = b x d-cubed / 12]:::calc Process4[Compute Total Force F = gamma x h-bar x A]:::calc Process5[Compute CP Depth h-star = h-bar + I_G sin-squared theta divided by A h-bar]:::calc Output1[Total Force F = 88.29 kN]:::output Output2[Center of Pressure at 2 m depth]:::output Output3[CP is 0.5 m below Centroid]:::output Input1 --> Process1 Input2 --> Process1 Input1 --> Process3 Input2 --> Process3 Input2 --> Process2 Input3 --> Process4 Process2 --> Process4 Process1 --> Process4 Process2 --> Process5 Process3 --> Process5 Process1 --> Process5 Process4 --> Output1 Process5 --> Output2 Process2 --> Output3 Process5 --> Output3 classDef input ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Identify immersed surface parameters]):::start S1[Determine slant angle theta]:::step S2[Locate centroid C and depth h-bar]:::step S3[Compute area A]:::step S4[Compute moment of inertia I_G]:::step S5[Apply formula F = gamma h-bar A]:::step S6[Apply formula h-star = h-bar + I_G sin-squared theta divided by A h-bar]:::step S7[Compute slant distance l-star = h-star divided by sin theta]:::step End([Report F at depth h-star on surface]):::end Start --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> End classDef start fill:#4CAF50,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#FFF classDef step fill:#FFEB3B,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 classDef end fill:#F44336,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#FFF ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]** **Q1. Define Center of Pressure. When does it coincide with the Centroid of the immersed surface?** **Model Answer:** The **Center of Pressure** is defined as the point on the immersed surface where the **resultant hydrostatic pressure force** is assumed to act. It is the point through which the line of action of the total thrust passes. The Center of Pressure coincides with the Centroid under the following conditions: 1. When the immersed surface is **horizontal** (parallel to the free surface), since pressure is uniform across the surface. 2. When the surface lies entirely **at the free surface** (zero gauge pressure everywhere). For any other orientation (vertical or inclined submerged surface), the CP lies **below** the Centroid. > *Examiner's note (3 marks): Definition [2 marks] + Coincidence condition [1 mark]* --- > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Q2. A vertical rectangular gate of 2 m width and 1.5 m depth has its top edge at the free surface of water. Find the depth of the Center of Pressure.** **Model Answer:** For a vertical rectangle with top at the free surface: $$h^* = \frac{2d}{3} = \frac{2 \times 1.5}{3} = 1.0 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{h^* = 1.0 \text{ m below free surface}}$$ > *Examiner's note (3 marks): Direct formula application [2 marks] + Correct substitution and answer [1 mark]* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > Common student mistakes that lead to mark deductions in CP problems: > 1. **Forgetting units** — $\gamma$ must be in N/m³ (not kg/m³) or kN/m³ consistently. > 2. **Mixing slant and vertical distances** — when $\theta \neq 90°$, slant distance $l$ ≠ vertical depth $h$. Convert carefully. > 3. **Applying the wrong moment of inertia** — students often use $I_G$ when they need $I$ (about free surface), or vice versa. The parallel axis theorem is essential. > 4. **Confusing "depth of centroid from free surface" with "depth of top edge"** — always read the geometry carefully. > 5. **Forgetting $\sin^2\theta$ factor** — for inclined surfaces, omitting this gives a wrong answer. > 6. **Not stating "below centroid" or "below free surface"** — always specify the reference. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** Derive an expression for the depth of the Center of Pressure for a plane surface immersed in a liquid at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal free surface. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** A circular plate of diameter 2 m is immersed vertically in water with its **upper edge at the free surface**. Calculate: - (i) The total hydrostatic thrust on the plate - (ii) The depth of the Center of Pressure below the free surface **\[7 Marks\]** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation:** Consider an inclined plane surface of arbitrary shape submerged in a static homogeneous liquid. Let the surface make an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal free surface. Take the line of intersection of the plane (extended) with the free surface as the reference axis. Consider an elemental strip of area $dA$ parallel to the free surface, at slant distance $l$ from the reference axis. The vertical depth of this strip is: $$h = l \sin\theta$$ The pressure intensity on the strip: $$p = \gamma h = \gamma l \sin\theta$$ The force on the elemental strip: $$dF = p \cdot dA = \gamma l \sin\theta \, dA$$ **Total Force on the surface:** $$F = \int_A dF = \gamma \sin\theta \int_A l \, dA$$ Since $\int_A l \, dA = A \bar{l}$ (first moment of area): $$F = \gamma \sin\theta \cdot A \bar{l}$$ But $\bar{l} \sin\theta = \bar{h}$ (depth of centroid), so: $$\boxed{F = \gamma \bar{h} A}$$ **Location of Center of Pressure:** Taking moments about the reference axis: $$F \cdot l^* = \int_A l \cdot dF = \int_A l \cdot (\gamma l \sin\theta) dA = \gamma \sin\theta \int_A l^2 dA$$ Since $\int_A l^2 dA = I$ (second moment of area about reference axis): $$F \cdot l^* = \gamma \sin\theta \cdot I$$ $$l^* = \frac{\gamma \sin\theta \cdot I}{F} = \frac{\gamma \sin\theta \cdot I}{\gamma \bar{h} A} = \frac{I \sin\theta}{\bar{h} A}$$ Using the parallel axis theorem $I = I_G + A \bar{l}^2$: $$l^* = \frac{(I_G + A \bar{l}^2)\sin\theta}{\bar{h} A} = \frac{I_G \sin\theta}{\bar{h} A} + \frac{A \bar{l}^2 \sin\theta}{\bar{h} A}$$ Since $\bar{h} = \bar{l} \sin\theta$: $$l^* = \frac{I_G \sin\theta}{\bar{h} A} + \bar{l}$$ Multiplying both sides by $\sin\theta$ (to get vertical depth): $$\boxed{h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}}$$ This is the **general expression for the depth of Center of Pressure** for a plane surface. --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** - Diameter $D = 2$ m, so radius $R = 1$ m - Vertical surface: $\theta = 90°$, so $\sin^2\theta = 1$ - Upper edge at free surface - $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ **Step 1: Calculate area** $$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 4}{4} = \pi = 3.1416 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2: Depth of centroid** Since the upper edge is at the free surface and the plate is vertical (full diameter submerged vertically): $$\bar{h} = \frac{D}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3: Total Hydrostatic Thrust** $$F = \gamma \bar{h} A = 9.81 \times 1 \times 3.1416$$ $$\boxed{F = 30.82 \text{ kN}}$$ **Step 4: Moment of inertia of circle about centroidal axis** $$I_G = \frac{\pi D^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 16}{64} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 0.7854 \text{ m}^4$$ **Step 5: Depth of Center of Pressure** $$h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}} = 1 + \frac{0.7854 \times 1}{3.1416 \times 1}$$ $$h^* = 1 + 0.25 = 1.25 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{h^* = 1.25 \text{ m below free surface}}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** State the **parallel axis theorem** for moment of inertia. A square plate of side 2 m is immersed vertically in water with its top edge at a depth of 1 m below the free surface. Find the total hydrostatic thrust and the depth of the Center of Pressure. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** An inclined rectangular plate 3 m × 2 m (longer side along the slope) is submerged in water. The plate makes an angle of 30° with the horizontal free surface, and its **upper edge is at the free surface**. Determine: - (i) The total hydrostatic thrust on the plate - (ii) The position of the Center of Pressure measured along the inclined surface from the free surface line **\[7 Marks\]** --- **Model Solution:** **(a)** **Parallel Axis Theorem (Statement):** The moment of inertia of a plane area about any axis parallel to its centroidal axis is equal to the moment of inertia about the centroidal axis plus the product of the area and the square of the perpendicular distance between the two axes: $$I = I_G + A d^2$$ where $d$ is the distance between the two parallel axes. **Numerical Solution:** **Given:** - Square plate: side $a = 2$ m - Top edge at depth $h_1 = 1$ m - Vertical: $\theta = 90°$ **Area and Centroid:** $$A = a^2 = 4 \text{ m}^2$$ Centroid is at the geometric center of the square, so depth of centroid: $$\bar{h} = h_1 + \frac{a}{2} = 1 + 1 = 2 \text{ m}$$ **Moment of Inertia (about centroidal horizontal axis):** $$I_G = \frac{a^4}{12} = \frac{2^4}{12} = \frac{16}{12} = 1.333 \text{ m}^4$$ **Total Thrust:** $$F = \gamma \bar{h} A = 9.81 \times 2 \times 4 = 78.48 \text{ kN}$$ $$\boxed{F = 78.48 \text{ kN}}$$ **Depth of Center of Pressure:** $$h^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}} = 2 + \frac{1.333 \times 1}{4 \times 2} = 2 + 0.1667$$ $$\boxed{h^* = 2.1667 \text{ m below free surface}}$$ --- **(b)** **Given:** - Inclined rectangle: length along slope $L = 3$ m, width along slope $W = 2$ m - $\theta = 30°$, so $\sin\theta = 0.5$ - Upper edge at free surface **Step 1: Area** $$A = L \times W = 3 \times 2 = 6 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2: Depth of centroid** The slant distance of the centroid from the upper edge (which is on the free surface): $$\bar{l} = \frac{L}{2} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 \text{ m}$$ Vertical depth of centroid: $$\bar{h} = \bar{l} \sin\theta = 1.5 \times 0.5 = 0.75 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3: Moment of inertia about centroidal axis** $$I_G = \frac{W \cdot L^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times 27}{12} = 4.5 \text{ m}^4$$ **Step 4: Total Hydrostatic Thrust** $$F = \gamma \bar{h} A = 9.81 \times 0.75 \times 6 = 44.145 \text{ kN}$$ $$\boxed{F = 44.145 \text{ kN}}$$ **Step 5: Slant distance of CP from the free-surface line** Using the formula: $$l^* = \bar{l} + \frac{I_G}{A \bar{l}} = 1.5 + \frac{4.5}{6 \times 1.5} = 1.5 + \frac{4.5}{9} = 1.5 + 0.5$$ $$\boxed{l^* = 2.0 \text{ m along the slope from the free surface line}}$$ **Verification:** The vertical depth of CP would be $h^* = l^* \sin\theta = 2.0 \times 0.5 = 1.0$ m. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision — Print This Section!** - ✅ **Center of Pressure (CP)** = point of application of resultant hydrostatic thrust on a submerged surface. - ✅ **CP = Centroid** only for **horizontal surfaces** (parallel to free surface) or **surfaces at the free surface itself**. - ✅ For all other orientations, **CP is ALWAYS below the centroid** (in the direction of increasing depth). - ✅ **Total Hydrostatic Thrust:** $F = \gamma \bar{h} A$ — independent of surface orientation. - ✅ **General CP formula:** $h^* = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}$ — always measured vertically below the free surface. - ✅ **Slant distance form:** $l^* = \bar{l} + \dfrac{I_G}{A \bar{l}}$ — measured along the inclined surface. - ✅ **Vertical rectangle (top at free surface):** CP at $\dfrac{2d}{3}$ from top, i.e., $\dfrac{d}{3}$ from bottom. - ✅ **Vertical circle (top at free surface):** CP at $\dfrac{D}{2} + \dfrac{D}{12} = \dfrac{7D}{12}$ from top. - ✅ **Parallel Axis Theorem:** $I = I_G + A d^2$ — connect moment of inertia about any axis to the centroidal axis. - ✅ **Second moment of inertia (key shapes):** - Rectangle: $I_G = \dfrac{bd^3}{12}$ - Circle: $I_G = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$ - Triangle: $I_G = \dfrac{bh^3}{36}$ - ✅ **Sign convention:** Always use **gauge pressure** (atmospheric = 0) unless explicitly stated otherwise in the problem. - ✅ **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ (use 9.81, not 10, for KTU accuracy). - ✅ **Units consistency:** Force in N or kN, lengths in m, $\gamma$ in N/m³ or kN/m³, $I_G$ in m⁴. - ✅ **"Below free surface"** vs **"below centroid"** — always state your reference when giving CP location. - ✅ **Why CP shifts with depth:** As $\bar{h}$ increases (deeper immersion), $\dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}$ decreases → CP approaches centroid. > [!NOTE] > **End of Module 1 — Center of Pressure Notes (PCAUT302 | KTU 2024 Scheme)** > *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 specification. All formulas, derivations, and worked examples align with the KTU 2024 syllabus outcomes and Board examination patterns.*

topics.title: Energies in Flowing Fluid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Energies in Flowing Fluid ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition In fluid mechanics, a **flowing fluid** in motion possesses three distinct forms of mechanical energy per unit weight of the fluid. These energies arise from the fluid's position, motion, and pressure state. Collectively, they define the **Total Energy of a Flowing Fluid** and form the physical foundation of **Bernoulli's Equation**. The three energies per unit weight (commonly expressed in meters of fluid, i.e., *head*) are: 1. **Potential Energy / Datum Head (z)** — Energy due to elevation above a reference datum. 2. **Kinetic Energy / Velocity Head (v²/2g)** — Energy due to the fluid's motion. 3. **Pressure Energy / Pressure Head (p/ρg)** — Energy due to the internal fluid pressure (also called *flow energy*). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > The total energy per unit weight of a flowing fluid is termed the **Total Head (H)**. For an ideal (inviscid, incompressible) fluid along a streamline, the sum of these three heads remains **constant** — this is the celebrated **Bernoulli's Principle**. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy — Water Flowing Through a Pipe System:** > > Imagine water flowing out of an overhead tank (storage) through a pipe that discharges through a small nozzle at the bottom. > > - The water **high up in the tank** has a lot of **Potential Energy** (like a ball held up high — ready to fall). > - As water rushes through the pipe, it gains **Kinetic Energy** (like a rolling ball gaining speed). > - The water **inside the pipe under pressure** has **Pressure Energy** (like air trapped inside a pressurized balloon). > > As the water flows from the tank (high PE, low KE) toward the nozzle outlet (low PE, high KE), the total energy remains **conserved** — it just *transforms* from one form to another. This energy exchange is the heart of Bernoulli's equation. ### Energy Forms — Summary Definitions | Energy Type | Symbol | Per Unit Weight (Head) | Per Unit Mass | Per Unit Volume | |---|---|---|---|---| | Potential Energy | PE | $z$ | $gz$ | $\rho g z$ | | Kinetic Energy | KE | $\dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | $\dfrac{v^2}{2}$ | $\dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | | Pressure Energy | $E_p$ | $\dfrac{p}{\rho g}$ | $\dfrac{p}{\rho}$ | $p$ | | **Total Energy** | $E$ | $H = z + \dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | $\dfrac{p}{\rho} + gz + \dfrac{v^2}{2}$ | $p + \rho g z + \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | - **Standard Metric:** Acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81$ **m/s²** - **Fluid Density:** $\rho$ in **kg/m³** (for water, $\rho = 1000$ **kg/m³**) - **Specific Weight:** $\gamma = \rho g$ in **N/m³** (for water, $\gamma = 9810$ **N/m³**) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Energies — Conceptual Breakdown #### (A) Potential Energy (Datum Head, $z$) - **What:** The energy a fluid element possesses by virtue of its **vertical position** above an arbitrary horizontal reference datum. - **Why it exists:** Gravity pulls the fluid down; hence, fluid at higher elevation has the *potential* to do work. - **How to visualize:** Picture a hydroelectric dam — water stored at a high elevation contains enormous potential energy, which is later converted to kinetic energy in the turbines. - **Per unit weight (Head form):** $z$ [units: **m**] - **Per unit mass:** $gz$ [units: **J/kg**] - **Per unit volume:** $\rho g z$ [units: **Pa** or **N/m²**] #### (B) Kinetic Energy (Velocity Head, $v^2/2g$) - **What:** The energy a fluid element possesses due to its **motion** (velocity $v$). - **Why it exists:** A moving mass can do work upon impact (e.g., a moving fluid jet can turn a turbine blade). - **How to visualize:** A high-velocity water jet from a fire hose can knock down a person — the kinetic energy is real and measurable. - **Per unit weight (Head form):** $\dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ [units: **m**] - **Per unit mass:** $\dfrac{v^2}{2}$ [units: **J/kg**] - **Per unit volume:** $\dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ [units: **Pa** or **N/m²**] #### (C) Pressure Energy (Flow Energy, $p/\rho g$) - **What:** The energy a fluid element possesses due to the **internal pressure** acting on it. Also called *flow work* or *pressure head*. - **Why it exists:** In a flowing fluid, the upstream fluid does work on the downstream fluid across a control surface. This work per unit weight is the pressure energy. - **How to visualize:** Pressurized water in a municipal pipeline — even when stationary, it can gush out when a tap is opened, demonstrating stored pressure energy. - **Per unit weight (Head form):** $\dfrac{p}{\rho g}$ [units: **m**] - **Per unit mass:** $\dfrac{p}{\rho}$ [units: **J/kg**] - **Per unit volume:** $p$ [units: **Pa** or **N/m²**] > [!NOTE] > **Why "Per Unit Weight" is the Most Common Form in KTU Exams:** > Hydraulic engineers traditionally express energy as **head** (meters of fluid) because it directly relates to physical elevations. A pressure of 98.1 kPa corresponds to a head of 10 m of water — a very intuitive physical interpretation. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula Name | Equation (Per Unit Weight) | Units | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Datum Head | $z$ | m | Elevation energy | | 2 | Pressure Head | $\dfrac{p}{\rho g}$ | m | Pressure energy | | 3 | Velocity Head | $\dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | m | Kinetic energy | | 4 | **Total Head** (Bernoulli) | $H = z + \dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | m | Total energy per unit weight | | 5 | Total Energy (per unit mass) | $e = gz + \dfrac{p}{\rho} + \dfrac{v^2}{2}$ | J/kg | Specific energy | | 6 | Total Energy (per unit volume) | $E_v = p + \rho g z + \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pa | Energy density | | 7 | Continuity Equation | $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | m³/s | Mass conservation (incompressible) | | 8 | Bernoulli (two sections) | $\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2g}$ | m | Energy conservation | | 9 | Modified Bernoulli (real fluid) | $\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_L$ | m | With head loss $h_L$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Assumptions of Bernoulli's Equation (KTU Frequently Asked):** > 1. Fluid is **ideal** (inviscid — no viscosity, no friction). > 2. Flow is **steady**. > 3. Fluid is **incompressible** (constant $\rho$). > 4. Flow is **along a streamline** (or irrotational for the extended form). > 5. **No energy addition** by pumps, turbines, or other external devices (for the basic form). ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Venturi Meters & Orifice Meters:** Measure flow rate by relating pressure drop to velocity head. - **Pitot Tubes:** Measure fluid velocity via stagnation pressure (kinetic → pressure energy conversion). - **Aircraft Wing Lift:** Pressure difference (lower pressure on top) generates lift — direct application of Bernoulli. - **Hydraulic Machines (Pumps & Turbines):** Energy transformation in pumps (add energy) and turbines (extract energy) is governed by modified Bernoulli's equation. - **Carburettors & Atomizers:** High-velocity air → low pressure → fuel sucked in. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Total Energy Equation (Per Unit Weight Form) We derive the energy equation by considering a fluid element moving along a streamline from section **1** to section **2**. **Step 1: Consider an elemental fluid parcel of weight $dW = \rho g \, dV$.** The forces acting on this fluid element as it moves from section 1 to section 2 are: - Pressure force at section 1: $p_1 \, dA_1$ (pushing forward) - Pressure force at section 2: $p_2 \, dA_2$ (pushing backward) - Gravity force: $dW = \rho g \, dV$ (acting downward) **Step 2: Apply the Work-Energy Theorem.** The net work done by all forces = Change in kinetic energy of the fluid element. **Work done by pressure force at section 1:** $$dW_1 = p_1 \cdot dA_1 \cdot ds_1 = p_1 \cdot dV$$ **Work done by pressure force at section 2 (negative, as it opposes motion):** $$dW_2 = -p_2 \cdot dA_2 \cdot ds_2 = -p_2 \cdot dV$$ **Work done by gravity (negative, as element moves from $z_1$ down to $z_2$):** $$dW_g = -dW \cdot (z_1 - z_2) = -\rho g \, dV \cdot (z_1 - z_2)$$ **Step 3: Total Work Done on the Fluid Element** $$dW_{total} = p_1 \, dV - p_2 \, dV - \rho g \, dV \cdot (z_1 - z_2)$$ **Step 4: Change in Kinetic Energy of the Fluid Element** $$dKE = \frac{1}{2} \cdot dM \cdot (v_2^2 - v_1^2) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \, dV \cdot (v_2^2 - v_1^2)$$ **Step 5: Equate Work to Change in Kinetic Energy (Work-Energy Theorem)** $$p_1 \, dV - p_2 \, dV - \rho g \, dV \cdot (z_1 - z_2) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \, dV \cdot (v_2^2 - v_1^2)$$ **Step 6: Divide Both Sides by $\rho g \, dV$ (to convert to per unit weight form)** $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} - \frac{p_2}{\rho g} - (z_1 - z_2) = \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2g}$$ **Step 7: Rearrange to group all section-1 terms on one side and section-2 terms on the other side.** $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2g}$$ **Step 8: Conclude that the sum is constant along a streamline.** $$\boxed{\frac{p}{\rho g} + z + \frac{v^2}{2g} = \text{Constant} = H}$$ This constant $H$ is the **Total Head** — the **Total Energy per unit weight** of the flowing fluid. ### 3.2 Conversion Between the Three Forms of Energy Expression The same Bernoulli equation can be written in three equivalent forms: #### Form 1: Per Unit Weight (Head form) — *most common in KTU problems* $$\frac{p}{\rho g} + z + \frac{v^2}{2g} = H \quad [\text{m of fluid}]$$ #### Form 2: Per Unit Mass (Specific Energy form) Multiplying Form 1 by $g$: $$\frac{p}{\rho} + gz + \frac{v^2}{2} = gH \quad [\text{J/kg}]$$ #### Form 3: Per Unit Volume (Energy Density form) Multiplying Form 2 by $\rho$: $$p + \rho g z + \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = \rho g H \quad [\text{Pa or N/m}^2]$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** Water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) flows through a pipe. At section 1: - Diameter $D_1 = 0.2$ m - Velocity $v_1 = 2$ m/s - Pressure $p_1 = 200$ kPa - Elevation $z_1 = 10$ m At section 2: - Diameter $D_2 = 0.1$ m - Elevation $z_2 = 6$ m **Find:** (a) Velocity at section 2. (b) Pressure at section 2 (assuming ideal flow). (c) Total head at both sections. #### (a) Velocity at Section 2 (Continuity Equation) $$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 \implies \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 \cdot v_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 \cdot v_2$$ $$v_2 = v_1 \cdot \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2 = 2 \cdot \left(\frac{0.2}{0.1}\right)^2 = 2 \cdot 4 = 8 \text{ m/s}$$ #### (b) Pressure at Section 2 (Bernoulli's Equation) $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2g}$$ Substituting values (using $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho g = 9810$ N/m³): $$\frac{200{,}000}{9810} + 10 + \frac{2^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{p_2}{9810} + 6 + \frac{8^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$ $$20.387 + 10 + 0.204 = \frac{p_2}{9810} + 6 + 3.262$$ $$30.591 = \frac{p_2}{9810} + 9.262$$ $$\frac{p_2}{9810} = 30.591 - 9.262 = 21.329 \text{ m}$$ $$p_2 = 21.329 \times 9810 = 209{,}237 \text{ Pa} \approx 209.24 \text{ kPa}$$ #### (c) Total Head at Both Sections **At Section 1:** $$H_1 = \frac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = 20.387 + 10 + 0.204 = 30.591 \text{ m}$$ **At Section 2:** $$H_2 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} = 21.329 + 6 + 3.262 = 30.591 \text{ m}$$ **Verification:** $H_1 = H_2 = 30.591$ m ✓ (Confirms energy conservation in ideal flow.) ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Energy Transformation in a Flowing Fluid The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the three energy forms interconvert as fluid flows through different sections of a pipe system. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["SECTION 1
Inlet
High Pressure, Low Velocity"] --> B["VALVE / CONSTRICTION
Diameter reduces"] B --> C["SECTION 2
Outlet
Low Pressure, High Velocity"] A --> A1["Pressure Energy = p1 over rho g"] A --> A2["Potential Energy = z1"] A --> A3["Kinetic Energy = v1 squared over 2g"] C --> C1["Pressure Energy = p2 over rho g"] C --> C2["Potential Energy = z2"] C --> C3["Kinetic Energy = v2 squared over 2g"] A1 -- Energy Conversion --> C1 A2 -- Energy Conversion --> C2 A3 -- Energy Conversion --> C3 D["BERNOULLI CONSTANT H"] -.Binds.- A D -.Binds.- C E["ASSUMPTIONS
Steady, Incompressible, Inviscid Flow"] -.Governs.- D ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Energy Flow Along a Streamline This diagram maps the energy flow along a streamline from an upstream reservoir (high PE) to a nozzle outlet (high KE), a classic KTU problem archetype. ```mermaid flowchart LR Res["RESERVOIR
Section 1
z1 = High Elevation
p1 = Atmospheric
v1 approx 0"] -->|Potential Energy Dominant| Pipe["PIPE FLOW
Section 2
z2 = Lower
p2 = High
v2 = Moderate"] Pipe -->|Pressure Energy Dominant| Noz["NOZZLE EXIT
Section 3
z3 = Lowest
p3 = Atmospheric
v3 = Maximum"] Res -- gz1 --> PE["Potential Head z"] Pipe -- p2 over rho g --> PR["Pressure Head p over rho g"] Noz -- v3 squared over 2g --> KE["Velocity Head v squared over 2g"] PE --- Sum["TOTAL HEAD H
Sum of Three Heads
Constant along streamline"] PR --- Sum KE --- Sum ``` ### 4.3 Schematic of Pressure, Velocity, and Elevation Heads A conceptual block diagram showing the three heads of energy in a typical pipe flow scenario. ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["PRESSURE HEAD
Symbol p over rho g
Unit meters of fluid
Origin: Internal pressure"] P2["DATUM HEAD
Symbol z
Unit meters of fluid
Origin: Elevation above datum"] P3["VELOCITY HEAD
Symbol v squared over 2g
Unit meters of fluid
Origin: Fluid motion"] P1 --> ADD["ADD ALL THREE HEADS"] P2 --> ADD P3 --> ADD ADD --> RESULT["TOTAL HEAD H
H equals z plus p over rho g plus v squared over 2g
Unit meters of fluid
Conserved for ideal flow"] MOD["MODIFIED FOR REAL FLUID
Add Head Loss hL
Subtract Pump Head hP
Add Turbine Head hT"] --> RESULT ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the three energies possessed by a flowing fluid. Express the total energy per unit weight of the fluid. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A flowing fluid possesses three forms of mechanical energy: 1. **Potential Energy (PE):** Energy due to the elevation of the fluid above a reference datum. Per unit weight, it is called the **datum head** and is denoted by $z$. 2. **Kinetic Energy (KE):** Energy due to the motion of the fluid. Per unit weight, it is the **velocity head** given by $\dfrac{v^2}{2g}$. 3. **Pressure Energy ($E_p$):** Energy due to the internal pressure of the fluid. Per unit weight, it is the **pressure head** given by $\dfrac{p}{\rho g}$. The total energy per unit weight of a flowing fluid is the **Total Head**: $$H = z + \frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{v^2}{2g} \quad \text{(m of fluid)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip:** Award 1 mark each for the three energy definitions and 1 mark for the correct total head expression. Students often forget the units — write "(m of fluid)" for full credit. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** State Bernoulli's equation and list any four assumptions made in its derivation. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Bernoulli's Equation:** It states that in a steady, ideal, incompressible flow along a streamline, the sum of pressure head, velocity head, and datum (elevation) head remains constant. $$\frac{p}{\rho g} + z + \frac{v^2}{2g} = \text{Constant}$$ **Four Assumptions:** 1. The fluid is **ideal** (inviscid, no viscosity). 2. The flow is **steady** (velocities do not change with time). 3. The fluid is **incompressible** (density $\rho$ is constant). 4. The flow is **along a streamline** (or irrotational for the extended form). 5. There is **no external energy addition or extraction** (no pump or turbine). > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip:** State Bernoulli's equation first (1 mark). List any four assumptions clearly (0.5 marks each = 2 marks total). Writing the equation in head form is preferred over the J/kg form. ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** Derive Bernoulli's equation from the principle of conservation of energy for a steady, ideal, incompressible fluid flow along a streamline. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Water flows through a pipe of diameter 300 mm at a velocity of 4 m/s at section 1, where the pressure is 150 kPa and the elevation is 8 m. At section 2, the pipe diameter is 150 mm and the elevation is 5 m. Calculate: (i) the velocity at section 2, (ii) the pressure at section 2, and (iii) the total head at both sections. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (1 Mark): State the assumptions of Bernoulli's equation.** - Steady flow - Incompressible fluid - Inviscid (frictionless) fluid - Flow along a streamline - No energy addition or extraction **Step 2 (1 Mark):** Consider a fluid element of weight $dW = \rho g \, dV$ moving along a streamline from section 1 to section 2. **Step 3 (2 Marks):** Apply the work-energy theorem. The net work done on the element equals the change in kinetic energy: $$p_1 dV - p_2 dV - dW(z_1 - z_2) = \frac{1}{2} dm (v_2^2 - v_1^2)$$ Substituting $dW = \rho g \, dV$ and $dm = \rho \, dV$: $$p_1 dV - p_2 dV - \rho g \, dV (z_1 - z_2) = \frac{1}{2} \rho \, dV (v_2^2 - v_1^2)$$ **Step 4 (2 Marks):** Divide through by $\rho g \, dV$: $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} - \frac{p_2}{\rho g} - (z_1 - z_2) = \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2g}$$ **Step 5 (1 Mark):** Rearrange to obtain the final form: $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} = \text{Constant}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake — Marks Lost:** > Students often forget to **state the assumptions** before the derivation. The examiner allocates 1 full mark for this. Also, students sometimes write the energy equation in J/kg form instead of the head (m) form — KTU answers in the head form score higher in marks. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Given Data:** - $D_1 = 300$ mm $= 0.3$ m, $v_1 = 4$ m/s, $p_1 = 150$ kPa $= 150{,}000$ Pa, $z_1 = 8$ m - $D_2 = 150$ mm $= 0.15$ m, $z_2 = 5$ m - $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s² **(i) Velocity at Section 2 (2 Marks) — Continuity Equation:** $$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$$ $$v_2 = v_1 \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2 = 4 \times \left(\frac{0.3}{0.15}\right)^2 = 4 \times 4 = 16 \text{ m/s}$$ **(ii) Pressure at Section 2 (3 Marks) — Bernoulli's Equation:** $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2g}$$ $$\frac{150{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81} + 8 + \frac{4^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{p_2}{9810} + 5 + \frac{16^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$ $$15.290 + 8 + 0.815 = \frac{p_2}{9810} + 5 + 13.048$$ $$24.105 = \frac{p_2}{9810} + 18.048$$ $$\frac{p_2}{9810} = 6.057 \text{ m}$$ $$p_2 = 6.057 \times 9810 = 59{,}419 \text{ Pa} \approx 59.42 \text{ kPa}$$ **(iii) Total Head (2 Marks):** $$H_1 = 15.290 + 8 + 0.815 = 24.105 \text{ m}$$ $$H_2 = 6.057 + 5 + 13.048 = 24.105 \text{ m}$$ **Verification:** $H_1 = H_2 = 24.105$ m ✓ --- **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2023 — Internal Choice Alternative]** **(a)** Explain the physical significance of each term in Bernoulli's equation. How is the equation modified to account for (i) real fluid flow, (ii) presence of a pump, and (iii) presence of a turbine? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A pipe carrying oil ($\rho = 850$ kg/m³) has a section of 200 mm diameter where the pressure is 100 kPa and the velocity is 3 m/s. The pipe rises to an elevation 4 m higher where the diameter reduces to 100 mm. If the head loss between the two sections is 1.5 m of oil, find the pressure at the upper section. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Physical Significance of Each Term (3 Marks):** 1. **$p/\rho g$ — Pressure Head:** Represents the energy per unit weight due to the fluid's pressure. Indicates the height of a fluid column that would produce the given pressure. 2. **$v^2/2g$ — Velocity Head:** Represents the kinetic energy per unit weight of the fluid. Indicates the height through which the fluid must fall (in vacuum) to attain the given velocity. 3. **$z$ — Datum Head (Elevation Head):** Represents the potential energy per unit weight due to the fluid's elevation above a reference datum. **Modifications of Bernoulli's Equation (4 Marks):** **(i) For Real Fluid Flow:** A head loss term $h_L$ (representing energy dissipated due to friction) is added to the downstream side: $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$ **(ii) With a Pump:** The pump adds energy $h_p$ to the fluid. This is added to the upstream side: $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + h_p = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$ **(iii) With a Turbine:** The turbine extracts energy $h_t$ from the fluid. This is added to the downstream side: $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_t + h_L$$ > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall — Marks Lost:** > Many students add $h_L$ to the wrong side. Remember: energy is **lost** from the fluid, so $h_L$ always appears on the **downstream (right-hand) side** of the equation. Similarly, $h_p$ is added (energy input) on the **upstream side**, and $h_t$ on the **downstream side** (energy extracted). #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Given Data:** - $\rho = 850$ kg/m³, $\rho g = 850 \times 9.81 = 8338.5$ N/m³ - Section 1: $D_1 = 0.2$ m, $p_1 = 100$ kPa $= 100{,}000$ Pa, $v_1 = 3$ m/s, $z_1 = 0$ m (reference) - Section 2: $D_2 = 0.1$ m, $z_2 = 4$ m, $h_L = 1.5$ m - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 1 (1 Mark):** Find $v_2$ using continuity: $$v_2 = v_1 \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2 = 3 \times \left(\frac{0.2}{0.1}\right)^2 = 12 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute the velocity head at both sections: $$\frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{3^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{9}{19.62} = 0.459 \text{ m of oil}$$ $$\frac{v_2^2}{2g} = \frac{12^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{144}{19.62} = 7.339 \text{ m of oil}$$ **Step 3 (1 Mark):** Compute the pressure head at section 1: $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} = \frac{100{,}000}{8338.5} = 11.992 \text{ m of oil}$$ **Step 4 (3 Marks):** Apply the modified Bernoulli's equation (real fluid with head loss): $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$ $$11.992 + 0 + 0.459 = \frac{p_2}{8338.5} + 4 + 7.339 + 1.5$$ $$12.451 = \frac{p_2}{8338.5} + 12.839$$ $$\frac{p_2}{8338.5} = 12.451 - 12.839 = -0.388 \text{ m of oil}$$ **Step 5 (1 Mark):** Compute $p_2$: $$p_2 = -0.388 \times 8338.5 = -3235.3 \text{ Pa} \approx -3.24 \text{ kPa}$$ **Physical Interpretation:** A negative pressure (vacuum) at section 2 indicates that the pressure has dropped below atmospheric — this is physically possible but suggests cavitation risk in real systems. The negative sign indicates the pressure is below the reference atmospheric pressure. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Warning:** > A **negative pressure result** is physically meaningful (gauge vacuum) and should NOT be dismissed as an error. Many students mark themselves down for "wrong" answers when the calculation is correct. Always state the physical interpretation: "A negative gauge pressure indicates a partial vacuum." ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Three Energies of a Flowing Fluid:** Potential Energy ($z$), Pressure Energy ($p/\rho g$), and Kinetic Energy ($v^2/2g$). - **Total Head Formula:** $H = z + \dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ — always express in **meters of fluid**. - **Three Forms of Bernoulli's Equation:** - Per unit weight: $\dfrac{p}{\rho g} + z + \dfrac{v^2}{2g} = $ constant (m) — **preferred in KTU exams**. - Per unit mass: $\dfrac{p}{\rho} + gz + \dfrac{v^2}{2} = $ constant (J/kg). - Per unit volume: $p + \rho g z + \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2} = $ constant (Pa). - **Key Assumptions:** Steady, incompressible, inviscid, along a streamline, no external energy input/output. - **Continuity Equation (always paired with Bernoulli):** $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ (for incompressible flow). - **Modified Bernoulli (real fluid):** Add $h_L$ on the downstream side; add $h_p$ on the upstream side (pump); add $h_t$ on the downstream side (turbine). - **Energy Conservation Law:** For ideal flow, $H_1 = H_2$ (total head is constant); for real flow, $H_1 > H_2$ (energy is dissipated as $h_L$). - **Units to remember:** $\rho$ in kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho g = \gamma$ in N/m³ (specific weight), pressure in Pa or kPa (1 kPa = 1000 Pa). - **Continuity + Bernoulli together:** Most KTU problems require continuity first to find $v_2$, then Bernoulli to find $p_2$ — this is a **two-step problem pattern**. - **Quick Reference Conversions:** - Pressure (Pa) to Head (m): divide by $\rho g$ - Head (m) to Pressure (Pa): multiply by $\rho g$ - Velocity (m/s) to Head (m): $v^2 / (2g)$ - **Examiners' Pet Topic:** Be ready to (i) state the assumptions, (ii) write the equation in all three forms, and (iii) solve numerical problems combining continuity and Bernoulli.

topics.title: Head - Pressure
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Head - Pressure

# 💧 Head – Pressure (Pressure Head)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> *Pressure Head* is defined as the height of a column of fluid (of specific weight $\gamma = \rho g$) required to produce a given pressure at its base. It is a convenient way to **express fluid pressure in terms of an equivalent height (length) of fluid**, allowing pressure to be treated dimensionally as energy per unit weight.

Mathematically, the pressure head $h_p$ corresponding to a pressure $P$ is:

$$h_p = \frac{P}{\gamma} = \frac{P}{\rho g}$$

where:
- $P$ = Pressure intensity (in N/m² or Pa)
- $\rho$ = Mass density of fluid (in **kg/m³**)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²** at standard Earth gravity)
- $\gamma$ = Specific weight of fluid (in **N/m³**)

## 🌊 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a tall water tank. At the bottom, the water pushes against the walls with tremendous force. But here's the key insight — **we don't need to think in terms of Pascals (N/m²)**. Instead, we can ask: *"How tall a column of water would I need to stand on this point to produce the same force?"*

That imaginary height is the **pressure head**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of three diving pools — one filled with water, one with honey, and one with mercury. At the **same depth (1 m)**, each exerts a *different pressure*. Why? Because pressure head is not just about depth — it's about the **weight of the fluid column above**. Mercury is heavy (13.6× denser), so even a small height creates a huge pressure. This is why manometers use mercury — a tiny column "tells" a big pressure story.

## 🎯 Why Do Engineers Use "Head" Instead of "Pressure"?

In fluid mechanics, especially when analyzing **Bernoulli's equation** and **flow through pipes/pumps/turbines**, every term is conveniently expressed in **meters of fluid**. This allows:

1. **Direct addition/subtraction** of energy terms (pressure head + velocity head + elevation head).
2. **Unit uniformity** — all terms in meters (m).
3. **Pump/turbine head** is naturally a length, not a pressure.
4. **Simpler visualization** of fluid energy distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Pressure head is foundational to the study of **fluid statics, manometry, Bernoulli's equation, and hydraulic machines**. Mastery of $h_p = P/\gamma$ is mandatory before attempting orifice meter, venturi meter, or pump characteristic problems.

## 📐 Dimensions of Pressure Head

$$[h_p] = \frac{[P]}{[\gamma]} = \frac{ML^{-1}T^{-2}}{ML^{-2}T^{-2}} = L$$

So pressure head has the **dimension of length** — typically expressed in **metres (m) of fluid**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧠 Conceptual Logic Breakdown

The concept of pressure head is built on three foundational pillars of fluid statics:

### Pillar 1: Hydrostatic Pressure Principle
In a static fluid, the pressure at any depth $h$ below the free surface is governed by the **hydrostatic pressure equation**:

$$P = P_{atm} + \rho g h$$

When $P_{atm}$ is taken as datum (i.e., using **gauge pressure**), the term $\rho g h$ is precisely the **pressure head contribution** due to the fluid column.

### Pillar 2: Specific Weight Concept
Specific weight $\gamma = \rho g$ is the weight per unit volume of the fluid. It is this property that *links* pressure (force per area) to head (length):

$$P = \gamma h \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad h = \frac{P}{\gamma}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** Specific weight tells us **how heavy a column of fluid is per cubic metre**. Multiply it by height, and you get the pressure exerted at the base of that column.

### Pillar 3: Total Head Concept (Preview of Bernoulli)
In flowing fluids, the **total head** at a point is the sum of three components:

$$H_{total} = \underbrace{\frac{P}{\gamma}}_{\text{Pressure Head}} + \underbrace{\frac{V^2}{2g}}_{\text{Velocity Head}} + \underbrace{z}_{\text{Elevation Head}}$$

This unification is why pressure head is the *gateway concept* to hydraulic machinery analysis.

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **SI Unit** | **Typical KTU Use** |
|:-------------|:----------:|:------------|:-----------:|:--------------------|
| Pressure Head | $h_p$ | $\dfrac{P}{\gamma} = \dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | **m** | Expressing pressure as fluid column height |
| Hydrostatic Pressure | $P$ | $\rho g h$ | **Pa (N/m²)** | Pressure at depth $h$ in static fluid |
| Specific Weight | $\gamma$ | $\rho g$ | **N/m³** | Weight density of fluid |
| Gauge Pressure Head | $h_{gauge}$ | $\dfrac{P_{gauge}}{\gamma}$ | **m** | Pressure above atmospheric |
| Vacuum Pressure Head | $h_{vac}$ | $\dfrac{P_{vac}}{\gamma}$ | **m** | Pressure below atmospheric |
| Absolute Pressure Head | $h_{abs}$ | $\dfrac{P_{abs}}{\gamma}$ | **m** | Total pressure including atmospheric |
| Conversion (kPa to m of water) | $h$ | $\dfrac{P}{9.81}$ | **m** | Quick conversion: $1 \text{ kPa} \approx 0.102 \text{ m of water}$ |
| Conversion (bar to m of water) | $h$ | $\dfrac{10.2 \cdot P_{bar}}{1}$ | **m** | $1 \text{ bar} \approx 10.2 \text{ m of water}$ |
| Conversion (m of water to m of Hg) | $h_{Hg}$ | $\dfrac{h_{water}}{13.6}$ | **m of Hg** | Mercury manometer readings |
| Total Static Head | $H$ | $h_p + z$ | **m** | Combined head at a point |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick — The 13.6 Magic Number:** Since mercury is **13.6 times denser** than water, a 1 m column of mercury exerts the same pressure as a 13.6 m column of water. This is why U-tube manometers using mercury are compact.

## 🏗️ Real-World Engineering Applications

| **Application** | **Use of Pressure Head** |
|:----------------|:-------------------------|
| **Water Supply Networks** | Pump must develop head to overcome elevation + friction + pressure demand |
| **Hydraulic Turbines** | Net head across turbine = primary design parameter (Pelton, Francis, Kaplan) |
| **Manometers & Barometers** | Direct measurement of pressure as fluid column height |
| **Dam Design** | Hydrostatic pressure head determines wall thickness and base shear |
| **Submarine Engineering** | Hull must withstand crushing pressure head at ocean depths |
| **Aircraft Altimeters** | Use pressure head to infer altitude (barometric formula) |
| **BPV / PRV Settings** | Safety relief valves are calibrated in terms of pressure head |

## 🔁 Pressure Head in Different Fluids

The same numerical pressure corresponds to **different heads** in different fluids:

$$h_{water} = 13.6 \cdot h_{mercury}$$

$$h_{water} = 13.6 \cdot h_{mercury} = \frac{1}{0.000818} \cdot h_{air} \approx 1221 \cdot h_{air}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always quote the fluid when expressing head in metres!**
> Saying *"the pressure head is 5 m"* is ambiguous. You must say *"5 m of water"* or *"0.368 m of mercury"*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📌 Derivation 1: Pressure Head from Hydrostatic Equilibrium

**Starting Principle:** Consider a static fluid column of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$ under gravity.

**Step 1 — Force balance on the column:**
The weight of the fluid column acts downward:
$$W = \text{mass} \times g = (\rho \cdot A \cdot h) \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Pressure at the base:**
Pressure is force per unit area. The pressure at the base due to this column is:
$$P = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{\rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Simplification:**
$$P = \rho g h$$

**Step 4 — Introduce specific weight:**
Defining $\gamma = \rho g$:
$$P = \gamma h$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the head:**
Rearranging to express height (head) in terms of pressure:
$$h = \frac{P}{\gamma} = \frac{P}{\rho g}$$

> **Interpretation:** The height $h$ of a fluid column that would produce pressure $P$ at its base is the **pressure head** corresponding to pressure $P$.

---

## 📌 Derivation 2: Pressure Head Difference Between Two Points (Manometer Equation)

**Problem Setup:** A U-tube manometer connects points A and B in a pipe carrying fluid of specific weight $\gamma_1$. The manometer fluid (mercury) has specific weight $\gamma_m$. The difference in mercury levels is $h_m$.

**Starting Manometric Equation:**
$$P_A + \gamma_1 \cdot h_1 - \gamma_m \cdot h_m - \gamma_1 \cdot h_2 = P_B$$

**Rearranging:**
$$P_A - P_B = \gamma_m \cdot h_m - \gamma_1 (h_1 - h_2)$$

For the common case where the pipe is horizontal and the manometer taps are at the same elevation:
$$h_1 = h_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_A - P_B = (\gamma_m - \gamma_1) \cdot h_m$$

**Expressing as Head Difference:**
$$\frac{P_A - P_B}{\gamma_1} = \left(\frac{\gamma_m}{\gamma_1} - 1\right) \cdot h_m = (S_m - 1) \cdot h_m$$

where $S_m = \dfrac{\gamma_m}{\gamma_1}$ is the **specific gravity of manometer fluid** relative to pipe fluid.

---

## 📌 Derivation 3: Conversion Between Pressure Units and Head of Water

**Given:** A pressure of $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 101325 \text{ N/m}^2$

**Step 1 — Apply the pressure-head formula:**
$$h = \frac{P}{\rho g} = \frac{101325}{1000 \times 9.81}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator and denominator separately:**
- Numerator: $101325$
- Denominator: $9810$

**Step 3 — Division:**
$$h = \frac{101325}{9810} = 10.33 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 4 — Verify with mercury column:**
$$h_{Hg} = \frac{10.33}{13.6} = 0.76 \text{ m of Hg} = 760 \text{ mm of Hg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Standard atmospheric pressure equals **760 mm Hg** = **10.33 m of water** — a foundational result every KTU student must memorize.

---

## 📌 Derivation 4: Gauge, Vacuum, and Absolute Pressure Heads

The relationships are as follows:

**Step 1 — Absolute pressure:**
$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$

**Step 2 — Vacuum pressure (when gauge is negative):**
$$P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs} = -P_{gauge}$$

**Step 3 — Converting to heads by dividing by $\gamma$:**
$$h_{abs} = h_{atm} + h_{gauge}$$

$$h_{vac} = h_{atm} - h_{abs} = -h_{gauge}$$

> **Worked Example:** A pipe has gauge pressure $P_{gauge} = 200 \text{ kPa}$. Find the head in metres of water.

**Solution:**
$$h_{gauge} = \frac{P_{gauge}}{\rho g} = \frac{200 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 9.81} = \frac{200000}{9810}$$

$$h_{gauge} = 20.387 \text{ m of water}$$

**Absolute head** (assuming $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$):
$$h_{abs} = \frac{101.325 + 200}{9.81} = \frac{301.325}{9.81} = 30.72 \text{ m of water (abs)}$$

---

## 📊 Analytical Matrix — Pressure Head in Common Engineering Scenarios

| **Scenario** | **Pressure Type** | **Head Form** | **Example Value** |
|:-------------|:------------------|:--------------|:------------------|
| Domestic tap water | Gauge | $h = P_{tap} / \gamma$ | 20 m (≈ 2 bar) |
| Car tyre | Gauge | $h = P_{tyre} / \gamma$ | 30 m of water |
| Vacuum in condenser | Vacuum | $h_{vac} = (P_{atm} - P_{abs}) / \gamma$ | 0.7 m of Hg |
| Pump suction lift | Below atmospheric | Negative gauge head | -3 m |
| Deep-sea probe (1000 m ocean) | Absolute (huge) | $h = P_{abs} / \gamma_{sw}$ | ≈ 1000 m of seawater |
| Aircraft cabin pressurization | Differential | $\Delta h = \Delta P / \gamma$ | 2400 m equivalent |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
