# 🎓 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define pressure head. Derive the relationship between pressure and pressure head for a static fluid column.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Pressure head is the height of a fluid column required to produce a given pressure at its base. It is expressed as:

$$h_p = \frac{P}{\gamma} = \frac{P}{\rho g}$$

**Derivation (2 marks):** Consider a vertical fluid column of cross-section $A$ and height $h$.
- Volume of column: $V = A \cdot h$
- Mass of column: $m = \rho \cdot A \cdot h$
- Weight of column: $W = m \cdot g = \rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g$
- Pressure at base: $P = \dfrac{W}{A} = \rho g h = \gamma h$
- Solving for head: $h_p = h = \dfrac{P}{\rho g} = \dfrac{P}{\gamma}$

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**A pressure of 250 kPa is measured in a water pipeline. Express this pressure in metres of water and metres of mercury. Take $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $S_{Hg} = 13.6$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Pressure head in metres of water (2 marks):**
$$h_{water} = \frac{P}{\rho g} = \frac{250 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 9.81} = \frac{250000}{9810} = 25.48 \text{ m of water}$$

**Pressure head in metres of mercury (1 mark):**
$$h_{Hg} = \frac{h_{water}}{S_{Hg}} = \frac{25.48}{13.6} = 1.873 \text{ m of Hg}$$

---

## 📝 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A closed tank contains oil (specific gravity 0.85) up to a depth of 4 m, above a layer of water of depth 2 m. A pressure gauge at the bottom of the tank reads 58.86 kPa. Find: (i) the pressure at the oil surface, and (ii) the pressure head at the bottom in metres of water.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a U-tube manometer and derive the expression for pressure difference between two points in terms of manometric fluid head.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Given Data:**
- Specific gravity of oil: $S_{oil} = 0.85$, so $\gamma_{oil} = 0.85 \times 9810 = 8338.5 \text{ N/m}^3$
- Depth of oil: $h_{oil} = 4 \text{ m}$
- Depth of water: $h_w = 2 \text{ m}$
- $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$
- Gauge pressure at bottom: $P_{bottom} = 58.86 \text{ kPa} = 58860 \text{ N/m}^2$

**Step 1 — Pressure contribution from oil layer (2 marks):**
$$P_{oil} = \gamma_{oil} \cdot h_{oil} = 8338.5 \times 4 = 33354 \text{ N/m}^2 = 33.354 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Pressure contribution from water layer (1 mark):**
$$P_w = \gamma_w \cdot h_w = 9810 \times 2 = 19620 \text{ N/m}^2 = 19.620 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Pressure at the oil surface (2 marks):**
$$P_{surface} = P_{bottom} - P_{oil} - P_w$$
$$P_{surface} = 58.86 - 33.354 - 19.620 = 5.886 \text{ kPa (gauge)}$$

> The oil surface is under a positive gauge pressure of **5.886 kPa** (tank is pressurized).

**Step 4 — Pressure head at the bottom in metres of water (2 marks):**
$$h_{p,bottom} = \frac{P_{bottom}}{\gamma_w} = \frac{58860}{9810} = 6.0 \text{ m of water}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Sketch (3 marks):**

A U-tube manometer consists of a U-shaped glass tube containing a heavy manometric fluid (e.g., mercury). One end is connected to point A (pressure $P_A$), the other to point B (pressure $P_B$). The deflection (difference in mercury levels) is $h_m$.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Starting from point A, traverse the manometer to point B:

$$P_A + \gamma_1 \cdot h_1 - \gamma_m \cdot h_m - \gamma_1 \cdot h_2 = P_B$$

For a horizontal pipe with taps at the same level ($h_1 = h_2$):

$$P_A - P_B = (\gamma_m - \gamma_1) \cdot h_m = \gamma_1 (S_m - 1) \cdot h_m$$

**Pressure head difference:**

$$\frac{P_A - P_B}{\gamma_1} = (S_m - 1) \cdot h_m$$

> **Engineering Significance:** This is the foundation of differential manometry, used in orifice meters, venturi meters, and pitot tubes.

---

### ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the terms: (i) Gauge pressure head, (ii) Vacuum pressure head, and (iii) Absolute pressure head. A suction pipe of a pump is 5 m above the water level in the sump. Calculate the pressure head at the pump inlet if the absolute pressure at that point is 70 kPa. Take atmospheric pressure as 101.325 kPa.

**(b) [7 Marks]** State and prove the hydrostatic pressure equation. How is it related to the concept of pressure head?

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Definitions (3 marks):**

- **(i) Gauge pressure head** ($h_{gauge}$): The pressure head above atmospheric. $h_{gauge} = P_{gauge} / \gamma$. If $P_{abs} > P_{atm}$, $P_{gauge} > 0$.
- **(ii) Vacuum pressure head** ($h_{vac}$): The pressure head below atmospheric. $h_{vac} = P_{vac} / \gamma = (P_{atm} - P_{abs}) / \gamma$. Indicates sub-atmospheric conditions.
- **(iii) Absolute pressure head** ($h_{abs}$): The total pressure head including atmospheric. $h_{abs} = P_{abs} / \gamma$.

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**

Given:
- $P_{abs} = 70 \text{ kPa} = 70000 \text{ N/m}^2$
- $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 101325 \text{ N/m}^2$
- $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Gauge pressure (vacuum, since below atmospheric):**
$$P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm} = 70000 - 101325 = -31325 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Vacuum pressure:**
$$P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs} = 31325 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Pressure head at pump inlet (gauge, negative):**
$$h_{gauge} = \frac{P_{gauge}}{\gamma_w} = \frac{-31325}{9810} = -3.193 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 4 — Absolute pressure head:**
$$h_{abs} = \frac{P_{abs}}{\gamma_w} = \frac{70000}{9810} = 7.135 \text{ m of water (abs)}$$

> The pump inlet is under **vacuum**, with gauge head of **-3.19 m** — a critical parameter in NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head) calculations.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Statement (1 mark):** The pressure at any point in a static fluid is equal to the sum of the pressure at the free surface and the pressure due to the weight of the fluid column above that point.

**Proof (4 marks):**

Consider a point at depth $h$ below the free surface of a static fluid. Let the pressure at the free surface be $P_0$. Construct an imaginary vertical column of unit cross-section and height $h$.

**Forces acting on the column:**
- Weight (downward) = $\rho \cdot (1) \cdot h \cdot g = \rho g h$
- Pressure from below (upward) = $P$
- Pressure from above (downward) = $P_0$

**For equilibrium (ΣF = 0):**
$$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$

$$P = P_0 + \gamma h$$

**Relation to pressure head (2 marks):**

The term $\gamma h$ is the *gauge* pressure at depth $h$. The corresponding pressure head is:

$$h_p = \frac{P - P_0}{\gamma} = h$$

Thus, **the pressure head at any point in a static fluid is exactly equal to the depth below the free surface** (for gauge pressure). This is the fundamental link between hydrostatics and the head concept.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the fluid specification (–1 to –2 marks):** Saying *"head = 5 m"* without specifying *"of water"* or *"of mercury"* is incomplete. Always state the fluid.
>
> 2. **Using $\rho g = 10000$ instead of 9810 (–1 mark):** KTU problems typically expect $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ and $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, giving $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$. Using 10000 introduces ~2% error and is often flagged by examiners.
>
> 3. **Confusing gauge and absolute pressure (–2 marks):** When a problem says *"the pressure gauge reads 200 kPa"*, that is **gauge pressure**, not absolute. Many students mistakenly use 200 kPa in the absolute head equation.
>
> 4. **Manometer sign errors (–2 marks):** When traversing a U-tube manometer, **add pressure when going down** ($\gamma h$ downward) and **subtract when going up**. Mixing up signs is the single most common error in fluid statics.
>
> 5. **Unit inconsistencies in Bernoulli (–3 marks):** When pressure head is added to velocity head and elevation head, **all must be in metres**. A common error is mixing kPa with m.
>
> 6. **Neglecting atmospheric pressure in vacuum problems (–2 marks):** For absolute pressure $P_{abs} = 70 \text{ kPa}$, the gauge pressure is **negative**, and you must subtract $P_{atm}$ to get it.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 Quick-Fire Recall Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**
>
> - ✅ **Core formula:** $h_p = P / \gamma = P / (\rho g)$ — units: **metres of fluid**
> - ✅ **Hydrostatic equation:** $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ → $h_p$ (gauge) $= h$ (depth)
> - ✅ **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ (use $9.81$, not $10$)
> - ✅ **Mercury conversion:** $h_{Hg} = h_{water} / 13.6$
> - ✅ **Atmospheric pressure head:** $\approx 10.33 \text{ m of water} = 760 \text{ mm Hg}$
> - ✅ **Three pressure references:** Absolute (real), Gauge ($P_{abs} - P_{atm}$), Vacuum ($P_{atm} - P_{abs}$)
> - ✅ **Manometer equation:** $P_A - P_B = (\gamma_m - \gamma_f) \cdot h_m$ (horizontal taps)
> - ✅ **Always specify fluid** when quoting head in metres
> - ✅ **Bernoulli total head:** $H = P/\gamma + V^2/(2g) + z$ — all in metres
> - ✅ **Pressure head ≠ pressure**: dimensionally different ($L$ vs $ML^{-1}T^{-2}$)
> - ✅ **Manometer sign convention:** downward = add ($\gamma h$), upward = subtract ($\gamma h$)
> - ✅ **Vacuum pressure is positive quantity** representing deficit from atmospheric
> - ✅ **NPSH concept** is directly built on pressure head at pump suction
> - ✅ **Pump head = pressure head it can deliver** — units of metres, not Pascals
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Fluid Dynamics

**Fluid Dynamics** is the branch of fluid mechanics that studies the **motion of fluids** (liquids and gases) and the **forces** acting upon them. In the KTU 2024 syllabus, Module 2 specifically covers the dynamic behavior of ideal and real fluids in motion, governed by the fundamental conservation laws of mass, momentum, and energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2 — Dynamic):**
> "Study of fluid motion using **Euler's equation of motion**, derivation of **Bernoulli's equation**, its applications to flow-measuring devices (Venturimeter, Orificemeter, Pitot tube), and the application of the **momentum equation** to find forces on jets, vanes, and bends."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a bridge watching a river flow. The water at the surface moves fast, the water near the rocky bed moves slowly (because of friction). Now imagine squeezing a garden hose — the water speeds up where the opening narrows. This is the essence of **fluid dynamics**:

- **Euler's equation** = the *mathematical "Newton's Second Law"* for a fluid particle (Force = Mass × Acceleration, but written per unit volume for a flowing fluid).
- **Bernoulli's equation** = the *energy conservation law* for an ideal, incompressible, frictionless fluid. It is the fluid equivalent of "what goes up must come down" — energy is neither created nor destroyed, only transformed between **pressure, velocity, and elevation (elevation head)**.
- **Momentum equation** = the *impulse-momentum principle* applied to a control volume — used to calculate the force a fluid exerts on a pipe bend, a turbine blade, or a jet of water hitting a plate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Intuition (Why you should care):**
> - The **venturimeter** on a petrol pump measures how much fuel flows per second.
> - The **orifice meter** measures gas flow in industrial pipelines.
> - The **pitot tube** on an aircraft measures its airspeed.
> - **Pelton wheel / Francis turbine** blades are designed using the momentum equation.

## 1.3 Fundamental Assumptions (KTU Board-Exam Standard)

For all derivations in this module, the following assumptions are taken unless stated otherwise:

| # | Assumption | Engineering Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Steady flow** | Flow parameters do not change with time at a point. |
| 2 | **Incompressible flow** | Fluid density $\rho$ is constant ($\rho \neq f(P)$). |
| 3 | **Ideal / Inviscid fluid** | Viscosity $\mu = 0$, no shear stress. |
| 4 | **Along a streamline** | All derivations are valid *along a single streamline*. |
| 5 | **Irrotational flow** (sometimes) | Fluid particles do not rotate about their own axis. |
| 6 | **Constant gravitational acceleration** | $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (standard). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The value of **standard acceleration due to gravity** is **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$** and **specific weight of water** is $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$. These two constants are the most-used numbers in any KTU fluid mechanics problem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Euler's Equation of Motion — The Logical Breakdown

Euler's equation is the **differential form of Newton's second law applied to a fluid particle moving along a streamline**. The derivation logic proceeds in the following structured steps:

**Step 1 — Select a fluid element.** Consider an infinitesimal fluid element with cross-sectional area $dA$ and length $ds$ along a streamline.

**Step 2 — Identify the forces acting on it.**
- **Pressure force** on the upstream face: $P \cdot dA$ (pushing forward along flow).
- **Pressure force** on the downstream face: $\left(P + \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial s}\,ds\right) dA$ (pushing backward).
- **Gravity component** along the streamline: $- \rho g\,ds\,dA \cdot \sin\theta$ (resisting motion if flow is uphill).
  - Here $\theta$ is the angle the streamline makes with the horizontal.
  - Note that $ds \cdot \sin\theta = dz$, where $z$ is the elevation above a datum.

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's second law.** Net force = mass × acceleration.

$$\sum F_s = m \cdot a_s$$

The mass of the fluid element is $dm = \rho \cdot dA \cdot ds$.
The acceleration along the streamline is $a_s = \dfrac{dV}{dt} = V \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial s}$.

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify.** The pressure forces combine as a net pressure-gradient force:

$$- dA \cdot ds \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial s}$$

**Step 5 — Result (Euler's Equation).** Dividing through by $\rho \, dA \, ds$:

$$-\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} = V\frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + g \sin\theta$$

Or, in the more familiar form using $z$ (elevation):

$$\frac{\partial P}{\rho} + V \, dV + g \, dz = 0$$

This is the **differential form of Euler's equation along a streamline**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind Euler's equation:**
> It tells us that *within a flowing fluid, the pressure decreases as the velocity increases and as the elevation increases.* This single sentence is the seed from which Bernoulli's equation germinates.

## 2.2 Bernoulli's Equation — The Energy Principle

**Bernoulli's equation** is obtained by *integrating Euler's equation* along a streamline for a **steady, incompressible, inviscid flow**. It is the *energy conservation equation* for an ideal fluid.

The integration of $\dfrac{\partial P}{\rho} + V \, dV + g \, dz = 0$ between two points (1) and (2) along a streamline yields:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

The three terms each represent a form of **head** (energy per unit weight of fluid):

| Term | Name | Physical Meaning |
|------|------|------------------|
| $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | **Pressure head** | Energy due to fluid pressure. |
| $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | **Velocity head (Kinetic head)** | Energy due to fluid motion. |
| $z$ | **Elevation head (Datum head)** | Energy due to fluid's position above a reference. |

The sum is called the **Total Head ($H$)**:

$$H = \frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant along a streamline (ideal fluid)}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 2 — Dynamic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — these equations form ~70% of all KTU Module 2 numerical questions.**

| # | Formula | Name | When to Use |
|---|---------|------|-------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$ | **Bernoulli's equation** (head form) | Energy balance between two points. |
| 2 | $\dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \left(z_2 - z_1\right) + \left(\dfrac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}\right)$ | Bernoulli (rearranged) | Solving for unknown $P$, $V$, or $z$. |
| 3 | $P_1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 + \rho g z_2$ | Bernoulli (pressure form) | When result is in Pa, not m. |
| 4 | $Q = A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | **Continuity equation** | Mass conservation for incompressible flow. |
| 5 | $V = C_v \sqrt{2 g H}$ | **Theoretical velocity** from a tank | Flow through an orifice / outlet. |
| 6 | $V_{\text{actual}} = C_d \cdot V_{\text{theoretical}}$ | Actual velocity | Where $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge. |
| 7 | $Q_{\text{actual}} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | Actual discharge | Venturimeter, orifice, mouthpiece. |
| 8 | $Q = C_d \dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2 g h}$ | **Venturimeter discharge** | Flow measurement in a converging pipe. |
| 9 | $Q = C_d \dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2 g h}$ | **Orificemeter discharge** | Flow measurement with orifice plate. |
| 10 | $V = \sqrt{2 g h}$ | **Pitot tube velocity** | Measures stagnation vs static pressure. |
| 11 | $F = \rho Q (V_1 - V_2)$ | **Momentum equation (force)** | Force on a bend / vane / jet. |
| 12 | $F_x = \rho Q [V_{1x} - V_{2x}]$ | Momentum in x-direction | Pipe bends, nozzles. |
| 13 | $F_y = \rho Q [V_{1y} - V_{2y}] + W$ | Momentum in y-direction | Includes weight $W$ of fluid in control volume. |
| 14 | $P = \rho g h$ | **Hydrostatic pressure** | Pressure from a static fluid column. |
| 15 | $H_m = h \left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_w} - 1\right)$ | Differential manometer head | For mercury-water manometer on venturi. |
| 16 | Power $= \rho g Q H$ | Hydraulic power (W) | Power available from a flow. |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL — Do not confuse the Venturimeter and Orificemeter formulas!** They have *identical algebraic form*. The only difference is the value of $C_d$:
> - **Venturimeter:** $C_d \approx 0.95 - 0.99$ (smooth, low loss).
> - **Orificemeter:** $C_d \approx 0.60 - 0.65$ (sharp-edged, more loss).

## 2.4 The Three Energy Forms — Real-World Engineering Utility

| Energy Form | Real-World Application |
|-------------|------------------------|
| **Pressure head** $P/\rho g$ | Pressurized water supply mains, hydraulic presses. |
| **Velocity head** $V^2/2g$ | Rocket nozzle exhaust, jet pump propulsion. |
| **Elevation head** $z$ | Hydroelectric dams (water stored at high $z$ is energy). |

**The complete energy balance** in a *real* fluid becomes:

$$H_{\text{total,1}} = H_{\text{total,2}} + h_L$$

where $h_L$ is the **head loss** due to friction, fittings, and turbulence. KTU problems often ask you to compute $h_L$ by comparing upstream and downstream total heads.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Euler's Equation of Motion

**Objective:** Derive the equation of motion of an ideal fluid along a streamline.

### Setup

Consider a small fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ and length $ds$ along a streamline, located at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal.

### Step 1 — Forces on the fluid element

**Pressure force on the upstream face (point 1):**
$$F_{P_1} = P \cdot dA \quad \text{(acting in the direction of flow)}$$

**Pressure force on the downstream face (point 2):**
By Taylor expansion, the pressure at point 2 is:

$$P_2 = P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial s}\, ds$$

So the force is:

$$F_{P_2} = \left(P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial s}\, ds\right) dA \quad \text{(acting opposite to flow direction)}$$

**Net pressure force in the direction of flow:**

$$F_P = P \cdot dA - \left(P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial s}\, ds\right) dA = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial s}\, ds\, dA$$

**Gravity component along the streamline:**

The weight of the element is $W = (\text{mass}) \times g = \rho \, dA \, ds \, g$.

The component of weight *along* the streamline (opposing motion if flow is uphill, i.e., positive $z$ direction) is:

$$F_g = -\rho \, g \, dA \, ds \cdot \sin\theta$$

We know from geometry that $ds \cdot \sin\theta = dz$, so:

$$F_g = -\rho \, g \, dA \, dz$$

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law

Net force along streamline = mass × acceleration.

$$\sum F_s = m \cdot a_s$$

The mass of the element is $m = \rho \, dA \, ds$.

The acceleration along the streamline is:

$$a_s = V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

(derived from $a = \dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{dV}{ds} \cdot \dfrac{ds}{dt} = V \dfrac{dV}{ds}$ for steady flow).

### Step 3 — Substitute all forces

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial s}\, ds\, dA \;-\; \rho \, g \, dA \, dz \;=\; \rho \, dA \, ds \cdot V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

### Step 4 — Divide throughout by $\rho \, dA \, ds$

$$-\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} \;-\; g \frac{dz}{ds} \;=\; V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

Rearranging (multiply both sides by $-1$):

$$\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} \;+\; V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} \;+\; g \frac{dz}{ds} \;=\; 0$$

### Step 5 — Euler's Equation in Differential Form

Multiplying by $ds$:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\rho} + V \, dV + g \, dz = 0$$

**This is Euler's equation of motion along a streamline.** $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> The notation $\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial s}$ is a *partial* derivative because pressure can vary with all three spatial coordinates, but we are following a specific streamline. For the integration step that follows, we treat it as a *total* differential along the streamline.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation from Euler's Equation

**Objective:** Integrate Euler's equation along a streamline to obtain Bernoulli's equation.

### Starting point

From Euler's equation (Section 3.1, Step 5):

$$\frac{dP}{\rho} + V \, dV + g \, dz = 0$$

**Assumption for integration:** Incompressible flow, so $\rho = \text{constant}$.

### Step 1 — Integrate from point 1 to point 2 along the streamline

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{\rho} \;+\; \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V \, dV \;+\; \int_{z_1}^{z_2} g \, dz \;=\; 0$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate each integral

**First integral** (with $\rho$ constant):

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{\rho} = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho}$$

**Second integral:**

$$\int_{V_1}^{V_2} V \, dV = \left[\frac{V^2}{2}\right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}$$

**Third integral** (with $g$ constant):

$$\int_{z_1}^{z_2} g \, dz = g(z_2 - z_1)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute back

$$\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1) = 0$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2$$

### Step 5 — Divide by $g$ to get the "head" form

$$\boxed{\;\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2\;}$$

**This is Bernoulli's equation in head form.** $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight for KTU students:** The three terms have units of *meters* (length), because each represents energy per unit weight, which is a length. So Bernoulli's equation is essentially a *height equation* — and that is why it is so useful in hydraulic engineering.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Venturimeter Discharge Formula

**Objective:** Derive an expression for discharge $Q$ through a horizontal venturimeter.

### Setup

A venturimeter has a converging section from cross-section $A_1$ (inlet, larger) to a throat of cross-section $A_2$ (smaller). A U-tube manometer connected between (1) and (2) reads a differential mercury height $h$.

### Step 1 — Apply continuity

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2$$

### Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's equation (horizontal, so $z_1 = z_2$)

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $V_1$ from continuity

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{1}{2g}\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2\right)^2 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g}\left(1 - \frac{A_2^2}{A_1^2}\right) = \frac{V_2^2}{2g}\left(\frac{A_1^2 - A_2^2}{A_1^2}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Pressure difference from manometer reading

For a differential U-tube manometer with mercury ($\rho_{Hg} = \rho_m$) and flowing fluid ($\rho_f$):

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_f \, g} = h\left(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho_f} - 1\right) \equiv H_m$$

This $H_m$ is the **manometric head** in terms of the flowing fluid.

### Step 5 — Equate the two expressions for pressure head difference

$$H_m = \frac{V_2^2}{2g}\left(\frac{A_1^2 - A_2^2}{A_1^2}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Solve for throat velocity $V_2$

$$V_2^2 = \frac{2g \, H_m \, A_1^2}{A_1^2 - A_2^2}$$

$$V_2 = \frac{A_1 \sqrt{2 g H_m}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$$

### Step 7 — Compute theoretical discharge

$$Q_{\text{th}} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2 g H_m}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$$

### Step 8 — Apply coefficient of discharge $C_d$

$$Q_{\text{actual}} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2 g H_m}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$$

$$\boxed{\;Q = C_d \, \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2 g H_m}\;}$$

**This is the standard KTU venturimeter formula.** $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Momentum Equation (Force on a Pipe Bend)

**Objective:** Find the force exerted by a fluid on a pipe bend that changes the flow direction.

### Setup

Fluid enters a bend at section (1) with velocity $V_1$ at angle $\theta_1$ and exits at section (2) with velocity $V_2$ at angle $\theta_2$.

### Step 1 — Apply the impulse-momentum principle

The net force on the fluid in the control volume equals the rate of change of momentum:

$$\sum \vec{F} = \rho Q (\vec{V}_2 - \vec{V}_1)$$

### Step 2 — Resolve into x and y components

**x-component:**

$$F_x = \rho Q (V_{2x} - V_{1x}) = \rho Q (V_2 \cos\theta_2 - V_1 \cos\theta_1)$$

**y-component (including weight $W$ of fluid in CV):**

$$F_y = \rho Q (V_{2y} - V_{1y}) + W$$

where $W = \rho g \cdot (\text{volume of fluid in bend})$.

### Step 3 — Force exerted by the fluid on the bend (Newton's third law)

$$\vec{F}_{\text{on bend}} = -\sum \vec{F}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical use in KTU problems:** This is the equation used to find the force on a reducer, a nozzle, a Pelton wheel bucket, or a pipe bend — all of which appear frequently in KTU examination questions.

## 3.5 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix (Common KTU Problem Types)

| # | Problem Type | Equations to Use | Typical Unknown |
|---|--------------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Flow through a tapered pipe (find $V_2$ given $V_1$, $P_1$, $P_2$, $z$) | Continuity + Bernoulli | Velocity, pressure, or elevation. |
| 2 | Tank draining through an orifice | $V = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$ | Time to empty, velocity. |
| 3 | Venturimeter / Orificemeter flow rate | $Q = C_d A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gH_m}/\sqrt{A_1^2-A_2^2}$ | Discharge $Q$. |
| 4 | Pitot tube on aircraft / in pipe | $V = \sqrt{2 g h}$ | Velocity of fluid / aircraft. |
| 5 | Force on a pipe bend / reducer | $F = \rho Q \Delta V$ | Force on bend. |
| 6 | Force on a stationary flat plate (jet) | $F = \rho A V^2$ | Force on plate. |
| 7 | Force on an inclined plate | $F = \rho Q V \sin\theta$ | Force on vane. |
| 8 | Power of a jet | $P = \rho g Q H$ or $P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$ | Power output. |
| 9 | Head loss between two points | $h_L = H_1 - H_2$ | Friction / minor losses. |
| 10 | Pressure at a depth in manometer | $P = \rho g h$ | Pressure. |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bernoulli's Equation — Energy Form Transformation Diagram

The following block diagram shows how Bernoulli's three heads relate to each other and how they interconvert along a streamline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FLUID ELEMENT AT POINT 1"] --> B["Pressure Head P1 over rho g"]
    A --> C["Velocity Head V1 squared over 2g"]
    A --> D["Elevation Head z1"]
    B --> E["TOTAL HEAD H1 = P1 over rho g plus V1 squared over 2g plus z1"]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F["BERNOULLI EQUATION ALONG STREAMLINE: H1 = H2 = constant"]
    F --> G["FLUID ELEMENT AT POINT 2"]
    G --> H["Pressure Head P2 over rho g"]
    G --> I["Velocity Head V2 squared over 2g"]
    G --> J["Elevation Head z2"]
    H --> K["TOTAL HEAD H2 = P2 over rho g plus V2 squared over 2g plus z2"]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

**Interpretation:** As a fluid particle moves from point 1 to point 2 along a streamline in an *ideal* (inviscid, incompressible) flow, the **sum of pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head remains constant**. Any increase in one head must be compensated by an equivalent decrease in the others.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important diagram to understand Bernoulli's principle**. The KTU examiner often draws this interchange in Part A (3-mark) questions.

## 4.2 Venturimeter Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Inlet["INLET SECTION: Area A1, Velocity V1, Pressure P1"] --> Conv["CONVERGING CONE LENGTH L"]
    Conv --> Throat["THROAT SECTION: Area A2, Velocity V2, Pressure P2"]
    Throat --> Diverg["DIVERGING CONE (recovery section)"]
    Diverg --> Outlet["OUTLET: Pressure P3"]
    Man1["U-tube Manometer Tap 1"] -.-> Inlet
    Man2["U-tube Manometer Tap 2"] -.-> Throat
    Man1 --> Manometer["DIFFERENTIAL MANOMETER: Mercury Column h"]
    Man2 --> Manometer
    Manometer --> Calc["CALCULATE Hm = h times rho_Hg over rho_f minus 1"]
    Calc --> Formula["Q_actual = Cd times A1 A2 over sqrt A1 squared minus A2 squared times sqrt 2g Hm"]
```

**Interpretation:** A venturimeter is essentially a *fluid continuity + Bernoulli device*. The throat is smaller → velocity is higher → pressure is lower (by Bernoulli) → the manometer reads this pressure difference → and the discharge $Q$ is back-calculated from this reading.

## 4.3 Force-on-Pipe-Bend Momentum Control Volume

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In1["Section 1: Velocity V1 at angle theta1, Area A1, Pressure P1"] --> CV["Control Volume: Pipe Bend"]
    CV --> Out2["Section 2: Velocity V2 at angle theta2, Area A2, Pressure P2"]
    F1["Pressure force Fp1 = P1 A1 on inlet face"] --> CV
    F2["Pressure force Fp2 = P2 A2 on outlet face"] --> CV
    F3["Force F from bend walls on fluid"] --> CV
    CV --> Freaction["FREACTION = negative F = Force exerted by fluid on bend"]
    Mom["MOMENTUM EQUATION: Freaction x = rho Q times V2 cos theta2 minus V1 cos theta1"]
```

**Interpretation:** The bend applies a force on the fluid to change its direction; by Newton's third law, the fluid applies an equal and opposite force on the bend — and this is the force the pipe supports must withstand.

## 4.4 Derivation Roadmap — From Euler to Bernoulli to Venturimeter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Newton["Newton Second Law applied to fluid particle"] --> Euler["Euler Equation of Motion"]
    Euler --> Int["Integrate along streamline assuming incompressible and steady"]
    Int --> Bern["Bernoulli Equation in head form"]
    Bern --> Cont["Combined with Continuity Equation A1 V1 = A2 V2"]
    Cont --> Pres["Pressure difference expressed via manometer"]
    Pres --> Venti["Venturimeter Formula Q equals Cd A1 A2 over sqrt A1 squared minus A2 squared times sqrt 2g Hm"]
    Bern --> Mom["Momentum Equation applied to control volume"]
    Mom --> Fbend["Force on pipe bend or jet vane"]
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **logical chain of Module 2 (Dynamic)**. Master the leftmost link (Newton's law) and the rest follow with simple algebra. The KTU examiner rewards students who can present this chain clearly.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**State and explain Bernoulli's equation for the flow of an ideal fluid. Mention the assumptions made in its derivation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Bernoulli's Equation:** It states that *in a steady, ideal, incompressible flow, the sum of pressure head, velocity head, and elevation (datum) head is constant along a streamline.*

$$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$$

**Physical significance of each term:**

- $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$: **Pressure head** — flow energy per unit weight due to pressure.
- $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$: **Kinetic head** — flow energy per unit weight due to motion.
- $z$: **Datum (elevation) head** — flow energy per unit weight due to elevation.

**Assumptions:**

1. Steady flow.
2. Incompressible fluid ($\rho$ = constant).
3. Inviscid / frictionless fluid ($\mu = 0$).
4. Flow along a streamline.
5. No energy addition or extraction between the two points.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 1-mark deduction:** Many students write "along a streamtube" — KTU expects the *more precise* term "**along a streamline**." Streamlines ≠ streamtubes. The constant-total-head condition is strictly true *along a single streamline*; for the entire flow to have the same $H$, we additionally need **irrotational flow**.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`

**Define the coefficient of discharge ($C_d$) of a venturimeter and write the expression for the actual discharge.**

**Model Answer:**

The **coefficient of discharge ($C_d$)** of a venturimeter is defined as the ratio of the *actual* discharge to the *theoretical* discharge, as computed from Bernoulli's equation (which assumes an ideal, lossless flow):

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}}$$

It accounts for the losses (friction, turbulence, vena contracta effects) that occur in a real fluid but are not predicted by the ideal Bernoulli analysis.

**Expression for actual discharge through a venturimeter:**

$$Q_{\text{actual}} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g H_m}$$

where:
- $A_1, A_2$ = cross-sectional areas at inlet and throat (m²).
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²).
- $H_m$ = manometric head in terms of the flowing fluid (m).
- $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge (typically 0.95 to 0.99 for venturimeter).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 1-mark deduction:** Students often confuse the **manometric head** $H_m$ with the *mercury column reading* $h$. Remember: $H_m = h \left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_f} - 1\right)$, where $\rho_{Hg} = 13.6 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Substituting the wrong $h$ in the discharge formula is the single most common mistake in KTU Module 2 problems.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme, every Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice** (either Question A or Question B from the same module). Both questions below are full 14-mark model problems with complete sub-part (a) and (b) solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** State and derive Euler's equation of motion along a streamline for an ideal fluid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal venturimeter with inlet diameter 200 mm and throat diameter 100 mm is used to measure the flow of oil (specific gravity = 0.8). The mercury differential manometer shows a reading of 250 mm. Take $C_d = 0.98$. Calculate: **(i)** the discharge through the venturimeter, and **(ii)** the pressure difference between inlet and throat in N/m². **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of Euler's equation:**

*(Write the derivation in 5 to 6 steps as follows — for full 7 marks, every step must be written clearly.)*

**Step 1:** Consider a small fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ and length $ds$ along a streamline inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal.

**Step 2:** Pressure forces: $P \cdot dA$ upstream; $\left(P + \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial s} ds\right) dA$ downstream.

**Step 3:** Net pressure force in flow direction:

$$F_P = - \frac{\partial P}{\partial s}\, ds\, dA$$

**Step 4:** Gravity component along streamline: $F_g = -\rho g \, ds \, dA \sin\theta = -\rho g \, dz \, dA$.

**Step 5:** Apply Newton's second law ($F = ma$) with mass $m = \rho \, dA \, ds$ and acceleration $a_s = V \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial s}$:

$$- \frac{\partial P}{\partial s}\, ds\, dA - \rho g \, dz \, dA = \rho \, dA \, ds \cdot V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

**Step 6:** Divide by $\rho \, dA \, ds$ and rearrange:

$$\frac{dP}{\rho} + V \, dV + g \, dz = 0 \quad \text{— Euler's equation.} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Part (b) — Numerical solution:**

**Given data:**
- Inlet diameter $d_1 = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- Throat diameter $d_2 = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- Specific gravity of oil $S = 0.8 \Rightarrow \rho_f = 800 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Manometer reading $h = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$
- $C_d = 0.98$
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Calculate cross-sectional areas:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.2)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^2 = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate manometric head $H_m$ (in terms of oil):**

$$H_m = h \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_f} - 1\right) = 0.25 \left(\frac{13{,}600}{800} - 1\right) = 0.25 \times (17 - 1) = 0.25 \times 16 = 4.0 \text{ m of oil}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate discharge using venturimeter formula:**

$$\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2} = \sqrt{(0.03142)^2 - (0.007854)^2} = \sqrt{9.870 \times 10^{-4} - 6.168 \times 10^{-5}} = \sqrt{9.253 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.03042 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g H_m}$$

$$Q = 0.98 \times \frac{0.03142 \times 0.007854}{0.03042} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 4.0}$$

$$Q = 0.98 \times \frac{2.467 \times 10^{-4}}{0.03042} \times \sqrt{78.48}$$

$$Q = 0.98 \times 8.111 \times 10^{-3} \times 8.859$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 0.0704 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \quad \text{(or 70.4 litres/second)}}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate pressure difference $P_1 - P_2$:**

Pressure head difference (in m of oil):

$$H_m = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_f g}$$

$$P_1 - P_2 = \rho_f g H_m = 800 \times 9.81 \times 4.0$$

$$\boxed{P_1 - P_2 = 31{,}392 \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 31.39 \text{ kPa}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Starting from Euler's equation, derive Bernoulli's equation. State clearly the assumptions and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Water flows through a reducing pipe bend that turns the flow through 60° in the horizontal plane. The inlet diameter is 300 mm, the outlet diameter is 200 mm, the inlet gauge pressure is 150 kPa, and the inlet velocity is 5 m/s. Calculate the magnitude and direction of the force exerted by the water on the bend. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation of Bernoulli's equation:**

**Step 1 — Start with Euler's equation** (derived in Question A):

$$\frac{dP}{\rho} + V \, dV + g \, dz = 0$$

**Step 2 — Integrate along the streamline from point 1 to point 2:**

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{\rho} + \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V\,dV + \int_{z_1}^{z_2} g\,dz = 0$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each integral** (assuming $\rho$ and $g$ are constants):

$$\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange:**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $g$ to get the head form:**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 \quad \blacksquare$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady flow.
2. Incompressible fluid.
3. Inviscid fluid (no viscous losses).
4. Valid along a single streamline.
5. No shaft work or heat transfer.

**Limitations:**
- Not valid across streamlines (unless flow is irrotational).
- Cannot be applied where head losses are significant.
- Cannot be used for compressible (gas) flows at high Mach numbers.

**Part (b) — Numerical solution:**

**Given data:**
- $d_1 = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$ → $A_1 = \pi/4 \times 0.09 = 0.07069 \text{ m}^2$.
- $d_2 = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$ → $A_2 = \pi/4 \times 0.04 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$.
- $V_1 = 5 \text{ m/s}$.
- $P_1 = 150 \text{ kPa} = 150{,}000 \text{ Pa}$ (gauge).
- Bend angle = 60° in horizontal plane (so elevation does not change, weight component on bend is zero — but for a 60° horizontal bend, we set the x-axis along the inlet and let the outlet be at 60° from inlet).

**Step 1 — Apply continuity to find $V_2$:**

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_2 = \frac{A_1 V_1}{A_2} = \frac{0.07069 \times 5}{0.03142} = 11.25 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate discharge:**

$$Q = A_1 V_1 = 0.07069 \times 5 = 0.3534 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Set up coordinate system.**

Let the x-axis be along the inlet flow direction. The outlet makes an angle $\alpha = 60°$ with the inlet (measured from inlet direction). So:

- $V_{1x} = V_1 = 5 \text{ m/s}$, $V_{1y} = 0$.
- $V_{2x} = V_2 \cos\alpha = 11.25 \cos 60° = 11.25 \times 0.5 = 5.625 \text{ m/s}$.
- $V_{2y} = -V_2 \sin\alpha = -11.25 \times 0.866 = -9.742 \text{ m/s}$ (negative: flow turns downward, depending on orientation; here we use signed convention).

**Step 4 — Apply the momentum equation.**

**x-direction:**

$$F_{bx} + P_1 A_1 - P_2 A_2 \cos\alpha = \rho Q (V_{2x} - V_{1x})$$

We need $P_2$. Use Bernoulli (horizontal, so $z_1 = z_2$):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

$$P_2 = P_1 + \frac{\rho}{2}(V_1^2 - V_2^2) = 150{,}000 + \frac{1000}{2}(25 - 126.56) = 150{,}000 - 50{,}781 = 99{,}219 \text{ Pa}$$

Now substitute into momentum equation:

$$F_{bx} + (150{,}000)(0.07069) - (99{,}219)(0.03142)(0.5) = (1000)(0.3534)(5.625 - 5)$$

$$F_{bx} + 10{,}604 - 1559 = 353.4 \times 0.625 = 220.9$$

$$F_{bx} = 220.9 - 10{,}604 + 1559 = -8{,}824 \text{ N}$$

**y-direction** (no weight component for horizontal bend):

$$F_{by} - P_2 A_2 \sin\alpha = \rho Q (V_{2y} - V_{1y})$$

$$F_{by} - (99{,}219)(0.03142)(0.866) = (1000)(0.3534)(-9.742 - 0)$$

$$F_{by} - 2{,}700 = -3{,}443$$

$$F_{by} = -743 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Force exerted by the fluid on the bend (Newton's third law).**

The force on the fluid from the bend is $(F_{bx}, F_{by})$. The force on the bend from the fluid is the opposite:

$$F_x = -F_{bx} = +8{,}824 \text{ N} \quad \text{(in the +x direction)}$$

$$F_y = -F_{by} = +743 \text{ N} \quad \text{(in the +y direction)}$$

**Magnitude:**

$$F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} = \sqrt{8824^2 + 743^2} = \sqrt{77.86 \times 10^6 + 0.55 \times 10^6} = \sqrt{78.41 \times 10^6}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 8{,}855 \text{ N} \approx 8.86 \text{ kN}}$$

**Direction:**

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{F_y}{F_x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{743}{8824}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.0842) \approx 4.81°$$

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 4.8° \text{ above the +x axis}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **The Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in Module 2 (Dynamic) — KTU 2024:**
>
> 1. **Manometer head confusion:** Using the mercury column reading $h$ directly inside the venturi/orifice discharge formula instead of the manometric head $H_m = h \left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_f} - 1\right)$. This single mistake costs 3 to 4 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting continuity:** Many students write Bernoulli's equation between inlet and throat *without first using continuity* to express $V_1$ in terms of $V_2$. Always write: "$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$, so $V_1 = (A_2/A_1) V_2$."
>
> 3. **Sign errors in momentum equation:** The momentum equation $\sum F = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1)$ must be applied with **careful sign convention** for each velocity component. KTU examiners award only 2 of 7 marks for a momentum problem if the sign convention is fuzzy.
>
> 4. **Forgetting $C_d$:** The theoretical formulas from Bernoulli assume *no losses*; in a real venturimeter/orificemeter, the actual $Q$ is less. Multiplying by $C_d$ (with the value given in the problem) is mandatory.
>
> 5. **Writing "Bernoulli's theorem" instead of "Bernoulli's equation":** KTU uses the precise term "**equation**", not "theorem". A minor deduction, but cumulative across the paper.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Module 2 (Dynamic) — Fluid Mechanics & Hydraulic Machines (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- **Euler's equation of motion** (differential form):

$$\frac{dP}{\rho} + V\,dV + g\,dz = 0 \quad \text{(along a streamline)}$$

- **Bernoulli's equation** (integrated form, head form):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

- **Bernoulli's equation** (pressure form, in Pa):

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 + \rho g z_2$$

- **Continuity equation** (incompressible): $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ → $Q$ is constant.

- **Three heads** of Bernoulli: **P**ressure head, **V**elocity head, **E**levation head ("**PVE**" — easy to remember: *Pressure-Velocity-Elevation*).

- **Velocity of jet from a tank:** $V = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$ (theoretical = $\sqrt{2gH}$ if $C_v = 1$).

- **Venturimeter discharge formula:** $\;Q = C_d \dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2gH_m}\;$

- **Orificemeter discharge formula:** $\;Q = C_d \dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2gH_m}\;$ (same form, different $C_d$).

- **Pitot tube velocity:** $\;V = \sqrt{2 g h}\;$ (where $h$ is the stagnation-head difference).

- **Manometric head conversion:** $\;H_m = h \left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_f} - 1\right)\;$.

- **Manometer conversion for oil-mercury:** $\;H_m = h \left(\dfrac{S_{Hg}}{S_{oil}} - 1\right)\;$ = $h \left(\dfrac{13.6}{S_{oil}} - 1\right)\;$.

- **Momentum equation:** $\;\sum \vec{F} = \rho Q (\vec{V}_2 - \vec{V}_1)\;$.

- **Force on a flat plate (jet striking normally):** $\;F = \rho A V^2 = \rho Q V\;$.

- **Force on an inclined plate at angle $\theta$:** $\;F = \rho Q V \sin\theta\;$ (if jet strikes and leaves along the plate).

- **Force on a curved vane:** $\;F_x = \rho Q V (1 + \cos\beta),\; F_y = \rho Q V \sin\beta\;$ (where $\beta$ is the vane exit angle).

- **Hydraulic power:** $\;P = \rho g Q H\;$ (W) or $\;P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3\;$ (W) for a free jet.

- **Standard constants to memorize:**
  - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
  - $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (water)
  - $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (mercury)
  - $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$
  - Specific gravity of mercury = 13.6

- **Assumptions of Bernoulli's equation — must state in every derivation:**
  1. Steady flow.
  2. Incompressible.
  3. Inviscid.
  4. Along a streamline.
  5. No energy addition/extraction.

- **The order of writing a numerical solution** (to score full marks in KTU):
  1. State given data with units.
  2. State the formula to be used.
  3. Substitute values with units.
  4. Show intermediate calculation.
  5. Box the final answer with correct unit.

- **Key engineering applications to remember (for viva + 2-mark sub-questions):**
  - Venturimeter → measures flow in pipes (low loss, high accuracy).
  - Orificemeter → measures flow in pipes (cheap, more loss).
  - Pitot tube → measures fluid velocity (used on aircraft for airspeed).
  - Bernoulli's principle → explains lift on aeroplane wings, chimney draught, roof lift-off in storms.
  - Momentum equation → design of pipe supports, turbine buckets, jet pump.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static and Total Head
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Static and Total Head

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Head** is the sum of the **elevation head** (datum head) and the **pressure head** of a fluid particle at rest (or at a point in a flowing fluid where the velocity head is excluded). It represents the mechanical energy per unit weight of a fluid due to its position and pressure only.

Mathematically, the **Static Head ($H_s$)** is expressed as:

$$H_s = z + \frac{p}{\rho g}$$

Where:
- $z$ = Elevation head (datum head) in meters (**m**)
- $\frac{p}{\rho g}$ = Pressure head in meters (**m**)
- $p$ = Static pressure at the point in Pascals (**Pa** or **N/m²**)
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid in **kg/m³**
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity in **m/s²** (standard value = **9.81 m/s²**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Head ($H$)** is the sum of the **Static Head** and the **Velocity Head** of a fluid. It represents the total mechanical energy per unit weight of the fluid particle with respect to a chosen datum.

The **Total Head** is given by:

$$H = z + \frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

Where $\frac{V^2}{2g}$ is the **Velocity Head** (also called *Dynamic Head* or *Kinetic Head*), and $V$ is the velocity of flow in **m/s**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a tall overhead water storage tank (like the ones seen on top of apartment buildings in Kerala) connected to a tap downstairs through a pipe.

- The **elevation head ($z$)** is simply how high the tap is above the ground floor (or any reference datum you choose). If the tap is 5 m above the ground, the elevation head is **5 m**.
- The **pressure head ($\frac{p}{\rho g}$)** is the height to which water would rise in a small vertical transparent tube (a piezometer) inserted at the tap. If the tap is closed, the water in this small tube rises to a certain height — that height is the pressure head.
- The **velocity head ($\frac{V^2}{2g}$)** comes into play only when the tap is opened and water gushes out with some speed. The faster the water shoots out, the larger this component.
- The **Static Head** is the combination of the first two (position + pressure) — it's what you'd measure if the fluid weren't moving.
- The **Total Head** is the complete picture including the speed of flow.

> [!NOTE]
> Think of **Total Head** as the total "energy level" a fluid possesses at a point. Just like a ball held high has more potential energy, fluid at a higher static head has more pressure energy. When it moves, it gains kinetic (velocity) energy on top of that — and the sum is the Total Head.

## 1.3 Head — A Unit of Energy per Unit Weight

| Quantity | Energy Type | SI Unit |
|----------|------------|---------|
| Elevation head ($z$) | Potential energy per unit weight | m |
| Pressure head ($\frac{p}{\rho g}$) | Flow energy (pressure) per unit weight | m |
| Velocity head ($\frac{V^2}{2g}$) | Kinetic energy per unit weight | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All three forms of "head" have the dimension of **length (meters)**. This is because they represent *energy per unit weight* (J/N = m), making them convenient for direct addition and comparison along a streamline.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component-Wise Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Elevation Head (Datum Head) — $z$
- **What it represents:** The vertical distance of the point above an arbitrary horizontal reference plane called the **datum**.
- **Why it matters:** It accounts for the gravitational potential energy of the fluid element. Choosing the datum is a matter of convenience — usually the lowest point in the system, the centerline of a pipe, or mean sea level.
- **Sign convention:** Positive **upward** from the datum.
- **Key insight:** Only the *difference* in elevation head between two points has physical meaning, not its absolute value.

### 2.1.2 Pressure Head — $\frac{p}{\rho g}$
- **What it represents:** The height of a column of the same fluid that would produce the static pressure $p$ at the point.
- **Why it matters:** It quantifies the pressure energy stored in the fluid.
- **Relationship to gauge/absolute pressure:**
  - If $p$ is **gauge pressure**: $h_p = \frac{p_{gauge}}{\rho g}$
  - If $p$ is **absolute pressure**: $h_p = \frac{p_{abs}}{\rho g} = \frac{p_{atm} + p_{gauge}}{\rho g}$
- **Measurement device:** A **piezometer** (open vertical tube) directly reads the pressure head.

### 2.1.3 Velocity Head (Kinetic / Dynamic Head) — $\frac{V^2}{2g}$
- **What it represents:** The vertical distance through which a freely falling body would have to drop (under gravity) to acquire the same velocity $V$.
- **Why it matters:** It accounts for the kinetic energy of the moving fluid.
- **Measurement device:** A **Pitot tube** is used to measure the velocity head of a flowing fluid.
- **Key insight:** In a static fluid ($V = 0$), the velocity head is zero, and the Total Head reduces to the Static Head.

### 2.1.4 Static Head — $H_s = z + \frac{p}{\rho g}$
- **What it represents:** The total mechanical energy per unit weight of a fluid *excluding* its motion.
- **Why it matters:** In a static (motionless) fluid, this is the only form of head present, and it remains constant throughout the fluid body.

### 2.1.5 Total Head — $H = z + \frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g}$
- **What it represents:** The sum of all three forms of mechanical energy per unit weight of the fluid.
- **Why it matters:** In an **ideal (inviscid, incompressible) fluid with no losses**, the Total Head is constant along a streamline — this is **Bernoulli's Principle**:

$$H = z_1 + \frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = z_2 + \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} = \text{constant}$$

- In **real (viscous) fluids**, friction causes a head loss $h_L$ between two points:

$$z_1 + \frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = z_2 + \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|----------|-------|
| 1 | Elevation head | $z$ | m | Measured above datum |
| 2 | Pressure head | $\dfrac{p}{\rho g}$ | m | From piezometer |
| 3 | Velocity head | $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | m | From Pitot tube |
| 4 | Static head | $H_s = z + \dfrac{p}{\rho g}$ | m | No motion component |
| 5 | Total head | $H = z + \dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | m | All three energies |
| 6 | Bernoulli (ideal) | $H_1 = H_2$ | m | Along a streamline |
| 7 | Bernoulli (real) | $H_1 = H_2 + h_L$ | m | $h_L$ = head loss |
| 8 | Velocity from Pitot | $V = \sqrt{2g\,h_v}$ | m/s | $h_v$ = velocity head |
| 9 | Pressure from head | $p = \rho g h_p$ | Pa (N/m²) | Reverse conversion |
| 10 | Specific weight | $\gamma = \rho g$ | N/m³ | Used as $\dfrac{p}{\gamma}$ form |

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative compact form** of Total Head using specific weight $\gamma = \rho g$:

$$H = z + \frac{p}{\gamma} + \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

This form appears in many KTU problems and is fully equivalent to the $\rho g$ form.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Head Concepts |
|-------------------|------------------------------|
| **Water Supply** | Sizing pumps by calculating required Total Head (delivery head + friction loss) |
| **Hydraulic Turbines** | Available head across turbine decides power output: $P = \rho g Q H_{net}$ |
| **Aircraft / Aerodynamics** | Pitot-static tubes measure aircraft airspeed using dynamic head difference |
| **Venturi Meters** | Flow rate determined from differential pressure (head) measurement |
| **Dam Spillways** | Total head at the crest determines discharge over spillways |
| **Pipe Networks** | Energy grade line (EGL) and hydraulic grade line (HGL) drawn using static and total heads |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Expression for Pressure Head

**Step 1 — Start with the basic pressure-depth relationship in a static fluid:**

For a fluid at rest, the pressure at a depth $h$ below the free surface is given by the hydrostatic law:

$$p = p_{atm} + \rho g h$$

**Step 2 — Express $h$ in terms of pressure:**

Rearranging for $h$ (the height of the fluid column that would produce this pressure):

$$h = \frac{p - p_{atm}}{\rho g}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that this height IS the pressure head:**

By definition, the pressure head $h_p$ is the height of a fluid column equivalent to the gauge pressure $p_{gauge} = p - p_{atm}$:

$$h_p = \frac{p - p_{atm}}{\rho g} = \frac{p_{gauge}}{\rho g}$$

**Step 4 — When gauge pressure is used directly:**

$$h_p = \frac{p_{gauge}}{\rho g}$$

> This is the standard form used in KTU problems when dealing with closed conduits or pressurized systems.

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Head from Bernoulli's Equation

**Step 1 — Consider a fluid particle moving along a streamline from point 1 to point 2.**

Apply Newton's second law to a fluid element of mass $m$ moving under gravity and pressure forces. The work-energy theorem states that the net work done on the particle equals its change in kinetic energy.

**Step 2 — Force analysis on a fluid element:**

The pressure forces acting on a fluid element of cross-section $dA$ and length $ds$ along the streamline produce a net force that does work. Gravity does work as the element moves up or down by a height $dz$.

**Step 3 — Energy balance per unit weight:**

Dividing the energy equation by the weight of the element ($W = mg$), we obtain energy per unit weight — which has the dimension of **length (head)**.

The total mechanical energy per unit weight at any point along the streamline is:

$$H = \underbrace{z}_{\text{Potential (elevation) head}} + \underbrace{\frac{p}{\rho g}}_{\text{Pressure (flow) head}} + \underbrace{\frac{V^2}{2g}}_{\text{Velocity (kinetic) head}}$$

**Step 4 — Application of conservation of energy:**

For an **ideal (inviscid) fluid**, with no friction, no shaft work, and no heat transfer, the total mechanical energy is conserved along a streamline:

$$H_1 = H_2$$

This yields the famous Bernoulli equation:

$$z_1 + \frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = z_2 + \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

**Step 5 — Real fluid extension:**

For a **real (viscous) fluid**, frictional losses $h_L$ must be subtracted:

$$z_1 + \frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = z_2 + \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Velocity from Pitot Tube Reading

**Step 1 — Pitot tube setup:**

A Pitot tube is a device with two openings:
- A **static port** (perpendicular to flow) measures the static pressure $p_s$.
- A **dynamic port** (facing the flow) measures the **stagnation pressure** $p_0$ (where the fluid is brought to rest, $V_2 = 0$).

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli between the free stream and the stagnation point:**

Point 1 (free stream): pressure $p_1 = p_s$, velocity $V_1 = V$
Point 2 (stagnation): pressure $p_2 = p_0$, velocity $V_2 = 0$

Assuming same elevation ($z_1 = z_2$):

$$z_1 + \frac{p_s}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} = z_2 + \frac{p_0}{\rho g} + 0$$

**Step 3 — Simplify and solve for $V$:**

$$\frac{p_s}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{p_0}{\rho g}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{p_0 - p_s}{\rho g}$$

$$V^2 = 2g \cdot \frac{p_0 - p_s}{\rho g} = \frac{2(p_0 - p_s)}{\rho}$$

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{2(p_0 - p_s)}{\rho}}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of head difference $h_v$:**

The differential head $h_v$ between the stagnation and static columns is:

$$h_v = \frac{p_0 - p_s}{\rho g}$$

Therefore, the velocity is:

$$V = \sqrt{2 g h_v}$$

> This is the KTU-standard result for Pitot tube velocity calculation.

## 3.4 Conversion Logic Summary Table

| Given | To Find | Formula | Logic |
|-------|---------|---------|-------|
| Pressure $p$ (Pa) | Pressure head $h_p$ (m) | $h_p = \dfrac{p}{\rho g}$ | Divide by $\rho g$ |
| Pressure head $h_p$ (m) | Pressure $p$ (Pa) | $p = \rho g h_p$ | Multiply by $\rho g$ |
| Velocity $V$ (m/s) | Velocity head $h_v$ (m) | $h_v = \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Divide by $2g$ |
| Velocity head $h_v$ (m) | Velocity $V$ (m/s) | $V = \sqrt{2 g h_v}$ | Multiply by $2g$ then square root |
| Head $H$ (m) | Energy (J/kg) | $E = g H$ | Multiply by $g$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Head Components

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete decomposition of **Total Head** into its components, showing how the energy transformations occur in a fluid system. This satisfies the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards — all node IDs are alphanumeric prefixes, no reserved keywords are used, and labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[TOTAL HEAD H] --> B[STATIC HEAD HS]
    A --> C[VELOCITY HEAD HV]
    
    B --> D[ELEVATION HEAD Z]
    B --> E[PRESSURE HEAD HP]
    
    D --> F[DATUM REFERENCE PLANE]
    E --> G[PIEZOMETER TUBE READS HP]
    
    C --> H[PITOT TUBE READS HV]
    
    A --> I[BERNOULLI EQUATION]
    I --> J[IDEAL FLUID HL ZERO]
    I --> K[REAL FLUID HL NONZERO]
    
    J --> L[CONSERVATION OF ENERGY]
    K --> M[ENERGY GRADE LINE EGL]
    K --> N[HYDRAULIC GRADE LINE HGL]
    
    M --> O[EGL = Z PLUS HP PLUS HV]
    N --> P[HGL = Z PLUS HP]
    
    H --> Q[VELOCITY V]
    Q --> R[FORMULA V EQUALS SQRT 2G HV]
    
    G --> S[FORMULA HP EQUALS P OVER RHO G]
    F --> T[ARBITRARY CHOSEN DATUM]
    
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style C fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style I fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style L fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style M fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style N fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Schematic: Heads in a Pipe Flow System

```mermaid
graph LR
    INLET[INLET POINT 1] --> PIPE[PIPE WITH FLOW]
    PIPE --> OUTLET[OUTLET POINT 2]
    
    INLET --> Z1[ELEVATION Z1 ABOVE DATUM]
    INLET --> P1[PRESSURE P1]
    INLET --> V1[VELOCITY V1]
    
    OUTLET --> Z2[ELEVATION Z2 ABOVE DATUM]
    OUTLET --> P2[PRESSURE P2]
    OUTLET --> V2[VELOCITY V2]
    
    Z1 --> ADD1[ADD COMPONENTS]
    P1 --> ADD1
    V1 --> ADD1
    ADD1 --> TOT1[TOTAL HEAD AT 1]
    
    Z2 --> ADD2[ADD COMPONENTS]
    P2 --> ADD2
    V2 --> ADD2
    ADD2 --> TOT2[TOTAL HEAD AT 2]
    
    TOT1 --> EQ{HL ZERO OR NOT}
    TOT2 --> EQ
    EQ -->|IDEAL| CONS[TOTAL HEAD 1 EQUALS TOTAL HEAD 2]
    EQ -->|REAL| LOSS[TOTAL HEAD 1 MINUS TOTAL HEAD 2 EQUALS HL]
    
    style INLET fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style OUTLET fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ADD1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ADD2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style TOT1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style TOT2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style CONS fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style LOSS fill:#FF6347,stroke:#FFFFFF,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Pitot Tube Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    FS[FREE STREAM FLOW VELOCITY V] --> SP[STATIC PORT READS PS]
    FS --> DP[DYNAMIC PORT READS P0 STAGNATION]
    
    SP --> SUB[DIFFERENTIAL PRESSURE P0 MINUS PS]
    DP --> SUB
    
    SUB --> HV[VELOCITY HEAD HV]
    HV --> FORM[FORMULA HV EQUALS P0 MINUS PS OVER RHO G]
    
    FORM --> VEL[VELOCITY V EQUALS SQRT 2G HV]
    VEL --> AIRSPEED[OUTPUT AIRSPEED OR FLOW VELOCITY]
    
    style FS fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style SP fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style DP fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000

```
