<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define the following terms with respect to fluid flow:**
**(a) Static Head**
**(b) Velocity Head**
**(c) Total Head**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Static Head:** The static head at a point in a fluid is the sum of the elevation head (datum head) and the pressure head at that point. It represents the mechanical energy per unit weight of a fluid due to its position and pressure only, excluding the kinetic energy of motion.

$$H_s = z + \frac{p}{\rho g}$$

**(b) Velocity Head:** The velocity head is the kinetic energy per unit weight of the flowing fluid, defined as the height to which a fluid would rise if it were brought to rest isentropically. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$h_v = \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

where $V$ is the velocity of the fluid in m/s and $g$ is acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²).

**(c) Total Head:** The total head at a point is the sum of the static head and the velocity head, representing the total mechanical energy per unit weight of the fluid particle at that point.

$$H = z + \frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> All three heads are expressed in **meters (m)** as they represent energy per unit weight of the fluid (J/N = m).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Explain how a Pitot tube is used to measure the velocity of flow in a pipe. Derive the expression for velocity in terms of the head difference.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Pitot tube** is a device used to measure the velocity of fluid flow at a specific point in a pipe. It consists of two concentric tubes:
- The **inner tube** has its opening facing the flow direction and measures the **stagnation (total) pressure** $p_0$ at the point where the fluid is brought to rest.
- The **outer tube** has openings perpendicular to the flow and measures the **static pressure** $p_s$ of the flowing fluid.

**Derivation:**

Applying Bernoulli's equation between a point in the free stream (point 1) and the stagnation point (point 2), assuming the same elevation:

$$\frac{p_s}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{p_0}{\rho g} + 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{p_0 - p_s}{\rho g} = h_v$$

$$V = \sqrt{2 g h_v} = \sqrt{\frac{2(p_0 - p_s)}{\rho}}$$

The head difference $h_v$ is the **velocity head** of the flow.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern:** Students must answer ONE full question from the choice. Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Detailed model solutions are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a)** A pipe carrying water has a diameter of 200 mm at section 1 and 100 mm at section 2. The pressures at sections 1 and 2 are 100 kPa and 50 kPa respectively. The pipe is inclined at 30° to the horizontal, with section 2 being 2 m higher than section 1. Calculate the discharge through the pipe, assuming no head loss. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(b)** Define **(i)** Energy Grade Line (EGL) and **(ii)** Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL). With a neat sketch, explain how these lines are drawn for flow through a pipe with sudden expansion.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Given Data:**
- Diameter at section 1: $D_1 = 200$ mm = 0.2 m
- Diameter at section 2: $D_2 = 100$ mm = 0.1 m
- Pressure at section 1: $p_1 = 100$ kPa = $100 \times 10^3$ Pa
- Pressure at section 2: $p_2 = 50$ kPa = $50 \times 10^3$ Pa
- Elevation difference: $z_2 - z_1 = 2$ m (section 2 is higher)
- Head loss: $h_L = 0$ (ideal flow)
- Density of water: $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1: Calculate cross-sectional areas.**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.2)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^2 = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Apply continuity equation.**

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

$$V_2 = V_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = V_1 \cdot \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2 = V_1 \cdot \left(\frac{0.2}{0.1}\right)^2 = 4 V_1$$

**Step 3: Apply Bernoulli's equation (no head loss).**

$$z_1 + \frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = z_2 + \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

Taking section 1 as datum: $z_1 = 0$ and $z_2 = 2$ m

**Step 4: Calculate the pressure heads.**

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} = \frac{100 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 9.81} = \frac{100}{9.81} = 10.194 \text{ m}$$

$$\frac{p_2}{\rho g} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 9.81} = \frac{50}{9.81} = 5.097 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Substitute into Bernoulli's equation.**

$$0 + 10.194 + \frac{V_1^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 2 + 5.097 + \frac{(4V_1)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$10.194 + \frac{V_1^2}{19.62} = 7.097 + \frac{16 V_1^2}{19.62}$$

**Step 6: Solve for $V_1$.**

$$10.194 - 7.097 = \frac{16 V_1^2 - V_1^2}{19.62}$$

$$3.097 = \frac{15 V_1^2}{19.62}$$

$$V_1^2 = \frac{3.097 \times 19.62}{15} = \frac{60.76}{15} = 4.051 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$V_1 = \sqrt{4.051} = 2.013 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7: Calculate discharge.**

$$Q = A_1 \times V_1 = 0.03142 \times 2.013 = 0.0632 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 0.0632 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 63.2 \text{ L/s}}$$

---

#### Part (b) Solution:

**(i) Energy Grade Line (EGL):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Energy Grade Line (EGL)** is the locus of points representing the **Total Head** ($H = z + \frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g}$) of the fluid above a chosen datum, plotted along the length of the flow.

For an **ideal fluid**, the EGL is a **horizontal straight line** at a constant elevation equal to the total head.
For a **real fluid**, the EGL **continuously drops** in the direction of flow due to frictional head loss.

**(ii) Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL)** is the locus of points representing the **Static Head** ($H_s = z + \frac{p}{\rho g}$) of the fluid above a chosen datum, plotted along the length of the flow.

The HGL lies **below the EGL by an amount equal to the velocity head** ($\frac{V^2}{2g}$). It is the level to which water would rise in piezometer tubes installed at various points along the pipe.

**Sketch for Pipe with Sudden Expansion:**

For a sudden expansion, the EGL shows a **sudden drop** equal to the local loss at the expansion. The HGL also drops by the same amount because the static head reduces. The vertical distance between EGL and HGL remains equal to the velocity head at each section.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** Water flows through a pipe of diameter 300 mm with a velocity of 3 m/s. The pipe is divided into two parallel pipes of diameters 150 mm and 200 mm. If the flow divides equally, calculate the velocity in each branch and the corresponding velocity heads.

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **Pitot-static tube**. Derive the expression for the velocity of flow in terms of the difference of pressure heads.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Given Data:**
- Main pipe diameter: $D = 300$ mm = 0.3 m
- Main pipe velocity: $V = 3$ m/s
- Branch 1 diameter: $D_1 = 150$ mm = 0.15 m
- Branch 2 diameter: $D_2 = 200$ mm = 0.2 m
- Flow divides equally: $Q_1 = Q_2 = \frac{Q}{2}$

**Step 1: Calculate total discharge in main pipe.**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.3)^2 = 0.07069 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q = A \times V = 0.07069 \times 3 = 0.2121 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate discharge in each branch (equal division).**

$$Q_1 = Q_2 = \frac{Q}{2} = \frac{0.2121}{2} = 0.1060 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate cross-sectional area of each branch.**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.2)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Calculate velocity in each branch.**

$$V_1 = \frac{Q_1}{A_1} = \frac{0.1060}{0.01767} = 6.0 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_2 = \frac{Q_2}{A_2} = \frac{0.1060}{0.03142} = 3.375 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5: Calculate velocity heads.**

$$h_{v1} = \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{(6.0)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{36}{19.62} = 1.835 \text{ m}$$

$$h_{v2} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} = \frac{(3.375)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{11.391}{19.62} = 0.5805 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{V_1 = 6.0 \text{ m/s}, \quad h_{v1} = 1.835 \text{ m}, \quad V_2 = 3.375 \text{ m/s}, \quad h_{v2} = 0.5805 \text{ m}}$$

---

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Construction of Pitot-Static Tube:**

A Pitot-static tube consists of two concentric tubes:
1. **Inner tube (Pitot tube):** Has a single opening at its tip facing directly into the flow. It measures the **stagnation pressure** ($p_0$) at the point where the fluid is brought to rest.
2. **Outer tube (Static tube):** Has multiple openings on its circumference, perpendicular to the flow direction. These measure the **static pressure** ($p_s$) of the undisturbed flow.

The two tubes are connected to the two limbs of a **U-tube differential manometer** (or a precise pressure transducer) which indicates the difference in pressure heads.

**Working Principle:**

When the Pitot-static tube is inserted into a flowing fluid, the inner tube's opening is hit head-on by the fluid, causing a stagnation point at the opening. The pressure recorded here is the sum of static and dynamic pressures (i.e., stagnation/total pressure). The outer tube's perpendicular openings sense only the static pressure of the undisturbed flow.

**Derivation for Velocity:**

Let point 1 be in the free stream (undisturbed) and point 2 be the stagnation point.

At point 1: pressure = $p_s$, velocity = $V$, elevation = $z$ (same for both points)
At point 2: pressure = $p_0$, velocity = 0, elevation = $z$

Applying Bernoulli's equation between points 1 and 2 (along the same streamline):

$$z + \frac{p_s}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} = z + \frac{p_0}{\rho g} + 0$$

$$\frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{p_0 - p_s}{\rho g}$$

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{2(p_0 - p_s)}{\rho}} = \sqrt{2 g h_v}$$

where $h_v = \frac{p_0 - p_s}{\rho g}$ is the differential head shown by the manometer.

$$\boxed{V = \sqrt{2 g h_v}}$$

This is the KTU standard result.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (Most Common — 1 to 2 marks lost):** Students often mix units. Pressure in kPa and area in mm², or velocity in cm/s with g in m/s². **Always convert to SI units first** (Pa, m², m/s, m/s²) before substituting.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Datum:** The elevation head $z$ depends on the chosen datum. **Always state the datum explicitly** (e.g., "taking the centerline of the main pipe as datum, $z_1 = 0$"). Don't mix datums within a single problem.
> 
> 3. **Wrong Sign for Elevation Difference:** When section 2 is higher than section 1, $z_2 - z_1 > 0$ (positive). When it is lower, it is negative. A sign error here propagates throughout and gives the wrong velocity.
> 
> 4. **Using $V^2 / 2g$ vs $V^2 / 2gh$ Confusion:** Velocity head is $\frac{V^2}{2g}$ (length units), not $\frac{V^2}{2gh}$ (which would be dimensionless). Never confuse with piezometric head.
> 
> 5. **Pitot Tube Derivation Sign:** In the derivation, students sometimes write $p_s - p_0$ instead of $p_0 - p_s$. Since $p_0 > p_s$ always, the head difference is positive, and the resulting $V$ is real.
> 
> 6. **Missing the "no head loss" condition:** If the problem states $h_L = 0$, use the ideal Bernoulli equation. If losses are present, write $H_1 = H_2 + h_L$ correctly.
> 
> 7. **Continuity Equation Oversight:** When two diameters are given, students sometimes forget to relate $V_1$ and $V_2$ via $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$. This leaves two unknowns with one equation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Static and Total Head**

- **Static Head** = Elevation head ($z$) + Pressure head ($\frac{p}{\rho g}$)
- **Total Head** = Static Head + Velocity head ($\frac{V^2}{2g}$)
- All three components have the unit **meter (m)**, representing energy per unit weight.
- **Elevation head $z$** is measured from an **arbitrary datum** (only differences are physically meaningful).
- **Pressure head $\frac{p}{\rho g}$** is the height the fluid rises in a **piezometer**.
- **Velocity head $\frac{V^2}{2g}$** is measured using a **Pitot tube** and equals the differential head $h_v$.
- **Bernoulli's equation** (ideal fluid): $z_1 + \frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = z_2 + \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$ = constant.
- **Real fluid modification:** Add head loss $h_L$ on the downstream side.
- **Continuity equation:** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ is always paired with Bernoulli to solve flow problems.
- **Pitot tube velocity formula:** $V = \sqrt{2 g h_v}$ — the **most asked** derivation in KTU.
- **EGL** plots Total Head along the flow length; **HGL** plots Static Head. EGL − HGL = Velocity Head.
- For water: $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ and $\gamma = \rho g = 9810$ N/m³ — **memorize these**.
- **Unit conversions to remember:** 1 kPa = 1000 Pa, 1 atm = 101.325 kPa, 1 m³/s = 1000 L/s.
- **Sign convention:** Pressures are often given as **gauge pressure** in KTU problems unless stated otherwise. If absolute, subtract $p_{atm}$ to get gauge.
- The **datum choice is yours** — choose the lowest point or centerline to simplify algebra (avoid negative values when possible).
- **Common KTU problem types:** (1) Find discharge given two sections and pressures, (2) Find velocity from Pitot reading, (3) Sketch EGL/HGL, (4) Compute static and total heads at given points.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuity Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continuity Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Continuity Equation** is the mathematical statement of the **principle of conservation of mass** applied to a flowing fluid. For a fluid in motion, it expresses that the rate of change of mass within a control volume is exactly balanced by the net mass flow rate entering or leaving that volume.

In its most general differential form (for a 3D, unsteady, compressible flow), the continuity equation is expressed as:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = **Mass density** of the fluid (kg/m³)
- $\vec{V}$ = **Velocity vector** of the fluid flow (m/s)
- $\nabla \cdot$ = **Divergence operator**
- $t$ = Time (s)

For a **steady, incompressible flow** (the most common case in KTU problems), this reduces to the elegant scalar form:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$

For a **1-D streamline flow** (typical for pipe/nozzle problems), it simplifies to the celebrated area-velocity relation:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = \text{constant}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The continuity equation is the *first* governing equation students must master in Fluid Mechanics. It applies universally — to ideal and real fluids, to compressible and incompressible flows, and to laminar as well as turbulent regimes. It is the mathematical backbone of problems involving **venturimeters, orifices, nozzles, pipes of varying cross-section, and open channel flow**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose" Intuition

Imagine you are watering your garden using a flexible hose. If you press your thumb partially over the opening at the nozzle end, what happens?

- The **cross-sectional area** through which water escapes becomes *smaller*.
- The **velocity** of water coming out becomes *much greater* — the water sprays out forcefully!

Now ask yourself: *Did the amount (mass) of water leaving the pipe per second change?* **No!** The pipe is delivering the same mass of water per second throughout its length. What changed is just the *shape* of the channel through which it flows. Where the area is small, the fluid must speed up. Where the area is large, the fluid slows down.

> **The rule is simple: Mass in = Mass out. The fluid just rearranges its speed to obey this rule.**

**Another everyday example — Traffic Flow:**
Consider vehicles moving on a highway. On a 4-lane wide road, cars move slowly. As the road narrows to 2 lanes (say, near a toll booth), the cars must speed up to maintain the same *number of cars per second* passing any point. This is the continuity equation in action — applied to "car density" instead of fluid density!

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Typical Range (Water) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (water at 4°C) | $\rho$ | **kg/m³** | 1000 |
| Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | **N·s/m²** or Pa·s | $1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | **m²/s** | $1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Gravitational Acceleration | $g$ | **m/s²** | 9.81 |
| Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q$ | **m³/s** | — |
| Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m}$ | **kg/s** | — |
| Velocity | $V$ | **m/s** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Incompressibility Assumption:** For liquids (especially water) and for gases moving at less than ~Mach 0.3, we treat $\rho$ = **constant**. This single assumption is what reduces the complex vector equation into the simple "AV = constant" form used in 90% of KTU numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physical Logic — Step by Step

The continuity equation is not an empirical law — it is a **direct mathematical consequence of mass conservation**. Let us break down the reasoning:

### Step 1: Identify the Control Volume
A **control volume (CV)** is an imaginary region in space through which the fluid flows. Its boundary is called the **control surface (CS)**. We apply conservation of mass to whatever fluid is currently inside this volume.

### Step 2: Apply Conservation of Mass
Conservation of mass demands:

$$\left(\text{Rate of mass accumulation inside CV}\right) = \left(\text{Net rate of mass flow IN through CS}\right)$$

In symbolic form:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

- The **first integral** captures how mass is *building up* (or depleting) inside the CV.
- The **second integral** is the *net outflow* of mass across the boundary.

### Step 3: Apply the Divergence Theorem
Using Gauss's divergence theorem, the surface integral can be converted into a volume integral:

$$\int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \, dV$$

Combining both terms into a single volume integral:

$$\int_{CV} \left[\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V})\right] dV = 0$$

### Step 4: Localize the Equation
Since the control volume is *arbitrary*, the integrand itself must vanish identically:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0}$$

This is the **general continuity equation** in differential form.

### Step 5: Apply Engineering Simplifications
Depending on the problem type, we simplify:

| Flow Type | Simplification | Resulting Form |
|---|---|---|
| Steady flow | $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$ | $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ |
| Incompressible | $\rho = \text{const}$ | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ |
| 1-D incompressible steady | $V$ along one axis | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ |
| Compressible steady | $\rho \neq \text{const}$ | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ |

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Form

**Why does incompressibility make the equation so simple?**
Because if $\rho$ is truly constant, then *any* parcel of fluid that enters a control volume must displace an *equal volume* of fluid leaving. There is no "compression" to absorb or release mass. Hence, $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ — physically meaning fluid elements are neither converging nor diverging at a net rate.

**Why is the 1-D form $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ valid only for streamline flow?**
Because we assume the velocity is *uniform* across each cross-section and *parallel* to the pipe axis. In real flows, there is a velocity profile (parabolic for laminar flow in pipes), but for KTU problems, the *average* velocity is used.

**Why does it fail for supersonic flows?**
At high Mach numbers, density changes become significant (e.g., in rocket nozzles, supersonic wind tunnels). The simple $A V = \text{const}$ relation breaks down, and we must use the compressible form: $\rho A V = \text{const}$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. Every numerical in the KTU exam will use at least one of these.**

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Applicability | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General Differential Form | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | All flows | — |
| 2 | Incompressible Differential | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ | Liquid / low-Mach gas | — |
| 3 | 1-D Cartesian Incompressible | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$ | Incompressible 3-D | 1/s |
| 4 | 1-D Streamline (Incompressible) | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | Pipes, nozzles, venturi | m³/s |
| 5 | 1-D Compressible Streamline | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ | Compressible flow | kg/s |
| 6 | Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ | Any 1-D flow | kg/s |
| 7 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q = A V$ | Incompressible | m³/s |
| 8 | Continuity in Cylindrical Coords | $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$ | Pipe flow, axisymmetric | 1/s |
| 9 | Continuity in Stream Tube | $\rho dA \cdot V = \text{const along tube}$ | Streamline analysis | kg/s |
| 10 | Average Velocity | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{1}{A}\int_A u \, dA$ | Real pipe flow | m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols used in the table:** $u, v, w$ are velocity components along $x, y, z$ axes; $v_r, v_\theta, v_z$ are radial, tangential, and axial components in cylindrical coordinates.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

The continuity equation is not just an academic exercise. It is the silent workhorse behind:

1. **Venturimeter and Orifice Meter Design:** Used to measure discharge in pipelines. The flow area contracts, velocity increases, and pressure drops — a direct application of $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$.

2. **Aircraft Wing Aerodynamics:** The air above the curved upper surface of a wing has a longer path → higher velocity (continuity) → lower pressure (Bernoulli) → lift.

3. **Blood Flow Modeling in Biomedical Engineering:** Continuity is used to model blood flow through arteries, where the cross-section changes due to blockages (stenosis).

4. **Rocket Nozzle Design:** Compressible form $\rho A V = \text{const}$ is critical — as gas expands, density drops, and both area and velocity change to conserve mass.

5. **Open Channel Flow (Hydraulic Jumps):** Water depth changes abruptly in a hydraulic jump; continuity ensures $q = V_1 y_1 = V_2 y_2$ where $y$ is the depth.

6. **Dam Spillway Design:** Engineers use continuity to size the spillway opening for a given flood discharge.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — General Differential Form (from First Principles)

### Setup: An Infinitesimal Control Volume
Consider a small rectangular fluid element of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$ in a 3D Cartesian coordinate system, located at point $(x, y, z)$ at time $t$.

```
            z
            |
            |  w (velocity in z)
            | /
            |/
            *-------- y  (v in y)
           /|
          / |
         /  |------> x  (u in x)
        u
```

The fluid density at the centre of the element is $\rho$, and the velocity components are $u, v, w$ along the $x, y, z$ axes respectively.

### Step 1: Mass Inside the Element
The mass contained within the element at time $t$ is:

$$m = \rho \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$$

### Step 2: Mass Flux In — x Direction (Left Face)
The mass entering per unit time through the left face (at $x$) is:

$$\dot{m}_{x,in} = \rho u \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 3: Mass Flux Out — x Direction (Right Face)
On the right face (at $x + dx$), the density and velocity have changed slightly. Using a **Taylor series expansion**:

$$\rho_{x+dx} = \rho + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} dx$$

$$u_{x+dx} = u + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} dx$$

The mass leaving per unit time through the right face is:

$$\dot{m}_{x,out} = \left(\rho + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} dx\right)\left(u + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} dx\right) dy \, dz$$

### Step 4: Net Mass Accumulation in x-Direction
The *net rate of mass leaving* in the $x$-direction is $\dot{m}_{x,out} - \dot{m}_{x,in}$. The mass accumulation (rate of change) inside the element due to $x$-flow is:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \rho u}{\partial x}\right) dx \, dy \, dz$$

(after neglecting second-order differentials like $dx^2$)

### Step 5: Summing All Three Directions
By identical reasoning in the $y$ and $z$ directions, the total net mass outflow rate is:

$$\left(\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z}\right) dx \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 6: Rate of Change of Mass Inside the Element
Mass inside the element changes at the rate:

$$\frac{\partial m}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \cdot dx \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 7: Apply Conservation of Mass
**Rate of mass accumulation = Net rate of mass inflow** (i.e., negative of net outflow):

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \cdot dx \, dy \, dz = -\left(\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z}\right) dx \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 8: Cancel the Volume Element
Since $dx \, dy \, dz \neq 0$, divide both sides:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$$

### Step 9: Recognize the Divergence Operator
The last three terms are precisely $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V})$ in Cartesian form:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0} \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reduction to 1-D Incompressible Form

Starting from the general form:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Assumption 1 — Steady flow:** $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$

**Assumption 2 — Incompressible:** $\rho = \text{constant}$, so it can be pulled out of partial derivatives.

$$0 + \rho\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}\right) = 0$$

Dividing by $\rho$:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Assumption 3 — 1-D flow along x-axis:** $v = 0$, $w = 0$, and $u = u(x)$ only.

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad u = f(t)$$

For steady flow, $u$ is constant. Consider a stream tube with cross-section $A$ perpendicular to the flow. The volumetric flow rate is:

$$Q = \int_A u \, dA = u \cdot A$$

Since the fluid is incompressible, the volumetric flow rate must be conserved between two cross-sections (1 and 2) along a stream tube (this is rigorously proven via the Reynolds Transport Theorem, but for KTU purposes it is stated as a direct consequence of the incompressibility assumption):

$$Q_1 = Q_2$$

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

$$\boxed{\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \dot{m} = \text{constant}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Continuity Equation in Cylindrical Coordinates

For flows in pipes, jets, and around cylindrical bodies, cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$ are more natural. The derivation follows the same procedure but applied to a volume element $r \, d\theta \cdot dr \cdot dz$.

The result is:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0}$$

For **incompressible, axisymmetric, fully-developed pipe flow** (where $v_r = 0$, $v_\theta = 0$, and $v_z = v_z(r)$ only):

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d (r v_z)}{dr} = 0$$

Integrating:

$$r v_z = C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_z = \frac{C_1}{r}$$

This result shows that in an *inviscid* sink/source flow, velocity varies as $1/r$. (For real viscous pipe flow, a different result is obtained — the parabolic Poiseuille profile — but that comes from the Navier-Stokes equations, not just continuity.)

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Patterns

| Problem Type | Given Data | Continuity Equation Form | Goal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pipe diameter changes | $D_1, V_1, D_2$ | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | Find $V_2$ |
| Branching pipe | $\dot{m}_{in}$, two outlets | $\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2$ | Find $V_2$ |
| Tank draining | $D_{tank}, D_{orifice}$ | $A_1 \frac{dh}{dt} = -A_2 V_2$ | Find $\frac{dh}{dt}$ |
| Compressible flow | $P_1, T_1, A_1, A_2, V_1$ | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ (use ideal gas law) | Find $V_2$ |
| Stream tube in 2-D flow | Velocity field $u(x,y), v(x,y)$ | $\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$ | Verify / find stream function |
| Open channel | Width $b$, depth $y$ | $Q = b_1 y_1 V_1 = b_2 y_2 V_2$ | Find new depth |

---

## 3.5 Illustrative Numerical — Worked Example 1

> **Problem:** Water flows steadily through a pipe that tapers from a diameter of **20 cm** at section 1 to **10 cm** at section 2. The velocity at section 1 is **2 m/s**. Find: (a) velocity at section 2, (b) volumetric flow rate, (c) mass flow rate.

### Solution:

**(a) Velocity at section 2:**

Using continuity: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$

$$V_2 = \frac{A_1 V_1}{A_2} = \frac{(\pi/4)(0.20)^2 \times 2}{(\pi/4)(0.10)^2}$$

$$V_2 = 2 \times \left(\frac{0.20}{0.10}\right)^2 = 2 \times 4 = \boxed{8 \text{ m/s}}$$

> **Key insight:** When diameter halves, area quarters, and velocity *quadruples*.

**(b) Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = A_1 V_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.20)^2 \times 2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.04)(2)$$

$$Q = 0.02\pi = \boxed{0.0628 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**(c) Mass flow rate (for water, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³):**

$$\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1000 \times 0.0628 = \boxed{62.8 \text{ kg/s}}$$

---

## 3.6 Illustrative Numerical — Worked Example 2 (Compressible)

> **Problem:** Air flows through a duct. At section 1, the velocity is **100 m/s**, the pressure is **200 kPa**, and the temperature is **400 K**. The duct area is **0.05 m²**. At section 2, the area is **0.02 m²**, pressure is **150 kPa**, and temperature is **350 K**. Find the velocity at section 2.

### Solution:

Using ideal gas law: $\rho = \dfrac{P}{RT}$, with $R = 287$ J/(kg·K) for air.

**Density at section 1:**

$$\rho_1 = \frac{200{,}000}{287 \times 400} = \frac{200{,}000}{114{,}800} = 1.742 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Density at section 2:**

$$\rho_2 = \frac{150{,}000}{287 \times 350} = \frac{150{,}000}{100{,}450} = 1.493 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Apply mass continuity:** $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$

$$V_2 = \frac{\rho_1 A_1 V_1}{\rho_2 A_2} = \frac{1.742 \times 0.05 \times 100}{1.493 \times 0.02}$$

$$V_2 = \frac{8.71}{0.02986} = \boxed{291.7 \text{ m/s}}$$

> **Verification:** Since the area *decreased* (0.05 → 0.02) and density *decreased* (1.742 → 1.493), the velocity *must* increase substantially. Our result of 291.7 m/s is consistent. ✓

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**State the continuity equation for a 1-D, steady, incompressible flow along a stream tube. Mention the physical principle on which it is based.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Physical Principle:** The continuity equation is based on the **principle of conservation of mass**.

**Statement:** For a 1-D, steady, incompressible flow, the volumetric flow rate is constant along a stream tube. Therefore:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q = \text{constant}$$

Where $A$ is the cross-sectional area perpendicular to the flow and $V$ is the average flow velocity at that section.

**Significance:** It implies that if the cross-section decreases, the velocity must increase proportionally to maintain a constant mass flow rate.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for the principle, 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for the interpretation/significance.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between the differential and integral forms of the continuity equation. State the integral form.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Aspect | Differential Form | Integral Form |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Point-wise (local) | Whole control volume (global) |
| Use | Field analysis, CFD | Practical engineering calculations |
| Order | First-order PDE | Integral equation |

**Integral Form of Continuity Equation:**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

Where the first term is the rate of mass accumulation inside the control volume (CV), and the second term is the net mass efflux through the control surface (CS) with outward unit normal $\hat{n}$.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for difference, 2 marks for correctly stating the integral form with all terms explained.)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Module 2 Part B questions typically carry 14 marks divided as 7 + 7 across two sub-parts. Students answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**Q.A. (a)** Derive the continuity equation in differential form in Cartesian coordinates for a compressible fluid flow. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**Q.A. (b)** Water flows through a pipe that tapers from 30 cm diameter at section 1 to 15 cm diameter at section 2. If the velocity at section 1 is 1.5 m/s, calculate: (i) the velocity at section 2, (ii) the volumetric flow rate, and (iii) the mass flow rate. Take $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A. (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Define the Control Volume (1 mark)**
Consider an infinitesimal rectangular fluid element of dimensions $dx, dy, dz$ located at point $(x, y, z)$ in a 3D Cartesian frame. Let $\rho$ be the density and $u, v, w$ be the velocity components along $x, y, z$ axes respectively.

**Step 2: Mass Inside the Element (1 mark)**
The mass contained is $m = \rho \, dx \, dy \, dz$.

**Step 3: Net Mass Outflow in x-Direction (1.5 marks)**
- Mass flow IN through left face: $\rho u \, dy \, dz$
- Mass flow OUT through right face (at $x + dx$): $\left(\rho + \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial x}dx\right)\left(u + \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}dx\right) dy \, dz$
- Net outflow in x: $\dfrac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} \, dx \, dy \, dz$

**Step 4: Similar Terms for y and z (1 mark)**
By identical reasoning, net outflows in y and z are $\dfrac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} dx \, dy \, dz$ and $\dfrac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} dx \, dy \, dz$ respectively.

**Step 5: Rate of Mass Accumulation (1 mark)**
$\dfrac{\partial m}{\partial t} = \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} dx \, dy \, dz$

**Step 6: Apply Conservation of Mass and Simplify (1.5 marks)**
Rate of mass accumulation = -Net mass outflow rate:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$$

This is the **general differential form of the continuity equation** in Cartesian coordinates for a compressible flow.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A. (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $D_1 = 30$ cm = 0.30 m, $D_2 = 15$ cm = 0.15 m
- $V_1 = 1.5$ m/s
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³

**Areas:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.30)^2 = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 = 0.0177 \text{ m}^2$$

**(i) Velocity at Section 2 (3 marks):**
Using $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$:

$$V_2 = \frac{A_1 V_1}{A_2} = \frac{0.0707 \times 1.5}{0.0177} = \frac{0.1060}{0.0177} = \boxed{6.0 \text{ m/s}}$$

**(ii) Volumetric Flow Rate (2 marks):**

$$Q = A_1 V_1 = 0.0707 \times 1.5 = \boxed{0.106 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**(iii) Mass Flow Rate (2 marks):**

$$\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1000 \times 0.106 = \boxed{106 \text{ kg/s}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**

**Q.B. (a)** With a neat sketch, derive the continuity equation for a steady, 1-D, incompressible flow through a variable area duct. Show that $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$. **(7 Marks)**

**Q.B. (b)** A horizontal pipe of 20 cm diameter carries water at a velocity of 3 m/s. The pipe is divided into three parallel branches, each of diameter 10 cm, with equal velocities in each branch. Find: (i) the velocity in each branch, and (ii) the discharge in the main pipe. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B. (a) — 7 Marks

**Sketch (described textually — 1 mark):** A horizontal duct with cross-sections $A_1$ and $A_2$ at sections 1 and 2 respectively, with fluid entering at velocity $V_1$ and leaving at velocity $V_2$.

**Step 1: Mass Flow Rate Definition (1.5 marks)**
The mass flow rate at any section is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A V$$

where $A$ is the cross-sectional area, $V$ is the average velocity, and $\rho$ is the density.

**Step 2: Apply Conservation of Mass (2 marks)**
For a **steady** flow, the mass entering the duct per unit time must equal the mass leaving per unit time (assuming no accumulation):

$$\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out}$$

$$\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$$

**Step 3: Simplification for Incompressible Flow (1.5 marks)**
For an **incompressible** fluid, $\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho = \text{constant}$. Cancelling $\rho$:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q = \text{constant}$$

This is the desired result.

**Step 4: General Form (1 mark)**
For **compressible** flow, density cannot be cancelled, and the general form $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ holds.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B. (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Main pipe: $D = 20$ cm = 0.20 m, $V_{main} = 3$ m/s
- Three parallel branches: $d = 10$ cm = 0.10 m each, $V_{branch} = ?$

**Areas:**

$$A_{main} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.20)^2 = 0.0314 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{branch} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = 0.00785 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 1: Apply Continuity with Branching (3 marks)**
By conservation of mass, the discharge in the main pipe equals the sum of discharges in the three branches:

$$Q_{main} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3$$

$$A_{main} \cdot V_{main} = 3 \times (A_{branch} \cdot V_{branch})$$

(assuming equal velocities in each branch as given)

**Step 2: Solve for $V_{branch}$ (2 marks):**

$$V_{branch} = \frac{A_{main} \cdot V_{main}}{3 \cdot A_{branch}} = \frac{0.0314 \times 3}{3 \times 0.00785}$$

$$V_{branch} = \frac{0.0942}{0.02355} = \boxed{4.0 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 3: Discharge in Main Pipe (2 marks):**

$$Q_{main} = A_{main} \cdot V_{main} = 0.0314 \times 3 = \boxed{0.0942 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Verification:** Total branch discharge = $3 \times 0.00785 \times 4.0 = 0.0942$ m³/s ✓ (matches main pipe discharge)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert cm to m:** A *very* common mistake. If diameters are in cm, areas come out 10,000 times too large. Always convert to SI units *before* substitution. (Penalty: 1–2 marks)
>
> 2. **Mixing up diameters and radii:** $A = \pi D^2 / 4$, NOT $\pi r^2$ with $r = D$ directly. Watch the formula.
>
> 3. **In branching pipe problems:** Students often equate $A_{main} V_{main} = A_{branch} V_{branch}$ and *forget to multiply by the number of branches (n)*. The correct form is $A_{main} V_{main} = n \cdot A_{branch} V_{branch}$. (Penalty: 1–2 marks)
>
> 4. **Forgetting units in the final answer:** KTU examiners are strict. Always quote velocity in m/s, flow rate in m³/s, and mass flow rate in kg/s.
>
> 5. **Not stating the principle:** In Part A derivations, always *begin* by stating "The continuity equation is based on the **principle of conservation of mass**." This is worth at least 1 mark in itself.
>
> 6. **Writing $\Delta V / \Delta x$ instead of $\partial V / \partial x$:** In differential forms, always use the **partial derivative symbol** $\partial$, not the regular differential $d$.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Must-Know" Bullets for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

- ✅ The continuity equation is the **mathematical statement of conservation of mass** for a flowing fluid.
- ✅ **General differential form:** $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$
- ✅ **Steady flow assumption** removes the time derivative: $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$
- ✅ **Incompressible assumption** allows $\rho$ to come out of the divergence: $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$
- ✅ **1-D incompressible form (the workhorse!):** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$
- ✅ **1-D compressible form:** $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$
- ✅ **Volumetric flow rate:** $Q = AV$ (units: m³/s)
- ✅ **Mass flow rate:** $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ (units: kg/s)
- ✅ In Cartesian form, $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$
- ✅ In cylindrical form, $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$
- ✅ **Branching pipe rule:** $Q_{in} = \sum Q_{out}$, i.e., $\dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$
- ✅ For **tanks draining/filling:** $A_{tank} \dfrac{dh}{dt} = -A_{orifice} V_{orifice}$ (sign convention critical)
- ✅ For **open channel flow:** $Q = b \cdot y \cdot V$ (where $b$ = width, $y$ = depth)
- ✅ Always quote: $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $R_{air} = 287$ J/(kg·K)
- ✅ The continuity equation does **not** involve pressure or forces — it is purely a kinematic (geometric) statement about mass conservation.
- ✅ For **derivation questions**, always start by drawing/sketching the control volume, defining the density and velocity components, applying Taylor series expansion, and using conservation of mass.

---

> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Euler's Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Euler's Equation of Motion** is the fundamental differential equation governing the motion of an **ideal (inviscid) fluid** along a streamline, derived by applying **Newton's Second Law of Motion** to an infinitesimal fluid element. It expresses the balance between inertial forces, pressure forces, and gravitational forces acting on a fluid particle, and serves as the parent equation from which **Bernoulli's Equation** is obtained upon integration.

The general form of Euler's Equation (vector form) is:

$$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$$

For flow along a streamline, the scalar form is:

$$\frac{dp}{\rho} + g \, dz + V \, dV = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Euler's Equation is treated as the foundation for deriving Bernoulli's Equation and forms the gateway to energy equations in fluid mechanics. It is a **high-frequency** module-2 topic in ESE (End Semester Evaluation).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **skating down a hill** on a frictionless surface. As you descend, you speed up — your *kinetic energy* increases at the cost of *potential energy*. If we treated the skater as a "fluid particle" with no friction (inviscid), and tracked how pressure, elevation, and velocity trade off, **Euler's Equation** is exactly the rule governing that trade-off at an *infinitesimally small* point.

- **Pressure** is the push from neighboring fluid particles.
- **Gravity** is the pull trying to pull particles downward.
- **Acceleration** is the rate at which velocity changes.

Euler's Equation is essentially **Newton's Law rewritten for a fluid particle** that has no internal friction. It says: *the net push minus the gravitational pull equals the fluid particle's mass times its acceleration*.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Quantities (Constants & Metrics)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) |
|--------|----------|--------------------|
| $p$ | Static Pressure | **N/m²** (Pa) |
| $\rho$ | Mass Density | **kg/m³** |
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** |
| $V$ | Flow velocity | **m/s** |
| $z$ | Elevation head | **m** |
| $\gamma = \rho g$ | Specific weight | **N/m³** |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 numerical problems, always use $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ unless explicitly stated otherwise. The density of **water** is taken as $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of Euler's Equation

For Euler's Equation to be strictly valid, the following idealizations must hold:

1. **Inviscid Fluid:** The fluid has **zero viscosity** — no internal shear stresses exist between fluid layers.
2. **Steady Flow:** Flow parameters (velocity, pressure, density) at a point do **not** change with time.
3. **Flow Along a Streamline:** Equation is derived along a single streamline (general vector form is streamline-independent).
4. **Continuous Fluid:** Fluid is treated as a continuum — no molecular gaps.
5. **Compressible or Incompressible:** General form accommodates compressible flow; integration requires the $\rho = f(p)$ relation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** Loss of marks most frequently occurs when students **forget to state assumptions** in derivations. Always enumerate at least 4 assumptions before starting the proof.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

The equation is derived by **applying Newton's Second Law** to a fluid element moving along a streamline:

- **Step 1 (Free Body):** Identify all forces (pressure + gravity) on an elementary fluid parcel.
- **Step 2 (Newton's Law):** Equate net force to mass × acceleration along the streamline.
- **Step 3 (Geometric Link):** Resolve gravity along the streamline using the geometric identity $ds \sin\theta = dz$.
- **Step 4 (Cancel Terms):** Cancel common area and length terms to obtain a point-form equation.
- **Step 5 (Final Form):** Rearrange into the canonical form: $\dfrac{dp}{\rho} + g\,dz + V\,dV = 0$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Name / Form | Applicable Case |
|---|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dp}{\rho} + g\,dz + V\,dV = 0$ | Euler's Equation (Differential) | General (along streamline) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial s} + g\dfrac{dz}{ds} + V\dfrac{dV}{ds} = 0$ | Euler's Equation (Expanded) | Along streamline of length $s$ |
| 3 | $\int \dfrac{dp}{\rho} + \int g\,dz + \int V\,dV = \text{const}$ | Integrated Form | Compressible / Incompressible |
| 4 | $\dfrac{p}{\rho} + gz + \dfrac{V^2}{2} = C$ | Bernoulli (Incompressible, $\rho = \text{const}$) | When $\rho$ is constant |
| 5 | $\dfrac{p}{\gamma} + z + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} = H$ | Bernoulli (Head form) | Hydraulic engineering problems |
| 6 | $\int \dfrac{dp}{\rho} + \dfrac{V^2}{2} + gz = \text{const}$ | Bernoulli (Compressible) | When $\rho = f(p)$ |
| 7 | $\rho\dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho\vec{g}$ | Euler's Equation (Vector form) | General 3-D inviscid flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Conversion Trick:** To convert Euler's form to **head form**, divide the entire equation by $g$ (acceleration). This yields terms in **meters (head)**, which is the standard form used in KTU numerical problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Euler's Equation is **not a textbook abstraction** — it is the cornerstone of:

- **Aerofoil and Aircraft Design:** Pressure distribution around wings (lift generation) uses inviscid flow theory derived from Euler's equations.
- **Venturi Meters and Orifice Plates:** Flow rate calculations in pipelines.
- **Pitot-Static Tubes:** Aircraft airspeed measurement.
- **Hydraulic Machines:** Foundation for analyzing turbines and pumps (especially in Module 4 of PCAUT302).
- **Open Channel Flow:** Basis for energy-depth relationships in weirs and spillways.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Geometric Setup

Consider an **ideal fluid** flowing along a streamline. Select an **infinitesimal fluid element** at a point on the streamline with the following geometry:

- Cross-sectional area perpendicular to streamline: $dA$
- Length of element along streamline: $ds$
- The streamline makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal.
- Pressure at the upstream face (section 1–1): $p$
- Pressure at the downstream face (section 2–2): $p + dp$

The mass of the fluid element is:

$$m = \rho \cdot dA \cdot ds$$

## 3.2 Force Analysis

### 3.2.1 Net Pressure Force (along streamline)

The pressure forces act **normal to the curved surface** of the element. By resolving along the streamline:

- Force at upstream face: $p \cdot dA$ (acts in the direction of flow)
- Force at downstream face: $(p + dp) \cdot dA$ (acts against the flow)
- Component of pressure force on the curved surface along streamline = $0$ (by symmetry, since pressure acts normal)

Net pressure force along streamline:

$$F_{p} = p \cdot dA - (p + dp) \cdot dA = -dp \cdot dA$$

### 3.2.2 Gravity Force (along streamline)

The weight of the fluid element acts vertically downward:

$$W = \rho \cdot dA \cdot ds \cdot g$$

Resolving along the streamline (the angle between the vertical and the streamline normal-to-horizontal direction):

The component of weight along the streamline is:

$$W_s = -\rho \cdot dA \cdot ds \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

Using the geometric relation $dz = ds \cdot \sin\theta$:

$$W_s = -\rho \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot dz$$

(The negative sign indicates that gravity acts *opposite* to the direction of increasing $z$ when flow is upward.)

## 3.3 Applying Newton's Second Law

For steady flow along a streamline, the acceleration of the fluid element is:

$$a_s = V \frac{dV}{ds}$$

Applying $F_{net} = m \cdot a_s$ along the streamline:

$$-dp \cdot dA - \rho \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot dz = (\rho \cdot dA \cdot ds) \cdot V \frac{dV}{ds}$$

## 3.4 Simplification

Divide the entire equation by $\rho \cdot dA$:

$$-\frac{dp}{\rho} - g \cdot dz = ds \cdot V \frac{dV}{ds}$$

Recognize that $ds \cdot \dfrac{dV}{ds} = dV$:

$$-\frac{dp}{\rho} - g \cdot dz = V \, dV$$

Rearranging (multiplying by $-1$):

$$\boxed{\frac{dp}{\rho} + g \, dz + V \, dV = 0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Euler's Equation of Motion along a Streamline** — the final integrated form requested in KTU 2024 derivations.

## 3.5 Extension to Bernoulli's Equation (for Incompressible Flow)

For an **incompressible fluid** ($\rho = \text{constant}$), integration is straightforward:

$$\int \frac{dp}{\rho} + \int g \, dz + \int V \, dV = \text{constant}$$

$$\frac{p}{\rho} + g z + \frac{V^2}{2} = C$$

Dividing throughout by $g$ gives the **head form**:

$$\frac{p}{\rho g} + z + \frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{C}{g} = H \, (\text{constant total head})$$

> [!NOTE]
> This transition is **THE most frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in PCAUT302 Module 2 — and a direct follow-up to Euler's Equation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
