# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define coefficient of discharge ($C_d$), coefficient of velocity ($C_v$), and coefficient of contraction ($C_c$) for an orifice meter. Establish the relation $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$.**

**Model Answer:**

The **coefficient of discharge ($C_d$)** is defined as the ratio of the actual discharge through the meter to the theoretical discharge calculated from the ideal Bernoulli equation:

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$

The **coefficient of velocity ($C_v$)** is defined as the ratio of the actual velocity of the fluid jet at the vena contracta to the theoretical velocity:

$$C_v = \frac{V_{act}}{V_{th}}$$

The **coefficient of contraction ($C_c$)** is defined as the ratio of the area of the contracted jet (vena contracta) to the area of the orifice:

$$C_c = \frac{A_{c}}{A_2}$$

**Derivation of the relation:**

$$Q_{act} = A_c \cdot V_{act} = (C_c A_2) \cdot (C_v V_{th}) = C_c C_v A_2 V_{th} = C_c C_v Q_{th}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = C_c C_v \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{C_d = C_c C_v}$$

This is the **master identity** for all orifice-type meters. **[3 Marks]**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A Pitot tube is used to measure the velocity of water in a pipe. The stagnation head is $150 \text{ mm}$ of water. Calculate the velocity at the point where the Pitot tube is placed. Take $C_v = 1.0$.**

**Model Answer:**

Given: $h = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$, $C_v = 1.0$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

For a Pitot tube, the velocity is given by:

$$V = C_v \sqrt{2gh}$$

Substituting:

$$V = 1.0 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.15}$$

$$V = \sqrt{2.943} = 1.715 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 1.72 \text{ m/s}}$$

**[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the discharge equation for a Venturimeter, clearly stating all assumptions. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A horizontal Venturimeter with inlet diameter $15 \text{ cm}$ and throat diameter $7.5 \text{ cm}$ is used to measure the flow of water. The differential mercury manometer shows a deflection of $20 \text{ cm}$. If the coefficient of discharge is $0.98$, determine: (i) the discharge through the meter, and (ii) the head loss in the diverging cone. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution for B-A (a):**

**Assumptions:**

1. Fluid is **incompressible** (constant density).
2. Flow is **steady** (does not change with time).
3. Flow is **irrotational** along a streamline.
4. Fluid is **inviscid** (no viscous losses) — to be later corrected by $C_d$.
5. Pipe is **horizontal** at the section of measurement.
6. No **external work** is done on or by the fluid between the two sections.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Apply the **continuity equation** between section 1 (inlet) and section 2 (throat):

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2$$

**Step 2:** Apply **Bernoulli's equation** between the same two sections (horizontal, so $z_1 = z_2$):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to get the piezometric head difference $h$:

$$h = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $V_1 = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} V_2$:

$$h = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{A_2}{A_1} \right)^2 \right]$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $V_2$ (throat velocity):

$$V_2 = \frac{A_1}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 6:** Multiply by throat area to get theoretical discharge:

$$Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 7:** Introduce $C_d$ for actual discharge:

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2gh}}$$

**[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution for B-A (b):**

Given: $d_1 = 15 \text{ cm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$, $d_2 = 7.5 \text{ cm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$, $h_{Hg} = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$, $C_d = 0.98$, $S_{Hg} = 13.6$.

**Step 1: Compute the equivalent head of water**

$$h = h_{Hg} \left( \frac{S_{Hg}}{S_w} - 1 \right) = 0.20 \times (13.6 - 1) = 0.20 \times 12.6 = 2.52 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 2: Compute areas**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^2 = 0.004418 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute the denominator**

$$A_1^2 = (0.01767)^2 = 3.123 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^4$$

$$A_2^2 = (0.004418)^2 = 1.952 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4$$

$$A_1^2 - A_2^2 = 2.928 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^4$$

$$\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2} = 0.01711 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Compute $\sqrt{2gh}$**

$$\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.52} = \sqrt{49.44} = 7.031 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5: Compute theoretical discharge**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2gh} = \frac{0.01767 \times 0.004418}{0.01711} \times 7.031$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{7.807 \times 10^{-5}}{0.01711} \times 7.031 = 4.563 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.031 = 0.03208 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 6: Compute actual discharge**

$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th} = 0.98 \times 0.03208 = 0.03144 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} \approx 31.44 \text{ litres/second}}$$

**Step 7: Compute head loss in diverging cone**

The head loss in the diverging cone of a Venturimeter is given by the standard formula:

$$H_L = \frac{0.08 h}{(1 - m^2)^2}$$

where $m = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$.

Compute $m$:

$$m = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{0.004418}{0.01767} = 0.25$$

$$m^2 = 0.0625$$

$$1 - m^2 = 0.9375$$

$$(1 - m^2)^2 = 0.8789$$

$$H_L = \frac{0.08 \times 2.52}{0.8789} = \frac{0.2016}{0.8789} = 0.2294 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{H_L \approx 0.229 \text{ m of water}}$$

**[7 Marks]**

---

### **Question B-B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a Pitot tube. Derive an expression for the velocity of flow using a Pitot tube. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An orifice meter with a $10 \text{ cm}$ diameter orifice is fitted in a $25 \text{ cm}$ diameter pipe carrying oil of specific gravity $0.85$. The differential mercury manometer reads $20 \text{ cm}$. If the coefficient of discharge is $0.62$, calculate the flow rate in litres per second. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution for B-B (a):**

**Working Principle:**

A Pitot tube is a small bent tube inserted into a flowing fluid with its open end facing **directly upstream**. As the fluid approaches the mouth of the tube, it is **gradually brought to rest** at the tip, converting its kinetic energy entirely into pressure energy. This causes the fluid to **rise up the tube** to a height corresponding to the **stagnation pressure**.

At the same time, a **static pressure tap** is provided on the side wall of the pipe (where the velocity is undisturbed) to measure the static pressure of the fluid.

The **difference between the stagnation pressure and the static pressure** gives the **velocity head**, from which the flow velocity is calculated.

**Sketch (text representation):**

```
        Flow direction →
            |
            |  ⌐ ← Static pressure tap
            |  |   (on side wall)
            |  |
   ───────────────────────  Pipe wall
            |
            |   Pitot tube opening
            |   ↓ (mouth faces flow)
   |‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|
   |           |
   |   Fluid   |  ← Fluid rises to stagnation height
   |           |
   ───────────────────────
```

**Derivation:**

Let point 1 be in the undisturbed free stream and point 2 be the stagnation point at the Pitot tube mouth.

**Apply Bernoulli's Equation:**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

**Boundary conditions:**
- $V_1 = V$ (free-stream velocity)
- $V_2 = 0$ (stagnation)
- $z_1 \approx z_2$ for small Pitot tubes

Substituting:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} = h$$

where $h$ is the **stagnation head** (rise of fluid in the Pitot tube above the static level).

Therefore:

$$\boxed{V = \sqrt{2gh}}$$

For practical applications, including the coefficient of velocity:

$$V = C_v \sqrt{2gh}$$

**[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution for B-B (b):**

Given: $d_2 = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$, $d_1 = 25 \text{ cm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$, $S_{oil} = 0.85$, $h_{Hg} = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$, $C_d = 0.62$, $S_{Hg} = 13.6$.

**Step 1: Compute the equivalent head of oil**

Since the flowing fluid is oil (not water), we must convert the mercury deflection to head of oil:

$$h = h_{Hg} \left( \frac{S_{Hg}}{S_{oil}} - 1 \right) = 0.20 \times \left( \frac{13.6}{0.85} - 1 \right)$$

$$h = 0.20 \times (16.0 - 1) = 0.20 \times 15.0 = 3.00 \text{ m of oil}$$

**Step 2: Compute areas**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.25)^2 = 0.04909 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.10)^2 = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute the denominator**

$$A_1^2 = (0.04909)^2 = 2.410 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^4$$

$$A_2^2 = (0.007854)^2 = 6.169 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4$$

$$A_1^2 - A_2^2 = 2.348 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^4$$

$$\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2} = 0.04846 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Compute $\sqrt{2gh}$**

$$\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 3.00} = \sqrt{58.86} = 7.672 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5: Compute theoretical discharge**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2gh} = \frac{0.04909 \times 0.007854}{0.04846} \times 7.672$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{3.856 \times 10^{-4}}{0.04846} \times 7.672 = 7.957 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.672 = 0.06105 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 6: Compute actual discharge**

$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th} = 0.62 \times 0.06105 = 0.03785 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to litres per second:

$$Q_{act} = 0.03785 \times 1000 = 37.85 \text{ L/s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} \approx 37.85 \text{ litres/second}}$$

**[7 Marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Critical Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Manometer fluid vs. flowing fluid confusion:** When the flowing fluid is **oil, petrol, or any liquid other than water**, you **MUST** divide $S_{Hg}$ by the specific gravity of the flowing fluid. Writing $h = h_{Hg}(S_{Hg}/S_w - 1)$ when oil is flowing costs **2 marks instantly**.
> 2. **Diameter vs. area:** Many students mistakenly use diameters $d_1, d_2$ directly in the discharge formula. The formula uses **areas $A_1, A_2$**, so always convert $d \to A$ using $A = \pi d^2 / 4$ first.
> 3. **Head loss direction confusion:** Head loss in a Venturimeter is always computed as $H_L = 0.08h / (1-m^2)^2$, where $m = A_2/A_1$. Students often swap $A_1$ and $A_2$, producing nonsensical answers. Remember: **$A_2$ is always the throat (smaller) area**.
> 4. **Pitot tube gives centerline velocity:** Students report Pitot tube velocity as the **average** velocity, losing 2 marks. The Pitot tube measures **point velocity**, which equals centerline (maximum) velocity. Use a traverse for average.
> 5. **Units consistency:** Always convert mm → m, cm → m before substituting into formulas. Carrying $h_{Hg}$ in cm while $g$ is in m/s² leads to wrong answers.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

✅ **Governing Equations**
- Continuity: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ (incompressible, steady flow)
- Bernoulli: $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$ (steady, incompressible, inviscid, along streamline)

✅ **Universal Discharge Formula** (Venturimeter & Orificemeter):
$$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$$

✅ **Master Coefficient Identity:**
$$C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$$

✅ **Manometer Conversion:**
$$h = h_{Hg} \left( \frac{S_{Hg}}{S_{fluid}} - 1 \right)$$
where $S_{fluid}$ is the specific gravity of the **flowing fluid**, not water (unless water is flowing).

✅ **Pitot Tube Velocity:**
$$V = C_v \sqrt{2gh}$$

✅ **Rotameter Discharge:**
$$Q = C_d \cdot A_f \sqrt{2g \dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho_f} h}$$
where $\Delta \rho = \rho_{float} - \rho_{fluid}$.

✅ **Typical $C_d$ Values:**
- Venturimeter: $0.95$ to $0.99$
- Orificemeter: $0.60$ to $0.65$
- Pitot-static tube: $C_v \approx 0.98$
- Rotameter: $0.95$ to $0.98$

✅ **Venturimeter Head Loss:**
$$H_L = \frac{0.08 h}{(1 - m^2)^2}, \quad m = \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

✅ **Hierarchy of Head Loss (from lowest to highest):**
$$\text{Pitot tube} < \text{Venturimeter} < \text{Rotameter} < \text{Orificemeter}$$

✅ **Key Engineering Insights:**
- Venturimeters are preferred in **large pipelines** due to **low head loss** and high $C_d$.
- Orificemeters are preferred for **cost-sensitive** applications where higher head loss is acceptable.
- Pitot tubes are **non-intrusive** in velocity — they don't obstruct the flow significantly.
- The **diverging cone** of a Venturimeter is intentionally made longer than the converging cone to **gradually recover pressure** and minimize separation losses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** Always (1) state the **assumptions** at the start of every derivation question, (2) clearly distinguish between $C_d$, $C_c$, and $C_v$ with definitions, (3) draw or describe a **neat sketch** of the device in descriptive questions, and (4) in numerical problems, **convert all units to SI before substitution**, and present the final answer with appropriate units in a **boxed format**. These four habits alone can lift your score by **8 to 12 marks** in a typical 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Rate Measurements: Venturi and Orifice Meters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flow Rate Measurements: Venturi and Orifice Meters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Venturi Meter — Formal Definition

A **Venturi meter** is a differential pressure type flow measurement device used to measure the volumetric or mass flow rate of a fluid flowing through a closed conduit (pipe). It consists of a converging conical section that gradually reduces the cross-sectional area to a minimum at the **throat**, followed by a gradually diverging section that restores the area back to the original pipe size. The pressure difference between the upstream section and the throat is measured using a differential manometer (typically a U-tube containing mercury for water flow or a lighter fluid for gas flow).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A venturi meter is an obstruction-type flow meter based on the principle of *Bernoulli's equation* and *continuity equation*, where a deliberate constriction in the flow path produces a measurable pressure drop that is mathematically related to the flow velocity.

## 1.2 Orifice Meter — Formal Definition

An **orifice meter** is also a differential pressure flow measuring device, but instead of a smooth gradual contraction, it uses a thin, flat, concentric plate with a sharp-edged circular hole (the **orifice**) drilled at its center. The plate is inserted perpendicular to the flow direction between two pipe flanges. The fluid is forced to contract sharply through the orifice opening, creating a **vena contracta** (the point of minimum cross-sectional area) slightly downstream of the plate. Pressure taps are provided upstream and at the vena contracta (or at specified standard locations) to record the pressure differential.

> [!NOTE]
> The **vena contracta** is the location in the flow stream just downstream of the orifice where the jet of fluid attains its minimum cross-sectional area before it begins to re-expand.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are watering your garden using a hose. When you partially cover the opening of the hose with your thumb, the water shoots out faster and travels much farther. What you are doing intuitively is:

- **Restricting** the flow area (just like a Venturi throat or an orifice)
- **Converting** pressure energy into kinetic energy
- Creating a **measurable pressure drop** (you can feel the hose "suck back" at the restriction)

A **Venturi meter** is like the hose with a **smooth, gradual nozzle** — water flows efficiently with minimal turbulence and energy loss. An **orifice meter** is like the hose with a **sharp-edged hole punched in a flat plate** — the water jet contracts abruptly, creating more turbulence, more energy loss, and a slightly less accurate but cheaper measurement.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Venturi Meter | Typical Orifice Meter |
|---|---|---|
| **Coefficient of Discharge (C_d)** | **0.95 – 0.99** | **0.60 – 0.65** |
| **Diameter Ratio (d/D)** | 0.25 – 0.75 | 0.20 – 0.80 |
| **Pressure Recovery** | Excellent (~90%) | Poor (~60%) |
| **Head Loss** | Negligible (0.1V²/2g) | Significant (≈0.5V²/2g) |
| **Cost** | High (precision cast) | Low (simple plate) |
| **Accuracy** | Very High | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **C_d (Coefficient of Discharge)** is a dimensionless number that accounts for the difference between the ideal (theoretical) discharge predicted by Bernoulli's equation and the actual discharge observed in practice. It encapsulates the effects of **viscous friction**, **turbulence**, and **vena contracta formation**.

## 1.5 Standard Pressure Tap Locations (ISO 5167)

- **Corner Taps**: Located flush with the orifice plate faces (very common, KTU standard).
- **D and D/2 Taps**: Located one pipe diameter upstream and either half a diameter downstream (D/2) or one diameter downstream (D).
- **Flange Taps**: Located 1 inch (25.4 mm) from the orifice plate faces.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Physical Principles

Both the Venturi meter and the orifice meter operate on **two fundamental fluid mechanics principles**:

### Principle 1: Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)
For an incompressible fluid flowing in a closed pipe:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q_{th}$$

where $A_1, V_1$ are the cross-sectional area and velocity at the **upstream section** (1), and $A_2, V_2$ are the area and velocity at the **throat or vena contracta** (2).

### Principle 2: Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy)
For a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

where $P$ is static pressure, $\rho$ is fluid density, $g$ is gravitational acceleration, and $z$ is the elevation head.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

**Step 1:** Apply Bernoulli's equation between the upstream section (1) and the throat (2). For a horizontally mounted meter, $z_1 = z_2$, so the elevation terms cancel.

**Step 2:** Recognize that the pressure head difference $h = \left(\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}\right)$ is what the manometer physically measures.

**Step 3:** Substitute the continuity relation $V_1 = \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right) V_2$ into Bernoulli's equation to eliminate one unknown velocity.

**Step 4:** Solve the resulting equation algebraically to obtain $V_2$ as a function of $h$ and the area ratio.

**Step 5:** Compute the **theoretical discharge** $Q_{th} = A_2 V_2$.

**Step 6:** Multiply by the **coefficient of discharge** $C_d$ to obtain the **actual discharge** $Q_{act}$. This is necessary because the ideal assumptions (inviscid, no vena contracta) are violated in practice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why C_d is required:** The ideal derivation assumes no energy loss and no flow contraction beyond the geometric throat. In reality, viscous dissipation and jet contraction (especially in orifice meters) reduce the actual flow. C_d "corrects" for these losses.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why we use a converging section?** A gradual contraction (Venturi) prevents flow separation and minimizes turbulence, preserving more of the inlet pressure head.
- **Why a diverging section after the throat?** To gradually decelerate the flow and recover a significant portion of the kinetic energy as pressure (pressure recovery), reducing permanent head loss.
- **Why does an orifice meter have lower C_d?** The sharp edge causes the jet to contract to a **vena contracta** smaller than the orifice opening, plus extensive turbulent eddies form, dissipating energy.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{th} = A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2gh}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$ | Theoretical discharge | m³/s |
| 2 | $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2gh}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$ | Actual discharge | m³/s |
| 3 | $h = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$ | Pressure head difference (for differential manometer with same fluid) | m |
| 4 | $h = x \left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)$ | Manometer reading for U-tube | m |
| 5 | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$ | Cross-sectional area of pipe or throat | m² |
| 6 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q_{act}$ | Mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 7 | $V_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2gh}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$ | Theoretical velocity at throat | m/s |
| 8 | $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ | Coefficient of discharge (product of contraction and velocity coefficients) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $H_L = \dfrac{(1 - C_d^2) V_2^2}{2g} \cdot \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$ | Head loss across meter | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For differential U-tube manometer** (mercury for water flow): the effective head $h$ is given by $h = x \left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) = 12.6x$, where $x$ is the manometer deflection in meters. The $12.6$ comes from $\left(\dfrac{13.6}{1.0} - 1\right)$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Water Treatment Plants**: Venturi meters measure chemical dosing flows and filter backwash rates.
- **Oil & Gas Pipelines**: Orifice meters are the **industry standard** for custody transfer of natural gas, crude oil, and refined products due to their simplicity and low cost.
- **HVAC Systems**: Venturi tubes measure air flow in large ventilation ducts.
- **Automotive Carburetors**: A miniature Venturi creates the pressure drop needed to draw fuel into the air stream.
- **Aircraft Pitot-Static Systems**: Modified Venturi principle for measuring airspeed.
- **Chemical Process Industries**: Flow measurement of acids, alkalis, slurries, and steam.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Venturi Meter Discharge Equation

### Setup and Assumptions

Consider a horizontal Venturi meter installed in a pipe carrying an incompressible fluid. Let:
- Section 1 = upstream of the converging cone (inlet)
- Section 2 = throat (minimum area)
- $A_1, A_2$ = cross-sectional areas at sections 1 and 2
- $V_1, V_2$ = average velocities at sections 1 and 2
- $P_1, P_2$ = static pressures at sections 1 and 2
- $z_1 = z_2$ (horizontal meter)

### Step 1 — Apply the Continuity Equation

Since the fluid is incompressible, the mass flow rate is conserved:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

Therefore, expressing $V_1$ in terms of $V_2$:

$$V_1 = \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right) V_2$$

This means the upstream velocity is smaller than the throat velocity by the area ratio factor.

### Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation (Inviscid, Steady, Incompressible)

For a horizontal venturi ($z_1 = z_2$):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

Rearranging to isolate the pressure head difference:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

Define the **piezometric head difference** measured by the manometer:

$$h = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$$

So:

$$h = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Continuity Relation

Substitute $V_1 = \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right) V_2$ into the equation:

$$h = \frac{V_2^2 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 V_2^2}{2g}$$

Factor out $V_2^2$:

$$h = \frac{V_2^2 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]}{2g}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Theoretical Throat Velocity

Multiply both sides by $2g$:

$$2gh = V_2^2 \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$$

Divide both sides by the bracket:

$$V_2^2 = \frac{2gh}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}$$

Take the square root:

$$V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$

This is the **theoretical velocity** at the throat if the flow were perfectly inviscid and there were no vena contracta formation.

### Step 5 — Compute Theoretical Volumetric Discharge

$$Q_{th} = A_2 \cdot V_2 = A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$

### Step 6 — Introduce the Coefficient of Discharge

The actual discharge is less than the theoretical due to viscous losses, turbulence, and (in orifice meters) jet contraction. We introduce $C_d$:

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = C_d \cdot A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}}$$

This is the **standard Venturi meter discharge equation** as required by KTU.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Orifice Meter Discharge Equation

The derivation of the orifice meter equation is **mathematically identical** to the Venturi meter derivation, with the following key differences in interpretation:

### Step 1 — Define the Sections

- Section 1 = upstream of the orifice plate (pipe cross-section)
- Section 2 = **vena contracta** (NOT the geometric orifice opening)

The vena contracta area $A_2$ is related to the actual orifice area $A_o$ by the **coefficient of contraction** $C_c$:

$$A_2 = C_c \cdot A_o$$

### Step 2 — Apply Continuity

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = C_c A_o V_2$$

So:

$$V_1 = C_c \left(\frac{A_o}{A_1}\right) V_2$$

### Step 3 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation (Same as Venturi)

$$h = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Solve

$$h = \frac{V_2^2 \left[1 - C_c^2 \left(\dfrac{A_o}{A_1}\right)^2\right]}{2g}$$

Solving for $V_2$:

$$V_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - C_c^2 (A_o/A_1)^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Actual Discharge

The actual discharge passes through the **orifice opening** $A_o$ at velocity $V_2$:

$$Q_{act} = C_c \cdot A_o \cdot V_2$$

Substituting:

$$Q_{act} = \frac{C_c \cdot A_o}{\sqrt{1 - C_c^2 (A_o/A_1)^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$$

### Step 6 — Group Coefficients into C_d

Define:

$$C_d = \frac{C_c}{\sqrt{1 - C_c^2 (A_o/A_1)^2}}$$

This is the **coefficient of discharge** for the orifice meter. It can also be shown that $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ where $C_v$ is the velocity coefficient accounting for viscous losses.

Therefore:

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = C_d \cdot A_o \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{1 - \left(\dfrac{C_c A_o}{A_1}\right)^2}} \approx C_d \cdot A_o \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_o}{A_1}\right)^2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The simplified form uses $A_o$ directly (not the vena contracta area) with the empirical $C_d$ already accounting for the contraction. This is the **standard working equation** used in KTU problems.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** A Venturi meter has a main pipe diameter of **200 mm** and a throat diameter of **100 mm**. The differential U-tube manometer using mercury shows a deflection of **150 mm**. The coefficient of discharge is **0.98**. Calculate the actual discharge of water through the meter. (Take $g = 9.81$ m/s²)

### Solution

**Given Data:**
- $D_1 = 200$ mm $= 0.20$ m
- $D_2 = 100$ mm $= 0.10$ m
- $x = 150$ mm $= 0.15$ m
- $C_d = 0.98$
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Calculate Cross-Sectional Areas**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.20)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.10)^2 = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Convert Manometer Reading to Equivalent Head of Water**

$$h = x \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) = 0.15 \times (13.6 - 1) = 0.15 \times 12.6 = 1.89 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Area Ratio Squared**

$$\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.007854}{0.03142}\right)^2 = (0.25)^2 = 0.0625$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Venturi Discharge Equation**

$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$

Substitute:

$$Q_{act} = 0.98 \times 0.007854 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.89}{1 - 0.0625}}$$

Calculate the numerator inside the square root:

$$2 \times 9.81 \times 1.89 = 37.0818$$

Calculate the denominator:

$$1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$

Calculate the ratio:

$$\frac{37.0818}{0.9375} = 39.5539$$

Take the square root:

$$\sqrt{39.5539} = 6.2893 \text{ m/s}$$

Multiply:

$$Q_{act} = 0.98 \times 0.007854 \times 6.2893 = 0.04840 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer with Units**

$$\boxed{Q_{act} \approx 0.0484 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 48.4 \text{ L/s}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Venturi vs. Orifice Comparison

| Parameter | Venturi Meter | Orifice Meter |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Cast with converging & diverging cones | Simple flat plate with sharp-edged hole |
| Cost | **High (5–10× orifice cost)** | **Low** |
| C_d Range | 0.95 – 0.99 | 0.60 – 0.65 |
| Head Loss | Low (~10% of $\Delta h$) | High (~50% of $\Delta h$) |
| Pressure Recovery | ~90% | ~60% |
| Maintenance | Self-cleaning (smooth) | Prone to fouling, edge erosion |
| Accuracy | ±0.5% to ±1% | ±1% to ±2% |
| Best For | Permanent installations, large flows | Custody transfer, low budgets |
| Discharge Equation | Identical form | Identical form |
| Reynolds Number Sensitivity | Low | Higher |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Venturi Meter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Inlet Pipe Section 1] -->|P1, V1, A1| B1[Converging Cone]
    B1 -->|Acceleration| C1[Throat Section 2]
    C1 -->|P2, V2, A2, min area| D1[Diverging Cone]
    D1 -->|Pressure Recovery| E1[Outlet Pipe Section]
    F1[Differential Manometer Inlet Tap 1] -.->|P1| A1
    G1[Differential Manometer Throat Tap 2] -.->|P2| C1
    H1[U-Tube Manometer] --- F1
    H1 --- G1
    H1 -->|Reading x| I1[Convert to Head h]
    I1 -->|Q = Cd A2 sqrt 2gh by 1 minus A2 over A1 squared| J1[Compute Actual Discharge]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Orifice Meter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Inlet Pipe Section 1] -->|P1, V1, A1| B2[Sharp Edged Orifice Plate]
    B2 -->|Jet Contraction| C2[Vena Contracta Section 2]
    C2 -->|P2, V2, A2| D2[Re-expanding Jet]
    D2 -->|Turbulent Dissipation| E2[Outlet Pipe]
    F2[Upstream Pressure Tap] -.->|P1| A2
    G2[Downstream Pressure Tap at Vena Contracta] -.->|P2| C2
    H2[U-Tube Differential Manometer] --- F2
    H2 --- G2
    H2 -->|Reading x| I2[Convert to Head h]
    I2 -->|Q equals Cd times A2 times sqrt of 2gh over 1 minus A2 over A1 squared| J2[Compute Actual Discharge]
```

## 4.3 Component-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UpstreamFlow [Upstream Pipe Region]
        U1[Pipe Inlet]
        U2[Straight Length - 10D Run]
        U3[Pressure Tap Point 1]
    end
    subgraph MeterBody [Metering Element]
        M1[Converging Section OR Orifice Plate]
        M2[Throat OR Vena Contracta]
        M3[Diverging Section optional]
    end
    subgraph DownstreamFlow [Downstream Pipe Region]
        D3[Pressure Tap Point 2]
        D4[Straight Length - 5D Run]
        D5[Pipe Outlet]
    end
    subgraph ManometerAssembly [Differential Manometer]
        MN1[Left Limb connected to Tap 1]
        MN2[Right Limb connected to Tap 2]
        MN3[Mercury or Heavy Fluid Interface]
        MN4[Scale to read deflection x]
    end
    U1 --> U2 --> U3
    U3 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> D5
    U3 -. Pressure Line .-> MN1
    D3 -. Pressure Line .-> MN2
    MN1 --> MN3
    MN2 --> MN3
    MN3 --> MN4
```

## 4.4 Pressure–Velocity Variation Along the Flow Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Section 1: High Pressure, Low Velocity] -->|Converging| P2[Throat: Low Pressure, High Velocity]
    P2 -->|Diverging| P3[Section 3: Pressure Partially Recovered, Velocity Decreased]
    P4[Section 1: Area A1 large] --> P5[Throat: Area A2 small] --> P6[Section 3: Area A3 equals A1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for the Diagrams:** The flow enters from the left at section 1, accelerates through the constriction (Venturi throat or orifice vena contracta) at section 2 where pressure drops to its minimum, and then either smoothly recovers (Venturi) or turbulently dissipates (orifice) as it exits on the right at section 3.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Coefficient of Discharge (C_d) for a Venturi meter. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The **coefficient of discharge (C_d)** of a Venturi meter is defined as the ratio of the **actual discharge** of fluid flowing through the meter to the **theoretical discharge** predicted by the ideal application of Bernoulli's equation and the continuity equation.

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$

It is a **dimensionless** number, always less than unity, and accounts for the combined effects of:
1. **Viscous friction losses** along the converging and diverging sections.
2. **Turbulence and eddy formation** in the flow.
3. **Minor deviations** from the ideal streamline flow assumed in Bernoulli's equation.

For a well-designed Venturi meter, $C_d$ typically ranges from **0.95 to 0.99**, indicating very high efficiency with minimal losses.

### Question 2: What is Vena Contracta? Why is it significant in orifice meter design? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

**Vena Contracta** is the location in the flow stream, just downstream of an orifice plate, where the contracting fluid jet attains its **minimum cross-sectional area** before it begins to expand again. The area at the vena contracta is always **less than** the geometric area of the orifice opening because the streamlines cannot make an abrupt 90° turn at the sharp edge; they continue to converge for a short distance due to inertia.

**Significance in Orifice Meter Design:**
- The **maximum velocity** and **minimum pressure** of the flow occur at the vena contracta, making it the ideal location for the downstream pressure tap.
- The **coefficient of contraction** $C_c$ is defined as $C_c = \dfrac{A_{vena\ contracta}}{A_{orifice}}$.
- The discharge coefficient $C_d$ for the orifice meter is fundamentally the product $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$, where $C_v$ is the velocity coefficient.
- The **extent of vena contracta** depends on the diameter ratio $d/D$ and the Reynolds number.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Question A (a):** Derive the expression for the discharge through a Venturi meter. State clearly the assumptions made. **[7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Assumptions:**
1. The fluid is **incompressible** and has **constant density** $\rho$.
2. The flow is **steady, uniform, and irrotational** along a streamline.
3. The meter is installed **horizontally** (or the elevation difference is negligible).
4. There are **no energy losses** between sections 1 and 2 (ideal flow).
5. The pressure taps are located at the **inlet section** and the **throat**.

**(ii) Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Apply the Continuity Equation** between section 1 (inlet) and section 2 (throat):

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right) V_2$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation** assuming horizontal flow ($z_1 = z_2$):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} = h$$

**Step 3 — Substitute** $V_1 = \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right) V_2$:

$$h = \frac{V_2^2 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 V_2^2}{2g} = \frac{V_2^2 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]}{2g}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_2$:**

$$V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical Discharge:**

$$Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Actual Discharge** (introducing $C_d$):

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = C_d \cdot A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}}$$

---

**Question A (b):** A Venturi meter is fitted in a pipe of **150 mm** diameter. The throat diameter is **75 mm**. The differential mercury manometer reads a deflection of **200 mm**. If $C_d = 0.98$, find: (i) the discharge of water, and (ii) the head loss across the meter. **[7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 0.150$ m, $D_2 = 0.075$ m
- $x = 0.200$ m (manometer reading)
- $C_d = 0.98$, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $\rho_{Hg}/\rho_w = 13.6$

**Step 1 — Calculate the Areas**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.150)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = 0.004418 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Equivalent Head of Water**

$$h = x\left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) = 0.200 \times 12.6 = 2.52 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Area Ratio Squared**

$$\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.004418}{0.01767}\right)^2 = (0.25)^2 = 0.0625$$

**Step 4 — Part (i): Compute Discharge**

$$Q_{act} = 0.98 \times 0.004418 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.52}{1 - 0.0625}}$$

$$Q_{act} = 0.98 \times 0.004418 \times \sqrt{\frac{49.4424}{0.9375}} = 0.98 \times 0.004418 \times \sqrt{52.7386}$$

$$Q_{act} = 0.98 \times 0.004418 \times 7.262 = \boxed{0.03144 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 31.44 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Step 5 — Part (ii): Head Loss**

The head loss formula is:

$$H_L = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} \left[1 - C_d^2 \left(1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right)\right] \cdot \left(\frac{1}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}\right) \cdot \left(1 - (A_2/A_1)^2\right)$$

A more direct approach using $h$:

$$H_L = h \left(1 - C_d^2\right) \cdot \frac{1}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2} \cdot \left[1 - (A_2/A_1)^2\right]$$

Simplifying the standard formula:

$$H_L = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} - \frac{V_2'^2}{2g} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} (1 - C_v^2)$$

Using the simplified form:

$$H_L = h \cdot \frac{(1 - C_d^2)}{C_d^2} \cdot \frac{1 - C_d^2 (A_2/A_1)^2}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$H_L = 2.52 \times \frac{1 - 0.98^2}{0.98^2} \times \frac{1 - 0.98^2 \times 0.0625}{1 - 0.0625}$$

$$H_L = 2.52 \times \frac{1 - 0.9604}{0.9604} \times \frac{1 - 0.06003}{0.9375}$$

$$H_L = 2.52 \times \frac{0.0396}{0.9604} \times \frac{0.93997}{0.9375}$$

$$H_L = 2.52 \times 0.04123 \times 1.00264 = 0.1042 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{H_L \approx 0.104 \text{ m of water} = 10.4 \text{ cm}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

**Question B (a):** Explain the working principle of an orifice meter with a neat sketch. Derive the equation for the discharge through it. **[7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**

An orifice meter consists of a thin flat plate (typically stainless steel) with a **sharp-edged circular hole** precisely drilled at its center. The plate is clamped between two pipe flanges with the hole concentric with the pipe.

When fluid flows through the pipe and encounters the orifice plate:
1. The fluid **contracts** sharply to form a jet of minimum area known as the **vena contracta**, located a short distance (≈ 0.5 × orifice diameter) downstream of the plate.
2. At the vena contracta, the **velocity is maximum** and the **pressure is minimum**.
3. **Pressure taps** are provided upstream and at (or near) the vena contracta.
4. The **pressure drop** between these two taps is measured using a differential manometer.
5. This pressure drop is **mathematically related to the flow rate** via Bernoulli's equation.

**Derivation:**

Let section 1 = upstream of the plate (pipe area $A_1$), section 2 = vena contracta (area $A_2$). Let orifice opening have area $A_o$.

**Continuity:**

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \text{where} \quad A_2 = C_c A_o$$

**Bernoulli's Equation** (horizontal flow):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

$$h = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

Substituting $V_1 = C_c(A_o/A_1)V_2$:

$$h = \frac{V_2^2 \left[1 - C_c^2 (A_o/A_1)^2\right]}{2g}$$

Solving for $V_2$:

$$V_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - C_c^2 (A_o/A_1)^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$$

The **actual discharge** passes through the orifice area $A_o$ at the velocity approaching vena contracta:

$$Q_{act} = C_c A_o V_2 = \frac{C_c A_o}{\sqrt{1 - C_c^2(A_o/A_1)^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$$

Defining the **coefficient of discharge**:

$$C_d = \frac{C_c}{\sqrt{1 - C_c^2(A_o/A_1)^2}}$$

Final equation:

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = C_d \cdot A_o \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{1 - (A_o/A_1)^2}}}$$

---

**Question B (b):** An orifice meter with a **100 mm** diameter orifice is installed in a **250 mm** diameter pipe. Water is flowing through the pipe and the differential manometer shows a deflection of **400 mm** of mercury. If the coefficient of discharge is **0.62**, determine: (i) the actual flow rate in L/s, and (ii) the velocity of water at the vena contracta. **[7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Given:**
- $D_o = 100$ mm $= 0.10$ m
- $D_1 = 250$ mm $= 0.25$ m
- $x = 400$ mm $= 0.40$ m of Hg
- $C_d = 0.62$, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Calculate Areas**

$$A_o = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.25)^2 = 0.04909 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent Head of Water**

$$h = x\left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) = 0.40 \times 12.6 = 5.04 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 3 — Area Ratio Squared**

$$\left(\frac{A_o}{A_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.007854}{0.04909}\right)^2 = (0.16)^2 = 0.0256$$

**Step 4 — Part (i): Actual Discharge**

$$Q_{act} = 0.62 \times 0.007854 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.81 \times 5.04}{1 - 0.0256}}$$

$$Q_{act} = 0.62 \times 0.007854 \times \sqrt{\frac{98.8728}{0.9744}}$$

$$Q_{act} = 0.62 \times 0.007854 \times \sqrt{101.4696}$$

$$Q_{act} = 0.62 \times 0.007854 \times 10.073 = \boxed{0.04904 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 49.04 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Step 5 — Part (ii): Velocity at Vena Contracta**

First compute the theoretical velocity at the orifice:

$$V_o = \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{1 - (A_o/A_1)^2}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.81 \times 5.04}{0.9744}} = \sqrt{101.47} = 10.07 \text{ m/s}$$

The actual velocity at vena contracta $V_2$ is related to orifice velocity by $C_v$. Using $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ and the standard relation:

$$V_2 = \frac{V_o}{C_c} = \frac{10.07}{C_c}$$

From $C_d = C_c / \sqrt{1 - C_c^2(A_o/A_1)^2}$, solving for $C_c$:

$$0.62 = \frac{C_c}{\sqrt{1 - 0.0256 C_c^2}}$$

$$0.62^2 (1 - 0.0256 C_c^2) = C_c^2$$

$$0.3844 - 0.00984 C_c^2 = C_c^2$$

$$0.3844 = 1.00984 C_c^2$$

$$C_c^2 = 0.3806 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_c = 0.6170$$

Therefore:

$$V_2 = \frac{10.07}{0.6170} = 16.32 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{V_2 \approx 16.32 \text{ m/s}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $C_d$**: Many students write the theoretical discharge equation and stop, losing 2–3 marks. Always remember the actual discharge $Q = C_d \times Q_{th}$.
> 2. **Wrong manometer formula**: Students often write $h = x \cdot \frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w}$ (which equals $13.6x$) instead of the correct $h = x \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) = 12.6x$. This is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing orifice area with pipe area**: In the discharge formula, $A_2$ refers to the **throat/orifice area**, not the pipe area. $A_1$ is the **upstream pipe area**.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency**: $D$ must be in **meters** (not mm) when computing area in m² and using $g = 9.81$ m/s².
> 5. **Forgetting the square on the area ratio**: The term is $\left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2$, not $\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$. A single squaring slip costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Skipping the assumption list**: KTU examiners explicitly ask for assumptions in derivation questions. Missing this is a 1–2 mark deduction.
> 7. **Round-off errors**: Keep at least 4 significant figures during intermediate steps; only round the final answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Core Equation (Both Meters):** $Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2gh}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$

- ✅ **C_d Values:** Venturi = **0.95–0.99**; Orifice = **0.60–0.65**.

- ✅ **Manometer Formula:** $h = x\left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)$; for Hg–water, $h = 12.6x$.

- ✅ **Bernoulli + Continuity** are the two governing principles. Always state them first.

- ✅ **Vena Contracta** is unique to orifice meters; downstream tap is placed here.

- ✅ **Assumptions to Always List:**
  - Steady, incompressible, inviscid flow
  - Horizontal meter (or note elevation correction)
  - No energy loss between sections
  - Uniform velocity profile at tapping points

- ✅ **Head Loss Order:** Venturi $\ll$ Orifice. Venturi recovers ~90% pressure; Orifice recovers ~60%.

- ✅ **Typical Pressure Tap Conventions:** Corner, Flange, D and D/2 taps (per ISO 5167).

- ✅ **Mass Flow Rate Conversion:** $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ (in kg/s).

- ✅ **Typical Exam Variants:**
  - Direct discharge calculation
  - Find diameter of throat/orifice for a given discharge
  - Find manometer deflection for a known flow
  - Compare Venturi vs. Orifice head losses
  - Derivation with assumptions
  - Numerical on coefficient of contraction and velocity coefficient

- ✅ **Memory Aid:** **"B-C-D"** = **B**ernoulli + **C**ontinuity → **D**ischarge (with $C_d$).

- ✅ **For Orifice Meters:** Sharp edge → Vena Contracta → Low $C_d$ → High head loss. **For Venturi Meters:** Smooth cone → No vena contracta → High $C_d$ → Low head loss.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Notches and Weirs (Description Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Notches and Weirs (Description Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Notch

A **notch** is a *small opening* provided in one side of a tank or reservoir, generally of regular geometric shape, through which liquid (usually water) may flow. The bottom edge of the opening, over which the liquid flows, is called the **sill** or **crest** of the notch. The sheet of water flowing over the notch is termed the **nappe** or **vein**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** A notch is essentially a *measuring device* cut into the wall of a tank at the laboratory or small-scale level. The depth of water over the crest is called the **head (H)**, and this head determines the discharge passing through.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of a Weir

A **weir** is a *barrier or obstruction* placed across an open channel (stream, river, or any flowing body) over which the liquid flows. It is essentially a notch built on a larger scale, used in rivers, canals, and spillways. The top of the weir is called the **crest**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Distinction:** If the obstruction has the same width as the channel, it is classified as a **weir**. If the obstruction is narrow compared to the channel width, it is often still called a notch, but in common engineering parlance, the terms are used interchangeably with "weir" generally implying the larger structure.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

Imagine a **bathroom sink with a small rectangular cut-out on its side** — that cut-out is a *notch*. Water rises inside the sink, and as soon as it touches the bottom of the cut-out, it starts spilling out. The higher the water rises, the more water flows out. The depth of water above the bottom edge is the **head (H)**, and the small sheet of water falling out is the **nappe**.

Now scale this up: a **small dam built across a river** with a flat top edge over which water spills is a *weir*. The same principle applies — engineers measure how high the water is above the crest, and using that, they calculate how much water (discharge) is flowing in the river. This is critical in **irrigation engineering, flood forecasting, and water supply management**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the difference:** A *notch* is on the **side of a tank**; a *weir* is across a **channel/river**. Both measure flow rate using the head over the crest.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Must-Know for KTU Exams)

- **Sill / Crest:** The bottom edge of the notch or the top of the weir over which water flows.
- **Nappe / Vein:** The sheet of water flowing over the notch or weir.
- **Head (H):** The vertical distance of the liquid surface above the crest.
- **Ventilation:** The space on the downstream side of the nappe that must be open to atmospheric pressure for accurate measurement.
- **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd):** A dimensionless factor (always < 1) that accounts for energy losses.
- **Suppressed Weir:** A weir whose crest extends fully across the channel width (no end contractions).
- **Contracted Weir:** A weir whose crest does not extend fully, causing side contractions of the nappe.

## 1.5 Fundamental Purpose

Both notches and weirs serve the **flow measurement function** — by knowing the head H and applying the relevant discharge equation, engineers can determine the volumetric flow rate (Q) of water. This is a **non-intrusive, low-cost, and reliable** method, which is why it remains a cornerstone topic in Fluid Mechanics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Notches and Weirs

Notches and weirs are classified based on multiple criteria. For KTU 2024 scheme, the following classification is **high-yield** and frequently asked.

### A. Based on Shape of the Opening (Geometric Classification)

1. **Rectangular Notch / Weir**
   - Opening is rectangular in shape.
   - Length L is constant, head H varies.
   - Used for medium-to-large discharges in canals and tanks.

2. **Triangular Notch / V-Notch / V-Weir**
   - Opening is in the shape of a V (triangular).
   - Angle 2θ is constant, head H varies.
   - Preferred for **small discharges** and **laboratory measurements** because it gives a more accurate reading at low flows.

3. **Trapezoidal Notch / Weir**
   - Combination of rectangular and triangular.
   - Side slopes are 1 horizontal to 4 vertical (Cipolletti weir is a special trapezoidal weir).
   - Compensates for the end contractions of a rectangular weir by adding the triangular portions.

4. **Stepped Notch / Weir**
   - Has steps in the opening; rarely used in practice.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** Among these, *rectangular*, *triangular*, and *trapezoidal (Cipolletti)* are the most frequently asked in Part A (3-mark) questions asking for "Classify notches/weirs based on shape."

### B. Based on Crest Width (Hydraulic Classification)

1. **Sharp-Crested Weir**
   - Crest width is very small (almost a knife-edge).
   - Nappe springs clear off the crest.
   - Used where accuracy of measurement is required.

2. **Broad-Crested Weir**
   - Crest width is sufficient to allow the nappe to become parallel to the crest (critical depth forms on the crest).
   - Used in large dams and spillways.

3. **Narrow-Crested Weir**
   - Intermediate width between sharp and broad.

4. **Ogee-Shaped Weir**
   - Shaped to match the lower nappe of a sharp-crested weir.
   - Used in large dams for maximum discharge efficiency.

### C. Based on the Effect of Nappe (Discharge Condition)

1. **Free Weir:** The downstream water level is below the crest (nappe has free air underneath).
2. **Submerged (Drowned) Weir:** The downstream water level is above the crest, partially submerging the nappe.
3. **Weir with End Contractions:** Crest length is less than channel width.
4. **Weir without End Contractions (Suppressed):** Crest extends fully across the channel.

## 2.2 Why Each Type Is Used — The "Why" Behind Design Choices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Logic for Choosing Notch/Weir Type:**
> - **Small discharge (lab use):** Use **triangular (V-notch)** — greater accuracy at low H.
> - **Large discharge (canal/river):** Use **rectangular or trapezoidal** — wider opening handles more flow.
> - **Canal headworks and dams:** Use **broad-crested or ogee-shaped** — structurally robust and hydraulically efficient.
> - **Field measurement in irrigation:** Use **Cipolletti (trapezoidal) weir** — eliminates end contraction correction.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table summarizes the **standard discharge formulas** that are tested in KTU examinations. For "Description Only" topics, students must be able to **write and explain** these formulas (derivation is excluded, but formula understanding is essential).

| **Type of Notch / Weir** | **Discharge Formula (Q)** | **Variables Explained** | **Typical Cd Value** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangular Notch (without end contractions) | $Q = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$ | L = length of notch, H = head over crest, g = 9.81 m/s² | 0.62 – 0.66 |
| Rectangular Weir (with end contractions, Francis formula) | $Q = 1.84 \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}$ | Empirical formula, L and H in metres, Q in m³/s | Empirical |
| Triangular (V-Notch) Notch | $Q = \frac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$ | θ = total angle of V-notch, H = head | 0.60 – 0.62 |
| Trapezoidal (Cipolletti) Weir | $Q = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$ | Same as rectangular; side slope 1:4 (horizontal:vertical) | 0.62 – 0.66 |

> [!TIP]
> **Note on Cd (Coefficient of Discharge):** $C_d$ is dimensionless and incorporates the **coefficient of contraction (Cc)** and the **coefficient of velocity (Cv)**. The relation is $C_d = C_c \times C_v$. Typical values: $C_c \approx 0.62$ and $C_v \approx 0.98$, giving $C_d \approx 0.61$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Irrigation Engineering:** Measurement of water diverted into fields from canals.
- **Hydrology:** Stream gauging for flood prediction and water resource planning.
- **Wastewater Treatment:** Measuring flow in sewage treatment plants.
- **Hydropower Plants:** Assessing inflows to penstocks.
- **Dam Spillways:** Ogee weirs are the primary discharge control structures in major dams (e.g., Bhakra Dam, Idukki Dam in Kerala).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** This module is listed as *"Description Only."* Therefore, students are **not required to derive** the discharge equations in the university exam. However, they must be able to **describe, classify, compare, and explain** the underlying hydraulic principles, the assumptions, and the meaning of each term in the formula. This section provides the **conceptual derivation path** (descriptive logic) to help you build strong descriptive answers.

## 3.1 Descriptive Derivation of Rectangular Notch Discharge

Although the full mathematical derivation is excluded, understanding the **logical flow** is essential for writing high-scoring descriptive answers.

### Logical Steps (Descriptive Path)

1. **Consider an elementary strip** of water at a depth `h` below the free surface (or equivalently, at a height `H − h` above the crest), with a small thickness `dh` and length `L` along the crest.

2. **Theoretical velocity of the strip** (by Torricelli's Law):

$$V_{th} = \sqrt{2g \cdot h}$$

This step comes from the energy equation: the potential energy of the strip at height `h` is converted into kinetic energy as it leaves the notch.

3. **Area of the elementary strip:**

$$dA = L \cdot dh$$

4. **Theoretical discharge through the strip:**

$$dQ_{th} = V_{th} \cdot dA = L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot h^{1/2} \cdot dh$$

5. **Total theoretical discharge** (integrate from h = 0 to h = H):

$$Q_{th} = L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \int_{0}^{H} h^{1/2} \, dh$$

$$Q_{th} = L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \left[\frac{2}{3} h^{3/2}\right]_{0}^{H} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

6. **Actual discharge** = Theoretical discharge × Coefficient of discharge:

$$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU "Description Only" Answers:** State the *assumptions* used, list the *final formula*, and explain the *physical meaning* of each variable. This earns full 14 marks in a descriptive question.

## 3.2 Descriptive Derivation of Triangular (V-Notch)

### Logical Steps

1. Consider a **horizontal strip** at depth `h` from the free surface, thickness `dh`, located at height `H − h` above the vertex of the V.

2. The **width of the strip** at this location is a function of `h`. By similar triangles:

$$b = 2(H - h) \tan(\theta/2)$$

3. The **area of the strip**:

$$dA = b \cdot dh = 2(H - h) \tan(\theta/2) \cdot dh$$

4. **Theoretical discharge through the strip**:

$$dQ_{th} = \sqrt{2gh} \cdot 2(H - h) \tan(\theta/2) \cdot dh$$

5. **Total theoretical discharge** (integrate from h = 0 to h = H):

$$Q_{th} = 2 \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \int_{0}^{H} h^{1/2} (H - h) \, dh$$

$$Q_{th} = 2 \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \left[\frac{2H \cdot h^{3/2}}{3} - \frac{2 \cdot h^{5/2}}{5}\right]_{0}^{H}$$

$$Q_{th} = 2 \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \left(\frac{2H^{5/2}}{3} - \frac{2H^{5/2}}{5}\right)$$

$$Q_{th} = 2 \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2} \cdot \left(\frac{10 - 6}{15}\right)$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{8}{15} \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$$

6. **Actual discharge** with coefficient of discharge:

$$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{8}{15} \cdot \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$$

## 3.3 Assumptions Used in Notch / Weir Flow (KTU High-Yield)

For a "Description Only" question, listing the assumptions is worth **3–4 marks** in Part B. The standard assumptions are:

1. The liquid is **incompressible** (water).
2. The flow is **steady** (discharge does not change with time).
3. The velocity of approach is **negligible** (or separately accounted for).
4. The nappe is **fully ventilated** (atmospheric pressure below the nappe).
5. The crest is **horizontal** and the sides are **vertical** (or at defined angles).
6. The flow is **two-dimensional** (no side effects).
7. The discharge coefficient $C_d$ is **constant** for the operating range of heads.

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis Matrix (MUST-KNOW for KTU 2024)

This table is a **favourite question pattern** in KTU Part A.

| **Parameter** | **Rectangular Notch** | **Triangular (V-Notch)** | **Trapezoidal (Cipolletti) Weir** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shape of Opening | Rectangle (L × constant) | V-shape (2θ constant) | Trapezoid (1:4 side slope) |
| Discharge (Q) varies as | $H^{3/2}$ | $H^{5/2}$ | $H^{3/2}$ |
| Suitable Discharge Range | Medium to high | Small to very small | Medium to high |
| Accuracy at Low Flow | Poor | Excellent | Moderate |
| End Contraction Correction | Required (if not suppressed) | Not required | Not required (compensated by side slope) |
| Construction Complexity | Simple | Simple | Slightly complex |
| Common Use | Canals, tanks | Laboratory, low-flow streams | Field irrigation channels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** Triangular notches are **more accurate at low heads** because discharge varies as $H^{5/2}$ (a small change in H produces a noticeable change in Q), whereas rectangular notches have $H^{3/2}$ dependence.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Notches and Weirs

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Notches and Weirs] --> B[Based on Shape]
    A --> C[Based on Crest Width]
    A --> D[Based on Nappe Condition]
    A --> E[Based on End Contraction]

    B --> B1[Rectangular]
    B --> B2[Triangular V Notch]
    B --> B3[Trapezoidal Cipolletti]
    B --> B4[Stepped]

    C --> C1[Sharp Crested]
    C --> C2[Broad Crested]
    C --> C3[Narrow Crested]
    C --> C4[Ogee Shaped]

    D --> D1[Free Weir]
    D --> D2[Submerged Drowned Weir]

    E --> E1[Contracted Weir]
    E --> E2[Suppressed Weir]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Notch Setup

```mermaid
graph TD
    W1[Water Source Tank] --> W2[Steady Inflow Pipe]
    W2 --> W3[Still Water Reservoir]
    W3 --> W4[Notch Opening in Tank Wall]
    W4 --> W5[Crest Sill Edge]
    W5 --> W6[Free Falling Nappe]
    W6 --> W7[Collecting Channel]
    W3 --> W8[Point Gauge for Head Measurement]
    W8 --> W9[Head H above crest]
    W9 --> W10[Apply Discharge Formula]
    W10 --> W11[Calculate Volumetric Flow Rate Q]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Comparison Flow Between Sharp-Crested and Broad-Crested Weirs

```mermaid

    A[Water Flow] --> B{Type of Weir}
    B -->|Sharp Crested| C[Knife Edge Crest]
    B -->|Broad Crested| D[Wide Flat Crest]

    D --> F[Critical Depth Forms on Crest]

```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Discharge Dependency on Head

