```mermaid
    H[Head H over crest] --> R[Rectangular Q proportional to H power 1.5]
    H --> T[Triangular Q proportional to H power 2.5]
    R --> M1[Moderate sensitivity to H]
    T --> M2[High sensitivity to H at low heads]
    M1 --> P1[Used for medium to high discharge]
    M2 --> P2[Used for low and precise discharge]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define a notch and a weir. State the difference between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **notch** is a small opening of regular geometric shape provided on one side of a tank or reservoir, through which liquid flows for measurement purposes. The bottom edge is called the **sill** or **crest**, and the depth of liquid above it is the **head (H)**.

A **weir** is a barrier or obstruction constructed across an open channel (stream or river) to measure the discharge of flowing water. It is essentially a notch of larger dimensions built into a flowing stream.

| **Aspect** | **Notch** | **Weir** |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Side of a tank/reservoir | Across an open channel/river |
| Scale | Small (lab) | Large (field) |
| Purpose | Lab measurement of flow | Field measurement of river/canal discharge |
| Sheet of water | Called nappe/vein | Same |

*(1 mark for definitions, 2 marks for difference table — full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Repeated Pattern]**
**Classify notches and weirs based on (a) shape of the opening, and (b) crest width.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(a) Based on shape of the opening:**
1. Rectangular notch/weir
2. Triangular (V-notch) notch/weir
3. Trapezoidal (Cipolletti) weir
4. Stepped notch/weir

**(b) Based on crest width:**
1. Sharp-crested weir (knife-edge)
2. Broad-crested weir
3. Narrow-crested weir
4. Ogee-shaped weir

*(1 mark each section, 1 mark for any two correct examples in each — total 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students write the discharge formula but **forget to define each variable** — this loses 2 marks.
> - Listing **assumptions** is a must in any descriptive question. Skipping it = 2–3 marks lost.
> - For classification questions, students often give only one basis (e.g., shape) and miss crest width or nappe condition. **Always give at least 2–3 bases** to score full marks.
> - Do not confuse **"notch"** with **"orifice"**. A notch has a free surface; an orifice is fully submerged.
> - Use proper units (m, m³/s) in numerical problems; KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the classification of notches and weirs. Explain any three types in detail with their discharge characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a Cipolletti weir? Explain why it is preferred over a rectangular weir with end contractions. State the discharge formula. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Classification of Notches and Weirs (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Notches and weirs are hydraulic structures used to measure the discharge of liquids. A notch is a small opening in a tank wall; a weir is a similar structure built across an open channel.

**Classification (2 marks):**
The classification is based on:
1. Shape of the opening
2. Width of the crest
3. Effect of the nappe
4. End contraction

**Three Types in Detail (4 marks):**

1. **Rectangular Notch / Weir:**
   - The opening is rectangular with length L and head H.
   - Discharge: $Q = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$
   - Used for medium-to-high discharges in canals and tanks.

2. **Triangular (V-Notch):**
   - The opening is V-shaped with apex angle 2θ.
   - Discharge: $Q = \frac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$
   - Most accurate for small discharges; widely used in laboratories.

3. **Trapezoidal (Cipolletti) Weir:**
   - The opening is trapezoidal with side slopes 1 horizontal : 4 vertical.
   - Discharge: $Q = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$
   - The sloped sides compensate for the end contractions in a rectangular weir.

**Conclusion:** The choice depends on the discharge range, accuracy required, and site conditions.

**(b) Cipolletti Weir (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A **Cipolletti weir** is a trapezoidal weir in which the side slopes are fixed at **1 horizontal to 4 vertical (1:4)**. The slope is specifically designed to compensate for the loss of discharge due to end contractions in a rectangular suppressed weir.

**Why preferred over rectangular weir with end contractions (3 marks):**
1. In a rectangular weir with end contractions, the effective length of the nappe is less than the actual crest length, requiring a correction (Francis formula).
2. The Cipolletti weir's sloped sides exactly compensate for the discharge lost due to end contractions, so **no end-contraction correction is needed**.
3. This makes the discharge formula simpler: $Q = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$, where L is the crest length of the bottom (horizontal) portion.
4. It is widely used in **irrigation channels and field installations** for its simplicity and reasonable accuracy.

**Discharge Formula (2 marks):**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

where:
- $Q$ = discharge (m³/s)
- $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge (≈ 0.62)
- $L$ = crest length (m)
- $H$ = head over crest (m)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Differentiate between a sharp-crested weir and a broad-crested weir. Mention the typical applications of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a submerged (drowned) weir? Under what conditions does submergence occur, and how does it affect the discharge measurement? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Sharp-Crested vs Broad-Crested Weir (7 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Sharp-Crested Weir** | **Broad-Crested Weir** |
|---|---|---|
| Crest Shape | Knife-edge (very thin) | Flat and wide |
| Nappe Behaviour | Springs clear of the crest as a free-falling sheet | Nappe adheres to the crest, critical depth forms |
| Accuracy | Very high (used in labs) | Moderate (used in field) |
| Discharge Formula | Theoretical analysis applies directly | Empirical or based on critical depth |
| Typical Application | Laboratory flow measurement, small streams | Large dams, spillways, canal headworks |
| Construction | Delicate, easily damaged | Robust, can withstand rough water |
| Head Loss | Lower | Higher |

**Applications (1 mark):**
- **Sharp-crested:** Used in research labs, small-scale flow measurement, calibration of larger weirs.
- **Broad-crested:** Used in **major dams (Idukki, Bhakra), spillways, and irrigation barrages** where high structural strength is required.

**(b) Submerged (Drowned) Weir (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A **submerged or drowned weir** is one in which the **downstream water level is above the crest level** of the weir. The nappe is no longer free; it is partially or fully submerged below the downstream water.

**Conditions for Submergence (3 marks):**
1. When the tail water (downstream) level rises above the crest due to a downstream obstruction, a wide river, or flooding.
2. When the discharge over the weir is high enough that the downstream depth exceeds the crest height.
3. The **submergence ratio** is defined as: $\sigma = \frac{h}{H}$, where $h$ = downstream depth above crest, $H$ = upstream head above crest.
4. If $\sigma > 0$, the weir is submerged.

**Effect on Discharge Measurement (2 marks):**
1. The discharge **decreases** compared to a free weir of the same upstream head, because the downstream water resists the flow.
2. The standard formula $Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$ is **no longer valid**; a **submergence correction factor** must be applied.
3. Experimental charts and empirical correction factors (e.g., from the **Central Water and Power Research Station, Pune**) are used to compute actual discharge.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Notch** = small opening in a tank side. **Weir** = barrier across a channel/river.
- The depth of liquid above the crest is called the **head (H)**.
- The sheet of water flowing over is the **nappe / vein**.
- **Sharp-crested** weirs are accurate; **broad-crested** weirs are robust.
- **Rectangular** notch: $Q \propto H^{3/2}$.
- **Triangular (V-Notch)**: $Q \propto H^{5/2}$ — best for low flows.
- **Trapezoidal (Cipolletti)**: Side slope 1:4; compensates for end contractions; no contraction correction needed.
- **Free weir:** downstream level below crest. **Submerged weir:** downstream level above crest.
- **Suppressed weir:** crest spans full channel width (no end contraction). **Contracted weir:** crest width < channel width.
- **Coefficient of discharge** $C_d = C_c \times C_v \approx 0.61$ to $0.66$.
- **Assumptions:** Steady flow, incompressible liquid, negligible velocity of approach, fully ventilated nappe, horizontal crest, constant $C_d$.
- **Memorize formulas** for rectangular, triangular, and trapezoidal — these are tested directly.
- For **"Description Only"** questions: list **classification + assumptions + formula + applications** to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity Measurements - Pitot Tube and Pitot-Static Tube
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Velocity Measurements - Pitot Tube and Pitot-Static Tube

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Pitot Tube — Formal Definition

A **Pitot Tube** is an open-ended tube inserted into a flowing fluid stream, with its open end (mouth) pointed directly upstream (i.e., facing the direction of flow). The fluid enters the tube and rises (in liquids) or generates a pressure (in gases) corresponding to the **stagnation pressure head** of the flow at that point. By measuring the height of the liquid column inside the tube relative to a reference piezometric level, the velocity of the flow can be determined using the principle of conservation of energy (Bernoulli's Equation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Stagnation Point**: The exact point on the Pitot tube's mouth where the approaching fluid stream comes to rest, converting its entire kinetic energy into a pressure (potential) head. This is the conceptual cornerstone of all velocity measurement devices in this module.

### 1.2 Pitot-Static Tube — Formal Definition

A **Pitot-Static Tube** (also called a Prandtl Tube) is an improved, dual-opening velocity measurement instrument. It consists of two concentric tubes:
- The **inner (central) tube** has an opening facing the flow (stagnation opening) — measures **total head** (static + velocity + elevation).
- The **annular space (outer tube)** has small openings on the side, perpendicular to the flow direction — measures **static head** only.

The velocity is then computed from the **difference between the total head and static head**, which yields the **velocity head** directly.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Straw-in-a-River" Experiment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy**: Imagine you are standing on a bridge and you lower a drinking straw straight down into a fast-flowing river, with the straw pointing directly INTO the oncoming current. The water will rush INTO the straw and climb up inside it to a certain height. Now, if you rotate the straw so it points PARALLEL to the flow (sideways), the water will NOT climb as high. 
> 
> - The **first case** (straw facing flow) = the Pitot tube — it "catches" the full energy of the flow.
> - The **second case** (straw sideways) = the static tap — it only feels the ambient pressure of the water at that depth.
> 
> The **difference** in water heights between these two cases is purely due to the **speed** of the river. A faster river → bigger difference. A still pond → zero difference.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Must Memorize for KTU)

- Acceleration due to gravity, **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Density of water at standard conditions, **ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³**
- Specific weight of water, **γ = 9810 N/m³**
- For air at standard conditions, **ρ_air = 1.225 kg/m³**
- Coefficient of Pitot tube, **C_v ≈ 0.98 to 1.00** (ideally 1.0; the coefficient accounts for minor losses and turbulence)

### 1.5 Why Two Devices? The Limitation of the Simple Pitot Tube

A pure Pitot tube requires an **external reference** (the static pressure of the undisturbed flow must be known or measured separately using a piezometer placed at the same depth on the pipe wall). This setup is cumbersome and introduces errors because the static and total pressure measurements occur at different points. The **Pitot-Static Tube solves this** by capturing both pressures at the SAME location, dramatically increasing accuracy and convenience.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. The fluid stream, moving with velocity **V**, approaches the mouth of the Pitot tube.
2. The fluid is **gradually decelerated** (isentropically, for ideal flow) until it reaches the **stagnation point** where velocity becomes **zero**.
3. By **Bernoulli's Equation** (conservation of energy for a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline), the lost kinetic energy must appear as a **gain in pressure head**.
4. This pressure rise is called the **stagnation pressure** or **total head**.
5. The stagnation head equals **sum** of the static head (ambient pressure) and the velocity head (V²/2g).
6. By subtracting the static head, the **velocity head alone** is isolated, and the velocity V is calculated.

### 2.2 The Three "Heads" Concept

| Head Type | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Head (Pressure Head)** | $h_s = \dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | Pressure energy of the fluid at rest at that point |
| **Velocity Head (Kinetic Head)** | $h_v = \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Kinetic energy per unit weight of fluid |
| **Stagnation / Total / Piezo Head** | $h_0 = h_s + h_v$ | Total mechanical energy per unit weight |
| **Elevation Head** | $h_z = z$ | Potential energy per unit weight above datum |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** for KTU 2024 Scheme Board Examinations on this topic. All derived problems in the exam will stem from these core equations.

| # | Device / Concept | Governing Formula | Variables & Units | Conditions of Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ideal Pitot Tube (velocity from stagnation head alone)** | $V = \sqrt{2 g h}$ | V = m/s; g = 9.81 m/s²; h = m | Requires external static pressure to be known |
| 2 | **Pitot-Static Tube (velocity from differential head)** | $V = \sqrt{2 g \, h_d}$ | h_d = (h_stagnation − h_static) in m | h_d is the piezometric difference between two tubes |
| 3 | **Pitot Tube with Coefficient C_v** | $V = C_v \sqrt{2 g \, h_d}$ | C_v = dimensionless (0.98–1.00) | Accounts for real-fluid non-idealities |
| 4 | **Dynamic Pressure (velocity head in pressure units)** | $P_d = \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | P_d = Pa (N/m²); ρ = kg/m³ | Forms the basis of all velocity probes |
| 5 | **Stagnation Pressure (Total Pressure)** | $P_0 = P_s + \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | P_0, P_s in Pa | For incompressible, isentropic flow |
| 6 | **Differential Manometer Equation (U-tube)** | $h_d = \left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right) \cdot y$ | y = manometer deflection in m; ρ_m = manometric fluid density | For inverted U-tube, sign is reversed |
| 7 | **Pitot-Static with Differential Manometer** | $V = C_v \sqrt{2 g \, y \left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)}$ | y = manometer reading in m | Combined instrument formula |
| 8 | **Discharge through Pitot Tube (for full pipe flow)** | $Q = A \cdot C_v \sqrt{2 g h_d}$ | A = pipe cross-section in m² | Used in pipeline flow surveys |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical Pipe Symbol Rule for Markdown Tables**: All absolute value or vertical bars in the formulas above have been explicitly written out (e.g., "V = √(2gh)" instead of "V = √|2gh|") to preserve the KTU markdown table rendering integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Engineering**: Pitot-static tubes are the **primary airspeed sensors** on commercial and military aircraft. They feed the Air Data Computer (ADC) which displays Indicated Airspeed (IAS) to pilots.
- **Hydrology & River Gauging**: Used in stream gauging stations to measure river velocity profiles.
- **Wind Tunnels**: To measure air velocity at specific test sections.
- **Pipeline Flow Surveys**: Used to estimate discharge in existing pipelines without interrupting service.
- **HVAC Systems**: For measuring airflow in ducts and ventilation systems.
- **Automotive Industry**: In race cars and high-performance vehicles, Pitot tubes help measure ram-air effect for engine tuning.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Velocity from a Simple Pitot Tube (Energy Approach)

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumptions**:
> 1. Steady, incompressible flow.
> 2. Inviscid (frictionless) flow along the streamline.
> 3. The Pitot tube is small enough not to disturb the upstream flow field significantly.
> 4. The fluid column rises to a height corresponding to the stagnation pressure of the flow at point (1).

**Setup**: Consider a fluid stream moving with velocity **V₁** at point (1), located sufficiently upstream of the Pitot tube mouth. At point (2), exactly at the stagnation point on the mouth of the Pitot tube, the velocity **V₂ = 0**. Both points lie on the same horizontal streamline, so z₁ = z₂.

**Step 1**: Write Bernoulli's Equation between point (1) and point (2):

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2
$$

**Step 2**: Apply the boundary conditions. At the stagnation point, V₂ = 0:

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + 0 + z_2
$$

**Step 3**: Since both points are at the same elevation (same horizontal streamline), z₁ = z₂. These terms cancel:

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g}
$$

**Step 4**: Rearrange to isolate the velocity term on one side:

$$
\frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} - \frac{P_1}{\rho g}
$$

**Step 5**: Define h = (P₂ − P₁) / (ρg) as the piezometric head difference, which is the height the fluid rises inside the Pitot tube above the static piezometric level at point (1):

$$
\frac{V_1^2}{2g} = h
$$

**Step 6**: Solve for V₁:

$$
V_1^2 = 2 g h
$$

$$
V_1 = \sqrt{2 g h}
$$

**Result (Boxed)**:

$$
\boxed{V = \sqrt{2 g h}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "h"?** In the case of a simple Pitot tube placed in an open channel or open pipe, h is the **height the liquid column rises inside the Pitot tube above the free surface** (or above the static piezometric level of the undisturbed flow). This rise equals the velocity head V²/(2g), which is the energy that was "trapped" by the stagnation phenomenon.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Velocity from a Pitot-Static Tube (Differential Head)

**Setup**: The Pitot-static tube has two openings. The stagnation tap (front) measures the total head h₀, and the static tap (side) measures the static head h_s. The differential head h_d = h₀ − h_s is observed via a differential manometer.

**Step 1**: Write Bernoulli's Equation between the upstream point (1) in the flow and the stagnation point (2):

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_0}{\rho g} + 0 + z_2
$$

Where P₀ is the stagnation (total) pressure and V at point (2) = 0.

**Step 2**: Simplify (z₁ = z₂):

$$
\frac{P_0}{\rho g} - \frac{P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{V_1^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 3**: Recognize that the static pressure P₁ at the same point is also tapped by the side opening of the Pitot-static tube. Let the static pressure head be h_s = P₁/(ρg), and the total head be h₀ = P₀/(ρg). The difference is:

$$
h_0 - h_s = \frac{P_0 - P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{V^2}{2g} = h_v
$$

**Step 4**: Define the differential head:

$$
h_d = h_0 - h_s
$$

**Step 5**: Equate to velocity head:

$$
h_d = \frac{V^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 6**: Solve for V:

$$
V = \sqrt{2 g h_d}
$$

**Result (Boxed)**:

$$
\boxed{V = \sqrt{2 g h_d}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: The differential head h_d isolates the velocity head directly. The static and elevation heads cancel out automatically because both measurements are taken at the **same physical location** in the flow. This is the main reason the Pitot-static tube is preferred over a simple Pitot tube in practical engineering.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Pitot-Static Tube with Inverted U-Tube Differential Manometer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip**: KTU frequently asks for the **velocity equation** when a differential manometer is connected across the two taps of a Pitot-static tube. This combined formula is a high-yield derivation.

**Setup**: An inverted U-tube manometer (using a lighter fluid, e.g., oil on top of water) is connected between the stagnation and static taps. The manometric deflection is **y**, and the manometric fluid has density **ρ_m**, while the flowing fluid has density **ρ** (with ρ_m < ρ).

**Step 1**: The differential head recorded by the manometer in terms of the manometric fluid deflection is given by the standard manometer equation:

$$
h_d = y \left( \frac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 \right)
$$

Wait — let me re-derive this carefully for the **inverted U-tube** case. In an inverted U-tube, the lighter manometric fluid is on top. The pressure balance gives:

**Step 1 (Pressure Balance)**: Starting from the stagnation tap, going through the manometer to the static tap:

$$
P_{stag} - \rho g \, a - \rho_m g \, y - \rho g \, b = P_{stat}
$$

Where a and b are the vertical heights of the flowing fluid in the two limbs. Since a − b = y:

$$
P_{stag} - P_{stat} = \rho g (a - b) - \rho_m g \, y
$$

Wait, this is getting complex. Let me use a standard, clean derivation for the inverted U-tube:

**Step 1**: Let the two limbs of the inverted U-tube have manometric fluid columns at different heights. The deflection (difference in manometric fluid levels) is **y**.

**Step 2**: The standard formula for differential head recorded by an inverted U-tube manometer (where ρ_m < ρ) is:

$$
h_d = y \left( 1 - \frac{\rho_m}{\rho} \right)
$$

**Step 3**: Substitute into V = √(2g h_d):

$$
V = \sqrt{2 g \, y \left( 1 - \frac{\rho_m}{\rho} \right)}
$$

**Step 4**: Including the coefficient of the Pitot-static tube C_v:

$$
\boxed{V = C_v \sqrt{2 g \, y \left( 1 - \frac{\rho_m}{\rho} \right)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For a Regular U-tube manometer** (heavier manometric fluid, ρ_m > ρ, e.g., mercury in water), the formula is:
> $$V = C_v \sqrt{2 g \, y \left( \frac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 \right)}$$
> Memorize both versions — KTU 2024 frequently tests this distinction.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for KTU Exams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiners' Favorite Question**: "Distinguish between a Pitot tube and a Pitot-Static tube." This comparison table is a guaranteed high-score item.

| S.No. | Feature | Pitot Tube | Pitot-Static Tube |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of openings | One (stagnation only) | Two (stagnation + static) |
| 2 | Pressure measured | Total / Stagnation pressure | Both total and static (differentially) |
| 3 | External static reference | Required (separate piezometer) | Not required (built-in) |
| 4 | Accuracy | Lower (errors in static measurement) | Higher (same-point measurement) |
| 5 | Construction | Simple single bent tube | Compound (concentric) tube |
| 6 | Typical use | Open channel flows, basic flow surveys | Aircraft, pipelines, wind tunnels |
| 7 | Formula (ideal) | V = √(2gh), h = stagnation rise | V = √(2gh_d), h_d = differential head |
| 8 | Sensitivity to disturbances | High (static tap is far away) | Low (measurements co-located) |
| 9 | Practicality in field | Limited | Widely used |
| 10 | Coefficient C_v | Slightly lower (~0.95–0.98) | Slightly higher (~0.98–1.00) |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical — Standard KTU-Style Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: A Pitot-static tube is inserted in a pipe carrying water. The differential manometer (using mercury) shows a deflection of 100 mm. Calculate the velocity of flow at the centerline of the pipe. Take C_v = 0.98, ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³, ρ_mercury = 13600 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s².

**Given**:
- y = 100 mm = 0.1 m
- ρ = 1000 kg/m³
- ρ_m = 13600 kg/m³
- C_v = 0.98
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Solution**:

**Step 1**: Write the Pitot-static tube with U-tube differential manometer formula:

$$
V = C_v \sqrt{2 g \, y \left( \frac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 \right)}
$$

**Step 2**: Calculate the bracket term:

$$
\frac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 = \frac{13600}{1000} - 1 = 13.6 - 1 = 12.6
$$

**Step 3**: Compute 2g·y:

$$
2 g y = 2 \times 9.81 \times 0.1 = 1.962 \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 4**: Multiply by the bracket term:

$$
1.962 \times 12.6 = 24.7212 \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 5**: Take the square root:

$$
\sqrt{24.7212} = 4.9721 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 6**: Apply C_v:

$$
V = 0.98 \times 4.9721 = 4.8726 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Result**:

$$
\boxed{V \approx 4.87 \, \text{m/s}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pitot-Static Tube Measurement System

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Selection Rationale**: A physical cross-sectional drawing of a Pitot-Static tube (with concentric tubes, side taps, and coaxial connection to a manometer) is too geometrically intricate to render cleanly in Mermaid syntax. Instead, the following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** maps the signal/energy path from the flow stream to the velocity readout, which is the system-level understanding KTU 2024 examiners test.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Flowing Fluid Stream with Velocity V] --> B[Stagnation Tap - Front Facing]
    A --> C[Static Tap - Side Facing]
    B --> D[Total Head h0 = hs + V squared over 2g]
    C --> E[Static Head hs]
    D --> F[Differential Manometer U-Tube]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Manometric Deflection y]
    G --> H[Compute Differential Head hd]
    H --> I[Apply Bernoulli Energy Equation]
    I --> J[Velocity V equals Cv times sqrt of 2g times hd]
    J --> K[Final Velocity Output in meters per second]
    L[Manometric Fluid Density rho_m] --> F
    M[Flowing Fluid Density rho] --> F
    N[Coefficient Cv approx 0.98] --> J
    O[Gravity g equals 9.81 m per s squared] --> J
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style K fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style F fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style J fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Simple Pitot Tube Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Open Channel or Pipe with Flow V] --> A2[Pitot Tube Mouth Faces Upstream]
    A2 --> A3[Fluid Decelerates to Zero at Stagnation Point]
    A3 --> A4[Stagnation Pressure Head h equals V squared over 2g]
    A4 --> A5[Liquid Column Rises to Height h in Tube]
    A5 --> A6[External Static Piezometer Measures hs Separately]
    A6 --> A7[Apply Bernoulli Equation P1 over rho g + V1 squared over 2g equals P2 over rho g]
    A7 --> A8[Velocity V equals sqrt of 2g times h]
    A8 --> A9[Read Velocity from Single Column Height]
    style A1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style A8 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style A9 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
```

### 4.3 Functional Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SIMPLE_PITOT[Simple Pitot Tube]
        SP1[Single Stagnation Opening] --> SP2[Measures Total Head Only]
        SP2 --> SP3[Requires External Static Reference]
        SP3 --> SP4[Lower Accuracy]
    end
    subgraph PITOT_STATIC[Pitot-Static Tube]
        PS1[Stagnation Opening] --> PS2[Measures Total Head]
        PS3[Side Static Opening] --> PS4[Measures Static Head]
        PS2 --> PS5[Co-located Differential Measurement]
        PS4 --> PS5
        PS5 --> PS6[Higher Accuracy]
    end
    SIMPLE_PITOT -.->|Improved by| PITOT_STATIC
    style PITOT_STATIC fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style SIMPLE_PITOT fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
```

### 4.4 Physical Setup Schematic Description (Verbal, for KTU Diagram Questions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Expectation**: When a question says "sketch a Pitot-static tube arrangement," you must draw:
> 
> 1. A **pipe cross-section** (circular) with the flow direction arrow showing **V**.
> 2. The **Pitot-static tube** inserted vertically (or at an angle) into the pipe, with:
>    - The **inner tube mouth** facing the flow (stagnation tap) at point (1).
>    - The **outer tube side holes** perpendicular to flow (static tap) at point (1'), both at the **same axial location**.
> 3. The two tubes connected to a **differential U-tube manometer** (containing mercury) outside the pipe.
> 4. The manometer shows a **deflection y** between the two limbs.
> 5. Label all relevant points (1, 2, A, B for manometer limbs), and indicate the densities (ρ for water, ρ_m for mercury).
> 
> Marks are awarded for: **correct orientation of taps**, **labeling of the stagnation and static openings**, **showing the manometer correctly**, and **writing the governing equation beneath the diagram**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are KTU 2024 Scheme Part A questions: concise, definition-based or formula-based, requiring a direct answer of 3–4 lines. Model answers follow each question.

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] (3 Marks)**
Define a Pitot tube and state the principle on which it works.

**Model Answer**:
A Pitot tube is a simple open-ended tube inserted into a flowing fluid with its open end facing the direction of flow. It works on the principle of **conversion of kinetic energy (velocity head) into pressure head** at the stagnation point. By measuring the height to which the fluid rises in the tube, the velocity of flow can be calculated using V = √(2gh), where h is the height of the fluid column above the static piezometric level of the flow.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] (3 Marks)**
What is the difference between a Pitot tube and a Pitot-static tube?

**Model Answer**:
A Pitot tube has a **single opening** facing the flow and measures only the **stagnation (total) head**. It requires a **separate external piezometer** to measure the static head.
A Pitot-static tube has **two openings**: a front stagnation tap and side static taps. It measures **both total head and static head at the same point**, eliminating the need for an external reference. The difference (h_d) gives the velocity head directly, making it **more accurate and practical** for engineering applications such as aircraft airspeed measurement.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**: Module 2 carries questions totalling 14 marks, with internal choice. The student answers EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each question is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**Q A(a) [7 Marks]**:
With the help of a neat sketch, derive an expression for the velocity of flow at the centerline of a pipe using a **Pitot-Static Tube** connected to a **differential U-tube mercury manometer**. State all assumptions clearly.

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step)**:

**Step 1 — Assumptions (1 mark)**:
- Steady, incompressible, inviscid flow.
- Flow along a streamline from point (1) to the stagnation point (2).
- Both taps of the Pitot-static tube are at the **same point** in the flow (same elevation).
- Manometer is a U-tube with mercury (ρ_m > ρ_water).

**Step 2 — Sketch Description (1 mark)**:
- Draw a horizontal pipe with flow direction arrow.
- Show the Pitot-static tube inserted with front (stagnation) and side (static) openings at the same location.
- Connect both openings to a U-tube manometer outside the pipe, showing mercury deflection y.

**Step 3 — Bernoulli's Equation between (1) and (2) (1 mark)**:

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2
$$

At point (2) [stagnation], V₂ = 0. Also z₁ = z₂:

$$
\frac{P_2}{\rho g} - \frac{P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{V_1^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Differential Head (1 mark)**:
The difference (P₂ − P₁)/(ρg) is the **velocity head h_v** = V²/(2g). This is recorded by the differential manometer as:

$$
h_v = y \left( \frac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Final Formula (1 mark)**:

$$
V = C_v \sqrt{2 g \, y \left( \frac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 \right)}
$$

**Step 6 — Mention Coefficient (1 mark)**:
Note that in ideal conditions C_v = 1.0, but in practice C_v ≈ 0.98 to account for minor losses, non-uniform stagnation, and viscous effects.

**Step 7 — Units Check (1 mark)**:
V = √(m/s² × m) = m/s ✓

---

**Q A(b) [7 Marks]**:
A Pitot-static tube is used to measure the velocity of water flowing in a pipe. The differential mercury manometer connected across the two taps records a deflection of **120 mm**. If the coefficient of the Pitot-static tube is **0.98**, calculate the velocity of flow. Take ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³, ρ_mercury = 13600 kg/m³, and g = 9.81 m/s².

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step)**:

**Given**:
- y = 120 mm = 0.12 m
- C_v = 0.98
- ρ = 1000 kg/m³
- ρ_m = 13600 kg/m³
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 1 — Apply the combined formula (1 mark)**:

$$
V = C_v \sqrt{2 g \, y \left( \frac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 \right)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute (ρ_m/ρ) − 1 (1 mark)**:

$$
\frac{13600}{1000} - 1 = 13.6 - 1 = 12.6
$$

**Step 3 — Compute 2gy (1 mark)**:

$$
2 \times 9.81 \times 0.12 = 2.3544 \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply (1 mark)**:

$$
2.3544 \times 12.6 = 29.66544 \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Take square root (1 mark)**:

$$
\sqrt{29.66544} = 5.4466 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply C_v (1 mark)**:

$$
V = 0.98 \times 5.4466 = 5.3377 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 7 — Final Answer (1 mark)**:

$$
\boxed{V \approx 5.34 \, \text{m/s}}
$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q B(a) [7 Marks]**:
Explain the working principle of a **Pitot tube** with a neat sketch. Derive the expression for the velocity of flow in terms of the height of the liquid column. Mention its limitations.

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step)**:

**Step 1 — Definition and Working (2 marks)**:
A Pitot tube is a glass or metal tube bent at 90°, with one end (mouth) inserted into the flowing fluid stream, **pointing upstream (facing the flow)**. The other end is open to the atmosphere. As fluid approaches the mouth, it is decelerated isentropically until it comes to rest at the **stagnation point**. The kinetic energy is converted into pressure head, causing the fluid to rise inside the tube to a height **h** above the free surface of the open channel (or above the static piezometric level).

**Step 2 — Sketch (1 mark)**:
- Draw an open channel or pipe with flow.
- Show the Pitot tube bent at 90°, mouth facing the flow.
- Indicate the stagnation point and the column height h.
- Mark an external static piezometer at the same depth (since Pitot tube needs external reference).

**Step 3 — Bernoulli's Equation (2 marks)**:
Between the upstream point (1) and stagnation point (2):

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + 0 + z_2
$$

With z₁ = z₂ and rearranging:

$$
V_1 = \sqrt{2 g h}, \quad \text{where} \quad h = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g}
$$

**Step 4 — Limitations (2 marks)**:
1. **Requires external static reference** — the static pressure must be measured separately, introducing errors.
2. **Sensitive to misalignment** — even a small angle from the flow direction causes large velocity errors.
3. **Measures velocity at a single point** — not suitable for average flow velocity unless a traverse is done.
4. **Not suitable for highly turbulent or pulsating flows** — readings fluctuate significantly.

---

**Q B(b) [7 Marks]**:
A Pitot tube placed at the centerline of a pipe carrying oil (specific gravity = 0.8) shows a rise of oil column of **150 mm** above the static piezometric level. Calculate:
- (i) The velocity of oil at the centerline.
- (ii) The discharge through the pipe, if the pipe diameter is **300 mm**.

Take g = 9.81 m/s² and C_v = 1.0.

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step)**:

**Given**:
- h = 150 mm = 0.15 m
- S (oil) = 0.8 → ρ_oil = 800 kg/m³
- D = 300 mm = 0.3 m
- g = 9.81 m/s²
- C_v = 1.0

**Part (i) — Velocity (3 marks)**:

**Step 1**: Apply Pitot tube formula:

$$
V = C_v \sqrt{2 g h}
$$

**Step 2**: Compute 2gh:

$$
2 \times 9.81 \times 0.15 = 2.943 \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 3**: Take square root:

$$
V = 1.0 \times \sqrt{2.943} = 1.0 \times 1.7155 = 1.7155 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Result (i)**:

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{centerline}} \approx 1.72 \, \text{m/s}}
$$

**Part (ii) — Discharge (4 marks)**:

**Step 1**: Cross-sectional area of pipe:

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.3)^2 = 0.07069 \, \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2**: Discharge (Q = A × V_centerline, since Pitot tube measures the local point velocity; for mean velocity, a correction factor would be needed, but the problem implies local-to-discharge conversion):

$$
Q = A \times V = 0.07069 \times 1.7155 = 0.1213 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3**: Convert to litres per second for clarity:

$$
Q = 0.1213 \times 1000 = 121.3 \, \text{L/s}
$$

**Result (ii)**:

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 0.1213 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 121.3 \, \text{L/s}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Note for KTU 2024**: In real-world practice, V_centerline ≠ V_mean. The mean velocity is V_mean = V_centerline × 0.85 (approx, depending on Reynolds number and turbulence). The problem here has been simplified for the exam. Always read the question carefully to see if it asks for centerline velocity or mean velocity.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Board Exams**:
> 
> 1. **Mixing up the manometer formula sign**: For a regular U-tube (heavier manometric fluid, ρ_m > ρ), use (ρ_m/ρ − 1). For an **inverted** U-tube (lighter manometric fluid, ρ_m < ρ), use **(1 − ρ_m/ρ)**. Using the wrong sign gives a negative value under the square root, which is mathematically invalid. **2-mark penalty** for this.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the coefficient C_v**: If the problem mentions "coefficient of Pitot tube = 0.98" or any value other than 1.0, you MUST include C_v in the final formula. A full 1 mark is deducted if omitted.
> 
> 3. **Unit conversion errors**: Heights are often given in mm in the problem statement. Convert to meters BEFORE substituting into the formula. 1 cm = 0.01 m. This is a **frequent 1-mark deduction**.
> 
> 4. **Confusing ρ and ρ_m**: ρ is the **flowing fluid** density; ρ_m is the **manometric fluid** density. In a water-mercury system, ρ_water = 1000, ρ_mercury = 13600. Inverting these will give wildly incorrect results.
> 
> 5. **Not labeling the sketch**: In derivation questions, an unlabeled sketch earns only **partial marks** (typically 0.5 out of 1). Always label: stagnation point, static tap, deflection y, manometric fluid, flowing fluid.
> 
> 6. **Stating only the final formula without the derivation**: KTU examiners award 5+ marks for the **derivation steps** and only 1–2 marks for the final boxed formula. Show every algebraic transition.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Velocity Measurements**

- ✅ **Pitot Tube**: Single-opening device, measures stagnation (total) head, requires external static reference.
- ✅ **Pitot-Static Tube**: Two-opening device, measures total and static head at the **same point**, no external reference needed.
- ✅ **Stagnation Point**: The point on the device's mouth where the fluid velocity is reduced to **zero**.
- ✅ **Bernoulli's Equation** is the foundational principle for both devices — energy conservation between upstream and stagnation points.
- ✅ **Master Formula**: $V = \sqrt{2 g h_d}$ (where h_d is the differential/velocity head).
- ✅ **With Coefficient**: $V = C_v \sqrt{2 g h_d}$ (C_v typically 0.95–1.00).
- ✅ **Regular U-tube manometer formula**: $V = C_v \sqrt{2 g y \left( \frac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 \right)}$
- ✅ **Inverted U-tube manometer formula**: $V = C_v \sqrt{2 g y \left( 1 - \frac{\rho_m}{\rho} \right)}$
- ✅ **Real-world applications**: Aircraft airspeed sensors, river gauging, wind tunnels, pipeline surveys, HVAC ducts.
- ✅ **Difference between the two devices**: Accuracy, practicality, and need for external static reference — Pitot-static is superior in all three.
- ✅ **Assumption to state in every derivation**: Steady, incompressible, inviscid, irrotational flow along a streamline.
- ✅ **Always convert units** (mm → m) before substitution.
- ✅ **Standard values to memorize**: g = 9.81 m/s², ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³, ρ_mercury = 13600 kg/m³, ρ_air = 1.225 kg/m³.
- ✅ **For KTU numerical problems**: Always show the substitution step, the bracket calculation, and the final velocity with **units**.
- ✅ **Always draw a labeled sketch** in derivation questions — partial marks depend on it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reynolds Experiment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reynolds Experiment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reynolds Experiment (Osborne Reynolds, 1883):**
> A classical experimental investigation conducted to visualize and characterize the **flow regime** of a real fluid flowing through a closed conduit (pipe) by injecting a filament of colored dye, thereby determining the critical condition at which the flow transitions from **streamline (laminar)** motion to **turbulent** motion.

The experiment quantitatively establishes the dimensionless parameter now universally known as the **Reynolds Number ($Re$)**, which compares the **inertial forces** to the **viscous forces** within a flowing fluid.

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid ($\text{kg/m}^3$)
- $V$ = Mean velocity of flow ($\text{m/s}$)
- $D$ = Diameter of the pipe ($\text{m}$)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of the fluid ($\text{Ns/m}^2$)
- $\nu$ = Kinematic viscosity of the fluid ($\text{m}^2/\text{s}$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Critical Values (as per KTU accepted conventions):**
> - **Lower Critical Reynolds Number, $Re_c$ = 2300**
> - **Upper Critical Reynolds Number = 4000**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine walking through a crowded train station:
- **Laminar flow** is like a disciplined queue of passengers moving one behind the other in perfectly straight lines. Each person follows the person ahead smoothly — no mixing, no crossing paths.
- **Turbulent flow** is like the moment the gate opens and everyone rushes in different directions. Particles cross paths randomly, creating chaotic eddies and swirls.

In **Reynolds Experiment**, the colored dye acts like a "tracked passenger":
- When flow is **laminar**, the dye slides in a clean, undisturbed straight line through the pipe.
- When flow becomes **turbulent**, the dye splashes, diffuses, and mixes chaotically with the surrounding water, painting the entire cross-section.

This visual distinction is the cornerstone for identifying which mathematical model (Navier-Stokes laminar solution vs. empirical turbulent correlations) is valid for a given engineering scenario.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in engineering?**
> Friction losses, heat transfer coefficients, mixing rates, and chemical reaction efficiencies differ by orders of magnitude between laminar and turbulent regimes. A wrong assumption can lead to under-designed pipes (laminar assumed, turbulent reality → bursting pumps) or oversized, uneconomical systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The experiment operates on a simple but brilliant principle: **visualization of streamlines using a tracer dye**.

1. **Setup Assembly:** Water flows from a steady overhead tank into a horizontal transparent glass tube of uniform diameter $D$. A bell-mouth entry ensures a fully-developed, uniform velocity profile at the test section.
2. **Dye Injection:** A thin filament of a colored dye (typically **potassium permanganate solution** or **fluorescein**) is injected at the inlet through a fine needle aligned with the pipe's central axis.
3. **Flow Observation:** As water flows steadily, the experimenter observes the behavior of the dye streak while gradually increasing the flow rate using a control valve at the downstream end.
4. **Three Distinct Regimes:**
   - **Regime I — Laminar Flow:** At very low velocities, the dye forms a **sharp, straight, unbroken filament** parallel to the pipe axis. Individual fluid layers glide over each other without any cross-mixing.
   - **Regime II — Transitional Flow:** At moderate velocities, the dye filament begins to **oscillate and waver** within the pipe. Sinusoidal disturbances appear and eventually break the filament into eddies. The flow is unstable and unpredictable.
   - **Regime III — Turbulent Flow:** At high velocities, the dye **diffuses and disperses instantly**, coloring the entire cross-section of the pipe uniformly. Fluid particles follow irregular, chaotic, three-dimensional paths with intense cross-mixing.

## 2.2 Why These Regimes Exist — The Physical "How"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Engineering Insight:**
> Two competing forces govern the flow behavior:
> - **Inertial Forces** (proportional to $\rho V^2$) → tend to destabilize flow, cause mixing, and produce eddies.
> - **Viscous Forces** (proportional to $\mu V / D$) → tend to damp disturbances, maintain orderly layer motion, and resist mixing.
>
> The **Reynolds Number** is simply the dimensionless ratio of these two forces.

When $Re$ is **low** (viscous forces dominant): disturbances are damped out instantly → **laminar flow** prevails.
When $Re$ is **high** (inertial forces dominant): disturbances grow and propagate → **turbulent flow** emerges.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Parameter** | **Expression** | **Significance** | **Standard Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless flow regime indicator | Dimensionless |
| Reynolds Number (kinematic form) | $Re = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Used when $\nu$ is given | Dimensionless |
| Critical Reynolds Number | $Re_c = 2300$ | Lower limit for transition | Dimensionless |
| Critical Velocity | $V_c = \dfrac{Re_c \cdot \mu}{\rho D}$ | Velocity at transition onset | $\text{m/s}$ |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Property of fluid | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| Mean Velocity | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | From continuity | $\text{m/s}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of Water Distribution Networks:** Determines whether Darcy-Weisbach or Hagen-Poiseuille equations apply for head loss.
- **Chemical Process Industry:** Predicts mixing behavior in reactors and heat exchangers.
- **Aerospace Engineering:** Boundary layer transition on aircraft wings (uses $Re_x = \rho V x / \mu$).
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Blood flow through arteries — whether laminar (healthy) or turbulent (stenosis indicator).
- **Hydraulic Machinery:** Pump and turbine performance curves are regime-specific.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Velocity Expression

The critical velocity is defined as the mean velocity at which the Reynolds Number equals its critical value.

$$
Re_c = \frac{\rho V_c D}{\mu}
$$

Rearranging to solve for the critical velocity $V_c$:

$$
V_c = \frac{Re_c \cdot \mu}{\rho \cdot D}
$$

Since $\nu = \mu / \rho$, this can equivalently be written as:

$$
V_c = \frac{Re_c \cdot \nu}{D}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Reynolds Number in Terms of Discharge (Q)

By the continuity equation, the mean velocity in a circular pipe is:

$$
V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{Q}{\frac{\pi}{4} D^2} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}
$$

Substituting this expression for $V$ into the Reynolds Number definition:

$$
Re = \frac{\rho \cdot \left(\dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}\right) \cdot D}{\mu}
$$

Multiplying the $D$ term into the numerator:

$$
Re = \frac{4 \rho Q}{\pi \mu D}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:**
> This compact form is extremely useful in KTU problems where discharge $Q$ (in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$) is given instead of velocity $V$.

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem Statement:**
Water at $20\,^\circ\text{C}$ flows through a smooth circular pipe of diameter $50\,\text{mm}$. Given:
- Density, $\rho = 998\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- Dynamic viscosity, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Ns/m}^2$
- Mean velocity, $V = 1.5\,\text{m/s}$

Determine the flow regime.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the known quantities:**
- $D = 50\,\text{mm} = 0.05\,\text{m}$
- $V = 1.5\,\text{m/s}$
- $\rho = 998\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Ns/m}^2$

**Step 2 — Apply the Reynolds Number formula:**

$$
Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$
Re = \frac{998 \times 1.5 \times 0.05}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator and denominator separately:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 998 \times 1.5 \times 0.05 = 74.85
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} = 0.001002
$$

**Step 5 — Perform the division:**

$$
Re = \frac{74.85}{0.001002} = 74{,}700.6
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with critical Reynolds Number:**

$$
Re = 74{,}700.6 \gg Re_c = 2300
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $Re \gg 4000$, the flow is firmly **TURBULENT**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Flow Regime Identification Table

| **Reynolds Number Range** | **Flow Regime** | **Dye Behavior** | **Engineering Implication** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Re < 2000$ | Laminar (Streamline) | Straight, sharp, unbroken dye filament | Hagen-Poiseuille equation valid; minimal mixing |
| $2000 \leq Re \leq 4000$ | Transitional | Wavy, oscillating dye; intermittent breakup | Unstable; design must avoid this zone |
| $Re > 4000$ | Turbulent | Instant diffusion, fully mixed coloration | Empirical friction factor correlations required |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Reynolds Experimental Setup (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Overhead Constant Head Tank] -->|Stabilized flow| B[Bell Mouth Entry]
    B --> C[Smooth Transparent Glass Test Pipe]
    D[Dye Reservoir KMnO4 Solution] -->|Thin filament injection| C
    C --> E[Flow Control Valve at Downstream]
    E --> F[Measuring Tank with Volumetric Scale]
    C -.->|Visual Observation Zone| G[Observation - Laminar Phase]
    C -.->|Visual Observation Zone| H[Observation - Transitional Phase]
    C -.->|Visual Observation Zone| I[Observation - Turbulent Phase]
    F --> J[Calculate Discharge Q]
    J --> K[Compute Re = rho V D by mu]
    K --> L{Flow Regime Classification}
    L -->|Re under 2000| M[Laminar Flow]
    L -->|Re 2000 to 4000| N[Transitional Flow]
    L -->|Re above 4000| O[Turbulent Flow]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Force-Balance Logic Behind Reynolds Number

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inertial Force ~ rho V squared] --> C[Dimensionless Ratio]
    B[Viscous Force ~ mu V by D] --> C
    C --> D{Re = rho V D by mu}
    D -->|Re Small| E[Viscous Dominates - Laminar]
    D -->|Re Large| F[Inertial Dominates - Turbulent]
    D -->|Re at 2300| G[Transition Threshold]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description (For Examiner Reference)

The physical apparatus consists of the following in-line components from upstream to downstream:

1. **Overhead Tank** — Maintains a constant hydraulic head ensuring steady, time-independent flow.
2. **Stilling Chamber** — Dampens inlet disturbances to establish a uniform velocity profile.
3. **Bell-Mouth Entry** — Smoothly accelerates water into the test pipe without inducing artificial turbulence.
4. **Dye Injection Needle** — Inserted at the pipe entrance along the central axis to release a fine tracer filament.
5. **Transparent Test Section** — Long, straight glass pipe of length $\geq 100D$ to allow full development of the flow pattern.
6. **Control Valve** — A downstream valve used to vary the discharge rate and therefore the Reynolds number.
7. **Collecting Tank with Volumetric Gauge** — Enables measurement of discharge $Q$ by the rise in water level over a known time interval.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always draw the schematic with the dye injection point, observation zone, and control valve clearly labelled. Examiners allocate marks specifically for a correctly labelled diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Reynolds Number. State its physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
The Reynolds Number is a dimensionless quantity that represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a flowing fluid. For flow in a pipe of diameter $D$, it is expressed as:

$$
Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}
$$

**Physical Significance (1.5 Marks):**
It serves as a criterion for determining the nature of the flow. A low Reynolds Number indicates that viscous forces dominate, resulting in **laminar (streamline) flow**, while a high Reynolds Number indicates that inertial forces dominate, leading to **turbulent flow**. The critical value for pipe flow is $Re_c = 2300$ (lower) and $4000$ (upper).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the three flow regimes observed in Reynolds Experiment with their corresponding Reynolds Number ranges.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Flow Regime** | **Reynolds Number Range** |
|---|---|
| Laminar (Streamline) flow | $Re < 2000$ |
| Transitional (Critical) flow | $2000 \leq Re \leq 4000$ |
| Turbulent flow | $Re > 4000$ |

The lower critical Reynolds Number for pipe flow is **2300**, below which the flow is always laminar regardless of initial disturbances.

---

## PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **Reynolds Experiment** to demonstrate the different flow regimes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An oil of viscosity $0.9\,\text{Ns/m}^2$ and relative density $0.9$ is flowing through a circular pipe of diameter $100\,\text{mm}$. Determine the **discharge** at which the flow will be just critical (laminar-turbulent transition). Take $Re_c = 2300$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Description of Reynolds Experiment (7 Marks)

**Apparatus (2 Marks):**
The setup consists of:
- A constant-head overhead water tank
- A transparent horizontal glass test pipe of uniform diameter $D$
- A dye reservoir containing a colored tracer (potassium permanganate) connected to a fine injection needle at the pipe entry
- A downstream flow control valve to vary discharge
- A collecting tank with volumetric gauge to measure discharge

**Procedure (2 Marks):**
1. Water is allowed to flow steadily through the transparent pipe.
2. A thin filament of dye is injected at the inlet along the axis.
3. The control valve is gradually opened/closed to vary the velocity.
4. The behavior of the dye filament is visually observed.

**Observations (3 Marks):**
- **Laminar Flow ($Re < 2000$):** The dye forms a straight, sharp, unbroken colored line parallel to the pipe axis. Fluid layers move without cross-mixing.
- **Transitional Flow ($2000 \leq Re \leq 4000$):** The dye filament begins to waver and oscillate. At $Re = 2300$, it breaks into eddies and turbulent patches intermittently.
- **Turbulent Flow ($Re > 4000$):** The dye diffuses instantly across the entire pipe cross-section. Fluid particles undergo chaotic, irregular motion with intense mixing.

**Sketch (To be drawn by student):**
[Sketch showing: overhead tank → bell-mouth entry → transparent test pipe with dye injection → observation zone → control valve → collecting tank. The observation zone should show three states: straight dye, wavy dye, and fully mixed dye.]

---

#### Part (b) — Solution to Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $\mu = 0.9\,\text{Ns/m}^2$
- Specific gravity, $S = 0.9 \Rightarrow \rho = 0.9 \times 1000 = 900\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- $D = 100\,\text{mm} = 0.1\,\text{m}$
- $Re_c = 2300$ (for critical/transition condition)

**Step 1 — Recall the Reynolds Number formula:**

$$
Re_c = \frac{\rho V_c D}{\mu}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for critical velocity $V_c$:**

$$
V_c = \frac{Re_c \cdot \mu}{\rho \cdot D}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values:**

$$
V_c = \frac{2300 \times 0.9}{900 \times 0.1}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator and denominator:**

$$
V_c = \frac{2070}{90} = 23.0\,\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply continuity equation to find discharge $Q$:**

$$
Q = A \cdot V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot V_c
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.1)^2 \times 23.0
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.01 \times 23.0 = 0.1806\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 0.1806\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 180.6\,\text{litres/s}}
$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the **significance of Reynolds Number** as a dimensionless group. Derive the expression for critical velocity in terms of kinematic viscosity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Water flows through a pipe of diameter $200\,\text{mm}$ at a velocity of $3\,\text{m/s}$. Given $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, determine the **Reynolds Number** and classify the flow regime. If the temperature rises and $\nu$ decreases to $0.6 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, will the flow regime change? Justify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Significance and Derivation (7 Marks)

**Significance of Reynolds Number (4 Marks):**
The Reynolds Number is a **dimensionless parameter** that has the same numerical value in any system of units. This makes it a universal criterion for dynamic similarity between model and prototype in fluid flow problems.

It physically represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces:

$$
Re = \frac{\rho V^2 / L}{\mu V / L^2} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}
$$

Its significance includes:
1. **Flow Regime Classification:** Determines whether flow is laminar, transitional, or turbulent.
2. **Dynamic Similarity:** Two geometrically similar flows have identical behavior if their Reynolds Numbers match.
3. **Model Testing:** Enables prediction of prototype behavior from small-scale laboratory models (used in ship hull design, aircraft, dams).
4. **Pipe Sizing:** Guides whether laminar or turbulent friction factor correlations apply.

**Derivation of Critical Velocity (3 Marks):**
The critical velocity corresponds to $Re = Re_c = 2300$:

$$
Re_c = \frac{V_c \cdot D}{\nu}
$$

Solving for $V_c$:

$$
\boxed{V_c = \frac{Re_c \cdot \nu}{D}}
$$

where $\nu = \mu / \rho$ is the kinematic viscosity.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data (Initial Condition):**
- $D = 200\,\text{mm} = 0.2\,\text{m}$
- $V = 3\,\text{m/s}$
- $\nu_1 = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$

**Step 1 — Compute Initial Reynolds Number:**

$$
Re_1 = \frac{V \cdot D}{\nu_1} = \frac{3 \times 0.2}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
Re_1 = \frac{0.6}{10^{-6}} = 6 \times 10^5 = 600{,}000
$$

**Classification (Initial):**
Since $Re_1 = 600{,}000 \gg 4000$, the flow is firmly **TURBULENT**.

---

**Step 2 — Compute New Reynolds Number after temperature rise:**

Given $\nu_2 = 0.6 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$:

$$
Re_2 = \frac{V \cdot D}{\nu_2} = \frac{3 \times 0.2}{0.6 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
Re_2 = \frac{0.6}{6 \times 10^{-7}} = 1 \times 10^6 = 1{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compare and Justify:**

$$
Re_2 = 1{,}000{,}000 \gg Re_1 = 600{,}000
$$

Both values lie deep within the turbulent regime ($Re > 4000$). The flow regime **does not change** — it remains turbulent.

**Physical Interpretation:**
As temperature rises, kinematic viscosity $\nu$ decreases (water becomes "thinner"), which **increases** the Reynolds Number. Since both initial and final $Re$ values are vastly above $4000$, the regime remains turbulent regardless of temperature change.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Forgetting to convert mm to m in pipe diameter. Always carry units explicitly through every step.
> 2. **Mixing $\mu$ and $\nu$:** Students often confuse dynamic viscosity ($\mu$, in $\text{Ns/m}^2$) with kinematic viscosity ($\nu$, in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$). The formula changes accordingly.
> 3. **Forgetting Relative Density:** When "relative density" or "specific gravity" is given, multiply by $1000$ to get $\rho$.
> 4. **Critical Value Confusion:** Always state **both** lower (2300) and upper (4000) critical Reynolds Numbers in definitional questions.
> 5. **Diagram Labelling:** In descriptive answers, a labelled diagram of Reynolds apparatus fetches at least 2 marks — never omit it.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Reynolds Experiment** is the foundational experimental method to visualize and classify flow regimes by injecting a tracer dye into a transparent pipe.
- **Reynolds Number** $Re = \rho V D / \mu = V D / \nu$ is the **dimensionless ratio of inertial to viscous forces**.
- **Three flow regimes:** Laminar ($Re < 2000$), Transitional ($2000 \leq Re \leq 4000$), Turbulent ($Re > 4000$).
- **Critical Reynolds Number** $Re_c = 2300$ is the lower threshold beyond which flow instability begins.
- **Critical velocity** $V_c = Re_c \cdot \nu / D$ defines the velocity at which transition occurs.
- **Dye behavior** is the visual diagnostic: straight line (laminar), wavy/breaking (transitional), instant diffusion (turbulent).
- **Engineering relevance:** Dictates choice of friction factor equations, heat transfer correlations, pump characteristics, and design of mixing equipment.
- **Always remember:** Convert all dimensions to SI units ($\text{m, m/s, kg/m}^3, \text{Ns/m}^2$) before substituting into any formula.
- **Discharge-based Reynolds Number:** $Re = 4\rho Q / (\pi \mu D)$ — useful when velocity is not directly given.
- **Dynamic Similarity Principle:** Two flows are dynamically similar if their Reynolds Numbers are equal — cornerstone of model testing in KTU theory questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Reynolds Number (Re)** is a fundamental **dimensionless quantity** in fluid mechanics that represents the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** within a fluid flow. It is universally used to predict and categorize the nature of fluid flow — specifically, to distinguish between **laminar flow** and **turbulent flow** regimes.

Mathematically, the Reynolds Number is defined as:

$$Re = \frac{\text{Inertial Forces}}{\text{Viscous Forces}} = \frac{\rho \cdot V \cdot D}{\mu} = \frac{V \cdot D}{\nu}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (kg/m³)
- $V$ = Mean velocity of flow (m/s)
- $D$ = Characteristic length / Pipe diameter (m)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of fluid (N·s/m² or Pa·s)
- $\nu$ = Kinematic viscosity of fluid (m²/s)
- $\nu = \mu / \rho$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Reynolds Number is the *single most important* dimensionless parameter in boundary layer theory and viscous flow analysis (Module 3). Every problem on dimensional analysis, flow classification, or pipe flow transition MUST begin with the calculation of Re.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context:** The Reynolds Number was introduced by **Sir Osborne Reynolds (1883)** through his famous dye-injection experiment on water flowing through a glass pipe. He established that the transition from laminar to turbulent flow occurs at a critical Reynolds number for a given geometry.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Real-World Plain English)

Imagine you are pushing a crowd of people (representing fluid particles) through a **narrow corridor** (representing a pipe).

- If the crowd moves **slowly and orderly** (low velocity), with everyone following a strict line — this is **LAMINAR FLOW**. The internal "stickiness" (viscosity) dominates, and the orderly behavior wins. The Reynolds Number is **LOW**.

- If the crowd moves **very fast and chaotically**, with people bumping into each other, swirling, and creating random eddies — this is **TURBULENT FLOW**. The forward momentum (inertia) dominates, and chaos wins. The Reynolds Number is **HIGH**.

> **Real-World Example:** A thin stream of honey from a spoon flows smoothly and in one piece (**laminar, low Re**), while the same honey poured fast through a wide tube creates swirling, irregular patterns (**turbulent, high Re**). Water flowing slowly from a tap is laminar; water shooting from a fire hydrant at full pressure is highly turbulent.

## 1.3 Physical Significance of Reynolds Number

| Reynolds Number Regime | Physical Behavior | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Re < 2000 | **Laminar Flow** — Streamlines are smooth, parallel, and well-defined | Low friction losses, predictable flow, common in capillary tubes, lubrication systems |
| 2000 < Re < 4000 | **Critical / Transition Zone** — Flow is unstable, intermittently laminar and turbulent | Design engineers AVOID this range; calculations are non-reliable |
| Re > 4000 | **Turbulent Flow** — Chaotic, eddying, random particle motion | High momentum transfer, enhanced mixing, higher friction losses |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constant Highlight:** The critical Reynolds number for flow in a circular pipe is **Re_critical ≈ 2300** (for transition onset) and **Re ≈ 4000** (for fully turbulent). These are empirical benchmarks, NOT universal constants — they depend on geometry, surface roughness, and inlet conditions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Why Does Reynolds Number Matter?

The Reynolds Number encapsulates the **fundamental competition** between two competing physical forces inside any moving fluid:

1. **Inertial Forces** ($\rho V^2$ per unit area) — The fluid's resistance to changes in motion; the "momentum" pushing it forward chaotically.
2. **Viscous Forces** ($\mu V / D$ per unit area) — The fluid's internal "friction" or "stickiness" that damps out disturbances and tries to keep the flow orderly.

**The Core Logic Chain (KTU Board Favourite):**

- **Step 1:** When $\text{Re} \ll 1$ (very small), viscous forces DOMINATE. Fluid particles are glued to their neighbors. Disturbances are instantly damped out. → **LAMINAR**
- **Step 2:** When $\text{Re} \gg 1$ (very large), inertial forces DOMINATE. Tiny disturbances grow into chaotic eddies. → **TURBULENT**
- **Step 3:** At some critical intermediate value of Re, the flow undergoes a **bifurcation** from order to chaos. This critical value is the **transition Reynolds Number**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula / Expression | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Standard form using dynamic viscosity | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Standard form using kinematic viscosity | Most used in KTU problems |
| 3 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity definition | m²/s |
| 4 | $Re_c = 2300$ | Critical Re (lower limit) for pipe flow | Laminar → Transition |
| 5 | $Re_c = 4000$ | Critical Re (upper limit) for pipe flow | Transition → Turbulent |
| 6 | $Re_x = \dfrac{\rho V x}{\mu}$ | Local Reynolds Number (boundary layer) at distance $x$ from leading edge | Used over flat plates |
| 7 | $V_{crit} = \dfrac{Re_c \cdot \mu}{\rho D}$ | Critical velocity for transition in a pipe | m/s |
| 8 | $Re = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi D \nu}$ | Re expressed in terms of discharge $Q$ | Useful for open channel / volumetric flow |
| 9 | $Re = \dfrac{2 \rho V D}{\mu}$ (annular) | Re for flow between two parallel plates or annulus | Different characteristic length |
| 10 | $F_{inertial} \propto \rho V^2 D^2$ | Inertial force magnitude | Derivation basis |
| 11 | $F_{viscous} \propto \mu V D$ | Viscous force magnitude (from Newton's law of viscosity) | Derivation basis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked to "show that Reynolds Number is dimensionless", you MUST show the units of $\rho V D / \mu$ explicitly, substituting kg, m, s. Marks are awarded for the full unit cancellation chain, not just the final result.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Reynolds Number in Real-World Applications

- **Chemical Engineering:** Reactor design, mixing operations, heat exchanger sizing — knowing Re helps select appropriate correlations for Nusselt Number, friction factor.
- **Aerospace Engineering:** Aircraft wing design — boundary layer transition from laminar to turbulent is governed by local Re.
- **Civil Engineering:** Water supply pipe networks, sewage systems, open channel flow in rivers and spillways.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Blood flow through arteries (Re ≈ 100–2000), design of stents and artificial heart valves.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Lubrication systems, hydraulic machinery, HVAC duct design, turbine blade cooling.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Pollutant dispersion in rivers and atmosphere.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Real-World Connection:** The boundary layer theory (Module 3, Topic 2) depends ENTIRELY on Re. The concept of laminar sub-layer, transition, and turbulent boundary layer zones are all defined using critical Re thresholds. Master Re first — everything else builds on it.

## 2.4 The Three Classic Force Scales — Physical Derivation Basis

To fully grasp the Reynolds Number, we analyze three competing forces per unit volume:

$$F_{inertial} = \rho \cdot V \cdot \frac{dV}{dx} \sim \frac{\rho V^2}{L}$$

$$F_{viscous} = \mu \cdot \nabla^2 V \sim \frac{\mu V}{L^2}$$

$$F_{gravity} = \rho \cdot g \quad \text{(relevant in Froude Number, NOT Re)}$$

The ratio:

$$\frac{F_{inertial}}{F_{viscous}} = \frac{\rho V^2 / L}{\mu V / L^2} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = Re$$

This confirms the definition arises naturally from dimensional analysis of the Navier-Stokes equation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION 1: Proving Reynolds Number is Dimensionless (Dimensional Analysis Method)

**Given:**
- $\rho$ = density (kg/m³)
- $V$ = velocity (m/s)
- $D$ = pipe diameter (m)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (kg/(m·s) or N·s/m²)

**Step 1: Write the assumed functional relationship.**

We assume the inertial force $F_i$ depends on $\rho$, $V$, and $D$ (since these are the dominant parameters for inertial effects in pipe flow).

$$F_i = f(\rho, V, D)$$

By the Buckingham Pi Theorem, we propose:

$$F_i = K \cdot \rho^a \cdot V^b \cdot D^c$$

**Step 2: Write the dimensional form of each variable.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[F_i] &= M L T^{-2} \\
[\rho] &= M L^{-3} \\
[V] &= L T^{-1} \\
[D] &= L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute dimensions into the assumed equation.**

$$M L T^{-2} = (M L^{-3})^a \cdot (L T^{-1})^b \cdot (L)^c$$

**Step 4: Expand the right-hand side by distributing the exponents.**

$$M L T^{-2} = M^a L^{-3a} \cdot L^b T^{-b} \cdot L^c$$

$$M L T^{-2} = M^a \cdot L^{(-3a + b + c)} \cdot T^{-b}$$

**Step 5: Equate exponents of M, L, and T on both sides.**

For **M (mass):** $1 = a \;\Rightarrow\; a = 1$

For **T (time):** $-2 = -b \;\Rightarrow\; b = 2$

For **L (length):** $1 = -3a + b + c = -3(1) + 2 + c = -1 + c \;\Rightarrow\; c = 2$

**Step 6: Substitute the exponents back into the original equation.**

$$F_i = K \cdot \rho^1 \cdot V^2 \cdot D^2 = K \cdot \rho V^2 D^2$$

**Step 7: Similarly, derive the viscous force expression.**

Using Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = \mu \frac{dV}{dy}$. The viscous force on a fluid layer of area $A$ is $F_v = \tau \cdot A$.

By similar dimensional reasoning, the viscous force on a characteristic length scale $D$ is:

$$F_v = K' \cdot \mu V D$$

**Step 8: Form the ratio of inertial to viscous force.**

$$Re = \frac{F_i}{F_v} = \frac{K \cdot \rho V^2 D^2}{K' \cdot \mu V D} = \frac{K}{K'} \cdot \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$$

**Step 9: Define the Reynolds Number.**

For a smooth circular pipe, experimental verification (Reynolds' dye experiment) gives $K/K' = 1$:

$$\boxed{Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}}$$

**Step 10: Verify dimensionless nature explicitly.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[Re] &= \frac{[\rho][V][D]}{[\mu]} = \frac{(kg/m^3)(m/s)(m)}{(kg/(m \cdot s))} \\
&= \frac{kg \cdot m^3 / (m^3 \cdot s)}{(kg \cdot m^{-1} \cdot s^{-1})} = \frac{kg \cdot m^{-1} \cdot s^{-1}}{kg \cdot m^{-1} \cdot s^{-1}} \\
&= M^0 L^0 T^0 = \text{Dimensionless} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION 2: Critical Velocity Expression (Pipe Flow Transition)

**Objective:** Derive an expression for the velocity at which the flow transitions from laminar to turbulent in a circular pipe.

**Step 1: Start from the Reynolds Number definition.**

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$$

**Step 2: Solve algebraically for velocity V.**

$$V = \frac{Re \cdot \mu}{\rho D}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the critical Reynolds number Re_c.**

$$V_{critical} = \frac{Re_c \cdot \mu}{\rho D}$$

**Step 4: Apply the standard value of Re_c for pipe flow.**

For **lower critical** (laminar → transition): $Re_c = 2300$

$$V_{lower} = \frac{2300 \cdot \mu}{\rho D}$$

For **upper critical** (transition → turbulent): $Re_c = 4000$

$$V_{upper} = \frac{4000 \cdot \mu}{\rho D}$$

---

## 3.3 ANALYTICAL MATRIX: KTU-Type Numerical Solved Examples

### **Worked Example 1: Determine Flow Regime in a Water Pipe**

**Problem (KTU-Style 7-mark):** Water at 20°C flows through a 50 mm diameter pipe at an average velocity of 3 m/s. Given $\mu_{water} = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m² and $\rho_{water} = 998$ kg/m³, determine the flow regime.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify given data.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m} \\
V &= 3 \text{ m/s} \\
\mu &= 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·s/m}^2 \\
\rho &= 998 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Reynolds Number formula.**

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values.**

$$Re = \frac{998 \times 3 \times 0.05}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 4: Compute the numerator.**

$$\text{Numerator} = 998 \times 3 \times 0.05 = 149.7$$

**Step 5: Compute the denominator.**

$$\text{Denominator} = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} = 0.001002$$

**Step 6: Divide.**

$$Re = \frac{149.7}{0.001002} = 149401.2 \approx 1.494 \times 10^5$$

**Step 7: Compare with critical Reynolds number.**

Since $Re = 1.494 \times 10^5 \gg 4000$, the flow is **HIGHLY TURBULENT**.

---

### **Worked Example 2: Find Critical Velocity for Transition**

**Problem:** Oil of viscosity 0.1 N·s/m² and density 900 kg/m³ flows through a 25 mm diameter pipe. Find the velocity at which transition from laminar to turbulent flow occurs.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify given data.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &= 0.1 \text{ N·s/m}^2 \\
\rho &= 900 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
D &= 0.025 \text{ m} \\
Re_c &= 2300 \text{ (lower critical value)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the critical velocity formula.**

$$V_c = \frac{Re_c \cdot \mu}{\rho D}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values.**

$$V_c = \frac{2300 \times 0.1}{900 \times 0.025}$$

**Step 4: Compute the denominator.**

$$\text{Denominator} = 900 \times 0.025 = 22.5$$

**Step 5: Divide.**

$$V_c = \frac{230}{22.5} = 10.222 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6: Conclude.**

$$\boxed{V_{critical} \approx 10.22 \text{ m/s}}$$

If oil flows slower than 10.22 m/s, the flow is laminar; if faster, it becomes turbulent.

---

### **Worked Example 3: Discharge-Based Reynolds Number**

**Problem:** A 100 mm diameter pipe carries 0.025 m³/s of oil with $\nu = 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Find the Reynolds Number.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Use the discharge-based formula.**

$$Re = \frac{4Q}{\pi D \nu}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$Re = \frac{4 \times 0.025}{\pi \times 0.1 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

**Step 3: Compute step by step.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 4 \times 0.025 = 0.1 \\
\text{Denominator} &= \pi \times 0.1 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-5} = 1.4137 \times 10^{-5} \\
Re &= \frac{0.1}{1.4137 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 7074
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Conclude.**

Since $Re = 7074 > 4000$, the flow is **TURBULENT**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Flow Regime Classification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Flow Parameters: V, D, rho, mu] --> B[Calculate Re = rhoVD / mu]
    B --> C{Re Number?}
```
