```mermaid
    C -->|Re between 2000 and 4000| E[TRANSITION ZONE]
    C -->|Re greater than 4000| F[TURBULENT FLOW]
    D --> G[Smooth Parallel Streamlines]
    D --> H[Low Friction Losses]
    D --> I[Used in Viscometers and Lubrication]
    E --> J[Unstable Intermittent Behavior]
    E --> K[Engineering Design Avoids This Zone]
    F --> L[Chaotic Eddy Motion]
    F --> M[High Mixing and Momentum Transfer]
    F --> N[Used in Combustion and Heat Exchangers]
    G --> O[Engineering Decision]
    H --> O
    I --> O
    J --> O
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Reynolds Number — Force Competition Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fluid Flow System] --> B[Two Competing Forces]
    B --> C[Inertial Force: rho V squared D squared]
    B --> D[Viscous Force: mu V D]
    C --> E{Which Dominates?}
    D --> E
    E -->|Inertia wins Re high| F[TURBULENT FLOW]
    E -->|Viscosity wins Re low| G[LAMINAR FLOW]
    E -->|Balanced Re critical| H[TRANSITION POINT]
    F --> I[Order to Chaos Transition]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Critical Reynolds Number Re_critical]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Dimensional Analysis of Reynolds Number

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identifying Variables: rho, V, D, mu] --> B[Writing Dimensional Form]
    B --> C[M to the power a L to the power b T to the power c]
    C --> D[Applying Buckingham Pi Theorem]
    D --> E[Forming Pi Group: rho V D over mu]
    E --> F[Substituting Units]
    F --> G[kg m to power -1 s to power -1 cancels]
    G --> H[Dimensionless Quantity Confirmed]
    H --> I[Reynolds Number Defined]
    I --> J[Used to Predict Flow Regime]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Block: Reynolds Dye Experiment (Conceptual Reconstruction)

The original Reynolds experiment (1883) used a horizontal glass pipe with a **dye injector** (a thin stream of colored water) introduced at the centerline. A **flow control valve** regulated the velocity. By varying the velocity and observing the dye behavior, Reynolds established the three regimes:

- **Low velocity:** Dye travels as a single, unbroken, straight line. → LAMINAR
- **Moderate velocity:** Dye line wavers, breaks up, shows intermittent bursts. → TRANSITION
- **High velocity:** Dye instantly diffuses and mixes throughout the entire pipe cross-section. → TURBULENT

```
[Constant Head Tank] --> [Straight Glass Test Pipe] --> [Discharge Valve]
                              |
                              v
                       [Dye Injector]
                              |
                              v
                    [Flow Observation Zone]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1: Define Reynolds Number and state its physical significance.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 3]`

**Model Answer:**

The Reynolds Number (Re) is defined as the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

It is a **dimensionless** number. Its physical significance lies in its ability to **predict the flow regime** — whether the flow is laminar, transitional, or turbulent — without performing a full experiment. It also indicates the relative dominance of inertia versus viscosity, helping engineers scale models, design pipes, and predict heat/mass transfer behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** Definition (1 mark) + Formula (1 mark) + Physical significance (1 mark).

### **Question A2: What is the critical Reynolds Number for flow in a circular pipe? What happens at this value?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 3]`

**Model Answer:**

For flow in a smooth circular pipe, the **lower critical Reynolds number** is **Re_c = 2300**, and the **upper critical Reynolds number** is **Re_c = 4000**.

- For **Re < 2000:** The flow is **laminar** — stable, predictable, streamline flow.
- For **2000 < Re < 4000:** The flow is in the **transition zone** — unstable and unpredictable.
- For **Re > 4000:** The flow is **turbulent** — chaotic, with eddies and mixing.

At the critical Reynolds number, the flow undergoes a **bifurcation** from order to chaos — a fundamental change in flow structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** Critical value (1 mark) + Three regimes (1 mark) + Phenomenon at critical point (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from first principles using dimensional analysis, derive the expression for the Reynolds Number as the ratio of inertial to viscous forces. Show explicitly that the resulting group is dimensionless.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 75 mm diameter pipe carries oil of density 850 kg/m³ and dynamic viscosity $1.5 \times 10^{-2}$ N·s/m² at a velocity of 2.5 m/s. Determine: (i) the Reynolds Number, (ii) the type of flow, and (iii) the critical velocity at which the flow becomes turbulent.

#### **Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Dimensional Analysis Derivation [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Define the functional relationship.**

Inertial force depends on density, velocity, and characteristic length:

$$F_i = K \cdot \rho^a \cdot V^b \cdot D^c$$

**Step 2: Write dimensions for each variable.**

$[F_i] = MLT^{-2}$, $[\rho] = ML^{-3}$, $[V] = LT^{-1}$, $[D] = L$

**Step 3: Substitute and equate exponents.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M^1 L^1 T^{-2} &= M^a L^{-3a} \cdot L^b T^{-b} \cdot L^c \\
&= M^a L^{(-3a+b+c)} T^{-b}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Solve the system.**

- Mass: $1 = a \Rightarrow a = 1$
- Time: $-2 = -b \Rightarrow b = 2$
- Length: $1 = -3(1) + 2 + c \Rightarrow c = 2$

**Step 5: Get inertial force expression.**

$$F_i = K \cdot \rho V^2 D^2$$

**Step 6: Viscous force from Newton's law.**

$$F_v = K' \cdot \mu V D$$

**Step 7: Form the ratio.**

$$Re = \frac{F_i}{F_v} = \frac{K \cdot \rho V^2 D^2}{K' \cdot \mu V D} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} \quad (K/K' = 1)$$

**Step 8: Show dimensionless nature.**

$[Re] = \dfrac{(ML^{-3})(LT^{-1})(L)}{ML^{-1}T^{-1}} = M^0 L^0 T^0$ ✓

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Identify data.** $D = 0.075$ m, $V = 2.5$ m/s, $\rho = 850$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.5 \times 10^{-2}$ N·s/m²

**Step 2: Calculate Re.**

$$Re = \frac{850 \times 2.5 \times 0.075}{1.5 \times 10^{-2}} = \frac{159.375}{0.015} = 10625$$

**Step 3: Determine flow type.**

Since $Re = 10625 > 4000$ → **TURBULENT FLOW**

**Step 4: Calculate critical velocity (using Re_c = 2300 for lower critical):**

$$V_c = \frac{2300 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-2}}{850 \times 0.075} = \frac{34.5}{63.75} = 0.541 \text{ m/s}$$

**Conclusion:** The actual velocity (2.5 m/s) is well above the critical velocity (0.541 m/s), confirming turbulent flow.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the Reynolds Experiment for determining the flow regime. Discuss the observations and conclusions, with a clear distinction between laminar, transitional, and turbulent flow.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fluid with kinematic viscosity $\nu = 3 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s flows through a 60 mm diameter pipe at a rate of 5 liters per second. Calculate: (i) the average velocity, (ii) the Reynolds Number, (iii) the friction factor (use $f = 0.079 / Re^{0.25}$ for turbulent flow if applicable), and (iv) the head loss over 100 m length using Darcy-Weisbach equation.

#### **Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Reynolds Experiment Explanation [7 Marks]

**Apparatus:** A horizontal glass pipe connected to a constant-head water tank. A **dye reservoir** feeds a thin stream of colored dye (potassium permanganate) into the center of the pipe through a fine needle. A **discharge valve** at the outlet controls the flow rate.

**Procedure:** Water is allowed to flow at progressively increasing velocities, and the behavior of the dye is observed carefully.

**Observations:**

- **Low Velocity (Laminar):** The dye thread travels as a single, **straight, unbroken line** down the entire length of the pipe. No mixing occurs. The flow is **orderly** and **streamlined**.
- **Moderate Velocity (Transition):** The dye thread begins to **waver** and **oscillate**. At certain instants, it remains straight; at others, it suddenly breaks up into eddies. The flow is **unstable** and **intermittent**.
- **High Velocity (Turbulent):** The dye thread **breaks up almost immediately** and diffuses randomly across the entire pipe cross-section. The fluid particles follow **irregular, chaotic paths** with **eddy formation** and **cross-stream mixing**.

**Conclusions:**

1. Flow regime is determined by a dimensionless number now called the **Reynolds Number**.
2. For pipe flow: **Re < 2000** is laminar; **2000–4000** is transition; **Re > 4000** is turbulent.
3. The transition is **abrupt**, not gradual — it is a **bifurcation phenomenon**.
4. Reynolds Number serves as a **similarity parameter** for dynamic similarity in model-prototype scaling.

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Identify data.** $D = 0.06$ m, $Q = 5$ L/s $= 0.005$ m³/s, $\nu = 3 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $L = 100$ m

**Step 2: Calculate average velocity.**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.005}{\frac{\pi}{4}(0.06)^2} = \frac{0.005}{2.827 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.768 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Reynolds Number.**

$$Re = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{1.768 \times 0.06}{3 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.1061}{3 \times 10^{-5}} = 3536.67$$

**Step 4: Determine flow type and friction factor.**

Since $Re \approx 3537$ falls in the transition zone (2000–4000), but for engineering design we use the turbulent correlation:

$$f = \frac{0.079}{Re^{0.25}} = \frac{0.079}{(3536.67)^{0.25}} = \frac{0.079}{7.717} = 0.01024$$

**Step 5: Calculate head loss using Darcy-Weisbach.**

$$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2gD} = \frac{0.01024 \times 100 \times (1.768)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.06}$$

$$h_f = \frac{0.01024 \times 100 \times 3.126}{1.1772} = \frac{3.201}{1.1772} = 2.72 \text{ m}$$

**Conclusion:** Head loss over 100 m = **2.72 m** of fluid.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently make the following mistakes — lose 2–3 marks each:
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions** — pipe diameter must be in METERS (not mm) when velocity is in m/s. Use $D = 0.06$ m, NOT 60.
> 2. **Mixing up critical Re values** — KTU accepts BOTH 2300 (lower) and 4000 (upper). For design problems, use **2300** for conservative (safe) design.
> 3. **Using $\mu$ instead of $\nu$** — If the problem gives kinematic viscosity, the formula simplifies to $Re = VD/\nu$. Students incorrectly include $\rho$ when not needed.
> 4. **Not classifying the flow** — After computing Re, MUST state explicitly whether laminar/turbulent/transition with a clear comparison against 2300 and 4000.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Print This Section!**

- **Core Definition:** $Re = \rho V D / \mu = V D / \nu$. Memorize BOTH forms.
- **Dimensionless Verification:** Always substitute units in derivation — examiners award marks for explicit unit cancellation.
- **Critical Re for Pipe Flow:** Lower = **2300**; Upper = **4000**. These are NOT universal — they apply only to **smooth, circular pipes with calm inlet conditions**.
- **Laminar:** Re < 2000; **Turbulent:** Re > 4000; **Transition:** 2000 < Re < 4000.
- **Characteristic Length:** For pipes, $D$ is the diameter. For non-circular ducts, use **hydraulic diameter** $D_h = 4A/P$.
- **For flat plates (boundary layer):** Use **local Re** $Re_x = \rho V x / \mu$, where $x$ is distance from the leading edge. Transition at $Re_x \approx 5 \times 10^5$.
- **Physical Meaning:** Re represents the **ratio of inertial to viscous forces**. Low Re → viscous-dominated (slow, orderly); High Re → inertia-dominated (fast, chaotic).
- **Three Equivalent Force Scales:** $F_i \sim \rho V^2 D^2$, $F_v \sim \mu V D$, hence $Re = F_i / F_v$.
- **Velocity Formulas:** $V_c = Re_c \mu / (\rho D)$ for critical velocity; $V = Q/A$ for average velocity.
- **Engineering Tip:** In design, always use **Re_c = 2300** (conservative limit) to decide the maximum permissible velocity for guaranteed laminar flow.
- **Dimensional Analysis Link:** Reynolds Number is the most common example of the Buckingham Pi Theorem in KTU Module 3. Practice it thoroughly.
- **Boundary Layer Connection:** The local Re governs laminar-turbulent transition on flat plates and airfoils — frequently asked in Part B.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Reynolds Number is **NOT** the only dimensionless number in fluid mechanics. Don't confuse with Froude Number (gravity/inertia), Mach Number (compressibility), Euler Number (pressure/inertia), or Strouhal Number (unsteady effects).
- **Examiner's Phrase Bank:** Use terms like "dimensionless parameter," "dynamic similarity," "similarity criterion," "flow regime classification" — these are KTU buzzwords.

> **End of Notes — Reynolds Number, Module 3, PCAUT302**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laminar and Turbulent Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laminar and Turbulent Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In fluid mechanics, the nature of fluid motion through a conduit (pipe) is broadly classified into two principal regimes based on the internal fluid particle trajectory and the degree of mixing between adjacent fluid layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Laminar Flow (Streamline Flow):** A flow regime in which the fluid particles move along well-defined, smooth, parallel paths called *streamlines*, with no macroscopic mixing between adjacent layers. The velocity at any point in the flow field remains constant with respect to time (steady laminar flow) and the fluid motion occurs in an orderly, layer-by-layer fashion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Turbulent Flow:** A flow regime characterized by **chaotic, irregular, and random fluctuations** in fluid particle velocity and pressure. Macroscopic eddies, swirls, and intense cross-stream mixing dominate. The velocity at any fixed point fluctuates rapidly and randomly about a time-averaged mean value.

The transition between these two regimes is governed by a dimensionless parameter known as the **Reynolds Number (Re)**, first defined experimentally by **Osborne Reynolds in 1883**.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are pouring honey (highly viscous) versus water through a thin straw:

- **Honey through a straw:** The honey glides in perfectly smooth, parallel layers. You can see the central core move slightly faster than the wall layer. There is no swirling. This is the **laminar regime**.
- **Water through the same straw at a higher pressure:** The water rushes chaotically. You can hear the gurgling, and if you inject a tiny drop of ink, it instantly smears into the entire cross-section due to random eddies. This is the **turbulent regime**.

In essence, **viscosity tries to keep things orderly** (laminar), while **inertia tries to disrupt that order** (turbulent). The Reynolds Number is precisely the ratio of these two competing effects.

---

### 1.3 Reynolds Number — The Master Switch

The Reynolds Number is a **dimensionless similarity parameter** that predicts the flow pattern.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reynolds Number (Re):**
> 
> $$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$
> 
> Where:
> - $\rho$ = density of the fluid ($\text{kg/m}^3$)
> - $V$ = mean velocity of flow ($\text{m/s}$)
> - $D$ = diameter of the pipe ($\text{m}$)
> - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity ($\text{N·s/m}^2$)
> - $\nu = \mu/\rho$ = kinematic viscosity ($\text{m}^2/\text{s}$)

For flow in a circular pipe, the empirical critical limits (verified for water in smooth pipes) are:

| Flow Regime | Reynolds Number Range |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Laminar Flow** | $Re < 2000$ |
| **Critical / Transitional Zone** | $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ |
| **Turbulent Flow** | $Re > 4000$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These critical limits ($Re_{critical} = 2000$ for laminar-to-transitional, and $4000$ for transitional-to-turbulent) are **NOT universal physical constants**. They depend on the geometry, surface roughness, and inlet conditions. KTU examiners expect the standard textbook value of **Re = 2000** for the onset of turbulence in smooth circular pipes.

---

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics to Memorize

- **Dynamic viscosity of water at 20°C:** $\mu \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{N·s/m}^2$
- **Kinematic viscosity of water at 20°C:** $\nu \approx 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Density of water at 4°C:** $\rho \approx 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
- **Critical Reynolds number for pipe flow:** $Re_{cr} = 2000$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Osborne Reynolds' Experiment — The Birth of the Concept

In 1883, Osborne Reynolds conducted a landmark experiment using:
- A horizontal glass tube with a bell-mouthed entrance (to ensure uniform inlet).
- A tank supplying water at controlled velocities.
- A dye injection system (a thin stream of colored dye introduced at the centerline).

**Step-by-step experimental logic:**

1. **At very low velocities:** The dye filament remained a **distinct, straight, unbroken line** along the entire length of the tube. → **Laminar Flow.**
2. **As velocity increased gradually:** Beyond a critical velocity, the dye filament began to waver and then suddenly **broke up into a chaotic, fully-mixed mass**. → **Turbulent Flow.**
3. **The critical velocity** at which this transition occurred was used to define the **critical Reynolds Number**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 'Critical' and not 'Exact'?**
> The transition is not a single sharp point but a **hysteresis zone** ($2000 \le Re \le 4000$) because turbulence is highly sensitive to inlet disturbances, pipe vibrations, and wall roughness. A perfectly still inlet can sustain laminar flow up to $Re = 100{,}000$ in theory, but for engineering design, $Re = 2000$ is the **conservative safe limit**.

---

### 2.2 Physical Characteristics Comparison Table

| Property | Laminar Flow | Turbulent Flow |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fluid particle motion** | Smooth, parallel, layer-by-layer | Chaotic, random, swirling eddies |
| **Velocity profile** | Parabolic ($u \propto 1 - (r/R)^2$) | Blunter, logarithmic / power-law profile |
| **Friction factor (f)** | $f = 64 / Re$ (theoretical) | $f = 0.316 / Re^{0.25}$ (Blasius, smooth pipes) |
| **Head loss $\propto$** | $V$ (linearly) | $V^{1.75}$ to $V^2$ |
| **Shear stress** | Governed solely by viscous forces | Governed by viscous + Reynolds stresses |
| **Diffusion (mixing)** | Molecular diffusion only | Vigorous eddy diffusion |
| **Energy dissipation** | Low | High (exponential with velocity) |

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds Number — Regime identifier |
| 2 | $V_{cr} = \dfrac{2000 \, \nu}{D}$ | Critical velocity for laminar–turbulent transition |
| 3 | $u(r) = u_{max}\!\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{r}{R}\right)^{2}\right]$ | Parabolic velocity profile (laminar, pipe) |
| 4 | $u_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta p \, R^{2}}{4 \mu L}$ | Centerline (max) velocity |
| 5 | $V_{mean} = \dfrac{u_{max}}{2} = \dfrac{\Delta p \, R^{2}}{8 \mu L}$ | Mean (average) velocity |
| 6 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \, \Delta p \, R^{4}}{8 \mu L} = \dfrac{\pi \, \Delta p \, D^{4}}{128 \mu L}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille discharge equation |
| 7 | $h_f = \dfrac{32 \, \mu \, L \, V}{\rho g D^{2}}$ | Head loss for laminar flow (Darcy) |
| 8 | $\Delta p = \dfrac{32 \, \mu \, L \, V}{D^{2}}$ | Pressure drop for laminar flow |
| 9 | $\tau(r) = -\dfrac{r}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{dp}{dx}\right)$ | Shear stress distribution in pipe |
| 10 | $\tau_{wall} = \dfrac{\Delta p \, R}{2 L}$ | Wall shear stress |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention used:** The pressure gradient $\dfrac{dp}{dx}$ is **negative** in the direction of flow (pressure decreases). The magnitude $\Delta p / L$ is used as a positive quantity in practice. The negative sign in the velocity equation arises because the maximum velocity occurs at the center ($r=0$) where $\dfrac{dp}{dx} < 0$.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic systems design:** Engineers use Reynolds Number to determine whether flow in an oil pipeline is laminar (predictable friction) or turbulent (requires empirical correlations).
- **Chemical reactors:** Laminar flow ensures uniform residence time for controlled reactions; turbulent flow enhances mixing for fast reactions.
- **Blood flow in arteries:** Pulsatile flow has local $Re$ in the range of $100$ to $4000$ — mostly laminar, but can become turbulent at stenoses (blockages), causing the audible "bruit".
- **Aerodynamics:** The concept of a turbulent boundary layer on aircraft wings reduces drag compared to a laminar layer (via delayed separation), but increases skin friction.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Velocity Profile for Steady Laminar Flow in a Circular Pipe (Hagen–Poiseuille Flow)

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible, Newtonian fluid.
2. Flow is laminar, fully developed, and axisymmetric.
3. No slip at the wall ($u(R) = 0$).
4. Flow is unidirectional along the pipe axis ($x$): $v = w = 0$, $u = u(r)$.
5. Body forces (gravity) act only along the $x$-direction and are neglected initially for a horizontal pipe.

**Step 1: Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates**

For fully developed, axisymmetric, unidirectional flow ($u = u(r)$ only, with $v = w = 0$ and $\partial / \partial \theta = 0$), the $r$-momentum and $\theta$-momentum equations reduce to $0 = 0$. The **$x$-momentum** equation becomes:

$$\rho \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial r} + \frac{w}{r}\frac{\partial u}{\partial \theta}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu\!\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\frac{\partial u}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial \theta^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\right] + \rho g_x$$

**Step 2: Simplification using fully developed flow**

Since $u = u(r)$ only, all $x$-derivatives vanish: $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0$. Steady state means $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = 0$. The equation reduces to:

$$0 = -\frac{dp}{dx} + \mu \cdot \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{du}{dr}\right)$$

*(The gravity term is dropped for a horizontal pipe; for an inclined pipe it would simply modify $p$ to $p - \rho g x \sin\theta$.)*

**Step 3: First integration**

Rearranging and multiplying by $r$:

$$\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{du}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\, r$$

Integrate once with respect to $r$:

$$r\frac{du}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\, r^{2} + C_1$$

**Step 4: Apply the boundary condition at the centerline**

The velocity must be **finite (and maximum) at the centerline** $r = 0$. Since $r \to 0$ and the LHS would diverge unless the coefficient of $r^2$ on the RHS balances, we have:

$$C_1 = 0 \quad \text{(to keep } \frac{du}{dr} \text{ finite at } r=0\text{)}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{du}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\, r$$

**Step 5: Second integration**

$$u(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\, r^{2} + C_2$$

**Step 6: Apply the no-slip condition at the wall**

At $r = R$: $u(R) = 0$.

$$0 = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\, R^{2} + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\, R^{2}$$

**Step 7: Final velocity profile**

$$\boxed{\,u(r) = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)\,}$$

> **Interpretation note:** The pressure gradient $\dfrac{dp}{dx}$ is **negative** in the flow direction (pressure drops as fluid moves forward). The negative sign in front of the entire expression combined with a negative $\dfrac{dp}{dx}$ makes $u(r)$ **positive** (forward flow), and at $r=0$ the velocity is **maximum**.

**Step 8: Maximum velocity (centerline)**

Setting $r = 0$:

$$u_{max} = -\frac{R^{2}}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dx} = \frac{\Delta p \, R^{2}}{4 \mu L}$$

where $\Delta p / L = -\dfrac{dp}{dx}$ is the magnitude of the pressure gradient.

**Step 9: Parabolic profile in normalized form**

Dividing the velocity equation by $u_{max}$:

$$\frac{u(r)}{u_{max}} = 1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{2}$$

This is a **parabola** in $r$, with vertex at $r=0$ and zero at $r=R$. This is the signature velocity profile of laminar pipe flow.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Mean Velocity and Hagen–Poiseuille Discharge

**Step 1: Mean velocity definition**

$$V_{mean} = \frac{1}{\pi R^{2}} \int_{0}^{R} u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

**Step 2: Substitute the velocity profile**

$$V_{mean} = \frac{1}{\pi R^{2}} \int_{0}^{R} \left[-\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)\right] 2\pi r \, dr$$

**Step 3: Pull out constants**

$$V_{mean} = -\frac{1}{2\mu R^{2}}\frac{dp}{dx} \int_{0}^{R} \left(R^{2} r - r^{3}\right) dr$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integral**

$$\int_{0}^{R} \left(R^{2} r - r^{3}\right) dr = \left[\frac{R^{2} r^{2}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{R} = \frac{R^{4}}{2} - \frac{R^{4}}{4} = \frac{R^{4}}{4}$$

**Step 5: Final mean velocity**

$$V_{mean} = -\frac{1}{2\mu R^{2}}\frac{dp}{dx} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4} = -\frac{R^{2}}{8\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}$$

$$V_{mean} = \frac{\Delta p \, R^{2}}{8 \mu L} = \frac{u_{max}}{2}$$

**Step 6: Volumetric flow rate (Hagen–Poiseuille Equation)**

$$Q = V_{mean} \cdot A = \frac{\Delta p \, R^{2}}{8 \mu L} \cdot \pi R^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,Q = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta p}{8 \mu L} = \frac{\pi D^{4} \Delta p}{128 \mu L}\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Wall Shear Stress and Friction Factor

**Step 1: Shear stress from Newton's law of viscosity**

$$\tau_{rx} = \mu \frac{du}{dr} = \mu \cdot \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\, r = \frac{r}{2}\frac{dp}{dx}$$

With $\dfrac{dp}{dx} < 0$, the shear stress on the fluid is in the negative $x$-direction (drag), meaning the wall exerts a retarding force. The **magnitude** of shear stress at the wall ($r = R$):

$$\tau_{wall} = \frac{R}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta p}{L}$$

**Step 2: Force balance on the pipe (control volume)**

Pressure force on the fluid in a pipe segment of length $L$ = $\Delta p \cdot \pi R^2$.
Wall shear force = $\tau_{wall} \cdot 2\pi R \cdot L$.

For steady fully developed flow, these balance:

$$\Delta p \cdot \pi R^{2} = \tau_{wall} \cdot 2\pi R \cdot L \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau_{wall} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot R}{2L}$$

This confirms the same result — a beautiful consistency check.

**Step 3: Friction factor derivation**

The Darcy–Weisbach friction factor is defined by:

$$h_f = \frac{4 \tau_{wall} L}{\rho g D} = \frac{f L V^{2}}{2 g D}$$

Equating both expressions for $h_f$:

$$\frac{4 \tau_{wall} L}{\rho g D} = \frac{f L V^{2}}{2 g D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f = \frac{8 \tau_{wall}}{\rho V^{2}}$$

**Step 4: Substitute $\tau_{wall}$ in terms of $\mu$ and $V$**

Since $V = \dfrac{\Delta p R^{2}}{8 \mu L}$ and $\tau_{wall} = \dfrac{\Delta p R}{2L}$:

$$\tau_{wall} = \frac{4 \mu V}{R} = \frac{8 \mu V}{D}$$

**Step 5: Final laminar friction factor**

$$f = \frac{8 \cdot (8 \mu V / D)}{\rho V^{2}} = \frac{64 \mu}{\rho V D} = \frac{64}{Re}$$

$$\boxed{\,f_{laminar} = \frac{64}{Re}\,}$$

> This is one of the **most important KTU exam results** — the friction factor for laminar flow is purely a function of Reynolds Number, with no dependence on surface roughness.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Example — Worked Solution

**Problem:** Oil with $\mu = 0.1 \; \text{N·s/m}^2$, $\rho = 900 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ flows through a horizontal pipe of diameter $D = 50 \; \text{mm}$ and length $L = 10 \; \text{m}$ at a mean velocity of $V = 1.0 \; \text{m/s}$. Determine: (a) Reynolds Number, (b) Pressure drop, (c) Friction factor, (d) Head loss, (e) Maximum velocity at centerline.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Reynolds Number**

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{900 \times 1.0 \times 0.05}{0.1} = 450$$

Since $Re = 450 < 2000$, the flow is **laminar**. ✅

**Part (b): Pressure drop**

$$\Delta p = \frac{32 \mu L V}{D^{2}} = \frac{32 \times 0.1 \times 10 \times 1.0}{(0.05)^{2}} = \frac{32}{0.0025} = 12{,}800 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Part (c): Friction factor**

$$f = \frac{64}{Re} = \frac{64}{450} = 0.1422$$

**Part (d): Head loss**

$$h_f = \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^{2}} = \frac{32 \times 0.1 \times 10 \times 1.0}{900 \times 9.81 \times (0.05)^{2}} = \frac{32}{2.207} = 14.5 \; \text{m of oil}$$

**Part (e): Maximum velocity**

$$u_{max} = 2 V_{mean} = 2 \times 1.0 = 2.0 \; \text{m/s}$$

```mermaid

    style S fill:#ffe1e1
    style U fill:#e1f5ff
    style V fill:#e1f5ff
```

**Interpretation:** The above flow block visually traces the **parabolic velocity profile** of laminar flow. At the pipe wall, the **no-slip condition** enforces $u = 0$. Moving radially inward, velocity rises parabolically to its **maximum at the centerline** ($r = 0$). The profile is symmetric about the centerline — a key fingerprint distinguishing laminar from turbulent flow.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**
Define Reynolds Number. State its significance in classifying fluid flow regimes.

**Model Answer:**

> Reynolds Number is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow. It is mathematically expressed as:
> 
> $$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$
> 
> where $\rho$ is the fluid density, $V$ is the mean velocity, $D$ is the pipe diameter, $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity, and $\nu$ is the kinematic viscosity.
> 
> **Significance:**
> - For pipe flow, $Re < 2000$ → **Laminar flow** (viscous forces dominate, orderly motion).
> - For $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ → **Transitional flow** (unstable, intermittent).
> - For $Re > 4000$ → **Turbulent flow** (inertial forces dominate, chaotic motion).
> 
> It is a **similarity parameter**, meaning two geometrically similar flows with the same Reynolds Number exhibit dynamically similar behavior regardless of the actual fluid or scale.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
State the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** for laminar flow through a circular pipe and explain the physical meaning of each term.

**Model Answer:**

> The Hagen–Poiseuille equation gives the volumetric discharge for steady, fully developed, laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a horizontal circular pipe:
> 
> $$Q = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta p}{8 \mu L} = \frac{\pi D^{4} \Delta p}{128 \mu L}$$
> 
> **Physical meaning of each term:**
> - $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate ($\text{m}^3/\text{s}$)
> - $\Delta p$ = Pressure drop across pipe length $L$ ($\text{Pa}$)
> - $R, D$ = Radius and diameter of pipe ($\text{m}$)
> - $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of fluid ($\text{Pa·s}$)
> - $L$ = Length of pipe ($\text{m}$)
> 
> **Key insight:** Discharge is **inversely proportional to viscosity** (more viscous fluids flow less) and **inversely proportional to length** (longer pipes lose more pressure). Most critically, $Q \propto D^{4}$ — a small change in diameter produces a **dramatic change** in flow rate (a 2× diameter increase → 16× flow).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the velocity distribution equation for steady, laminar, incompressible flow of a Newtonian fluid through a horizontal circular pipe. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A liquid with viscosity $\mu = 0.05 \; \text{N·s/m}^2$ and specific gravity $0.9$ flows through a horizontal pipe of diameter $D = 100 \; \text{mm}$ and length $L = 30 \; \text{m}$ with a mean velocity of $0.5 \; \text{m/s}$. Calculate: (i) Reynolds Number and verify the flow regime, (ii) Maximum velocity, (iii) Pressure drop, (iv) Friction factor, (v) Head loss. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Complete Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a): Derivation of Velocity Distribution**

**Step 1: State the assumptions clearly**
- Steady, incompressible, Newtonian fluid.
- Fully developed, axisymmetric, unidirectional flow.
- No-slip at the wall: $u(R) = 0$.
- Velocity depends only on $r$: $u = u(r)$.
- Horizontal pipe (gravity term vanishes in the $x$-momentum equation).

**Step 2: Write the Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates** for the $x$-direction. Since $v = w = 0$ and all $x$-derivatives vanish:

$$0 = -\frac{dp}{dx} + \mu \cdot \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{du}{dr}\right)$$

**Step 3: First integration**

$$r\frac{du}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\, r^{2} + C_1$$

**Step 4: Apply boundary condition at $r = 0$** (velocity must be finite) → $C_1 = 0$.

$$\frac{du}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\, r$$

**Step 5: Second integration**

$$u(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\, r^{2} + C_2$$

**Step 6: Apply no-slip at $r = R$** → $C_2 = -\dfrac{1}{4\mu}\dfrac{dp}{dx}\, R^{2}$.

**Step 7: Final result**

$$u(r) = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right) = u_{max}\!\left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{2}\right]$$

This is a **parabolic velocity profile**, maximum at the centerline and zero at the wall.

---

#### **Part (b): Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $\mu = 0.05 \; \text{N·s/m}^2$
- $\rho = 0.9 \times 1000 = 900 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- $D = 0.1 \; \text{m}$, $L = 30 \; \text{m}$
- $V_{mean} = 0.5 \; \text{m/s}$
- $R = 0.05 \; \text{m}$

**(i) Reynolds Number and Flow Regime:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{900 \times 0.5 \times 0.1}{0.05} = 900$$

Since $Re = 900 < 2000$, the flow is **definitely laminar**. ✅

**(ii) Maximum velocity:**

$$u_{max} = 2 V_{mean} = 2 \times 0.5 = 1.0 \; \text{m/s}$$

**(iii) Pressure drop:**

$$\Delta p = \frac{32 \mu L V}{D^{2}} = \frac{32 \times 0.05 \times 30 \times 0.5}{(0.1)^{2}} = \frac{24}{0.01} = 2400 \; \text{Pa}$$

**(iv) Friction factor (Darcy):**

$$f = \frac{64}{Re} = \frac{64}{900} = 0.0711$$

**(v) Head loss:**

$$h_f = \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^{2}} = \frac{32 \times 0.05 \times 30 \times 0.5}{900 \times 9.81 \times (0.1)^{2}}$$

$$h_f = \frac{24}{88.29} = 0.2718 \; \text{m of liquid}$$

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model — ALTERNATIVE]**

**(a)** Explain Osborne Reynolds' experiment in detail. How does the Reynolds Number distinguish between laminar, transitional, and turbulent flow regimes? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a steady, laminar flow of a fluid with $\mu = 0.8 \; \text{N·s/m}^2$ and $\rho = 1200 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ through a horizontal pipe of diameter $D = 60 \; \text{mm}$ and length $L = 20 \; \text{m}$, the measured pressure drop is $\Delta p = 1500 \; \text{Pa}$. Calculate: (i) Mean velocity, (ii) Discharge, (iii) Maximum velocity at the centerline, (iv) Velocity at $r = 15 \; \text{mm}$ from the centerline, (v) Wall shear stress. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Complete Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a): Reynolds' Experiment Explanation**

Osborne Reynolds (1883) conducted an experiment to visualize the transition from laminar to turbulent flow using:
- A glass tube with controlled water inflow.
- A bell-mouthed inlet to ensure smooth entry.
- A **dye injection** mechanism at the centerline.

**Observations:**
1. At very low velocities, the dye filament traveled as a **single, sharp, unbroken straight line** — this indicated **streamline (laminar) flow**.
2. As the velocity was gradually increased, the dye line began to oscillate. At a **critical velocity**, the dye filament **suddenly burst into a chaotic, dispersed cloud**, mixing uniformly with the bulk fluid — this indicated **turbulent flow**.

**Critical Reynolds Number Definition:**

$$Re_{cr} = \frac{V_{cr} D}{\nu} \approx 2000$$

**Flow Regime Classification:**
- $Re < 2000$ → **Laminar** (viscous forces dominate, dye travels as a line).
- $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ → **Transitional** (unstable, intermittent bursting).
- $Re > 4000$ → **Turbulent** (inertial forces dominate, dye disperses).

**Physical Interpretation:** The Reynolds Number is the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces**:
- **Low $Re$** → viscosity dominates → flow is smooth.
- **High $Re$** → inertia dominates → small disturbances grow into turbulence.

---

#### **Part (b): Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $\mu = 0.8 \; \text{N·s/m}^2$, $\rho = 1200 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- $D = 0.06 \; \text{m}$, $R = 0.03 \; \text{m}$, $L = 20 \; \text{m}$
- $\Delta p = 1500 \; \text{Pa}$

**(i) Mean velocity (from Hagen–Poiseuille rearranged):**

$$V = \frac{\Delta p \, D^{2}}{32 \mu L} = \frac{1500 \times (0.06)^{2}}{32 \times 0.8 \times 20} = \frac{1500 \times 0.0036}{512} = \frac{5.4}{512} = 0.01055 \; \text{m/s}$$

**(ii) Discharge:**

$$Q = \frac{\pi D^{4} \Delta p}{128 \mu L} = \frac{\pi \times (0.06)^{4} \times 1500}{128 \times 0.8 \times 20}$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-5} \times 1500}{2048} = \frac{0.06107}{2048} = 2.98 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

Alternatively, $Q = V \cdot A = 0.01055 \times \pi \times (0.03)^{2} = 0.01055 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} = 2.98 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ ✅

**(iii) Maximum velocity:**

$$u_{max} = 2 V = 2 \times 0.01055 = 0.0211 \; \text{m/s}$$

**(iv) Velocity at $r = 15 \; \text{mm} = 0.015 \; \text{m}$:**

$$u(r) = u_{max}\!\left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{2}\right] = 0.0211 \times \left[1 - \left(\frac{0.015}{0.03}\right)^{2}\right]$$

$$u = 0.0211 \times [1 - 0.25] = 0.0211 \times 0.75 = 0.01583 \; \text{m/s}$$

**(v) Wall shear stress:**

$$\tau_{wall} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot R}{2L} = \frac{1500 \times 0.03}{2 \times 20} = \frac{45}{40} = 1.125 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Verification of laminar flow:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{1200 \times 0.01055 \times 0.06}{0.8} = \frac{0.7596}{0.8} = 0.95$$

Since $Re = 0.95 \ll 2000$, the assumption of laminar flow is **strongly valid**. ✅

---
