Re = \frac{VD}{\nu} = \frac{3 \times 0.075}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.25 \times 10^5
$$

**Step 2: Calculate relative roughness** **(1.5 marks)**

$$
\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.046 \times 10^{-3}}{0.075} = 6.13 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Swamee-Jain equation** **(3 marks)**

$$
f = \frac{0.25}{\left[ \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{6.13 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{(2.25 \times 10^5)^{0.9}} \right) \right]^2}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
\frac{6.13 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} = 1.657 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
(2.25 \times 10^5)^{0.9} = 68934.3
$$

$$
\frac{5.74}{68934.3} = 8.327 \times 10^{-5}
$$

Sum:

$$
1.657 \times 10^{-4} + 8.327 \times 10^{-5} = 2.490 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Logarithm:

$$
\log_{10}(2.490 \times 10^{-4}) = -3.604
$$

Square:

$$
(-3.604)^2 = 12.99
$$

Friction factor:

$$
\boxed{\, f = \frac{0.25}{12.99} = 0.0192 \,}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate head loss** **(3.5 marks)**

$$
h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = 0.0192 \times \frac{100}{0.075} \times \frac{(3)^2}{2 \times 9.81}
$$

$$
h_f = 0.0192 \times 1333.33 \times \frac{9}{19.62}
$$

$$
h_f = 0.0192 \times 1333.33 \times 0.4587
$$

$$
\boxed{\, h_f = 11.74 \text{ m} \,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — leads to **0.5 to 1 mark** deduction.
> 2. **Confusing Darcy vs Fanning friction factor** — if the question states "Fanning", use $f_{\text{Fanning}} = f_{\text{Darcy}}/4$. This error loses **2-3 marks**.
> 3. **Using Blasius in rough turbulent regime** — Blasius is valid only for smooth pipes. Using it for cast iron or commercial steel loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Not verifying laminar assumption** — if $Re > 2000$, you MUST use the Moody chart or Swamee-Jain, not $f = 64/Re$. This is a **3-mark penalty**.
> 5. **Arithmetic mistakes in $\log_{10}$ or exponents** — be careful with the Swamee-Jain formula; show all intermediate steps to earn partial credit even if the final number is wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam!**

### Core Equation (Memorize!)
- $h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ — head loss in **meters**
- $\Delta p = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ — pressure drop in **Pa**
- $\tau_w = f \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{8}$ — wall shear stress in **Pa**

### Friction Factor (The Big Three)
- **Laminar:** $f = 64/Re$ — applies when $Re < 2000$
- **Turbulent (smooth):** Blasius $f = 0.316/Re^{0.25}$ — for $Re < 10^5$
- **Turbulent (rough/transitional):** Swamee-Jain explicit formula or Colebrook-White implicit

### Friction Factor Conventions
- **Darcy** $f$ is **4×** the **Fanning** $f'$
- Most KTU problems use **Darcy** unless explicitly stated otherwise

### Key Physical Insights
- $h_f \propto V^2$ in turbulent flow (quadratic!)
- $h_f \propto V$ in laminar flow (linear)
- Larger diameter → smaller head loss (inverse relationship)
- Rougher pipe → larger $f$ in fully rough regime

### Units You MUST Know
- $V$: m/s
- $D$, $L$: m
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $\rho$: $\text{kg/m}^3$
- $\nu$: $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- $\mu$: $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s} = \text{kg/(m}\cdot\text{s)}$

### Common Exam Traps
- Question asks for **Fanning** but you solve for **Darcy** — always re-read!
- Forgetting that **Blasius does NOT apply** to rough pipes
- Using $f = 64/Re$ when flow is actually turbulent
- Not converting mm to m before plugging in (very common!)

### Quick Conversions to Remember
- $1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $1 \text{ kPa} = 10^3 \text{ Pa}$
- $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $\log_{10}(x) = \ln(x)/\ln(10)$ for calculator-free environments

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chezy's Formula
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chezy's Formula

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — CHEZY'S FORMULA

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Chezy's Formula** is a fundamental empirical relation in **open channel flow** that expresses the **mean velocity of water flowing in a prismatic channel under steady, uniform flow conditions**. The formula was first proposed by the French Engineer **Antoine de Chézy in 1769** while designing a water supply canal for the city of Paris. It is mathematically stated as:

$$
V = C\sqrt{RS}
$$

where:
- $V$ = mean velocity of flow (m/s)
- $C$ = **Chezy's coefficient** (m$^{1/2}$/s), a constant depending on the roughness of channel surface and flow characteristics
- $R$ = **Hydraulic radius** (m) $= A/P$ where $A$ is the cross-sectional area of flow and $P$ is the wetted perimeter
- $S$ = **Slope of the energy line** (bed slope in uniform flow, dimensionless)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 HIGHLIGHT:** Chezy's formula is **valid only for turbulent flow in open channels** under **steady and uniform flow** conditions. It is one of the most tested topics in the KTU end-semester examinations under Module 3 of PCAUT302.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive Explanation

Imagine a **long, gently sloping concrete drainpipe** lying on a slope. Water enters at the high end and exits at the low end. Intuitively, we know two things:
1. The **steeper** the slope, the **faster** water moves (so velocity $\propto \sqrt{S}$).
2. The **bigger** the cross-section relative to its wetted boundary, the **less the wall friction** slows the water. This is the **hydraulic radius $R$** — the bigger it is, the more "slippery-smooth" the flow feels (so velocity $\propto \sqrt{R}$).

Chezy simply combined these two ideas into one elegant formula: $V = C\sqrt{RS}$. The constant $C$ acts as a **"friction dial"** — lower $C$ means rougher surface (like a cobblestone canal), higher $C$ means smoother surface (like a polished cement flume).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in KTU Board Problems:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81$ m/s$^2$ (sometimes taken as $9.8$ or $10$ m/s$^2$ in simplified problems)
> - **Chezy's $C$ is NOT dimensionless** — its units are m$^{1/2}$/s
> - Hydraulic radius $R$ has units of metres (m)
> - Slope $S$ is dimensionless (m/m)

### 1.3 Historical Context

In 1769, the French government appointed Chezy to design a canal for the **Yvette River water supply to Paris**. While observing uniform flow in existing channels, Chezy concluded that:
- The **force causing flow** (component of gravity along the channel) is balanced by the **frictional resistance** at the boundary.
- For **turbulent flow**, this friction is **proportional to the square of the velocity** (similar to Newton's law of high-speed resistance).

This assumption of $\tau \propto V^2$ was a stroke of practical insight and has stood the test of time as a cornerstone of open channel hydraulics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Assumptions of Chezy's Formula

For Chezy's formula to be applicable, the following assumptions must hold:

- The flow is **steady** (does not change with time).
- The flow is **uniform** (depth and velocity constant along the length).
- The channel is **prismatic** (cross-section shape and size do not change along length).
- The **bed slope $S$ is small** ($\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$).
- The flow is **turbulent** (Reynolds number is high).
- The **wetted perimeter is fully rough** (boundary friction depends only on roughness, not on viscosity).
- Resistance varies with the **square of the velocity** (Newton's law of high-Reynolds flow).

### 2.2 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

| Step | Logic | Why it Matters in Engineering |
|------|-------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Force balance: gravity component = boundary friction | Physical basis — gives meaning to the formula |
| 2 | Friction $\propto V^2$ (turbulent regime) | Empirical foundation — works for rivers, canals |
| 3 | Introduce Chezy's constant $C = \sqrt{\rho g / k}$ | Hides all the messy constants into one practical number |
| 4 | Obtain $V = C\sqrt{RS}$ | The final usable form for design of canals |
| 5 | Use $Q = AV$ to get discharge | Real engineering output: $Q = AC\sqrt{RS}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Real-World Use:** Chezy's formula is the backbone of **irrigation canal design**, **sewer design**, **spillway rating**, and **flood estimation in rivers**. Every minor irrigation project in Kerala (e.g., the Kuttanad or Karappuzha canal networks) implicitly uses Chezy's relation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $V = C\sqrt{RS}$ | Chezy's velocity formula | $V$ in m/s, $C$ in m$^{1/2}$/s |
| 2 | $Q = AV = AC\sqrt{RS}$ | Chezy's discharge formula | $Q$ in m$^3$/s |
| 3 | $R = A/P$ | Hydraulic radius definition | $A$ in m$^2$, $P$ in m |
| 4 | $P = B + 2y$ (rectangular) | Wetted perimeter for rectangular channel | $B$ = bed width, $y$ = depth |
| 5 | $A = By$ (rectangular) | Area of rectangular channel | Standard cross-section |
| 6 | $P = B + 2y\sqrt{1+m^2}$ (trapezoidal) | Wetted perimeter for trapezoidal channel | $m$ = side slope (1V:mH) |
| 7 | $A = (B + my)y$ (trapezoidal) | Area of trapezoidal channel | Most common KTU exam type |
| 8 | $C = \sqrt{8g/f}$ | Chezy's $C$ from Darcy friction factor $f$ | $f$ is dimensionless Darcy factor |
| 9 | $C = \dfrac{1}{n}R^{1/6}$ | Chezy's $C$ from Manning's roughness $n$ | $n$ in s/m$^{1/3}$ (SI) |
| 10 | $C = \dfrac{157.6}{1.81 + \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{R}}}$ | **Bazin's formula** for $C$ | $K$ = Bazin's roughness coefficient |
| 11 | $C = \dfrac{23 + \dfrac{0.00155}{S} + \dfrac{1}{n}}{1 + \left[23 \cdot \dfrac{0.00155}{S} + \dfrac{1}{n}\right] \cdot \dfrac{n}{\sqrt{R}}}$ | **Kutter's formula** for $C$ | Most general; $n$ = Kutter's roughness |
| 12 | $C = 42 R^{1/2}$ | **Powell's empirical formula** (approx) | Quick estimate for $R$ in m |
| 13 | $V^2 = (1/k) \cdot gRS$ | Velocity-squared form from force balance | Useful for proof derivations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU NOTE:** For trapezoidal and circular section problems, KTU examiners frequently give a numerical $C$ value directly so students do not have to calculate it. Always check the problem statement.

### 2.4 Where Chezy's Formula Lives in Engineering

- **Irrigation Engineering:** Design of unlined and lined canals in Kerala's paddy tracts (Kuttanad, Palakkad).
- **Sewer Design:** Storm water and sanitary sewer gravity flow systems.
- **Hydraulic Structures:** Spillway capacity, stilling basin design.
- **River Hydraulics:** Flood routing, river training works.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Flow in natural streams, ecological flow assessment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Derivation of Chezy's Formula from First Principles

**Consider** a length $L$ of a prismatic open channel with bed slope $S$, conveying water under **steady, uniform flow**.

**Step 1 — Identify the volume of water in the channel length $L$:**
The volume is $V_{ol} = A \cdot L$, where $A$ is the cross-sectional area of flow.

**Step 2 — Identify the weight of water in this length:**
The weight $W$ is the volume times the specific weight of water $\rho g$:

$$
W = \rho g A L
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the component of this weight along the direction of flow:**
The bed slope $S = \tan\theta$ where $\theta$ is the angle the bed makes with the horizontal. The component of weight acting **along the flow** (i.e., pulling water down the slope) is:

$$
F_{gravity} = W \sin\theta = \rho g A L \sin\theta
$$

Since the slope is small, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = S$, so:

$$
F_{gravity} = \rho g A L S
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the resisting frictional force at the channel boundary:**
Under uniform flow, this gravity component is exactly balanced by the **boundary shear force** $F_{friction}$ acting over the wetted area. The boundary shear stress is $\tau_0$ (units: N/m$^2$), and it acts over the **wetted perimeter $P$** for the length $L$:

$$
F_{friction} = \tau_0 \cdot P \cdot L
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the equilibrium condition (uniform flow means no acceleration):**

$$
F_{gravity} = F_{friction}
$$

$$
\rho g A L S = \tau_0 P L
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the equation:**
Cancel $L$ from both sides:

$$
\rho g A S = \tau_0 P
$$

Divide both sides by $P$:

$$
\tau_0 = \rho g \left(\dfrac{A}{P}\right) S
$$

Recognising that $R = A/P$ is the **hydraulic radius**:

$$
\tau_0 = \rho g R S
$$

This is the **fundamental boundary shear stress relation** in open channel flow — it is exact and follows directly from a force balance.

**Step 7 — Apply Chezy's empirical law of resistance:**
Chezy observed (similar to Newton) that for turbulent flow, the boundary shear stress is proportional to the **square of the mean velocity**:

$$
\tau_0 = k V^2
$$

where $k$ is a proportionality constant (depends on roughness, units: kg/m).

**Step 8 — Equate the two expressions for $\tau_0$:**

$$
k V^2 = \rho g R S
$$

**Step 9 — Solve for velocity squared:**

$$
V^2 = \left(\dfrac{\rho g}{k}\right) R S
$$

**Step 10 — Take the square root and define Chezy's constant:**
The quantity $C = \sqrt{\rho g / k}$ bundles together $\rho$, $g$, and the friction-related $k$ into a single empirical constant. Then:

$$
\boxed{V = C \sqrt{R S}}
$$

This is **Chezy's Formula** — derived from Newton's resistance law under uniform flow assumption.

> [!NOTE]
> **SI Unit Check for $C$:**
> $[\rho g] = \text{kg}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^2)$ and $[k] = \text{kg/m}$. So $[\rho g / k] = \text{m}/\text{s}^2$. Taking square root: $[C] = \sqrt{\text{m}}/\text{s} = \text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Chezy's $C$ from Darcy-Weisbach Equation

The Darcy-Weisbach equation for head loss in a pipe (also applicable to open channels via equivalent hydraulic radius) is:

$$
h_f = \dfrac{f L V^2}{2g D}
$$

For a non-circular channel, the equivalent diameter is $D = 4R$, so:

$$
h_f = \dfrac{f L V^2}{8 g R}
$$

The energy slope (in uniform flow, this equals the bed slope) is:

$$
S = \dfrac{h_f}{L} = \dfrac{f V^2}{8 g R}
$$

Rearranging for $V$:

$$
V = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 g}{f}} \cdot \sqrt{R S}
$$

Comparing with Chezy's formula $V = C\sqrt{RS}$:

$$
\boxed{C = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 g}{f}}}
$$

where $f$ is the Darcy-Weisbach friction factor (dimensionless).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Cross-Reference:** This relation is often asked as a 3-mark or 7-mark sub-question. The factor $f$ is the **Darcy friction factor**, not the Fanning friction factor. Some texts use $f' = 4f$ (Fanning), in which case $C = \sqrt{2g/f'}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Chezy's $C$ from Manning's Equation

Manning's equation for mean velocity in open channel flow is:

$$
V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}
$$

Rewriting Chezy's formula as $V = C R^{1/2} S^{1/2}$:

$$
C R^{1/2} S^{1/2} = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}
$$

Cancel $S^{1/2}$ from both sides:

$$
C R^{1/2} = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3}
$$

Solve for $C$:

$$
C = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3 - 1/2} = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{4/6 - 3/6} = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{1/6}
$$

$$
\boxed{C = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{1/6}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Unit Reminder for $n$ (SI system):** $n$ has units of s/m$^{1/3}$. Do not use the CGS or FPS value of $n$ directly when working in SI units — it must be converted first.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Conceptual Flow of Chezy's Derivation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Prismatic Channel with Slope S] --> A1[Consider Length L of Channel]
    A1 --> A2[Weight of Water W = rho times g times A times L]
    A2 --> A3[Driving Force Along Flow = W sin theta approx W S]
    A3 --> A4[Resisting Force = Shear Stress tau0 times P times L]
    A4 --> A5[Equilibrium: Gravity Force = Friction Force]
    A5 --> A6[Simplify to tau0 = rho g R S]
    A6 --> A7[Chezy Assumption: tau0 proportional to V squared]
    A7 --> A8[Substitute tau0 = k V squared]
    A8 --> A9[Obtain V squared = rho g by k times R times S]
    A9 --> A10[Define Chezy C as sqrt rho g by k]
    A10 --> A11[Final Result: V = C sqrt R S]
    A11 --> A12[Extend to Discharge: Q = A C sqrt R S]
    A12 --> A13[Engineering Application: Canal and River Design]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Inter-relation of Chezy's $C$ with Other Resistance Formulas

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Darcy Weisbach Equation] --> B1[S equals f V squared by 8gR]
    B1 --> C1[C equals sqrt 8g by f]
    C1 --> D1[Chezy Formula V equals C sqrt RS]
    A2[Manning Equation] --> B2[V equals 1 by n R to power 2/3 S to power 1/2]
    B2 --> C2[C equals 1 by n R to power 1/6]
    C2 --> D1
    A3[Bazin Formula] --> B3[C equals 157.6 by 1.81 plus K by sqrt R]
    B3 --> C3[Empirical Roughness Coefficient K]
    C3 --> D1
    A4[Kutter Formula] --> B4[C equals 23 plus 0.00155 by S plus 1 by n over denominator]
    B4 --> C4[Most General Empirical Expression]
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Application: Q equals A C sqrt RS in Canal and Sewer Design]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to Read the Diagrams:** Each box represents a logical step (input → equation → intermediate result → final form). The arrows show the algebraic flow from the original assumption (left) to the final Chezy form (right). KTU examiners love asking "show that" derivations — mastering these flows is essential.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> State Chezy's formula for the velocity of flow in an open channel. List any **four assumptions** on which it is based.

**Model Answer:**

Chezy's formula states that the mean velocity of flow in an open channel under steady uniform flow conditions is given by:

$$
V = C\sqrt{RS}
$$

where $V$ is the mean velocity, $C$ is Chezy's coefficient, $R$ is the hydraulic radius, and $S$ is the bed slope.

**Assumptions:**
1. The flow is **steady and uniform** in a **prismatic channel**.
2. The **bed slope is small** (so $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = S$).
3. The flow is **turbulent** and the **resistance varies as the square of the velocity**.
4. The **wetted perimeter is fully rough**, so friction is independent of viscosity.

*(Any four valid assumptions for 3 marks; full mark awarded for stating the formula correctly and four well-listed assumptions.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

> Define **hydraulic radius**. Express Chezy's constant $C$ in terms of **(a)** Darcy-Weisbach friction factor $f$, and **(b)** Manning's roughness coefficient $n$.

**Model Answer:**

**Hydraulic Radius:** It is defined as the ratio of the cross-sectional area of flow to the wetted perimeter.

$$
R = \dfrac{A}{P}
$$

**(a)** From Darcy-Weisbach equation, by equating the energy slope to the bed slope:

$$
C = \sqrt{\dfrac{8g}{f}}
$$

**(b)** Comparing Chezy's formula with Manning's equation, we get:

$$
C = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{1/6}
$$

*(For 3 marks: 1 mark for $R$ definition, 1 mark for relation (a), 1 mark for relation (b).)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**QUESTION A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Model Paper Pattern]**

> **(a)** Derive Chezy's formula for the velocity of flow in an open channel from first principles, clearly stating the assumptions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A rectangular channel of width **5 m** carries water at a depth of **2 m**. The bed slope is **1 in 2000**. If Chezy's constant is **50 m$^{1/2}$/s**, calculate the discharge through the channel. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The full derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key steps to write in the answer book are:

1. Consider length $L$ of a prismatic channel with cross-sectional area $A$, wetted perimeter $P$, and bed slope $S$.
2. Weight of water in length $L$: $W = \rho g A L$.
3. Driving force (component along flow): $F_g = W \sin\theta \approx \rho g A L S$ (since slope is small).
4. Resisting shear force: $F_f = \tau_0 \cdot P \cdot L$.
5. For uniform flow, $F_g = F_f$, giving $\tau_0 = \rho g R S$.
6. Chezy's empirical law: $\tau_0 = k V^2$.
7. Equate: $k V^2 = \rho g R S \Rightarrow V^2 = \dfrac{\rho g}{k} R S$.
8. Define $C = \sqrt{\rho g / k}$, hence $V = C\sqrt{RS}$.

*Assumptions (to be listed):* steady, uniform, prismatic channel, small slope, turbulent flow, $R$ constant, friction $\propto V^2$.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Bed width $B = 5$ m
- Depth of flow $y = 2$ m
- Bed slope $S = 1/2000 = 0.0005$
- Chezy's constant $C = 50$ m$^{1/2}$/s

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area $A$:**
For a rectangular channel:

$$
A = B \cdot y = 5 \times 2 = 10 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Wetted perimeter $P$:**

$$
P = B + 2y = 5 + 2(2) = 5 + 4 = 9 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Hydraulic radius $R$:**

$$
R = \dfrac{A}{P} = \dfrac{10}{9} = 1.1111 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Velocity $V$ using Chezy's formula:**

$$
V = C\sqrt{RS} = 50 \times \sqrt{1.1111 \times 0.0005}
$$

Computing inside the square root:

$$
R \times S = 1.1111 \times 0.0005 = 5.5556 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Square root:

$$
\sqrt{5.5556 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.02357
$$

Therefore:

$$
V = 50 \times 0.02357 = 1.1785 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Discharge $Q$:**

$$
Q = A \times V = 10 \times 1.1785 = 11.785 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 11.79 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

---

**QUESTION B. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Model Paper Pattern]**

> **(a)** A trapezoidal channel has a bottom width of **3 m**, side slopes of **1.5 H : 1 V**, and a bed slope of **1 in 1500**. The depth of flow is **1.8 m**. Using Chezy's formula with $C = 55$ m$^{1/2}$/s, calculate the velocity and discharge of flow. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Show that Chezy's constant $C$ is related to Manning's roughness coefficient $n$ by the relation $C = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{1/6}$, where $R$ is the hydraulic radius. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Bottom width $B = 3$ m
- Side slope $m = 1.5$ (H:V ratio)
- Bed slope $S = 1/1500$
- Depth $y = 1.8$ m
- $C = 55$ m$^{1/2}$/s

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area $A$ (trapezoidal):**

$$
A = (B + my) \cdot y = (3 + 1.5 \times 1.8) \times 1.8
$$

$$
A = (3 + 2.7) \times 1.8 = 5.7 \times 1.8 = 10.26 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Wetted perimeter $P$ (trapezoidal):**

$$
P = B + 2y\sqrt{1 + m^2} = 3 + 2(1.8)\sqrt{1 + (1.5)^2}
$$

$$
P = 3 + 3.6 \times \sqrt{1 + 2.25} = 3 + 3.6 \times \sqrt{3.25}
$$

$$
P = 3 + 3.6 \times 1.8028 = 3 + 6.4900 = 9.4900 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Hydraulic radius $R$:**

$$
R = \dfrac{A}{P} = \dfrac{10.26}{9.4900} = 1.0811 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Velocity using Chezy's formula:**

$$
V = C\sqrt{RS} = 55 \times \sqrt{1.0811 \times \dfrac{1}{1500}}
$$

Inside the square root:

$$
R \times S = 1.0811 \times 0.0006667 = 7.2072 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Square root:

$$
\sqrt{7.2072 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.02685
$$

Therefore:

$$
V = 55 \times 0.02685 = 1.4767 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Discharge:**

$$
Q = A \times V = 10.26 \times 1.4767 = 15.15 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{V \approx 1.48 \text{ m/s}, \quad Q \approx 15.15 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

---

**(b) Proof of relation $C = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{1/6}$ (7 marks):**

The full derivation is given in **Section 3.3** of these notes. Summary steps for the answer book:

**Step 1:** Chezy's formula:

$$
V = C \sqrt{R S} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = C \, R^{1/2} S^{1/2}
$$

**Step 2:** Manning's equation (for SI units):

$$
V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}
$$

**Step 3:** Equate the two expressions for $V$:

$$
C \, R^{1/2} S^{1/2} = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}
$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $S^{1/2}$:

$$
C \, R^{1/2} = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3}
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $C$:

$$
C = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot R^{2/3 - 1/2} = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot R^{4/6 - 3/6} = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot R^{1/6}
$$

$$
\boxed{C = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{1/6}} \quad \text{(Proved)}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks on Chezy's Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use SI units for Manning's $n$:** If $n$ is given in FPS units (e.g., 0.025) but the rest of the problem is in SI, you must convert $n_{SI} = n_{FPS} \times 1.486$. Forgetting this can lose **up to 2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing $C$ with Manning's $C$ in other formulas:** Always check whether the problem gives $C$ directly or asks you to compute it from $f$, $n$, $K$, etc. Writing the wrong formula in the first step can cost **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Trapezoidal cross-section algebra errors:** Be careful with the side slope convention. KTU uses $m$ = **horizontal to vertical** (e.g., 1.5 H : 1 V means $m = 1.5$). Mixing $m$ and $1/m$ is a common mistake losing **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting to state assumptions** in derivation questions: A derivation without the assumptions listed loses **at least 1 mark** in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Rounding too early:** Always carry 4–5 significant digits in intermediate steps; round only the final answer to 2–3 decimal places.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Chezy's Formula**

- **Core formula:** $V = C\sqrt{RS}$ and $Q = AC\sqrt{RS}$ — must be memorised.
- **Hydraulic radius $R = A/P$** — know formulas for $A$ and $P$ for **rectangular**, **trapezoidal**, **triangular**, and **circular** sections.
- **SI unit of $C$** is **m$^{1/2}$/s** (NOT dimensionless). $C$ depends on **roughness** of channel surface.
- **Three equivalent expressions for $C$:**
  * From Darcy-Weisbach: $C = \sqrt{8g/f}$
  * From Manning: $C = R^{1/6}/n$
  * From Bazin: $C = 157.6/(1.81 + K/\sqrt{R})$
- **Assumptions** to always list: steady, uniform, prismatic, small slope, turbulent flow, $\tau_0 \propto V^2$.
- **Chezy's formula is valid only for turbulent flow** — not for laminar flow in open channels.
- **Energy slope $\approx$ bed slope** in uniform flow — this is the bridge between Chezy and open channel flow.
- **Trapezoidal wetted perimeter:** $P = B + 2y\sqrt{1 + m^2}$ — KTU's most-tested cross-section.
- **Trapezoidal area:** $A = (B + my)y$.
- **Hydraulic radius most economical** for a trapezoidal channel is $R_{opt} = y/2$ (for $m = 0$, rectangular; in general, related to half-depth).
- **Manning's $n$ values to remember (typical):** smooth cement = 0.012, unfinished concrete = 0.014, earth canal = 0.025, rough stony canal = 0.04.
- **C conversion factor for SI:** Always confirm $n$ is in SI; multiply FPS value of $n$ by **1.486** for SI.

---

*End of Module 3 — Chezy's Formula Notes*
*Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Protocol (2024 Scheme)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Major and Minor Losses in Pipes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Major and Minor Losses in Pipes ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Head Loss (or Energy Loss)** in a pipe is the irreversible loss of mechanical energy (pressure head, velocity head, or elevation head) sustained by a flowing fluid as it moves from one point to another due to viscous dissipation, turbulence, and geometric discontinuities. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the total head loss $h_L$ in a piping system is universally partitioned into two distinct, additive categories: $$ h_L = h_f + h_m $$ where: - $h_f$ = **Major (frictional) loss** — the continuous, distributed energy dissipated by viscous shear along the straight length of a uniform pipe. - $h_m$ = **Minor (local) loss** — the localized, concentrated energy dissipated by flow separation, eddying, and reattachment at fittings, bends, valves, expansions, contractions, and entries/exits. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Terminology Note:** The term "minor" is a historical misnomer. In short industrial pipe runs with many fittings, the *sum* of minor losses can easily exceed the major loss. The KTU examiner expects you to compute **both** rigorously and never assume one is negligible unless explicitly justified. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Road Trip" Intuition) Imagine a long highway journey from City A to City B in a car. - **Major Loss ($h_f$)** corresponds to the **rolling friction on the road surface** itself. The longer the highway (pipe length $L$), the more energy the engine must supply to overcome this distributed resistance. Even on a perfectly straight, smooth road, you burn fuel continuously. - **Minor Losses ($h_m$)** correspond to **specific obstacles** along the route: a toll booth, a speed-breaker, a sharp hairpin bend, a sudden narrow bridge, a roundabout, or exiting onto an unpaved track. Each "event" causes a sudden burst of energy dissipation (braking, acceleration loss, turbulence), and the energy lost at *each* event is proportional to the *square* of the car's speed (just like in fluid mechanics, the loss is proportional to $V^2$). The **total energy spent** by your engine is the sum of continuous rolling friction + losses at every individual obstacle. Similarly, the **total head loss** in a pipe equals the cumulative frictional loss along the length **plus** the losses at every fitting, valve, bend, expansion, and contraction. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (or **9.81 m/s²**) - Kinematic viscosity of water at 20 °C: $\nu \approx 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ - Density of water: $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - Reynolds Number threshold for turbulent flow: $\text{Re} > 4000$ > [!NOTE] > **Why pipes lose energy at all:** Real fluids are **viscous**. Viscosity causes shear stress at the pipe wall. This shear force, acting over the pipe surface, does negative work on the fluid — converting useful mechanical energy (pressure head) directly into heat (thermal energy) that is irretrievably lost to the surroundings. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Major Head Loss (Frictional Loss) The major head loss is governed by the **Darcy–Weisbach equation** — the single most important equation in the entire pipe-flow module and the most-tested formula in KTU university exams. **The Physics Behind It:** Consider a fluid element sliding down an inclined pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$. The driving force is the pressure drop $\Delta P \cdot A$, and the resisting force is the wall shear stress $\tau_0$ acting on the cylindrical surface $\pi D L$. Balancing these forces on a steady, uniform flow: $$ \Delta P \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \tau_0 \cdot \pi D L $$ Solving for $\Delta P$ and dividing by $\rho g$ to get the head loss $h_f$: $$ h_f = \dfrac{4 \tau_0 L}{\rho g D} $$ The friction factor $f$ is defined such that $\tau_0 = f \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{8}$ (this is a *definition*, not an empirical result). Substituting back yields the canonical Darcy–Weisbach form: $$ \boxed{h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}} $$ where: - $f$ = **Darcy friction factor** (dimensionless, $\text{f} \in [0.008, 0.08]$ typically) - $L$ = Length of pipe [m] - $D$ = Internal diameter of pipe [m] - $V$ = Mean velocity of flow [m/s] - $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity [m/s²] > [!IMPORTANT] > **Crucial Distinction:** The Darcy friction factor $f$ is **4 times** the Fanning friction factor used in some American textbooks. KTU and all standard European/Indian engineering texts use **Darcy**. Do not mix the two or your answer will be **wrong by a factor of 4** (worth full marks lost in valuation). ### 2.2 Friction Factor Relationships The friction factor $f$ depends on the **Reynolds Number** ($\text{Re} = \dfrac{VD}{\nu}$) and the **relative roughness** ($\dfrac{\varepsilon}{D}$), where $\varepsilon$ is the surface roughness of the pipe material. **Three Flow Regimes:** 1. **Laminar Flow** ($\text{Re} < 2000$): $$ f = \dfrac{64}{\text{Re}} $$ Friction is purely viscous, independent of roughness. 2. **Turbulent Flow in Smooth Pipes** (Blasius equation, $\text{Re} < 10^5$): $$ f = \dfrac{0.316}{\text{Re}^{0.25}} $$ 3. **Fully Turbulent Flow** (Colebrook–White equation, the most accurate universal correlation): $$ \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{\text{Re}\sqrt{f}}\right] $$ This is implicit in $f$ and usually solved by iteration or read from the **Moody Diagram**. 4. **Complete Turbulence (Fully Rough Zone)** — Churchill equation (universal, explicit): $$ f = 8 \left[\left(\dfrac{8}{\text{Re}}\right)^{12} + \dfrac{1}{(A+B)^{3/2}}\right]^{1/12} $$ with $A = \left[2.457 \ln \dfrac{1}{(7/\text{Re})^{0.9} + 0.27 \varepsilon/D}\right]^{16}$ and $B = \left(\dfrac{37530}{\text{Re}}\right)^{16}$. This equation spans **laminar, transitional, and turbulent** zones and is the safest closed-form choice for KTU numericals. ### 2.3 Minor Head Losses (Local Losses) A local loss is computed as a fraction of the velocity head at the point of disturbance: $$ \boxed{h_m = K \dfrac{V^2}{2g}} $$ where $K$ is a dimensionless **loss coefficient** determined experimentally for each fitting type. **Equivalent Length Method:** Sometimes it is convenient to express a minor loss as an "equivalent length" $L_e$ of straight pipe that would produce the same frictional loss: $$ K \dfrac{V^2}{2g} = f \dfrac{L_e}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad L_e = \dfrac{K D}{f} $$ The total equivalent length of the system is then $L_{eq} = L + \sum L_{e,i}$, and the entire piping system is treated as one straight pipe of that length. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Equivalent length is the *preferred* method in industrial pipe-network design (e.g., HVAC, chemical plants, water supply networks) because it unifies major and minor losses into a single equivalent pipeline, enabling direct use of the Darcy–Weisbach equation for the whole network. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Formula** | **Symbol Definitions** | **Applicable Regime** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | $f$: Darcy friction factor, $L$: length, $D$: diameter, $V$: velocity | All steady pipe flows | $h_f$ in metres | | $f = 64/\text{Re}$ | $\text{Re} = VD/\nu$ | Laminar ($\text{Re} < 2000$) | Dimensionless | | $f = 0.316/\text{Re}^{0.25}$ | Blasius formula | Turbulent, smooth pipes, $\text{Re} < 10^5$ | Dimensionless | | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log\!\left[\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{\text{Re}\sqrt{f}}\right]$ | Colebrook–White | Fully turbulent, rough pipes | Dimensionless | | $h_m = K \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | $K$: loss coefficient, $V$: velocity at fitting | Local losses at fittings, valves, etc. | $h_m$ in metres | | $h_L = h_f + \sum h_m$ | Total head loss | Complete pipe system | Metres | | $L_e = KD/f$ | Equivalent length | Converting $K$ to fictitious pipe length | Metres | | $\Delta P = \rho g h_L$ | Pressure drop equivalent to head loss | Energy equation applications | Pa (N/m²) | | Power lost: $P = \rho g Q h_L$ | $Q$: volumetric flow rate | Pumping power calculations | Watts (W) | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Water Supply Networks (KUWASCO, KWA, Municipal Corporations):** Engineers size pumps and pipe diameters using these loss formulas to ensure adequate pressure at every consumer tap. 2. **Hydraulic Presses & Industrial Hydraulic Systems:** Sizing of return lines, flow-control valves, and heat-exchanger piping in CNC machines uses minor loss coefficients for sharp bends and sudden expansions. 3. **Fire-Fighting Sprinkler Networks (IS 15105, NFPA 13):** Equivalent length method is mandated to compute the available pressure at the most remote sprinkler head. 4. **Oil & Gas Pipeline Design (PNGRB, ASME B31.3):** Long-distance cross-country pipelines use the Colebrook equation (or the Chen approximation in design software) to predict pumping-station spacing. 5. **HVAC & Building Services:** Chilled-water and condenser-water piping systems in Kerala commercial buildings (Kochi Metro stations, IT parks in Trivandrum) use $K$-factor look-up tables for valves and fittings. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Darcy–Weisbach Equation (from first principles) This derivation is a **high-yield KTU long-answer (14-mark) template**. **Step 1 — Control Volume Setup.** Consider a uniform, horizontal pipe of constant diameter $D$ carrying a steady, fully-developed flow over a length $L$. The cross-sectional area is $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ and the wetted perimeter (the cylindrical inner wall) is $P = \pi D$. **Step 2 — Force Balance on Fluid Element.** Apply Newton's second law to the fluid column between sections 1 and 2. For a horizontal pipe with no elevation change, the only net force driving the flow is the pressure difference: $$ F_{net} = (P_1 - P_2) \cdot A = \Delta P \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} $$ **Step 3 — Resistive Wall Shear.** The viscous fluid drags on the pipe wall. The wall shear stress $\tau_0$ (in N/m²) acts tangentially over the entire cylindrical surface $\pi D L$, opposing the motion. The total retarding force is: $$ F_{resist} = \tau_0 \cdot \pi D L $$ **Step 4 — Steady-State Force Balance.** Since the flow is steady (no acceleration), the net force equals the resistive force: $$ \Delta P \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \tau_0 \cdot \pi D L $$ **Step 5 — Solve for Pressure Drop.** $$ \Delta P = \dfrac{4 \tau_0 L}{D} $$ **Step 6 — Convert Pressure Drop to Head Loss.** The head loss $h_f$ in metres of fluid is defined as the pressure drop divided by the specific weight $\rho g$: $$ h_f = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = \dfrac{4 \tau_0 L}{\rho g D} $$ **Step 7 — Define the Darcy Friction Factor.** We define a dimensionless coefficient $f$ that absorbs the wall shear stress: $$ \tau_0 \equiv f \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{8} $$ This definition comes from dimensional analysis: shear stress has units of N/m², and the only combination of $\rho$, $V$ with the right units is $\rho V^2$. The factor 8 is a historical convention that makes the formula collapse to $f = 64/\text{Re}$ in laminar flow. **Step 8 — Substitute $\tau_0$ into the Head Loss Equation.** $$ h_f = \dfrac{4 L}{\rho g D} \cdot \left(f \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{8}\right) $$ **Step 9 — Simplify the Algebra.** $$ h_f = \dfrac{4 f L \rho V^2}{8 \rho g D} = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g} $$ We have arrived at the **Darcy–Weisbach equation**: $$ \boxed{h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this derivation is loved by KTU examiners:** It directly tests the student's ability to (i) draw a control volume, (ii) apply a force balance, (iii) convert a pressure drop to a head loss, and (iv) introduce an empirical coefficient. Skipping any of these steps can cost up to **2 marks** in valuation. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Borda–Carnot Equation for Sudden Expansion Loss This is another **favourite KTU question** (often appearing as a 7-mark sub-part). **Step 1 — Geometry.** A pipe of cross-section $A_1$ carrying mean velocity $V_1$ suddenly expands into a larger pipe of cross-section $A_2$ (velocity $V_2$). The flow separates at the sharp edge, creating a turbulent mixing region. **Step 2 — Apply the Momentum Equation.** Between sections 1 (just inside the small pipe) and 2 (far downstream in the large pipe, where flow is reattached), the momentum flux change equals the net pressure force minus the wall shear: $$ (P_1 - P_2) A_2 = \rho Q (V_1 - V_2) $$ For a sudden expansion, wall shear is negligible on the assumption that the mixing zone is short. Continuity gives $Q = A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$, so: $$ P_1 - P_2 = \rho (V_1 - V_2) V_1 $$ **Step 3 — Apply the Energy Equation (Bernoulli with Loss).** $$ \dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_L $$ **Step 4 — Eliminate the Pressures Using the Momentum Result.** $$ h_L = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} $$ Substituting $P_1 - P_2$ from Step 2: $$ h_L = \dfrac{\rho V_1 (V_1 - V_2)}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} $$ $$ h_L = \dfrac{V_1(V_1 - V_2)}{g} + \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + V_2)}{2g} $$ **Step 5 — Factor Out $(V_1 - V_2)$ and Simplify.** $$ h_L = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{g}\left[V_1 + \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}\right] = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{g} \cdot \dfrac{3V_1 + V_2}{2} $$ **Step 6 — Express in Terms of $V_1$ Only (Final Form).** Using continuity, $V_2 = V_1 \cdot A_1/A_2$: $$ \boxed{h_L = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g} = K \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g}, \quad K = \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "1 minus area ratio squared" formula** is the Borda–Carnot result. It is exact, requires no empirical coefficient, and is the only minor loss formula derivable purely from first principles (momentum + continuity). KTU examiners frequently award a full 7 marks to a clean derivation of this. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration (KTU Exam-Style) > **Problem (Module 3, KTU 2019 Scheme):** Water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\nu = 10^{-6}$ m²/s) flows at a mean velocity of 2.5 m/s through a commercial steel pipe ($\varepsilon = 0.046$ mm) of diameter 100 mm and length 50 m. The pipe has two $90°$ standard elbows ($K = 0.9$ each), one fully open globe valve ($K = 10$), and exits into a reservoir ($K_{exit} = 1.0$). Compute the total head loss and the pumping power required to deliver $Q = 0.02$ m³/s. **Step 1 — Reynolds Number.** $$ \text{Re} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 0.1}{10^{-6}} = 2.5 \times 10^5 \quad (\text{Turbulent}) $$ **Step 2 — Relative Roughness.** $$ \dfrac{\varepsilon}{D} = \dfrac{0.046 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1} = 4.6 \times 10^{-4} $$ **Step 3 — Friction Factor (Colebrook).** Substituting into the Colebrook equation and solving iteratively (or using the Swamee–Jain explicit approximation): $$ f = \dfrac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{\text{Re}^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2} $$ $$ f = \dfrac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4.6 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{(2.5 \times 10^5)^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2} $$ $$ f = \dfrac{0.25}{[\log_{10}(1.243 \times 10^{-4} + 1.063 \times 10^{-4})]^2} = \dfrac{0.25}{[\log_{10}(2.306 \times 10^{-4})]^2} $$ $$ f = \dfrac{0.25}{(-3.637)^2} = \dfrac{0.25}{13.23} \approx 0.0189 $$ **Step 4 — Major Head Loss.** $$ h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g} = 0.0189 \times \dfrac{50}{0.1} \times \dfrac{(2.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81} $$ $$ h_f = 0.0189 \times 500 \times 0.3185 = 3.01 \text{ m} $$ **Step 5 — Sum of Minor Loss Coefficients.** $$ \sum K = 2(0.9) + 10 + 1.0 = 12.8 $$ **Step 6 — Total Minor Head Loss.** $$ h_m = 12.8 \times \dfrac{(2.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 12.8 \times 0.3185 = 4.08 \text{ m} $$ **Step 7 — Total Head Loss.** $$ h_L = h_f + h_m = 3.01 + 4.08 = 7.09 \text{ m} $$ **Step 8 — Pumping Power.** $$ P = \rho g Q h_L = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.02 \times 7.09 = 1391 \text{ W} \approx 1.39 \text{ kW} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** In this example, the minor losses (4.08 m) actually *exceed* the major loss (3.01 m), confirming the earlier warning that "minor" does not mean small. The valve is the dominant contributor. --- ### 3.4 Typical Loss Coefficients ($K$-Values) — Quick Reference Table | **Fitting / Component** | **$K$-value (typical)** | **Source / Notes** | |---|---|---| | Sharp-edged entrance | 0.50 | Borda–Carnot: $K = 0.5$ | | Re-entrant (Borda) entrance | 0.80–1.0 | Pipe projects into reservoir | | Well-rounded entrance | 0.04 | Radius = $0.2 D$ | | Exit to reservoir | 1.0 | Full velocity head lost | | Sudden expansion | $(1 - A_1/A_2)^2$ | **Borda–Carnot, exact** | | Sudden contraction | $0.5(1 - A_2/A_1)$ | Approximate | | $90°$ standard elbow | 0.9 | Threaded, $r/D = 1$ | | $90°$ long-radius elbow | 0.3 | Smooth, $r/D = 3$ | | $45°$ elbow | 0.4 | Standard | | Tee, line flow | 0.6 | Through-run | | Tee, branch flow | 1.8 | Side-branch | | Gate valve, fully open | 0.17 | Smooth bore | | Globe valve, fully open | 10.0 | Highest $K$ among common valves | | Swing check valve | 2.0–2.5 | Depends on design | | Foot valve with strainer | 2.5 | Pump suction | --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Total Head Loss Decomposition ```mermaid graph TD A["Fluid Enters Pipe
