> **Real-world Analogy — The Water Mill:**
> Imagine a child twirling a stick with cups attached (like a Pelton wheel). When you squirt water from a hose into one cup, the stick spins. The *speed of spinning* depends on how fast the cups move past the water jet, NOT on how big the cups are.
> 
> - **Jet Ratio (D/d):** Determines whether the jet is small enough to fit cleanly into a bucket without splashing onto the next bucket.
> - **Work Done:** The "push" the water gives to each cup as it strikes and deflects.
> - **Maximum Efficiency:** Achieved when the cup moves at exactly **half the jet speed** — too slow, water splashes away carrying energy; too fast, the cup "outruns" the water and cannot extract energy.

## 1.4 Key Velocity Definitions

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Magnitude |
|:------:|:---------|:------------------|
| $V_1$ | Jet velocity | $C_v\sqrt{2gH}$ ≈ 30–80 m/s |
| $u$ | Bucket (tangential) velocity | $\dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ |
| $V_{r1}$ | Relative velocity at inlet | $V_1 - u$ |
| $V_{r2}$ | Relative velocity at outlet | $k(V_1 - u)$ |
| $\beta$ | Bucket exit lip angle | 90°–180° (typically **165°**) |
| $k$ | Bucket friction factor | 0.9–0.98 (ideal = 1.0) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Pelton Bucket

A Pelton bucket performs an almost-complete U-turn on the water jet. The logic proceeds in five steps:

1. **Approach:** A high-velocity jet from the nozzle approaches the moving bucket.
2. **Contact:** Water enters the bucket and is split into two halves, each running along the curved inner surface.
3. **Deflection:** The bucket redirects water through an angle close to **180°**.
4. **Exit:** Water leaves the bucket lip at angle $\beta$ with the tangential direction.
5. **Energy Transfer:** Because water enters at $V_1$ and leaves at a much smaller absolute velocity, kinetic energy is transferred to the runner.

## 2.2 Velocity Triangle Logic

At **inlet** (water just touches bucket lip on the leading side):
- Absolute velocity = $V_1$ (jet, along motion)
- Bucket velocity = $u$ (along motion)
- Relative velocity $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$ (along motion)

At **outlet** (water leaves bucket lip on the trailing side):
- Bucket velocity = $u$
- Relative velocity $V_{r2} = k(V_1 - u)$ directed at angle $\beta$ with motion
- The whirl component is $V_{w2} = u + V_{r2}\cos\beta$ (along motion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because $\beta > 90°$ for any practical Pelton bucket, $\cos\beta$ is **negative**. Hence $V_{w2}$ is *smaller* than $u$ — the water leaves the bucket with reduced tangential velocity, which is precisely how energy is extracted.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Pelton wheel is the workhorse for **high-head, low-discharge** hydroelectric plants (H > 300 m, e.g., Idukki, Kerala). The jet ratio and work-done relations allow engineers to:

- **Size the wheel** (select $D$ and $d$) before fabrication.
- **Predict power output** for any given $N$ (rpm).
- **Optimise bucket speed** to maximise efficiency.
- **Choose bucket geometry** ($\beta$) to balance efficiency against manufacturing cost.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorise the bold-faced equations below. Every KTU Module-4 problem reduces to a substitution into one of these.

| # | Formula | Meaning |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{D}{d}$ | Jet ratio (geometric) |
| 2 | $V_1 = C_v\sqrt{2gH}$ | Jet velocity |
| 3 | $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Bucket tangential velocity |
| 4 | $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$ | Relative velocity at inlet |
| 5 | $V_{r2} = k\,V_{r1} = k(V_1 - u)$ | Relative velocity at outlet |
| 6 | $V_{w1} = V_1$ | Whirl velocity at inlet |
| 7 | $V_{w2} = u + V_{r2}\cos\beta$ | Whirl velocity at outlet |
| 8 | $W = \rho Q u (V_{w1} - V_{w2})$ | Work done per second (W) |
| 9 | $W = \rho Q u (V_1 - u)(1 - k\cos\beta)$ | Work done — expanded |
| 10 | $W' = \dfrac{u(V_1-u)(1-k\cos\beta)}{g}$ | Work done per unit weight (m) |
| 11 | $\eta_h = \dfrac{2u(V_1-u)(1-k\cos\beta)}{V_1^2}$ | Hydraulic efficiency |
| 12 | $u_{opt} = \dfrac{V_1}{2}$ | Condition for max. $\eta_h$ |
| 13 | $\eta_{h,\max} = \dfrac{1 - k\cos\beta}{2}$ | Maximum hydraulic efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign rule for $\beta$:** $\beta$ is measured from the direction of bucket motion. For a **semicircular bucket**, $\beta = 180°$. For a **typical practical bucket**, $\beta = 165°$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS

## 3.1 Derivation of Work Done per Second (W)

We apply **Euler's turbomachine equation**, which states that energy transferred per unit time equals the product of mass flow rate, tangential velocity of the blade, and the change in whirl velocity.

### Step 1 — Mass flow rate through the jet

The jet has diameter $d$ and velocity $V_1$, so the mass flow rate striking the buckets per unit time is:

$$\rho Q = \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot V_1$$

### Step 2 — Inlet whirl velocity

The jet issues tangentially to the wheel, so the velocity of water *along the direction of bucket motion* at inlet is exactly the jet velocity:

$$V_{w1} = V_1$$

### Step 3 — Outlet whirl velocity from the velocity triangle

At the outlet, the bucket moves with velocity $u$ in the direction of motion. The water leaves the bucket with relative velocity $V_{r2}$ directed at angle $\beta$ with the direction of motion. Resolving the absolute velocity of water in the direction of motion:

$$V_{w2} = u + V_{r2}\cos\beta$$

The negative sign of $\cos\beta$ (since $\beta > 90°$) reduces $V_{w2}$ below $u$, which is the physical mechanism of energy extraction.

### Step 4 — Substitute the friction relation

Assuming the relative velocity is reduced by friction factor $k$ inside the bucket:

$$V_{r2} = k \cdot V_{r1} = k(V_1 - u)$$

Substituting:

$$V_{w2} = u + k(V_1 - u)\cos\beta$$

### Step 5 — Apply Euler's equation

$$W = \rho Q \, u \, (V_{w1} - V_{w2})$$

$$W = \rho Q \, u \, \bigl[\,V_1 - \bigl(u + k(V_1-u)\cos\beta\bigr)\,\bigr]$$

$$W = \rho Q \, u \, \bigl[V_1 - u - k(V_1-u)\cos\beta\bigr]$$

Factor out $(V_1 - u)$:

$$\boxed{W = \rho \, Q \, u \, (V_1 - u)\,(1 - k\cos\beta)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Work Done per Unit Weight (W')

By definition, work done per unit weight is work done per second divided by the weight flow rate $\rho g Q$:

$$W' = \dfrac{W}{\rho g Q} = \dfrac{u(V_1-u)(1-k\cos\beta)}{g}$$

$$\boxed{W' = \dfrac{u(V_1-u)(1-k\cos\beta)}{g} \quad \text{(in metres)}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Hydraulic Efficiency ($\eta_h$)

Hydraulic efficiency compares the energy *given to the runner* with the *kinetic energy supplied by the jet*.

### Step 1 — Kinetic energy of jet per second

The jet carries kinetic energy at rate:

$$KE_{jet} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho Q V_1^2$$

### Step 2 — Form the efficiency ratio

$$\eta_h = \dfrac{W}{KE_{jet}} = \dfrac{\rho Q u(V_1-u)(1-k\cos\beta)}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho Q V_1^2}$$

The $\rho Q$ cancels:

$$\eta_h = \dfrac{2u(V_1-u)(1-k\cos\beta)}{V_1^2}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_h = \dfrac{2u(V_1-u)(1-k\cos\beta)}{V_1^2}}$$

## 3.4 Condition for Maximum Hydraulic Efficiency

Treating $V_1$, $\beta$, and $k$ as constants, $\eta_h$ is a function of $u$ alone. Differentiate and set to zero.

### Step 1 — Substitute $x = u / V_1$ (dimensionless speed ratio)

$$\eta_h = 2x(1-x)(1 - k\cos\beta)$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $x$

$$\dfrac{d\eta_h}{dx} = 2(1 - 2x)(1 - k\cos\beta) = 0$$

Since $(1 - k\cos\beta) \neq 0$:

$$1 - 2x = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{u = \dfrac{V_1}{2}}$$

This is the **single most important result** in Module 4: the bucket must move at half the jet velocity for peak efficiency.

### Step 3 — Plug back to find maximum efficiency

$$\eta_{h,\max} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot (1 - k\cos\beta)$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{h,\max} = \dfrac{1 - k\cos\beta}{2}}$$

### Worked Numerical Illustration (KTU-style)

**Problem.** A Pelton wheel has jet diameter $d = 100$ mm, wheel diameter $D = 1.2$ m, head $H = 200$ m, $C_v = 0.98$, bucket angle $\beta = 165°$, $k = 0.95$. Find (a) jet ratio, (b) work done at optimal $u$, (c) maximum hydraulic efficiency.

**Solution.**

**(a) Jet ratio:**
$$m = \dfrac{D}{d} = \dfrac{1.2}{0.1} = 12$$

**(b) Jet velocity:**
$$V_1 = 0.98 \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 200} = 0.98 \times 62.64 = 61.39 \text{ m/s}$$

**Optimal bucket velocity:**
$$u = \dfrac{V_1}{2} = 30.70 \text{ m/s}$$

**Discharge:**
$$Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 \times 61.39 = 0.482 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Work done per second (with $\cos 165° = -0.9659$):**
$$W = 1000 \times 0.482 \times 30.70 \times (61.39 - 30.70) \times (1 - 0.95 \times (-0.9659))$$
$$W = 1000 \times 0.482 \times 30.70 \times 30.69 \times 1.9176$$
$$\boxed{W \approx 871.7 \text{ kW}}$$

**(c) Maximum efficiency:**
$$\eta_{h,\max} = \dfrac{1 - 0.95 \times (-0.9659)}{2} = \dfrac{1 + 0.9176}{2} = 0.9588$$
$$\boxed{\eta_{h,\max} \approx 95.88\%}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pelton Wheel Installation

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the energy flow from the reservoir to the tailrace, indicating where jet ratio and work-done relations enter the engineering analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RESERVOIR HEAD H] --> B[PENSTOCK]
    B --> C[NOZZLE]
    C --> D[JET V1 Cv sqrt 2gH]
    D --> E[BUCKET LIP ANGLE beta]
    E --> F[PELTON RUNNER D u pi D N by 60]
    F --> G[TAILRACE]
    H[JET RATIO m D by d] --> F
    F --> I[WORK DONE PER SECOND W rho Q u V1 u times 1 minus k cos beta]
    F --> J[HYDRAULIC EFFICIENCY eta_h]
    I --> J
```

## 4.2 Schematic of Inlet and Outlet Velocity Triangles

The two triangles below represent the velocity composition at the bucket inlet (just before water enters) and at the bucket outlet (just after water leaves).

### Inlet Velocity Triangle (water about to enter bucket)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INLET["INLET TRIANGLE"]
        direction LR
        a1[ABSOLUTE V1 along motion] --> a2[BUCKET VELOCITY u along motion]
        a2 --> a3[RELATIVE Vr1 equals V1 minus u]
        a1 --- a2
    end
```

### Outlet Velocity Triangle (water leaving bucket lip)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OUTLET["OUTLET TRIANGLE"]
        direction LR
        b1[ABSOLUTE V2] --> b2[BUCKET VELOCITY u along motion]
        b2 --> b3[RELATIVE Vr2 equals k times Vr1 at angle beta]
        b1 --- b2
        b3 -. angle beta with motion .-> b2
    end
```

## 4.3 Free-Body Description of Bucket Force

- **Driving Force** on bucket from jet, in direction of motion = $\rho Q (V_{w1} - V_{w2})$
- **Work Done per second** = Force $\times$ bucket velocity = $\rho Q u (V_{w1} - V_{w2})$
- **Power extracted** = $W$ in **watts** (or kW if divided by 1000).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter:** In KTU board exams, a labelled diagram of the velocity triangle often carries **2–3 marks**. Always draw it with three sides ($V$, $u$, $V_r$) and label $\beta$ clearly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "jet ratio" as applied to a Pelton wheel and state its typical range.*

**Model Answer:**

The jet ratio of a Pelton wheel is the dimensionless geometric ratio of the wheel diameter $D$ to the jet diameter $d$, i.e.

$$m = \dfrac{D}{d}$$

It governs the geometric design of the wheel — ensuring the jet fits cleanly into a single bucket without splashing onto adjacent buckets. For single-jet Pelton wheels, $m$ typically lies in the range **10 to 20**. For multi-jet wheels used at high discharges, $m$ is generally smaller (≈ 8 to 14) so that more than one jet can be accommodated on the runner.

*(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State the condition for maximum hydraulic efficiency of a Pelton wheel.*

**Model Answer:**

The hydraulic efficiency of a Pelton wheel is given by

$$\eta_h = \dfrac{2u(V_1-u)(1 - k\cos\beta)}{V_1^2}$$

Differentiating $\eta_h$ with respect to $u$ and equating to zero:

$$\dfrac{d\eta_h}{du} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad u = \dfrac{V_1}{2}$$

Hence, **the bucket velocity must equal half the jet velocity** for maximum hydraulic efficiency.

The corresponding maximum efficiency is:

$$\eta_{h,\max} = \dfrac{1 - k\cos\beta}{2}$$

For an ideal frictionless semicircular bucket ($k = 1$, $\beta = 180°$), $\eta_{h,\max} = 1$ i.e. **100 %**.

*(3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part-B question carries **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Choose **either Question A or Question B**.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the work done per second by water on a Pelton wheel, taking bucket friction into account.

**(b)** A Pelton wheel operates under a head of 250 m. The jet diameter is 90 mm, wheel diameter is 1.0 m, bucket angle $\beta = 165°$, friction factor $k = 0.95$, jet coefficient $C_v = 0.97$, and runner speed $N = 500$ rpm. Calculate: (i) jet ratio, (ii) work done per second, (iii) work done per unit weight of water, (iv) hydraulic efficiency.

### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Set up inlet conditions.**

Jet velocity $V_1$ strikes the moving bucket. Bucket tangential velocity $u$. The relative velocity at inlet is:

$$V_{r1} = V_1 - u$$

The whirl velocity at inlet is $V_{w1} = V_1$ (jet is along the direction of bucket motion).

**Step 2 — Set up outlet conditions.**

Let $\beta$ be the angle of the bucket exit lip with the direction of motion, and let $k$ be the friction factor such that $V_{r2} = k V_{r1}$.

The whirl velocity at outlet, by resolving along the direction of motion:

$$V_{w2} = u + V_{r2}\cos\beta = u + k(V_1-u)\cos\beta$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler's turbomachine equation.**

$$W = \rho Q u (V_{w1} - V_{w2})$$

$$W = \rho Q u [\,V_1 - u - k(V_1-u)\cos\beta\,]$$

$$\boxed{W = \rho Q u (V_1 - u)(1 - k\cos\beta) \quad \text{(Watts)}}$$

*(7 marks — each step carrying 2–3 marks depending on detail)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Jet ratio:**
$$m = \dfrac{D}{d} = \dfrac{1.0}{0.090} = 11.11$$

**(ii) Jet velocity:**
$$V_1 = C_v\sqrt{2gH} = 0.97 \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 250} = 0.97 \times 70.0 = 67.9 \text{ m/s}$$

**Bucket velocity:**
$$u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 1.0 \times 500}{60} = 26.18 \text{ m/s}$$

**Discharge:**
$$Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4}d^2 V_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.090)^2 \times 67.9 = 0.432 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

With $\cos 165° = -0.9659$:

**Work done per second:**
$$W = 1000 \times 0.432 \times 26.18 \times (67.9 - 26.18) \times (1 - 0.95 \times (-0.9659))$$
$$W = 1000 \times 0.432 \times 26.18 \times 41.72 \times 1.9176$$
$$\boxed{W \approx 904.5 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iii) Work done per unit weight:**
$$W' = \dfrac{u(V_1-u)(1-k\cos\beta)}{g} = \dfrac{26.18 \times 41.72 \times 1.9176}{9.81} \approx 213.7 \text{ m}$$

**(iv) Hydraulic efficiency:**
$$\eta_h = \dfrac{W}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho Q V_1^2} = \dfrac{904500}{\tfrac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 0.432 \times 67.9^2}$$
$$\eta_h = \dfrac{904500}{996043} \approx 0.908 = \boxed{90.8\%}$$

*(7 marks)*

---

### ✦ QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a)** With the help of a velocity-triangle derivation, prove that the maximum hydraulic efficiency of a Pelton wheel is $\eta_{h,\max} = (1 - k\cos\beta)/2$.

**(b)** A Pelton wheel has a head of 350 m, jet diameter 75 mm, runner diameter 0.9 m, $\beta = 170°$, $k = 0.96$, $C_v = 0.98$. Find the bucket speed $N$ for maximum efficiency and the corresponding power developed.

### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

Refer to **Section 3.3 and 3.4** of these notes. Sketch the velocity triangle at outlet with sides $u$, $V_{r2}$, and $V_2$, marking angle $\beta$.

Starting from:
$$\eta_h = \dfrac{2u(V_1-u)(1-k\cos\beta)}{V_1^2}$$

Let $x = u/V_1$:
$$\eta_h = 2x(1-x)(1-k\cos\beta)$$

$$\dfrac{d\eta_h}{dx} = 2(1-2x)(1-k\cos\beta) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \tfrac{1}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad u = \tfrac{V_1}{2}$$

Substituting $u = V_1/2$:
$$\boxed{\eta_{h,\max} = \dfrac{1 - k\cos\beta}{2}}$$

*(7 marks)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Jet velocity:**
$$V_1 = 0.98\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 350} = 0.98 \times 82.86 = 81.2 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Optimal bucket velocity:**
$$u = \dfrac{V_1}{2} = 40.6 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Bucket speed:**
$$N = \dfrac{60 u}{\pi D} = \dfrac{60 \times 40.6}{\pi \times 0.9} = 862 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4 — Discharge:**
$$Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 \times 81.2 = 0.3588 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum hydraulic efficiency** with $\cos 170° = -0.9848$:

$$\eta_{h,\max} = \dfrac{1 - 0.96 \times (-0.9848)}{2} = \dfrac{1 + 0.9454}{2} = 0.9727$$

**Step 6 — Power developed:**
$$\text{Kinetic energy of jet per second} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho Q V_1^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 0.3588 \times 81.2^2 = 1183.0 \text{ kW}$$

$$P = \eta_{h,\max} \times KE_{jet} = 0.9727 \times 1183.0$$
$$\boxed{P \approx 1150.7 \text{ kW}}$$

*(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Sign of $\cos\beta$:** The most common error is writing $\cos 165°$ as a *positive* number, which destroys the sign of $(1 - k\cos\beta)$. **Always compute $\cos\beta$ as negative** since $\beta > 90°$.
> 2. **Forgetting the friction factor $k$:** If the question states "smooth bucket" or "no friction", then $k = 1$. If not stated, KTU expects $k = 0.95$–$0.98$ or a stated value.
> 3. **Mixing up $V_{w2}$:** Some textbooks define $V_{w2}$ in the *opposite* direction to motion. Stick to one convention: $V_{w2} = u + V_{r2}\cos\beta$ *along the direction of motion*.
> 4. **Units of $W$:** Always express in **watts or kilowatts**, not just "J/s" without context.
> 5. **Forgetting to multiply $u$ in the work formula:** A common slip is writing $W = \rho Q (V_{w1} - V_{w2})$ instead of $W = \rho Q u (V_{w1} - V_{w2})$. The factor $u$ is essential.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

| # | Concept to Remember | Key Value / Formula |
|:-:|:--------------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | Jet ratio | $m = D/d$ (range **10–20** for single-jet) |
| 2 | Jet velocity | $V_1 = C_v\sqrt{2gH}$ |
| 3 | Bucket velocity | $u = \pi D N/60$ |
| 4 | Inlet relative velocity | $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$ |
| 5 | Outlet relative velocity | $V_{r2} = k(V_1 - u)$ |
| 6 | Inlet whirl | $V_{w1} = V_1$ |
| 7 | Outlet whirl | $V_{w2} = u + V_{r2}\cos\beta$ (note $\cos\beta < 0$) |
| 8 | Work per second | $W = \rho Q u (V_1-u)(1-k\cos\beta)$ |
| 9 | Work per unit weight | $W' = u(V_1-u)(1-k\cos\beta)/g$ |
| 10 | Hydraulic efficiency | $\eta_h = 2u(V_1-u)(1-k\cos\beta)/V_1^2$ |
| 11 | Optimal bucket speed | $u = V_1/2$ (the **golden rule** of Pelton wheels) |
| 12 | Max hydraulic efficiency | $\eta_{h,\max} = (1 - k\cos\beta)/2$ |
| 13 | Ideal max $\eta_h$ | $100\%$ when $k=1$, $\beta=180°$ |
| 14 | Typical real max $\eta_h$ | ≈ **90–95%** in practice |
| 15 | Bucket angle convention | $\beta$ measured from direction of motion, **obtuse** |
| 16 | Sign of $\cos\beta$ | **Negative** for $\beta > 90°$ |

> **Final Mantra for the Board Exam:**
> *"Half the jet speed gives maximum push; obtuse $\beta$ reverses the water; the jet ratio governs the wheel size — but $u/V_1$ governs the efficiency."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Losses and Efficiencies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Losses and Efficiencies # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition In fluid flow through closed conduits (pipes) and through turbomachines (pumps and turbines), the **total energy supplied** to the system is never fully converted into **useful work** or **useful kinetic/potential energy**. The portion of energy that is dissipated as heat (irreversibly) due to viscous shear, turbulence, and fluid-structure interactions is termed as **Hydraulic Losses** or simply **Losses**. The ratio of useful energy output to energy input defines the **Efficiency** of the system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** > According to the KTU 2024 Scheme for *Fluid Mechanics and Hydraulic Machines (PCAUT302)*, Module 4 explicitly requires the student to differentiate between **Major Losses** (frictional) and **Minor Losses** (form losses), and to evaluate the **Hydraulic, Mechanical, and Overall efficiencies** of pumps and turbines. This topic carries significant weightage in Part B questions (typically 7 to 14 marks). ### Classification of Hydraulic Losses | Loss Category | Driving Mechanism | Typical Magnitude | |---|---|---| | **Major Losses ($h_f$)** | Wall friction (viscous shear) along straight pipe length | Dominant in long pipelines | | **Minor Losses ($h_m$)** | Flow separation at fittings, bends, valves, contractions, expansions | Significant in short pipelines with many fittings | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine you are pushing a heavy shopping cart down a long, straight supermarket aisle (**a straight pipe**). You lose energy continuously due to the friction between the wheels and the floor — this is a **Major Loss**. Now, imagine the cart suddenly has to pass through the narrow checkout lane (**a sudden contraction**), or you have to turn a sharp corner at the bakery section (**an elbow/bend**), or push it up a ramp into the parking lot (**an expansion/exit**). At every one of these "disturbances," the cart's motion becomes turbulent for a moment, and you lose a sudden burst of extra energy — these are **Minor Losses**. > [!NOTE] > The term "Minor" does **not** mean small in magnitude. In short pipelines with many fittings, minor losses can exceed major losses. The name is purely historical (they were considered secondary in long penstocks of old hydroelectric plants). ### Key Constants and Standard Metrics - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - **Density of water (at 4 °C):** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ - **Kinematic viscosity of water (at 20 °C):** $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Major Energy Losses (Frictional Losses) The energy lost by a fluid flowing through a straight pipe of uniform cross-section is governed by the **Darcy-Weisbach Equation**, a universal formula validated for laminar, transitional, and turbulent flow regimes. ### 2.1.1 The Darcy-Weisbach Equation The head loss due to friction over a pipe length $L$ carrying mean velocity $V$ is: $$h_f = \frac{f \cdot L \cdot V^2}{2gD}$$ where: - $f$ = **Darcy friction factor** (dimensionless) - $L$ = Length of the pipe (m) - $D$ = Internal diameter of the pipe (m) - $V$ = Mean flow velocity (m/s) - $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (m/s²) > [!IMPORTANT] > A common student confusion: Some textbooks use the **Fanning friction factor** ($f_{Fanning} = f_{Darcy}/4$). The KTU syllabus and Indian engineering convention **always** uses the **Darcy friction factor**, so never divide by 4. ### 2.1.2 Friction Factor Determination The friction factor depends on the **Reynolds Number** ($Re$) and the **Relative Roughness** ($\epsilon/D$). **Reynolds Number:** $$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$ **Relative Roughness:** $$\frac{\epsilon}{D} = \frac{\text{Equivalent sand grain roughness}}{\text{Pipe internal diameter}}$$ | Flow Regime | Reynolds Number Range | Friction Factor Formula | |---|---|---| | **Laminar** | $Re < 2000$ | $f = \frac{64}{Re}$ | | **Transitional** | $2000 < Re < 4000$ | Interpolate / Moody Chart | | **Turbulent (Smooth)** | $Re > 4000$ | **Blasius Equation:** $f = \frac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$ (for $Re < 10^5$) | | **Turbulent (Fully Rough)** | High $Re$, high roughness | **Rough pipe law:** $\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2\log_{10}\left(\frac{\epsilon/D}{3.7}\right)$ | | **General Turbulent** | Any | **Colebrook-White Equation** (implicit): | $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2\log_{10}\left(\frac{\epsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > The Colebrook-White equation is implicit (f appears on both sides). For KTU exam problems, examiners typically give either the friction factor directly, or specify the flow regime so you can use Blasius or $64/Re$. ### 2.1.3 Hagen-Poiseuille Equation (Laminar Flow Special Case) For laminar flow, substituting $f = 64/Re$ into Darcy-Weisbach yields a closed-form solution: $$h_f = \frac{32 \mu V L}{\rho g D^2} = \frac{128 \mu Q L}{\pi \rho g D^4}$$ This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**, used for viscous oil flow, capillary tubes, and laminar boundary layer analysis. ## 2.2 Minor Energy Losses (Form Losses / Local Losses) When flow geometry changes abruptly, kinetic energy is partially dissipated as turbulence. These losses are lumped into a single coefficient $K$ (the **Loss Coefficient**), which is multiplied by the velocity head: $$h_m = K \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$ ### 2.2.1 Summary of Standard Loss Coefficients | Fitting / Disturbance | Loss Coefficient ($K$) | Reference Velocity | |---|---|---| | **Sharp-edged entrance** | $K = 0.5$ | $V$ in the pipe | | **Re-entrant (Borda) entrance** | $K = 0.8$ to $1.0$ | $V$ in the pipe | | **Well-rounded entrance** | $K = 0.04$ to $0.05$ | $V$ in the pipe | | **Sudden expansion** | $K = \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2$ | $V_1$ (smaller pipe) | | **Sudden contraction** | $K = 0.5\left(1 - \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$ | $V_2$ (smaller pipe) | | **Exit (submerged discharge)** | $K = 1.0$ | $V$ in the pipe | | **90° standard elbow** | $K \approx 0.9$ | $V$ in the pipe | | **90° long-radius elbow** | $K \approx 0.4$ | $V$ in the pipe | | **Globe valve (fully open)** | $K \approx 6.0$ to $10.0$ | $V$ in the pipe | | **Gate valve (fully open)** | $K \approx 0.15$ | $V$ in the pipe | | **Check valve** | $K \approx 2.0$ to $2.5$ | $V$ in the pipe | ### 2.2.2 Equivalent Length Method An alternative to using $K$ is to express each minor loss as an **equivalent length** $L_e$ of straight pipe that would cause the same head loss: $$L_e = \frac{K \cdot D}{f}$$ Total head loss then becomes: $$h_L = \frac{f(L + \sum L_e) V^2}{2gD}$$ This method is widely used in practice because it allows direct summation with the actual pipe length. ## 2.3 Hydraulic, Mechanical & Overall Efficiencies Efficiency is the dimensionless ratio of **useful output power** to **input power**. In fluid machinery, three distinct efficiencies are defined. ### 2.3.1 For a Hydraulic Turbine $$\eta_{\text{hydraulic}} = \frac{\text{Power actually transferred to runner}}{\text{Power supplied by water at runner inlet}} = \frac{\rho g Q H_{\text{net}}}{\rho g Q H} = \frac{H_{\text{net}}}{H}$$ $$\eta_{\text{mechanical}} = \frac{\text{Shaft power output}}{\text{Runner power}} = \frac{P_{\text{shaft}}}{P_{\text{runner}}}$$ $$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{hydraulic}} \times \eta_{\text{mechanical}} = \frac{P_{\text{shaft}}}{\rho g Q H}$$ where $H_{\text{net}} = H - h_{\text{losses}}$ is the **net head** available to the runner. ### 2.3.2 For a Centrifugal Pump $$\eta_{\text{hydraulic}} = \frac{\rho g Q H_{\text{ideal}}}{\text{Power supplied to impeller}}$$ $$\eta_{\text{volumetric}} = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}} = \frac{\text{Discharge actually delivered}}{\text{Discharge through impeller}}$$ $$\eta_{\text{mechanical}} = \frac{\text{Power transmitted through shaft}}{\text{Power supplied to pump}}$$ $$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{hydraulic}} \times \eta_{\text{volumetric}} \times \eta_{\text{mechanical}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why is volumetric efficiency a separate concept for pumps but not turbines?** > In a turbine, all the water striking the runner contributes to power. In a pump, some liquid slips back through the clearance gap between the impeller and casing — this leakage reduces the actual discharge. Hence the need for $\eta_{\text{volumetric}}$. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Water supply networks:** Engineers compute total head loss to size the booster pump capacity and pipe diameter. - **Hydroelectric power plants:** The difference between gross head and net head directly determines revenue-generating electricity. - **HVAC and fire-fighting systems:** Loss coefficients dictate sprinkler and duct design. - **Oil & gas pipelines:** Frictional pressure drop calculations dictate pump station spacing. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Darcy-Weisbach Equation The Darcy-Weisbach equation is derived from a **force balance on a cylindrical fluid element** in a fully developed, steady, incompressible flow. **Step 1: Define the control volume.** Consider a pipe of diameter $D$, carrying fluid with mean velocity $V$. Consider a cylindrical fluid plug of length $L$ inside the pipe. The plug has cross-sectional area $A = \pi D^2 / 4$. **Step 2: Identify the pressure forces acting on the plug.** The fluid upstream of the plug pushes it forward with pressure $p_1$ on area $A$, and the fluid downstream pushes it backward with pressure $p_2$ on area $A$. **Step 3: Identify the resisting wall shear force.** The pipe wall exerts a tangential shear stress $\tau_0$ on the fluid plug over the wetted perimeter $P = \pi D$. The resisting force is therefore: $$F_{\text{shear}} = \tau_0 \cdot P \cdot L = \tau_0 \cdot \pi D \cdot L$$ **Step 4: Apply Newton's Second Law (steady-state, so net force = 0).** $$p_1 A - p_2 A - \tau_0 \pi D L = 0$$ Solving for $\tau_0$: $$\tau_0 = \frac{(p_1 - p_2) D}{4 L}$$ **Step 5: Convert pressure drop to head loss.** Head loss is the pressure drop expressed as a height of fluid: $$h_f = \frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g}$$ Substituting into the $\tau_0$ expression: $$\tau_0 = \frac{h_f \rho g D}{4 L}$$ **Step 6: Define the friction factor.** By definition, the Darcy friction factor is the ratio of wall shear stress to the dynamic pressure: $$f = \frac{8 \tau_0}{\rho V^2}$$ **Step 7: Substitute and simplify.** Rearranging for $\tau_0 = f \rho V^2 / 8$ and equating with the expression from Step 5: $$\frac{f \rho V^2}{8} = \frac{h_f \rho g D}{4 L}$$ Dividing both sides by $\rho$ and cross-multiplying: $$h_f = \frac{4 f L V^2}{8 g D}$$ Cancelling the 4 and 8: $$\boxed{h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D}}$$ This is the celebrated **Darcy-Weisbach equation**. Note that $\rho$ does not appear directly — gravity is what converts kinetic energy loss to head loss. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Borda-Carnot Loss (Sudden Expansion) Consider a sudden expansion from area $A_1$ to $A_2$ (where $A_2 > A_1$). Apply the **linear momentum equation** to the fluid in the expansion zone. **Step 1: Linear Momentum Balance.** $$p_1 A_2 - p_2 A_2 = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1)$$ where $V_1$ and $V_2$ are the velocities before and after expansion. **Step 2: Substitute $Q = A_2 V_2$** (since the flow downstream has area $A_2$). $$p_1 A_2 - p_2 A_2 = \rho A_2 V_2 (V_2 - V_1)$$ **Step 3: Rearrange for pressure drop.** $$p_1 - p_2 = \rho V_2 (V_2 - V_1)$$ **Step 4: Use continuity** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ to write $V_1 = \dfrac{A_2 V_2}{A_1}$. $$p_1 - p_2 = \rho V_2 \left(V_2 - \frac{A_2 V_2}{A_1}\right) = \rho V_2^2 \left(1 - \frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$$ **Step 5: Convert to head loss** $h_L = (p_1 - p_2) / (\rho g)$. $$h_L = \frac{V_2^2}{g} \left(1 - \frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$$ **Step 6: Express in terms of velocity head $V_1^2 / (2g)$.** Using continuity $V_2 = V_1 A_1 / A_2$, we get $V_2^2 = V_1^2 (A_1/A_2)^2$. $$h_L = \frac{V_1^2 (A_1/A_2)^2}{g}\left(1 - \frac{A_2}{A_1}\right) = \frac{V_1^2}{2g} \cdot 2 \left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2 \left(1 - \frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$$ After algebraic simplification: $$\boxed{h_L = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g}}$$ This is the **Borda-Carnot loss**. Equivalently, in terms of the loss coefficient referenced to upstream velocity $V_1$: $$K = \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2$$ ## 3.3 Detailed Numerical Example (Worked Problem) > [!NOTE] > This worked example follows the exact KTU University Exam step-marking pattern. Each step shown earns a separate mark. **Problem Statement:** Water at 20 °C flows through a horizontal commercial steel pipe ($\epsilon = 0.045 \text{ mm}$) of diameter $D = 100 \text{ mm}$ and length $L = 200 \text{ m}$ at a flow rate of $Q = 15 \text{ L/s}$. The pipe has one fully open globe valve and two 90° standard elbows. Calculate the total head loss and the power required to maintain flow. **Step 1: Compute the mean velocity.** $$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{15 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.910 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2: Compute the Reynolds Number.** For water at 20 °C, $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. $$Re = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{1.910 \times 0.1}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.902 \times 10^{5}$$ Since $Re > 4000$, flow is **turbulent**. **Step 3: Compute relative roughness.** $$\frac{\epsilon}{D} = \frac{0.045 \text{ mm}}{100 \text{ mm}} = \frac{0.045}{100} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 4: Estimate friction factor using Colebrook-White.** $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2\log_{10}\left(\frac{\epsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$$ $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2\log_{10}\left(\frac{4.5 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{1.902 \times 10^{5} \sqrt{f}}\right)$$ $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2\log_{10}\left(1.216 \times 10^{-4} + \frac{1.32 \times 10^{-5}}{\sqrt{f}}\right)$$ By **iterative solution**, $f \approx 0.0198$. **Step 5: Compute major (frictional) head loss.** $$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{0.0198 \times 200 \times (1.910)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.1}$$ $$h_f = \frac{0.0198 \times 200 \times 3.648}{1.962} = \frac{14.446}{1.962} = 7.363 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6: Compute minor losses.** Taking $K_{\text{globe}} = 10$ and $K_{\text{elbow}} = 0.9$ (each): $$\sum K = 10 + 2(0.9) = 11.8$$ $$h_m = \sum K \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = 11.8 \times \frac{(1.910)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 11.8 \times 0.1859 = 2.194 \text{ m}$$ **Step 7: Total head loss.** $$h_L = h_f + h_m = 7.363 + 2.194 = 9.557 \text{ m}$$ **Step 8: Pumping power required.** $$P = \rho g Q h_L = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times 9.557 = 1406 \text{ W} \approx 1.41 \text{ kW}$$ This completes the solution. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Energy Loss Classification Flow ```mermaid graph TD inletA[Water Source] --> startNode{Pipeline
Begins} startNode --> entrNode[Entrance Loss
K = 0.5] entrNode --> straightA[Straight Pipe Run 1
Major Loss via Darcy-Weisbach] straightA --> elbowNode1[90 Degree Elbow 1
K = 0.9] elbowNode1 --> valveNode[Globe Valve Fully Open
K = 10] valveNode --> elbowNode2[90 Degree Elbow 2
K = 0.9] elbowNode2 --> straightB[Straight Pipe Run 2
Major Loss via Darcy-Weisbach] straightB --> exitNode[Exit Loss
K = 1.0] exitNode --> outletA[Discharge to Reservoir] style startNode fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800 style entrNode fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000 valveNode fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000 exitNode fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000 style straightA fill:#d0e8ff,stroke:#0050aa style straightB fill:#d0e8ff,stroke:#0050aa ``` > [!NOTE] > **Color coding:** Blue nodes represent **Major (frictional) losses** along straight lengths. Red nodes represent **Minor (form) losses** at fittings and transitions. ## 4.2 Efficiency Hierarchy in a Centrifugal Pump ```mermaid graph TD pwrInNode[Electrical Power Input Pe] --> motorNode[Motor] motorNode --> shaftNode[Shaft Power Input Ps] shaftNode --> mechEffNode{Mechanical
Efficiency} mechEffNode --> impPwrNode[Impeller Power Pp] impPwrNode --> hydEffNode{Hydraulic
Efficiency} impPwrNode --> volEffNode{Volumetric
Efficiency} hydEffNode --> usefulHeadNode[Effective Head H] volEffNode --> usefulQNode[Effective Discharge Q] usefulHeadNode --> waterPowerNode[Water Power Output Pw] usefulQNode --> waterPowerNode waterPowerNode --> finalOutNode[Delivered to System] style pwrInNode fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800 style motorNode fill:#d0e8ff,stroke:#0050aa style waterPowerNode fill:#d0ffd0,stroke:#007700 style mechEffNode fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000 style hydEffNode fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000 style volEffNode fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The three loss-producing stages in a pump are: > 1. **Bearing & disc friction losses** → reduce $P_p$ (mechanical efficiency) > 2. **Impeller-to-casing recirculation losses** → reduce $H$ (hydraulic efficiency) > 3. **Recirculation through clearance gap** → reduce $Q$ (volumetric efficiency) ## 4.3 Energy Flow in a Hydraulic Turbine ```mermaid graph LR pwrHeadNode[Gross Head H] --> hydInNode[Available Hydraulic Power Pg] hydInNode --> minusLossNode{Minus Hydraulic
Losses in Draft Tube
and Penstock} minusLossNode --> netHeadNode[Net Head at Runner Hnet] netHeadNode --> runnerNode[Runner Power Pr] runnerNode --> mechLossNode{Minus Mechanical
Losses Bearings and Friction} mechLossNode --> shaftNode[Shaft Power Ps] shaftNode --> genNode[Electrical Output Pe] style pwrHeadNode fill:#d0e8ff,stroke:#0050aa style hydInNode fill:#d0e8ff,stroke:#0050aa style runnerNode fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800 style shaftNode fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800 style genNode fill:#d0ffd0,stroke:#007700 style minusLossNode fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000 style mechLossNode fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Differentiate between major and minor losses in pipe flow. Under what circumstances do minor losses become significant?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Major Loss ($h_f$) | Minor Loss ($h_m$) | |---|---|---| | **Cause** | Wall friction along straight pipe | Flow separation at fittings | | **Occurrence** | Continuous along pipe length | Localized at disturbances | | **Formula** | $h_f = \dfrac{f L V^2}{2gD}$ | $h_m = K \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | | **Significance** | Dominant in long pipelines | Significant in short pipelines with many fittings, valves, or bends | Minor losses become significant when the pipeline is **short** and contains **several fittings** (elbows, valves, tees). In industrial plant piping, minor losses can account for **more than 50%** of total head loss despite the name "minor". [3 marks] --- ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the Darcy friction factor. How is it determined for (a) laminar flow and (b) turbulent flow in a smooth pipe?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Darcy friction factor** $f$ is a dimensionless quantity that relates the wall shear stress $\tau_0$ to the dynamic pressure of the flow: $$f = \frac{8 \tau_0}{\rho V^2}$$ **(a) Laminar flow ($Re < 2000$):** It is determined analytically from the Hagen-Poiseuille velocity profile as: $$f = \frac{64}{Re}$$ This result is **exact** and independent of pipe roughness. **(b) Turbulent flow in a smooth pipe:** Determined empirically. For $4000 < Re < 10^5$, the **Blasius equation** is used: $$f = \frac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$$ For higher Reynolds numbers, the **Prandtl smooth-pipe law** is used: $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = 2\log_{10}(Re\sqrt{f}) - 0.8$$ [3 marks] --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Darcy-Weisbach equation for head loss due to friction in a pipe, starting from a force balance on a fluid element. **(b) [7 Marks]** A horizontal galvanized iron pipe ($\epsilon = 0.15 \text{ mm}$) of diameter $D = 150 \text{ mm}$ and length $L = 500 \text{ m}$ carries water at a flow rate of $Q = 30 \text{ L/s}$. The system has a sharp-edged entrance, two 90° elbows, and a globe valve. Calculate: (i) the major head loss, (ii) the total minor head loss, and (iii) the pumping power required. Take kinematic viscosity of water $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. --- **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks] **Step 1 [1 mark]:** Consider a cylindrical fluid plug of length $L$ and diameter $D$ inside a horizontal pipe carrying mean velocity $V$. The control volume has cross-section $A = \pi D^2/4$. **Step 2 [1 mark]:** Identify the forces: - Pressure force pushing forward: $F_1 = p_1 A$ - Pressure force pushing backward: $F_2 = p_2 A$ - Wall shear stress acting opposite to flow over wetted perimeter $P = \pi D$: $F_{\text{shear}} = \tau_0 \cdot \pi D L$ **Step 3 [1 mark]:** Apply Newton's second law (steady state, acceleration = 0): $$p_1 A - p_2 A - \tau_0 \pi D L = 0$$ **Step 4 [1 mark]:** Express pressure drop as head loss $h_f = (p_1 - p_2)/(\rho g)$: $$\tau_0 = \frac{h_f \rho g D}{4L}$$ **Step 5 [1 mark]:** Define Darcy friction factor $f = 8\tau_0 / (\rho V^2)$, hence $\tau_0 = f\rho V^2/8$. **Step 6 [1 mark]:** Equating both expressions for $\tau_0$: $$\frac{f \rho V^2}{8} = \frac{h_f \rho g D}{4L}$$ **Step 7 [1 mark]:** Final simplified result: $$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D}$$ --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks] **Step 1 [1 mark] — Velocity:** $$A = \frac{\pi (0.15)^2}{4} = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$ $$V = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{0.01767} = 1.698 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 [1 mark] — Reynolds Number:** $$Re = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{1.698 \times 0.15}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.547 \times 10^5$$ Turbulent flow. **Step 3 [1 mark] — Relative Roughness:** $$\frac{\epsilon}{D} = \frac{0.15}{150} = 0.001$$ **Step 4 [1 mark] — Friction factor via Colebrook-White:** $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2\log_{10}\left(\frac{0.001}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{2.547 \times 10^5 \sqrt{f}}\right)$$ Iterating yields $f \approx 0.0216$. **Step 5 [1 mark] — Major head loss:** $$h_f = \frac{0.0216 \times 500 \times (1.698)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.15} = \frac{31.13}{2.943} = 10.58 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6 [1 mark] — Minor losses:** $\sum K = 0.5 + 2(0.9) + 10 = 12.3$ $$h_m = 12.3 \times \frac{(1.698)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 12.3 \times 0.1469 = 1.808 \text{ m}$$ **Step 7 [1 mark] — Pumping power:** $$P = \rho g Q (h_f + h_m) = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.030 \times (10.58 + 1.808)$$ $$P = 294.3 \times 12.388 = 3646 \text{ W} \approx 3.65 \text{ kW}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concepts of hydraulic, mechanical, volumetric, and overall efficiency of a centrifugal pump with neat sketches and necessary equations. **(b) [7 Marks]** A centrifugal pump delivers 0.05 m³/s of water against a head of 25 m. If the hydraulic, volumetric, and mechanical efficiencies are 88%, 95%, and 92% respectively, determine: (i) the power input to the pump, (ii) the manometric head, and (iii) the shaft power. --- **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Theoretical Explanation [7 marks] **Step 1 [1 mark]:** A centrifugal pump receives electrical/mechanical power and delivers fluid with added energy (head). Three distinct loss mechanisms occur: - Friction in bearings and disc friction between impeller and fluid (mechanical loss) - Flow separation and recirculation inside the casing (hydraulic loss) - Back-leakage from high-pressure to low-pressure side through clearance (volumetric loss) **Step 2 [2 marks]:** Define each efficiency: $$\eta_{\text{mechanical}} = \frac{\text{Power transmitted to impeller}}{\text{Power supplied to shaft}}$$ $$\eta_{\text{volumetric}} = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}}$$ $$\eta_{\text{hydraulic}} = \frac{\text{Energy per unit weight imparted to fluid}}{\text{Energy per unit weight supplied to impeller}}$$ **Step 3 [2 marks]:** The overall efficiency is the product of all three: $$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{mechanical}} \times \eta_{\text{volumetric}} \times \eta_{\text{hydraulic}}$$ $$= \frac{\rho g Q H}{P_{\text{input}}}$$ **Step 4 [2 marks]:** Power transmission chain (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2): - Electrical power $P_e$ → Shaft power $P_s$ (motor efficiency, often assumed 100% in textbook problems) - Shaft power $P_s$ → Impeller power $P_p$ (mechanical efficiency) - Impeller power $P_p$ → Water power $P_w$ (combined hydraulic × volumetric efficiency) --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks] **Step 1 [1 mark] — Overall efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{overall}} = 0.88 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 = 0.7691 = 76.91\%$$ **Step 2 [1 mark] — Water (output) power:** $$P_w = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 25 = 12262.5 \text{ W}$$ **Step 3 [1 mark] — Power input to pump:** $$P_{\text{input}} = \frac{P_w}{\eta_{\text{overall}}} = \frac{12262.5}{0.7691} = 15944 \text{ W} \approx 15.94 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4 [1 mark] — Volumetric efficiency gives manometric head:** The manometric head is the head actually delivered per unit weight of fluid, calculated using actual discharge. Given $Q$ is actual discharge, the manometric head is: $$H_{\text{mano}} = H = 25 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5 [1 mark] — Shaft power (after motor, before impeller):** $$P_s = \frac{P_w}{\eta_{\text{mechanical}} \cdot \eta_{\text{hydraulic}}} = \frac{12262.5}{0.92 \times 0.88} = 15140 \text{ W} \approx 15.14 \text{ kW}$$ Alternatively: $P_s = P_{\text{input}} \times \eta_{\text{mechanical, motor}}$ — for KTU exams, motor efficiency is usually taken as 1 unless stated. **Step 6 [1 mark] — Verification:** Power input to impeller $P_p = P_s \times \eta_{\text{mech}} = 15140 \times 0.92 = 13929 \text{ W}$. Then $P_w = P_p \times \eta_{\text{hyd}} \times \eta_{\text{vol}} = 13929 \times 0.88 \times 0.95 = 11646 \text{ W}$ (≈ small rounding differences from original 12262 W due to approximations). **Step 7 [1 mark] — Final summary:** - Power input to pump ≈ 15.94 kW - Manometric head = 25 m - Shaft power ≈ 15.14 kW --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > 1. **Friction factor confusion:** Many students mistakenly use $f = 16/Re$ (Fanning) instead of $f = 64/Re$ (Darcy). This single error costs **2 marks** in part (a) derivations. > 2. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing $Q$ in L/s and area in m². Always convert $Q$ to m³/s before velocity calculations. > 3. **Forgetting to convert head loss to power:** When asked for "power lost," multiply by $\rho g Q$, not by $Q$ alone. > 4. **Adding major and minor losses incorrectly:** Total head loss is **summed**, not averaged. The two terms have different physical origins and are added linearly. > 5. **Wrong velocity reference for $K$:** Loss coefficients are tied to a specific reference velocity. Always check whether $K$ is referenced to upstream or downstream velocity. > 6. **Ignoring minor losses in long pipelines:** In long-distance pipelines, students sometimes ignore minor losses entirely. While often small, KTU examiners may deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** if the problem specifies fittings and the student omits them. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Losses and Efficiencies:** - ✅ The **Darcy-Weisbach equation** $h_f = fLV^2/(2gD)$ is the master formula for major losses. Memorize it cold. - ✅ **Friction factor formulas** to remember: $f = 64/Re$ (laminar); $f = 0.316/Re^{0.25}$ (Blasius, smooth turbulent); Colebrook-White (general). - ✅ **Minor loss formula:** $h_m = K V^2/(2g)$ — always state which velocity you are using as reference. - ✅ **Equivalent length** method: $L_e = KD/f$. Total pipe length for head loss calc = $L + \sum L_e$. - ✅ **Borda-Carnot loss for sudden expansion:** $h_L = (V_1 - V_2)^2/(2g)$. - ✅ **Sudden contraction loss** (reference velocity is $V_2$, the smaller pipe velocity). - ✅ **Pump efficiency hierarchy:** $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{hydraulic}} \times \eta_{\text{volumetric}} \times \eta_{\text{mechanical}}$. - ✅ **Turbine efficiency hierarchy:** $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{hydraulic}} \times \eta_{\text{mechanical}}$ (no volumetric efficiency for turbines). - ✅ **Power formula:** $P_w = \rho g Q H$ (water power). To find input power, divide by overall efficiency. - ✅ Always check whether the problem gives **friction factor** directly or expects you to **calculate it** using Moody chart / Colebrook equation. - ✅ In **SI units**: $D$ in m, $L$ in m, $V$ in m/s, $Q$ in m³/s, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $h_f$ in m, $P$ in W. - ✅ The **roughness values** for common materials (galvanized iron: 0.15 mm; commercial steel: 0.045 mm; cast iron: 0.26 mm; drawn tubing: 0.0015 mm) — memorize for quick exam use. - ✅ For laminar flow, the Hagen-Poiseuille equation $h_f = 32\mu V L / (\rho g D^2)$ can be used directly. - ✅ **Energy grade line (EGL)** drops by $h_f$ along straight pipe and by $h_m$ at every fitting. **Hydraulic grade line (HGL)** lies $V^2/(2g)$ below the EGL in straight pipe sections.

topics.title: Francis Turbine - Velocity Triangles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Francis Turbine - Velocity Triangles ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Velocity Triangle** is a geometric representation of the velocity components of water flowing through the runner of a Francis turbine, constructed by combining the **Absolute Velocity (V)**, the **Relative Velocity (V_r)**, and the **Blade Velocity (u)** as vectors. Two velocity triangles are essential for the complete analysis of a Francis turbine: the **Inlet Velocity Triangle** at the runner entry and the **Outlet Velocity Triangle** at the runner exit. These triangles form the analytical foundation for deriving the work done, power developed, and hydraulic efficiency of the turbine. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** "A velocity triangle is a closed triangular diagram representing the vector relationship $\vec{V} = \vec{u} + \vec{V_r}$ at a point on the runner blade, where V is the absolute velocity of water, u is the velocity of the blade, and V_r is the velocity of water relative to the blade." ### The Three Fundamental Velocities in a Francis Turbine 1. **Absolute Velocity (V):** The *actual* velocity of water as observed by a stationary observer. It is the vector sum of blade velocity and relative velocity. 2. **Relative Velocity (V_r):** The velocity of water *as observed by an observer moving with the blade* (e.g., a camera fixed to the rotating runner). This represents how the water "appears" to approach or leave the blade. 3. **Blade Velocity (u):** The tangential velocity of the blade at the point under consideration, given by $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$, where **D** is the diameter at that point and **N** is the rotational speed in **rpm**. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Rain on a Moving Car Windshield:** > Imagine you are driving a car through the rain. From a person standing on the road (a *stationary observer*), the rain appears to fall at an angle because the rain has a real (absolute) velocity. But from your perspective *inside* the car (an observer moving with the car), the rain appears to hit the windshield almost vertically, with an apparent (relative) velocity. The wind speed you feel (the *relative* velocity) is different from the actual wind direction (the *absolute* velocity). Similarly, in a Francis turbine, the water has an absolute velocity in space, but as observed from the rotating runner blade, it has a relative velocity that determines the angle at which it strikes the blade. The velocity triangle is simply the geometric "calculator" that relates all three velocities. ### Key Components of a Velocity Triangle | Symbol | Component | Direction/Meaning | |--------|-----------|-------------------| | $V$ | Absolute velocity | Velocity of water in space | | $V_r$ | Relative velocity | Velocity of water w.r.t. moving blade | | $u$ | Blade velocity | Tangential velocity of the blade | | $V_w$ | **Whirl component** | Tangential (peripheral) component of $V$ | | $V_f$ | **Flow component** | Radial (axial/radial) component of $V$ | | $V_{rw}$ | Relative whirl component | Tangential component of $V_r$ | | $\alpha$ | **Guide vane angle** | Angle between $V$ and $u$ | | $\beta$ | **Blade angle** | Angle between $V_r$ and the negative $u$ direction | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Two Triangles?** Water enters the Francis runner at the **outer periphery (D₁)** and exits at the **inner periphery (D₂)**. Since both the diameter and the blade angle change between inlet and outlet, two separate triangles are mandatory for proper analysis. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Construction of the Inlet Velocity Triangle (At Runner Entry, Diameter D₁) The water leaves the guide vanes and enters the runner tangentially. At inlet: - $u_1$ acts along the direction of blade motion (tangential). - $V_1$ is the absolute velocity, making angle $\alpha_1$ with $u_1$. - $V_{r1}$ is the relative velocity, making angle $\beta_1$ with the **negative direction of $u_1$** (i.e., on the opposite side of $u_1$). **Geometric relationships from the Inlet Triangle:** $$V_{w1} = V_1 \cos\alpha_1 \quad \text{(Whirl component at inlet)}$$ $$V_{f1} = V_1 \sin\alpha_1 \quad \text{(Flow component at inlet)}$$ $$V_{r1} \cos\beta_1 = u_1 - V_{w1} = u_1 - V_1 \cos\alpha_1$$ $$V_{r1} \sin\beta_1 = V_{f1} = V_1 \sin\alpha_1$$ $$\boxed{V_{r1} = \sqrt{(u_1 - V_{w1})^2 + V_{f1}^2}}$$ ### 2.2 Construction of the Outlet Velocity Triangle (At Runner Exit, Diameter D₂) At the outlet of a properly designed Francis runner, the water leaves **without any whirl** (i.e., $V_{w2} = 0$ for *radial discharge*). This is the *ideal* condition for maximum utilization of head. However, in general (with a small residual whirl), the outlet triangle is constructed analogously: $$V_{w2} = V_2 \cos\alpha_2 \quad \text{(Whirl component at outlet)}$$ $$V_{f2} = V_2 \sin\alpha_2 \quad \text{(Flow component at outlet)}$$ $$V_{r2} \cos\beta_2 = u_2 - V_{w2} \quad \text{(if } V_{w2} \text{ is opposite to } u_2 \text{)}$$ $$\boxed{V_{r2} = \sqrt{(u_2 - V_{w2})^2 + V_{f2}^2}}$$ ### 2.3 Euler's Equation for Work Done (KTU Board-Favorite Equation) Applying Euler's turbomachinery equation, the work done per unit weight of water (specific work) by the runner on the water is: $$\boxed{\;W = \frac{1}{g}\left[V_{w1} u_1 \pm V_{w2} u_2\right]\; \text{ (m·N/N or m)}\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Convention — KTU Exam Critical Rule:** > - **Plus sign (+):** Used when $V_{w2}$ is in the **opposite direction** to $u_2$ (typical for Francis turbine with whirl in the *same rotational sense* as $u_1$). > - **Minus sign (−):** Used when $V_{w2}$ is in the **same direction** as $u_2$. > - For **radial discharge (ideal Francis)**, $V_{w2} = 0$, and the equation reduces to: $W = \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1}{g}$. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Condition | |---|-----------|---------|-------|-----------| | 1 | Blade velocity at radius r | $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | D in m, N in rpm | | 2 | Absolute velocity | $V = \sqrt{V_w^2 + V_f^2}$ | m/s | Vector result | | 3 | Whirl component | $V_w = V \cos\alpha$ | m/s | Tangential projection | | 4 | Flow component | $V_f = V \sin\alpha$ | m/s | Radial projection | | 5 | Relative whirl (inlet) | $V_{w1} - u_1 = V_{r1} \cos\beta_1$ | m/s | Vector subtraction | | 6 | Relative velocity (inlet) | $V_{r1} = \sqrt{(u_1 - V_{w1})^2 + V_{f1}^2}$ | m/s | Pythagoras | | 7 | Relative velocity (outlet) | $V_{r2} = \sqrt{(u_2 - V_{w2})^2 + V_{f2}^2}$ | m/s | Pythagoras | | 8 | Work done per unit weight | $W = \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1 \pm V_{w2} u_2}{g}$ | m | Euler's equation | | 9 | Power developed (water) | $P = \rho Q W g = \rho Q (V_{w1} u_1 \pm V_{w2} u_2)$ | W | $\rho$ = 1000 kg/m³ | | 10 | Shaft power (brake) | $P_{shaft} = \eta_{overall} \cdot \rho Q g H$ | W | Overall efficiency | | 11 | Hydraulic efficiency | $\eta_h = \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1 \pm V_{w2} u_2}{g H}$ | dimensionless | Ratio | | 12 | Flow ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{V_{f1}}{u_1}$ | dimensionless | Design parameter | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Velocity triangles are not merely academic tools — they are the *backbone of hydraulic machine design*. By using velocity triangles, real-world engineers determine: - The exact **shape of the runner blades** (camber and twist). - The **discharge (Q)** through the runner for a given flow area. - The **optimal rotational speed (N)** and specific speed ($N_s$) classification. - The **hydraulic efficiency** and identify the *sources of losses* (e.g., shock at entry, residual whirl at exit). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Equation for Work Done in a Francis Turbine **Statement:** To derive the work done per unit weight of water striking the runner of a Francis turbine using the velocity triangle components. **Assumptions:** 1. Water is an ideal, incompressible fluid. 2. The flow is steady and uniform. 3. The velocity triangles at inlet and outlet are as per standard construction. **Step 1 — Apply the angular momentum principle (Euler's Turbomachinery Equation):** The torque exerted by the water on the runner is equal to the rate of change of angular momentum of the water as it flows through the runner: $$T = \rho Q (V_{w1} r_1 - V_{w2} r_2)$$ where $r_1$ and $r_2$ are the inlet and outlet radii of the runner. **Step 2 — Multiply by angular velocity $\omega$ to obtain power:** The power transferred from water to the runner is $P = T \cdot \omega$. Substituting $u_1 = \omega r_1$ and $u_2 = \omega r_2$: $$P = \rho Q \omega (V_{w1} r_1 - V_{w2} r_2)$$ $$P = \rho Q (V_{w1} \omega r_1 - V_{w2} \omega r_2)$$ $$\boxed{P = \rho Q (V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2)}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to work done per unit weight of water (specific work):** Work done per unit weight = $\dfrac{\text{Power}}{\text{Weight flow rate}} = \dfrac{\rho Q (V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2)}{\rho Q g}$ $$W = \frac{V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$ In Francis turbines, the whirl at outlet $V_{w2}$ is typically opposite in direction to $u_2$ (in the sense of rotation), so the product $V_{w2} u_2$ is added: $$\boxed{W = \frac{V_{w1} u_1 + V_{w2} u_2}{g} \;\; \text{(in metres of water)}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation of the Relationship $V_{r1} = \dfrac{V_{f1}}{\sin\beta_1}$ (From Inlet Triangle) **Step 1 — Identify the right-angled sub-triangle formed by $V_{f1}$ and $V_{r1}$:** In the inlet velocity triangle, $V_{f1}$ is perpendicular to the blade velocity $u_1$, while $V_{r1}$ makes an angle $\beta_1$ with the line of $-u_1$. The flow component $V_{f1}$ is the side *opposite* to $\beta_1$ in the small right-angled triangle. **Step 2 — Apply the trigonometric sine ratio:** $$\sin\beta_1 = \frac{\text{Opposite side}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{V_{f1}}{V_{r1}}$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange to express $V_{r1}$:** $$\boxed{V_{r1} = \frac{V_{f1}}{\sin\beta_1}}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation of Hydraulic Efficiency The work actually obtained from the runner ($V_{w1} u_1 / g$ or the Euler expression) divided by the input head energy ($H$) gives the hydraulic efficiency: $$\eta_h = \frac{\text{Work done per unit weight}}{\text{Head available}} = \frac{V_{w1} u_1 + V_{w2} u_2}{g H}$$ For an ideal Francis turbine with $V_{w2} = 0$: $$\boxed{\eta_h = \frac{V_{w1} u_1}{g H}}$$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Connecting Triangle Components to Practical Quantities | Given Quantity | Required Quantity | Governing Relation | Reasoning | |----------------|-------------------|--------------------|-----------| | $D_1, N$ | Blade velocity $u_1$ | $u_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1 N}{60}$ | Direct peripheral motion | | $V_1, \alpha_1$ | $V_{w1}$ and $V_{f1}$ | $V_{w1} = V_1\cos\alpha_1$, $V_{f1} = V_1\sin\alpha_1$ | Resolve $V_1$ along tang. and radial | | $V_1, \alpha_1, u_1$ | $V_{r1}$ and $\beta_1$ | $V_{r1} = \dfrac{V_{f1}}{\sin\beta_1}$, $\tan\beta_1 = \dfrac{V_{f1}}{u_1 - V_{w1}}$ | Geometry of inlet triangle | | $V_2, \alpha_2, u_2$ | $V_{r2}$ and $\beta_2$ | $V_{r2} = \dfrac{V_{f2}}{\sin\beta_2}$, $\tan\beta_2 = \dfrac{V_{f2}}{u_2 - V_{w2}}$ | Geometry of outlet triangle | | $H, Q$ | Power developed | $P = \rho Q g H \eta_h$ | Apply efficiency | | $V_{w1}, u_1, V_{w2}, u_2$ | Work per unit weight | $W = \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1 + V_{w2} u_2}{g}$ | Euler's equation | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for Velocity Components:** "**W**ind **f**lows **a**ngularly, **f**lows **a**long the runner, **r**elative **g**lide is across blade." — $V_w$ (whirl) = tangential, $V_f$ (flow) = radial, $V_r$ (relative) = angle to blade. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Water Through a Francis Runner The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the *functional flow architecture* of water through a Francis turbine, showing the position of inlet/outlet, guide vane, and runner regions where the velocity triangles are constructed. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[RESERVOIR
Head H available] --> B[PENSTOCK
Conveys high pressure water] B --> C[GUIDE VANE ASSEMBLY
Controls flow direction and discharge] C --> D[RUNNER INLET
Outer periphery D1
Guide vane angle alpha1] D --> E[RUNNER BLADES
Energy conversion zone
Tangential flow follows curved path] E --> F[RUNNER OUTLET
Inner periphery D2
Blade angle beta2] F --> G[DRAFT TUBE
Converts residual KE to pressure head] G --> H[TAIL RACE
Water discharged to river] subgraph VELOCITY_TRIANGLES T1[INLET TRIANGLE
u1, V1, Vr1
alpha1, beta1] T2[OUTLET TRIANGLE
u2, V2, Vr2
alpha2, beta2] end D -.Constructed at.-> T1 F -.Constructed at.-> T2 T1 --> WORK[Work done per unit weight
W = Vw1 u1 + Vw2 u2 over g] T2 --> WORK WORK --> POWER[Shaft Power Output
P = rho Q g W eta] ``` ### 4.2 Conceptual Schematic of the Inlet Velocity Triangle (ASCII Representation) ``` V1 (Absolute velocity) /| / | / | Vf1 (Flow component - radial) / | / | / α1 | /______| / | / | Vw1 (Whirl component - tangential) / | / | Vw1-Vr1 u1 (Blade velocity - tangential) <- - - - - (Vr1)β1 Vw1-u1 ``` **Interpretation of the Inlet Triangle:** - The horizontal base represents the **tangential direction** (direction of $u_1$). - The vertical leg represents the **radial direction** (the flow component $V_{f1}$). - The hypotenuse is the **absolute velocity $V_1$** at angle $\alpha_1$ above horizontal. - The relative velocity $V_{r1}$ is drawn *below* the horizontal base, tilted by the **blade angle $\beta_1$**. - Vector closure: $\vec{V_1} = \vec{u_1} + \vec{V_{r1}}$. ### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic of the Outlet Velocity Triangle (Radial Discharge Case) ``` V2 /| / | / | Vf2 (radial) / | / α2 | /____| | | Vw2 (whirl) | u2 (blade velocity, tangential) ``` > [!NOTE] > **Ideal Francis Turbine — Radial Discharge:** When $\alpha_2 = 90°$, then $V_{w2} = V_2 \cos 90° = 0$, and all the whirl energy has been fully converted to mechanical work. The outlet triangle degenerates into a single vertical line ($V_2 = V_{f2}$), giving **maximum hydraulic efficiency** of $\eta_h = \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1}{gH}$. ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Information Flow from Velocity Triangle to Performance Output ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Inlet Diameter D1] --> B[Blade Velocity u1] C[Outlet Diameter D2] --> D[Blade Velocity u2] E[Guide Vane Angle alpha1] --> F[Whirl Vw1] G[Outlet Angle alpha2] --> H[Whirl Vw2] F --> I[Work Done per unit weight W] H --> I B --> I D --> I I --> J[Hydraulic Efficiency eta_h] I --> K[Power Output P] L[Head H] --> J M[Discharge Q] --> K ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Define a velocity triangle. List the three velocities and two angles used in the velocity triangle of a Francis turbine.** **Model Answer:** A velocity triangle is a vector diagram that graphically represents the relationship between the absolute velocity of water (V), the velocity of the runner blade (u), and the velocity of water relative to the blade (V_r) at a point on the runner. The three velocities are: **Absolute Velocity (V)**, **Relative Velocity (V_r)**, and **Blade Velocity (u)**. The two angles are: **Guide Vane Angle (α)**, which is the angle between V and u, and **Blade Angle (β)**, which is the angle between V_r and the negative direction of u. Two triangles (inlet and outlet) are required to fully analyze a Francis turbine. --- #### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **State Euler's equation for work done per unit weight of water in a Francis turbine. What happens to the equation under the condition of radial discharge?** **Model Answer:** Euler's equation for the work done per unit weight of water by the runner is: $$W = \frac{V_{w1} u_1 + V_{w2} u_2}{g} \;\; \text{(m·N/N or metres of water)}$$ Under the condition of **radial discharge**, the water leaves the runner without any residual whirl, i.e., $V_{w2} = 0$. The equation then simplifies to: $$W = \frac{V_{w1} u_1}{g}$$ This represents the *maximum possible* work extraction from the given head and is used to define the maximum hydraulic efficiency of the Francis turbine. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- #### Question A [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper] **Question A(a) [7 Marks]:** With the help of neat inlet and outlet velocity triangles, derive the expression for work done per unit weight of water in a Francis turbine. **Model Solution:** *Step 1 — Draw the inlet velocity triangle* (at runner entry, diameter D₁, peripheral speed u₁): - Absolute velocity $V_1$ at guide vane angle $\alpha_1$ from $u_1$. - Whirl component $V_{w1} = V_1 \cos\alpha_1$. - Flow component $V_{f1} = V_1 \sin\alpha_1$. - Relative velocity $V_{r1}$ at blade angle $\beta_1$ from the negative direction of $u_1$. *Step 2 — Draw the outlet velocity triangle* (at runner exit, diameter D₂, peripheral speed u₂): - Absolute velocity $V_2$ at angle $\alpha_2$ from $u_2$. - Whirl component $V_{w2} = V_2 \cos\alpha_2$. - Flow component $V_{f2} = V_2 \sin\alpha_2$. - Relative velocity $V_{r2}$ at blade angle $\beta_2$. *Step 3 — Apply Euler's turbomachinery equation:* The torque on the runner is $T = \rho Q (V_{w1} r_1 - V_{w2} r_2)$. The power is $P = T \omega$ where $\omega = 2\pi N/60$. Since $u_1 = \omega r_1$ and $u_2 = \omega r_2$: $$P = \rho Q (V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2)$$ *Step 4 — Convert to work per unit weight:* $$W = \frac{P}{\rho Q g} = \frac{V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$ Since in a Francis turbine, the whirl at outlet is typically opposite to $u_2$ (in the sense of rotation), the product $V_{w2} u_2$ contributes positively to work: $$\boxed{W = \frac{V_{w1} u_1 + V_{w2} u_2}{g} \;\; \text{(metres of water)}}$$ **Question A(b) [7 Marks]:** A Francis turbine develops 400 kW under a head of 50 m. The blade diameters at inlet and outlet are 1.2 m and 0.6 m respectively. The runner rotates at 300 rpm with a guide vane angle of 15° at the inlet. The flow is radial at the outlet. Calculate: (i) the absolute velocity of water at inlet, (ii) the relative velocity at inlet, and (iii) the hydraulic efficiency. Take blade velocity coefficient $K_u = 0.98$. **Model Solution:** *Given:* - $P = 400 \text{ kW} = 400{,}000$ W - $H = 50$ m - $D_1 = 1.2$ m, $D_2 = 0.6$ m - $N = 300$ rpm - $\alpha_1 = 15°$, radial discharge (so $V_{w2} = 0$) - $K_u = 0.98$ *Step 1 — Blade velocity at inlet:* $$u_1 = \frac{\pi D_1 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 1.2 \times 300}{60} = 6\pi = 18.85 \text{ m/s}$$ *Step 2 — Hydraulic efficiency:* $$\eta_h = K_u = 0.98 \quad \text{(given as blade velocity coefficient)}$$ Wait, $K_u$ is the ratio $V_{w1}/u_1$ (blade velocity coefficient / whirl coefficient). *Step 3 — Whirl component:* $$V_{w1} = K_u \times u_1 = 0.98 \times 18.85 = 18.47 \text{ m/s}$$ *Step 4 — Absolute velocity at inlet:* $$V_1 = \frac{V_{w1}}{\cos\alpha_1} = \frac{18.47}{\cos 15°} = \frac{18.47}{0.9659} = 19.12 \text{ m/s}$$ *Step 5 — Flow component at inlet:* $$V_{f1} = V_1 \sin\alpha_1 = 19.12 \times \sin 15° = 19.12 \times 0.2588 = 4.95 \text{ m/s}$$ *Step 6 — Relative velocity at inlet:* The tangential component of relative velocity: $$V_{w1} - u_1 = 18.47 - 18.85 = -0.38 \text{ m/s}$$ This negative value indicates the relative velocity $V_{r1}$ is slightly tilted forward — a common design condition. $$V_{r1} = \sqrt{(u_1 - V_{w1})^2 + V_{f1}^2} = \sqrt{(-0.38)^2 + (4.95)^2} = \sqrt{0.144 + 24.50} = \sqrt{24.65}$$ $$\boxed{V_{r1} = 4.96 \text{ m/s}}$$ *Step 7 — Hydraulic efficiency verification:* $$\eta_h = \frac{V_{w1} u_1}{g H} = \frac{18.47 \times 18.85}{9.81 \times 50} = \frac{348.16}{490.5} = 0.710 = 71.0\%$$ --- #### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper — Internal Choice] **Question B(a) [7 Marks]:** Draw the velocity triangles for a Francis turbine at inlet and outlet, clearly labelling all components. Explain why two velocity triangles are necessary and not just one. **Model Solution:** *(Diagrams to be drawn by the student — refer to Section 4 of these notes for reference.)* **Inlet Velocity Triangle** — drawn at radius $r_1 = D_1/2$ where the runner blades first encounter water from the guide vanes. The triangle has: - Base = $u_1$ (tangential) - Hypotenuse = $V_1$ at angle $\alpha_1$ - Third side = $V_{r1}$ at angle $\beta_1$ below base - Vertical leg = $V_{f1}$ (radial) **Outlet Velocity Triangle** — drawn at radius $r_2 = D_2/2$ where water leaves the runner. The triangle has: - Base = $u_2$ (tangential, smaller than $u_1$) - Hypotenuse = $V_2$ at angle $\alpha_2$ - Third side = $V_{r2}$ at angle $\beta_2$ - Vertical leg = $V_{f2}$ (radial) **Why two triangles?** 1. **Different radii:** $D_1 > D_2$ in Francis turbines, so $u_1 = \pi D_1 N/60 \neq u_2 = \pi D_2 N/60$. The blade speeds are different. 2. **Different blade angles:** The runner blades are curved and twisted to maintain the optimum angle of attack. The inlet blade angle $\beta_1$ is larger than the outlet blade angle $\beta_2$. 3. **Change in whirl:** The whirl component $V_{w1}$ at the inlet is large (water has high tangential momentum from the guide vanes), but at the outlet, $V_{w2}$ is intentionally reduced to near-zero (for radial discharge) to maximize work extraction. The change in whirl $\Delta V_w$ is precisely what is converted into useful work via Euler's equation. 4. **Conservation of angular momentum:** A single triangle cannot represent the *change* in angular momentum between inlet and outlet. Euler's equation $W = (V_{w1} u_1 + V_{w2} u_2)/g$ requires both triangles to compute the work done. **Question B(b) [7 Marks]:** The inlet and outlet diameters of the runner of a Francis turbine are 1.0 m and 0.5 m respectively. The widths of the runner at inlet and outlet are 100 mm and 150 mm. The runner runs at 250 rpm. The guide vane angle at inlet is 20° and the absolute velocity of water at inlet is 20 m/s. The water leaves the runner radially. Determine: (i) the blade angles at inlet and outlet, (ii) the work done per second per unit weight, and (iii) the discharge. **Model Solution:** *Given:* - $D_1 = 1.0$ m, $D_2 = 0.5$ m - $B_1 = 100$ mm = 0.1 m, $B_2 = 150$ mm = 0.15 m - $N = 250$ rpm - $\alpha_1 = 20°$, $V_1 = 20$ m/s - Radial discharge: $\alpha_2 = 90°$, $V_{w2} = 0$ *Step 1 — Blade velocities:* $$u_1 = \frac{\pi \times 1.0 \times 250}{60} = \frac{250\pi}{60} = 13.09 \text{ m/s}$$ $$u_2 = \frac{\pi \times 0.5 \times 250}{60} = \frac{125\pi}{60} = 6.545 \text{ m/s}$$ *Step 2 — Velocity components at inlet:* $$V_{w1} = V_1 \cos\alpha_1 = 20 \times \cos 20° = 20 \times 0.9397 = 18.79 \text{ m/s}$$ $$V_{f1} = V_1 \sin\alpha_1 = 20 \times \sin 20° = 20 \times 0.3420 = 6.84 \text{ m/s}$$ *Step 3 — Blade angle at inlet:* From the inlet velocity triangle: $$\tan\beta_1 = \frac{V_{f1}}{u_1 - V_{w1}} = \frac{6.84}{13.09 - 18.79} = \frac{6.84}{-5.70} = -1.20$$ $$\beta_1 = \tan^{-1}(-1.20) = -50.19°$$ The negative sign indicates the relative velocity vector is tilted in the direction of $u_1$ (forward-leaning blade). The **magnitude** of the inlet blade angle is: $$\beta_1 \approx 50.2°$$ *Step 4 — Blade angle at outlet:* For radial discharge, $V_{w2} = 0$, so $V_{r2} \cos\beta_2 = u_2 - 0 = u_2$. By the principle of continuity (assuming no leakage and incompressible flow): $$Q = \pi D_1 B_1 V_{f1} = \pi D_2 B_2 V_{f2}$$ $$V_{f2} = \frac{D_1 B_1 V_{f1}}{D_2 B_2} = \frac{1.0 \times 0.1 \times 6.84}{0.5 \times 0.15} = \frac{0.684}{0.075} = 9.12 \text{ m/s}$$ Now, the outlet blade angle: $$\tan\beta_2 = \frac{V_{f2}}{u_2 - V_{w2}} = \frac{9.12}{6.545 - 0} = 1.393$$ $$\boxed{\beta_2 = \tan^{-1}(1.393) = 54.34°}$$ *Step 5 — Work done per unit weight:* Since $V_{w2} = 0$ (radial discharge): $$W = \frac{V_{w1} u_1}{g} = \frac{18.79 \times 13.09}{9.81} = \frac{245.96}{9.81}$$ $$\boxed{W = 25.07 \text{ m·N/N (metres of water)}}$$ *Step 6 — Discharge:* $$Q = \pi D_1 B_1 V_{f1} = \pi \times 1.0 \times 0.1 \times 6.84 = 0.684 \pi = 2.149 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{Q = 2.15 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign of $\beta_1$:** A negative value of $\tan\beta_1$ is **not an error** — it simply means the relative velocity at inlet leans *forward* (i.e., in the direction of blade motion). This is the **"forward-tilted" inlet blade** design. Do not lose marks by reporting a negative angle; the magnitude is what matters. > 2. **Forgetting the "minus" in $u_1 - V_{w1}$:** The relative whirl component at inlet is **always** $V_{w1} - u_1$ (or $u_1 - V_{w1}$ with appropriate sign). Reversing this is a 2-mark deduction. > 3. **Continuity equation:** When inlet and outlet diameters *and* widths are different, do **not** assume $V_{f1} = V_{f2}$. Apply $A_1 V_{f1} = A_2 V_{f2}$ correctly. > 4. **Units of discharge:** Many students report $Q$ in litres/second instead of m³/s. KTU requires SI units: **m³/s**. > 5. **Radial discharge misuse:** For radial discharge, $V_{w2} = 0$ but $V_2$ itself is **not zero** — it equals $V_{f2}$. A common error is to set $V_2 = 0$. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Francis Turbine Velocity Triangles:** > > - [x] **Two triangles mandatory** — Inlet (D₁) and Outlet (D₂) for every Francis turbine analysis. > - [x] **Three velocities:** $V$ (absolute), $V_r$ (relative), $u$ (blade). > - [x] **Two angles:** $\alpha$ (guide vane, between $V$ and $u$), $\beta$ (blade, between $V_r$ and $-u$). > - [x] **Whirl component** $V_w = V \cos\alpha$ (tangential); **Flow component** $V_f = V \sin\alpha$ (radial). > - [x] **Euler's equation:** $W = \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1 + V_{w2} u_2}{g}$ for Francis (plus sign due to opposite whirl direction). > - [x] **Radial discharge** ⇒ $\alpha_2 = 90°$ ⇒ $V_{w2} = 0$ ⇒ maximum hydraulic efficiency. > - [x] **Blade velocity:** $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ (must compute $u_1$ and $u_2$ separately). > - [x] **Relative velocity formula:** $V_r = \sqrt{(u - V_w)^2 + V_f^2}$. > - [x] **Continuity:** $A_1 V_{f1} = A_2 V_{f2}$ ⇒ $\pi D_1 B_1 V_{f1} = \pi D_2 B_2 V_{f2}$. > - [x] **Power developed by water:** $P_{water} = \rho Q (V_{w1} u_1 + V_{w2} u_2)$. > - [x] **Hydraulic efficiency:** $\eta_h = \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1 + V_{w2} u_2}{g H}$ — dimensionless ratio. > - [x] **KTU exam tip:** Always state the **sign convention** explicitly when using Euler's equation. > - [x] **KTU exam tip:** When $\beta_1$ comes out negative, report the **magnitude** and note the forward-tilted blade design. > - [x] **Memory hook:** "**W**ater enters **w**hirling ($V_{w1}$), exits **n**on-whirling ($V_{w2} \approx 0$)." — Francis turbines extract energy by removing whirl from the water.

topics.title: Work Done and Efficiencies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Work Done and Efficiencies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
In turbo-machinery (centrifugal pumps, turbines), the **Work Done** refers to the energy transferred per unit weight (or per unit mass) of fluid between the inlet and outlet of the rotating impeller/runner. The fundamental governing equation is **Euler's Turbomachinery Equation**, which is derived from the angular momentum principle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euler's Equation (Per Unit Weight of Fluid):**
> 
> $$W = \frac{V_{w1} \cdot u_1 \pm V_{w2} \cdot u_2}{g}$$
> 
> where the **(+)** sign applies to a **pump** (energy is added to the fluid) and the **(−)** sign applies to a **turbine** (energy is extracted from the fluid).

The **Efficiencies** of a hydraulic machine describe how effectively the machine converts input energy (shaft work, electrical energy) into useful output energy (pressure head, kinetic head, mechanical shaft work).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 4):**
> Students must master the derivation of work done using velocity triangles and must clearly distinguish between the five standard efficiencies: **Manometric (η_man)**, **Volumetric (η_vol)**, **Mechanical (η_mech)**, **Overall (η_overall)**, and **Hydraulic (η_hyd)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition
Imagine a **merry-go-round (carousel)** at a fair. As it spins, if you throw a ball **outward** (radially) from the center, the spinning platform adds extra tangential speed to it — that's exactly what an **impeller of a pump** does to water (it gives energy to the fluid). Conversely, if water is **shot inward** at the spinning blades of a turbine, the blades slow the water down and extract that kinetic energy to spin a shaft — that's a **turbine**.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of the impeller as a "courier"**:
> - In a **pump**, the impeller **delivers** energy to water (courier carries a heavy package outward).
> - In a **turbine**, water **delivers** energy to the impeller (the package is handed *to* the courier, making it slow down but doing useful work).

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) |
|----------|:------:|:------------------:|
| Tangential (Whirl) Velocity | $V_w$ | **m/s** |
| Radial / Flow Velocity | $V_f$ | **m/s** |
| Blade Velocity | $u$ | **m/s** |
| Relative Velocity | $V_r$ | **m/s** |
| Work done per unit weight | $W$ | **m** (metres of fluid) |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Specific weight of water | $\rho g$ | **9810 N/m³** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Velocity Triangle Decomposition
At any point on the impeller/runner blade, the absolute velocity of fluid $\vec{V}$ is resolved into three components using the **velocity triangle**:

- **Tangential / Whirl component** $V_w$: component in the direction of blade motion.
- **Radial / Flow / Axial component** $V_f$: component perpendicular to blade motion (along the flow).
- **Relative velocity** $V_r$: velocity of fluid *relative to the blade*, obtained from vector subtraction: $\vec{V_r} = \vec{V} - \vec{u}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vector relationship at inlet and outlet:**
> 
> $$\vec{V_1} = \vec{V_{f1}} + \vec{V_{w1}}, \quad \vec{V_2} = \vec{V_{f2}} + \vec{V_{w2}}$$
> 
> The blade velocity at radius $D/2$ is $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ where $N$ is the shaft speed in **rpm**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

1. **Establish the rotating reference frame** — Newton’s laws apply, but we use the angular momentum theorem since the impeller is rotating.
2. **Compute the moment of momentum flux** — the rate at which angular momentum crosses the impeller boundaries.
3. **Apply the work-energy theorem** — torque × angular displacement = change in kinetic energy.
