**(i) Specific speed of Turbine A (2 marks):**

$$
N_{s,A} = \frac{300 \times \sqrt{500}}{30^{1.25}}
$$

$$
\sqrt{500} = 22.361
$$

$$
30^{1.25} = 30 \cdot 30^{0.25} = 30 \cdot \sqrt{\sqrt{30}} = 30 \cdot \sqrt{5.477} = 30 \cdot 2.340 = 70.21
$$

$$
N_{s,A} = \frac{300 \times 22.361}{70.21} = \frac{6708.3}{70.21} \approx 95.5
$$

**(ii) Speed of Turbine B (3 marks):**

Since geometrically similar, $N_{s,A} = N_{s,B} = 95.5$.

$$
N_B = \frac{N_{s,B} \cdot H_B^{5/4}}{\sqrt{P_B}}
$$

$$
H_B^{5/4} = 60^{1.25} = 60 \cdot 60^{0.25} = 60 \cdot \sqrt{\sqrt{60}} = 60 \cdot \sqrt{7.746} = 60 \cdot 2.783 = 166.97
$$

$$
\sqrt{P_B} = \sqrt{4000} = 63.246
$$

$$
N_B = \frac{95.5 \times 166.97}{63.246} = \frac{15945.6}{63.246} \approx 252.1 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(iii) Diameter Ratio $D_A / D_B$ (2 marks):**

From $D \propto \sqrt{H}/N$:

$$
\frac{D_A}{D_B} = \frac{\sqrt{H_A}/N_A}{\sqrt{H_B}/N_B} = \frac{\sqrt{30}/300}{\sqrt{60}/252.1}
$$

$$
\frac{\sqrt{30}}{300} = \frac{5.477}{300} = 0.01826
$$

$$
\frac{\sqrt{60}}{252.1} = \frac{7.746}{252.1} = 0.03073
$$

$$
\frac{D_A}{D_B} = \frac{0.01826}{0.03073} \approx 0.594
$$

So Turbine A's runner diameter is about **59.4%** of Turbine B's runner diameter.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $5/4$ exponent:** Writing $H^{5/2}$ instead of $H^{5/4}$ — a very common mistake. Always verify: *Specific speed is $\propto 1/H^{5/4}$*, not $1/H^{5/2}$.
>
> 2. **Mixing up $N_s$ with $N_u$:** $N_u$ uses $\sqrt{H}$ in the denominator; $N_s$ uses $H^{5/4}$ and includes $\sqrt{P}$. Examiners award only 1–2 marks if confused.
>
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** Using $P$ in HP with $H$ in meters, or vice versa, gives numerically different $N_s$. Always declare the unit system explicitly.
>
> 4. **Forgetting "geometrically similar":** Without similarity, you cannot compare two turbines. Examiners specifically look for this phrase.
>
> 5. **Skipping the classification step:** A 14-mark question that ends without naming the turbine type is considered incomplete — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
>
> 6. **Arithmetic in $H^{1.25}$:** Many students make errors in fractional exponent calculations. Show intermediate steps like $H^{1.25} = H \cdot H^{0.25}$ to earn method marks even if the final number is slightly off.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Specific Speed of Turbine:**
>
> - **Definition:** Speed of a geometrically similar turbine producing 1 kW under 1 m head.
> - **Formula:** $N_s = N\sqrt{P}/H^{5/4}$ — **dimensionless.**
> - **Range:** Pelton (8–70), Francis (60–1500), Kaplan (1500–3000).
> - **Low $N_s$** = high head, slow, tangential/impulse flow.
> - **High $N_s$** = low head, fast, axial flow.
> - **$N_s$ is invariant** for all geometrically similar turbines — model-to-prototype scaling key.
> - **Unit speed:** $N_u = N/\sqrt{H}$.
> - **Unit discharge:** $Q_u = Q/\sqrt{H}$.
> - **Unit power:** $P_u = P/H^{3/2}$.
> - **Cavitation** tendency increases with $N_s$.
> - **Exam tip:** Always identify turbine type after computing $N_s$.
> - **Examiner trap:** $H^{5/4}$, not $H^{5/2}$. Memorize the formula in the **exact** form.
> - **Affinity laws:** $Q \propto N D^3$, $H \propto N^2 D^2$, $P \propto N^3 D^5$ — recall cold for derivations.
> - **Real-world use:** Model testing in laboratories, prototype design, turbine selection in hydro-power plants.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pumps: Positive Displacement Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pumps: Positive Displacement Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Positive Displacement Pump (PDP)** is a hydraulic machine that moves a fixed quantity of fluid per cycle by trapping a discrete amount of fluid, mechanically forcing (displacing) it from the suction side to the discharge side of the pump chamber. The delivery volume per cycle is theoretically fixed by the geometry of the displacement chamber and is essentially independent of the discharge pressure or the system head against which it operates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Positive Displacement Pumps fall under the **Hydraulic Machines** classification where flow rate $Q$ is primarily a function of geometry ($Q \propto N \cdot V_d$), unlike **rotodynamic pumps** where $Q$ depends on head.

### Classification Hierarchy of Positive Displacement Pumps

| Type | Mechanism | KTU Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reciprocating Pumps** | Piston/Plunger with back-and-forth motion | Very High (Most numerical questions) |
| **Rotary Pumps** | Gears, vanes, lobes, or screws rotating | Moderate (Theory only) |

**Reciprocating pumps** are further divided into:
- **Single-acting pump** — One suction and one delivery stroke per cycle (one revolution of crank).
- **Double-acting pump** — Two suctions and two deliveries per cycle.

**Rotary pumps** include:
- **Gear pump** (external and internal gear type).
- **Vane pump**.
- **Screw (or lobe) pump**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Syringe Principle

Imagine a medical syringe. When you pull the plunger back, a fixed volume of liquid is drawn into the barrel (suction). When you push the plunger forward, that exact same volume is expelled out (discharge). 

- Now imagine multiple syringes working in sequence — that is a **reciprocating pump**.
- Now imagine a rotating gear trapping fluid between its teeth and the casing, sweeping it around to the discharge side — that is a **gear pump** (rotary type).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In a PDP, fluid is **pushed** (positive displacement), whereas in a centrifugal pump, fluid is **pushed by centrifugal force** created by an impeller. This is why PDPs are excellent for delivering fluids at very high pressures with constant volume flow.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard metrics are used universally in KTU problems:
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - **Density of water:** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Specific weight of water:** $w = \gamma = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$
> - **1 HP (metric)** = $735.5 \text{ W}$ (sometimes $746 \text{ W}$ used)
> - **1 kW** = $1.36 \text{ HP}$

## 1.4 Components of a Reciprocating Pump

1. **Cylinder / Barrel** — The chamber in which the piston reciprocates.
2. **Piston or Plunger** — The displacing element driven by a connecting rod from a crank.
3. **Suction Valve** — Allows fluid in only during the suction stroke.
4. **Delivery Valve** — Allows fluid out only during the delivery stroke.
5. **Crank, Connecting Rod, and Slider-Crank Mechanism** — Converts rotary motion (from prime mover) into reciprocating motion.
6. **Air Vessel** — A closed chamber (filled partly with air) connected to the suction and delivery pipes to smoothen flow pulsations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of a Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump

A **single-acting reciprocating pump** has only one side of the piston in contact with water. Each complete revolution of the crank produces **one suction stroke and one delivery stroke**.

### Stroke-by-Stroke Operation

**Step 1 — Suction Stroke:** The crank rotates from $0°$ to $180°$. The piston moves from the Inner Dead Centre (IDC) to the Outer Dead Centre (ODC). This creates a partial vacuum in the cylinder. Atmospheric pressure pushes the liquid up the suction pipe, opening the suction valve (which is non-return).

**Step 2 — Delivery Stroke:** The crank rotates from $180°$ to $360°$. The piston moves from ODC back to IDC. The water in the cylinder is pushed out through the delivery valve (also non-return) into the delivery pipe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why non-return valves?** They prevent backflow during the alternate stroke. Suction valve closes during delivery; delivery valve closes during suction.

## 2.2 Theoretical Discharge ($Q_{th}$)

$$Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{(Single-acting pump)}$$

where:
- $A = \text{Cross-sectional area of piston (m}^2\text{)} = \frac{\pi}{4}D^2$
- $L = \text{Stroke length (m)}$
- $N = \text{Speed of crank (rpm)}$

For a **double-acting pump** (water on both sides of piston):

$$Q_{th} = \frac{(2A - a) \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$$

where $a = \text{Area of piston rod (m}^2\text{)}$. If piston rod area is neglected:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{2 \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$$

## 2.3 Actual Discharge, Slip & Coefficient of Discharge

Due to leakage past valves and through clearances, **actual discharge is less than theoretical discharge**.

$$\text{Slip} \; (S) = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** Slip is defined as a **positive quantity**. In some KTU numericals, the term "negative slip" is used when $Q_{act} > Q_{th}$ (rare; occurs due to pipe vibrations).

$$\text{Percentage Slip} = \frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Coefficient of Discharge} \; (C_d) = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$

For reciprocating pumps, **$C_d$ ranges from 0.85 to 0.99**.

## 2.4 Work Done Per Stroke (Single-Acting Pump)

Consider the piston moving against a constant head $H_s$ (suction head) and pushing against a static head $H_d$ (delivery head). Total head $H = H_s + H_d$.

### Work Done During Delivery Stroke

The pressure head acting on the piston during delivery stroke is $(H_d + x)$, where $x$ is the height of the water column above the piston at angle $\theta$ due to acceleration.

$$\text{Work done (Delivery)} = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot (H_d + x_{avg}) \cdot L$$

For a sinusoidal crank motion: $x_{avg} = \dfrac{r \cdot \omega^2}{2g} \cdot L_{eq}$ where $r$ is crank radius.

The **simplified work done expression** that KTU uses most often is:

$$W_{\text{total}} = W_{\text{delivery}} + W_{\text{suction}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot (H_d + H_s)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Without acceleration head consideration**, the work done per second by the pump is simply $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_{total}$ (similar to the general energy equation).

## 2.5 Work Done With Acceleration Effects (KTU High-Yield)

When the motion of the piston is non-uniform, **acceleration head** $h_a$ must be considered.

$$h_a = \frac{r \cdot \omega^2}{g} \cdot \cos\theta$$

where $r = L/2$ is the crank radius.

For a single-acting pump, the **maximum acceleration head** is:

$$h_{a,\max} = \frac{r \cdot \omega^2}{g} = \frac{L \cdot \omega^2}{2g}$$

The pressure head in the cylinder during the delivery stroke becomes $(H_d + h_a)$ and during suction $(H_s - h_a)$ at worst.

### Work Done Per Second (Including Acceleration)

$$\boxed{W_{\text{net}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N \cdot \left[H_s + H_d - \frac{r^2 \omega^2}{g L}\right]}$$

(Per cycle, average acceleration head correction factor for single-acting pump = $\frac{r \cdot \omega^2}{2g}$.)

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table. ~70% of KTU Module 4 problems are solved using these formulas.**

| Parameter | Formula | Units | KTU Marks Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Theoretical discharge (single-acting) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | ★★★★★ |
| Theoretical discharge (double-acting) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{2A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | ★★★★ |
| Slip | $S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | ★★★★★ |
| Coefficient of discharge | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | dimensionless | ★★★★ |
| Work done per second (no acceleration) | $W = \rho g Q_{act} \cdot H$ | W | ★★★★★ |
| Power required | $P = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta}$ | W | ★★★★★ |
| Max acceleration head | $h_{a,max} = \dfrac{r \cdot \omega^2}{g}$ | m | ★★★ |
| Crank radius | $r = L/2$ | m | ★★★★★ |
| Angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | ★★★★★ |
| Percentage slip | $\%\text{Slip} = \dfrac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | % | ★★★★ |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where are PDPs used in Kerala & Indian Industry?**
> - **Kerala Water Authority** — High-pressure municipal water supply (booster pumping stations).
> - **Cochin Shipyard & Refineries** — High-viscosity fluid transfer (fuel oil, lubricants).
> - **Oil and gas sector (Kochi Refinery)** — Positive displacement pumps handle viscous crude oil at high pressure.
> - **Hydraulic presses** — Industrial applications requiring constant flow at variable pressure.
> - **Pharmaceutical & food industries** — Metering pumps (a type of PDP) for precise dosing.

**Why choose PDP over centrifugal?**
- Constant flow rate regardless of head variations.
- Excellent for **high-pressure, low-to-medium flow** applications.
- Better suited for **viscous fluids** (oil, syrups, sludge).
- Higher efficiency at constant operating point.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Work Done Per Second by a Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump (Without Acceleration Head)

Let us derive the energy delivered by the pump per second using first principles.

### Given Parameters
- Piston area = $A$ ($\text{m}^2$)
- Stroke length = $L$ (m)
- Crank speed = $N$ (rpm)
- Suction head = $H_s$ (m)
- Delivery head = $H_d$ (m)
- Total static head = $H = H_s + H_d$ (m)

### Step 1 — Identify Work Per Stroke

During the **delivery stroke**, the piston has to push the water against a total head equal to the **delivery head $H_d$** measured from the cylinder level to the delivery reservoir water surface.

Force exerted on piston during delivery = Pressure $\times$ Area

$$F_{\text{delivery}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H_d \cdot A$$

Distance moved by piston during delivery = Stroke length $L$

$$W_{\text{delivery per stroke}} = F_{\text{delivery}} \cdot L = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot H_d \cdot L$$

### Step 2 — Work Done During Suction Stroke

During suction, the piston pulls water up. The atmosphere does work on the water, but from the pump shaft's perspective, the **useful mechanical work input** must overcome the inertia and create suction. By energy conservation, the work input during suction equals the energy needed to lift water from suction reservoir to the cylinder.

$$W_{\text{suction per stroke}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot H_s \cdot L$$

### Step 3 — Total Work Per Cycle

For a single-acting pump, one cycle = one delivery + one suction = one revolution.

$$W_{\text{cycle}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot H_d + \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot H_s$$

$$W_{\text{cycle}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot (H_s + H_d) = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot H$$

### Step 4 — Work Per Second (Power Delivered to Water)

Number of cycles per second = $\dfrac{N}{60}$

$$W_{\text{per second}} = W_{\text{cycle}} \times \frac{N}{60}$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{water}} = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N \cdot H}{60}}$$

**Alternative form using $Q_{th}$:**
Since $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$, we get $W_{\text{water}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{th} \cdot H$.

> [!NOTE]
> **For the actual case, KTU uses $Q_{act}$**: $\quad W_{\text{water}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H$

## 3.2 Derivation: Acceleration Head for Reciprocating Pump

### Step 1 — Crank-Piston Kinematics

Let $r$ be the crank radius and $\theta$ the crank angle from IDC. The piston displacement from IDC is:

$$y = r(1 - \cos\theta)$$

### Step 2 — Velocity of Piston

$$v = \frac{dy}{dt} = r \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = r \cdot \omega \cdot \sin\theta$$

### Step 3 — Acceleration of Piston

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = r \cdot \omega^2 \cdot \cos\theta$$

### Step 4 — Pressure Head Due to Acceleration

The water column in the suction pipe must accelerate with this piston motion. The required pressure head is:

$$h_a = \frac{a}{g} = \frac{r \cdot \omega^2 \cdot \cos\theta}{g}$$

### Step 5 — Maximum Acceleration Head

Maximum occurs at $\theta = 0°$ (i.e., at IDC, start of stroke):

$$\boxed{h_{a,\max} = \frac{r \cdot \omega^2}{g} = \frac{L \cdot \omega^2}{2g}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Problem Walkthrough (Solved Example)

### Problem Statement (KTU Model Question)

A single-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of 150 mm and a stroke of 300 mm. It runs at 60 rpm and lifts water against a suction head of 4 m and a delivery head of 20 m. Find:
1. Theoretical discharge
2. Actual discharge if slip is 3%
3. Power required if overall efficiency is 80%

### Given Data
- $D = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- $L = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.30 \text{ m}$
- $N = 60 \text{ rpm}$
- $H_s = 4 \text{ m}$
- $H_d = 20 \text{ m}$
- Slip = 3%
- $\eta_{\text{overall}} = 0.80$

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Piston Area**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2$$

$$\boxed{A = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Discharge**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} = \frac{0.01767 \times 0.30 \times 60}{60}$$

$$Q_{th} = 0.01767 \times 0.30 = 0.00530 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = 5.30 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 5.30 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Step 3 — Actual Discharge**

$$\text{Slip} = 3\% \text{ of } Q_{th} = 0.03 \times 0.00530 = 1.59 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_{act} = Q_{th} - \text{Slip} = 0.00530 - 0.000159$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = 5.141 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Step 4 — Total Head**

$$H = H_s + H_d = 4 + 20 = 24 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Power Required**

Water power (output): $\quad P_{\text{water}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H$

$$P_{\text{water}} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.005141 \times 24$$

$$P_{\text{water}} = 1210.4 \text{ W} = 1.21 \text{ kW}$$

Shaft power (input): $\quad P_{\text{shaft}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{water}}}{\eta_{\text{overall}}}$

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{1210.4}{0.80}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{shaft}} = 1513.0 \text{ W} \approx 1.51 \text{ kW}}$$

## 3.4 Derived Relations Summary (For Analytical Speed)

| Result | Expression |
| :--- | :--- |
| Discharge at speed change | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ (geometry fixed) |
| Power scaling with speed | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ (at constant head) |
| Head independence | $Q$ does NOT change with $H$ for PDP |
| Crank radius relation | $r = \dfrac{L}{2}$ |
| Maximum velocity | $v_{max} = r\omega$ (at $\theta = 90°$) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, 3 marks]`
**Define slip and coefficient of discharge for a positive displacement pump. State their typical relationship.**

**Model Answer:**
Slip is defined as the difference between the theoretical discharge and the actual discharge of a positive displacement pump:
$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$$

Coefficient of discharge $C_d$ is the ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge:
$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$

For reciprocating pumps, $C_d$ typically ranges from **0.85 to 0.99**, depending on valve design, pump wear, and operating speed. Higher $C_d$ indicates better sealing and lower leakage losses.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, 3 marks]`
**Differentiate between single-acting and double-acting reciprocating pumps.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Single-Acting | Double-Acting |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Working sides of piston | One side only | Both sides |
| Suction strokes per cycle | 1 | 2 |
| Delivery strokes per cycle | 1 | 2 |
| Discharge per cycle | $A \cdot L$ | $(2A - a) \cdot L$ |
| Water column continuity | Pulsating | More uniform |
| Construction | Simple | Complex (with piston rod) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Part B questions in ESE (End Semester Exam) carry 14 marks. You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each Part B has two sub-parts (typically 7 marks each).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the theoretical discharge of a single-acting reciprocating pump. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A single-acting reciprocating pump has a cylinder diameter of 200 mm and stroke length of 400 mm. It runs at 50 rpm. The suction head is 5 m and the delivery head is 25 m. If the slip is 4% and overall efficiency is 75%, calculate: (i) actual discharge, (ii) power required. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Derivation of Theoretical Discharge:**

**Step 1:** Volume of water delivered per stroke = Cross-sectional area of piston $\times$ Stroke length.

$$V = A \cdot L$$

where $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^2$ and $L$ is the stroke length.

**Step 2:** For a single-acting pump, one complete revolution of the crank produces one delivery stroke.

**Step 3:** Number of deliveries per minute = $N$ (where $N$ is rpm).

**Step 4:** Therefore, theoretical discharge per minute:

$$Q_{th,\text{min}} = A \cdot L \cdot N \quad \text{(m}^3/\text{min)}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to per second by dividing by 60:

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{(m}^3/\text{s)}}$$

For a double-acting pump, water is delivered on both sides of the piston during one revolution. Accounting for piston rod area $a$:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{(2A - a) \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$$

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $D = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$
- $L = 400 \text{ mm} = 0.40 \text{ m}$
- $N = 50 \text{ rpm}$
- $H_s = 5 \text{ m}$, $H_d = 25 \text{ m}$
- Slip = 4%, $\eta = 0.75$

**Step 1 — Piston Area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.20)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Discharge:**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{0.03142 \times 0.40 \times 50}{60} = \frac{0.6283}{60}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = 0.01047 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Step 3 — Actual Discharge (with 4% slip):**

$$\text{Slip} = 0.04 \times 0.01047 = 4.189 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_{act} = 0.01047 - 0.0004189$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = 0.01005 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 10.05 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Step 4 — Total Head:**

$$H = H_s + H_d = 5 + 25 = 30 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Water Power:**

$$P_{\text{water}} = \rho g Q_{act} H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.01005 \times 30$$

$$P_{\text{water}} = 2957.7 \text{ W} \approx 2.96 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Shaft Power:**

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{P_{\text{water}}}{\eta} = \frac{2957.7}{0.75}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{shaft}} = 3943.6 \text{ W} \approx 3.94 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a single-acting reciprocating pump with a neat sketch. Discuss the effect of acceleration head on the work done. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A double-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of 250 mm, stroke 500 mm, and piston rod diameter 50 mm. It runs at 40 rpm against suction and delivery heads of 6 m and 30 m respectively. Calculate the theoretical discharge and the power required if the slip is 3% and overall efficiency is 78%. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Working Principle:**
A single-acting reciprocating pump has one piston working in a cylinder. The piston is connected via a connecting rod to a crank driven by a prime mover (motor).

- **Suction Stroke (0° to 180°):** Piston moves from IDC to ODC. A partial vacuum forms in the cylinder. Atmospheric pressure pushes water up the suction pipe, opening the suction valve. The delivery valve remains closed.
- **Delivery Stroke (180° to 360°):** Piston moves from ODC to IDC. Water is pushed through the delivery valve into the delivery pipe. The suction valve closes.

**Effect of Acceleration Head:**
Since the piston follows simple harmonic motion, its velocity is not constant. The acceleration is maximum at the start and end of each stroke (at IDC and ODC) and zero at mid-stroke. This non-uniform motion requires an additional pressure head $h_a = \dfrac{r\omega^2}{g}\cos\theta$ to accelerate the water column in the pipes.

- During delivery, the effective head becomes $H_d + h_a$ at the start.
- During suction, the effective suction head becomes $H_s - h_a$ at the end.

This acceleration head can cause **separation** in the suction pipe if $h_a > H_s$, leading to vapor cavities and pump damage. To mitigate this, **air vessels** are installed which absorb the pressure fluctuations.

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $D = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$, $L = 500 \text{ mm} = 0.50 \text{ m}$
- Piston rod $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$, $N = 40 \text{ rpm}$
- $H_s = 6 \text{ m}$, $H_d = 30 \text{ m}$
- Slip = 3%, $\eta = 0.78$

**Step 1 — Areas:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.25)^2 = 0.04909 \text{ m}^2$$

$$a = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2 = 0.001963 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Discharge (Double-Acting):**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{(2A - a) \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{(2 \times 0.04909 - 0.001963) \times 0.50 \times 40}{60}$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{(0.09818 - 0.001963) \times 20}{60} = \frac{0.09622 \times 20}{60}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = 0.03207 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Step 3 — Actual Discharge:**

$$Q_{act} = Q_{th} - 0.03 \times Q_{th} = 0.97 \times 0.03207$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = 0.03111 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Step 4 — Total Head and Power:**

$$H = 6 + 30 = 36 \text{ m}$$

$$P_{\text{water}} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.03111 \times 36 = 10{,}988.9 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{10{,}988.9}{0.78}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{shaft}} = 14{,}088.4 \text{ W} \approx 14.09 \text{ kW}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Unit Errors:** Forgetting to convert mm to m is the **#1 reason** for losing 2-3 marks in Part B. Always write "0.15 m" instead of "150 mm" in the formula.
> - **Confusing Slip:** Some students mistakenly add slip to $Q_{th}$ instead of subtracting. **Remember**: $Q_{act} = Q_{th} - \text{Slip}$.
> - **Double-Acting Formula:** Many students write $Q_{th} = \dfrac{2 A L N}{60}$ and forget to subtract the piston rod area $a$. If rod area is given, subtract it. If not given, you can ignore it.
> - **Power Confusion:** $P_{\text{shaft}} > P_{\text{water}}$. Always divide water power by efficiency, never multiply.
> - **Crank Radius:** $r = L/2$ (not $r = L$). This is a frequent mistake in acceleration head derivations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision List for KTU Exam:**

- [x] Positive Displacement Pump = constant flow, independent of head.
- [x] **$Q_{th}$ (single-acting)** $= A \cdot L \cdot N / 60$.
- [x] **$Q_{th}$ (double-acting)** $= (2A - a) \cdot L \cdot N / 60$.
- [x] **Slip** $= Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ (positive quantity).
- [x] **$C_d$** $= Q_{act} / Q_{th}$ (ranges 0.85 - 0.99).
- [x] **Work done per second** (no acceleration) $= \rho g Q H$.
- [x] **Acceleration head** $h_a = r \omega^2 \cos\theta / g$.
- [x] **Max acceleration head** $h_{a,\max} = r\omega^2/g = L\omega^2/(2g)$.
- [x] **Power required** $P = \rho g Q_{act} H / \eta_{\text{overall}}$.
- [x] **$r = L/2$** and $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.
- [x] **Air vessel purpose:** Smoothens flow pulsations, prevents water hammer.
- [x] PDPs preferred for **viscous fluids** and **high-pressure** applications.
- [x] **Cannot run against closed valve** — risk of pipe burst (unlike centrifugal).
- [x] Rotary pumps (gear, vane, screw) deliver **continuous, non-pulsating flow**.
- [x] **Volume per revolution** is fixed by geometry in ALL PDPs.
- [x] **Negative slip** occurs when $Q_{act} > Q_{th}$ (rare; vibration-induced flow).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reciprocating Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Reciprocating Pumps # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reciprocating Pump:** A **positive displacement pump** that converts the rotational mechanical energy of a prime mover (typically an electric motor) into the reciprocating (back-and-forth linear) motion of a piston/plunger inside a closed cylinder, thereby displacing a fixed volume of fluid from the suction side to the delivery side against the system head. The discharge is essentially independent of the operating head pressure, making it a **constant discharge (constant volume) machine.** The classification under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 4)** is restricted to the following sub-types: | Sub-Type | Description | |---|---| | **Single-Acting Pump** | One suction and one delivery stroke per complete crank revolution (1 cycle/rev). | | **Double-Acting Pump** | One suction and one delivery stroke on **each side** of the piston per revolution (2 cycles/rev). | ## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Bicycle Pump" Concept > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box:** Have you ever used a bicycle pump? When you pull the handle outward, a vacuum is created inside the barrel — atmospheric pressure pushes air through the bottom (inlet) valve into the chamber. When you push the handle inward, the inlet valve slams shut, the compressed air is forced out through the side (outlet) valve into the tyre. **A reciprocating pump is simply a mechanized, high-precision, sealed version of this bicycle pump**, working hundreds of cycles per minute to lift water from a sump to a delivery tank. > The piston (or plunger) is connected to a **crank and connecting rod (slider-crank mechanism)**. The crank rotates at a uniform angular velocity $\omega$, but the piston velocity is **purely sinusoidal (simple harmonic)** at low crank speeds. ## 1.3 Principal Components of a Reciprocating Pump 1. **Cylinder** – the chamber in which the piston reciprocates. 2. **Piston / Plunger** – the displacing member. 3. **Suction Valve** – opens during the suction stroke; allows fluid entry; closes during delivery. 4. **Delivery Valve** – opens during the delivery stroke; allows fluid to leave; closes during suction. 5. **Crank, Connecting Rod, Crosshead** – converts rotary motion to reciprocating motion. 6. **Suction Pipe** – connects the sump/reservoir to the cylinder. 7. **Delivery Pipe** – delivers water from the cylinder to the required height. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):** The student must be proficient in the **analytical treatment** of single-acting pumps, including coefficient of discharge, slip, negative slip, and indicator diagram analysis. Numerical problems on acceleration head, friction head, and the **use of air vessels** are high-yield. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Working Cycle (Single-Acting Pump) The single-acting reciprocating pump operates in **two distinct strokes per revolution**: - **Suction Stroke (Forward Stroke):** As the piston retracts, the cylinder volume behind it increases. The delivery valve remains **closed** (held by the delivery water column + atmospheric pressure). The suction valve **opens**, and water is pushed up the suction pipe by atmospheric pressure acting on the sump surface. The cylinder fills with water. - **Delivery Stroke (Return Stroke):** The piston now advances. The suction valve **closes** (the returning water column traps the valve shut). Pressure inside the cylinder rises, exceeds the delivery head, and forces the **delivery valve open**. Water is pushed up the delivery pipe to the required height. > [!NOTE] > The single-acting pump delivers water **only during half** of every crank revolution. This pulsating discharge is a key disadvantage, and is the primary reason for the use of **air vessels**. ## 2.2 Theory of Slip, Negative Slip & Coefficient of Discharge In a real pump, the **actual discharge** is always less than the **theoretical discharge** because: 1. **Leakage** past the piston rings, stuffing box, and valves. 2. **Delayed valve closure** due to inertia of the water column and valve mechanism. 3. **Compression/expansion** of water (and any entrapped air) at the instant of valve opening/closing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Slip (Qs):** The difference between the theoretical discharge and the actual discharge. Always **positive** in well-designed pumps. > $$Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$$ > > **Negative Slip:** In rare cases (e.g., water with high air content, or extremely long delivery pipe), the actual discharge may **exceed** the theoretical discharge. This phenomenon, caused by the inertia of the heavy water column continuing to flow into the cylinder *after* the piston has already started the next stroke, is called **negative slip**. > > **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd):** > $$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{Q_{th} - Q_s}{Q_{th}}$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Single & Double-Acting) | # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Theoretical Discharge (Single-Acting) | $Q_{th}$ | $\dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | | 2 | Theoretical Discharge (Double-Acting) | $Q_{th}$ | $\dfrac{(2A - a) \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | | 3 | Slip | $Q_s$ | $Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | | 4 | Coefficient of Discharge | $C_d$ | $\dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | dimensionless | | 5 | Velocity of Piston (approx. SHM) | $V$ | $\omega r \left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta\right)$ | $\text{m/s}$ | | 6 | Acceleration of Piston | $a_p$ | $\omega^2 r \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | | 7 | Acceleration Head (Instantaneous) | $h_a$ | $\dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g}\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)$ | $\text{m}$ | | 8 | Max Acceleration Head (at $\theta=0$) | $h_{a,\max}$ | $\dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g}\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{L}\right)$ | $\text{m}$ | | 9 | Work Done / sec (no friction) | $W$ | $\rho g \cdot Q_{th} \cdot H_s$ | $\text{W (J/s)}$ | | 10 | Work Done / sec (with friction) | $W$ | $\rho g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot (H_s + h_f)$ | $\text{W (J/s)}$ | **Where:** $A$ = piston area; $a$ = piston rod (shank) area; $L$ = stroke length; $N$ = rpm; $\omega = 2\pi N/60$; $r$ = crank radius $= L/2$; $H_s$ = static suction head or total head; $h_f$ = total friction head loss. ## 2.4 Indicator Diagram — The Diagnostic Tool of a Pump The **Indicator Diagram** is a Pressure–Volume (or Pressure–Stroke) graph automatically drawn by a *Crosby Indicator* attached to the cylinder. It is the most important diagnostic for evaluating a pump's internal hydraulic losses. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Under ideal frictionless conditions, the indicator diagram is a perfect **rectangle** (ABCD). In reality, it deviates. Three types of diagrams are studied: > 1. **Theoretical Diagram (no friction, no acceleration):** Perfect rectangle. > 2. **Diagram with Acceleration Heads Only:** The horizontal constant-pressure lines become **curved** (parabolic). > 3. **Diagram with Acceleration + Friction:** Rounded corners; vertical line showing valve lift losses. ### 2.4.1 Curve of Acceleration Head ($h_a$ vs $\theta$) The acceleration head is **positive** (pressure rising) during the **first half** of the stroke ($\theta = 0$ to $\pi$) and **negative** (pressure dropping) during the second half ($\theta = \pi$ to $2\pi$). > At $\theta = 0$ (start of delivery): $h_{a,\max} = +\dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g}\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{L}\right)$ — pressure peak. > At $\theta = \pi$ (end of delivery): $h_{a,\min} = -\dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g}\left(1 - \dfrac{r}{L}\right)$ — pressure trough. ## 2.5 Air Vessels — Engineering Solution to Pulsation > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Imagine pushing water through a long, thin straw very fast — the water "sloshes" and the flow is uneven. If we attach a small chamber with a trapped pocket of air near the pump outlet, the air cushion **absorbs** the pressure surges during the fast part of the stroke and **releases** energy during the slow part. The flow becomes smooth. **Air Vessel (Suction Side):** Placed close to the pump on the suction pipe, it prevents the suction pipe column from accelerating/decelerating. **Acceleration head in the suction pipe is eliminated.** **Air Vessel (Delivery Side):** Placed close to the pump on the delivery pipe, it prevents the delivery pipe column from accelerating/decelerating. The long delivery pipe (after the air vessel) is treated as **static**; only the short pipe between the cylinder and the air vessel still has acceleration head effects. > Without an air vessel, the pump must accelerate/decelerate the **entire** water column length in both pipes — a tremendous power loss and risk of separation. The use of an air vessel reduces the maximum acceleration head drastically and **doubles the discharge** (theoretically) at the same speed, or permits **doubling the speed** at the same discharge. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Boiler feed pumps** in thermal power plants (high pressure, low flow). - **Oil extraction** in the petroleum industry (positive displacement of viscous crude). - **Hydraulic presses** and jacks (automotive workshops, scrap yards). - **Metering pumps** in chemical dosing plants (precise, pulsation-free dosing). - **High-rise building water supply** systems where constant flow is critical. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Discharge of a Single-Acting Pump Let us define the system parameters: - $A$ = cross-sectional area of the piston, in $\text{m}^2$ - $L$ = length of stroke, in $\text{m}$ - $N$ = speed of the crank, in $\text{rpm}$ - $r$ = crank radius $= L/2$, in $\text{m}$ **Step 1 — Volume displaced per stroke:** In one complete forward (suction) or return (delivery) stroke, the piston sweeps a volume equal to the area of the piston multiplied by the stroke length. The volume of water theoretically drawn in and then pushed out per stroke is: $$V_{stroke} = A \times L \quad [\text{m}^3]$$ **Step 2 — Number of strokes per minute:** In a single-acting pump, the piston completes one suction stroke and one delivery stroke per revolution. Therefore, the number of delivery strokes per minute equals the rpm of the crank: $$n_{strokes} = N \quad [\text{strokes/min}]$$ **Step 3 — Theoretical discharge per second:** The total theoretical volume discharged in one minute is $(A \cdot L \cdot N)$. To convert to per-second discharge, divide by 60: $$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Discharge of a Double-Acting Pump A double-acting pump has a piston **with a rod (shank)** on one side. The effective area of the piston is $A$ on the rodless face and $(A - a)$ on the rod face, where $a$ is the cross-sectional area of the piston rod. **Step 1 — Volume during the forward stroke (away from the rod):** The rodless face is active. Effective area $= A$. Volume per stroke $= A \cdot L$. **Step 2 — Volume during the return stroke (toward the rod):** The face with the rod is active. Effective area $= A - a$. Volume per stroke $= (A - a) \cdot L$. **Step 3 — Total volume per revolution:** $$V_{rev} = A \cdot L + (A - a) \cdot L = (2A - a) \cdot L$$ **Step 4 — Convert to per-second discharge:** $$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{(2A - a) \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Acceleration Head in a Reciprocating Pump > This is the **most critical derivation** in the KTU Module 4 syllabus. It is essential to master the position, velocity, and acceleration of the piston in a slider-crank mechanism. **Step 1 — Geometry of the slider-crank mechanism:** Let the crank rotate at angular velocity $\omega$. At any instant, the crank has rotated through an angle $\theta$ measured from the **inner dead centre (IDC)**. The piston displacement $x$ from IDC is: $$x = r(1 - \cos\theta) + \frac{r^2}{2L}(1 - \cos 2\theta)$$ Here, $r$ is the crank radius and $L$ is the connecting rod length. **Step 2 — Differentiate $x$ to get the piston velocity $V$:** The first term $r(1 - \cos\theta)$ gives $r\sin\theta \cdot \omega$ upon differentiation. The second term gives $\frac{r^2}{2L} \cdot 2\sin 2\theta \cdot \omega = \frac{r^2 \omega}{L}\sin 2\theta$. Combining: $$V = \frac{dx}{dt} = \omega r \sin\theta + \frac{\omega r^2}{L}\sin 2\theta$$ Factoring out $\omega r$: $$V = \omega r \left(\sin\theta + \frac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta\right)$$ **Step 3 — Differentiate $V$ to get the piston acceleration $a_p$:** Differentiate term by term with respect to time, noting $\frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega$: $$a_p = \frac{dV}{dt} = \omega^2 r \cos\theta + \frac{2\omega^2 r^2}{L}\cos 2\theta$$ Factoring out $\omega^2 r$: $$a_p = \omega^2 r \left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)$$ **Step 4 — Convert acceleration into acceleration head ($h_a$):** The acceleration head is the head required to produce this acceleration in the water column of length $L_{col}$ (full length of pipe). For a single-acting pump with pipe of length $L_p$ (since the pump has only one pipe — either suction or delivery — active at a time): $$h_a = \frac{L_p \cdot a_p}{g} = \frac{\omega^2 r \cdot L_p}{g}\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)$$ For the limiting case at $\theta = 0$ (start of stroke) and using $L_p = L$ (full length of pipe) and $\omega = 2\pi N/60$: $$\boxed{h_{a,\max} = \frac{L}{g}\omega^2 r \left(1 + \frac{r}{L}\right) = \frac{\omega^2 r L}{g}\left(1 + \frac{r}{L}\right)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** When an **air vessel is fitted on the delivery side**, only the short pipe between the cylinder and the air vessel experiences acceleration. The student must replace $L$ (the long pipe length) with the **short effective pipe length** in the formula. This is a common 7-mark derivation question in KTU ESE. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Work Done by the Pump (with and without Friction) **Case 1 — Ideal, No Friction:** The work done per second against the static head $H_s$ only is: $$W_1 = \rho g \cdot Q_{th} \cdot H_s \quad [\text{W (J/s)}]$$ **Case 2 — With Friction Losses:** In addition to $H_s$, the pump must overcome: - Friction head in suction pipe: $h_{f,s}$ - Friction head in delivery pipe: $h_{f,d}$ - Velocity head at delivery: $\dfrac{v_d^2}{2g}$ - Velocity head at suction: $\dfrac{v_s^2}{2g}$ (often negligible) - Acceleration head: $h_a$ (if no air vessel) The total work done per second is: $$W_2 = \rho g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot \left[H_s + h_{f,s} + h_{f,d} + \frac{v_d^2}{2g} + \frac{v_s^2}{2g} + h_a \right]$$ > **Note:** The energy spent on acceleration head is **not permanently lost**; it is recovered during the deceleration phase. The work done on the delivery stroke is partially **reclaimed** during the suction stroke. Hence, the *net* work done per cycle is theoretically the same as the no-acceleration case, provided there is no friction. This is why the KTU formula for work done (with friction) uses the **actual** discharge and a **total** head that includes both static and friction heads. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Indicator Diagram Interpretation | Region on Diagram | Meaning (Delivery Stroke) | Meaning (Suction Stroke) | |---|---|---| | **Horizontal top line (theoretical)** | Constant pressure during delivery | Constant vacuum during suction | | **Curved line replacing horizontal** | $h_a$ adds to pressure at start, subtracts at end | $h_a$ adds to vacuum at start, reduces at end | | **Vertical drop at valve opening** | Delivery valve lift loss | Suction valve lift loss | | **Area enclosed by curve** | Indicated work / cycle (used in IP calculation) | Indicated work / cycle | | **Area of rectangle vs. area inside curve** | Shows $h_f$ (friction) losses | Shows $h_f$ losses on suction | > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Error:** Students often confuse the **acceleration head curve** with a sinusoidal curve. It is actually a **parabolic (second-order) curve** in $\cos\theta$ and $\cos 2\theta$. For very long connecting rods ($L \to \infty$), the second term vanishes and the acceleration head becomes a pure cosine — the simple harmonic motion (SHM) approximation. The KTU exam expects the full expression. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Components & Flow > [!NOTE] > A reciprocating pump's physical drawing is highly mechanical and not easily rendered in Mermaid. Therefore, the following is a **block-level functional architecture** mapping the energy flow, fluid flow, and control valve logic — as permitted under the diagram fallback policy. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["PRIME MOVER
Electric Motor / Engine"] -->|Rotary Shaft| nodeB["CRANKSHAFT
Radius r, angular velocity w"] nodeB -->|Slider Crank Linkage| nodeC["CONNECTING ROD
Length L"] nodeC -->|Linear Reciprocation| nodeD["PISTON PLUNGER
Inside Cylinder"] nodeD -->|Displaces Fluid| nodeE["CYLINDER CHAMBER
Volume varies with stroke"] nodeE -->|Suction Stroke Open| nodeF["SUCTION VALVE
Closes on Delivery"] nodeE -->|Delivery Stroke Open| nodeG["DELIVERY VALVE
Closes on Suction"] nodeF -->|Inflow| nodeH["SUCTION PIPE
From Sump"] nodeG -->|Outflow| nodeI["DELIVERY PIPE
To Delivery Tank"] nodeI -.Air Vessel.-> nodeJ["AIR VESSEL DELIVERY
Cushion at Constant Pressure"] nodeH -.Air Vessel.-> nodeK["AIR VESSEL SUCTION
Prevents Cavitation"] nodeD -->|Mounted On| nodeL["CROSSHEAD AND STUFFING BOX
Seals Piston Rod"] nodeA -->|Power Input W| nodeM["INDICATOR DIAGRAM
Pressure vs Stroke"] nodeM -->|Diagnostic| nodeN["FRICTION AND ACCELERATION
HEAD ESTIMATION"] style nodeA fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333 style nodeD fill:#93c47d,stroke:#333 style nodeF fill:#6fa8dc,stroke:#333 style nodeG fill:#6fa8dc,stroke:#333 style nodeJ fill:#e69138,stroke:#333 style nodeM fill:#c27ba0,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Pressure–Stroke Indicator Diagram > The following flowchart represents the **state machine of pressure in the cylinder** during one complete cycle, mimicking the four quadrants of the indicator diagram. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeS1["INNER DEAD CENTRE
Suction Stroke Begins
Pressure LOW Vacuum"] nodeS2["MID SUCTION
Constant Vacuum
Theoretical Line"] nodeS3["OUTER DEAD CENTRE
End of Suction
Valve Cross Over"] nodeS4["START OF DELIVERY
Acceleration Head PEAK
Pressure ABOVE Static"] nodeS5["MID DELIVERY
Constant Pressure
Theoretical Line"] nodeS6["END OF DELIVERY
Acceleration Head MIN
Pressure BELOW Static"] nodeS1 -->|"Forward stroke
Piston retracts"| nodeS2 nodeS2 -->|"Piston reaches ODC"| nodeS3 nodeS3 -->|"Delivery valve opens
Pressure surges"| nodeS4 nodeS4 -->|"Decelerating phase"| nodeS5 nodeS5 -->|"Piston at IDC"| nodeS6 nodeS6 -->|"Delivery valve closes"| nodeS1 style nodeS1 fill:#cfe2f3 style nodeS3 fill:#fce5cd style nodeS4 fill:#ea9999 style nodeS6 fill:#ea9999 style nodeS5 fill:#d9ead3 ``` ## 4.3 Component Layout Schematic (Functional Block View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PUMPUNIT["PUMP ASSEMBLY"] pA["Cylinder"] pB["Piston with Rod"] pC["Suction Valve"] pD["Delivery Valve"] end subgraph DRIVETRAIN["DRIVE MECHANISM"] dA["Motor"] dB["Crank and Connecting Rod"] dC["Crosshead"] end subgraph AUXILIARY["AUXILIARIES"] aux1["Suction Pipe with Strainer"] aux2["Delivery Pipe"] aux3["Air Vessel on Suction"] aux4["Air Vessel on Delivery"] aux5["Pressure Gauge and Indicator"] end dA --> dB --> dC --> pB pB -.Slides in.-> pA pC --- pA pD --- pA aux1 --> pC pD --> aux2 aux3 --- aux1 aux4 --- aux2 aux5 --- pA style PUMPUNIT fill:#fff2cc style DRIVETRAIN fill:#d9d2e9 style AUXILIARY fill:#c9daf8 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagrammatic Takeaway:** Note the **two air vessels** — one on the suction and one on the delivery. In the KTU ESE, students are often asked: *"Where is the air vessel more effective — suction or delivery?"* The answer: **on the suction side**, it prevents **separation of water column** (cavitation). **On the delivery side**, it reduces **pulsation and shock** in the long delivery pipe and reduces the **acceleration head the pump must overcome**, increasing efficiency. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define slip and negative slip in a reciprocating pump. Under what conditions does negative slip occur?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Slip (1 mark):** The difference between the theoretical discharge and the actual discharge of a reciprocating pump. $Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$. It arises due to leakage past piston rings, valves, and stuffing box, and due to delayed valve closure caused by water inertia. - **Negative Slip (2 marks):** In rare operating conditions, the actual discharge **exceeds** the theoretical discharge. This is called negative slip. It occurs when: 1. The delivery pipe is **very long** and the moving water column has high inertia. 2. The pump is running at **very high speed**. 3. The delivery water column, due to its inertia, continues to flow into the cylinder **after** the piston has already started the next (suction) stroke, momentarily increasing the cylinder volume without the piston physically moving. 4. Also occurs in pumps handling **aerated water** (water with dissolved/entrapped air). > **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **What is an air vessel? Why is it provided on the suction side and delivery side of a reciprocating pump?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Air Vessel (1 mark):** A closed chamber, partially filled with air, connected to the suction or delivery pipe close to the pump cylinder. The trapped air acts as a **cushion** to absorb pressure pulsations. - **On Suction Side (1 mark):** Prevents acceleration and deceleration of the long suction water column, thereby avoiding **separation of the water column (cavitation)** at high pump speeds. It also ensures a uniform flow in the suction pipe. - **On Delivery Side (1 mark):** Absorbs pressure surges during the delivery stroke, releasing it during the suction stroke. This **doubles the discharge** at the same speed (or allows doubling the speed for the same discharge) and reduces shock loading on the delivery pipe. --- ## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > ### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024] > > **(a)** Derive the expression for the **acceleration head** of the water in the suction and delivery pipes of a reciprocating pump. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A single-acting reciprocating pump, running at **60 rpm**, has a stroke length of **30 cm** and a piston diameter of **20 cm**. The suction and delivery pipes are each **6 m long**. The suction head is **4 m** and the delivery head is **20 m**. Determine: **(i)** the theoretical discharge, **(ii)** the maximum acceleration head, and **(iii)** the work done per second if the coefficient of discharge is **0.90** and friction head loss in suction and delivery pipes is **2.5 m** combined. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution #### Part (a) — Derivation of Acceleration Head (7 marks) Refer to **Section 3.3** above for the full derivation. Key steps for the answer sheet: **Step 1:** Define the slider-crank mechanism. Piston displacement from inner dead centre (IDC) is: $$x = r(1 - \cos\theta) + \frac{r^2}{2L}(1 - \cos 2\theta)$$ **Step 2:** Differentiate to get velocity of the piston: $$V = \omega r \left(\sin\theta + \frac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta\right)$$ **Step 3:** Differentiate again to get acceleration: $$a_p = \omega^2 r \left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)$$ **Step 4:** Acceleration head is the head required to accelerate the water column of length $L_p$: $$h_a = \frac{L_p \cdot a_p}{g} = \frac{\omega^2 r L_p}{g}\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)$$ **Step 5:** Maximum acceleration head occurs at $\theta = 0$: $$h_{a,\max} = \frac{\omega^2 r L_p}{g}\left(1 + \frac{r}{L}\right)$$ #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** $N = 60$ rpm, $L = 30$ cm $= 0.3$ m, $D = 20$ cm $= 0.2$ m, $L_p = 6$ m, $H_s = 4$ m, $H_d = 20$ m, $C_d = 0.90$, $h_f = 2.5$ m. **Step 1 — Piston area:** $$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.2)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Theoretical discharge:** $$Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} = \frac{0.03142 \times 0.3 \times 60}{60} = 0.009425 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 9.425 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Crank radius and angular velocity:** $$r = \frac{L}{2} = 0.15 \text{ m}, \quad \omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 60}{60} = 2\pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 6.283 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 4 — Maximum acceleration head:** $$h_{a,\max} = \frac{\omega^2 r L_p}{g}\left(1 + \frac{r}{L}\right) = \frac{(6.283)^2 \times 0.15 \times 6}{9.81}\left(1 + \frac{0.15}{0.3}\right)$$ $$= \frac{39.48 \times 0.15 \times 6}{9.81} \times 1.5 = \frac{35.53}{9.81} \times 1.5 = 3.622 \times 1.5 = \boxed{5.43 \text{ m}}$$ **Step 5 — Actual discharge and work done:** $$Q_{act} = C_d \times Q_{th} = 0.90 \times 0.009425 = 0.008483 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ Total head against which pump works: $$H_{total} = H_s + H_d + h_f = 4 + 20 + 2.5 = 26.5 \text{ m}$$ Work done per second: $$W = \rho g Q_{act} H_{total} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.008483 \times 26.5$$ $$W = 9810 \times 0.008483 \times 26.5 = 83.22 \times 26.5 = \boxed{2205.3 \text{ W} \approx 2.205 \text{ kW}}$$ --- > ### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] (INTERNAL CHOICE) > > **(a)** What is meant by the **indicator diagram** of a reciprocating pump? Sketch and explain the indicator diagram with and without acceleration head and friction. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A double-acting reciprocating pump has a piston diameter of **25 cm** and a piston rod diameter of **5 cm**. The stroke length is **40 cm**. The pump runs at **50 rpm** and lifts water through a total static head of **30 m**. The slip is **3%** of the theoretical discharge. If the friction head loss in the suction and delivery pipes combined is **1.8 m**, determine: **(i)** the actual discharge in LPM, and **(ii)** the power required to drive the pump. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution #### Part (a) — Indicator Diagram Explanation (7 marks) **Definition (1 mark):** An indicator diagram is a graph of **pressure (or head) vs. piston displacement (stroke length)** recorded automatically by a Crosby Indicator attached to the cylinder. It is the **pressure-volume analogue of the pump's working cycle**. **Theoretical Diagram (no friction, no acceleration) — 2 marks:** - It is a **perfect rectangle** $ABCD$. - $A$ → $B$: constant pressure line during delivery (pressure $= H_s$ above atmospheric). - $B$ → $C$: instantaneous pressure drop at end of delivery (valve closes, suction begins). - $C$ → $D$: constant vacuum line during suction. - $D$ → $A$: instantaneous pressure rise at end of suction (delivery valve opens). **Diagram with Acceleration Head — 2 marks:** - The constant pressure and constant vacuum lines become **curved (parabolic)**. - At the **beginning of the delivery stroke** ($\theta = 0$), the curve is **above** the theoretical line by $h_{a,\max}$. - At the **end of the delivery stroke** ($\theta = \pi$), the curve is **below** the theoretical line. - Similarly for the suction stroke — the vacuum is **deeper** at the start and **shallower** at the end. **Diagram with Friction — 2 marks:** - The corners of the rectangle become **rounded** due to valve lift losses. - The areas between the curved actual diagram and the ideal rectangle represent **friction losses**. - The area of the actual diagram represents the **indicated work** (work actually done by the pump on the fluid). #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** $D = 25$ cm $= 0.25$ m, $d_{rod} = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m, $L = 40$ cm $= 0.4$ m, $N = 50$ rpm, $H_s = 30$ m, slip $= 3\%$, $h_f = 1.8$ m. **Step 1 — Piston and rod areas:** $$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.25)^2 = 0.04909 \text{ m}^2, \quad a = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2 = 0.001963 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Theoretical discharge (double-acting):** $$Q_{th} = \frac{(2A - a) L N}{60} = \frac{(2 \times 0.04909 - 0.001963) \times 0.4 \times 50}{60}$$ $$= \frac{(0.09818 - 0.001963) \times 20}{60} = \frac{0.09622 \times 20}{60} = \frac{1.9243}{60}$$ $$Q_{th} = 0.03207 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 32.07 \text{ L/s} = 32.07 \times 60 = \boxed{1924.2 \text{ LPM}}$$ **Step 3 — Actual discharge (after subtracting 3% slip):** $$Q_{act} = Q_{th} - Q_s = Q_{th}(1 - 0.03) = 0.03207 \times 0.97 = 0.03111 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = \boxed{1866.5 \text{ LPM}}$$ **Step 4 — Total head and power:** $$H_{total} = H_s + h_f = 30 + 1.8 = 31.8 \text{ m}$$ $$P = \frac{\rho g Q_{act} H_{total}}{1000} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.03111 \times 31.8}{1000}$$ $$P = 9.81 \times 0.03111 \times 31.8 = 9.81 \times 0.9893 = \boxed{9.71 \text{ kW}}$$ --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Mark-Loss Zones in Reciprocating Pump Problems (KTU Board):** > 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Forgetting to convert cm to m. The acceleration head formula is highly sensitive to units. **Always convert L, D, and r to meters first.** > 2. **Confusing Single-Acting vs. Double-Acting Discharge Formula:** Single-acting uses $A \cdot L \cdot N / 60$. Double-acting uses $(2A - a) \cdot L \cdot N / 60$. Mixing them up leads to a **complete wrong answer**. > 3. **Forgetting the Rod Area in Double-Acting Pumps:** Many students write $2A \cdot L \cdot N / 60$ for double-acting pumps. The piston rod reduces the effective area on one side, so the correct term is $(2A - a)$, not $2A$. > 4. **Using $Q_{th}$ instead of $Q_{act}$ in the Work Done Formula:** The work done formula uses **actual** discharge. If the problem gives $C_d$ or slip percentage, you **must** use $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th}$ or $Q_{act} = (1 - \text{slip fraction}) \cdot Q_{th}$. > 5. **Ignoring Friction Head:** The pump works against **static head + friction head**. Forgetting $h_f$ leads to an underestimation of the required power — a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction in a 14-mark problem. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Reciprocating pumps** are **positive displacement** machines — discharge is theoretically independent of head. - [x] **Single-acting** → 1 delivery stroke per revolution → $Q_{th} = ALN/60$. - [x] **Double-acting** → 2 delivery strokes per revolution → $Q_{th} = (2A - a)LN/60$. - [x] **Slip** $Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ is always **positive** in normal pumps. - [x] **Negative slip** can occur in long delivery pipes at high speeds (water inertia effect). - [x] **Coefficient of discharge** $C_d = Q_{act}/Q_{th}$ — typically **0.85 to 0.99** in well-designed pumps. - [x] **Piston velocity** in a slider-crank: $V = \omega r (\sin\theta + \frac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta)$. - [x] **Piston acceleration** in a slider-crank: $a_p = \omega^2 r (\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta)$. - [x] **Max acceleration head** (at $\theta=0$): $h_{a,\max} = \dfrac{\omega^2 r L_p}{g}\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{L}\right)$. - [x] **Indicator diagram** = perfect rectangle (ideal) → curved lines (with acceleration) → rounded corners (with friction). - [x] **Air vessels** double the discharge (or double the permissible speed) by decoupling the long pipe from the cylinder. - [x] **Air vessel on suction side** prevents water column separation (cavitation). - [x] **Air vessel on delivery side** reduces pressure pulsation and shock. - [x] **Work done with friction** = $\rho g Q_{act} (H_s + h_{f,s} + h_{f,d} + v^2/2g + h_a)$. - [x] **Acceleration head is not a permanent loss** — energy is recovered during the deceleration phase, BUT this recovery is reduced by friction. - [x] **Most common KTU mistake:** using $Q_{th}$ instead of $Q_{act}$ in power calculations. > 🏁 **End of Module 4 Notes — Reciprocating Pumps. Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE!**

topics.title: Air Vessels and Their Purposes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air Vessels and Their Purposes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Vessel:** An air vessel is a large, closed, airtight cast-iron chamber fitted either on the delivery pipe or on the suction pipe (or both) of a reciprocating pump. It is partially filled with water at the bottom and **compressed air at the top**, which acts as a cushion (or "accumulator") to neutralize the pulsating nature of the discharge from the reciprocating pump.

The compressed air inside the vessel stores energy during the delivery stroke (when pressure rises) and releases it during the suction stroke (when pressure would otherwise fall), thereby ensuring a **nearly continuous and uniform rate of flow** in the pipeline.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pulse Smoother"

Think of an **automobile shock absorber** or a **syringe connected to a balloon**:
- If you press a syringe whose outlet is connected to a thin pipe, water comes out in spurts (pulsating).
- Now imagine attaching a small balloon (filled with air) to the outlet pipe just before the syringe. As you push the plunger, water enters the balloon and compresses the trapped air. When you pull the plunger back, the balloon re-expands and pushes water out — the discharge is now **smooth and continuous**.

The balloon is doing exactly what an **air vessel** does: absorbing pressure peaks and releasing stored energy during low-pressure periods.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Module-4 question bank frequently asks students to (a) define the air vessel, (b) state its purposes (typically 4–6 bullets), and (c) explain how it reduces acceleration head on the suction side. Memorize the purposes in the order presented below.

## 1.3 Types of Air Vessels (Based on Location)

| S.No. | Type | Location | Primary Purpose |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Delivery-side air vessel | On the delivery pipe, close to the pump | Smoothens delivery flow, reduces friction & inertia losses |
| 2 | Suction-side air vessel | On the suction pipe, close to the pump | Prevents separation (cavitation) on suction side, allows higher pump speeds |

## 1.4 Six Engineering Purposes of an Air Vessel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Big Six" Purposes — High-Yield KTU Answer Points:**

1. **Continuous, uniform rate of discharge** is obtained in the delivery pipe (instead of intermittent gushes).
2. **Saving of power** required to drive the pump (reduction of friction and acceleration work).
3. The pump may be run at a **higher speed** without the risk of separation in the suction pipe.
4. The **maximum velocity and acceleration** of water in the delivery pipe are greatly reduced.
5. The **maximum speed** of water in the suction pipe is limited to a safe value (suppresses separation).
6. The **efficiency of the pump** is improved because slip and friction head are reduced.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Notation Adopted Throughout the Analysis

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| $A$ | Cross-sectional area of pump piston | $\text{m}^2$ |
| $a$ | Cross-sectional area of delivery/suction pipe | $\text{m}^2$ |
| $L$ | Length of stroke of piston | $\text{m}$ |
| $r$ | Crank radius $= L/2$ | $\text{m}$ |
| $N$ | Pump speed | $\text{rpm}$ |
| $\omega$ | Angular velocity of crank $= 2\pi N / 60$ | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| $L_d$ | Length of delivery pipe | $\text{m}$ |
| $L_s$ | Length of suction pipe | $\text{m}$ |
| $f$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction coefficient | dimensionless |
| $d$ | Diameter of pipe | $\text{m}$ |
| $H$ | Total static head $= H_s + H_d$ | $\text{m}$ |
| $\rho$ | Density of water $= 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity $= 9.81$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ |

**Assumption:** The motion of the piston (and hence the water in the pipe, without air vessel) is **simple harmonic**. This is the standard KTU-board assumption.

## 2.2 The "Without Air Vessel" Scenario — Pulsating Flow

With simple harmonic motion, the velocity of the piston is:

$$
v_p \;=\; r\,\omega\,\sin\theta
$$

Because the water column in the delivery pipe has cross-section $a$ (versus $A$ for the piston), the **velocity of water in the pipe is amplified** by the area ratio:

$$
u \;=\; \frac{A}{a}\,v_p \;=\; \frac{A}{a}\,r\omega\sin\theta
$$

This means the water velocity in the delivery pipe **starts from zero, peaks at $u_{\max} = (A/a)\,r\omega$ at mid-stroke, and falls back to zero**. The energy spent accelerating this long column of water every stroke is largely wasted as friction heat.

## 2.3 The "With Air Vessel" Scenario — Uniformised Flow

The air vessel contains compressed air that pushes back when the piston decelerates. The net effect is that the water in the delivery pipe **moves with a constant average velocity $\bar u$** — no pulsation, no acceleration, no kinetic-energy waste.

$$
\bar u \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi} u\,d\theta \;=\; \frac{2\,A\,r\,\omega}{\pi\,a}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The area ratio $A/a$ is typically very large (pipes are much smaller than the cylinder), so $\bar u$ can be **much smaller** than $u_{\max}$. Lower velocity $\Rightarrow$ lower friction loss $\Rightarrow$ less power consumed.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the formulas in this table — they appear in nearly every KTU Module-4 derivation/numerical.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Velocity of piston (SHM) | $v_p = r\omega\sin\theta$ | $\theta$ measured from inner dead centre |
| 2 | Velocity of water in delivery pipe (no AV) | $u = (A/a)\,r\omega\sin\theta$ | Same waveform as piston, scaled up |
| 3 | Maximum velocity in pipe | $u_{\max} = (A/a)\,r\omega$ | At $\theta = 90°$ |
| 4 | Average uniform velocity with AV | $\bar u = 2\,A\,r\omega / (\pi a)$ | = Mean velocity over forward stroke |
| 5 | Ratio of friction losses (no AV / with AV) | $\dfrac{W_{f,\text{no AV}}}{W_{f,\text{AV}}} = \dfrac{\pi^2}{8} \approx 1.2337$ | Always $> 1$ |
| 6 | Friction work saved per second | $W_{s,f} = \left(\dfrac{\pi^2}{8} - 1\right) \rho g Q\,h_{f,\text{AV}}$ | $Q = ALN/60$ |
| 7 | Friction head (with AV, uniform flow) | $h_{f,\text{AV}} = \dfrac{4 f L_d}{2 g d} \cdot \dfrac{4 A^2 r^2 \omega^2}{\pi^2 a^2}$ | Darcy–Weisbach form |
| 8 | Inertia work saved per cycle (single-acting) | $W_{s,i}^{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{\rho L_d A^2 r^2 \omega^2}{2 a}$ | $= \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,u_{\max}^2$ where $m = \rho a L_d$ |
| 9 | Inertia work saved per second | $W_{s,i} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \rho L_d A^2 r^2 N^3}{108000\, a}$ | Use $N$ in rpm |
| 10 | Total saving in work per second | $W_{s,\text{total}} = W_{s,f} + W_{s,i}$ | Add both components |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Audit (always check):** $A$ in m², $r$ in m, $\omega$ in rad/s, $N$ in rpm, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $g$ in m/s². Output of work formulas will be in **watts (J/s)**. Converting $N$ (rpm) to $\omega$ (rad/s): $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Beyond reciprocating pumps, the air-vessel principle powers modern **hydraulic accumulators** (used in presses, lifts, braking systems), **water-hammer arrestors** on long pipelines, **pulsation dampeners** on positive-displacement chemical-metering pumps, and the **nitrogen pre-charge** systems in fire-fighting and municipal water-supply networks. The same physics — compressible cushion absorbing kinetic energy — recurs everywhere fluid flow must be smooth despite an inherently pulsating source.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Saving in Work Against Friction (Single-Acting Pump, Delivery Side)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Show that fitting an air vessel reduces the friction-head loss in the delivery pipe by a fixed factor of $\pi^2/8 \approx 1.2337$.

**Step 1 — Set up the friction head without air vessel.**

By the Darcy–Weisbach equation, the **instantaneous** friction head in the delivery pipe is:

$$
h_f(\theta) \;=\; \frac{4\,f\,L_d}{2\,g\,d}\,u^2 \;=\; \frac{4\,f\,L_d}{2\,g\,d}\left(\frac{A}{a}\,r\omega\right)^{\!2}\sin^2\theta
$$

**Step 2 — Average the friction head over the forward stroke.**

The average friction head over one forward stroke ($0 \le \theta \le \pi$) is:

$$
\bar h_{f,\text{no AV}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi} h_f(\theta)\,d\theta
$$

Substituting and pulling out the constants:

$$
\bar h_{f,\text{no AV}} \;=\; \frac{4\,f\,L_d}{2\,g\,d\,\pi}\left(\frac{A}{a}\right)^{\!2} r^2\omega^2 \int_0^{\pi}\sin^2\theta\,d\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral.**

Using the standard result $\displaystyle\int_0^{\pi}\sin^2\theta\,d\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$:

$$
\bar h_{f,\text{no AV}} \;=\; \frac{4\,f\,L_d}{2\,g\,d}\left(\frac{A}{a}\right)^{\!2} r^2\omega^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\pi}\cdot\frac{\pi}{2} \;=\; \frac{4\,f\,L_d}{2\,g\,d}\left(\frac{A}{a}\right)^{\!2}\frac{r^2\omega^2}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute friction head with air vessel (uniform flow).**

With the air vessel, velocity in the delivery pipe is uniform at $\bar u = 2 A r \omega / (\pi a)$. The friction head is therefore constant:

$$
h_{f,\text{AV}} \;=\; \frac{4\,f\,L_d}{2\,g\,d}\,\bar u^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{4\,f\,L_d}{2\,g\,d}\left(\frac{2\,A\,r\,\omega}{\pi\,a}\right)^{\!2} \;=\; \frac{4\,f\,L_d}{2\,g\,d}\cdot\frac{4\,A^2 r^2\omega^2}{\pi^2 a^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Form the ratio.**

$$
\frac{\bar h_{f,\text{no AV}}}{h_{f,\text{AV}}} \;=\; \dfrac{\dfrac{A^2 r^2 \omega^2}{2 a^2}}{\dfrac{4 A^2 r^2 \omega^2}{\pi^2 a^2}} \;=\; \frac{\pi^2}{8}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute saving in friction work per second.**

Let $Q = ALN/60$ be the discharge. Then the friction power (work/sec) **without** air vessel is $P_{f,\text{no AV}} = \rho g Q \,\bar h_{f,\text{no AV}}$ and **with** air vessel is $P_{f,\text{AV}} = \rho g Q\, h_{f,\text{AV}}$. The saving is:

$$
\boxed{\,W_{s,f} \;=\; \left(\frac{\pi^2}{8}-1\right)\,\rho\,g\,Q\,h_{f,\text{AV}} \;=\; \rho\,g\,Q\left(\bar h_{f,\text{no AV}} - h_{f,\text{AV}}\right)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The factor $(\pi^2/8 - 1) \approx 0.2337$ means **≈ 23 % of the friction power is saved** by fitting the air vessel.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Saving in Work Against Inertia (Single-Acting Pump, Delivery Side)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Quantify the kinetic-energy loss that occurs every stroke as the long water column is accelerated and then abruptly decelerated. The air vessel "traps" this kinetic energy in the compressed air and returns it the next stroke.

**Step 1 — Mass of water in the delivery pipe.**

$$
m \;=\; \rho \cdot a \cdot L_d
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum velocity attained at mid-stroke.**

$$
u_{\max} \;=\; \frac{A}{a}\,r\,\omega
$$

**Step 3 — Kinetic energy stored at the end of the forward stroke.**

$$
\text{KE}_{\max} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,m\,u_{\max}^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,(\rho a L_d)\left(\frac{A r \omega}{a}\right)^{\!2} \;=\; \frac{\rho L_d A^2 r^2 \omega^2}{2 a}
$$

**Step 4 — What happens per cycle (single-acting pump)?**

| Phase | Event | Energy Budget |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Forward stroke (0 → π) | Piston accelerates water from 0 to $u_{\max}$ | Pump does extra work $= \text{KE}_{\max}$, stored as KE |
| Return stroke (π → 2π) | No pumping; water column decelerates 0 to 0 | Stored KE → friction heat (wasted) |

So **per cycle**, an amount of energy $\text{KE}_{\max}$ is wasted — supplied by the pump on the forward stroke and lost as heat on the return stroke.

**Step 5 — Convert to work per second.**

A single-acting pump runs at $N$ strokes/min $\Rightarrow$ $N/60$ cycles per second:

$$
W_{s,i}^{\text{per sec}} \;=\; \frac{N}{60}\cdot\frac{\rho L_d A^2 r^2 \omega^2}{2 a}
$$

**Step 6 — Replace $\omega$ by $N$.**

Since $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$, we have $\omega^2 = 4\pi^2 N^2 / 3600$:

$$
W_{s,i} \;=\; \frac{N}{60}\cdot\frac{\rho L_d A^2 r^2}{2 a}\cdot\frac{4\pi^2 N^2}{3600} \;=\; \frac{4\pi^2 \rho L_d A^2 r^2 N^3}{60\cdot 2\cdot 3600\,a} \;=\; \frac{\pi^2 \rho L_d A^2 r^2 N^3}{108000\,a}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,W_{s,i} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}\,\rho\,L_{d}\,A^{2}\,r^{2}\,N^{3}}{108000\,a}\ \ \text{watts (saving per second)}\,}
$$

**Step 7 — Total saving per second.**

$$
\boxed{\,W_{s,\text{total}} \;=\; W_{s,f} \;+\; W_{s,i}\,}
$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Reciprocating Pump with Air Vessel

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a typical single-acting reciprocating pump fitted with an air vessel on the delivery side. Each block represents a physical sub-system; arrows show the direction of fluid flow and energy transfer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sumpA[Sump Water Source] -->|Suction Lift Hs| pipeB[Suction Pipe with Strainer]
    pipeB -->|Negative Pressure| valveC[Suction Non Return Valve]
    valveC -->|Admission| cylD[Pump Cylinder and Piston]
    cylD -->|Crank and Connecting Rod| crankE[Crank Shaft with Flywheel]
    cylD -->|Discharge Stroke| valveF[Delivery Non Return Valve]
    valveF -->|Pulsating Flow| pipeG[Delivery Pipe Length Ld]
    pipeG -->|Branch Connection| airV[Air Vessel Compressed Air on Top Water Below]
    airV -->|Uniform Flow| tankH[Delivery Tank at Height Hd]

    airV -.->|Stores Energy in Air Cushion| airV
    crankE -.->|Rotational Input| cylD

    style sumpA fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style cylD fill:#ffe599,stroke:#806000
    style airV fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:3px
    style tankH fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Water is lifted from the sump by the piston's negative-pressure action, pushed through the delivery valve into the delivery pipe. **At the branch point, the air vessel is fitted in parallel with the pipe** — water can either flow past it (towards the tank) or accumulate in it (compressing the air). The compressed air cushion then re-feeds the pipe during the suction stroke.

## 4.2 Operational State Machine — How the Air Vessel "Smooths" the Flow

The following diagram captures the **two-phase cycle** that produces uniform discharge from inherently pulsating piston motion.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stateA[State 1 Piston at Inner Dead Centre] -->|Forward Stroke Begins theta 0| stateB[State 2 Piston Accelerates]
    stateB -->|Mid Stroke theta pi by 2| stateC[State 3 Piston at Max Velocity umax]
    stateC -->|Forward Stroke Ends theta pi| stateD[State 4 Piston at Outer Dead Centre]
    stateD -->|Return Stroke Begins| stateE[State 5 Piston Decelerates Air Cushion Pushes Water Out]
    stateE -->|Mid Return theta 3pi by 2| stateF[State 6 Piston at Min Velocity 0]
    stateF -->|Return Stroke Ends theta 2pi| stateA

    stateB -. Compressed Air Stores Energy .-> stateB
    stateE -. Compressed Air Releases Energy .-> stateE

    style stateC fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000
    style stateF fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13
    style airV2[ ]:::hidden
    classDef hidden display:none
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of the cycle:**
> - During the forward stroke (States 2 → 3 → 4), part of the water **diverts into the air vessel**, compressing the trapped air.
> - During the return stroke (States 5 → 6 → 1), the **compressed air expands** and pushes this stored water back into the delivery pipe.
> - The net result: water in the delivery pipe **between the air vessel and the tank moves at nearly constant velocity $\bar u$** regardless of the piston's instantaneous position.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]**

### Q1. Define an air vessel. List any four purposes of fitting an air vessel in a reciprocating pump. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
An **air vessel** is a closed, airtight chamber partially filled with compressed air at the top and water at the bottom, fitted on the delivery or suction pipe of a reciprocating pump to neutralize the pulsating nature of flow.

**Four purposes:**

1. To obtain a **continuous and uniform rate of discharge** in the delivery pipe.
2. To **save power** required to drive the pump (reducing friction and acceleration losses).
3. To allow the pump to be run at a **higher speed** without risk of separation in the suction pipe.
4. To **reduce the maximum velocity and acceleration** of water in the delivery/suction pipe.

*(Any four of the six listed in §1.4 accepted.)*

---

### Q2. How does fitting an air vessel on the suction pipe prevent separation of water in a reciprocating pump? `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
Separation occurs when the **acceleration head** $H_a = (L_s/g)\cdot(A/a)\,r\omega^2\cos\theta$ exceeds the available suction head during the early part of the stroke. Fitting an air vessel on the suction pipe causes the water in the suction pipe to move with **nearly uniform velocity**, eliminating the acceleration term. Thus the maximum suction-side acceleration head is suppressed to a safe value below the available NPSH, preventing column separation and allowing the pump to run at higher speeds safely.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / May 2025 Pattern]**

### Question A — Friction-Saving Focus `[14 Marks]`

**(a)** *Derive an expression for the work saved per second against friction by fitting an air vessel on the delivery pipe of a single-acting reciprocating pump. State clearly the assumptions made.* `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. List notation (A, a, r, ω, N, L_d, f, d) and the SHM assumption for piston motion.
2. Write $v_p = r\omega\sin\theta$ and $u = (A/a)\,r\omega\sin\theta$.
3. Write Darcy friction head $h_f = 4fL_d u^2/(2gd)$ and average over $[0,\pi]$ using $\int\sin^2\theta\,d\theta = \pi/2$ → obtain $\bar h_{f,\text{no AV}} = (4fL_d/(2gd))(A^2 r^2\omega^2/(2a^2))$.
4. With air vessel, $\bar u = 2Ar\omega/(\pi a)$, giving $h_{f,\text{AV}} = (4fL_d/(2gd))(4A^2 r^2\omega^2/(\pi^2 a^2))$.
5. Form ratio $= \pi^2/8$.
6. Work saved $= (\pi^2/8 - 1)\cdot \rho g Q\,h_{f,\text{AV}}$ where $Q = ALN/60$.

*(Full step-by-step derivation is given in §3.1 above — present it verbatim with units.)*

**(b)** *A single-acting reciprocating pump has a piston of diameter $150\ \text{mm}$ and stroke length $300\ \text{mm}$, running at $60\ \text{rpm}$. The delivery pipe is $30\ \text{m}$ long with diameter $75\ \text{mm}$. Taking $f = 0.006$, calculate the work saved per second against friction when an air vessel is fitted on the delivery pipe.* `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

*Given:* $D = 0.15\ \text{m} \Rightarrow A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m}^2$; $L = 0.3\ \text{m} \Rightarrow r = 0.15\ \text{m}$; $N = 60\ \text{rpm} \Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi(60)/60 = 2\pi\ \text{rad/s}$; $d = 0.075\ \text{m} \Rightarrow a = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$; $L_d = 30\ \text{m}$; $f = 0.006$.

**Step 1 — Average velocity with air vessel:**

$$
\bar u = \frac{2 A r \omega}{\pi a} = \frac{2 \times 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.15 \times 2\pi}{\pi \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.40\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Friction head (with air vessel):**

$$
h_{f,\text{AV}} = \frac{4 f L_d}{2 g d}\,\bar u^{\,2} = \frac{4 \times 0.006 \times 30}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.075}\times (2.40)^2 = 0.4893 \times 5.76 = 2.819\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Discharge:**

$$
Q = \frac{A L N}{60} = \frac{1.767 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.3 \times 60}{60} = 5.30 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Friction work saved per second:**

$$
W_{s,f} = \left(\frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1\right)\,\rho\,g\,Q\,h_{f,\text{AV}}
$$

$$
W_{s,f} = (1.2337 - 1) \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 5.30 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.819
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{s,f} \approx 34.26\ \text{W}}
$$

---
