### Question B — Inertia-Saving Focus `[14 Marks]` (Internal Choice)

**(a)** *Derive an expression for the work saved per second against inertia of the water column by fitting an air vessel on the delivery pipe of a single-acting reciprocating pump.* `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. Mass of water in delivery pipe: $m = \rho\, a\, L_d$.
2. Max velocity in pipe: $u_{\max} = (A/a)\,r\omega$.
3. KE at end of forward stroke: $\text{KE} = \tfrac{1}{2} m u_{\max}^2 = \rho L_d A^2 r^2 \omega^2 / (2a)$.
4. Per cycle, this KE is wasted (becomes friction heat during the return stroke).
5. Per second with $N$ strokes/min: $W_{s,i} = (N/60) \cdot \rho L_d A^2 r^2 \omega^2/(2a)$.
6. Substituting $\omega = 2\pi N/60$:

$$
W_{s,i} = \frac{\pi^2 \rho L_d A^2 r^2 N^3}{108000\,a}
$$

*(Full step-by-step derivation is given in §3.2 above.)*

**(b)** *A single-acting reciprocating pump has bore $200\ \text{mm}$ and stroke $400\ \text{mm}$. The delivery pipe is $25\ \text{m}$ long and $100\ \text{mm}$ in diameter. The pump runs at $50\ \text{rpm}$. Calculate the work saved per second against inertia of the water column if an air vessel is fitted. Take $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$.* `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

*Given:* $D = 0.2\ \text{m} \Rightarrow A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.2)^2 = 3.142 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m}^2$; $L = 0.4\ \text{m} \Rightarrow r = 0.2\ \text{m}$; $d = 0.1\ \text{m} \Rightarrow a = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$; $L_d = 25\ \text{m}$; $N = 50\ \text{rpm}$.

**Step 1 — Mass of water in delivery pipe:**

$$
m = \rho\, a\, L_d = 1000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 = 196.35\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum velocity in pipe:**

$$
u_{\max} = \frac{A}{a}\,r\,\omega = \frac{3.142 \times 10^{-2}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} \times 0.2 \times \frac{2\pi \times 50}{60} = 4 \times 0.2 \times 5.236 = 4.189\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Wasted KE per cycle:**

$$
\text{KE} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,u_{\max}^{\,2} = 0.5 \times 196.35 \times (4.189)^2 = 1722.7\ \text{J}
$$

**Step 4 — Cycles per second:**

$$
\frac{N}{60} = \frac{50}{60} = 0.8333\ \text{cycles/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Work saved per second:**

$$
W_{s,i} = 0.8333 \times 1722.7 = 1435.6\ \text{W}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{s,i} \approx 1.436\ \text{kW}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks on Air-Vessel questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\pi^2/8$ factor:** Many students write the formula for average velocity $\bar u = 2Ar\omega/(\pi a)$ correctly but then incorrectly compute the ratio of friction losses as "1" (instead of $\pi^2/8$). The $\pi^2/8$ arises specifically from $\int_0^\pi \sin^2\theta\,d\theta = \pi/2$ and is **non-negotiable**.
>
> 2. **Mixing up $r$ and $L$:** Use $r = L/2$ (crank radius = half stroke). The formulas above are derived with $r$, not $L$. **Substitute $L/2$ for $r$ consistently.**
>
> 3. **Wrong unit of $N$:** The compact formula $W_{s,i} = \pi^2\rho L_d A^2 r^2 N^3 / (108000\,a)$ requires **$N$ in rpm**. If you compute $\omega$ first and use $W = \tfrac{N}{60}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}m u_{\max}^2$, then $\omega$ must be in rad/s. Keep these straight.
>
> 4. **Not stating assumptions:** KTU answer scripts demand explicit mention of the **simple-harmonic-motion assumption** for full marks in derivation questions. Add it before Step 1.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to multiply by $\rho g$:** Friction-power formulas need $\rho g Q h_f$, not just $Q h_f$. Many students drop the $\rho g$ and lose 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Stating only friction saving:** When the question asks for "work saved by fitting an air vessel", many students compute **only the friction saving** $W_{s,f}$, forgetting the **inertia saving** $W_{s,i}$. The total saving is $W_{s,f} + W_{s,i}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Air Vessels and Their Purposes"**

- **Definition:** Closed chamber with compressed air on top + water below, fitted on the suction or delivery pipe of a reciprocating pump.
- **Six core purposes** (memorize): uniform discharge, power saving, higher safe running speed, reduced max velocity/acceleration, suppressed separation on suction, improved efficiency.
- **Two locations:** delivery-side (most common) and suction-side (to prevent separation).
- **Without AV:** water velocity in delivery pipe $= (A/a)\,r\omega\sin\theta$ — pulsating, max at mid-stroke.
- **With AV:** water velocity becomes uniform at $\bar u = 2Ar\omega/(\pi a)$.
- **Friction ratio:** $\pi^2/8 \approx 1.2337$ (memorize this number — it is the single most-tested constant in this topic).
- **Friction saving formula:** $W_{s,f} = (\pi^2/8 - 1)\cdot\rho g Q h_{f,\text{AV}}$.
- **Inertia saving (single-acting, per cycle):** $W_{s,i}^{\text{cycle}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,u_{\max}^2$ where $m = \rho a L_d$.
- **Inertia saving (per second, single-acting):** $W_{s,i} = \pi^2 \rho L_d A^2 r^2 N^3 / (108000\,a)$ with $N$ in rpm.
- **Total saving per second:** $W_{s,\text{total}} = W_{s,f} + W_{s,i}$.
- **Limitation on suction side:** Suction-side air vessel removes the acceleration head, eliminating column-separation risk and permitting higher pump speeds.
- **Double-acting pumps:** Save less inertia work (because water never fully stops between strokes), but the **same $\pi^2/8$ friction ratio** applies because the velocity waveform remains sinusoidal within each half-stroke.
- **Always state the SHM assumption** at the start of any derivation in the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slip
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Slip

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Reciprocating Pumps** (a core sub-topic of Module 4: Hydraulic Machines), **Slip** is defined as the difference between the *theoretical discharge* (the volume of liquid that *should* ideally be delivered per stroke, based on geometric piston displacement) and the *actual discharge* (the volume of liquid that is *actually* delivered per stroke as measured at the pump outlet).

Mathematically, the **Slip (in volume per stroke)** is expressed as:

$$
S_L = Q_{\text{theoretical}} - Q_{\text{actual}}
$$

The non-dimensional form, **Percentage Slip (%Slip)**, is universally adopted in KTU board examinations:

$$
\% \text{Slip} = \frac{Q_{\text{theoretical}} - Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim standard):** *"Slip of a reciprocating pump is the difference between the theoretical discharge and the actual discharge of the pump, expressed as a percentage of the theoretical discharge."* — Memorize this phrasing for **2-mark definition questions**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **bailer bucket (ചിരട്ട ബക്കറ്റ്)** used in a Kerala village well:

- You dip the bucket into the well (the well = the suction/source).
- As you lift it, theoretically, the bucket *should* hold its full geometric volume of water.
- But in reality, some water **leaks out** through the gaps in the bamboo weave before you reach the top.
- Therefore, the **actual water delivered** is *less* than the **theoretical capacity** of the bucket.

This "lost" water is exactly analogous to **Slip** in a reciprocating pump:

| Component in Analogy | Real Pump Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Bamboo gaps | Piston rings, valve seat leakage |
| Water sloshing out | Delayed valve closure (return flow) |
| Geometric bucket volume | $A \times L$ (piston area × stroke length) |
| Water remaining in bucket | Actual discharge $Q_{\text{act}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Punchline:** Slip is essentially the *"escaped water"* — the liquid that *should* have been pumped out, but never made it to the delivery pipe.

### 1.3 Why Slip Occurs — The Three Primary Culprits

1. **Internal Leakage:** Liquid bypasses the piston through clearances between the piston, piston rod, stuffing box, and valve seats. (Think of it as the gaps in the bamboo bucket.)
2. **Valve Lag (Valve Delays):** The suction and delivery valves do not open/close instantaneously at the exact BDC/BDC (Bottom Dead Centre) and TDC (Top Dead Centre) points. They open *slightly late* and close *slightly late*, causing a backflow of liquid into the cylinder.
3. **Air & Gas Release:** Dissolved air in the water expands during the suction stroke and contracts during the delivery stroke, altering the effective cylinder volume.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Constants to Remember:** The acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ and the density of water $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ are the only standard metrics needed for most slip problems under KTU 2024 scheme.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Discharge — The "Ideal World" Benchmark

For a **single-acting reciprocating pump** running at $N$ revolutions per minute (rpm) with a piston of cross-sectional area $A$ and stroke length $L$:

- Volume swept per stroke = $A \times L$
- Number of strokes per minute = $N$
- **Theoretical discharge** $Q_{\text{th}}$:

$$
Q_{\text{th}} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \quad \left[ \text{m}^3/\text{s} \right]
$$

For a **double-acting pump** (liquid is pumped on *both* the forward and return strokes), the effective piston area differs on the two strokes. If the piston rod has cross-sectional area $a$:

- Forward stroke swept volume = $A \cdot L$
- Return stroke swept volume = $(A - a) \cdot L$
- **Theoretical discharge** $Q_{\text{th}}$:

$$
Q_{\text{th}} = \frac{L \cdot N}{60} \cdot (2A - a)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** In a double-acting pump, students often forget to subtract the piston rod area on the return stroke. The correct swept volume is $(A-a)$, *not* $A$.

### 2.2 Why Slip is a "Design Penalty" (The Engineering 'Why')

A pump with **100% efficiency in volumetric displacement** would have **zero slip**. In reality:

- A **well-designed pump** has a slip of **2% to 5%**.
- A **worn-out pump** can have a slip of **15% to 25%**, indicating the need for maintenance.
- Slip is **not a constant**; it depends on the **delivery head (pressure)** — higher pressure → more leakage → more slip.

### 2.3 Negative Slip — The Counter-Intuitive Case

> [!CAUTION]
> **Negative Slip** is a *favourite KTU board question* and frequently catches students off-guard.

Normally, $Q_{\text{act}} < Q_{\text{th}}$, so slip is **positive**. However, under certain high-speed, high-head conditions, $Q_{\text{act}} > Q_{\text{th}}$, giving a **negative slip** value.

**Condition for Negative Slip:**
- The delivery valve **closes late** (after the piston has started its return stroke).
- During the time gap $\Delta t$, a portion of the water that had entered the delivery pipe **flows back** into the cylinder.
- The column of water in the delivery pipe, now at a high pressure $H_d$, decelerates the reverse flow.
- After the column momentum is destroyed, the water has lost some volume compared to what was ideally delivered in the *early* part of the stroke.

In **indicator diagram (cathode ray oscillograph)** terms, negative slip is visible as a portion of the **delivery line** dropping *below* the atmospheric line on the diagram, indicating reverse flow.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slip volume | $S_L = Q_{\text{th}} - Q_{\text{act}}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Always positive in normal operation |
| 2 | Percentage slip | $\%S = \dfrac{(Q_{\text{th}} - Q_{\text{act}})}{Q_{\text{th}}} \times 100$ | dimensionless | Negative if $Q_{\text{act}} > Q_{\text{th}}$ |
| 3 | Coefficient of discharge | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{\text{act}}}{Q_{\text{th}}}$ | dimensionless | $C_d < 1$ for positive slip, $C_d > 1$ for negative slip |
| 4 | $Q_{\text{th}}$ (single-acting) | $Q_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | One delivery per revolution |
| 5 | $Q_{\text{th}}$ (double-acting) | $Q_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{L \cdot N \cdot (2A - a)}{60}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Two deliveries per revolution |
| 6 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_{\text{vol}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{act}}}{Q_{\text{th}}} \times 100$ | % | $\eta_{\text{vol}} = 100 - \%\text{Slip}$ |
| 7 | Slip relation to $C_d$ | $\%S = (1 - C_d) \times 100$ | % | Direct conversion formula |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipeline Note:** In KTU problems, do not confuse **Slip** with **Separation of flow** (in boundary layer theory, Module 1/2). They are entirely different concepts despite similar names.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Condition Monitoring:** In industrial plants, operators measure slip monthly. A *rising* slip trend is the **first warning sign** of worn piston rings, scored cylinder walls, or eroded valve seats.
- **Pump Sizing:** Engineers design pumps with a target slip of 3–4% to leave a safety margin against real-world degradation.
- **High-Rise Pumping:** In tall buildings (e.g., Keralas high-rises in Kochi), the high delivery head amplifies slip; engineers must oversize the pump to compensate.
- **Oil & Gas Industry:** In **slip-flow regimes** (different context — micro-channels), the term "slip" reappears in rarefied gas dynamics, but for PCAUT302, always interpret slip as *pump slip*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Percentage Slip Formula

**Starting Point (Definitions):**

Let:
- $V_d$ = volume of liquid actually delivered per stroke (after slip losses) $[\text{m}^3]$
- $V_s$ = volume swept by the piston per stroke $= A \times L$ $[\text{m}^3]$
- $V_{\text{slip}}$ = volume lost due to slip per stroke $[\text{m}^3]$

**Step 1: Express Slip as a Volume Difference**

By definition, the volume lost to slip equals the geometric swept volume minus the actual volume delivered:

$$
V_{\text{slip}} = V_s - V_d = A \cdot L - V_d
$$

**Step 2: Convert to Discharge Form**

Multiplying both sides by the number of working strokes per second ($N/60$):

$$
Q_{\text{slip}} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} - \frac{V_d \cdot N}{60}
$$

Recognising the first term as $Q_{\text{th}}$ and the second term as $Q_{\text{act}}$:

$$
Q_{\text{slip}} = Q_{\text{th}} - Q_{\text{act}}
$$

**Step 3: Non-Dimensionalize via Percentage**

To express slip as a *percentage* of the theoretical discharge, we divide by $Q_{\text{th}}$ and multiply by 100:

$$
\%S = \frac{Q_{\text{th}} - Q_{\text{act}}}{Q_{\text{th}}} \times 100
$$

**Step 4: Relate to Coefficient of Discharge**

Since $C_d = Q_{\text{act}} / Q_{\text{th}}$:

$$
\%S = \left( 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{act}}}{Q_{\text{th}}} \right) \times 100 = (1 - C_d) \times 100
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Compact Form (KTU exam-ready):**
> $$\boxed{\; \%S = (1 - C_d) \times 100 \;}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Problem — Single-Acting Pump

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Style Question]**
> A single-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of **150 mm** and a stroke of **300 mm**. It runs at **60 rpm** and delivers water against a head of **20 m**. If the actual discharge measured is **4.5 litres/second**, determine:
> (i) Theoretical discharge
> (ii) Percentage slip
> (iii) Coefficient of discharge

**Given Data (extracted cleanly):**

$$
D = 0.150 \text{ m}, \quad L = 0.300 \text{ m}, \quad N = 60 \text{ rpm}, \quad Q_{\text{act}} = 4.5 \text{ L/s} = 0.0045 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Part (i): Theoretical Discharge**

Piston cross-sectional area:

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.150)^2
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0225 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2
$$

Swept volume per stroke:

$$
V_s = A \cdot L = 0.01767 \times 0.300 = 0.005301 \text{ m}^3
$$

Strokes per second:

$$
n = \frac{N}{60} = \frac{60}{60} = 1 \text{ stroke/s}
$$

Theoretical discharge:

$$
Q_{\text{th}} = V_s \cdot n = 0.005301 \times 1 = 0.005301 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 5.301 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Part (ii): Percentage Slip**

$$
\%S = \frac{Q_{\text{th}} - Q_{\text{act}}}{Q_{\text{th}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\%S = \frac{0.005301 - 0.0045}{0.005301} \times 100
$$

$$
\%S = \frac{0.000801}{0.005301} \times 100 = 15.11\%
$$

**Part (iii): Coefficient of Discharge**

$$
C_d = \frac{Q_{\text{act}}}{Q_{\text{th}}} = \frac{0.0045}{0.005301} = 0.8489 \approx 0.849
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Error:** Students frequently forget to convert **litres/second to m³/s**. Losing this 1 mark is the most common slip in the *exam* itself!

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Problem — Double-Acting Pump with Negative Slip

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style Question]**
> A double-acting reciprocating pump has a piston diameter of **200 mm**, piston rod diameter of **40 mm**, and a stroke of **400 mm**. It runs at **50 rpm**. In a test, it was found that the pump actually delivers **9.8 litres/second** against a high head. Calculate:
> (i) Theoretical discharge
> (ii) Percentage slip and identify whether it is positive or negative
> (iii) Slip volume per second

**Given Data:**

$$
D = 0.200 \text{ m}, \quad d = 0.040 \text{ m}, \quad L = 0.400 \text{ m}, \quad N = 50 \text{ rpm}, \quad Q_{\text{act}} = 0.0098 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Part (i): Theoretical Discharge**

Piston area $A$:

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.200)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2
$$

Piston rod area $a$:

$$
a = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.040)^2 = 0.001257 \text{ m}^2
$$

For a **double-acting** pump, swept volume per revolution:

$$
V_{\text{per rev}} = A \cdot L + (A - a) \cdot L = L \cdot (2A - a)
$$

$$
V_{\text{per rev}} = 0.400 \times (2 \times 0.03142 - 0.001257)
$$

$$
V_{\text{per rev}} = 0.400 \times (0.06284 - 0.001257) = 0.400 \times 0.061583 = 0.02463 \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}
$$

Revolutions per second:

$$
n = \frac{N}{60} = \frac{50}{60} = 0.8333 \text{ rev/s}
$$

Theoretical discharge:

$$
Q_{\text{th}} = V_{\text{per rev}} \times n = 0.02463 \times 0.8333 = 0.02053 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 20.53 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Part (ii): Percentage Slip**

$$
\%S = \frac{0.02053 - 0.0098}{0.02053} \times 100
$$

$$
\%S = \frac{0.01073}{0.02053} \times 100 = 52.26\%
$$

> [!CAUTION]
> **Re-reading the question:** A 52% slip is unrealistically high — this would indicate a severely damaged pump. However, if the problem states that the *actual* discharge is somehow *higher* than theoretical (which can happen due to negative slip effects), we would see a negative percentage. **Always verify the context.**

**Part (iii): Slip Volume per Second**

$$
Q_{\text{slip}} = Q_{\text{th}} - Q_{\text{act}} = 0.02053 - 0.0098 = 0.01073 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 10.73 \text{ L/s}
$$

### 3.4 Reverse-Engineering Slip from Indicator Diagram (KTU Favourite!)

**Conceptual Setup:** In KTU problems, you may be given the **area of the slip region** $A_{\text{slip}}$ on the indicator diagram (in $\text{mm}^2$) and the **scale factors** (length scale $X$ in $\text{mm/m}$, force scale $Y$ in $\text{N/mm}^2$ per $\text{mm}$ of head, or simply head scale).

**Slip expressed as equivalent head loss:**

$$
H_{\text{slip}} = \frac{A_{\text{slip}}}{L_{\text{stroke}}} \times \text{(head scale factor)}
$$

Then the actual water column delivered per stroke:

$$
h_{\text{act}} = h_{\text{ideal}} - h_{\text{slip}}
$$

where $h_{\text{ideal}}$ corresponds to the full stroke length on the indicator diagram.

> [!NOTE]
> **Full KTU derivation for indicator-diagram-based slip problems** is covered in the *Indicator Diagrams* sub-topic of Module 4. For this *Slip*-specific notes file, the key takeaway is: **Slip on the diagram is represented by the small enclosed area between the ideal and actual delivery/suction curves.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Flow of Fluid Through a Reciprocating Pump with Slip Points

The following block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a reciprocating pump, highlighting the three primary *slip-loss* points as colour-coded blocks (visualised in supporting Mermaid syntax):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sump / Source Reservoir] --> B[Suction Pipe with Foot Valve]
    B --> C[Suction Valve in Cylinder]
    C --> D[Cylinder Chamber with Piston]
    D --> E[Delivery Valve in Cylinder]
    E --> F[Delivery Pipe to Tanker / Tank]

    G[Slip Point 1 - Foot Valve Leakage] -.leakage.-> B
    H[Slip Point 2 - Suction Valve Late Closure] -.reverse flow.-> C
    I[Slip Point 3 - Piston Ring Clearance Leakage] -.leakage.-> D
    J[Slip Point 4 - Delivery Valve Late Closure] -.reverse flow.-> E

    K[Theoretical Discharge Qth equals A times L times N over 60] --> L[Actual Discharge Qact]
    L --> M[Slip equals Qth minus Qact]
    M --> N[Percentage Slip equals Slip divided by Qth times 100]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — Slip Direction in Forward and Return Strokes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FORWARD_STROKE
        F1[Suction Valve OPEN] --> F2[Cylinder fills with water]
        F2 --> F3[Delivery Valve shut]
        F3 --> F4[Slip leakage back to sump via piston rings]
    end

    subgraph RETURN_STROKE
        R1[Suction Valve shut] --> R2[Cylinder discharges water]
        R2 --> R3[Delivery Valve OPEN]
        R3 --> R4[Slip leakage back to cylinder via delayed valve]
    end

    FORWARD_STROKE --> RETURN_STROKE
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Why Negative Slip Occurs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Piston at TDC end of delivery stroke] --> B[Delivery valve should close]
    B --> C{Inertia of water column in delivery pipe}
    C --> D[Water column keeps moving upward briefly]
    D --> E[Delivery valve still open]
    E --> F[Reverse flow into cylinder for short duration delta t]
    F --> G[Net actual volume is LESS than ideal at this instant]
    G --> H{Pressure rebuilds and valve finally closes}
    H --> I[Net result: Qact greater than Qth locally, creating NEGATIVE slip on the indicator]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Block Map — Classification of Slip Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SLIP in Reciprocating Pumps] --> B[Positive Slip]
    A --> C[Negative Slip]
    A --> D[Zero Slip - Ideal Pump]

    B --> B1[Cause: Leakage past piston and valves]
    B --> B2[Qact less than Qth]
    B --> B3[Typical in worn-out or low-speed pumps]

    C --> C1[Cause: Delayed valve closure, inertia effects]
    C --> C2[Qact greater than Qth locally]
    C --> C3[Typical in high-speed high-head pumps]

    D --> D1[Cause: Perfect sealing, instantaneous valve action]
    D --> D2[Qact equals Qth]
    D --> D3[Theoretical limit, never achieved in practice]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*Define slip and percentage slip in a reciprocating pump. State two causes of slip.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Slip:** It is the difference between the theoretical discharge and the actual discharge of a reciprocating pump. (1 mark)
- **Percentage slip:** It is the ratio of slip to the theoretical discharge, expressed as a percentage. (1 mark)
  $$\%S = \frac{Q_{\text{th}} - Q_{\text{act}}}{Q_{\text{th}}} \times 100$$
- **Two causes:** (i) Leakage of liquid past the piston and through valve seats; (ii) Delayed opening and closing of suction and delivery valves. (1 mark)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by negative slip in a reciprocating pump? Under what condition does it occur?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** When the actual discharge of a reciprocating pump exceeds the theoretical discharge, the slip is said to be *negative*.
- **Condition (1.5 marks):** Negative slip occurs when the delivery valve does not close at the exact end of the delivery stroke. Due to the inertia of the water column in the delivery pipe, the water continues to move upward briefly even after the piston has begun its return stroke, causing a momentary reverse flow. Once the water column decelerates and the valve finally closes, the net delivered volume is less than the *ideal* expected at that early instant, but the *integrated* actual discharge may exceed the theoretical — yielding negative slip.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **As per KTU 2024 ESE Regulations:** *Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into sub-parts. Students answer ONE full question from a choice of TWO.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Module 4 Choice Question Set 1]**

**(a)** A single-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of **200 mm** and a stroke of **350 mm**. It runs at **45 rpm**. If the pump has a slip of **8%**, calculate: (7 marks)
  (i) Theoretical discharge in L/s
  (ii) Actual discharge in L/s
  (iii) Coefficient of discharge

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the phenomenon of *negative slip* in a reciprocating pump. Under what operating conditions does it arise? (7 marks)

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Given:**
$$
D = 0.200 \text{ m}, \quad L = 0.350 \text{ m}, \quad N = 45 \text{ rpm}, \quad \%S = 8\%
$$

**Step 1: Piston Area**
$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.200)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2 \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

**Step 2: Theoretical Discharge**
$$
Q_{\text{th}} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} = \frac{0.03142 \times 0.350 \times 45}{60}
$$
$$
Q_{\text{th}} = \frac{0.4949}{60} = 0.008248 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 8.248 \text{ L/s} \quad (3 \text{ marks})
$$

**Step 3: Actual Discharge**
$$
\%S = \frac{Q_{\text{th}} - Q_{\text{act}}}{Q_{\text{th}}} \times 100
$$
$$
8 = \frac{8.248 - Q_{\text{act}}}{8.248} \times 100
$$
$$
Q_{\text{act}} = 8.248 \times \left(1 - \frac{8}{100}\right) = 8.248 \times 0.92 = 7.588 \text{ L/s} \quad (2 \text{ marks})
$$

**Step 4: Coefficient of Discharge**
$$
C_d = 1 - \frac{\%S}{100} = 1 - 0.08 = 0.92 \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Explanation of Negative Slip (5 marks):**

In a reciprocating pump, the delivery valve is supposed to close the moment the piston begins its return (suction) stroke. However, at high pump speeds and high delivery heads, the water column in the delivery pipe has substantial *kinetic energy* and *momentum*.

Due to this inertia, the water continues to rush upward into the delivery pipe for a short duration $\Delta t$ *after* the piston has reversed direction. During this interval, the delivery valve remains open, and a small quantity of water from the delivery pipe flows **back** into the cylinder.

**Consequence:** The actual volume of water that leaves the cylinder per stroke becomes *slightly more* than the theoretical (geometric) volume — because the *brief* backflow is dominated by the initial forward overshoot from inertia. When measured against the ideal curve, the *net* discharge exceeds the theoretical, yielding a **negative slip**.

**Operating Conditions (2 marks):**
1. **High pump speed** (high $N$) — increases water column inertia.
2. **High delivery head** (high $H_d$) — increases static head and dynamic effects.
3. **Long delivery pipe** — adds to column mass and inertia.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice Question Set 2]**

**(a)** A double-acting reciprocating pump has a piston diameter of **250 mm**, piston rod diameter of **50 mm**, and stroke length of **400 mm**. It runs at **40 rpm** and has a slip of **6%**. Calculate: (7 marks)
  (i) Theoretical discharge
  (ii) Actual discharge
  (iii) Slip volume per minute

**(b)** Discuss the various causes of slip in a reciprocating pump. How is slip related to the coefficient of discharge? (7 marks)

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Given:**
$$
D = 0.250 \text{ m}, \quad d = 0.050 \text{ m}, \quad L = 0.400 \text{ m}, \quad N = 40 \text{ rpm}, \quad \%S = 6\%
$$

**Step 1: Piston and Rod Areas**
$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.250)^2 = 0.04909 \text{ m}^2 \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$
$$
a = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.050)^2 = 0.001963 \text{ m}^2 \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})
$$

**Step 2: Theoretical Discharge (Double-Acting)**
$$
Q_{\text{th}} = \frac{L \cdot N \cdot (2A - a)}{60}
$$
$$
Q_{\text{th}} = \frac{0.400 \times 40 \times (2 \times 0.04909 - 0.001963)}{60}
$$
$$
Q_{\text{th}} = \frac{16 \times (0.09818 - 0.001963)}{60} = \frac{16 \times 0.09622}{60}
$$
$$
Q_{\text{th}} = \frac{1.5395}{60} = 0.02566 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 25.66 \text{ L/s} \quad (3 \text{ marks})
$$

**Step 3: Actual Discharge**
$$
Q_{\text{act}} = Q_{\text{th}} \times (1 - 0.06) = 25.66 \times 0.94 = 24.12 \text{ L/s} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})
$$

**Step 4: Slip Volume per Minute**
$$
Q_{\text{slip}} = Q_{\text{th}} - Q_{\text{act}} = 25.66 - 24.12 = 1.54 \text{ L/s}
$$
$$
\text{Per minute: } 1.54 \times 60 = 92.4 \text{ L/min} \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Causes of Slip (5 marks):**

1. **Leakage of liquid past the piston** (2 marks)
   The piston does not fit the cylinder perfectly. A small clearance exists between the piston rings and the cylinder wall. Under high delivery pressure, water leaks back through this clearance from the delivery side to the suction side, reducing the actual volume delivered per stroke.

2. **Delayed closure of the suction and delivery valves** (2 marks)
   The valves do not open and close instantaneously. There is a small time lag during which water may flow in the *reverse* direction (back into the cylinder from the delivery pipe, or back into the sump from the suction pipe). This reverse flow reduces the actual discharge.

3. **Dissolved gases in water** (1 mark)
   Air dissolved in the pumped water expands during the suction stroke (lower pressure) and contracts during the delivery stroke, altering the effective cylinder volume and reducing the actual discharge.

**Relation to Coefficient of Discharge (2 marks):**

The coefficient of discharge $C_d$ is defined as the ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge:

$$
C_d = \frac{Q_{\text{act}}}{Q_{\text{th}}}
$$

Since $\%S = (1 - C_d) \times 100$, we can also write:

$$
C_d = 1 - \frac{\%S}{100}
$$

Thus, a **higher slip** corresponds to a **lower $C_d$**, indicating poorer volumetric performance. A perfectly ideal pump has $C_d = 1$ and $\%S = 0$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways KTU Students Lose Marks on Slip Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Forgetting to convert $D$ from **mm to m** before computing $A = (\pi/4) D^2$. This error propagates and loses 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Single-acting vs Double-acting Mix-up:** Using $A \cdot L \cdot N / 60$ for a double-acting pump. *Always* check whether the piston has a rod!
> 3. **Confusing $Q_{\text{act}}$ and $Q_{\text{th}}$:** When the question asks for *actual* discharge, do not accidentally compute the *theoretical* one. Read the question twice.
> 4. **Sign Convention for Negative Slip:** If the result is negative, *say so explicitly*. A negative percentage with no interpretation loses 1 mark.
> 5. **No Sketch in Part (b):** A neat labelled diagram of a pump with slip regions marked fetches *at least 1–2 marks* in KTU valuation, even if the explanation is incomplete.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Punch List for "Slip":**

- **Slip** = $Q_{\text{th}} - Q_{\text{act}}$, expressed as **% of $Q_{\text{th}}$**.
- **Three causes:** piston leakage, valve lag, dissolved gases.
- **Negative slip** → $Q_{\text{act}} > Q_{\text{th}}$; occurs at *high speed, high head*.
- **Single-acting:** $Q_{\text{th}} = A \cdot L \cdot N / 60$.
- **Double-acting:** $Q_{\text{th}} = L \cdot N \cdot (2A - a) / 60$.
- **Coefficient of discharge** $C_d = Q_{\text{act}} / Q_{\text{th}}$; relation: $\%S = (1 - C_d) \times 100$.
- **Volumetric efficiency** $\eta_{\text{vol}} = 100 - \%\text{Slip}$.
- **Normal slip range** for healthy pump: **2% to 5%**; warning sign: **> 10%**.
- **Slip is NOT constant** — it **increases with delivery head**.
- **Key units:** $A$ in $\text{m}^2$, $L$ in $\text{m}$, $N$ in rpm, $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$.
- **Memorize one formula:**
  $$\boxed{\; \%\text{Slip} = \left( 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{act}}}{Q_{\text{th}}} \right) \times 100 \;}$$
- **Examiner loves:** A neat sketch of the indicator diagram *and* the reciprocating pump cylinder with slip points marked.
- **Avoid confusion with:** Separation (boundary layer), Cavitation (Module 3), and Slipstream (aerodynamics). These are *unrelated* topics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Negative Slip and Work Done and Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Negative Slip and Work Done and Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Negative Slip – Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slip (S)** in a reciprocating pump is defined as the difference between the theoretical discharge (Q_th) and the actual discharge (Q_act) of the pump per unit time.
>
> $$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$$
>
> **Negative Slip** is an abnormal operating condition in which the *actual discharge exceeds the theoretical discharge*, i.e., $Q_{act} > Q_{th}$, making the slip mathematically negative ($S < 0$).

### Conditions Producing Negative Slip
Negative slip arises from the **inertia (kinetic energy) of the water column** in the delivery pipe. It is typically observed when:
- The **delivery pipe is very short** (so the water column has low mass and high velocity).
- The **pump is running at a high speed** (high piston velocity).
- The **delivery valve closes suddenly** before the delivery stroke is completed.
- The **frictional resistance in the delivery pipe is very small**.

### Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are blowing up a balloon through a thin straw. As you blow, the air inside the balloon builds up pressure. If you suddenly stop blowing, the pressurized air inside rushes back through the straw and even pulls extra air from your lungs — *more air moved backward in the pipe than the theoretical amount the balloon was supposed to lose*. Similarly, in a reciprocating pump, the inertia of the moving water column in a short delivery pipe **continues to push water OUT of the cylinder** *even after the delivery stroke should have ended*. This extra volume pushed out by inertia is what causes the actual discharge to exceed the theoretical discharge — producing **negative slip**.

## 1.2 Work Done and Efficiency – Formal KTU Definition

**Work done (W)** is the useful energy transferred to water by the pump per unit time. It is calculated as:
$$W = \rho g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

Where **H** is the total head the pump must overcome, and **ρg = 9810 N/m³** is the specific weight of water (often written as **w = 9.81 kN/m³**).

**Efficiency (η)** in a pump is the ratio of useful energy output (water lifted) to the energy input (shaft work), expressed as a fraction or percentage. The three principal efficiencies for KTU syllabus are:
- **Mechanical Efficiency (η_m)** – accounts for frictional losses.
- **Volumetric Efficiency (η_v)** – accounts for slip / leakage.
- **Overall Efficiency (η_o)** – product of the above.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Students must be able to derive the work done per stroke for single-acting and double-acting reciprocating pumps, and identify the conditions leading to negative slip. Numerical problems on overall efficiency and slip are frequently asked as 7-mark sub-parts in the End Semester Examination (ESE).*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Negative Slip Occurs – The Physics

During the **delivery stroke**, the piston pushes water through the delivery pipe. The water in the delivery pipe gains kinetic energy and momentum. At the end of the delivery stroke:
- The piston momentarily stops / reverses direction.
- The **non-return (delivery) valve tries to close** because pressure inside the cylinder drops.
- However, the **inertia of the water column** in the delivery pipe keeps pushing water *out* of the cylinder for a brief moment.
- This causes an **extra volume of water (ΔV)** to be discharged, which the piston did not physically account for.

This **extra discharged volume** makes $Q_{act} > Q_{th}$, hence the slip becomes negative.

### Mathematical Quantification
$$\text{Negative Slip} = -(Q_{act} - Q_{th}) = -(w \cdot A \cdot L) / g$$

Where:
- **w** = velocity of water in the delivery pipe
- **A** = area of delivery pipe
- **L** = length of delivery pipe
- **g** = 9.81 m/s²

This expression is derived from Newton's second law: the **retarding force** required to stop the water column in time **t** is $F = (w \cdot A \cdot L)/t$. The extra pressure thus produced causes the delivery valve to remain open longer than the stroke duration.

## 2.2 Work Done – Step-by-Step Logic

The work done by a reciprocating pump is calculated as the energy required to lift water from the **suction level** to the **delivery level**, plus work to overcome friction.

### Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump
For one complete cycle (one suction + one delivery stroke):
- **Suction Stroke:** Work done against suction head = $\rho g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot H_s$
- **Delivery Stroke:** Work done against delivery head = $\rho g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot H_d$

$$W_{per \, cycle} = \rho g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot (H_s + H_d)$$

If the pump runs at **N** revolutions per second, and considering that work is done only on the delivery stroke:
$$W_{per \, second} = \rho g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N \cdot (H_s + H_d)$$

### Double-Acting Reciprocating Pump
Here, the suction and delivery happen on **both sides of the piston**. The discharge per second is doubled, and the formula becomes:
$$W_{per \, second} = \rho g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N \cdot (H_s + 2H_d)$$

## 2.3 Efficiencies – The Three Pillars

| **Efficiency** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m$ | $\dfrac{W_{useful}}{W_{useful} + W_{friction}}$ | Fraction of input shaft power that is delivered to water (rest lost to friction in moving parts). |
| Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v$ | $\dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100\%$ | Fraction of theoretical volume that actually reaches the delivery pipe. |
| Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o$ | $\eta_m \times \eta_v$ | Combined effect of mechanical and volumetric losses. |
| Manometric Efficiency | $\eta_{man}$ | $\dfrac{(H_{th} - h_f)}{H} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency considering only head losses in pipes. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Equation** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical Discharge (Single-Acting) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | m³/s |
| 2 | Theoretical Discharge (Double-Acting) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{2 \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | m³/s |
| 3 | Slip | $S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ | m³/s |
| 4 | Negative Slip (Magnitude) | $\vert S \vert = \dfrac{w \cdot A \cdot L}{g}$ | m³/s |
| 5 | Work Done (Single-Acting) | $W = \rho g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N \cdot (H_s + H_d)$ | W (Watts) |
| 6 | Work Done (Double-Acting) | $W = \rho g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N \cdot (H_s + 2 H_d)$ | W (Watts) |
| 7 | Power Input (Shaft Power) | $P_{in} = \dfrac{W}{\eta_{overall}}$ | W |
| 8 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | % |
| 9 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{W}{W + W_f}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_m \times \eta_v$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula sheet, the **vertical bar** is used with the LaTeX command `\vert` so it does not break markdown table syntax.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Water Supply Schemes:** Municipal corporations use these formulas to size pumps for overhead tanks and distribution networks.
- **Oil & Gas Industry:** Positive displacement pumps (a generalization of reciprocating pumps) use these principles to transport viscous crude oil.
- **Hydraulic Presses:** Work done principles of reciprocating pumps govern the energy balance of hydraulic press systems.
- **Fire-Fighting Systems:** Negative slip considerations are crucial in selecting high-pressure pumps where delivery pipes are short.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Work Done for a Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump

### Given:
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the piston (m²)
- $L$ = length of stroke (m)
- $N$ = speed of the pump in **revolutions per second (rps)**
- $H_s$ = suction head (m)
- $H_d$ = delivery head (m)
- $\rho$ = density of water (1000 kg/m³)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)

### Step 1: Volume of Water Handled Per Stroke
The volume swept by the piston in one stroke is:
$$V = A \times L \quad \text{(m³ per stroke)}$$

### Step 2: Mass of Water Lifted Per Stroke
The mass of this water column is:
$$m = \rho \times A \times L \quad \text{(kg)}$$

### Step 3: Work Done in Suction Stroke
During the suction stroke, the piston moves back, creating a vacuum. The work is done **by atmospheric pressure on the water surface** to lift water through suction head $H_s$. The force the piston must overcome on the suction side is:
$$F_s = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot H_s$$
Work done in suction stroke:
$$W_s = F_s \times L = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot H_s$$

> **Note:** The work done on the suction stroke is *positive* for the water (water is lifted), but the *piston is being pulled* — so the external agent on the crank does negative work on the piston during suction. The work transferred to the water is still $\rho g A L H_s$.

### Step 4: Work Done in Delivery Stroke
During the delivery stroke, the piston pushes water up through the delivery head $H_d$. The force is:
$$F_d = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot H_d$$
Work done in delivery stroke:
$$W_d = F_d \times L = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot H_d$$

### Step 5: Total Work Per Cycle
$$W_{cycle} = W_s + W_d = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot (H_s + H_d) \quad \text{(Joules per cycle)}$$

### Step 6: Total Work Per Second
The pump completes **N** cycles per second, so the power (work per second) is:
$$W_{per \, second} = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N \cdot (H_s + H_d) \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

> **Final Compact Form:**
>
> $$\boxed{W = w \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N \cdot (H_s + H_d) \quad \text{where } w = \rho g = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation for Double-Acting Reciprocating Pump

### Concept:
A double-acting pump performs **suction and delivery on both sides** of the piston. The piston rod occupies a portion of the area on the crank-side; this is the "piston rod area" $a$.

### Areas:
- **Water side area (full area):** $A$
- **Crank side area (reduced area):** $A - a$

### Step 1: Work on Water Side (Full Area A)
- **Delivery stroke (water side):** $\rho g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot H_d$
- **Suction stroke (water side):** $\rho g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot H_s$

### Step 2: Work on Crank Side (Reduced Area A - a)
- **Delivery stroke (crank side):** $\rho g \cdot (A-a) \cdot L \cdot H_d$
- **Suction stroke (crank side):** $\rho g \cdot (A-a) \cdot L \cdot H_s$

### Step 3: Total Work Per Cycle
$$\begin{aligned}
W_{cycle} &= \rho g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot H_d + \rho g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot H_s + \rho g \cdot (A-a) \cdot L \cdot H_d + \rho g \cdot (A-a) \cdot L \cdot H_s \\
&= \rho g \cdot L \cdot H_d \cdot [A + (A-a)] + \rho g \cdot L \cdot H_s \cdot [A + (A-a)] \\
&= \rho g \cdot L \cdot H_d \cdot (2A - a) + \rho g \cdot L \cdot H_s \cdot (2A - a) \\
&= \rho g \cdot L \cdot (2A - a) \cdot (H_s + H_d)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Work Per Second (Multiplying by N rps)
$$\boxed{W = \rho g \cdot L \cdot N \cdot (2A - a) \cdot (H_s + H_d)}$$

> **Alternative Form (KTU Preferred):** When $a$ is neglected (small compared to $A$):
>
> $$W = \rho g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N \cdot (H_s + 2H_d)$$
>
> This is because delivery happens on **both** sides (contributing $2H_d$ effectively), while suction happens on the **water side only** (contributing $H_s$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Negative Slip Magnitude

### Setup:
Consider a delivery pipe of length $L_d$, cross-section $A_d$, with water moving at velocity $w$.

### Step 1: Mass of Water in the Delivery Pipe
$$m = \rho \cdot A_d \cdot L_d$$

### Step 2: Retarding Force Required to Stop the Water
By Newton's second law, the force needed to bring this water column to rest in time $t$ is:
$$F = m \cdot \frac{dw}{dt} = \rho \cdot A_d \cdot L_d \cdot \frac{w}{t}$$

### Step 3: Equivalent Pressure Head
This force, when expressed as an additional head, is:
$$h_{extra} = \frac{F}{\rho g A_d} = \frac{L_d \cdot w}{g \cdot t}$$

### Step 4: Extra Volume Discharged
This extra head keeps the delivery valve open for an additional time, during which water is discharged at velocity $w$ through area $A_d$. The extra volume is:
$$\Delta V = A_d \cdot w \cdot t_{extra}$$

By equating the inertia head to the velocity head for the time the valve stays open beyond the stroke, we obtain the standard result:
$$\vert S \vert = \frac{w \cdot A_d \cdot L_d}{g}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = Q_{th} + \frac{w \cdot A_d \cdot L_d}{g} \quad \text{(when negative slip occurs)}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| **Given** | **Find** | **Approach** |
|---|---|---|
| A, L, N, H_s, H_d, η_o | Power input (kW) | $P = \rho g A L N (H_s + H_d) / \eta_o$ |
| Q_th, Q_act | Slip & η_v | $S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$; $\eta_v = Q_{act}/Q_{th} \times 100$ |
| w, A_d, L_d | Negative slip volume | $\vert S \vert = w A_d L_d / g$ |
| η_m, η_v | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_m \times \eta_v$ |
| Q_act, H, η_o | Power required | $P = \rho g Q_{act} H / \eta_o$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram – Negative Slip Phenomenon

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Piston starts delivery stroke] --> B[Water gains kinetic energy in delivery pipe]
    B --> C{Piston reaches end of stroke}
    C --> D[Piston reverses direction]
    D --> E{Cylinder pressure drops}
    E --> F[Delivery valve attempts to close]
    F --> G{Water inertia in pipe}
    G -- Yes --> H[Water column continues moving out]
    H --> I[Delivery valve remains open longer]
    I --> J[Extra volume discharged beyond stroke]
    J --> K[Q_act greater than Q_th]
    K --> L[Negative Slip Occurs]
    G -- No --> M[Normal positive slip: leakage losses]
    M --> N[Q_act less than Q_th]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram – Energy Flow in a Reciprocating Pump

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Shaft Power Input P_in] --> M1[Mechanical Losses W_friction]
    M1 --> M2[Useful Work to Water W_useful]
    S --> V1[Volumetric Losses Slip S]
    V1 --> V2[Actual Discharge Q_act]
    M2 --> H1[Effective Head H]
    V2 --> H1
    H1 --> P[Effective Power Output]
    P --> E[Overall Efficiency eta_o]
    S --> E
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram – Work Done Components (Single-Acting Pump)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pump Cycle] --> S1[Suction Stroke]
    P --> D1[Delivery Stroke]
    S1 --> WS[Work: rho g A L H_s]
    D1 --> WD[Work: rho g A L H_d]
    WS --> SUM[Total Work per Cycle]
    WD --> SUM
    SUM --> MULT[Multiply by N rps]
    MULT --> OUT[Power Output in Watts]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram – Efficiency Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Overall Efficiency eta_o] --> M[Mechanical Efficiency eta_m]
    O --> V[Volumetric Efficiency eta_v]
    M --> MF[Friction Losses in Moving Parts]
    V --> SL[Slip / Leakage Losses]
    MF --> CE[Combined Effect]
    SL --> CE
    CE --> AP[Actual Pump Performance]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define slip in a reciprocating pump. Under what conditions does negative slip occur?**

#### Model Answer:
**Slip** is the difference between the theoretical discharge and the actual discharge of a reciprocating pump per unit time, expressed as:
$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$$

**Negative slip** occurs when the actual discharge exceeds the theoretical discharge ($Q_{act} > Q_{th}$). It arises due to the **inertia of water** in the delivery pipe, which is pronounced when:
- The delivery pipe is very short.
- The pump runs at very high speed.
- The frictional resistance in the delivery pipe is very small.
- The delivery valve closes suddenly due to high velocity.

In such cases, the inertia of the water column keeps the delivery valve open for a brief extra duration, allowing additional water to be discharged — hence $Q_{act} > Q_{th}$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between mechanical efficiency, volumetric efficiency, and overall efficiency of a reciprocating pump.**

#### Model Answer:
| **Aspect** | **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_m$)** | **Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_v$)** | **Overall Efficiency ($\eta_o$)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $\dfrac{W_{useful}}{W_{useful} + W_{friction}}$ | $\dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100\%$ | $\eta_m \times \eta_v$ |
| **Losses Accounted** | Friction in piston, crankshaft, bearings | Leakage past valves, slip | All combined losses |
| **Range (Typical)** | 80–95% | 85–98% | 70–90% |

---

## 5.2 Part B – Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

A single-acting reciprocating pump has the following specifications:
- **Bore** = 150 mm, **Stroke** = 300 mm
- **Suction head** = 4 m, **Delivery head** = 16 m
- **Speed** = 60 rpm
- **Actual discharge** = 5.5 litres/sec
- **Frictional head loss** = 2 m
- Take water density = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s²

#### Part (a) – 7 Marks
**Calculate the theoretical discharge, percentage slip, and volumetric efficiency of the pump.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate Piston Area (A)**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.150)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0225 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Convert Speed to rps**
$$N = \frac{60}{60} = 1 \text{ rps}$$

**Step 3: Theoretical Discharge (Q_th)**
For a single-acting pump:
$$Q_{th} = \frac{A \times L \times N}{60}$$
$$Q_{th} = \frac{0.01767 \times 0.300 \times 1}{60} = \frac{0.005301}{60} = 8.835 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to litres/sec:
$$Q_{th} = 8.835 \times 10^{-5} \times 1000 = 0.08835 \text{ L/s}$$

Wait, this seems very low. Let me re-check the formula. The standard formula for theoretical discharge is:
$$Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{(with N in rpm)}$$

Re-doing:
$$Q_{th} = \frac{0.01767 \times 0.300 \times 60}{60} = 0.005301 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 5.301 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 4: Slip (S)**
$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 5.301 - 5.5 = -0.199 \text{ L/s}$$

> **Negative slip is observed** (since the result is negative).

**Step 5: Volumetric Efficiency (η_v)**
$$\eta_v = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100 = \frac{5.5}{5.301} \times 100 = 103.75\%$$

> A volumetric efficiency **greater than 100%** is the hallmark of negative slip.

**Answers:** $Q_{th} = 5.301$ L/s, Slip = $-0.199$ L/s, $\eta_v = 103.75\%$

---

#### Part (b) – 7 Marks
**Calculate the work done per second and the power required to drive the pump if the overall efficiency is 80%.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Total Head (H)**
$$H = H_s + H_d + h_f = 4 + 16 + 2 = 22 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Work Done Per Second (W)**
$$W = \rho g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H$$
$$W = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 5.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 22$$
$$W = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0055 \times 22$$
$$W = 1187.01 \text{ W} \approx 1.187 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Power Input (Shaft Power)**
$$P_{in} = \frac{W}{\eta_o} = \frac{1.187}{0.80} = 1.484 \text{ kW}$$

**Answers:** $W = 1.187$ kW, $P_{in} = 1.484$ kW

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

A double-acting reciprocating pump has the following specifications:
- **Bore** = 200 mm, **Stroke** = 400 mm
- **Piston rod diameter** = 50 mm
- **Suction head** = 5 m, **Delivery head** = 25 m
- **Speed** = 50 rpm
- **Mechanical efficiency** = 90%, **Volumetric efficiency** = 95%
- Take g = 9.81 m/s²

#### Part (a) – 7 Marks
**Calculate the theoretical discharge and the actual discharge of the pump.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Areas**
- Piston area: $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.200)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$
- Rod area: $a = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.050)^2 = 0.001963 \text{ m}^2$

**Step 2: Convert Speed to rps**
$$N = \frac{50}{60} = 0.8333 \text{ rps}$$

**Step 3: Theoretical Discharge (Double-Acting)**
$$Q_{th} = \frac{(2A - a) \times L \times N}{60}$$
$$Q_{th} = \frac{(2 \times 0.03142 - 0.001963) \times 0.400 \times 50}{60}$$
$$Q_{th} = \frac{(0.06283 - 0.001963) \times 0.400 \times 50}{60}$$
$$Q_{th} = \frac{0.06087 \times 0.400 \times 50}{60} = \frac{1.2174}{60} = 0.02029 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$Q_{th} = 20.29 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 4: Actual Discharge**
$$Q_{act} = \eta_v \times Q_{th} = 0.95 \times 20.29 = 19.28 \text{ L/s}$$

**Answers:** $Q_{th} = 20.29$ L/s, $Q_{act} = 19.28$ L/s

---

#### Part (b) – 7 Marks
**Determine the work done per second, the power required at the shaft, and the overall efficiency of the pump.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Work Done Per Second (Double-Acting Pump)**
Using the KTU standard form:
$$W = \rho g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N \cdot (H_s + 2H_d)$$
$$W = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.03142 \times 0.400 \times 0.8333 \times (5 + 2 \times 25)$$
$$W = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.03142 \times 0.400 \times 0.8333 \times 55$$
$$W = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.03142 \times 0.400 \times 0.8333 \times 55$$
$$W = 5650.65 \text{ W} \approx 5.65 \text{ kW}$$

Let me re-check the calculation more carefully:
$$\begin{aligned}
W &= 9810 \times 0.03142 \times 0.400 \times \left(\frac{50}{60}\right) \times 55 \\
&= 9810 \times 0.03142 \times 0.400 \times 0.8333 \times 55 \\
&= 9810 \times 0.03142 \times 18.333 \\
&= 9810 \times 0.5761 \\
&= 5650.6 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

So $W \approx 5.65$ kW

**Step 2: Power Input (Shaft Power)**
$$P_{in} = \frac{W}{\eta_m} = \frac{5.65}{0.90} = 6.278 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Overall Efficiency**
$$\eta_o = \eta_m \times \eta_v = 0.90 \times 0.95 = 0.855 = 85.5\%$$

**Answers:** $W = 5.65$ kW, $P_{in} = 6.278$ kW, $\eta_o = 85.5\%$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU Board Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Forgetting the 2A - a form:** For double-acting pumps with a piston rod, students often use $2A$ instead of $(2A - a)$. The rod occupies area, so the effective area on the crank side is $(A - a)$, not $A$. **Loss: 1-2 marks.**
> 2. **Sign of Slip:** Always state explicitly whether slip is positive or negative. Many students compute the magnitude but forget to write the sign. A negative slip answer in KTU valuation often receives full marks for the magnitude but partial credit for the sign convention. **Loss: 0.5-1 mark.**
> 3. **Units Mismatch:** Always convert N from rpm to rps when using $\rho g$ in W/m³. Common error: using $g = 981 \text{ cm/s}^2$ with N in rpm inconsistently. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Confusing H_s and H_d:** Suction head does *positive* work on the water column (it lifts water up to the cylinder), while delivery head does *positive* work on water (lifts it further up). Both are *added* in the formula. Students sometimes subtract them by mistake. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 5. **Overall Efficiency Formula:** $\eta_o = \eta_m \times \eta_v$ — students sometimes incorrectly use $\eta_o = \eta_m + \eta_v$ (additive instead of multiplicative). **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Slip Definition:** Slip = Theoretical Discharge − Actual Discharge.
- [x] **Negative Slip Condition:** $Q_{act} > Q_{th}$ due to water inertia in short delivery pipes at high speeds.
- [x] **Magnitude of Negative Slip:** $\vert S \vert = (w \cdot A \cdot L)/g$ where $w$ is water velocity in delivery pipe.
- [x] **Work Done (Single-Acting):** $W = \rho g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N \cdot (H_s + H_d)$
- [x] **Work Done (Double-Acting):** $W = \rho g \cdot L \cdot N \cdot (2A - a) \cdot (H_s + H_d)$
- [x] **Work Done (Double-Acting KTU form):** $W = \rho g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N \cdot (H_s + 2H_d)$ *(rod area neglected)*
- [x] **Volumetric Efficiency > 100%:** Indicates negative slip; this is the **diagnostic signature**.
- [x] **Mechanical Efficiency Formula:** $\eta_m = W_{useful} / (W_{useful} + W_{friction})$
- [x] **Overall Efficiency:** $\eta_o = \eta_m \times \eta_v$
- [x] **Shaft Power (Input):** $P_{in} = P_{out} / \eta_o$
- [x] **Unit Check:** $\rho g = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ (or **w = 9.81 kN/m³**); $N$ in rps for power in Watts, or use N in rpm with 60 in denominator.
- [x] **Negative Slip Mitigation:** Lengthening the delivery pipe and reducing pump speed eliminates negative slip.
- [x] **Real-World Insight:** Municipal water supply pumps operating at moderate speeds with long delivery mains rarely exhibit negative slip; high-pressure industrial cleaning pumps with short hoses often do.
- [x] **Quick Memory Hook for KTU Exam:** *"Negative slip happens when water is too eager to leave — inertia wins over the piston."*
- [x] **Cross-Verification Tip:** Always verify that $Q_{act} \leq Q_{th}$ for normal positive slip; if your calculation gives $Q_{act} > Q_{th}$, the question is implicitly about **negative slip**.
- [x] **Diagram to Remember:** During the delivery stroke of a single-acting pump, both $H_s$ and $H_d$ contribute to work; for double-acting, $H_d$ effectively contributes *twice* because delivery occurs on both piston faces.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indicator Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Indicator Diagram # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Indicator Diagram** is a graphical representation that records the instantaneous variation of **fluid pressure (or head)** acting on the piston of a reciprocating fluid machine (pump or compressor) plotted against the **piston displacement (stroke length or volume)** during one complete cycle of operation. It is obtained experimentally using a mechanical instrument called an **Indicator** or **Indicator Gear** fitted on the cylinder of the machine. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *"An indicator diagram is a plot of pressure (or head) versus crank angle (or stroke length / volume) of a reciprocating pump or compressor, used to study the actual working cycle, energy losses, and the performance of the machine."* In a **reciprocating pump**, the indicator diagram is usually plotted as: - **X-axis:** Stroke length $S$ (or crank angle $\theta$) - **Y-axis:** Pressure head $H$ (in metres of fluid) In a **reciprocating air compressor**, it is plotted as: - **X-axis:** Volume $V$ of air in the cylinder - **Y-axis:** Pressure $P$ of air ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Black Box of a Cyclist"** > > Imagine you are cycling and you have a **fitness watch (indicator)** strapped to your wrist. The watch plots your *heart rate (pressure)* against *time (stroke)*. The **theoretical ideal** is a perfectly flat line — your heart rate is constant and at its most efficient level. The **actual line** you see is a wavy, jagged curve — your heart races up hills, dips down on flats, and spikes when you fight wind. > > The **Indicator Diagram does the exact same job for a reciprocating pump**: > - The **theoretical (ideal) diagram** = a perfect rectangle (no losses). > - The **actual diagram** = a distorted shape recorded by the indicator. > - The **gap (area) between them** = the energy lost in friction, acceleration, leaks, and valve resistance. > > The larger the distortion, the "unhealthier" (less efficient) the pump is. ## 1.3 Why Is It Studied in KTU Module 4? | Purpose | Engineering Significance | |---|---| | **Performance Evaluation** | Determines the **slip** of a pump, **volumetric efficiency**, and actual discharge | | **Loss Quantification** | Quantifies friction, acceleration, and valve losses | | **Design Verification** | Validates theoretical design against experimental reality | | **Fault Diagnosis** | Identifies leaks, valve damage, and worn piston rings | > [!NOTE] > Standard operating constants referenced in KTU problems: > - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ > - **Water density:** $\rho_w = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$ > - **Mercury density:** $\rho_{Hg} = 13600\ \text{kg/m}^3$ > - **Atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325\ \text{kPa} = 10.33\ \text{m of water}$ --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Types of Indicator Diagrams There are **two fundamental types** studied in KTU examinations: ### 2.1.1 Theoretical / Ideal / Synthetic Indicator Diagram - A **perfect rectangle** drawn assuming: - No acceleration of water in the suction/delivery pipes. - No friction in pipes, valves, or piston packing. - No leakage past the piston or valves. - Suction and delivery strokes are instantaneous at constant pressure. - **Represents 100% ideal performance** — never obtained in practice. ### 2.1.2 Actual Indicator Diagram - Recorded by the **mechanical indicator** fitted on the cylinder. - Has **rounded, slanted, and irregular** edges. - The deviation from the theoretical shape reveals the magnitude of various losses. > [!IMPORTANT] > **In KTU Module 4, students must memorise the *causes of deviation* between theoretical and actual diagrams.** ## 2.2 Causes of Deviation (Highly Important for KTU) The **four primary causes** that distort the theoretical rectangle are: | Sl. No. | Cause | Effect on Diagram | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Acceleration of water** in suction & delivery pipes | Lifts suction line up, drops delivery line down (slope) | | 2 | **Friction in pipes, valves, and passages** | Lowers pressure uniformly below theoretical line | | 3 | **Resistance in suction and delivery valves** (wire drawing) | Delays opening of valves → rounded corners | | 4 | **Leakage past piston and valves** | Reduces the height of the diagram (loss of area) | ## 2.3 Theoretical (Synthetic) Indicator Diagram — Geometry For a **single-acting reciprocating pump** (most common KTU case): - **Suction stroke:** Pressure = Suction head $H_s$ (above atmospheric datum, negative gauge) - **Delivery stroke:** Pressure = Delivery head $H_d$ (above atmospheric datum, positive gauge) - **Total height of rectangle** = $H_s + H_d$ ``` P/Head ↑ | D----------------C ← Delivery line (theoretical) | | | | | | Height = Hs + Hd | | | | A----------------B ← Suction line (theoretical) | +------------------------→ Stroke 0 s/2 s ``` Where: - $A \to B$ = Suction stroke (piston moves outward) - $B \to C$ = Delivery stroke begins (valve opens) - $C \to D$ = End of delivery stroke - $D \to A$ = End of cycle, suction valve opens ## 2.4 Effect of Acceleration on the Indicator Diagram > [!NOTE] > This is one of the **most frequently tested concepts** in KTU Part B questions. The piston of a reciprocating pump follows **simple harmonic motion (SHM)** approximately. The velocity $v$ and acceleration $a$ of the piston are: $$v = \omega r \left(\sin\theta + \frac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta\right)$$ $$a = \omega^2 r \left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)$$ Where: - $\omega$ = angular velocity of crank (rad/s) - $r$ = crank radius = $S/2$ - $L$ = length of connecting rod - $\theta$ = crank angle from inner dead centre (IDC) ### 2.4.1 Maximum Acceleration Head At the **start of the stroke** ($\theta = 0$): $$h_a = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g} \cdot \frac{A}{a_p}$$ Where: - $A$ = cross-sectional area of the pipe - $a_p$ = cross-sectional area of the piston - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity For a **single-acting pump** at the **beginning of suction stroke**: - Pressure in cylinder drops → suction head **rises** by $h_a$ - This is plotted as the rising line **A → A'** in the actual diagram At the **end of suction stroke** (or beginning of delivery): - Pressure surges → delivery head **drops** by $h_a$ ## 2.5 Effect of Friction and Valve Resistance - **Friction head** $h_f$ causes a uniform lowering of the entire delivery line and a uniform raising of the suction line. - **Valve resistance (wire drawing)** causes the **rounded corners** of the diagram. - Suction valve opens **slightly late** → rounded corner at start of suction. - Delivery valve opens **slightly late** → rounded corner at start of delivery. - Valves close **slightly late** → rounded corners at end of each stroke. ## 2.6 Leakage and Its Effect - If water leaks past the piston or valves, the **height of the actual diagram** is reduced. - A leakage line is often drawn **parallel to and below** the theoretical line, separated by a head equal to the leakage loss $h_l$. ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU question on this topic.** | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Crank radius | $r = S/2$ | m | | 2 | Angular velocity | $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ | rad/s | | 3 | Max. velocity of piston | $v_{max} = \omega r \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{2L}\right)$ | m/s | | 4 | Max. acceleration of piston | $a_{max} = \omega^2 r \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{L}\right)$ | m/s² | | 5 | Acceleration head (general) | $h_a = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g} \cdot \dfrac{A}{a_p}$ | m | | 6 | Theoretical discharge | $Q_{th} = A_{piston} \cdot S \cdot N/60$ | m³/s | | 7 | Actual discharge (via slip) | $Q_{act} = Q_{th} - Q_{slip}$ | m³/s | | 8 | Coefficient of discharge | $C_d = Q_{act}/Q_{th}$ | dimensionless | | 9 | Slip percentage | $\%\text{Slip} = \dfrac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | % | | 10 | Indicator work per cycle | $W = \text{Area of indicator diagram} \times a_p$ | J | | 11 | Work done per second (pump) | $W/\text{sec} = \rho g \cdot a_p \cdot A_{diagram} \cdot N/60$ | W | | 12 | Work done per stroke (compressor) | $W = \int P\,dV$ | J | | 13 | Slip coefficient | $K_s = 1 - C_d$ | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > In the formula table above, **vertical bars** for absolute value or any other delimiter have been deliberately avoided. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ in LaTeX if needed. ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Pump Manufacturing Industry:** Indicator diagrams are used as a **quality control tool** to certify newly manufactured pumps before delivery. - **Maintenance & Troubleshooting:** A worn diagram (excessive rounding, large leaks) tells the maintenance engineer exactly which component to replace. - **Compressor Stations:** In natural gas compressor stations, indicator cards are used to detect **valve leakage** and **piston ring wear** without disassembling the unit. - **Marine Engineering:** Indicator diagrams of marine pumps and compressors are mandatory in **classification society surveys** (e.g., Lloyd's Register). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Work Done Per Cycle from the Indicator Diagram > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is the **foundation** for almost every KTU numerical problem on reciprocating pumps and compressors. ### Statement The work done per cycle by a reciprocating pump is equal to the **area of the indicator diagram** multiplied by the **cross-sectional area of the piston**. ### Step-by-Step Derivation Consider an elemental strip of the indicator diagram at pressure head $H$ and thickness $dx$ along the stroke direction. **Step 1:** Pressure on the piston face at this instant: $$P = \rho g H$$ **Step 2:** Force exerted by fluid on the piston over the piston area $a_p$: $$F = P \cdot a_p = \rho g H \, a_p$$ **Step 3:** Work done by this force as the piston moves a small distance $dx$: $$dW = F \cdot dx = \rho g H \, a_p \, dx$$ **Step 4:** Total work done per complete cycle is the sum (integral) of all such elemental works. The product $a_p \, dx$ represents an elemental volume of the cylinder. Therefore: $$W_{cycle} = \int dW = \int \rho g H \, a_p \, dx = \rho g \, a_p \int H \, dx$$ **Step 5:** The integral $\int H \, dx$ is, by definition, the **area under the indicator diagram curve**. $$\boxed{W_{cycle} = \rho g \cdot a_p \cdot A_{diagram}}$$ Where: - $A_{diagram}$ = net area enclosed by the indicator diagram (in m² when axes are H in m and S in m) - $a_p$ = piston cross-sectional area (m²) - $\rho$ = density of fluid (kg/m³) - $g$ = 9.81 m/s² **Step 6:** Work done **per second** (i.e., power input to the fluid): $$P_{fluid} = W_{cycle} \cdot \frac{N}{60}$$ $$\boxed{P_{fluid} = \rho g \cdot a_p \cdot A_{diagram} \cdot \frac{N}{60}\ \text{watts}}$$ > [!NOTE] > For a **double-acting pump**, multiply the single-acting result by 2 (since there are two strokes per cycle). ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Acceleration Head for a Single-Acting Pump ### Step 1: Crank-Piston Kinematics The displacement $x$ of the piston from the Inner Dead Centre (IDC) at crank angle $\theta$ is: $$x = r(1 - \cos\theta) + \frac{r^2}{2L}(1 - \cos 2\theta)$$ ### Step 2: Velocity by Differentiating $$v = \frac{dx}{dt} = \omega r \left(\sin\theta + \frac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta\right)$$ ### Step 3: Acceleration by Differentiating Again $$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \omega^2 r \left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)$$ ### Step 4: Maximum Acceleration At $\theta = 0$ (start of stroke, IDC), $\cos\theta = 1$ and $\cos 2\theta = 1$, giving: $$a_{max} = \omega^2 r \left(1 + \frac{r}{L}\right)$$ ### Step 5: Convert to Head Using Newton's Second Law The pressure fluctuation in the cylinder required to accelerate the water column of mass $\rho A L$ in the pipe (where $A$ = pipe area, $L$ = pipe length) is: $$\Delta P = \rho \cdot A \cdot a_{max} \cdot \frac{1}{a_p} = \rho A \cdot a_{max} \cdot \frac{1}{a_p}$$ Wait — more carefully, the force needed to accelerate the water column is $F = (\rho A L) a$, and this is delivered via pressure $P = F/a_p$ on the piston. So: $$\Delta P = \frac{\rho A L \cdot a_{max}}{a_p}$$ Dividing by $\rho g$ to get the **head** $h_a$: $$h_a = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot a_{max}}{a_p \cdot g}$$ Substituting $a_{max}$: $$h_a = \frac{A L}{a_p g} \cdot \omega^2 r \left(1 + \frac{r}{L}\right)$$ $$\boxed{h_a = \frac{\omega^2 r L}{g} \cdot \frac{A}{a_p} \left(1 + \frac{r}{L}\right) = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g} \cdot \frac{A}{a_p} (L + r)}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Slip and Coefficient of Discharge ### Step 1: Theoretical Discharge For a single-acting pump: $$Q_{th} = \frac{a_p \cdot S \cdot N}{60} = \frac{a_p \cdot 2r \cdot N}{60}$$ ### Step 2: Actual Discharge $$Q_{act} = Q_{th} - Q_{slip}$$ ### Step 3: Coefficient of Discharge $$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$ ### Step 4: Slip Coefficient $$K_s = 1 - C_d = \frac{Q_{slip}}{Q_{th}}$$ ### Step 5: Percentage Slip $$\%\text{Slip} = K_s \times 100 = (1 - C_d) \times 100$$ > [!NOTE] > In KTU 2024 scheme problems, you are often given the indicator diagram dimensions (length and height) and the pump speed, and you must compute the work done and the power required. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Area Computation for Common Diagram Shapes KTU problems often ask you to compute the area of a simplified indicator diagram. The standard simplifications are: | Diagram Shape | Area Formula | KTU Use | |---|---|---| | Rectangle | $L \times H$ | Theoretical diagram | | Rectangle − Triangle (rounded corner) | $L \times H - \dfrac{1}{2} b h$ | Effect of valve resistance | | Trapezoid (sloped line) | $\dfrac{1}{2}(a+b) \times H$ | Acceleration effects | | Sum of rectangles + triangles | Manual partitioning | Actual (composite) diagram | **Example — Actual diagram with acceleration slope + friction drop:** $$A_{actual} = L(H_s + H_d) - \text{(area lost due to acceleration)} - \text{(area lost due to friction)} - \text{(area lost due to leakage)}$$ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Indicator Diagram Process ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Crank rotates via power input] --> B[Piston executes SHM in cylinder] B --> C[Suction stroke begins
Low pressure created] C --> D[Suction valve opens
Water enters cylinder] D --> E[Delivery stroke begins
High pressure created] E --> F[Delivery valve opens
Water pushed out] F --> G[Indicator stylus traces
Pressure vs Stroke on drum] G --> H[Indicator Diagram recorded
on graph paper] H --> I[Compare actual vs theoretical] I --> J{Are deviations
within limits?} J -- Yes --> K[Pump is healthy
Approved for service] J -- No --> L[Identify cause:
Leak / Friction /
Wire drawing] L --> M[Maintenance action:
Replace valves /
Re-bore cylinder] ``` ## 4.2 Schematic of the Actual Indicator Diagram (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid block-beta columns 3 block:label1["Pressure\nHead H (m)"]:3 block:label2["Delivery line (theoretical = Hd)"]:3 D["C\n(End of delivery)"]:1 space:1 E["D\n(Start of suction)"]:1 F["Slope due to\nacceleration head"]:2 G["Suction line (theoretical = -Hs)"]:3 H["A\n(Start of suction)"]:1 I["B\n(End of suction)"]:1 space:1 J["Stroke length S (m)"]:3 ``` ## 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram: Theoretical vs Actual ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph THEORETICAL["THEORETICAL INDICATOR DIAGRAM"] T1[Perfect rectangle] --> T2[Height = Hs + Hd] T2 --> T3[Width = Stroke S] T3 --> T4[Area = S x Hs + Hd] end subgraph ACTUAL["ACTUAL INDICATOR DIAGRAM"] A1[Rounded corners] --> A2[Sloped suction side] A2 --> A3[Sloped delivery side] A3 --> A4[Reduced overall area] end THEORETICAL -.->|Compare area| DELTA ACTUAL -.->|Compare area| DELTA DELTA[Delta Area = Energy lost to:
1. Acceleration
2. Friction
3. Wire drawing
4. Leakage] ``` ## 4.4 Component-Level Schematic: Indicator Gear Attachment ```mermaid graph TB PISTON["Piston rod
(reciprocating motion)"] -->|mechanical link| LEVER["Reducing lever
(motion reduction)"] LEVER -->|rotational input| DRUM["Indicator drum
(rotates with crank)"] PRESS["Cylinder pressure
(P or H)"] -->|via bore| STYLUS["Indicator stylus
(vertical motion)"] STYLUS -->|traces| PAPER["Graph paper on drum
(P vs S plot)"] PAPER --> DIAGRAM["Indicator Diagram
(closed curve)"] ``` ## 4.5 Causal Block Diagram: Sources of Distortion ```mermaid graph TB IDEAL["Theoretical Diagram
(perfect rectangle)"] --> DISTORT DISTORT{Distortion Sources} ACC["Acceleration of fluid
(sloped lines)"] --> DISTORT FRIC["Pipe friction
(uniform shift)"] --> DISTORT WIRE["Wire drawing
(rounded corners)"] --> DISTORT LEAK["Leakage
(height reduction)"] --> DISTORT DISTORT --> ACTUAL["Actual Diagram
(distorted shape)"] ACTUAL --> METRIC[Performance Metric] METRIC --> SLIP["Slip percentage"] METRIC --> CD["Coefficient of discharge"] METRIC --> POWER["Power required"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define an indicator diagram. List any four causes of deviation between the theoretical and actual indicator diagrams of a reciprocating pump.** **Model Answer:** An **indicator diagram** is a graphical plot of the fluid pressure (or pressure head) inside the cylinder of a reciprocating pump/compressor against the piston stroke (or volume) during one complete working cycle, obtained using a mechanical indicator fitted on the cylinder. **Four causes of deviation:** 1. **Acceleration of water** in the suction and delivery pipes, causing sloped lines instead of horizontal. 2. **Friction** in pipes, valves, and piston packing, which lowers the effective pressure head. 3. **Wire drawing** (valve resistance) at the suction and delivery valves, causing rounded corners of the diagram. 4. **Leakage** of fluid past the piston and valves, reducing the height of the actual diagram. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Explain the term "slip" of a reciprocating pump and write the expression for the percentage slip.** **Model Answer:** **Slip** of a reciprocating pump is defined as the **difference between the theoretical discharge and the actual discharge** of the pump per unit time. It occurs primarily because some water leaks back through the delivery valve during the delivery stroke. $$\text{Slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$$ **Percentage slip:** $$\%\ \text{Slip} = \frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100 = (1 - C_d) \times 100$$ Where $C_d$ is the **coefficient of discharge** of the pump. For a healthy pump, slip is typically 2–5%. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 4 questions may carry internal choice. Below are **two alternative 14-mark questions**, each with two 7-mark sub-parts. --- ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a mechanical indicator used for drawing the indicator diagram of a reciprocating pump. **(7 marks)** **b)** A single-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of **150 mm** and a stroke of **300 mm**. The pump runs at **60 rpm** and delivers water against a head of **30 m**. The suction head is **5 m**. Assuming no losses, calculate: - (i) The theoretical discharge in litres per second. - (ii) The work done per second. - (iii) The power required to drive the pump. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Part (a) — Mechanical Indicator A mechanical indicator consists of: 1. **Indicator cylinder** — a small cylinder connected via a bore to the pump cylinder. 2. **Indicator piston** — loaded by a calibrated spring; its motion is proportional to cylinder pressure. 3. **Reducing lever** — connects the pump piston rod to a drum (linkage ratio ~ 6:1). 4. **Drum** — rotates with the crank, carrying a small graph card. 5. **Stylus/pen** — attached to the indicator piston, traces pressure vs stroke on the card. **Working:** As the pump piston reciprocates, the drum rotates back and forth (representing stroke). Simultaneously, cylinder pressure pushes the indicator piston up against the spring, moving the stylus vertically.
