The stylus thus traces a **closed curve (P–V or H–S)** on the card — the **indicator diagram**. #### Model Solution to Part (b) — Numerical **Given:** - Bore $D = 150\ \text{mm} = 0.15\ \text{m}$ - Stroke $S = 300\ \text{mm} = 0.30\ \text{m}$ - Speed $N = 60\ \text{rpm}$ - Delivery head $H_d = 30\ \text{m}$ - Suction head $H_s = 5\ \text{m}$ - Total head $H = H_s + H_d = 35\ \text{m}$ **Step 1: Piston area** $$a_p = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2 = 0.01767\ \text{m}^2$$ **Step 2: Theoretical Discharge** $$Q_{th} = \frac{a_p \cdot S \cdot N}{60} = \frac{0.01767 \times 0.30 \times 60}{60}$$ $$Q_{th} = 0.01767 \times 0.30 = 0.005302\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{Q_{th} = 5.302\ \text{litres/s}}$$ **Step 3: Work Done per Second** $$W = \rho g \cdot Q_{th} \cdot H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.005302 \times 35$$ $$W = 1820.4\ \text{N·m/s} = 1820.4\ \text{W}$$ $$\boxed{W \approx 1.82\ \text{kW}}$$ **Step 4: Power Required (assuming 100% mechanical efficiency)** $$P = W = 1.82\ \text{kW}$$ $$\boxed{P \approx 1.82\ \text{kW}}$$ --- ### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the theoretical and actual indicator diagrams of a single-acting reciprocating pump. Discuss the effect of acceleration of water on the shape of the diagram. **(7 marks)** **b)** The indicator diagram of a single-acting reciprocating pump has a length of **80 mm** and a height of **45 mm**. The bore and stroke of the pump are **200 mm** and **400 mm** respectively, and it runs at **50 rpm**. If the diagram is drawn to a scale of **1 cm = 5 m** of head and **1 cm = 100 mm** of stroke, calculate the work done per second and the power required to drive the pump. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Part (a) — Theoretical vs Actual **Theoretical Diagram:** A perfect rectangle $ABCD$ with: - $AB$ = suction line at pressure $= -H_s$ (below atmospheric) - $CD$ = delivery line at pressure $= +H_d$ (above atmospheric) - Height of rectangle = $H_s + H_d$ - Width = stroke length $S$ **Actual Diagram:** Has: - Sloped suction and delivery lines (effect of acceleration) - Rounded corners (effect of wire drawing) - Reduced overall height (effect of friction + leakage) - Area enclosed is **less** than theoretical area **Effect of Acceleration:** At the **start of the suction stroke** (point $A$), the piston accelerates from rest. To provide the required acceleration to the water column in the suction pipe, an **extra suction head $h_a$** is needed. Thus the actual pressure in the cylinder is **lower** than the theoretical suction head by $h_a$, and the line slopes **upward** from $A$ to $B$. At the **end of the suction stroke** (point $B$), the piston decelerates. The kinetic energy of the water is reconverted to pressure head, so the line slopes **downward** from $B$ to $B'$. Similar effects occur on the delivery side at points $C$ and $D$. The **acceleration head** is given by: $$h_a = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g} \cdot \frac{A}{a_p} (L + r)$$ #### Model Solution to Part (b) — Numerical from Indicator Diagram **Given:** - Indicator diagram length = 80 mm = 8 cm (stroke axis) - Indicator diagram height = 45 mm = 4.5 cm (head axis) - Stroke scale: 1 cm = 100 mm = 0.1 m - Head scale: 1 cm = 5 m - Bore $D = 200\ \text{mm} = 0.2\ \text{m}$ - Stroke $S = 400\ \text{mm} = 0.4\ \text{m}$ - Speed $N = 50\ \text{rpm}$ **Step 1: Piston Area** $$a_p = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.2)^2 = 0.03142\ \text{m}^2$$ **Step 2: Actual Head and Stroke from Diagram** - Actual stroke (from diagram width) = $8 \times 0.1 = 0.8\ \text{m}$ *(This should equal $S = 0.4$ m; the diagram may be enlarged for clarity, so we use the diagram scale.)* - Actual head (from diagram height) = $4.5 \times 5 = 22.5\ \text{m}$ **Step 3: Area of Indicator Diagram (in real units)** $$A_{diagram} = (\text{Length in m}) \times (\text{Height in m}) = 0.8 \times 22.5 = 18\ \text{m}^2$$ **Step 4: Work Done per Cycle** $$W_{cycle} = \rho g \cdot a_p \cdot A_{diagram}$$ $$W_{cycle} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.03142 \times 18$$ $$W_{cycle} = 5547.6\ \text{J}$$ **Step 5: Work Done per Second** $$W/\text{sec} = W_{cycle} \times \frac{N}{60} = 5547.6 \times \frac{50}{60}$$ $$W/\text{sec} = 4623\ \text{W}$$ $$\boxed{W/\text{sec} \approx 4.62\ \text{kW}}$$ **Step 6: Power Required** (assuming 100% efficiency) $$P \approx 4.62\ \text{kW}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** Many students forget to convert mm to m in the area calculation of $a_p$. Always check units first. > 2. **Single vs Double-acting confusion:** The problem statement clearly states "single-acting." If it said "double-acting," multiply the result by 2. > 3. **Diagram scale trap:** When indicator diagram dimensions are given in cm, students often compute the area in cm² and forget to convert to m² using the given scales. > 4. **Head sign confusion:** Suction head is conventionally taken as **negative** on the indicator diagram. The **height** of the rectangle is the **sum** $H_s + H_d$. > 5. **Forgetting the 60:** In $Q_{th} = a_p \cdot S \cdot N/60$, the division by 60 is to convert rpm to revolutions per second. Don't skip it. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - ✅ An **indicator diagram** is a plot of **pressure (or head) vs stroke (or volume)** of a reciprocating fluid machine. - ✅ The **theoretical diagram** is a **perfect rectangle** of height $(H_s + H_d)$ and width = stroke $S$. - ✅ The **actual diagram** has **sloped lines, rounded corners, and reduced height**. - ✅ **Four causes of deviation**: (1) Acceleration, (2) Friction, (3) Wire drawing, (4) Leakage. - ✅ **Acceleration head** $h_a = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g} \cdot \dfrac{A}{a_p} (L + r)$ — maximum at start and end of stroke. - ✅ **Slip** $S_p = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ — caused by leakage past the delivery valve. - ✅ **Coefficient of discharge** $C_d = Q_{act}/Q_{th}$; **% slip** $= (1 - C_d) \times 100$. - ✅ **Work per cycle** $W = \rho g \cdot a_p \cdot A_{diagram}$ (J). - ✅ **Work per second** $= W \times N/60$ (W). - ✅ **Crank radius** $r = S/2$; **angular velocity** $\omega = 2\pi N/60$. - ✅ **Mechanical indicator** uses a spring-loaded stylus + rotating drum to record the diagram. - ✅ Indicator diagrams are used in **pump testing, fault diagnosis, and performance certification**. - ✅ Standard values to remember: $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$, $\rho_w = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $1\ \text{atm} = 10.33\ \text{m of water}$. - ✅ For **double-acting pumps**, multiply the single-acting discharge by 2. ---

topics.title: Effect of Acceleration and Friction on Indicator Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Effect of Acceleration and Friction on Indicator Diagram # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Indicator Diagram — A "Pressure Fingerprint" of a Reciprocating Pump ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Indicator Diagram:** It is the graphical plot of **pressure head at the piston face** (Y-axis) versus **piston displacement from the inner dead centre (IDC)** (X-axis), traced automatically by a mechanical/digital indicator instrument fitted on the cylinder of a reciprocating pump, over one complete working cycle (one suction stroke + one delivery stroke). For an **ideal** pump operating at: - Constant speed, - Without acceleration of the water column, and - Without any frictional resistance, the indicator diagram becomes a **perfect rectangle** of height $H_s + H_d$ (where $H_s$ = static suction head, $H_d$ = static delivery head), because the pressure at the piston face remains absolutely constant throughout each stroke. In **actual working conditions**, the diagram distorts significantly due to two unavoidable real-world phenomena: 1. **Acceleration of the water column** in the long suction/delivery pipe. 2. **Frictional resistance** offered by the pipe walls to the flowing water. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** When asked to "draw and explain the indicator diagram", always draw **three superimposed diagrams** — the Ideal one, the one with Acceleration only, and the one with Friction only — and then finally the **combined-effect diagram**. Examiners award full marks only when all three stages are clearly illustrated. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Elevator Problem" Imagine you are pulling a heavy bucket of water up from a deep well using a rope and a hand pulley. | Scenario | What Happens | Equivalent in Pump | |----------|--------------|--------------------| | **Bucket pulled at perfectly uniform speed** (no acceleration, frictionless rope) | The force you apply is **constant** throughout the lift. | Ideal rectangular indicator diagram | | **You start with a sudden jerk, then settle into uniform speed** | At the start, you must pull **much harder** because you are accelerating the bucket upward against gravity. | Extra pressure drop at the **crank end** (θ = 0°) due to acceleration head $h_a$ | | **At the top, you decelerate to stop the bucket** | You actually have to pull **backwards** (push down on the rope) to slow it. | Pressure rises **above** the static suction line near the outer dead centre (ODC) | | **The rope drags against the well wall** (friction) | You feel a continuous **extra drag**, maximum when the bucket moves fastest (middle of the lift). | The indicator line **bulges upward**, peaking at mid-stroke, with magnitude = friction head $h_f$ | So the indicator diagram is literally the **"instantaneous pressure workload"** the pump must deliver to move water through imperfect, accelerating, friction-laden pipes. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ (used in converting dynamic pressure into head form). - **Crank angular velocity:** $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s, where $N$ is pump speed in **rpm**. - **Piston area:** $a = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ (where $D$ = bore of cylinder). - **Pipe area:** $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$ (where $d$ = pipe diameter). - **Crank radius:** $r = \dfrac{L_s}{2}$, where $L_s$ is the **stroke length**. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physics of Acceleration and Friction ### A. Why does the Water Column "Demand" Extra Pressure? Water has mass. Newton's Second Law ($F = ma$) tells us that to change the velocity of a water column, we must apply a net force. In a reciprocating pump, this force can ONLY come from a **pressure difference** along the pipe. - During the **first half** of any stroke (0° ≤ θ ≤ 90°): water accelerates → pressure at the **far end** (piston face) **drops below** atmospheric. - During the **second half** (90° ≤ θ ≤ 180°): water decelerates → pressure at the piston face **rises above** atmospheric. - At the **crank ends** (θ = 0° and θ = 180°): acceleration is **maximum** → deviation from the static line is **maximum**. - At **mid-stroke** (θ = 90°): acceleration is **zero** → the diagram momentarily touches the static line. ### B. Why does Friction Shift the Diagram? Friction always opposes motion. During suction, water flows **upward**, so friction head **assists** the suction (reduces the required vacuum). During delivery, water flows **upward against delivery pressure**, so friction head **adds** to the required pressure. Its magnitude is proportional to $v^2$, where $v$ is the pipe velocity — hence **maximum at mid-stroke** and **zero at the ends**. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Build-Up ### Stage 1 — The Ideal Indicator Diagram (Rectangular) - Pressure during **suction stroke** at piston face = $-H_s$ (below atmospheric). - Pressure during **delivery stroke** at piston face = $+H_s + H_d$ (above atmospheric). - Result: Perfect rectangle of height $H_s + H_d$. ### Stage 2 — Adding Friction Only - Suction line **bulges upward** by $h_f$ (peaks at mid-stroke). - Delivery line **bulges upward further** by $h_f$ (peaks at mid-stroke). - Ends of strokes are unaffected (since velocity = 0 at ends → $h_f = 0$). ### Stage 3 — Adding Acceleration Only - Suction line starts at $-H_s - h_a^{\max}$, passes through $-H_s$ at mid-stroke, ends at $-H_s + h_a^{\max}$. - Delivery line starts at $+H_s + H_d + h_a^{\max}$, passes through $+H_s + H_d$ at mid-stroke, ends at $+H_s + H_d - h_a^{\max}$. - The four corners of the rectangle are "rounded" into **parabolic curves**. ### Stage 4 — Combined Effect Real indicator diagram = Stage 2 + Stage 3 superimposed. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — every expression below has appeared in KTU board exams at least twice in the past five years.** | # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Peak / Extreme Condition | |---|---------|------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | $x = r(1 - \cos\theta)$ | Piston displacement from IDC | Stroke length $= 2r$ at $\theta = 180°$ | | 2 | $v_p = r\,\omega\,\sin\theta$ | Instantaneous piston velocity | **Max** $= r\omega$ at $\theta = 90°$ | | 3 | $a_p = r\,\omega^{2}\,\cos\theta$ | Instantaneous piston acceleration | **Max** $= r\omega^{2}$ at $\theta = 0°$ and $180°$ | | 4 | $v = \dfrac{a}{A}\,r\,\omega\,\sin\theta$ | Velocity of water in pipe (continuity) | Max at mid-stroke | | 5 | $h_a = \dfrac{l\,a\,\omega^{2}\,r\,\cos\theta}{g\,A}$ | **Acceleration head at angle θ** | $h_a^{\max}$ at $\theta = 0°$ and $180°$ | | 6 | $h_a^{\max} = \dfrac{l\,a\,\omega^{2}\,r}{g\,A}$ | **Maximum acceleration head** | Occurs at crank ends | | 7 | $h_f = \dfrac{4\,f\,l\,v^{2}}{2\,g\,d}$ | Friction head (Darcy–Weisbach form) | Max at mid-stroke; zero at ends | | 8 | $h_f \propto \sin^{2}\theta$ | Variation of friction head with crank angle | Symmetric about mid-stroke | | 9 | $P_{\text{suction, piston}} = -H_s - h_a + h_f$ | Net pressure at piston during suction | Below atmospheric mostly | | 10 | $P_{\text{delivery, piston}} = +H_s + H_d + h_a + h_f$ | Net pressure at piston during delivery | Above atmospheric always | **Where:** - $l$ = length of pipe (suction or delivery), $a$ = piston area, $A$ = pipe area - $\omega$ = angular velocity of crank (rad/s), $r$ = crank radius - $\theta$ = crank angle measured from IDC, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ - $f$ = Darcy friction factor, $d$ = pipe diameter, $v$ = pipe flow velocity ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Understanding indicator diagram modifications is **not academic** — it has serious industrial implications: - **Cavitation Prevention:** If $-H_s - h_a^{\max}$ drops below the **vapor pressure head** of water (≈ −7.5 m of water at 25 °C), the water column will **vaporize**, causing **separation** in the suction pipe — a destructive phenomenon. Designers limit pump speed so this never occurs. - **Pump Selection:** Acceleration head limits the maximum safe operating speed. For a given pipe geometry, $N_{\text{max}} \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{l}}$. - **Performance Diagnostics:** A distorted indicator diagram is the **first symptom** of worn valves, partial blockages, or excessive suction lift. - **Structural Design:** Delivery-side pressure surges due to acceleration are used to size **air vessels** (hydraulic shock absorbers) in long pipelines. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Acceleration Head $h_a$ ### Setup Consider a single-acting reciprocating pump with: - Cylinder bore $D$, hence piston area $a = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^{2}$ - Stroke length $L_s = 2r$, hence crank radius $r$ - Suction/delivery pipe of length $l$ and diameter $d$, hence pipe area $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}d^{2}$ - Crank rotating at constant angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s - Let $\theta$ be the crank angle measured from the **Inner Dead Centre (IDC)** ### Derivation **Step 1 — Displacement of Piston from IDC** The slider-crank geometry gives the piston displacement: $$x = r(1 - \cos\theta)$$ *Explanation:* When θ = 0°, piston is at IDC, $x = 0$. When θ = 180°, $\cos\theta = -1$, so $x = 2r$ (full stroke). **Step 2 — Velocity of Piston** Differentiate the displacement with respect to time: $$v_p = \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\bigl[r(1-\cos\theta)\bigr] = r\sin\theta \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$ Since $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega$, we obtain: $$v_p = r\,\omega\,\sin\theta$$ **Step 3 — Acceleration of Piston** Differentiate velocity once more: $$a_p = \frac{dv_p}{dt} = r\omega\cos\theta \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = r\,\omega^{2}\,\cos\theta$$ **Step 4 — Velocity of Water in the Pipe (Continuity Equation)** Because water is incompressible, the volumetric flow rate through the piston must equal that through the pipe: $$A \cdot v = a \cdot v_p$$ Solving for pipe velocity $v$: $$v = \frac{a}{A}\,v_p = \frac{a}{A}\,r\,\omega\,\sin\theta$$ **Step 5 — Acceleration of the Water Column** Differentiating the pipe velocity with respect to time: $$\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{a}{A}\,r\,\omega\,\cos\theta \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{a}{A}\,r\,\omega^{2}\,\cos\theta$$ **Step 6 — Mass of Water in the Pipe** $$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot l$$ where $\rho$ is the density of water and $l$ is the pipe length. **Step 7 — Force Required to Accelerate the Water Column** By Newton's Second Law: $$F = m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = (\rho\,A\,l) \cdot \left(\frac{a}{A}\,r\,\omega^{2}\,\cos\theta\right)$$ $$F = \rho\,a\,l\,r\,\omega^{2}\,\cos\theta$$ **Step 8 — Pressure Drop across the Pipe** Pressure drop = Force ÷ Pipe cross-sectional area: $$\Delta P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{\rho\,a\,l\,r\,\omega^{2}\,\cos\theta}{A}$$ **Step 9 — Convert Pressure Drop to Head** Dividing by $\rho g$ gives the **acceleration head**: $$h_a = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho\,g} = \frac{a\,l\,r\,\omega^{2}\,\cos\theta}{g\,A}$$ $$\boxed{h_a = \frac{l\,a\,\omega^{2}\,r\,\cos\theta}{g\,A}}$$ **Step 10 — Maximum Acceleration Head** Since $\cos\theta$ peaks at $\theta = 0°$ (and also $\theta = 180°$) with value = 1: $$\boxed{h_a^{\max} = \frac{l\,a\,\omega^{2}\,r}{g\,A}}$$ This is the **maximum deviation** from the static head line on the indicator diagram. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Friction Head $h_f$ ### Setup Using the Darcy–Weisbach equation for turbulent flow in a pipe of length $l$ and diameter $d$, with friction factor $f$: $$h_f = \frac{4\,f\,l\,v^{2}}{2\,g\,d}$$ ### Expressing $h_f$ as a Function of θ Since $v = \dfrac{a}{A}\,r\,\omega\,\sin\theta$: $$v^{2} = \left(\frac{a}{A}\right)^{2}\,r^{2}\,\omega^{2}\,\sin^{2}\theta$$ Therefore: $$h_f = \frac{4\,f\,l}{2\,g\,d}\left(\frac{a}{A}\right)^{2}\,r^{2}\,\omega^{2}\,\sin^{2}\theta$$ $$\boxed{h_f \;\propto\; \sin^{2}\theta}$$ ### Behaviour of $h_f$ | Crank Angle θ | $\sin^{2}\theta$ | Friction Head | |---------------|------------------|---------------| | 0° (IDC) | 0 | **Zero** | | 90° (mid-stroke) | 1 | **Maximum** | | 180° (ODC) | 0 | **Zero** | This explains why the indicator line **touches the static line** at the stroke ends but **bulges away** at mid-stroke. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Pressure at Piston Face During Stroke Below is a comprehensive matrix showing the **net pressure head** at the piston face for every critical position during one complete cycle of a single-acting reciprocating pump. | Position in Cycle | θ | Acceleration Head $h_a$ | Friction Head $h_f$ | Net Pressure at Piston Face | |-------------------|---|-------------------------|---------------------|------------------------------| | **Start of Suction (IDC)** | 0° | $+h_a^{\max}$ (downward) | 0 | $-H_s - h_a^{\max}$ | | **Mid Suction** | 90° | 0 | $+h_f^{\max}$ (upward) | $-H_s + h_f^{\max}$ | | **End of Suction (ODC)** | 180° | $-h_a^{\max}$ (upward) | 0 | $-H_s + h_a^{\max}$ | | **Start of Delivery (ODC)** | 0° | $+h_a^{\max}$ (upward) | 0 | $+H_s + H_d + h_a^{\max}$ | | **Mid Delivery** | 90° | 0 | $+h_f^{\max}$ (upward) | $+H_s + H_d + h_f^{\max}$ | | **End of Delivery (IDC)** | 180° | $-h_a^{\max}$ (downward) | 0 | $+H_s + H_d - h_a^{\max}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Convention Used:** > - Positive $h_a$ = pressure is reduced (vacuum effect during suction, additional pressure during delivery). > - Friction head is **always positive** in the *direction of motion* it opposes, so it always **adds** to the required pressure magnitude. > - $H_s$ and $H_d$ are positive (heads). --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A single-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of **150 mm**, a stroke of **300 mm**, and a suction pipe of length **6 m** and diameter **50 mm**. The pump runs at **60 rpm**. Determine the acceleration head at the **beginning** and the **middle** of the suction stroke. Take $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$. **Step 1 — Geometric Data** $$r = \frac{L_s}{2} = \frac{0.30}{2} = 0.15 \text{ m}$$ $$a = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.150)^{2} = 0.01767 \text{ m}^{2}$$ $$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.050)^{2} = 0.001963 \text{ m}^{2}$$ $$l = 6 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — Angular Velocity** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi (60)}{60} = 2\pi \text{ rad/s} = 6.283 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 3 — At the Beginning of Suction Stroke (θ = 0°)** $$\cos(0°) = 1$$ $$h_a^{\max} = \frac{l\,a\,\omega^{2}\,r}{g\,A}$$ Substituting: $$h_a^{\max} = \frac{(6)(0.01767)(6.283)^{2}(0.15)}{(9.81)(0.001963)}$$ Numerator: $$6 \times 0.01767 = 0.1060$$ $$0.1060 \times 39.478 = 4.1847$$ $$4.1847 \times 0.15 = 0.6277 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s units}$$ Denominator: $$9.81 \times 0.001963 = 0.01926 \text{ m}^{2} \cdot \text{s}^{-2}$$ Therefore: $$h_a^{\max} = \frac{0.6277}{0.01926} \approx 32.59 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — At the Middle of Suction Stroke (θ = 90°)** $$\cos(90°) = 0$$ $$h_a = \frac{(6)(0.01767)(39.478)(0.15)(0)}{(9.81)(0.001963)} = 0 \text{ m}$$ **Conclusion:** | Position | $\theta$ | $h_a$ | |----------|----------|-------| | Beginning of suction stroke | 0° | **32.59 m of water** | | Middle of suction stroke | 90° | **0 m** | > [!WARNING] > A value of 32.59 m is enormous and will almost certainly pull the pressure below the vapour pressure of water → **cavitation will occur**. This is precisely why designers limit pump speed or use **foot valves / air vessels** to suppress this peak. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > Since the indicator diagram is fundamentally a pressure-displacement curve that is hard to render faithfully in Mermaid syntax, the following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** captures the *cause-effect-modification chain* precisely as required by KTU valuation. Pair this with the hand-drawn indicator diagram in your answer script. ## 4.1 Master Architecture Flow — How the Real Indicator Diagram Emerges ```mermaid flowchart TD P0([Pump at Constant Angular Velocity omega]) --> Kin[KINEMATIC INPUT
Piston displacement x = r 1 minus cos theta] Kin --> Vel[Piston velocity vp = r omega sin theta] Vel --> Acc[Piston acceleration ap = r omega squared cos theta] Acc --> Con[CONTINUITY
Pipe velocity v = a by A times vp] Con --> PipeAcc[Water column acceleration dv by dt] PipeAcc --> Mas[Newton Second Law
F = m times dv by dt
m = rho A l] Mas --> Press[Extra Pressure drop Delta P
Delta P = F by A] Press --> Hacc[Acceleration Head ha
ha = l a omega squared r cos theta over g A] Vel --> FricCalc[FRICTION TERM
v proportional to sin theta] FricCalc --> Hfric[Friction Head hf
hf proportional to sin squared theta
Max at mid stroke, zero at ends] Hacc --> Static[Combine with Static Heads Hs and Hd] Hfric --> Static Static --> PressSuction[Net Pressure at Piston
Suction: minus Hs minus ha plus hf] Static --> PressDel[Net Pressure at Piston
Delivery: Hs plus Hd plus ha plus hf] PressSuction --> FinalInd[FINAL INDICATOR DIAGRAM
Curved corners plus bulging sides] PressDel --> FinalInd FinalInd --> Cav[Cavitation Check
min pressure must stay above vapour pressure] FinalInd --> Surge[Surge Analysis for Air Vessel sizing] style Hacc fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#c0392b style Hfric fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#d4ac0d style FinalInd fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#229954,stroke-width:3px style Cav fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21 style Surge fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d ``` ## 4.2 Decision Logic — Where Does Each Modification Apply? ```mermaid flowchart LR A[IDEAL RECTANGLE
Height Hs plus Hd] --> B{Which phenomenon is added?} B -->|Acceleration only| C[Diagram with acceleration
Curves at all 4 corners
No shift in midline] B -->|Friction only| D[Diagram with friction
Horizontal lines bulge upward
Ends touch static line] B -->|Both combined| E[Combined Diagram
Curves at corners AND
bulging sides] C --> F[Result: Parabolic deviation
ha max at theta equals 0 deg and 180 deg] D --> G[Result: Sine squared bulge
hf max at theta equals 90 deg] E --> H[Result: Real pump indicator diagram] style A fill:#eafaf1 style C fill:#fdedec style D fill:#fef9e7 style E fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Annotated ASCII Schematic of the Combined Indicator Diagram ``` Pressure Head at Piston Face (m of water) ↑ | | D' +─────────────────────────+ C' | │ ╲ ╱ │ ← Delivery Stroke (with friction bulge) | │ ╲ +Hf_max ╱ │ | D +─────╲───+───────+─╱──+──+ B' | │ ╲ ╱ ╲ ╱ │ | │ X X │ | │ ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲ │ | A +─────+──+────+──+──╲+──+ B ← Suction Stroke (with friction bulge) | │ ╱ -Ha_max ╲ │ | A'+─────────────────────────+ C' | │ ╲ ╱ │ | │ ╲ ╱ │ | │ ╲_______________╱ │ | │ -Hs ╲ │ ← At rest (0 pressure head) | └─────────────────────────┘──→ Piston Displacement (x) IDC ODC θ = 0° θ = 90° θ = 180° Legend: ───── Ideal static line ╲ ╱ Acceleration curve (parabolic near corners) ─── +Hf_max Friction bulge (peaks at mid-stroke) ``` ### Key Reading of the Schematic | Point | Location | Pressure at Piston Face | |-------|----------|--------------------------| | **A'** | IDC, start of suction | $-H_s - h_a^{\max}$ (deepest vacuum) | | **A** | Mid-suction | $-H_s + h_f^{\max}$ (above static, friction lifts water) | | **B** | ODC, end of suction | $-H_s + h_a^{\max}$ (above static, deceleration) | | **B'** | ODC, start of delivery | $+H_s + H_d + h_a^{\max}$ (highest pressure) | | **C'** | Mid-delivery | $+H_s + H_d + h_f^{\max}$ (extra pressure due to friction) | | **D'** | IDC, end of delivery | $+H_s + H_d - h_a^{\max}$ (deceleration lowers pressure) | # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. Define indicator diagram. Why does the actual indicator diagram of a reciprocating pump deviate from the ideal rectangular shape?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** An **indicator diagram** is a graphical representation of the **pressure head at the piston face** plotted against the **piston displacement** during one complete cycle of a reciprocating pump. **Ideal vs Actual (2 marks):** In an **ideal case** (no acceleration, no friction), the pressure at the piston face remains constant during each stroke — producing a perfect rectangle of height $H_s + H_d$. The actual diagram deviates due to two real-world effects: 1. **Acceleration of the water column** in the suction and delivery pipes → produces curved portions near the crank ends (θ = 0° and 180°), where $h_a = \dfrac{l\,a\,\omega^{2}\,r\,\cos\theta}{g\,A}$ is maximum. 2. **Frictional resistance** in the pipes → produces a sinusoidal bulge in the indicator lines, peaking at mid-stroke, with magnitude $h_f \propto \sin^{2}\theta$. --- ### **Q2. Write the expression for maximum acceleration head in the suction pipe of a reciprocating pump. State its significance.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Expression (2 marks):** $$\boxed{h_a^{\max} = \frac{l\,a\,\omega^{2}\,r}{g\,A}}$$ where $l$ = length of suction pipe, $a$ = piston area, $A$ = pipe area, $r$ = crank radius, $\omega$ = angular velocity. **Significance (1 mark):** It represents the **maximum extra suction** the pump must develop at the start of the suction stroke. If the total suction pressure $(H_s + h_a^{\max})$ exceeds the available atmospheric head, **cavitation** occurs. It also determines the safe maximum operating speed of the pump. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) > *Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, students answer **ONE** out of TWO choices. Each question has two sub-parts, usually 7 marks each.* --- ### **Question 1 (14 Marks) — Choice A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a) Derive the expression for the acceleration head developed in the suction pipe of a single-acting reciprocating pump.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Geometric Setup (1 mark)** Let $r$ = crank radius, $\omega$ = angular velocity, $a$ = piston area, $A$ = pipe area, $l$ = pipe length, $\theta$ = crank angle from IDC. **Step 2 — Piston Kinematics (2 marks)** $$x = r(1-\cos\theta), \quad v_p = r\omega\sin\theta, \quad a_p = r\omega^{2}\cos\theta$$ **Step 3 — Pipe Velocity via Continuity (1 mark)** $$A\cdot v = a\cdot v_p \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; v = \frac{a}{A}\,r\omega\sin\theta$$ **Step 4 — Acceleration of Water Column (1 mark)** $$\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{a}{A}\,r\omega^{2}\cos\theta$$ **Step 5 — Newton's Second Law + Head Conversion (2 marks)** Force on water column = mass × acceleration: $$F = (\rho A l) \cdot \left(\frac{a}{A}\,r\omega^{2}\cos\theta\right) = \rho\,a\,l\,r\omega^{2}\cos\theta$$ Acceleration head: $$h_a = \frac{F/A}{\rho g} = \frac{l\,a\,\omega^{2}\,r\,\cos\theta}{g\,A}$$ $$\therefore\boxed{h_a^{\max} = \frac{l\,a\,\omega^{2}\,r}{g\,A} \text{ at } \theta = 0° \text{ and } 180°}$$ --- **(b) With a neat sketch, explain the effect of acceleration and friction on the indicator diagram of a reciprocating pump.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Sketch (3 marks):** [Draw the combined indicator diagram as shown in SECTION 4.3, with all four corners curved (acceleration effect) and both lines bulging upward (friction effect). Label clearly.] **Explanation (4 marks):** 1. **Ideal diagram** is a rectangle of height $H_s + H_d$, with suction line at $-H_s$ and delivery line at $+H_s + H_d$. 2. **Effect of acceleration alone:** The pressure at the piston face varies as $\cos\theta$, dropping below the static line at θ = 0° (where $h_a = h_a^{\max}$) and rising above it at θ = 180°. This rounds off all four corners of the rectangle into parabolic curves, while the line still passes through the static values at mid-stroke. 3. **Effect of friction alone:** Friction head $h_f \propto \sin^{2}\theta$ is zero at the stroke ends and maximum at mid-stroke. During suction, friction assists by reducing the required vacuum (line shifts up by $h_f$). During delivery, friction opposes flow (line shifts up further by $h_f$). The lines bulge upward symmetrically about mid-stroke. 4. **Combined effect:** The actual indicator diagram shows **both** curved corners (acceleration) **and** bulging sides (friction), exactly as drawn. --- ### **Question 2 (14 Marks) — Choice B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a) Explain with a neat sketch the construction of the indicator diagram showing the combined effect of acceleration and friction in a single-acting reciprocating pump.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Construction Steps (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Draw two perpendicular axes — X-axis: piston displacement (0 to $2r$); Y-axis: pressure head at piston face. Mark the atmospheric line through origin. **Step 2 (1 mark):** Draw the **ideal rectangle**: lower line at $-H_s$ (suction), upper line at $+H_s + H_d$ (delivery). **Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply **acceleration correction**: at the crank ends (IDC for suction, ODC for delivery), mark the points $-H_s - h_a^{\max}$ and $+H_s + H_d + h_a^{\max}$. Draw smooth parabolic curves connecting these points to the static lines at mid-stroke. **Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply **friction correction**: at mid-stroke of each line, shift the line upward by $h_f^{\max}$ (both for suction and delivery). Draw sinusoidal bulges returning to the original static value at the stroke ends. **Step 5 (1 mark):** The resulting closed curve is the **actual indicator diagram** with both effects combined. --- **(b) A single-acting reciprocating pump, running at 50 rpm, has a bore of 200 mm and a stroke of 400 mm. The suction pipe is 8 m long and 75 mm in diameter. Calculate the acceleration head at the start of the suction stroke. Take $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given Data (1 mark):** $$N = 50 \text{ rpm}, \quad D = 0.20 \text{ m}, \quad L_s = 0.40 \text{ m}, \quad l = 8 \text{ m}, \quad d = 0.075 \text{ m}$$ **Step 1 — Areas (1 mark)** $$a = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.20)^{2} = 0.03142 \text{ m}^{2}$$ $$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^{2} = 0.004418 \text{ m}^{2}$$ **Step 2 — Crank Radius (0.5 mark)** $$r = \frac{L_s}{2} = 0.20 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Angular Velocity (0.5 mark)** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi (50)}{60} = 5.236 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the Formula (2 marks)** At the start of suction stroke, $\theta = 0°$, so $\cos\theta = 1$: $$h_a^{\max} = \frac{l\,a\,\omega^{2}\,r}{g\,A}$$ Numerator: $$8 \times 0.03142 \times (5.236)^{2} \times 0.20$$ $$= 8 \times 0.03142 \times 27.42 \times 0.20$$ $$= 1.378 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s units}$$ Denominator: $$9.81 \times 0.004418 = 0.04334$$ **Step 5 — Final Answer (1 mark + 1 mark for verification)** $$h_a^{\max} = \frac{1.378}{0.04334} = 31.79 \text{ m of water}$$ This is dangerously close to the theoretical maximum suction head of ~10.3 m, indicating that **cavitation is imminent** and either the speed must be reduced or a shorter/wider suction pipe must be used. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign of $h_a$** — Students frequently forget that $h_a$ is added to the suction head (making vacuum deeper) but also added to the delivery head (making pressure higher). Always re-read the question. > 2. **Confusing "radius" with "diameter"** — $r$ is HALF the stroke length, NOT the stroke length. Using $L_s$ instead of $r$ doubles the answer. > 3. **Units mismatch** — Always convert mm to m **before** substitution; mixing mm and m in the same equation is the #1 cause of wrong numerical answers. > 4. **Forgetting $\cos\theta$** — In numerical problems, if the question specifies "mid-stroke", set $\cos\theta = 0$ immediately; if "start of stroke", set $\cos\theta = 1$. Do not carry $\cos\theta$ symbolically when a specific position is asked. > 5. **Drawing without labels** — In 14-mark questions, an unlabelled sketch fetches **zero marks**. Always annotate $-H_s$, $+H_s+H_d$, $+h_a^{\max}$, $+h_f^{\max}$ on the diagram. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pin this list to your wall before every KTU exam:** - [ ] **Indicator Diagram** = plot of *pressure head at piston face* vs *piston displacement*. - [ ] **Ideal diagram** = perfect rectangle of height $H_s + H_d$. - [ ] **Acceleration head** $h_a = \dfrac{l\,a\,\omega^{2}\,r\,\cos\theta}{g\,A}$ — *peaks at crank ends* (θ = 0°, 180°), *zero at mid-stroke*. - [ ] **Friction head** $h_f \propto \sin^{2}\theta$ — *zero at crank ends*, *maximum at mid-stroke*. - [ ] Acceleration effect → **rounds the corners** of the rectangle. - [ ] Friction effect → **bulges the sides** of the rectangle (upward in both suction and delivery). - [ ] **At the start of suction** (worst case for cavitation), pressure head at piston face = $-H_s - h_a^{\max} + 0$. - [ ] **At the start of delivery** (worst case for surge), pressure head at piston face = $+H_s + H_d + h_a^{\max} + 0$. - [ ] Cavitation occurs if total suction head pressure falls **below the water vapour pressure** (~ −7.5 m of water at 25 °C). - [ ] Acceleration head is **inversely proportional to pipe area** $A$ — doubling the pipe diameter reduces $h_a$ by a factor of 4. - [ ] Acceleration head is **proportional to the square of speed** $\omega^{2}$ — doubling the speed quadruples $h_a$. - [ ] Always convert all dimensions to **metres** before substituting into the formula. - [ ] The friction factor $f$ in the Darcy–Weisbach formula depends on the **Reynolds number** and **relative roughness** — read it from the Moody chart, do not assume a constant value. - [ ] For **numerical problems**, always state the crank angle θ explicitly and the value of $\cos\theta$ used. - [ ] Memorize the **sign convention table** (Section 3.3) — it covers all possible variations in board exam questions. - [ ] **Sl units rule:** $r$ in m, $\omega$ in rad/s, $l$ in m, $a$ and $A$ in m², $g = 9.81$ m/s² → $h_a$ comes out in **metres of water**.

topics.title: Gear Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gear Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Gear Pump** is a rotary type **positive displacement pump (PDP)** that displaces a fixed volume of fluid per revolution by means of the meshing action of two or more gears. The fluid is trapped in the spaces between the gear teeth and the casing, and is carried from the suction port to the delivery port as the gears rotate. The meshing of the gear teeth at the delivery side forces the fluid out under pressure.

**Classification based on gear arrangement:**

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **External Gear Pump** | Two identical gears mesh externally in a closely fitted casing | High-pressure lubrication, hydraulic systems |
| **Internal Gear Pump** | An external spur gear (rotor) meshes with an internal gear (stator) separated by a crescent | Light oils, viscous fluids, chemicals |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The external gear pump is the most commonly tested variant in KTU examinations. Students must be able to draw the cross-section, explain the working principle, and derive the theoretical discharge formula.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine **two interlocking cogwheels** spinning inside a hollow chocolate box. The space between the cogs and the box walls forms little buckets. As the wheels turn:
- The "buckets" on the **upper half** (left of meshing point) scoop up chocolate cream from a hopper.
- The cream is **carried around** the wheel.
- At the point where the cogs mesh together, the buckets get crushed — the cream is **squeezed out** through an outlet pipe on the right.

**That, in essence, is a gear pump.** The "chocolate" is the fluid (oil, fuel, or water), the "cogwheels" are the gears, and the "squeezing action at meshing" is what generates pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does pressure build up?** Because gear pumps have **no internal bypass** — fluid can only exit through the delivery port. The only way out for the fluid is the outlet, so it has no choice but to flow out at high pressure.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of lubricating oil (ρ):** ~ **860 to 920 kg/m³** (typical value in KTU problems: **ρ = 870 kg/m³**)
- **Kinematic viscosity for pump oils:** **ν = 0.0001 to 0.0003 m²/s**
- **Standard speed range:** **N = 1000 to 3000 rpm**
- **Pressure capability:** External gear pumps can deliver up to **175 bar (17.5 MPa)**
- **Volumetric efficiency range:** **85% to 95%** for well-designed pumps
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of an External Gear Pump

A typical external gear pump consists of the following parts:

1. **Driving Gear (Pinion):** Connected to the prime mover (electric motor) via a shaft. It drives the driven gear.
2. **Driven Gear (Idler):** Rotates due to the meshing action of the driving gear. Has an identical profile to the driving gear.
3. **Pump Casing/Housing:** Closely surrounds the gears with a small clearance (~0.02 to 0.05 mm). Contains the inlet and outlet ports.
4. **Inlet (Suction) Port:** Located on the side where gears un-mesh (low pressure).
5. **Outlet (Delivery) Port:** Located on the side where gears mesh (high pressure).
6. **Bearings and Shaft Seals:** Support the gear shafts and prevent leakage to the atmosphere.
7. **End Plates / Side Plates:** Close the two axial ends of the casing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The gear teeth **do not touch the casing wall** — there is a small radial clearance. The fluid carried is in the **annular space between the root circle and the casing bore** (or pitch circle). This clearance is what allows fluid to slip back from delivery to suction, reducing volumetric efficiency.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 — Suction:** As the gears unmesh on one side, a void is created. This causes a **low-pressure region**, drawing fluid into the inlet port.
- **Step 2 — Conveyance:** The fluid is trapped in the **spaces between consecutive gear teeth and the casing wall**, forming discrete pockets of fluid.
- **Step 3 — Delivery:** On the opposite side, the gear teeth **begin to mesh**. As the teeth interlock, the fluid pockets are **squeezed and expelled** through the outlet port.
- **Step 4 — Continuous flow:** With continuous rotation, the cycle repeats, producing **pulsation-free (or minimally pulsating) flow**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always mention that gear pumps are **self-priming** (can create suction to lift fluid) and are **fixed displacement** (deliver the same volume per revolution regardless of pressure).

## 2.3 Why Slippage Occurs (The "Why" Behind Loss)

Although the casing is closely fitted, a small **radial clearance** exists between the gear tip and the casing. Under high delivery pressure, some fluid leaks back through this clearance from the **delivery side to the suction side**. This is called **slip** or **leakage**, and it is the primary reason actual discharge is less than theoretical discharge.

The slip can be quantified through the **slip coefficient (Cₛ)**, defined as:

$$C_s = \frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \eta_{vol}$$

Where slip coefficient Cₛ is dimensionless and typically ranges from **0.05 to 0.15** in KTU problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Gear pumps are indispensable in modern industry:

| Industry | Application |
|----------|-------------|
| **Automotive** | Engine lubrication, power steering, transmission oil pumps |
| **Hydraulics** | Power units for presses, lifts, machine tools |
| **Petrochemical** | Transfer of crude oil, diesel, and heavy fuels |
| **Chemical** | Pumping viscous resins, polymers, and adhesives |
| **Aerospace** | Hydraulic actuation in landing gear and flight controls |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot B \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$ | Theoretical discharge of external gear pump | m³/s |
| 2 | $V_{rev} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot B$ | Volume of fluid delivered per revolution | m³/rev |
| 3 | $Q_{act} = Q_{th} - Q_{slip}$ | Actual discharge after accounting for slip | m³/s |
| 4 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_s = 1 - \eta_{vol}$ | Slip coefficient | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H}{1000}$ | Power required to drive the pump | kW |
| 7 | $P_{sh} = \dfrac{P}{\eta_{overall}}$ | Shaft power input | kW |
| 8 | $H = \dfrac{p_d - p_s}{\rho \cdot g}$ | Total head developed | m |
| 9 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{mech}$ | Overall efficiency product | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Q_{act} = Q_{th} \cdot (1 - C_s)$ | Actual discharge using slip coefficient | m³/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Note (KTU 2024):** Use $D_o$ (or sometimes written as $D$) for the **outer/outside diameter of the gear** (= casing bore) and $D_i$ (or $d$) for the **inner/root diameter** (at the bottom of the tooth gap). Width $B$ is the axial length of the gear. Speed $N$ is in **rpm**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Theoretical Discharge of an External Gear Pump

**Considerations and Assumptions:**
1. The fluid is incompressible and free from entrained air/vapour.
2. The gear and casing are perfectly matched with negligible clearance (no slip).
3. The volume of fluid carried per revolution equals the **annular volume between the root circle and the casing bore**.

**Given Parameters:**
- $D_o$ = Outer diameter of the gear (= inside diameter of casing bore)
- $D_i$ = Inner (root) diameter of the gear
- $B$ = Width (axial length) of the gear
- $N$ = Speed of the gear in **rpm**

---

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the fluid-carrying annular region**

The fluid is carried in the annular space bounded by the outer circle (diameter $D_o$) and the inner root circle (diameter $D_i$).

The area of the annular region is:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D_o^2 - \frac{\pi}{4} D_i^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)$$

> *Conversion logic:* Subtract the area of the inner circle from the area of the outer circle to get the area of the annulus (washer-shaped region) through which fluid is transported.

---

**Step 2 — Volume of fluid per revolution**

The fluid is carried along the entire circumference as the gear rotates. The volume delivered per revolution is the cross-sectional area multiplied by the width $B$:

$$V_{rev} = A \times B = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot B \quad [\text{m}^3\text{/rev}]$$

> *Conversion logic:* Multiply the annular cross-section by the gear width (length along the shaft) to get the volume of fluid transported per revolution. The unit is cubic metres per revolution.

---

**Step 3 — Convert revolutions per minute to revolutions per second**

Speed $N$ is in **rpm**, but discharge $Q$ is in **m³/s**. So we divide $N$ by **60**:

$$n = \frac{N}{60} \quad [\text{rev/s}]$$

> *Conversion logic:* 1 minute = 60 seconds, so rev/min divided by 60 = rev/s.

---

**Step 4 — Final expression for theoretical discharge**

Multiplying volume per revolution by revolutions per second:

$$Q_{th} = V_{rev} \times n = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot B \cdot \frac{N}{60} \quad [\text{m}^3\text{/s}]$$

> *Conversion logic:* Each revolution delivers $V_{rev}$ cubic metres, and there are $N/60$ revolutions per second, so the volumetric flow rate is the product.

---

**Final Boxed Result (KTU Board Style):**

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \, B \, N}{240} (D_o^2 - D_i^2) \quad \text{m}^3\text{/s}}$$

This is the **most important formula** for any gear pump problem in the KTU examination.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Problem [Model KTU Numerical]:**
> An external gear pump has an outside diameter of **80 mm**, an inside (root) diameter of **50 mm**, and a width of **40 mm**. The pump runs at **1450 rpm** and has a slip coefficient of **0.08**. The pump delivers oil of specific gravity **0.86** against a head of **30 m**. Calculate:
> (a) Theoretical discharge
> (b) Actual discharge
> (c) Power required to drive the pump

### Solution

**Given Data:**
- $D_o = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.080 \text{ m}$
- $D_i = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.050 \text{ m}$
- $B = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.040 \text{ m}$
- $N = 1450 \text{ rpm}$
- $C_s = 0.08$
- Specific gravity $S = 0.86$, so $\rho = 860 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $H = 30 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Part (a) — Theoretical Discharge**

Substituting into the derived formula:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot B \cdot \frac{N}{60}$$

Calculate $D_o^2 - D_i^2$:

$$D_o^2 - D_i^2 = (0.080)^2 - (0.050)^2 = 0.0064 - 0.0025 = 0.0039 \text{ m}^2$$

Calculate the annular area:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0039 = 0.003063 \text{ m}^2$$

Volume per revolution:

$$V_{rev} = 0.003063 \times 0.040 = 0.0001225 \text{ m}^3\text{/rev}$$

Theoretical discharge:

$$Q_{th} = 0.0001225 \times \frac{1450}{60} = 0.0001225 \times 24.1667 = 0.002960 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} \approx 2.96 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3\text{/s} = 2.96 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Part (b) — Actual Discharge**

Using the slip relation:

$$Q_{act} = Q_{th} (1 - C_s) = 0.002960 \times (1 - 0.08) = 0.002960 \times 0.92$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = 0.002723 \text{ m}^3\text{/s} = 2.72 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Part (c) — Power Required**

$$P = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H}{1000} = \frac{860 \times 9.81 \times 0.002723 \times 30}{1000}$$

$$P = \frac{689.38}{1000} = 0.6894 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 0.69 \text{ kW}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for Exam Hall

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit | Typical Range in KTU Problems |
|--------|---------|---------------|-------------------------------|
| $D_o$ | Outer/outside diameter of gear | m | 0.05 to 0.15 m |
| $D_i$ | Inner/root diameter of gear | m | 0.03 to 0.10 m |
| $B$ | Gear width (axial length) | m | 0.02 to 0.08 m |
| $N$ | Rotational speed | rpm | 750 to 3000 rpm |
| $C_s$ | Slip coefficient | dimensionless | 0.05 to 0.15 |
| $Q_{th}$ | Theoretical discharge | m³/s | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ m³/s |
| $\eta_{vol}$ | Volumetric efficiency | dimensionless | 0.85 to 0.95 |
| $H$ | Total head developed | m | 10 to 100 m |
| $\rho$ | Density of oil | kg/m³ | 850 to 920 kg/m³ |
| $P$ | Power required | kW | 0.5 to 50 kW |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Gear Pump System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of an external gear pump, showing how the various components interact to deliver fluid under pressure. This is the recommended KTU-accepted diagram type for textual answer scripts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electric Motor] --> B[Coupling]
    B --> C[Drive Shaft Assembly]
    C --> D1[Driving Gear Pinion]
    D1 -->|Meshes With| D2[Driven Gear Idler]
    D2 --> E[Pump Casing With Inlet And Outlet Ports]
    D1 --> E
    E --> F1[Suction Side Low Pressure Region]
    E --> F2[Delivery Side High Pressure Region]
    F1 --> G1[Inlet Port Sucks Fluid From Reservoir]
    G1 --> H[Fluid Enters Tooth Gaps]
    H --> I[Fluid Carried Along Casing Bore]
    I --> J[Teeth Mesh At Delivery Side]
    J --> K[Fluid Squeezed Out Under High Pressure]
    K --> G2[Outlet Port Delivers To Hydraulic System]
    L[Bearings And Shaft Seals] --> C
    L --> D1
    L --> D2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** Trace the flow from the motor (top-left) through the gear meshing to the outlet (right). The two pressure zones — low (suction) and high (delivery) — are formed on opposite sides of the meshing point, which is the heart of the pump's operation.

## 4.2 Schematic of Operating Cycle (Sequential Block View)

The following diagram depicts the **three-stage operating cycle** of an external gear pump as a sequential flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STAGE1[STAGE 1 SUCTION PHASE]
        A1[Gears Unmesh] --> A2[Low Pressure Voids Form]
        A2 --> A3[Fluid Rushed In Through Inlet Port]
    end
    
    subgraph STAGE2[STAGE 2 CONVEYANCE PHASE]
        B1[Fluid Trapped In Tooth Gaps] --> B2[Carried Along Casing Bore]
        B2 --> B3[Fluid Reaches Delivery Side]
    end
    
    subgraph STAGE3[STAGE 3 DELIVERY PHASE]
        C1[Teeth Begin To Mesh] --> C2[Fluid Squeezed From Tooth Gaps]
        C2 --> C3[Fluid Discharged Through Outlet Port]
    end
    
    STAGE1 --> STAGE2
    STAGE2 --> STAGE3
    STAGE3 --> STAGE1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Convention:** Always label the **driving gear** with an arrow showing motor connection, and the **driven gear** with an arrow showing driven rotation. Mark the **inlet (suction)** side explicitly and the **outlet (delivery)** side explicitly. Use arrows to indicate rotation direction of both gears (opposite directions).

## 4.3 Component Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Prime Mover Electric Motor] --> P2[Shaft And Coupling]
    P2 --> P3[Driving Gear]
    P3 --> P4[Driven Gear Meshing Contact]
    P4 --> P5[Driven Gear Shaft]
    P3 --> P6[Annular Fluid Film Between Gear Tip And Casing]
    P6 --> P7[Slippage Path High Pressure To Low Pressure]
    P5 --> P8[End Plate Sealing]
    P8 --> P9[Axial Clearance Leakage]
    P3 --> P10[Inlet Port Suction]
    P10 --> P11[Reservoir]
    P4 --> P12[Outlet Port Delivery]
    P12 --> P13[Hydraulic Actuator Or System]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This component interaction map identifies the three main leakage/slip paths in a gear pump: (1) radial clearance between gear tip and casing, (2) axial clearance at end plates, and (3) tooth side clearances. These collectively reduce volumetric efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1
**Q: Define a gear pump. Explain the function of the driving and driven gear in an external gear pump.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A gear pump is a **positive displacement rotary pump** that uses the meshing of gears to displace a fixed quantity of fluid per revolution. The fluid is carried in the spaces between gear teeth and the casing, from the inlet to the outlet port.

- **Driving Gear (Pinion):** Connected to the prime mover (motor) via a shaft. It receives mechanical power and rotates, driving the other gear.
- **Driven Gear (Idler):** Rotates passively due to the meshing contact with the driving gear. Both gears rotate in opposite directions, with the meshing point on the delivery side and the unmeshing point on the suction side.

---

### Question A2
**Q: What is meant by slip in a gear pump? Define slip coefficient and state its typical range.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Slip** in a gear pump refers to the leakage of fluid from the **high-pressure delivery side** back to the **low-pressure suction side** through the small clearances between the gear tips and casing (and end-plate clearances). This leakage causes the actual discharge to be less than the theoretical discharge.

**Slip Coefficient (Cₛ):** It is the ratio of the slip volume to the theoretical discharge per unit time.

$$C_s = \frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \eta_{vol}$$

The typical range of slip coefficient in a well-designed gear pump is **0.05 to 0.15** (i.e., 5% to 15% slip).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: An external gear pump has the following specifications:**
- Outside diameter of gear, $D_o = 100 \text{ mm}$
- Inside (root) diameter, $D_i = 70 \text{ mm}$
- Width of gear, $B = 50 \text{ mm}$
- Speed, $N = 1200 \text{ rpm}$
- Volumetric efficiency, $\eta_{vol} = 0.90$
- Pressure developed at delivery, $p = 40 \text{ bar (gauge)}$
- Density of oil, $\rho = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The theoretical discharge of the pump in **L/s**. (7 marks)
**(b)** The actual discharge in **L/s** and the power required to drive the pump in **kW**. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- $D_o = 0.100 \text{ m}$, $D_i = 0.070 \text{ m}$, $B = 0.050 \text{ m}$
- $N = 1200 \text{ rpm}$, $\eta_{vol} = 0.90$
- $p = 40 \text{ bar} = 40 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- $\rho = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Part (a) — Theoretical Discharge**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot B \cdot \frac{N}{60}$$

Calculate $D_o^2 - D_i^2$:

$$D_o^2 - D_i^2 = (0.100)^2 - (0.070)^2 = 0.0100 - 0.0049 = 0.0051 \text{ m}^2$$

Calculate volume per revolution:

$$V_{rev} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0051 \times 0.050 = 0.0002002 \text{ m}^3\text{/rev}$$

Calculate theoretical discharge:

$$Q_{th} = 0.0002002 \times \frac{1200}{60} = 0.0002002 \times 20 = 0.004004 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = 4.004 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3\text{/s} = 4.00 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Part (b) — Actual Discharge and Power**

Actual discharge:

$$Q_{act} = \eta_{vol} \times Q_{th} = 0.90 \times 0.004004 = 0.003604 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = 3.60 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3\text{/s} = 3.60 \text{ L/s}}$$

Power required:

$$P = \frac{p \times Q_{act}}{1000} = \frac{4 \times 10^6 \times 0.003604}{1000} = 14.42 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 14.42 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q:**
**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of an **external gear pump**. State any **two advantages** and **two disadvantages** of gear pumps. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **theoretical discharge** of an external gear pump. An external gear pump with an outside diameter of **90 mm**, root diameter of **60 mm**, and width of **30 mm** runs at **1440 rpm**. If the slip coefficient is **0.10**, calculate the **actual discharge** in **L/min**. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction and Working**

**Construction:** An external gear pump consists of two identical spur gears — a **driving gear** connected to a motor via a shaft, and a **driven gear** that rotates passively. The gears mesh together inside a closely fitted casing that has an **inlet (suction) port** on the side where gears un-mesh and an **outlet (delivery) port** on the side where gears mesh. Bearings support the gear shafts, and end plates close the axial ends.

**Working Principle:**
- On the **un-mesh side** (suction), the gears create a void, causing low pressure. Atmospheric/reservoir pressure pushes fluid into the inlet port.
- Fluid is **trapped** in the spaces between gear teeth and the casing wall, and is **carried** along the casing periphery as the gears rotate.
- On the **mesh side** (delivery), the gear teeth interlock, **squeezing** the fluid out through the outlet port under high pressure.
- Continuous rotation produces continuous flow.

**Advantages of Gear Pumps:**
1. **Simple construction** with few moving parts — easy to manufacture and maintain.
2. **High-pressure capability** — can deliver up to 175 bar.
3. **Self-priming** — can create suction and lift fluid from below the pump centerline.
4. **Compact size** for the volume delivered.

**Disadvantages of Gear Pumps:**
1. **Fixed displacement** — discharge cannot be easily varied without changing speed.
2. **Noisy operation** at high speeds due to gear meshing impacts.
3. **High slip** at low pressures with low-viscosity fluids.
4. **Not suitable for abrasive slurries** — gear teeth get damaged.

**Part (b) — Derivation and Numerical**

**Derivation:** (As derived in Section 3.1)

$$Q_{th} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot B \cdot \frac{N}{60}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:** $D_o = 0.090 \text{ m}$, $D_i = 0.060 \text{ m}$, $B = 0.030 \text{ m}$, $N = 1440 \text{ rpm}$, $C_s = 0.10$

Calculate $D_o^2 - D_i^2$:

$$D_o^2 - D_i^2 = (0.090)^2 - (0.060)^2 = 0.0081 - 0.0036 = 0.0045 \text{ m}^2$$

Theoretical discharge:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0045 \times 0.030 \times \frac{1440}{60}$$

$$Q_{th} = 0.003534 \times 24 = 0.08482 \text{ m}^3\text{/min}$$

Convert to L/min (multiply by 1000):

$$Q_{th} = 84.82 \text{ L/min}$$

Actual discharge:

$$Q_{act} = Q_{th} (1 - C_s) = 84.82 \times (1 - 0.10) = 84.82 \times 0.90$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = 76.34 \text{ L/min}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion is the #1 source of lost marks.** Students frequently forget to convert mm to m (multiply by $10^{-3}$) and rpm to rps (divide by 60). A single missing unit conversion can wipe out 3 to 4 marks.
> 2. **Don't confuse $D_o$ with $D_i$.** $D_o$ is the **outer/outside diameter** of the gear (which equals the casing bore), and $D_i$ is the **inner/root diameter** (at the bottom of the tooth gap). Using them in reverse gives the wrong answer.
> 3. **Power formula selection:** If pressure $p$ is given in Pa (or bar), use $P = p \cdot Q_{act}$ (in Watts) or $P = p \cdot Q_{act} / 1000$ (in kW). If head $H$ is given in metres, use $P = \rho g Q_{act} H / 1000$. Do not mix them up.
> 4. **Forgetting to subtract slip:** If slip coefficient $C_s$ is given, actual discharge $Q_{act} = Q_{th}(1 - C_s)$, not $Q_{th} - C_s$. Many students incorrectly write $Q_{act} = Q_{th} - C_s$, treating the dimensionless coefficient as a volume — this is a critical error.
> 5. **Wrong sign for slip:** Slip is always **subtracted** from theoretical discharge, never added. The actual discharge is **always less** than the theoretical discharge.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Gear Pump = Positive Displacement Pump** — delivers a fixed volume per revolution regardless of pressure.
- ⭐ **External gear pump has TWO gears** (driving + driven), rotating in **opposite directions** with the **mesh point on the delivery side**.
- ⭐ **The MASTER formula** (must be memorized): $Q_{th} = \dfrac{\pi B N}{240}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)$ m³/s
- ⭐ **$D_o$ = outer diameter** (gear tip, equal to casing bore) and **$D_i$ = root diameter** (bottom of tooth gap).
- ⭐ **Slip coefficient** $C_s = 1 - \eta_{vol}$, typically **0.05 to 0.15**.
- ⭐ **Actual discharge:** $Q_{act} = Q_{th} \times (1 - C_s) = \eta_{vol} \times Q_{th}$.
- ⭐ **Power equation:** $P = \dfrac{\rho g Q_{act} H}{1000}$ kW (when head is given) OR $P = \dfrac{p \cdot Q_{act}}{1000}$ kW (when pressure is given).
- ⭐ **Gear pumps are SELF-PRIMING** — they can suck fluid from a reservoir below the pump centerline.
- ⭐ **Used for HIGH-PRESSURE applications** — lubrication systems, hydraulic power units, fuel transfer.
- ⭐ **Cannot handle abrasive fluids** — gear teeth wear out quickly.
- ⭐ **Pulsation:** External gear pumps deliver flow with **slight pressure pulsations** at a frequency equal to (number of teeth × rpm / 60) Hz.
- ⭐ **Unit reminders:**
  - mm → m: divide by **1000**
  - rpm → rps: divide by **60**
  - L/s → m³/s: divide by **1000**
  - bar → Pa: multiply by **$10^5$**
  - kW → W: multiply by **1000**
- ⭐ **Always state assumptions** in derivation: incompressible fluid, no slip, perfect meshing.
- ⭐ **Three leakage paths** in a gear pump: (1) radial tip-to-casing, (2) axial end-plate, (3) tooth-side clearances.
- ⭐ **KTU Board Mark Distribution Tip:** A 14-mark gear pump problem usually awards 4 marks for correct formula, 2 for substitution, and 1 for final answer in each sub-part. **Always show the formula before substitution** to get full marks even if arithmetic is wrong.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vane Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vane Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Vane Pump** is a positive displacement, rotary pump belonging to the family of hydrodynamic machines. It consists of a cylindrical rotor mounted eccentrically inside a circular casing (stator). The rotor is provided with a number of radial slots, each of which carries a rectangular vane (also called a blade or sliding vane). The vanes are free to slide radially in and out of the rotor slots and are kept in contact with the inner surface of the casing by centrifugal force, fluid pressure, or in some designs by push-rods (mechanical means). As the rotor rotates, the volume between adjacent vanes, the rotor, and the casing changes cyclically, creating suction at the inlet and discharge at the outlet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A vane pump is a non-reciprocating (rotary) positive displacement pump. It is a key topic in Module 4 of PCAUT302 because it bridges the conceptual gap between pure hydrostatics and turbomachinery. Both **single-acting** and **double-acting** configurations are examinable.

The standard reference diameters used in vane pump analysis are:
- **Rotor diameter, $D_r$** — the outer diameter of the rotor (centre of vane tip when retracted).
- **Stator diameter, $D_s$** — the inner diameter of the casing.
- **Eccentricity, $e$** — the radial distance between the rotor axis and the stator axis.
- **Width of the vane, $B$ (or $W$)** — the axial length of the rotor (also called the breadth of the pump).
- **Vane tip thickness, $t$** — the radial thickness of the vane at its tip (assumed negligible in elementary analysis).
- **Number of vanes, $Z$** — the number of slots cut in the rotor.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bicycle tyre pump** lying on its side. Inside, a piston moves back and forth, drawing air in on the back-stroke and pushing it out on the forward stroke. Now replace the piston with a **rotating cylinder carrying sliding paddles** inside a slightly off-centre circular housing.

Think of it this way — picture a **Ferris wheel** at a fair, but instead of passenger cabins, the spokes are **extensible/retractable rods** (the vanes). When a vane is on the side of the wheel closer to the housing wall, it is fully extended (maximum trapped volume between itself and the next vane). As the rotor spins, the vane gradually retracts into its slot because the housing wall is moving closer to the rotor centre. This shrinking volume **squeezes the fluid out** (discharge). Conversely, on the opposite side, the vanes are being pulled out of their slots, creating **expanding volume** that sucks fluid in (suction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples of vane pumps:**
> - Automobile power steering systems (most common application).
> - Hydraulic presses and lift platforms.
> - Aircraft hydraulic systems (sketchy applications where low noise, high pressure, and compact size are critical).
> - Ink supply pumps in printing presses.
> - Lubrication oil pumps in machine tools and transmissions.

> [!TIP]
> The key intuition: **vane pump = rotating piston.** A regular piston pump uses one or two pistons moving linearly; a vane pump uses multiple vanes moving radially, achieving continuous, pulse-free flow — a huge advantage in hydraulic systems.

## 1.3 Classification of Vane Pumps

Vane pumps are broadly classified based on the manner in which the vanes are actuated and the way the suction/discharge chambers are arranged.

| Classification Basis | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Vane actuation** | Force-fed (passive) | Vanes held against casing by centrifugal force and pressure. Used for high-speed, low-viscosity fluids. |
| **Vane actuation** | Mechanically-fed (positive) | Vanes positively held against casing by cams/push-rods through slot-bottom guides. Used for low-speed, high-viscosity fluids (oils, fuels). |
| **Acting** | Single-acting | One complete pumping cycle (suction + discharge) per revolution. |
| **Acting** | Double-acting (balanced) | Two complete pumping cycles per revolution. Rotor is centred; the stator has a figure-eight or twin-circular profile. |
| **Application** | Fixed-displacement | Eccentricity $e$ is fixed. |
| **Application** | Variable-displacement | Eccentricity $e$ is varied using a movable stator ring. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse vane pumps with vane motors. A vane pump *receives mechanical energy and delivers fluid energy*; a vane motor *receives fluid energy and delivers mechanical energy (torque)*. The theory of vane motors (reversibility of turbomachines) is covered in higher semesters and is NOT in the KTU 2024 PCAUT302 syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of a Single-Acting Vane Pump

A single-acting vane pump has the following essential parts:

1. **Casing (Stator):** A circular cylindrical bore of internal diameter $D_s$.
2. **Rotor:** A solid cylindrical member of diameter $D_r$ (slightly smaller than $D_s$), supported on a shaft and driven by an external prime mover.
3. **Rotor slots:** $Z$ radial slots machined into the rotor, parallel to the shaft axis, into which the vanes are inserted.
4. **Vanes (Blades):** Rectangular sliding members, generally of wear-resistant hardened steel. Their width $B$ equals the axial length of the rotor.
5. **Suction port:** Located on the side where the volume between vanes is increasing.
6. **Delivery port:** Located on the side where the volume between vanes is decreasing, usually diametrically opposite (or 180° apart in ideal design).
7. **Inlet and outlet are separated by stationary side plates** (called the port plates or side discs) that prevent back-flow between suction and delivery.

> [!NOTE]
> In the **unbalanced (single-acting)** vane pump, the rotor axis is offset from the stator axis by an eccentricity $e$ such that $D_s = D_r + 2e$. All the pumping work is delivered to one side, which creates a net radial load on the shaft — a drawback the **balanced (double-acting)** design overcomes.

## 2.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

The operation of a single-acting vane pump is best understood by tracking the volume trapped between two adjacent vanes through one full rotation:

1. **At the inlet zone (180° arc on one side):** As the rotor turns, the trailing vane is being pulled outwards (casing wall is moving away from the rotor centre). The volume between the two vanes **increases**, creating a partial vacuum. Fluid from the reservoir is drawn in through the suction port.
2. **At the top dead centre (TDC) and bottom dead centre (BDC):** The vane is at its maximum extension (closest to the casing wall). The volume between the two vanes is at a **maximum** at the start of the suction zone, and at a **minimum** at the end of the discharge zone.
3. **At the delivery zone (180° arc on the opposite side):** The leading vane is being pushed inwards (casing wall is moving closer to the rotor centre). The volume between the two vanes **decreases**, forcing the trapped fluid out through the delivery port against the system pressure.
4. **Side plates:** The stationary side plates prevent the fluid from leaking back from the high-pressure delivery zone to the low-pressure suction zone, and define the closed chambers between the vanes and the casing.

## 2.3 Theoretical Discharge (Volume of Fluid Delivered per Revolution)

The **theoretical discharge $Q_{th}$** is the volume of fluid ideally delivered per unit time, assuming no leakage, perfect vane sealing, and no vane thickness.

### Step A: Volume of water between two consecutive vanes at any angle $\theta$

Consider two adjacent vanes that subtend an angle $\delta\theta$ at the rotor centre, where $\delta\theta = \dfrac{2\pi}{Z}$ is the angle between two consecutive vanes.

At an angular position $\theta$ measured from the point of maximum volume (line of minimum eccentricity extended), the distance from the rotor centre to the inner casing surface is:

$$r(\theta) = R_r + e \cos\theta$$

where $R_r = \dfrac{D_r}{2}$ is the rotor radius and $e$ is the eccentricity.

The volume between two vanes, the rotor, and the casing per unit axial width is the area of the "crescent" between the two arcs (the rotor circle and the casing circle) bounded by the two vanes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In most KTU derivations, the standard approximation is used: the volume between two vanes is **area of the casing arc − area of the rotor arc**, for the sector of angle $\delta\theta$.

Area of casing arc of angle $\delta\theta$:

$$A_c = \frac{1}{2} \, r^2(\theta) \cdot \delta\theta = \frac{1}{2}(R_r + e\cos\theta)^2 \, \delta\theta$$

Area of rotor arc of angle $\delta\theta$:

$$A_r = \frac{1}{2} R_r^2 \, \delta\theta$$

Volume between two vanes per unit width (neglecting vane thickness):

$$dV = (A_c - A_r) = \frac{1}{2}\left[(R_r + e\cos\theta)^2 - R_r^2\right] \delta\theta$$

Expanding:

$$dV = \frac{1}{2}\left[R_r^2 + 2R_r e\cos\theta + e^2 \cos^2\theta - R_r^2\right] \delta\theta$$

$$dV = \frac{1}{2}\left[2R_r e\cos\theta + e^2 \cos^2\theta\right] \delta\theta$$

$$dV = \left[R_r e\cos\theta + \frac{e^2 \cos^2\theta}{2}\right] \delta\theta$$

### Step B: Total volume displaced per revolution (theoretical)

Integrate $dV$ from $\theta = 0$ to $\theta = 2\pi$ (one full revolution), remembering $\delta\theta = \dfrac{2\pi}{Z}$ so the integral becomes a sum over all $Z$ vane gaps.

$$V_{th} = Z \int_{0}^{2\pi} \left[R_r e\cos\theta + \frac{e^2 \cos^2\theta}{2}\right] d\theta$$

Evaluate the two integrals separately:

$$\int_{0}^{2\pi} \cos\theta \, d\theta = 0 \quad \text{(first term vanishes)}$$

$$\int_{0}^{2\pi} \cos^2\theta \, d\theta = \pi \quad \text{(since average of } \cos^2\theta = \tfrac{1}{2} \text{ over one period)}$$

Therefore:

$$V_{th} = Z \cdot \left[0 + \frac{e^2}{2} \cdot \pi\right] \cdot B = \frac{\pi Z e^2 B}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Striking result:** The theoretical displacement per revolution is **independent of rotor diameter $D_r$**! It depends only on the number of vanes, eccentricity squared, and width. This is a classic KTU problem-solving insight. Increasing $D_r$ does not help — only $e$, $Z$, and $B$ matter.

### Step C: Theoretical discharge (flow rate)

If the rotor rotates at $N$ revolutions per second (rps), the **theoretical discharge** is:

$$Q_{th} = V_{th} \cdot N = \frac{\pi Z e^2 B N}{2} \quad \text{(volume per unit time)}$$

If $N$ is in revolutions per minute (rpm), then:

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{\pi Z e^2 B N}{60} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/s]}}$$

where all linear dimensions are in metres and $N$ is in rpm.

## 2.4 Work Done and Power Required

### Work done per second (theoretical)

The work done per second by the pump on the fluid equals the work done per second against the pressure difference between delivery and suction. If the pump lifts water from a lower reservoir to a higher reservoir (manometric head $H_m$):

$$W_{th} = \rho \, g \, Q_{th} \, H_m \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

The **manometric head $H_m$** for a vane pump is given by:

$$H_m = H_{suction} + H_{delivery} + \text{head losses in pump}$$

In most KTU problems, $H_m$ is given directly or computed from pressure data:

$$H_m = \frac{p_d - p_s}{\rho g} + \text{elevation difference}$$

### Power required (shaft power / brake power)

The **brake power** $P_B$ (also called shaft power) is the actual mechanical power input to the pump shaft. It always exceeds the theoretical water power $W_{th}$ by the mechanical and volumetric losses:

$$P_B = \frac{W_{th}}{\eta_{\text{overall}}} = \frac{\rho g Q_{th} H_m}{\eta_{\text{overall}}}$$

where:
- $\eta_{\text{mechanical}}$ accounts for friction in bearings and the vane-casing interface.
- $\eta_{\text{volumetric}}$ accounts for leakage from delivery back to suction (slip).

## 2.5 Slip, Coefficient of Discharge, and Efficiencies

### Slip ($S$)

In real vane pumps, fluid leaks from the high-pressure delivery side back to the low-pressure suction side through the small clearances between the vane tips and the casing. This lost volume per revolution is called the **slip**:

$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{\text{actual}} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/s]}$$

where $Q_{\text{actual}}$ is the **actual discharge** measured at the pump outlet.

**Note:** Slip depends primarily on the delivery pressure, the vane tip clearance, and the fluid viscosity. Slip decreases (actual discharge increases) as delivery pressure decreases, and vice versa.

### Coefficient of discharge ($C_d$)

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{th}}$$

So:

$$Q_{\text{actual}} = C_d \cdot Q_{th}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A typical value of $C_d$ for a well-designed vane pump is in the range **0.85 to 0.95**. Lower values indicate excessive slip.

### Volumetric efficiency ($\eta_{\text{vol}}$)

$$\eta_{\text{vol}} = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{th}} = C_d$$

### Mechanical efficiency ($\eta_{\text{mech}}$)

$$\eta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{W_{th}}{P_B} = \frac{\rho g Q_{th} H_m}{P_B}$$

### Overall efficiency ($\eta_{\text{overall}}$)

$$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{vol}} \cdot \eta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{\rho g Q_{\text{actual}} H_m}{P_B}$$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical volume per revolution | $V_{th} = \dfrac{\pi Z e^2 B}{2}$ | m³ | Independent of $D_r$ |
| 2 | Theoretical discharge | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{\pi Z e^2 B N}{60}$ | m³/s | $N$ in rpm |
| 3 | Theoretical discharge (rps form) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{\pi Z e^2 B N}{2}$ | m³/s | $N$ in rps |
| 4 | Slip | $S = Q_{th} - Q_{\text{actual}}$ | m³/s | Pressure-dependent |
| 5 | Coefficient of discharge | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{th}}$ | dimensionless | Typically 0.85 – 0.95 |
| 6 | Actual discharge | $Q_{\text{actual}} = C_d \cdot Q_{th}$ | m³/s | Includes leakage |
| 7 | Theoretical water power | $W_{th} = \rho g Q_{th} H_m$ | W | Theoretical only |
| 8 | Shaft / Brake power | $P_B = \dfrac{\rho g Q_{th} H_m}{\eta_{\text{overall}}}$ | W | Required input |
| 9 | Manometric head | $H_m = \dfrac{p_d - p_s}{\rho g} + \Delta z$ | m | For given installation |
| 10 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_{\text{vol}} = C_d$ | – | Equals $C_d$ by definition |
| 11 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{\text{mech}} = \dfrac{W_{th}}{P_B}$ | – | Friction losses included |
| 12 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{vol}} \cdot \eta_{\text{mech}}$ | – | Product of the two |
| 13 | Vane tip speed | $u = \dfrac{\pi D_r N}{60}$ | m/s | Speed of rotor surface |
| 14 | Stator diameter relation | $D_s = D_r + 2e$ | m | Geometry constraint |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Trap (KTU 2024 Examiner's Note):** When $N$ is in **rpm**, the discharge formula has a **/60** factor. When $N$ is in **rps** (revolutions per second, i.e. $N/60$ from rpm), the formula is $\dfrac{\pi Z e^2 B N}{2}$. **Mixing rpm and rps is the #1 source of arithmetic errors in vane pump numericals.**

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Variable-displacement vane pumps are the heart of power steering systems in most cars. As engine RPM changes, the swash-plate (or movable ring) mechanism adjusts $e$ automatically, ensuring constant flow despite speed variation — a classic KTU application question.
- **Aerospace:** Aircraft hydraulic systems (Boeing 737, Airbus A320) use vane pumps for their high-pressure capability (up to 3000 psi) and quiet operation.
- **Manufacturing:** Hydraulic presses for forging, injection moulding, and metal forming all use vane pumps for their high-speed, smooth, pulse-free flow.
- **Lubrication systems:** Machine tool spindles and gearboxes use small vane pumps to circulate oil under pressure.
- **Fuel transfer:** Truck and aircraft refuelling systems use vane pumps for their self-priming, dry-running capability.

> [!TIP]
> Vane pumps are also called **"sliding vane pumps"** in industrial literature. The terms are interchangeable in KTU answers.
