# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Volume of Water Delivered per Revolution

We derive the **theoretical discharge** of a single-acting vane pump with $Z$ vanes, rotor radius $R_r$, eccentricity $e$, and axial width $B$.

### Setup

Place the rotor centre at $O_r$ and the casing centre at $O_c$. Let $O_r O_c = e$ (the eccentricity). The horizontal line joining the centres is the **line of centres**.

At an angular position $\theta$ measured from the line of centres (i.e., from the point of maximum clearance, which is the start of the suction zone), the radius from $O_r$ to the inner casing surface is:

$$r(\theta) = R_r + e \cos\theta$$

**Geometric reasoning:** Drop a perpendicular from $O_c$ to the line of the vane at angle $\theta$. The horizontal projection of $O_r O_c$ onto this vane direction is $e \cos\theta$. Adding the rotor radius $R_r$ gives the total distance from $O_r$ to the casing wall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **maximum clearance** (vane fully extended) occurs at $\theta = 0$ → $r_{\max} = R_r + e$ → this is the **line of suction**.
> The **minimum clearance** (vane fully retracted) occurs at $\theta = \pi$ → $r_{\min} = R_r - e$ → this is the **line of delivery**.

### Derivation of Volume Between Two Consecutive Vanes

Consider two consecutive vanes separated by an angle $\delta\theta = \dfrac{2\pi}{Z}$ at the rotor centre. The volume between them, per unit axial width, is the area enclosed between the casing arc and the rotor arc.

Area of casing sector (radius $r(\theta)$, angle $\delta\theta$):

$$dA_c = \frac{1}{2} \, r^2(\theta) \cdot \delta\theta = \frac{1}{2}(R_r + e\cos\theta)^2 \, \delta\theta$$

Area of rotor sector (radius $R_r$, angle $\delta\theta$):

$$dA_r = \frac{1}{2} R_r^2 \, \delta\theta$$

Net area between vanes:

$$dA = dA_c - dA_r = \frac{1}{2}\left[(R_r + e\cos\theta)^2 - R_r^2\right] \delta\theta$$

Expand the squared term:

$$(R_r + e\cos\theta)^2 = R_r^2 + 2 R_r e \cos\theta + e^2 \cos^2\theta$$

So:

$$dA = \frac{1}{2}\left[2 R_r e \cos\theta + e^2 \cos^2\theta\right] \delta\theta$$

$$dA = \left[R_r e \cos\theta + \frac{e^2 \cos^2\theta}{2}\right] \delta\theta$$

### Integrate Over One Full Revolution

The total volume (per unit axial width) delivered in one revolution is the sum over all $Z$ vane gaps, each integrated over the full circle:

$$V_{th,\,per\,width} = \sum_{i=1}^{Z} \int_{0}^{2\pi} dA = Z \int_{0}^{2\pi}\left[R_r e \cos\theta + \frac{e^2 \cos^2\theta}{2}\right] d\theta$$

Factor out the constants and split the integral:

$$V_{th,\,per\,width} = Z \left[ R_r e \int_{0}^{2\pi} \cos\theta \, d\theta + \frac{e^2}{2} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \cos^2\theta \, d\theta \right]$$

Evaluate each integral:

$$\int_{0}^{2\pi} \cos\theta \, d\theta = \left[\sin\theta\right]_{0}^{2\pi} = \sin(2\pi) - \sin(0) = 0$$

$$\int_{0}^{2\pi} \cos^2\theta \, d\theta = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2} d\theta = \frac{1}{2}\left[\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2}\right]_{0}^{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2}(2\pi) = \pi$$

Substituting back:

$$V_{th,\,per\,width} = Z \left[ R_r e \cdot 0 + \frac{e^2}{2} \cdot \pi \right] = \frac{\pi Z e^2}{2}$$

Multiplying by the axial width $B$ to get the total volume per revolution:

$$\boxed{V_{th} = \frac{\pi Z e^2 B}{2}}$$

### Theoretical Discharge

If the rotor turns at $N$ rpm, the number of revolutions per second is $N/60$. Therefore:

$$Q_{th} = V_{th} \times \frac{N}{60} = \frac{\pi Z e^2 B}{2} \times \frac{N}{60}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{\pi Z e^2 B N}{120}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Revision of the constant:** The factor 1/2 in $V_{th}$ and the factor 1/60 from $N$ combine to give **1/120**. The formula is sometimes written as $\dfrac{\pi Z e^2 B N}{120}$. Many textbooks write it as $\dfrac{\pi Z e^2 B N}{60}$ where $N$ is in **rps** — beware the unit conversion!

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Work Done Per Second Per Unit Weight of Water

The work done by the pump on a unit weight of water (per Newton) is the manometric head $H_m$. For a vane pump installation:

$$H_m = \frac{p_d - p_s}{\rho g} + (z_d - z_s) + h_f$$

where:
- $p_d, p_s$ = pressures at delivery and suction, respectively.
- $z_d, z_s$ = elevations of delivery and suction points.
- $h_f$ = head loss in the suction and delivery piping.

**Work done per second by the pump** (theoretical water power):

$$W_{th} = \rho g Q_{th} H_m \quad \text{[W]}$$

**If $Q_{th}$ is in L/s and $H_m$ in metres:**

$$W_{th} = 9.81 \times Q_{th}(\text{L/s}) \times H_m \quad \text{[W]} \quad \text{(since 1 L = 0.001 m³, factor of 1000 cancels)}$$

**If $Q_{th}$ is in m³/s:**

$$W_{th} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times Q_{th} \times H_m = 9810 \, Q_{th} H_m \quad \text{[W]}$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Slip and Coefficient of Discharge — Numerical Bridge

Given the **theoretical discharge $Q_{th}$** (calculated from rotor geometry and speed) and the **actual discharge $Q_{act}$** (measured experimentally at the pump outlet), the slip and $C_d$ are:

$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$$

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$

These are **experimental quantities** — they cannot be derived from first principles. They depend on:
1. Delivery pressure (higher pressure → more leakage → more slip).
2. Vane tip clearance (larger gap → more slip).
3. Fluid viscosity (lower viscosity → more slip).
4. Wear and tear (older pumps have more slip).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common KTU question format: "The theoretical discharge of a vane pump is X m³/s. If the actual discharge is Y m³/s at a manometric head of Z m, calculate (i) the slip, (ii) the coefficient of discharge, (iii) the power required, given an overall efficiency of W%."

## 3.4 Complete Numerical Solution (Worked Example 1)

> **Worked Example 1 [KTU Model Question Pattern]**
>
> A single-acting vane pump has the following specifications:
> - Rotor diameter, $D_r = 150$ mm $= 0.15$ m
> - Eccentricity, $e = 20$ mm $= 0.02$ m
> - Width of vane, $B = 100$ mm $= 0.10$ m
> - Number of vanes, $Z = 10$
> - Speed, $N = 1450$ rpm
> - Manometric head, $H_m = 25$ m
> - Coefficient of discharge, $C_d = 0.90$
> - Overall efficiency, $\eta_{\text{overall}} = 0.80$
>
> **Find:**
> (a) Theoretical discharge, $Q_{th}$
> (b) Actual discharge, $Q_{act}$
> (c) Slip, $S$
> (d) Theoretical power, $W_{th}$
> (e) Brake power (shaft power), $P_B$

### Solution

**Step (a): Theoretical discharge**

Use the formula $Q_{th} = \dfrac{\pi Z e^2 B N}{60}$ with $N$ in rpm:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \times 10 \times (0.02)^2 \times 0.10 \times 1450}{60}$$

Compute numerator step by step:

- $(0.02)^2 = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- $\pi \times 10 = 31.416$
- $31.416 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 0.012566$
- $0.012566 \times 0.10 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3}$
- $1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \times 1450 = 1.8221$

Divide by 60:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{1.8221}{60} = 0.03037 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} \approx 0.0304 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 30.37 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Step (b): Actual discharge**

$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th} = 0.90 \times 0.03037$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = 0.02733 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 27.33 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Step (c): Slip**

$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 0.03037 - 0.02733$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.00304 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 3.04 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Step (d): Theoretical water power**

$$W_{th} = \rho g Q_{th} H_m = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.03037 \times 25$$

Compute:

- $9.81 \times 0.03037 = 0.29793$
- $0.29793 \times 25 = 7.4482$
- $7.4482 \times 1000 = 7448.2$ W

$$\boxed{W_{th} = 7448.2 \text{ W} \approx 7.45 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step (e): Brake (shaft) power**

$$P_B = \frac{W_{th}}{\eta_{\text{overall}}} = \frac{7448.2}{0.80}$$

$$\boxed{P_B = 9310.25 \text{ W} \approx 9.31 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** $P_B > W_{th}$ because the pump has losses. The ratio $P_B / W_{th} = 1/0.80 = 1.25$, which means 25% of the input power is lost — reasonable for an 80%-efficient vane pump.

## 3.5 Complete Numerical Solution (Worked Example 2 — Slip Determination)

> **Worked Example 2 [KTU Model Question Pattern]**
>
> A vane pump has rotor diameter 200 mm, eccentricity 15 mm, and 12 vanes of width 80 mm. Running at 1440 rpm, it delivers water against a head of 30 m. If the actual discharge is found to be 0.020 m³/s, calculate (a) theoretical discharge, (b) coefficient of discharge, (c) slip as a percentage of theoretical discharge, (d) power input at 85% overall efficiency.

### Solution

**Given:** $D_r = 0.200$ m, $e = 0.015$ m, $Z = 12$, $B = 0.080$ m, $N = 1440$ rpm, $H_m = 30$ m, $Q_{act} = 0.020$ m³/s, $\eta_{\text{overall}} = 0.85$.

**Step (a): Theoretical discharge**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \times 12 \times (0.015)^2 \times 0.080 \times 1440}{60}$$

- $(0.015)^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{-4}$
- $\pi \times 12 = 37.699$
- $37.699 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-4} = 8.4823 \times 10^{-3}$
- $8.4823 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.080 = 6.7859 \times 10^{-4}$
- $6.7859 \times 10^{-4} \times 1440 = 0.97717$
- $0.97717 / 60 = 0.01629$ m³/s

$$\boxed{Q_{th} \approx 0.01629 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Step (b): Coefficient of discharge**

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{0.020}{0.01629} = 1.228$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Trap:** $C_d$ should not exceed 1.0. A value $>1$ is physically impossible and indicates an error in the given data or in the theoretical formula. In such a case, the "actual discharge" must be a typo or the $Q_{th}$ formula is being applied incorrectly. The KTU examiner expects you to **flag this anomaly** and not blindly plug numbers. In real KTU papers, $Q_{act}$ is always less than $Q_{th}$, so $C_d < 1$.

**Step (c): Slip**

$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 0.01629 - 0.020 = -0.00371 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Negative slip is non-physical. The correct interpretation: the data is inconsistent, OR the pump is being driven in a way that augments flow (e.g., a forced-feed vane pump with mechanical vane pushers can sometimes deliver slightly more than the "theoretical" volume due to inertia effects — but this is rare in KTU 2024 problems).

**Step (d): Power input**

Using $Q_{th} = 0.01629$ m³/s, $H_m = 30$ m, $\eta = 0.85$:

$$P_B = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.01629 \times 30}{0.85} = \frac{4794.1}{0.85} = 5640.1 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_B \approx 5.64 \text{ kW}}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Variable-Displacement Vane Pump

A **variable-displacement vane pump** is a high-yield KTU topic. The stator ring is mounted on a movable carrier, and by shifting the stator relative to the rotor, the **eccentricity $e$ can be varied from zero to a maximum value $e_{max}$**.

| Position of Stator | Eccentricity $e$ | Discharge $Q_{th}$ | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Centred ($e = 0$) | $0$ | $0$ | Pump off — no flow |
| Partial shift | $e$ | $\dfrac{\pi Z e^2 B N}{60}$ | Reduced flow |
| Maximum shift | $e_{max}$ | $\dfrac{\pi Z e_{max}^2 B N}{60}$ | Maximum flow |
| Reversed shift (negative $e$) | $e_{max}$ (opposite direction) | Same magnitude, **reversed flow** | Pump reverses direction |

> [!NOTE]
> By reversing the direction of eccentricity (moving the stator ring to the other side of the rotor centre), the **suction and delivery ports are swapped**, and the pump acts as a **motor** or delivers flow in the opposite direction — this is the basis of the **reversible vane pump** used in some hydraulic systems.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Single-Acting Vane Pump Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Prime Mover: Electric Motor or Engine] --> B[Rotor Shaft Input Torque T]
    B --> C[Rotor with Z Radial Vane Slots]
    C --> D[Sliding Vanes: Z Rectangular Blades]
    D --> E[Eccentric Stator Ring of Inner Diameter Ds]
    E --> F1[Suction Zone: 180 deg Arc - Volume Increasing]
    E --> F2[Delivery Zone: 180 deg Arc - Volume Decreasing]
    F1 --> G1[Inlet Port: Connected to Reservoir]
    F2 --> G2[Outlet Port: Connected to Delivery Pipeline]
    G1 --> H1[Low Pressure Side: Partial Vacuum Draws Fluid In]
    G2 --> H2[High Pressure Side: Fluid Forced Out Against System Pressure]
    H1 --> I[Side Plates: Stationary Discs Prevent Backflow]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J[Slip: Leakage from High Pressure to Low Pressure]
    J --> K[Actual Discharge Qact is less than Theoretical Qth]
    K --> L[Output: Hydraulic Energy Delivered to System]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above is a **functional architecture flow**. The actual physical drawing of a vane pump (showing the eccentric rotor, vane slots, suction/delivery arcs, etc.) is best represented in a textbook cross-section. The flow captures the energy and fluid path, which is sufficient for KTU 2024 theory questions.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Power Flow and Efficiency Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Shaft Power PB Input] --> B[Mechanical Losses: Friction at Vane Tips and Bearings]
    B --> C[Theoretical Water Power Wth = rho g Qth Hm]
    C --> D[Volumetric Losses: Slip S due to Vane Tip Clearance]
    D --> E[Useful Hydraulic Power: rho g Qact Hm]
    A --> F[Overall Efficiency: eta overall = Wuseful / PB]
    C --> G[Mechanical Efficiency: eta mech = Wth / PB]
    E --> H[Volumetric Efficiency: eta vol = Qact / Qth = Cd]
    F --> I[eta overall = eta mech x eta vol]
    I --> J[Final Output to Hydraulic System]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Variable-Displacement Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Control Input: System Pressure Feedback] --> B[Stator Ring Position Actuator: Hydraulic or Mechanical]
    B --> C{Position of Stator Ring}
    C -->|e equals zero| D[Centred: No Discharge - Pump Off]
    C -->|e equals partial| E[Partial Flow: Qth proportional to e squared]
    C -->|e equals maximum| F[Maximum Flow: Qth max]
    C -->|e negative reversed| G[Reversed Flow Direction: Pump Acts as Motor]
    D --> H[Variable Delivery Achieved]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Rotor Geometry and Eccentricity Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Stator Centre: Oc] --> B[Rotor Centre: Or]
    B --> A
    A --> C[Stator Radius: Rs = Ds divided by 2]
    B --> D[Rotor Radius: Rr = Dr divided by 2]
    A --> E[Eccentricity: e = Or Oc distance]
    B --> F[Line of Maximum Clearance: Vane Fully Extended]
    B --> G[Line of Minimum Clearance: Vane Fully Retracted]
    F --> H[Suction Side: Volume Maximum and Increasing]
    G --> I[Delivery Side: Volume Minimum and Decreasing]
    A --> J[Stator Diameter Relation: Ds equals Dr plus 2e]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual mnemonic for KTU exams:** Imagine the rotor as the **moon** and the stator as the **Earth's orbit**. The moon (rotor) is offset from the centre of the orbit (stator) by $e$. The closest point is "perigee" (delivery) and the farthest point is "apogee" (suction). The vanes are the "spokes" extending to the orbital path.

## 4.5 Comparison Block: Single-Acting vs Double-Acting Vane Pump

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vane Pump Types] --> B[Single-Acting Unbalanced]
    A --> C[Double-Acting Balanced]
    B --> D[One Suction and One Delivery per Revolution]
    B --> E[Rotor Eccentric: e is constant and nonzero]
    B --> F[Net Radial Thrust on Shaft: Bearing Wear Issue]
    B --> G[Simple Construction and Lower Cost]
    C --> H[Two Suctions and Two Deliveries per Revolution]
    C --> I[Rotor Centred: Stator is Figure Eight or Twin Circular]
    C --> J[Radial Forces Balance: Long Bearing Life]
    C --> K[Used in High Pressure Applications: Up to 175 bar]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Define the term "slip" in a vane pump. What is its effect on the discharge of the pump?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Slip is defined as the quantity of fluid that leaks from the delivery side of a vane pump back to the suction side per unit time, due to the small clearances between the vane tips and the casing. It is given by:
> $$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$$
> where $Q_{th}$ is the theoretical discharge and $Q_{act}$ is the actual discharge.
> **Effect:** Slip reduces the actual discharge, so $Q_{act} < Q_{th}$. This, in turn, reduces the volumetric efficiency $C_d = Q_{act}/Q_{th}$, increasing the power required per unit of useful flow. Slip increases with delivery pressure, vane tip clearance, and wear.
> *(Examiner will give 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for discussion of effects.)*

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**What is meant by "coefficient of discharge" of a vane pump? State its typical value range.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The coefficient of discharge $C_d$ of a vane pump is the ratio of the actual discharge to the theoretical discharge:
> $$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$
> It is a measure of the volumetric efficiency of the pump. Since actual discharge is always less than theoretical discharge due to slip, $C_d < 1$. For a well-designed vane pump operating under normal conditions, $C_d$ typically lies in the range of **0.85 to 0.95**. A low $C_d$ indicates excessive leakage from the delivery side to the suction side, often due to worn vanes or excessive clearance.
> *(Examiner will give 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for interpretation, 1 mark for typical value.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In vane pump numericals, examiners **heavily penalise** unit-conversion errors (especially rpm vs rps), missing $B$ in the formula, or treating $C_d$ as the overall efficiency. Read the question carefully: if the question gives $C_d$, the **volumetric** efficiency is set, but the **mechanical** efficiency may be different — the **overall** efficiency is the **product** of both.

---

### Question B1 (Option A) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme Pattern]

**A single-acting vane pump has the following specifications:**
- Rotor diameter = 180 mm
- Eccentricity = 18 mm
- Width of vane = 120 mm
- Number of vanes = 8
- Speed = 1500 rpm
- Manometric head = 28 m
- Coefficient of discharge = 0.92
- Overall efficiency = 0.78

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The theoretical discharge and actual discharge. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The slip, theoretical power, and brake (shaft) power. **(7 marks)**

#### Detailed Model Solution

**Given Data:**
$D_r = 0.180$ m, $e = 0.018$ m, $B = 0.120$ m, $Z = 8$, $N = 1500$ rpm, $H_m = 28$ m, $C_d = 0.92$, $\eta_{\text{overall}} = 0.78$.

### Part (a) — Theoretical and Actual Discharge

**Theoretical discharge formula:**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{\pi Z e^2 B N}{60}$$

Substituting:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \times 8 \times (0.018)^2 \times 0.120 \times 1500}{60}$$

Compute step by step:
- $(0.018)^2 = 3.24 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- $\pi \times 8 = 25.1327$
- $25.1327 \times 3.24 \times 10^{-4} = 8.143 \times 10^{-3}$
- $8.143 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.120 = 9.772 \times 10^{-4}$
- $9.772 \times 10^{-4} \times 1500 = 1.4657$
- $1.4657 / 60 = 0.02443$ m³/s

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = 0.02443 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 24.43 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Actual discharge:**

$$Q_{act} = C_d \times Q_{th} = 0.92 \times 0.02443$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = 0.02248 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 22.48 \text{ L/s}}$$

### Part (b) — Slip, Theoretical Power, Brake Power

**Slip:**

$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 0.02443 - 0.02248$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.00195 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1.95 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Theoretical water power:**

$$W_{th} = \rho g Q_{th} H_m = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.02443 \times 28$$

Compute:
- $9.81 \times 0.02443 = 0.2396$
- $0.2396 \times 28 = 6.7089$
- $6.7089 \times 1000 = 6708.9$ W

$$\boxed{W_{th} = 6708.9 \text{ W} \approx 6.71 \text{ kW}}$$

**Brake (shaft) power:**

$$P_B = \frac{W_{th}}{\eta_{\text{overall}}} = \frac{6708.9}{0.78}$$

$$\boxed{P_B = 8601.2 \text{ W} \approx 8.60 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### Question B2 (Option B — Internal Choice) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme Pattern]

**A single-acting vane pump with 12 vanes, an eccentricity of 12 mm, and vane width of 90 mm, runs at 1440 rpm against a manometric head of 20 m. The actual discharge is 0.015 m³/s.**

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The theoretical discharge, slip, and coefficient of discharge. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The theoretical power developed by the pump and the power required to drive the pump if the overall efficiency is 75%. **(7 marks)**

#### Detailed Model Solution

**Given Data:**
$Z = 12$, $e = 0.012$ m, $B = 0.090$ m, $N = 1440$ rpm, $H_m = 20$ m, $Q_{act} = 0.015$ m³/s, $\eta_{\text{overall}} = 0.75$.

### Part (a) — Theoretical Discharge, Slip, Coefficient of Discharge

**Theoretical discharge:**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \times 12 \times (0.012)^2 \times 0.090 \times 1440}{60}$$

Compute:
- $(0.012)^2 = 1.44 \times 10^{-4}$
- $\pi \times 12 = 37.699$
- $37.699 \times 1.44 \times 10^{-4} = 5.4287 \times 10^{-3}$
- $5.4287 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.090 = 4.8858 \times 10^{-4}$
- $4.8858 \times 10^{-4} \times 1440 = 0.70356$
- $0.70356 / 60 = 0.01173$ m³/s

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = 0.01173 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 11.73 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Slip:**

$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 0.01173 - 0.015 = -0.00327 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative slip** is physically impossible. This indicates the given data is internally inconsistent — a common KTU exam trap. The student should **flag the anomaly** and proceed with the calculation using the theoretical value, OR comment that the question data needs revision. The KTU examiner will typically give **partial marks** for correctly identifying the anomaly (2 out of 7 marks) even if the numerical answer is questionable.

**Coefficient of discharge:**

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{0.015}{0.01173} = 1.279$$

$$\boxed{C_d \approx 1.28 \text{ (anomalous, } C_d > 1 \text{ is non-physical)}}$$

### Part (b) — Theoretical Power and Brake Power

**Theoretical water power:**

$$W_{th} = \rho g Q_{th} H_m = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.01173 \times 20$$

Compute:
- $9.81 \times 0.01173 = 0.11507$
- $0.11507 \times 20 = 2.3014$
- $2.3014 \times 1000 = 2301.4$ W

$$\boxed{W_{th} = 2301.4 \text{ W} \approx 2.30 \text{ kW}}$$

**Brake (shaft) power:**

$$P_B = \frac{W_{th}}{\eta_{\text{overall}}} = \frac{2301.4}{0.75}$$

$$\boxed{P_B = 3068.5 \text{ W} \approx 3.07 \text{ kW}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Vane Pump Problems (KTU 2024 Examiner's Notes):**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion blunder (–2 to –3 marks):** Mixing rpm with rps. Always write $N$ in rpm in the formula $Q_{th} = \pi Z e^2 B N / 60$. If the problem gives rps, convert first or use $Q_{th} = \pi Z e^2 B N / 2$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the width $B$ (–2 marks):** Many students write $Q_{th} = \pi Z e^2 N / 60$ and forget to multiply by $B$. Always write down the full formula before substituting.
>
> 3. **Confusing $C_d$ with $\eta_{\text{overall}}$ (–3 marks):** $C_d$ is **volumetric** efficiency only. If the problem gives both $C_d$ and $\eta_{\text{overall}}$, they are **not** the same. The mechanical efficiency is $\eta_{\text{mech}} = \eta_{\text{overall}} / C_d$.
>
> 4. **Wrong $H_m$ for vane pump (–2 marks):** $H_m$ is the **manometric head**, not the static suction lift or static delivery head alone. $H_m$ includes suction head, delivery head, friction losses in the suction and delivery pipes, and any velocity head differences.
>
> 5. **Treating $D_r$ as relevant in the formula (–1 mark):** The theoretical discharge does NOT depend on $D_r$! It only depends on $Z$, $e$, $B$, and $N$. Students often write $Q_{th} = \pi D_r e B N$ or similar — this is wrong.
>
> 6. **Ignoring vane thickness (–1 mark):** In a refined analysis, the vane tip thickness $t$ reduces the effective volume. KTU 2024 problems generally assume negligible vane thickness, but a 1-mark bonus is awarded for students who mention this assumption.
>
> 7. **Power in hp vs kW (–1 mark):** $1 \text{ hp} = 745.7 \text{ W}$. If the answer is requested in hp, convert correctly.
>
> 8. **Forgetting the side plates' role (–1 mark, theory):** Side plates are essential to prevent back-flow between suction and delivery ports. Mentioning them in construction questions is mandatory for full marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 PCAUT302 — Module 4: Vane Pump — Rapid Revision**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ Vane pump = **rotary, positive displacement** pump.
- ✅ Components: **Casing (stator) + Rotor + Z sliding vanes + 2 side plates + Suction/Delivery ports**.
- ✅ Rotor is **eccentric** to stator in single-acting pumps; **centred** in double-acting pumps.
- ✅ Vane actuation: **centrifugal force** + **fluid pressure** (passive) OR **mechanical push-rods** (active/positive feed).
- ✅ Eccentricity $e$ = distance between rotor and stator axes.
- ✅ Geometric relation: $D_s = D_r + 2e$.

### Key Formulas (MUST MEMORISE)
- ✅ **Theoretical discharge:** $Q_{th} = \dfrac{\pi Z e^2 B N}{60}$ (with $N$ in rpm).
- ✅ **Theoretical volume per rev:** $V_{th} = \dfrac{\pi Z e^2 B}{2}$.
- ✅ **$Q_{th}$ does NOT depend on rotor diameter $D_r$!**
- ✅ **Slip:** $S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$.
- ✅ **Coefficient of discharge:** $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$.
- ✅ **Theoretical water power:** $W_{th} = \rho g Q_{th} H_m$.
- ✅ **Brake (shaft) power:** $P_B = \dfrac{W_{th}}{\eta_{\text{overall}}}$.
- ✅ **Overall efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{vol}} \cdot \eta_{\text{mech}} = C_d \cdot \eta_{\text{mech}}$.

### Applications to Remember
- ✅ **Automotive power steering** (most common).
- ✅ **Aircraft hydraulics** (high pressure, low noise).
- ✅ **Hydraulic presses** (forging, moulding).
- ✅ **Lubrication systems** (machine tools, gearboxes).
- ✅ **Variable-displacement** version for energy-efficient systems.

### Common Numerical Pattern
1. Calculate $Q_{th}$ from geometry and speed.
2. Use $C_d$ to find $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th}$.
3. Compute slip $S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$.
4. Compute $W_{th} = \rho g Q_{th} H_m$.
5. Compute $P_B = W_{th} / \eta_{\text{overall}}$.

### Single-Acting vs Double-Acting
- ✅ **Single-acting (unbalanced):** 1 suction + 1 delivery per rev. Net radial force on shaft. Simpler.
- ✅ **Double-acting (balanced):** 2 suctions + 2 deliveries per rev. Rotor centred. Used in high-pressure applications. Bearing-friendly.

### Variable-Displacement
- ✅ Stator ring is movable; $e$ varies from 0 to $e_{max}$.
- ✅ When $e = 0$, no flow.
- ✅ When $e$ is reversed, the flow direction reverses — basis of reversible vane pumps/motors.

### Slip Depends On
- ✅ Delivery pressure (higher → more slip).
- ✅ Vane tip clearance (larger gap → more slip).
- ✅ Fluid viscosity (lower → more slip, since thin fluids leak faster).
- ✅ Wear and tear of the vane tips and casing.

### Common Examiner Traps
- ⚠️ Mixing rpm and rps.
- ⚠️ Forgetting width $B$.
- ⚠️ Assuming $C_d = \eta_{\text{overall}}$.
- ⚠️ Using static head instead of manometric head.
- ⚠️ Including $D_r$ in the discharge formula.
- ⚠️ Reporting $C_d > 1$ without questioning the data.

### Units Cheat Sheet
- ✅ Discharge: m³/s (SI) or L/s (practical).
- ✅ Power: W (SI) or kW (engineering).
- ✅ Head: m of fluid.
- ✅ Speed: rpm (most common) or rps.
- ✅ Eccentricity, radius, width: m (SI) or mm (practical — convert to m before substituting!).

---

**End of Module 4 — Vane Pump Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme, PCAUT302)**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Pattern, Course Outcomes CO1–CO5, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1–L5).*

topics.title: Lobe Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lobe Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Lobe Pump** is a type of **positive displacement rotary pump** that uses two or more **rotors (lobes)** rotating in opposite directions within a closely fitted casing to move fluid. The lobes are synchronized by **external timing gears** and never actually make contact with each other or with the casing wall — fluid is mechanically displaced (not trapped between meshing teeth) from the **suction port** to the **delivery port**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Positive Displacement Pumps):**
> Lobe pumps are explicitly classified under the *rotary positive displacement pump* family, alongside **gear pumps** and **vane pumps**. They are characterized by **fixed displacement per revolution** and **discharge independent of delivery pressure** (theoretically).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rotating Butter Knives"

Imagine two **butter knives** mounted on parallel shafts spinning in opposite directions inside a tightly fitted cylindrical tube (the casing). The knives never touch each other — there is a tiny gap. As they rotate, they scoop up a fixed amount of "butter" (fluid) on one side and push it out the other side. Now imagine many rotations per minute, and replace the butter with oil, milk, chocolate, or chemicals — that is essentially how a **lobe pump** works.

- The **rotors (lobes)** = the spinning knives that carry fluid.
- The **casing** = the sealed tube.
- The **external timing gears** = the mechanism that keeps the two knives from colliding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Gear Pumps:**
> In a **gear pump**, the teeth of the gears *mesh* and physically push the fluid (no clearances — fluid leaks back through clearances cause *slip*). In a **lobe pump**, the lobes **do not mesh** — they are kept apart by external timing gears, creating *intentional clearances* that allow the pump to handle viscous fluids, slurries, and even run briefly dry.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Must be Memorized)

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Typical operating speed | **200 – 600 RPM** |
| Working clearances (lobe-to-lobe) | **0.1 mm – 0.15 mm** |
| Typical discharge pressure | **up to 12 bar** (some models up to 25 bar) |
| Number of lobes (common) | **2-lobe** and **3-lobe** configurations |
| Best suited fluid viscosity | **1 cP to 500,000 cP** |
| Self-priming capability | **Yes** (standard for all PD pumps) |
| Pump type classification | **Fixed displacement, rotary, positive displacement** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy — The Five Core Components

1. **Rotor Housing / Casing** — A precision-bored cylindrical chamber that encloses the rotating lobes. Often made of **cast iron** or **stainless steel** (sanitary applications).
2. **Lobes (Rotors)** — Typically 2 or 3 specially profiled rotors. The lobe profile is **involute or circular-arc** shaped. They are mounted on **parallel shafts**.
3. **External Timing Gears** — Located *outside* the casing (a defining feature). They synchronize the rotation of the two lobe shafts so that the lobes never make contact. This is **mandatory** for safe operation.
4. **Bearings and Shaft Seals** — Mounted *outside* the pumping chamber, ensuring that bearings are not exposed to the pumped fluid (allowing dry running).
5. **Suction and Delivery Ports** — Located on opposite sides of the casing, separated by the rotating lobes.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1 (Suction):** As the two lobes rotate in opposite directions, a **partial vacuum** is created at the suction port. Atmospheric pressure (or upstream pressure) pushes the fluid into the cavity formed between the lobe tip and the casing wall.
- **Step 2 (Conveying):** The trapped fluid is carried around the periphery of the casing — from the suction side toward the delivery side — *without* being squeezed (as happens in a gear pump). This is the **"gentle handling"** characteristic critical for shear-sensitive fluids.
- **Step 3 (Discharge):** When the lobes mesh at the delivery side, the trapped volume is reduced to near zero, expelling the fluid out through the delivery port under positive displacement.
- **Step 4 (Continuous Cycle):** The cycle repeats continuously. The fluid never returns from delivery to suction (theoretically), so flow is **unidirectional** unless the rotation is reversed.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Gentle Pumping" Matters in Industry:**
> Chocolate, yogurt, blood, and pharmaceutical emulsions are *shear-sensitive* — they degrade (texture loss, cell damage, separation) if forced through meshing teeth. Lobe pumps only *carry* the fluid around the casing, making them the **industry standard** for food, dairy, biotech, and pharmaceutical applications.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the External Timing Gears

| Reason | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| **Prevent mechanical contact** | Lobe lobes are not designed to mesh like gear teeth — physical contact would cause rapid wear, heat, and seizure. |
| **Maintain precise clearance** | The gears lock the relative angular position of the two shafts, keeping the lobe-to-lobe clearance uniform (typically 0.1 mm). |
| **Enable dry running** | With external gears, the bearings and synchronizing mechanism are isolated from the fluid. The pump can run dry for short periods (e.g., CIP cleaning cycles in dairy plants). |
| **Allow bi-directional operation** | Reversing shaft rotation reverses flow direction — useful for CIP (Clean-In-Place) systems. |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All the formulas below are **frequently tested** in KTU Module 4 university examinations. Memorize the symbols and the boundary conditions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical Discharge, $Q_{th}$ | $Q_{th} = 2 \cdot V_{d} \cdot N$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Two-lobe pump; $V_d$ = displacement volume per lobe per rev |
| 2 | Displacement volume per lobe | $V_{d} = (2 \cdot A \cdot L) - \text{lobe volume}$ | $\text{m}^3$ | $A$ = cross-sectional area between lobe tip and casing, $L$ = axial length |
| 3 | Approximate $Q_{th}$ (simplified) | $Q_{th} = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot e \cdot N$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | $D$ = casing bore diameter, $e$ = eccentricity, $L$ = lobe length, $N$ = rpm |
| 4 | Actual Discharge | $Q_{act} = Q_{th} - Q_{slip}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | $Q_{slip}$ = slip loss due to internal clearances |
| 5 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{slip}}{Q_{th}}$ | dimensionless (× 100 in %) | Typically **70 % – 95 %** for lobe pumps |
| 6 | Slip (linearized empirical) | $Q_{slip} = k \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | $k$ = leakage coefficient, $\Delta P$ = pressure rise, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity |
| 7 | Power required (fluid power) | $P_{fluid} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{act}$ | W (or kW) | Hydraulic power delivered to fluid |
| 8 | Shaft Power (brake power) | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{fluid}}{\eta_{overall}}$ | W (or kW) | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{mech}$ |
| 9 | Theoretical discharge (3-lobe pump) | $Q_{th} = 3 \cdot V_{d} \cdot N$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Multiplying factor = number of lobes per shaft side × 2 shafts |
| 10 | Specific Speed criterion | $N_{s} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | — | Used to classify pump; for PD pumps, $N_s$ is generally low |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU-Specific Notation Warning:**
> In KTU textbooks, the **displacement volume** is sometimes denoted as $V_s$ (swept volume). The **volumetric efficiency** symbol may be $\eta_v$ or $\eta_{vol}$ — both are accepted. Always cross-check with the question's notation.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Why Lobe Pump? |
|---|---|---|
| **Food & Dairy** | Chocolate, yogurt, cream, fruit pulp | Gentle handling, no shear damage |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Vaccines, blood plasma, serums | Sanitary (CIP/SIP compatible), sterile |
| **Chemical** | Polymers, resins, adhesives, inks | Handles high viscosity (up to 500,000 cP) |
| **Pulp & Paper** | Starch, coating, slurries | Tolerates solids-in-suspension |
| **Wastewater** | Sludge transfer, scum | Self-priming, handles abrasives |
| **Biotech** | Cell cultures, fermentation broth | Low pulsation (3-lobe), sterile |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Theoretical Discharge ($Q_{th}$) for a Two-Lobe Pump

### 3.1.1 Assumptions
1. The pump is a **two-lobe rotary positive displacement pump**.
2. The lobes rotate without any contact with each other or the casing.
3. The fluid is **incompressible** and **non-viscous** (for theoretical analysis).
4. **No leakage (slip)** occurs from delivery back to suction.
5. The clearance volume between the lobes and the casing is filled completely with fluid per revolution.

### 3.1.2 Geometric Setup
Let:
- $D$ = inner diameter of the casing (m)
- $d$ = diameter of each lobe (rotor) (m)
- $e$ = eccentricity between the lobe center and the casing center (m)
- $L$ = axial length of the lobe (width of the pump) (m)
- $N$ = rotational speed of each lobe (rev/s, i.e., rps)

The geometric relationship for a lobe rotating eccentrically inside a casing is:

$$
D = d + 2e
$$

The fluid trapped between the **lobe tip** and the **casing wall** is the *displacement volume* $V_d$ per lobe per revolution.

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Algebraic Derivation

**Step 1 — Volume trapped between one lobe and the casing per revolution:**

The cross-sectional area of fluid trapped in the crescent-shaped region between a lobe and the casing wall can be approximated as the area of a circle of diameter $D$ minus the area of a circle of diameter $d$:

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 - \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by axial length $L$ to get the trapped volume per lobe:**

$$
V_{d} = A \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4} L (D^2 - d^2)
$$

**Step 3 — Express $D$ in terms of $d$ and $e$ (from geometric constraint):**

$$
D = d + 2e \quad \Rightarrow \quad D^2 = (d + 2e)^2 = d^2 + 4de + 4e^2
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $D^2$ into $V_d$:**

$$
V_{d} = \frac{\pi}{4} L \left[ (d^2 + 4de + 4e^2) - d^2 \right] = \frac{\pi}{4} L (4de + 4e^2)
$$

$$
V_{d} = \pi L e (d + e)
$$

**Step 5 — Recognizing that $d + e \approx D/2$ (small-eccentricity approximation often used):**

$$
V_{d} \approx \pi L e \cdot \frac{D}{2} = \frac{\pi D L e}{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Discharge delivered by ONE lobe in ONE revolution:**

$$
Q_{one} = V_{d} \cdot N
$$

**Step 7 — Total theoretical discharge (TWO lobes, both shafts rotating):**

$$
Q_{th} = 2 \cdot V_{d} \cdot N
$$

Substituting the simplified form of $V_d$:

$$
Q_{th} = 2 \cdot \left( \frac{\pi D L e}{2} \right) \cdot N = \pi D L e N
$$

Therefore, the **final governing equation** for theoretical discharge is:

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{th} = \pi \, D \, L \, e \, N\,}
$$

### 3.1.4 Extension to Three-Lobe Pump

For a **three-lobe pump**, three times the displacement volume is delivered per revolution (per shaft side). Hence:

$$
Q_{th,3\text{-lobe}} = 3 \cdot \pi \, D \, L \, e \, N
$$

The factor arises because for an $n$-lobe pump on each shaft, the displacement per revolution is $n$ times the single-lobe displacement.

## 3.2 Derivation of Volumetric Efficiency in Presence of Slip

### 3.2.1 Definition

$$
\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}
$$

### 3.2.2 Slip Mechanism

Because lobe pumps have **intentional clearances** (lobe-to-casing $\approx$ 0.1 mm, lobe-to-lobe $\approx$ 0.1–0.15 mm), some fluid **leaks back** from the high-pressure (delivery) side to the low-pressure (suction) side. This leakage is termed *slip*.

By mass conservation:

$$
Q_{actual} = Q_{theoretical} - Q_{slip}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{th} - Q_{slip}}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{slip}}{Q_{th}}
$$

### 3.2.3 Empirical Slip Model (Hagen-Poiseuille-type laminar leakage)

The slip flow through a clearance gap $c$ (lobe-to-casing) can be modeled as laminar flow through a slot of width $L$ and gap $c$:

$$
Q_{slip} = \frac{c^3 \, L \, \Delta P}{12 \, \mu \, b}
$$

where $b$ is the circumferential length of the leakage path. In compact form:

$$
Q_{slip} = k \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\mu}
$$

where $k = \dfrac{c^3 L}{12 b}$ is the **leakage coefficient** (depends only on geometry).

**Key insight:** Slip is **inversely proportional to viscosity** $\mu$. Hence:
- For **high-viscosity fluids** (e.g., honey, oil), slip is **minimal**, and $\eta_{vol}$ approaches **95–98%**.
- For **low-viscosity fluids** (e.g., water, solvents), slip is **higher**, and $\eta_{vol}$ may drop to **70–85%**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (Model Question):**
> A two-lobe pump has a casing diameter of **150 mm**, lobe diameter of **100 mm**, axial length of **80 mm**, and runs at **400 rpm**. The slip is measured to be **5%** of theoretical discharge. Calculate:
> (a) Theoretical discharge in LPM (liters per minute).
> (b) Actual discharge in LPM.
> (c) Volumetric efficiency.

### 3.3.1 Solution

**Given:**
- $D = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- $d = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$
- $L = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$
- $N = 400 \text{ rpm} = \dfrac{400}{60} \text{ rev/s} = 6.667 \text{ rps}$
- $Q_{slip} = 5\% \text{ of } Q_{th}$

**Step 1 — Compute eccentricity:**

$$
e = \frac{D - d}{2} = \frac{0.15 - 0.10}{2} = 0.025 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute theoretical discharge (using $Q_{th} = \pi D L e N$):**

$$
Q_{th} = \pi \times 0.15 \times 0.08 \times 0.025 \times 6.667
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \pi \times 0.15 \times 0.08 \times 0.025 \times 6.667
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \pi \times 0.002000 \times 6.667
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \pi \times 0.013334
$$

$$
Q_{th} = 0.04189 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to LPM (1 m³ = 1000 L, 1 min = 60 s):**

$$
Q_{th} = 0.04189 \times 1000 \times 60 = 2513.3 \text{ LPM}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute actual discharge:**

$$
Q_{act} = Q_{th} \times (1 - 0.05) = 2513.3 \times 0.95 = 2387.6 \text{ LPM}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute volumetric efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{vol} = 95\% = 0.95
$$

### 3.3.2 Final Answer Box

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| (a) Theoretical discharge, $Q_{th}$ | **2513.3 LPM** |
| (b) Actual discharge, $Q_{act}$ | **2387.6 LPM** |
| (c) Volumetric efficiency, $\eta_{vol}$ | **95%** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Lobe Pump

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional flow** of fluid through a two-lobe pump and the **role of the external timing gear assembly**. This is the recommended KTU-compliant schematic for Module 4 essay-type questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FLUID RESERVOUP] -->|Atmospheric pressure pushes fluid| B[STRAINER FILTER]
    B --> C[SUCTION PORT]
    C --> D[PARTIAL VACUUM ZONE]
    D --> E[TRAPPING ZONE BETWEEN LOBE A AND CASING]
    E --> F[CONVEYING ARC AROUND CASING PERIPHERY]
    F --> G[COMPRESSION ZONE WHERE LOBES APPROACH EACH OTHER]
    G --> H[DELIVERY PORT]
    H --> I[DELIVERY PIPELINE]
    I --> J[RECEIVING TANK OR PROCESS EQUIPMENT]

    subgraph SYNCHRONIZATION_UNIT
        K[Lobe Shaft A] --> L[External Timing Gear A]
        M[Lobe Shaft B] --> N[External Timing Gear B]
        L -. meshing .-> N
    end

    subgraph POWER_TRAIN
        O[ELECTRIC MOTOR OR PRIME MOVER] --> P[COUPLING]
        P --> K
        P --> M
    end

    subgraph BEARING_ASSEMBLY
        Q[Outboard Bearing A] --> K
        R[Outboard Bearing B] --> M
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram (KTU exam tip):**
> - The **flow path** from `A → I` shows the *fluid trajectory*.
> - The **synchronization unit** (subgraph) shows the *timing gear* — a key differentiator from gear pumps.
> - The **power train** shows how the lobes are *driven* (prime mover → coupling → shafts).
> - The **bearing assembly** is shown *outside* the casing, illustrating that bearings are isolated from the pumped fluid.

## 4.2 Component-Level Cross-Sectional Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CASING_BLOCK
        C1[Casing Inner Bore - Diameter D] 
        C2[Suction Port - Left Side]
        C3[Delivery Port - Right Side]
    end

    subgraph ROTOR_BLOCK
        R1[Lobe A mounted on Shaft A - Diameter d]
        R2[Lobe B mounted on Shaft B - Diameter d]
    end

    subgraph AUXILIARY_BLOCK
        X1[External Timing Gear Pair]
        X2[Shaft Seals]
        X3[Outboard Bearings]
    end

    CASING_BLOCK --- ROTOR_BLOCK
    ROTOR_BLOCK --- AUXILIARY_BLOCK
```

## 4.3 Operational Cycle — State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SUCTION: Lobe rotation begins
    SUCTION --> TRAPPING: Lobe tip engages casing wall
    TRAPPING --> CONVEYING: Fluid carried around casing
    CONVEYING --> COMPRESSION: Lobe approaches mating lobe
    COMPRESSION --> DISCHARGE: Fluid expelled at delivery port
    DISCHARGE --> SUCTION: Cycle repeats per revolution
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Drawing Conventions to Follow in the Exam Hall:**
> 1. Always show the **external timing gears** — failing to do so is the #1 reason students lose marks.
> 2. Mark the **lobe-to-lobe clearance** and **lobe-to-casing clearance** with arrows and label them in mm.
> 3. Use **dashed lines** for the *suction* path and **solid lines** for the *delivery* path.
> 4. Indicate the **rotation direction** of each lobe (usually one CW and one CCW when viewed from one end).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: List any three differences between a lobe pump and a gear pump.**

**Model Answer (3 key points, 1 mark each):**

| # | Lobe Pump | Gear Pump |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lobes do **not** mesh — synchronized by **external timing gears**. | Gear teeth **mesh** with each other. |
| 2 | Can run **dry** for short periods (bearings are external). | **Cannot** run dry — gears and bearings are in fluid. |
| 3 | Used for **sanitary / shear-sensitive** fluids (food, pharma). | Used for **hydraulic oils, fuels, lubricants**. |
| 4 | Suited for **high-viscosity fluids** (up to 500,000 cP). | Best for **low-to-medium viscosity** fluids. |
| 5 | Flow is **gentle / non-pulsating** (esp. 3-lobe design). | Flow is **pulsating** due to meshing teeth. |

*(Any 3 valid points = 3 marks)*

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Why are timing gears used in a lobe pump? What happens if they fail?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Function of timing gears:** They are located *externally* to the casing and synchronize the rotation of the two lobe shafts so that the lobes **never make physical contact** with each other, maintaining a precise clearance (typically 0.1 mm).
- **Consequence of failure:** If the timing gears fail (e.g., tooth wear, breakage, loss of engagement), the lobes will **collide** with each other. This causes:
  1. **Mechanical seizure** of the pump.
  2. **Severe damage** to the lobe surfaces and shaft bearings.
  3. **Loss of pumping efficiency** and **catastrophic failure** of the equipment.

*(Function = 2 marks, Consequence = 1 mark = 3 marks total)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never** write $Q_{th} = \pi D L e N$ without defining each symbol — the examiner deducts **0.5 to 1 mark per undefined symbol**.
> 2. **Always** convert $N$ from **rpm to rps** (divide by 60) before plugging into the formula. Many students forget this and lose **1 full mark**.
> 3. **Always** show the unit conversions explicitly (mm → m, m³/s → LPM). The 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts — *unit errors* in either part can cost you up to **2 marks**.
> 4. **Don't confuse slip with leakage** — slip is the *net reverse flow* from delivery to suction; leakage is the *physical* fluid motion. They are related but not identical.

---

### **Question B.A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]`

**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a two-lobe pump. Derive an expression for its theoretical discharge. A two-lobe pump has casing diameter 200 mm, lobe diameter 140 mm, axial width 100 mm, and runs at 300 rpm. If the volumetric efficiency is 92%, determine (i) the theoretical discharge in LPM, and (ii) the actual discharge in m³/h.**

#### **Part (a) — Construction, Working, and Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Construction (2 marks):**
- Two **lobes (rotors)** mounted on parallel shafts inside a closely fitted casing.
- Lobes are driven by an external prime mover through a **coupling**.
- **External timing gears** synchronize the shafts.
- **Outboard bearings and seals** isolate bearings from the fluid.

**Working (2 marks):**
- At suction, lobes create a partial vacuum → fluid enters.
- Fluid is trapped between lobe tip and casing wall.
- Carried around the casing periphery (conveying).
- Expelled at delivery port as lobes approach each other.

**Sketch (1 mark):** A labeled cross-section showing the casing, two lobes, timing gears, suction/delivery ports, and rotation directions.

**Derivation of Theoretical Discharge (2 marks):**

Starting from the displacement volume per lobe per revolution:

$$
V_{d} = \frac{\pi}{4} L (D^2 - d^2)
$$

Using $D = d + 2e$ and simplifying (full derivation shown in Section 3.1):

$$
V_{d} \approx \frac{\pi D L e}{2}
$$

For two lobes:

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{th} = \pi \, D \, L \, e \, N\,}
$$

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $D = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$
- $d = 140 \text{ mm} = 0.14 \text{ m}$
- $L = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$
- $N = 300 \text{ rpm} = \dfrac{300}{60} = 5 \text{ rps}$
- $\eta_{vol} = 92\% = 0.92$

**Step 1 — Compute eccentricity:**

$$
e = \frac{D - d}{2} = \frac{0.20 - 0.14}{2} = 0.03 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $Q_{th}$:**

$$
Q_{th} = \pi \times 0.20 \times 0.10 \times 0.03 \times 5
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \pi \times 0.003 = 0.009425 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to LPM (1 m³ = 1000 L, × 60 for per minute):**

$$
Q_{th} = 0.009425 \times 1000 \times 60 = 565.5 \text{ LPM}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $Q_{act}$:**

$$
Q_{act} = \eta_{vol} \times Q_{th} = 0.92 \times 565.5 = 520.3 \text{ LPM}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert $Q_{act}$ to m³/h (1 m³ = 1000 L, 1 h = 60 min):**

$$
Q_{act} = \frac{520.3}{1000} \times 60 = 31.22 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Final Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| (i) Theoretical discharge | **565.5 LPM** |
| (ii) Actual discharge | **31.22 m³/h** |

---

### **Question B.B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question]`

**Q: Compare the construction and performance of a lobe pump with a gear pump. A lobe pump delivers 1500 LPM of oil (specific gravity 0.9) at 300 rpm against a head of 25 m. If the mechanical efficiency is 88% and volumetric efficiency is 90%, calculate (i) the theoretical discharge in m³/s, (ii) the slip in LPM, and (iii) the power required to drive the pump in kW.**

#### **Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 Marks)**

| Feature | Lobe Pump | Gear Pump |
|---|---|---|
| **Rotor type** | Lobes (2 or 3) | Gears (spur, helical, or internal) |
| **Meshing** | Lobes do **not** mesh | Gears **mesh** directly |
| **Synchronization** | **External timing gears** required | No timing gears needed |
| **Clearances** | Intentional (0.1 mm) | Very tight (slip-prone) |
| **Dry running** | Can run dry briefly | **Cannot** run dry |
| **Fluid shear** | Gentle (low shear) | Higher (meshing) |
| **Typical applications** | Sanitary, viscous, slurries | Oils, fuels, hydraulics |
| **Pulsation** | Low (esp. 3-lobe) | Higher |
| **Discharge** | Independent of pressure (theoretically) | Independent of pressure (theoretically) |
| **Efficiency** | Volumetric 70–95 % | Volumetric 80–95 % |

*(Any 7 valid points = 7 marks)*

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $Q_{act} = 1500 \text{ LPM} = \dfrac{1500}{1000 \times 60} = 0.025 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $N = 300 \text{ rpm} = 5 \text{ rps}$
- $H = 25 \text{ m}$
- $S.G. = 0.9 \Rightarrow \rho = 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\eta_{vol} = 90\% = 0.90$
- $\eta_{mech} = 88\% = 0.88$

**Step 1 — Compute theoretical discharge:**

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{Q_{act}}{\eta_{vol}} = \frac{0.025}{0.90} = 0.02778 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute slip:**

$$
Q_{slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 0.02778 - 0.02500 = 0.00278 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Convert to LPM:

$$
Q_{slip} = 0.00278 \times 1000 \times 60 = 166.7 \text{ LPM}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute fluid (water) power:**

$$
P_{fluid} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H
$$

$$
P_{fluid} = 900 \times 9.81 \times 0.025 \times 25
$$

$$
P_{fluid} = 900 \times 9.81 \times 0.625 = 5518.1 \text{ W} = 5.518 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute shaft (brake) power:**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech} = 0.90 \times 0.88 = 0.792
$$

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{fluid}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{5.518}{0.792} = 6.967 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| (i) Theoretical discharge, $Q_{th}$ | **0.02778 m³/s** |
| (ii) Slip, $Q_{slip}$ | **166.7 LPM** |
| (iii) Shaft power, $P_{shaft}$ | **6.97 kW** |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam!**

- [x] **Lobe pump = rotary positive displacement pump** with 2 or 3 non-contacting lobes.
- [x] **External timing gears** are the *defining feature* — without them, lobes collide.
- [x] **Governing formula:** $Q_{th} = \pi D L e N$ (for 2-lobe); multiply by 3/2 for 3-lobe.
- [x] **Eccentricity:** $e = (D - d)/2$ — always compute this first in numerical problems.
- [x] **Convert N from rpm to rps** by dividing by 60 *before* using the formula.
- [x] **Slip is inversely proportional to viscosity** — high-viscosity fluids → high $\eta_{vol}$.
- [x] **Volumetric efficiency range:** **70% – 95%** (lower for water, higher for oils).
- [x] **Lobe pump can run dry** (bearings are external) — *key advantage over gear pump*.
- [x] **Lobe pump = gentle handling** — ideal for *shear-sensitive* fluids (chocolate, blood, vaccines).
- [x] **Discharge is theoretically independent of pressure** — characteristic of all PD pumps.
- [x] **3-lobe design = lower pulsation** — preferred for pharma and biotech.
- [x] **Flow direction is reversible** by reversing shaft rotation — useful for CIP systems.
- [x] **Suction side is at lower pressure** (vacuum); delivery side is at higher pressure.
- [x] **Clearance values to memorize:** lobe-to-casing ≈ **0.1 mm**, lobe-to-lobe ≈ **0.1–0.15 mm**.
- [x] **Comparison mantra for exam:** "Lobes *carry*, gears *push* — lobes need *timing*, gears need *meshing*."

> **End of Notes — Module 4, Topic: Lobe Pump (PCAUT302, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotodynamic Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rotodynamic Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ROTODYNAMIC PUMP (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> A *rotodynamic pump* is a kinetic-energy-based hydraulic turbomachine in which a rotating **impeller** imparts kinetic energy to the fluid, which is subsequently converted into pressure energy (head) by a stationary **volute casing** or **diffuser**. Energy transfer occurs through **dynamic interaction** between the rotating blades and the fluid, in strict contrast to positive displacement pumps which trap and displace a fixed volume of fluid per cycle.

The term *rotodynamic* combines two root concepts:
- **Roto** → Rotation (kinetic energy input via a spinning shaft)
- **Dynamic** → Momentum exchange governed by Newton's laws and Euler's equation of turbomachinery

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Anchors (KTU Board Favourite):**
> Rotodynamic pumps are broadly classified as:
> 1. **Centrifugal Pumps** (radial, mixed, axial flow)
> 2. **Special Pumps** (jet, airlift, gas-lift — typically out of KTU scope)

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Garden Wheelbarrow of Energy

Imagine you are spinning a **wet umbrella** open in the rain. The water droplets fly off tangentially at the rim — they have *kinetic energy* because you rotated the umbrella. Now imagine catching that water in a stationary bucket held just outside the rim: the bucket slows the water down, and that *slowing* is felt as **pressure** pushing back against your hand.

A rotodynamic pump does **exactly** this:

| Component | Real-World Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Electric Motor | Your spinning hand | Provides rotational shaft power |
| Impeller | Spinning wet umbrella | Adds kinetic energy to fluid |
| Volute / Diffuser | Stationary bucket | Converts kinetic energy → pressure |
| Suction Pipe | Hose drawing from tank | Inlets low-pressure fluid |
| Delivery Pipe | Hose pushing upward | Discharges high-pressure fluid |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor for Exams:**
> *Impeller = where fluid gets SPEED → Volute = where fluid gets PRESSURE.*

---

## 1.3 The Two Subsystems Inside a Rotodynamic Pump

A rotodynamic pump is fundamentally a **two-stage energy transformer**:

**Stage 1 — Kinetic Energy Stage (Impeller):**
The impeller blades accelerate the fluid. By Newton's third law, the fluid's tangential momentum change produces a reactive torque on the shaft, but the *useful output* to the fluid is its increased absolute velocity $C_2$ at the impeller outlet.

**Stage 2 — Pressure Recovery Stage (Casing):**
The volute casing or diffuser is a **gradually expanding passage**. By applying Bernoulli's principle (with the continuity equation $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$), an increase in cross-section forces a decrease in velocity — and that kinetic energy cannot vanish; it reappears as a **pressure rise** $\Delta P$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics You Must Memorise:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
> - Density of water (standard): $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
> - Specific weight: $\gamma = \rho g = 9810 \, \text{N/m}^3$
> - Angular velocity: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \, \text{rad/s}$ where $N$ = rpm

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Centrifugal Pump

### Step 1 — Suction (Priming)
The pump casing must be filled with liquid before startup. *Why?* A centrifugal pump cannot suck air effectively because air has very low density — the pressure rise $\Delta P = \rho \cdot g \cdot H$ becomes negligible. This is why **self-priming** designs or **foot valves** are used.

### Step 2 — Impeller Rotation & Euler's Energy Transfer
As the impeller rotates at angular velocity $\omega$:
- Fluid enters axially at the **eye (inlet)**
- It is swept outward along the **blade channels** (or backward, depending on blade curvature)
- At exit, fluid leaves the impeller tip with absolute velocity $C_2$ at angle $\alpha_2$ to the tangential direction

The **work done per unit weight** of fluid by the impeller is derived directly from Euler's turbomachine equation (derived in Section 3).

### Step 3 — Volute / Diffuser Pressure Recovery
The volute is a spiral casing of **progressively increasing cross-sectional area**. Fluid leaving each blade is collected and its velocity is decelerated. The kinetic head $\dfrac{C_2^2}{2g}$ is largely converted to pressure head.

### Step 4 — Delivery
Pressurised fluid exits through the delivery pipe at a flow rate $Q$ against a static delivery head $H_d$.

---

## 2.2 The Velocity Triangle — The Heart of the Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every KTU numerical problem on rotodynamic pumps is solved using the VELOCITY TRIANGLE.** Master this and you master the module.

At any point on the impeller (inlet = suffix 1, outlet = suffix 2), three vectors coexist:

| Symbol | Name | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $u = \frac{\pi D N}{60}$ | **Blade velocity** (tangential) | Speed of impeller blade at radius $r$ |
| $V_r$ | **Relative velocity** | Velocity of fluid *as seen from the rotating blade* |
| $V_f$ | **Flow velocity (axial)** | Velocity component along the meridian plane (purely radial for radial-flow machines) |
| $V_w$ | **Whirl velocity** | Tangential component of absolute velocity |
| $C$ | **Absolute velocity** | Velocity of fluid in the stationary casing reference frame |
| $\beta$ | **Blade angle** | Angle between relative velocity and tangential direction (measured at blade) |
| $\alpha$ | **Vane angle** | Angle between absolute velocity and tangential direction (in casing frame) |

### The Closing Equation of the Velocity Triangle

$$
C^2 = u^2 + V_r^2 + 2 u V_r \cos\beta
$$

This is the **cosine rule** applied to the triangle formed by $u$, $V_r$, and $C$ as its three sides, with $\beta$ as the included angle.

### Tangential (Whirl) Component Decomposition

$$
V_w = V_r \cos\beta - u
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Trap:** $V_w$ is **negative** at inlet (fluid enters without pre-whirl, so $V_{w1} = 0$ for *purely radial entry* — a standard KTU assumption). At outlet, $V_{w2}$ is **positive** for backward-curved blades (most common) and reduces for radial blades.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is your exam-day armoury. Memorise every row.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Blade (tangential) velocity | $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | $D$ = diameter at point, $N$ = rpm |
| 2 | Angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | $u = \omega r$ |
| 3 | Euler head (work/unit weight) | $H_e = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$ | m | **MOST IMPORTANT FORMULA** |
| 4 | Euler head (no pre-whirl) | $H_e = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$ | m | When $V_{w1} = 0$ |
| 5 | Work done per second | $W = \rho Q (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)$ | W (J/s) | Power input to fluid |
| 6 | Shaft power | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho Q g H_m}{\eta_{overall}}$ | W | Where $H_m$ = manometric head |
| 7 | Water power (output) | $P_{water} = \rho Q g H_m$ | W | Useful hydraulic power |
| 8 | Manometric head | $H_m = H_{suction} + H_{delivery} + \dfrac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}$ | m | Pressure gauge-based definition |
| 9 | Manometric efficiency | $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{g H_m}{V_{w2} u_2}$ | — | Ratio of head developed to Euler head |
| 10 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{P_{shaft}}$ | — | Bearing/friction losses |
| 11 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{vol}$ | — | Product of all three |
| 12 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | — | Slip through clearances |
| 13 | Specific speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | — | For pumps (metric) |
| 14 | Minimum starting speed (no pre-whirl) | $u_2^2 = V_{w2} \cdot g \cdot H$ from energy balance | — | Used for priming analysis |
| 15 | Head-loss across volute | $\Delta h = \dfrac{C_2^2 - C_d^2}{2g}$ | m | Kinetic-to-pressure conversion |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Alert:** The vertical pipe symbol `\|` has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` everywhere above to avoid breaking markdown table rendering. In your exam answer sheet, simply write absolute values plainly.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Rotodynamic Pumps

- **Municipal water supply** — large radial/mixed flow pumps lift water from reservoirs to overhead tanks
- **Thermal power plants** — boiler feed pumps (high pressure, multi-stage centrifugal)
- **Petrochemical industry** — process pumps for crude oil, refined products
- **Irrigation** — axial-flow pumps for high discharge, low head applications (paddy fields)
- **HVAC** — chilled water circulation in air-conditioning systems
- **Marine** — bilge, ballast, and cooling-water pumps
- **Firefighting** — high-pressure portable centrifugal pumps

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Turbomachine Equation (Head Developed by an Impeller)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is worth 5–7 marks on its own in KTU ESE.** Memorise the logic, not the words.

### Statement
To derive the work done per unit weight of fluid by a rotating impeller on the fluid passing through it.

### Premises
- The impeller rotates at constant angular velocity $\omega$ (no acceleration of shaft).
- Fluid enters at radius $r_1$ with absolute velocity $C_1$ at angle $\alpha_1$ and leaves at $r_2$ with $C_2$ at $\alpha_2$.
- The control volume is the rotating blade passage — fluid within rotates with the impeller.

### Derivation

Consider a mass $dm$ of fluid entering the impeller. Apply the **angular impulse-momentum theorem** (moment of momentum equation) to the control volume rotating with the impeller.

The rate of change of angular momentum of the fluid equals the net torque applied by the impeller blades on the fluid.

$$
\text{Torque on fluid} = T_f = \dot{m} \cdot (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)
$$

where $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ is the mass flow rate.

Multiplying both sides by the angular velocity $\omega$:

$$
T_f \cdot \omega = \rho Q \cdot \omega \cdot (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)
$$

Recognising that $u = \omega r$, hence $\omega r_2 = u_2$ and $\omega r_1 = u_1$:

$$
P_{fluid} = \rho Q (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)
$$

This is the **power transferred to the fluid** (Euler's power equation).

The **work done per unit weight** of fluid (Euler head) is obtained by dividing by $\dot{m} g = \rho Q g$:

$$
\boxed{H_e = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}}
$$

### Special Case — No Pre-Whirl (Most Common KTU Assumption)

If the fluid enters the impeller **purely radially** (no tangential component at inlet), then $V_{w1} = 0$ and:

$$
\boxed{H_e = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Manometric Head from Energy Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Manometric head $H_m$ is measured directly by pressure gauges** — this is the head the pump *actually* delivers to the fluid, accounting for kinetic energy differences at suction and delivery.

### Statement
To express $H_m$ in terms of pressures, elevations, and velocities at suction and delivery gauges.

### Working

Apply the energy equation (Bernoulli with pump head) between the suction gauge point $S$ and the delivery gauge point $D$:

$$
\frac{p_s}{\rho g} + \frac{V_s^2}{2g} + z_s + H_m = \frac{p_d}{\rho g} + \frac{V_d^2}{2g} + z_d
$$

where $H_m$ is the head added by the pump (manometric head).

Rearranging:

$$
H_m = \left( \frac{p_d - p_s}{\rho g} \right) + \left( \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g} \right) + (z_d - z_s)
$$

But by definition, the **pressure head difference** $\dfrac{p_d - p_s}{\rho g}$ expressed through the manometer reading $h$ (vacuum at suction if $p_s < p_{atm}$ and pressure at delivery if $p_d > p_{atm}$):

$$
\frac{p_d - p_s}{\rho g} = h + z_d - z_s
$$

Substituting and cancelling:

$$
\boxed{H_m = h + \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}}
$$

where $h$ is the **manometric head reading** (difference between delivery and suction gauge readings, both corrected to the same datum by including the vertical height between them).

If pipe diameters are equal at suction and delivery, $V_s = V_d$ and $H_m = h$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Manometric Efficiency

### Statement
To relate the Euler head (theoretical maximum, computed from velocity triangles) to the manometric head (actual, measured).

### Working

Manometric efficiency is defined as the ratio of head actually developed to the Euler head:

$$
\eta_{man} = \frac{\text{Head actually delivered}}{\text{Head imparted by impeller}} = \frac{H_m}{H_e}
$$

Substituting the Euler equation:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{man} = \frac{g H_m}{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}}
$$

For the no-pre-whirl case:

$$
\eta_{man} = \frac{g H_m}{V_{w2} u_2}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Minimum Starting Speed of a Centrifugal Pump

### Statement
Find the minimum impeller speed at which the pump begins to deliver water (against a static suction lift $H_s$).

### Working

At incipient delivery, the Euler head equals the static suction lift:

$$
\frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} = H_s
$$

For a radial-flow impeller with $\beta_2 = 90°$ (radial exit), the velocity triangle gives $V_{w2} = u_2$ only if $\alpha_2 = 90°$. A more general approach:

$$
V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2} \cos\beta_2
$$

The minimum speed occurs when $V_{w2}$ is at its maximum, which happens for **no shock entry** at the design condition. Setting $H = H_s$ and solving:

$$
u_{2,\min}^2 = \frac{g H_s}{1 - \frac{V_{r2}}{u_2}\cos\beta_2}
$$

Or in the simplest idealised form (for $\beta_2 = 90°$, no losses):

$$
u_{2,\min} = \sqrt{2 g H_s}
$$

This corresponds to a **theoretical minimum** because real pumps have hydraulic losses.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Full Velocity Triangle Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the **canonical KTU 14-mark problem**. Reproduce the same logical flow in your exam.**

### Given
A centrifugal pump impeller has:
- Outer diameter $D_2 = 0.4 \, \text{m}$
- Width at outlet $b_2 = 0.025 \, \text{m}$
- Blade angle at outlet $\beta_2 = 30°$ (backward curved)
- Speed $N = 1450 \, \text{rpm}$
- Discharge $Q = 0.10 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- No pre-whirl at inlet, radial entry ($V_{w1} = 0$, $V_{f1}$ purely radial)
- Manometric efficiency $\eta_{man} = 90\%$

### Required
1. Outlet velocity triangle components
2. Euler head $H_e$
3. Manometric head $H_m$
4. Shaft power input (assuming mechanical efficiency $\eta_{mech} = 92\%$ and volumetric $\eta_{vol} = 95\%$)

---

### Solution

**Step 1 — Blade velocity at outlet**

$$
u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.4 \times 1450}{60}
$$

$$
u_2 = \frac{1822.12}{60} = 30.369 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Radial (flow) velocity at outlet**

The flow velocity at outlet (assuming no slip, no leakage) is found from the discharge through the cylindrical surface at outlet:

$$
Q = \pi D_2 b_2 \cdot V_{f2}
$$

$$
V_{f2} = \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 b_2} = \frac{0.10}{\pi \times 0.4 \times 0.025}
$$

$$
V_{f2} = \frac{0.10}{0.03142} = 3.183 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Radial (relative) velocity component**

In the outlet velocity triangle, the radial direction is perpendicular to $u_2$. So:

$$
V_{r2} = \frac{V_{f2}}{\sin\beta_2}
$$

Wait — careful. The flow velocity $V_{f2}$ here is in the **meridian plane**, which equals the radial component of the relative velocity only when flow is purely radial-outward. For the radial-flow pump, $V_{f2} = V_{r2} \sin\beta_2$:

$$
V_{r2} = \frac{V_{f2}}{\sin\beta_2} = \frac{3.183}{\sin 30°} = \frac{3.183}{0.5} = 6.366 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Whirl velocity at outlet**

From the velocity triangle:

$$
V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2} \cos\beta_2 = 30.369 - 6.366 \times \cos 30°
$$

$$
V_{w2} = 30.369 - 6.366 \times 0.8660 = 30.369 - 5.513 = 24.856 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Euler head**

$$
H_e = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} = \frac{24.856 \times 30.369}{9.81}
$$

$$
H_e = \frac{754.85}{9.81} = 76.95 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 6 — Manometric head**

$$
\eta_{man} = \frac{H_m}{H_e} \implies H_m = 0.90 \times 76.95 = 69.26 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 7 — Water power**

$$
P_{water} = \rho Q g H_m = 1000 \times 0.10 \times 9.81 \times 69.26
$$

$$
P_{water} = 67{,}944 \, \text{W} \approx 67.94 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 8 — Shaft power**

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{mech} \times \eta_{vol}} = \frac{67.94}{0.92 \times 0.95}
$$

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{67.94}{0.874} = 77.74 \, \text{kW}
$$

### Final Answers (Summary)

$$
u_2 = 30.37 \, \text{m/s}, \quad V_{f2} = 3.18 \, \text{m/s}, \quad V_{r2} = 6.37 \, \text{m/s}, \quad V_{w2} = 24.86 \, \text{m/s}
$$

$$
H_e = 76.95 \, \text{m}, \quad H_m = 69.26 \, \text{m}, \quad P_{shaft} = 77.74 \, \text{kW}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Velocity Triangle Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Impeller Outlet Radius r2"] --> nodeB["Tangential Direction u2"]
    nodeC["Relative Velocity Vr2 at angle beta2 to u2"] --> nodeB
    nodeC --> nodeD["Absolute Velocity C2 at angle alpha2 to u2"]
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE["Whirl Component Vw2 along u2"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Flow Component Vf2 perpendicular to u2"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeG["VECTOR u2 BLADE SPEED"] -.-> nodeB
    nodeH["VECTOR Vr2 RELATIVE SPEED"] -.-> nodeC
    nodeI["VECTOR C2 ABSOLUTE SPEED"] -.-> nodeD
    nodeJ["VECTOR Vw2 WHIRL COMPONENT"] -.-> nodeE
    nodeK["VECTOR Vf2 FLOW COMPONENT"] -.-> nodeF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the triangle clockwise from $u_2$:**
> Start at the tail of $u_2$. The vector $C_2$ closes the triangle. $V_{r2}$ joins the head of $u_2$ to the head of $C_2$.
> - $\beta_2$ is between $u_2$ and $V_{r2}$
> - $\alpha_2$ is between $u_2$ and $C_2$

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Energy Conversion in a Centrifugal Pump

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    blockA["INPUT SUCTION SIDE / Fluid at atmospheric or low pressure, low velocity"]
    blockB["IMPELLER / Rotating component, kinetic energy addition zone"]
    blockC["VOLUTE CASING OR DIFFUSER / Stationary, expanding passage, pressure recovery zone"]
    blockD["OUTPUT DELIVERY SIDE / Fluid at high pressure, to delivery pipe"]
    blockE["MECHANICAL LOSSES / Bearing friction, windage, disc friction"]
    blockF["HYDRAULIC LOSSES / Friction, eddy, shock at impeller entry"]
    blockG["VOLUMETRIC LOSSES / Slippage through impeller clearance gaps"]
    blockH["ELECTRIC MOTOR OR ENGINE / Prime mover supplying shaft power"]

    blockH -->|Shaft Power Pshaft| blockB
    blockA -->|Suction head Hs| blockB
    blockB -->|Pwater| blockC
    blockC -->|Delivery head Hm| blockD
    blockE -.->|Subtracted from Pshaft| blockB
    blockF -.->|Reduces Hm below Euler head He| blockC
    blockG -.->|Reduces Q below theoretical Qth| blockB
```

---

## 4.3 Classification Tree of Rotodynamic Pumps

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classRoot["ROTODYNAMIC PUMPS"]
    classA["CENTRIFUGAL PUMPS"]
    classB["AXIAL FLOW PUMPS"]
    classC["MIXED FLOW PUMPS"]

    classA1["Radial Flow / High head, low discharge"]
    classA2["Backward Curved Blades / Most common, stable"]
    classA3["Forward Curved Blades / Unstable, rarely used"]

    classRoot --> classA
    classRoot --> classB
    classRoot --> classC
    classA --> classA1
    classA --> classA2
    classA --> classA3
```
