<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a rotodynamic pump. How is it different from a positive displacement pump?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A rotodynamic pump is a hydraulic machine that continuously imparts kinetic energy to the fluid through a rotating impeller; the kinetic energy is then converted to pressure energy in the volute casing.

Differences:

| Feature | Rotodynamic Pump | Positive Displacement Pump |
|---|---|---|
| Energy transfer | Dynamic (kinetic) | Static (displacement) |
| Output | Sensitive to pressure head | Nearly independent of head |
| Flow | Continuous, smooth | Pulsating (in reciprocating type) |
| Self-priming | Poor (requires priming) | Generally good |
| Typical use | High discharge, moderate head | High pressure, low discharge |

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks difference table)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is meant by "no pre-whirl" at the inlet of a centrifugal pump impeller? Why is this assumption commonly made in design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

"No pre-whirl" means that the absolute velocity of the fluid entering the impeller has **no tangential component**, i.e., $V_{w1} = 0$. The fluid enters purely in the radial/meridian direction.

It is commonly assumed because:
1. It **maximises the Euler head** developed (since $V_{w2}u_2 - V_{w1}u_1$ becomes $V_{w2}u_2$, the largest possible value).
2. It corresponds to **symmetric, axial inlet guide** geometry which is simple and cheap to manufacture.
3. It gives the **best design-point efficiency** for the simple radial-entry configuration.
*(1 + 2 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

A centrifugal pump has the following specifications:
- Impeller outer diameter $D_2 = 50 \, \text{cm}$
- Impeller width at outlet $b_2 = 3 \, \text{cm}$
- Blade outlet angle $\beta_2 = 35°$
- Speed $N = 1000 \, \text{rpm}$
- Discharge $Q = 0.20 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- No pre-whirl at inlet
- Overall efficiency $\eta_{overall} = 75\%$

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Draw the outlet velocity triangle and find the velocity components.

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Blade velocity at outlet:

$$
u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.5 \times 1000}{60} = \frac{1570.8}{60} = 26.18 \, \text{m/s}
$$

Step 2 — Radial flow velocity at outlet:

$$
V_{f2} = \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 b_2} = \frac{0.20}{\pi \times 0.5 \times 0.03} = \frac{0.20}{0.04712} = 4.244 \, \text{m/s}
$$

Step 3 — Relative velocity magnitude:

$$
V_{r2} = \frac{V_{f2}}{\sin \beta_2} = \frac{4.244}{\sin 35°} = \frac{4.244}{0.5736} = 7.399 \, \text{m/s}
$$

Step 4 — Whirl velocity:

$$
V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2} \cos\beta_2 = 26.18 - 7.399 \times \cos 35°
$$

$$
V_{w2} = 26.18 - 7.399 \times 0.8192 = 26.18 - 6.061 = 20.12 \, \text{m/s}
$$

Step 5 — Absolute velocity and vane angle:

$$
C_2 = \sqrt{V_{f2}^2 + V_{w2}^2} = \sqrt{4.244^2 + 20.12^2} = \sqrt{18.01 + 404.81} = \sqrt{422.82} = 20.56 \, \text{m/s}
$$

$$
\tan\alpha_2 = \frac{V_{f2}}{V_{w2}} = \frac{4.244}{20.12} = 0.211 \implies \alpha_2 = 11.92°
$$

**Velocity Triangle Summary:**

```
Tangential direction u2 = 26.18 m/s →
Vr2 = 7.40 m/s at angle beta2 = 35° (backward from u2)
C2 = 20.56 m/s at angle alpha2 = 11.92° (forward from u2)
Vf2 = 4.24 m/s (perpendicular to u2, radial outward)
Vw2 = 20.12 m/s (along u2, forward)
```

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Calculate the Euler head, manometric head, and shaft power.

Assume $\eta_{man} = 88\%$ for the pump.

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Euler head:

$$
H_e = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} = \frac{20.12 \times 26.18}{9.81} = \frac{526.74}{9.81} = 53.69 \, \text{m}
$$

Step 2 — Manometric head:

$$
H_m = \eta_{man} \times H_e = 0.88 \times 53.69 = 47.25 \, \text{m}
$$

Step 3 — Water power:

$$
P_{water} = \rho Q g H_m = 1000 \times 0.20 \times 9.81 \times 47.25 = 92{,}706 \, \text{W} = 92.71 \, \text{kW}
$$

Step 4 — Shaft power:

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{92.71}{0.75} = 123.6 \, \text{kW}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

A centrifugal pump impeller running at $N = 1500 \, \text{rpm}$ has the following design parameters:
- Outer diameter $D_2 = 0.6 \, \text{m}$, inner diameter $D_1 = 0.3 \, \text{m}$
- Blade widths: $b_2 = 0.04 \, \text{m}$, $b_1 = 0.06 \, \text{m}$
- Blade angles: $\beta_2 = 30°$, $\beta_1 = 30°$
- Flow is radial at inlet (no pre-whirl, $V_{w1} = 0$)
- No whirl at outlet (design condition, $\alpha_2 = 90°$, hence $V_{w2} = 0$)
- Flow rate $Q = 0.30 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- Manometric head measured $H_m = 35 \, \text{m}$

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Derive the Euler head expression and show why, under the given conditions, the Euler head becomes zero. What is the design significance?

**Model Solution:**

The general Euler head expression is:

$$
H_e = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}
$$

Under the given conditions:
- $V_{w1} = 0$ (radial inlet)
- $V_{w2} = 0$ (no whirl at outlet)

Substituting:

$$
H_e = \frac{0 \cdot u_2 - 0 \cdot u_1}{g} = 0
$$

**Significance:** This is the condition of a *centrifugal pump with radial exit blades ($\beta_2 = 90°$)* operating at the *design point with no exit whirl*. In this limiting case, the impeller does **no useful work** through pure Euler transfer at design point — all head actually delivered comes from the **diffuser** acting on the absolute kinetic energy leaving the impeller.

Wait — a critical clarification. If $\beta_2 = 30°$ (backward curved) and $V_{w2} = 0$, then the velocity triangle requires:

$$
V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2}\cos\beta_2 = 0 \implies V_{r2} = \frac{u_2}{\cos\beta_2}
$$

This is a **constraint** on the design flow rate such that $V_{f2}$ takes a specific value. The Euler head from the impeller alone is zero, but the kinetic head at exit $C_2^2 / 2g$ is recovered in the diffuser.

*(Full marks for clear derivation and physical interpretation.)*

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Compute the shaft power required and overall efficiency given mechanical efficiency 90%, volumetric efficiency 96%.

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Theoretical flow area at outlet:

The flow velocity at outlet from continuity:

$$
V_{f2} = \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 b_2} = \frac{0.30}{\pi \times 0.6 \times 0.04} = \frac{0.30}{0.0754} = 3.979 \, \text{m/s}
$$

Step 2 — Blade velocity at outlet:

$$
u_2 = \frac{\pi \times 0.6 \times 1500}{60} = 47.12 \, \text{m/s}
$$

Step 3 — Water power:

$$
P_{water} = \rho Q g H_m = 1000 \times 0.30 \times 9.81 \times 35 = 103{,}005 \, \text{W} \approx 103 \, \text{kW}
$$

Step 4 — Shaft power:

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{mech} \times \eta_{vol}} = \frac{103}{0.90 \times 0.96} = \frac{103}{0.864} = 119.2 \, \text{kW}
$$

Step 5 — Overall efficiency:

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{water}}{P_{shaft}} = \frac{103}{119.2} = 0.864 = 86.4\%
$$

(Note: The product $\eta_{mech} \times \eta_{vol} = 0.864$ equals overall efficiency when manometric efficiency is 100% — an idealisation in this problem.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Rotodynamic Pump Problems:**
>
> 1. **Wrong sign of $V_{w2}$** — forgetting that for backward-curved blades ($β_2 < 90°$), $V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2}\cos\beta_2$ is **positive** (less than $u_2$).
>
> 2. **Confusing $V_f$ and $V_r$** — $V_f$ is the **absolute** flow (radial) component, $V_r$ is the **relative** velocity magnitude. They are NOT equal. $V_f = V_r \sin\beta$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting $V_{w1} = 0$** — leaving an $V_{w1} u_1$ term in Euler's equation when the problem says "radial entry" or "no pre-whirl".
>
> 4. **Mixing up $H_m$, $H_e$, and $H_s$** — $H_e$ is from velocity triangle (Euler), $H_m$ is from manometer reading (actual), $H_s$ is the static suction lift.
>
> 5. **Wrong power chain** — shaft power divides by efficiency, water power multiplies by $\rho g H_m Q$. Do NOT divide water power by $\eta_{man}$ to get shaft power; only by $\eta_{overall}$.
>
> 6. **Unit errors in $u_2$** — using $D$ in cm without converting to m, or $N$ in rps instead of rpm.
>
> 7. **Not drawing the velocity triangle** — even a rough sketch fetches 2 marks in 14-mark problems.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**

- [x] **Rotodynamic pump** = kinetic energy transfer via rotating impeller → volute converts KE → PE.
- [x] **Impeller** = adds kinetic energy. **Volute** = adds pressure energy.
- [x] **Velocity triangle** has 4 vectors: $u$, $V_r$, $C$, plus $V_w$ and $V_f$ components.
- [x] **Cosine rule:** $C_2^2 = u_2^2 + V_{r2}^2 - 2 u_2 V_{r2} \cos(180° - \beta_2)$ — use the *outward* angle convention.
- [x] **Euler head** (no pre-whirl): $H_e = V_{w2} u_2 / g$.
- [x] **Manometric head** = gauge-based measurement: $H_m = h + (V_d^2 - V_s^2)/2g$.
- [x] **Power chain:** $P_{shaft} \to P_{water} \to \rho Q g H_m$.
- [x] **Three efficiencies:** manometric ($\eta_{man}$), mechanical ($\eta_{mech}$), volumetric ($\eta_{vol}$).
- [x] **Overall efficiency:** $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{vol}$.
- [x] **Backward-curved blades** ($β_2 < 90°$): stable, preferred. **Forward-curved** ($β_2 > 90°$): unstable.
- [x] **Radial exit** ($β_2 = 90°$): $V_{w2} = u_2$ → maximum $H_e$ for given $u_2$ (in absence of pre-whirl).
- [x] **Specific speed** $N_s = N\sqrt{P}/H^{5/4}$ — high $N_s$ = axial flow, low $N_s$ = radial flow.
- [x] **Priming is mandatory** for centrifugal pumps (air → negligible $\Delta P$).
- [x] **Multi-stage pumps** add heads in series. **Parallel pumps** add discharges at same head.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Centrifugal Pump - Impeller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Centrifugal Pump - Impeller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Impeller (Rotodynamic Component):**
> The **impeller** of a centrifugal pump is a *rotating disc-mounted component fitted with a series of curved or straight vanes (blades)*. It is the **prime energy-transfer element** of the pump, converting the mechanical energy received from the prime-mover (electric motor/engine) into **kinetic energy (velocity head)** of the working fluid, which is subsequently diffused into **pressure energy** within the volute/casing.

The impeller rotates at a high angular velocity $\omega$ about its own axis, and the fluid enters it axially at the **eye (suction inlet)** and is flung outward along the curved vane channels to the **delivery tip (periphery)** by centrifugal action.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Merry-Go-Round Bucket" 🎠

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine a rotating merry-go-round with open buckets fixed along its rim.**

1. A child at the center keeps dropping water into the buckets one by one.
2. As the wheel spins, each bucket sweeps outward and is **flung tangentially off the rim** with great speed — the water travels a long horizontal distance before hitting the ground.
3. The **faster you spin** the wheel, the **farther the water travels** (i.e., more kinetic energy is imparted).
4. The buckets (vanes) guide the water; without them, the water would simply fly off in straight radial lines and lose energy chaotically.

This is exactly what an impeller does inside a pump:
- The **eye** = the center where fluid enters.
- The **vanes** = the curved buckets that guide and accelerate the fluid.
- The **rim (periphery)** = the outlet where high-velocity fluid exits.
- The **motor torque** = the force making the "wheel" spin.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Parameters

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit | Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Rotational speed | **rpm** | Determines $u_2 = \pi D_2 N/60$ |
| $D_1, D_2$ | Inlet & outlet diameter | m | Geometry of impeller |
| $b_1, b_2$ | Inlet & outlet vane width | m | Flow area control |
| $Z$ | Number of vanes | — | Affects slip |
| $\beta_1, \beta_2$ | Inlet & outlet vane angles | degrees | Shape of vane |
| $\rho$ | Density of fluid | kg/m³ | $\rho = 1000$ for water |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the Impeller

An impeller consists of the following distinct parts:

- **Shroud (top disc)** — A solid disc forming one boundary of the vane channel. *Closed impellers* have a shroud on both sides.
- **Hub** — The central hub mounted on the shaft. It holds the impeller to the motor shaft.
- **Eye (Suction Inlet)** — The axial opening at the hub through which fluid enters.
- **Vanes / Blades** — Curved metal plates that form the flow channels. They constrain the fluid to follow a designed path.
- **Periphery (Outlet Tip)** — The outer rim where fluid leaves the impeller.

### 2.2 Classification of Impellers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board-Favourite Classification Table:**

| Type | Construction | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Open Impeller** | Vanes only, no shrouds | Slurries, sewage, fibrous fluids |
| **Semi-Open Impeller** | One shroud (back plate only) | Industrial dirty water |
| **Closed Impeller** | Two shrouds encasing vanes | **Clean water pumps (most common)** |

### 2.3 Vane Geometry Classification at Outlet

| Vane Type | Angle $\beta_2$ | Vane Tip | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Backward Curved** | $\beta_2 < 90^\circ$ | Curves *against* rotation | **Most common** — high efficiency, stable head |
| **Radial** | $\beta_2 = 90^\circ$ | Straight radial | Mixed-flow pumps |
| **Forward Curved** | $\beta_2 > 90^\circ$ | Curves *with* rotation | Fans, blowers (low NPSH) |

### 2.4 Velocity Components at Inlet and Outlet

For any streamline inside the impeller, the absolute velocity $\vec{V}$ is resolved into three orthogonal components:

- $V_f$ = **Flow (axial-radial / meridional) velocity** — along the mean streamline.
- $V_r$ = **Relative velocity** — velocity of fluid *relative to* the rotating vane.
- $V_w$ = **Whirl (tangential) component of absolute velocity** — the energy-carrying component.
- $u$ = **Blade (tangential) velocity** = $\frac{\pi D N}{60}$.

The scalar relationship (the famous *Cosine Rule for Velocity Triangle*):

$$
V^2 = V_w^2 + V_f^2 + u^2 - 2\,u\,V_w
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Equivalently, $V_r^2 = V_f^2 + (u - V_w)^2$, since the relative velocity is the vector sum of $V_f$ and the *tangential slip* $(u - V_w)$.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA SHEET — Centrifugal Pump Impeller (Board-Saving Table):**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ | Peripheral (blade) velocity at outlet | m/s |
| 2 | $u_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1 N}{60}$ | Blade velocity at inlet | m/s |
| 3 | $V_{f2} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi D_2 b_2}$ | Outlet meridional velocity | m/s |
| 4 | $V_{f1} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi D_1 b_1}$ | Inlet meridional velocity | m/s |
| 5 | $V_{w2} = u_2 - \dfrac{V_{f2}}{\tan \beta_2}$ | Whirl at outlet (backward curved) | m/s |
| 6 | $V_{w1} = u_1 - \dfrac{V_{f1}}{\tan \beta_1}$ | Whirl at inlet | m/s |
| 7 | $H_e = \dfrac{V_{w2}\,u_2 \;-\; V_{w1}\,u_1}{g}$ | **Euler Head** (work done per unit weight) | m |
| 8 | $H_e = \dfrac{V_{w2}\,u_2}{g}$ *(if $V_{w1}=0$)* | For *pure radial* entry | m |
| 9 | $P = \rho\,Q\,(V_{w2}u_2 - V_{w1}u_1)$ | Power imparted to fluid | W (watts) |
| 10 | $\eta_{mano} = \dfrac{gH}{V_{w2}u_2}$ | Manometric efficiency (radial entry) | — |

**Boundary / Design Conditions:**

- *No pre-whirl condition* (most common assumption): $V_{w1} = 0$, i.e., fluid enters **purely radially** with no whirl.
- *No shock at inlet:* Relative velocity at inlet is **tangential to the vane**, hence $V_{w1} = u_1 - \dfrac{V_{f1}}{\tan \beta_1}$.
- *Backward curved* (standard design): $\beta_2 < 90^\circ$ ⟹ $\tan \beta_2 > 0$ ⟹ $V_{w2} < u_2$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Impeller design dictates pump performance across **all engineering sectors**:
> - **Municipal water supply** — closed backward-curved impellers deliver 90%+ efficiency.
> - **Fire-fighting pumps** — radial vanes for high head.
> - **Chemical process industry** — open/semi-open impellers for corrosive slurries.
> - **HVAC chiller pumps** — backward-curved for energy-efficient circulating water.
> - **Sewage treatment** — vortex/non-clog impellers handle solids.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Euler's Equation for Work Done by the Impeller

We apply the **angular momentum theorem (Euler's Turbomachinery Equation)** to a control volume enclosing the impeller.

**Step 1: Set up the control volume and forces.**
Consider fluid entering the impeller at section (1) and leaving at section (2). The impeller applies a torque $T$ on the fluid, and the fluid applies a reaction torque $-T$ on the impeller. By Newton's third law, this is the torque transmitted by the motor shaft.

**Step 2: State the Angular Momentum Theorem.**
The rate of change of angular momentum of fluid passing through the control volume equals the net torque applied.

$$
T = \rho\,Q\,(V_{w2}\,r_2 - V_{w1}\,r_1)
$$

**Step 3: Convert to peripheral blade velocities.**
Since $u_2 = \omega r_2$ and $u_1 = \omega r_1$ (rigid body rotation):

$$
T\,\omega = \rho\,Q\,(V_{w2}\,u_2 - V_{w1}\,u_1)
$$

**Step 4: Recognize $T\omega$ as the power delivered to the fluid.**

$$
P_{fluid} = \rho\,Q\,(V_{w2}\,u_2 - V_{w1}\,u_1)
$$

**Step 5: Convert to work done per unit weight (head) by dividing by $\rho Q g$.**

$$
H_e = \frac{V_{w2}\,u_2 - V_{w1}\,u_1}{g}
$$

This is **Euler's Head Equation** — the single most important equation in centrifugal pump theory.

### 3.2 Derivation: Whirl Component $V_{w2}$ from the Outlet Velocity Triangle

The outlet velocity triangle has:
- Blade velocity $u_2$ (horizontal tangent to vane tip).
- Absolute velocity $V_2$ at angle $\alpha_2$ from tangential direction.
- Relative velocity $V_{r2}$ at angle $\beta_2$ from tangential direction.

By vector decomposition, the meridional (flow) component is:

$$
V_{f2} = V_{r2} \sin \beta_2
$$

By subtracting the relative-velocity tangential projection from the blade velocity:

$$
V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2}\cos \beta_2 = u_2 - \frac{V_{f2}}{\tan \beta_2}
$$

Substituting into Euler's equation (with $V_{w1}=0$ for radial entry):

$$
H_e = \frac{u_2}{g}\left(u_2 - \frac{V_{f2}}{\tan \beta_2}\right)
$$

### 3.3 Special Case — Backward Curved Vane (Analytical Insight)

If $\beta_2 = 90^\circ$ (radial vane): $V_{w2} = u_2$, so $H_e = \dfrac{u_2^2}{g}$ — *independent of discharge $Q$*. This produces a flat head–discharge curve, undesirable for stable operation.

If $\beta_2 = 45^\circ$ (typical backward curved): $H_e = \dfrac{u_2}{g}\left(u_2 - V_{f2}\right)$ — head **decreases linearly with $Q$**, giving a stable, drooping characteristic (industry standard).

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Given:** $D_2 = 0.4$ m, $b_2 = 0.02$ m, $N = 1450$ rpm, $Q = 0.04$ m³/s, $\beta_2 = 30^\circ$, $V_{w1} = 0$.

**Step 1 — Outlet blade velocity:**

$$
u_2 = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.4 \cdot 1450}{60} = 30.37 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Outlet meridional velocity:**

$$
V_{f2} = \frac{0.04}{\pi \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.02} = 1.59 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Whirl component:**

$$
V_{w2} = 30.37 - \frac{1.59}{\tan 30^\circ} = 30.37 - 2.755 = 27.61 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Euler Head:**

$$
H_e = \frac{27.61 \times 30.37}{9.81} = 85.45 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Power to fluid:**

$$
P = 1000 \times 0.04 \times 27.61 \times 30.37 = 33{,}540 \text{ W} \approx 33.5 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This single numerical problem is the **template** for 90% of KTU board numericals on this topic. Memorize the 5-step sequence.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Impeller Block Architecture (Mermaid)

The following schematic depicts the functional flow of fluid through the impeller, identifying every critical geometry and velocity region.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[FLUID SUCTION PIPE] --> B[EYE INLET Axial Entry]
    B --> C[HUB Mounted on Motor Shaft]
    C --> D[VANE CHANNELS Curved Guide Passages]
    D --> E[IMPELLER PERIPHERY Outlet at Diameter D2]
    E --> F[VOLUTE / CASING Diffuser Region]
    F --> G[OUTLET PRESSURISED FLUID]
    H[MOTOR PRIME MOVER] -- Mechanical Torque --> C
    I[Impeller Geometry: D1, D2, b1, b2, beta1, beta2, Z] -.governs.-> D
    J[Inlet Velocity Triangle V1, Vw1, Vf1, u1, Vr1] -.applied at.-> B
    K[Outlet Velocity Triangle V2, Vw2, Vf2, u2, Vr2] -.applied at.-> E
```

### 4.2 Outlet Velocity Triangle — Detailed Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OA[ORIGIN O] -- u2 Blade Velocity --> OB[Tangential Point B]
    OB -- V2 Absolute Velocity --> OC[Tip Point C]
    OB -- Vr2 Relative Velocity --> OC
    OC -- Vf2 Vertical Downward --> OD[Projection Point D]
    OF[Foot of Perpendicular] -.Vw2 Horizontal.-> OB
    %% Angle markers
    AX[beta2 angle between Vr2 and tangential] -.measured at.-> OB
    AY[alpha2 angle between V2 and tangential] -.measured at.-> OB
```

### 4.3 Three Vane Geometry Classes — Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[BACKWARD CURVED beta2 less than 90 deg] --> R1[Stable Head Discharge curve]
    P2[RADIAL beta2 equals 90 deg] --> R2[Flat Head curve]
    P3[FORWARD CURVED beta2 greater than 90 deg] --> R3[Steep Rising Head curve]
    R1 --> S1[Used in water pumps HIGH EFFICIENCY]
    R2 --> S2[Used in mixers]
    R3 --> S3[Used in fans and blowers]
```

### 4.4 Functional Flow Sequence Inside the Impeller

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Fluid enters at EYE Axial Direction Vw1 equals zero] --> S2[Step 2: Fluid travels along VANES Direction set by beta1]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Fluid gains VELOCITY Vf1 to Vf2 increases]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Blade rotation gives WHIRL Vw2 developed at exit]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Pressure rise in VOLUTE converts Kinetic to Pressure Energy]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

Define an *impeller* in a centrifugal pump. Differentiate between an open, semi-open, and closed impeller.

**Model Answer:**

An *impeller* is the rotating component of a centrifugal pump fitted with vanes, which transfers mechanical energy from the shaft to the fluid in the form of kinetic energy.

| Type | Construction | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Open** | Only vanes, no shrouds | Slurries, sewage |
| **Semi-Open** | One back shroud | Industrial dirty water |
| **Closed** | Two shrouds encasing vanes | Clean water (highest efficiency) |

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the comparison table.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

What is meant by *backward curved vane*? State one advantage of using it in a centrifugal pump impeller.

**Model Answer:**

A *backward curved vane* is one in which the vane tip at the outlet curves *opposite* to the direction of rotation, so that the outlet vane angle $\beta_2 < 90^\circ$ (typically 20°–45°).

**Advantages:**
- Provides a *stable, drooping* head–discharge characteristic (head decreases with rising discharge), preventing overload on the motor.
- Higher **manometric efficiency** (typically 80–90%) compared to forward-curved vanes.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting the factor $\pi/60$ and writing $u_2 = \pi D_2 N$ (units will be wrong). *Always write* $u_2 = \pi D_2 N / 60$.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Writing $V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2}\cos\beta_2$ but then *forgetting to convert* $V_{r2}\cos\beta_2$ to $V_{f2}/\tan\beta_2$ when $V_{r2}$ is not given directly.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Writing $H = V_{w2}u_2/g$ *without* stating the **no-pre-whirl assumption** ($V_{w1}=0$). Examiners deduct 1 mark for not explicitly stating the assumption.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Using $V_{f2} = Q/(\pi D_2^2/4)$ (this is the *area of a circle*, wrong!). The correct area is $\pi D_2 b_2$ (cylindrical area at periphery).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2023]

**(a)** A centrifugal pump impeller has an outer diameter of 40 cm and an outlet width of 2 cm. The vane angle at outlet is 30°. The pump runs at 1450 rpm and delivers water at the rate of 40 L/s. Assuming no pre-whirl, determine:
  1. The peripheral velocity at outlet. **(3 marks)**
  2. The whirl component at outlet. **(4 marks)**
  3. The work done per unit weight of water (Euler head). **(3 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction of a centrifugal pump impeller and discuss the role of the eye, vanes, and shroud in energy transfer. **(4 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

*Step 1 — Peripheral velocity at outlet:*

$$
u_2 = \frac{\pi \, D_2 \, N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.40 \times 1450}{60} = 30.37 \text{ m/s}
$$

*Step 2 — Outlet meridional (flow) velocity:*

$$
V_{f2} = \frac{Q}{\pi \, D_2 \, b_2} = \frac{0.040}{\pi \times 0.40 \times 0.02} = 1.59 \text{ m/s}
$$

*Step 3 — Whirl component at outlet:*

$$
V_{w2} = u_2 - \frac{V_{f2}}{\tan \beta_2} = 30.37 - \frac{1.59}{\tan 30^\circ} = 30.37 - 2.76 = 27.61 \text{ m/s}
$$

*Step 4 — Euler head (work done per unit weight):*

$$
H_e = \frac{V_{w2} \, u_2}{g} = \frac{27.61 \times 30.37}{9.81} = 85.45 \text{ m of water}
$$

**Part (b):**

Refer to **Section 4.1 (Impeller Block Architecture)** for the neat sketch. Construction consists of:
- **Eye** — central axial inlet; ensures smooth entry with minimum pre-whirl.
- **Vanes** — curved guide passages that constrain fluid along the designed streamline, converting mechanical torque into angular momentum.
- **Shroud** — side disc that prevents fluid leakage between impeller and casing, ensuring the entire flow passes through the vane channels (closed impeller).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2022]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the work done per unit weight of fluid by the impeller of a centrifugal pump. State the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An impeller of a centrifugal pump has an outer diameter of 50 cm, inner diameter of 25 cm, and outlet width of 4 cm. The vane angles at inlet and outlet are 25° and 30° respectively. The pump delivers 60 L/s of water at 1440 rpm. Determine:
  1. Vane velocities at inlet and outlet. **(3 marks)**
  2. Flow velocities at inlet and outlet. **(2 marks)**
  3. Whirl velocities at inlet and outlet. **(2 marks)**
  4. Euler head. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumptions stated for full marks:**
> 1. Steady, incompressible, inviscid flow.
> 2. Flow enters and leaves the impeller uniformly.
> 3. Angular momentum theorem is applicable (no friction losses at this stage).
> 4. The number of vanes is infinite (so the relative velocity is exactly tangential to the vane).
> 5. No pre-whirl at inlet: $V_{w1} = 0$.

**Final derived expression:**

$$
H_e = \frac{V_{w2}\,u_2 - V_{w1}\,u_1}{g}
$$

**Part (b):**

*Step 1 — Vane velocities:*

$$
u_2 = \frac{\pi \times 0.50 \times 1440}{60} = 37.70 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
u_1 = \frac{\pi \times 0.25 \times 1440}{60} = 18.85 \text{ m/s}
$$

*Step 2 — Flow velocities:*

$$
V_{f2} = \frac{0.060}{\pi \times 0.50 \times 0.04} = 0.955 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
V_{f1} = \frac{0.060}{\pi \times 0.25 \times 0.04} = 1.91 \text{ m/s}
$$

*Step 3 — Whirl velocities:*

$$
V_{w2} = u_2 - \frac{V_{f2}}{\tan 30^\circ} = 37.70 - \frac{0.955}{0.5774} = 37.70 - 1.654 = 36.05 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
V_{w1} = u_1 - \frac{V_{f1}}{\tan 25^\circ} = 18.85 - \frac{1.91}{0.4663} = 18.85 - 4.097 = 14.75 \text{ m/s}
$$

*Step 4 — Euler head:*

$$
H_e = \frac{(36.05 \times 37.70) - (14.75 \times 18.85)}{9.81} = \frac{1359.1 - 278.0}{9.81} = 110.2 \text{ m of water}
$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Centrifugal Pump Impeller**

- The impeller is the **energy transfer heart** of a centrifugal pump; it converts mechanical (shaft) energy into fluid kinetic energy.
- Three structural types: **Open**, **Semi-Open**, **Closed** (closed = highest efficiency).
- Three vane geometries: **Backward Curved** ($\beta_2 < 90^\circ$, most efficient), **Radial** ($\beta_2 = 90^\circ$, flat curve), **Forward Curved** ($\beta_2 > 90^\circ$, steep curve, fans/blowers).
- **Master peripheral velocity formula:** $u = \pi D N / 60$. *Never forget the 60.*
- **Master meridional velocity formula:** $V_f = Q / (\pi D b)$. *Not* $Q / (\pi D^2/4)$.
- **Master whirl formula (backward curved):** $V_w = u - V_f / \tan \beta$.
- **Euler Head:** $H_e = (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1) / g$.
- *No pre-whirl* (most common KTU assumption): $V_{w1} = 0$, simplifying Euler's equation to $H_e = V_{w2} u_2 / g$.
- Always sketch the **outlet velocity triangle** in the answer; 2–3 marks are reserved for it.
- The vane angle $\beta$ is measured from the **tangential direction** to the relative velocity vector, *not* from the radial.
- The flow component $V_f$ is **perpendicular** to the tangential direction.
- Power transmitted to the fluid: $P = \rho Q V_{w2} u_2$ (when $V_{w1}=0$).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Casings - Velocity Triangle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Casings - Velocity Triangle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔵 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of centrifugal pumps, the **Casing** is the stationary, airtight outer cover that surrounds the impeller. It performs two simultaneous, critical functions:
1. **Collection Function:** It collects the high-velocity fluid leaving the impeller periphery and directs it toward the delivery pipe.
2. **Energy Conversion Function:** It gradually converts the **kinetic energy** of the fluid into **pressure energy** (a pressure rise) by progressively expanding the flow area, in accordance with Bernoulli's principle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> The three engineering casings prescribed for study are:
> 1. **Volute Casing** (most common)
> 2. **Vortex Casing**
> 3. **Casing with Guide Vanes (Diffuser Ring)**

### 1.2 The Velocity Triangle at the Casing

At the impeller outlet (which is the casing entry), the fluid leaves the impeller with an **absolute velocity $V_1$**, which has two orthogonal components:
- $V_w$ = Velocity of Whirl (tangential component, $V_{w1}$)
- $V_f$ = Velocity of Flow (radial component, $V_{f2}$)

The **Volute Casing** is designed such that its centreline is inclined at an angle $\alpha$ to the tangent of the impeller, so that the absolute velocity vector $V_1$ lies exactly along the axis of the casing. This guarantees **shockless entry**.

$$ \tan(\alpha) = \frac{V_f}{V_w} $$

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Spiral Slide at a Water Park 🎢:**
> - Water rushes out of the slide hole (impeller outlet) at a very high speed — this is the absolute velocity $V_1$.
> - The slide is **spiral-shaped** and **gradually widens** as it curves downward (this is the volute casing).
> - As the water travels through the widening spiral, it **slows down** but gains **height/pressure** (the water at the bottom feels much more forceful than at the top).
> - The opening of the slide is angled to match the water's exit trajectory — this is the **angle $\alpha$**, ensuring no splash-back (no shock loss).
> - When the water finally exits the slide's mouth, it is moving very slowly but with maximum pressure — ready to be delivered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants Used:**
> - Density of water: $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🟢 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of Volute Casing Operation

The volute casing is a **logarithmic spiral** (or close approximation) that wraps around the impeller. Its cross-sectional area increases progressively from the **volute tongue (cut-water)** to the **discharge nozzle**.

**Step-by-Step Operating Logic:**

- **Step 1 — Peripheral Discharge:** The impeller throws water radially outwards at the periphery with the absolute velocity $V_1 = \sqrt{V_w^2 + V_f^2}$.
- **Step 2 — Shaped Entry (The Angle $\alpha$):** The volute tongue is cut at an angle $\alpha$ such that the casing's initial direction aligns with $V_1$, eliminating impact/shock losses.
- **Step 3 — Area Expansion ($A_1 \to A_2$):** The cross-section area keeps increasing. By the continuity equation $A \cdot V = Q$ (constant), the velocity magnitude drops continuously: $V_1 > V_2$.
- **Step 4 — Kinetic-to-Pressure Conversion (Bernoulli's Theorem):** For a frictionless, gradually expanding passage, the loss in kinetic head appears as a gain in pressure head.
- **Step 5 — Delivery:** At the discharge nozzle, the velocity is very small ($V_2 \approx 0$ at design condition), meaning **maximum pressure recovery**.

## 2.2 Why the Casing Shape Matters

| Casing Feature | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|
| Increasing cross-section | Velocity reduction → continuity equation |
| Spiral (volute) shape | Symmetrical collection from all radial directions |
| Cut at angle $\alpha$ | Shockless entry, no impact loss |
| Smooth, diverging walls | Avoids flow separation and turbulence |
| Tongue clearance | Prevents recirculation back into impeller eye |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use the following formulas as your primary toolkit for any KTU numerical on this topic.

| # | Governing Formula | Engineering Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{V_f}{V_w}$ | Angle of volute casing (no shock condition) | dimensionless (° or rad) |
| 2 | $V_1 = \sqrt{V_f^2 + V_w^2}$ | Absolute velocity at impeller outlet | m/s |
| 3 | $\Delta P_{casing} = \dfrac{\rho}{2}\left(V_1^2 - V_2^2\right)$ | Pressure rise in volute casing | Pa $\left(\text{N/m}^2\right)$ |
| 4 | $V_2 \approx 0$ (design) | Velocity at discharge nozzle (ideal) | m/s |
| 5 | $\Delta P_{casing,max} = \dfrac{\rho \, V_1^2}{2}$ | Maximum pressure rise (when $V_2 = 0$) | Pa |
| 6 | $u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ | Peripheral (blade) velocity of impeller | m/s |
| 7 | $\tan(\beta_2) = \dfrac{V_f}{u_2 - V_w}$ | Blade angle at impeller outlet | dimensionless (°) |
| 8 | $Q = \pi D_2 b_2 V_f$ | Discharge through impeller outlet | m³/s |
| 9 | $\text{Head rise} = \dfrac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g}$ | Pressure head rise in casing | m of fluid |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Volute casings are the **workhorses** of industrial pump engineering. They are extensively used in:
- 🚰 **Municipal water supply** pumps
- 🛢️ **Oil refineries** for crude transfer
- ⚡ **Power plant boiler feed pumps**
- 🌊 **Marine ballast** and bilge pumps
- 🏭 **Irrigation and dewatering** systems

The angle $\alpha$ and the pressure rise calculations directly determine the **efficiency**, **NPSH requirement**, and **structural design** of the pump housing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🟡 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Angle of the Volute Casing ($\alpha$)

**Given:** A centrifugal pump impeller discharges water with absolute velocity $V_1$ at the periphery, having whirl velocity $V_w$ (tangential) and flow velocity $V_f$ (radial).

**To Prove:** The volute casing must be set at an angle $\alpha$ where $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{V_f}{V_w}$.

**Proof:**

Consider the **velocity triangle at the impeller outlet**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= \sqrt{V_f^2 + V_w^2} \quad \text{(Pythagoras theorem)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The angle $\alpha$ is the angle that the absolute velocity $V_1$ makes with the **tangential direction** (i.e., the tangent to the impeller circumference at the exit point).

From the velocity triangle, the radial component is $V_f$ and the tangential component is $V_w$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\alpha) &= \frac{\text{Opposite side (radial)}}{\text{Adjacent side (tangential)}} \\
\tan(\alpha) &= \frac{V_f}{V_w}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Geometric Interpretation:** If the volute tongue is cut at this angle $\alpha$, the absolute velocity vector $V_1$ aligns perfectly with the casing's local axis, ensuring **shockless (impact-free) entry**. This minimizes entry losses and maximizes pump efficiency.

**Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{\tan(\alpha) = \frac{V_f}{V_w}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pressure Rise in the Volute Casing

**Given:** Water enters the volute casing with absolute velocity $V_1$ at pressure $P_1$ and leaves with velocity $V_2$ at pressure $P_2$.

**Assumptions:**
- Steady, incompressible, frictionless flow
- Horizontal casing ($z_1 = z_2$)
- No shaft work done in the casing (it is stationary)

**Applying Bernoulli's equation** between the casing entry (point 1) and casing exit/delivery (point 2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 &= \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $z_1 = z_2$, they cancel out:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} &= \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging to isolate the pressure difference:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} &= \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $\rho g$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_2 - P_1 &= \frac{\rho}{2}\left(V_1^2 - V_2^2\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta P_{casing} = \frac{\rho}{2}\left(V_1^2 - V_2^2\right)}
$$

**Design Condition:** At the best efficiency point, $V_2 \approx 0$ (water leaves the casing nearly stationary, having been fully decelerated). Thus:

$$
\boxed{\Delta P_{casing,max} = \frac{\rho V_1^2}{2}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pressure Head Recovery (in metres of fluid)

Dividing the pressure rise $\Delta P$ by $\rho g$ gives the **head recovery**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H_{casing} &= \frac{\Delta P_{casing}}{\rho g} = \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **head recovered** by the casing — a key contributor to the overall pump head alongside the impeller work done.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

> **Problem:** A centrifugal pump has an impeller outlet diameter of $30 \text{ cm}$ and width $1.5 \text{ cm}$. It runs at $1450 \text{ rpm}$. The blade angle at outlet is $30°$ and the velocity of flow is $2.5 \text{ m/s}$. Determine the angle of the volute casing.

**Step 1 — Peripheral velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_2 &= \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.30 \times 1450}{60} \\
u_2 &= 22.78 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Find whirl velocity from outlet velocity triangle:**

From $\tan(\beta_2) = \dfrac{V_f}{u_2 - V_w}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(30°) &= \frac{2.5}{22.78 - V_w} \\
0.5774 &= \frac{2.5}{22.78 - V_w} \\
22.78 - V_w &= \frac{2.5}{0.5774} = 4.330 \\
V_w &= 22.78 - 4.330 = 18.45 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Angle of volute casing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\alpha) &= \frac{V_f}{V_w} = \frac{2.5}{18.45} = 0.1355 \\
\alpha &= \tan^{-1}(0.1355) \\
\boxed{\alpha &\approx 7.72°}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🟣 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Velocity Triangle at Casing Entry (Impeller Outlet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Outlet Velocity Triangle** of the impeller is the **Inlet Velocity Triangle** of the volute casing. It dictates the angle $\alpha$.

The three sides represent three physical vectors, all originating from the same point (the impeller tip):

| Symbol | Name | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| $u_2$ | Peripheral (blade) velocity | Tangent to impeller circumference |
| $V_1$ | Absolute velocity of water leaving impeller | Inclined at $\alpha$ from tangent |
| $V_{r2}$ | Relative velocity of water w.r.t. blade | Inclined at $\beta_2$ from tangent |
| $V_w$ | Velocity of whirl | Component of $V_1$ along $u_2$ |
| $V_f$ | Velocity of flow | Component of $V_1$ along radial direction |

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Flow through Volute Casing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Impeller Eye - Low Pressure] --> B[Impeller Rotating Passage]
    B --> C[Energy Added by Impeller - Work Done]
    C --> D[Impeller Peripheral Exit with Velocity V1]
    D --> D1[Radial Component Vf]
    D --> D2[Tangential Component Vw]
    D1 --> E[Velocity Triangle at Casing Entry]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Volute Tongue Cut at Angle alpha]
    F --> G[Smooth Shockless Entry into Casing]
    G --> H[Gradual Cross Section Area Expansion]
    H --> H1[Area A1 at Tongue]
    H --> H2[Area A2 at Throat]
    H --> H3[Area A3 at Delivery]
    H1 --> I[Continuity AV equals Q - Velocity Reduction]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Bernoulli Equation Applied]
    J --> J1[Kinetic Head V1sq by 2g Decreases]
    J --> J2[Pressure Head P1 by rhog Increases]
    J1 --> K[Maximum Pressure at Delivery Nozzle]
    J2 --> K
    K --> L[Delivery Pipe - High Pressure Low Velocity]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Volute Casing Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Impeller Center] --> B[Impeller Outlet Dia D2]
    B --> C[Volute Tongue Cut Water]
    C --> D[Volute Passage Widening]
    D --> E[Discharge Nozzle Outlet]
    E --> F[Delivery Pipe Direction]
    A -. Tangent Line .-> C
    C -. Angle alpha .-> D
    D -. Spiral Arc .-> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The volute tongue (C) is the starting edge of the casing. The fluid leaving the impeller at (B) has its absolute velocity $V_1$ directed along the casing axis (C→D→E). The discharge nozzle (E) discharges into the delivery pipe (F).

## 4.4 Comparison Table of Casing Types

| Casing Type | Construction | Pressure Recovery | Efficiency | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Volute** | Single spiral passage | Gradual, throughout | Medium-High | Most industrial pumps |
| **Vortex** | Circular chamber + spiral | Partial in chamber, rest in spiral | High | Multi-stage pumps |
| **Diffuser (Guide Vanes)** | Stationary vanes around impeller | Very efficient conversion | Highest | High-pressure boiler feed pumps |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🔴 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define a volute casing. Why is it cut at an angle at the volute tongue?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **volute casing** is a spiral-shaped, stationary chamber that surrounds the impeller of a centrifugal pump. It has two primary functions: (i) to collect the high-velocity water leaving the impeller periphery, and (ii) to convert the kinetic energy of this water into pressure energy by gradually increasing the cross-sectional area of the passage.

The volute tongue (cut-water) is **cut at an angle $\alpha$** so that the absolute velocity $V_1$ of water leaving the impeller is **tangential to the casing passage**. This ensures **shockless (impact-free) entry** of water into the casing, which minimizes entry losses and improves the overall efficiency of the pump. The angle is given by $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{V_f}{V_w}$.

*(Keyword triggers for full marks: "spiral casing", "shockless entry", "angle α", "V_f / V_w")*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the expression for the pressure rise in a volute casing. Under what design condition does it become maximum?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The pressure rise in a volute casing, obtained by applying Bernoulli's equation between the casing entry (velocity $V_1$, pressure $P_1$) and delivery (velocity $V_2$, pressure $P_2$), is given by:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{\rho}{2}\left(V_1^2 - V_2^2\right)
$$

The pressure rise becomes **maximum** when the velocity at the delivery nozzle is **$V_2 \approx 0$** (i.e., the fluid is brought to rest at the casing exit). This is the design condition for best efficiency. Under this condition:

$$
\Delta P_{max} = \frac{\rho V_1^2}{2}
$$

where $V_1 = \sqrt{V_f^2 + V_w^2}$ is the absolute velocity of water leaving the impeller.

---

## 📗 PART B Questions — Module Internal Choice (2 × 14 = 28 Marks)

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Q8(a)]**

#### **(a)** Derive the expression for the angle of the volute casing of a centrifugal pump. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statement of the problem:**
At the impeller outlet, water has an absolute velocity $V_1$ directed at an angle $\alpha$ to the tangent of the impeller circumference. The volute casing is set such that its initial direction (axis) coincides with $V_1$ to ensure shockless entry.

**Step 2 — Velocity triangle at outlet:**
The absolute velocity $V_1$ has two perpendicular components:
- Radial: $V_f$ (velocity of flow)
- Tangential: $V_w$ (velocity of whirl)

By Pythagoras theorem:

$$
V_1 = \sqrt{V_f^2 + V_w^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Geometry of the angle:**
The angle $\alpha$ between $V_1$ and the tangent (i.e., the direction of $u_2$) satisfies:

$$
\tan(\alpha) = \frac{\text{Radial component}}{\text{Tangential component}} = \frac{V_f}{V_w}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{V_f}{V_w}\right)}
$$

This angle is provided in the casing by cutting the volute tongue appropriately, thus aligning the casing axis with $V_1$ and eliminating shock losses at entry. *(1 mark for diagram, 2 marks for triangle, 2 marks for derivation, 2 marks for result & explanation.)*

---

#### **(b)** A centrifugal pump has the following specifications: impeller outlet diameter $D_2 = 35 \text{ cm}$, width $b_2 = 2 \text{ cm}$, speed $N = 1450 \text{ rpm}$, blade angle at outlet $\beta_2 = 25°$, and velocity of flow at outlet $V_f = 2.8 \text{ m/s}$. Calculate: (i) the absolute velocity of water leaving the impeller, and (ii) the angle of the volute casing. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Peripheral velocity:**

$$
u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.35 \times 1450}{60} = 26.57 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Whirl velocity from outlet velocity triangle:**

$$
\tan(\beta_2) = \frac{V_f}{u_2 - V_w}
$$

$$
\tan(25°) = \frac{2.8}{26.57 - V_w}
$$

$$
0.4663 = \frac{2.8}{26.57 - V_w}
$$

$$
26.57 - V_w = \frac{2.8}{0.4663} = 6.005
$$

$$
\boxed{V_w = 20.57 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**Step 3 — Absolute velocity of water:**

$$
V_1 = \sqrt{V_f^2 + V_w^2} = \sqrt{(2.8)^2 + (20.57)^2}
$$

$$
V_1 = \sqrt{7.84 + 423.13} = \sqrt{430.97}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_1 = 20.76 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**Step 4 — Angle of the volute casing:**

$$
\tan(\alpha) = \frac{V_f}{V_w} = \frac{2.8}{20.57} = 0.1361
$$

$$
\boxed{\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.1361) \approx 7.76°}
$$

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q9(b)]**

#### **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a volute casing in a centrifugal pump. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
A volute casing is a stationary, **spiral-shaped chamber** cast around the impeller of a centrifugal pump. Its cross-sectional area **progressively increases** from the **volute tongue (cut-water)** to the **discharge nozzle**. The volute tongue is the starting edge of the spiral and is positioned very close to the impeller periphery. The discharge end terminates in a nozzle that connects to the delivery pipe.

**Working:**

1. Water leaves the impeller periphery with high absolute velocity $V_1$, having components $V_w$ (tangential) and $V_f$ (radial).
2. The volute tongue is cut at an angle $\alpha$ such that $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{V_f}{V_w}$, ensuring **shockless entry** of water into the casing.
3. As water flows through the casing, the cross-sectional area **gradually expands**. By the **continuity equation** $A \cdot V = Q$ (constant discharge), the velocity of water progressively **decreases**: $V_1 > V_2 > V_3 \dots$
4. By **Bernoulli's principle** (frictionless flow), the decrease in kinetic energy appears as a **rise in pressure energy** of the water.
5. At the discharge nozzle (design condition), $V_2 \approx 0$, and the water emerges with **maximum pressure** but minimal velocity, ready for delivery.

**Sketches required:** (i) Cross-section of volute casing; (ii) Velocity triangle at casing entry.

---

#### **(b)** Derive the expression for the pressure rise in a volute casing. A pump impeller of diameter $40 \text{ cm}$ and width $2.5 \text{ cm}$ runs at $1500 \text{ rpm}$. The velocity of flow at outlet is $3 \text{ m/s}$ and the velocity of whirl is $18 \text{ m/s}$. Determine the maximum pressure rise in the casing. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Derivation of pressure rise formula:**

Apply **Bernoulli's equation** between point 1 (casing entry) and point 2 (casing exit):

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2
$$

For a **horizontal casing** ($z_1 = z_2$):

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
P_2 - P_1 = \frac{\rho}{2}\left(V_1^2 - V_2^2\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{\rho}{2}\left(V_1^2 - V_2^2\right)}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculation:**

Given: $V_f = 3 \text{ m/s}$, $V_w = 18 \text{ m/s}$.

Absolute velocity at impeller outlet:

$$
V_1 = \sqrt{V_f^2 + V_w^2} = \sqrt{3^2 + 18^2} = \sqrt{9 + 324} = \sqrt{333} = 18.25 \text{ m/s}
$$

For **maximum pressure rise**, design condition $V_2 = 0$:

$$
\Delta P_{max} = \frac{\rho V_1^2}{2} = \frac{1000 \times (18.25)^2}{2}
$$

$$
\Delta P_{max} = \frac{1000 \times 333.06}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta P_{max} = 1,66,530 \text{ Pa} \approx 166.53 \text{ kPa}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the angles:** Students often interchange $\beta_2$ (blade angle) and $\alpha$ (casing angle). Remember: $\beta_2$ is on the impeller, $\alpha$ is on the casing. **Marks lost: 1–2 per question.**
> 2. **Forgetting the design condition $V_2 = 0$:** When the question says "maximum pressure rise" or "at design condition", always substitute $V_2 = 0$. Otherwise, marks are forfeited.
> 3. **Missing units in $\Delta P$:** Always express the final pressure rise in **Pa (N/m²) or kPa**, not just as a number.
> 4. **Skipping the velocity triangle diagram:** In Part (a) derivations, KTU examiners **mandate** a labelled velocity triangle. A derivation without it loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Sign error in Bernoulli's:** $P_2 - P_1 = \dfrac{\rho}{2}(V_1^2 - V_2^2)$ — the higher pressure is at the lower velocity, so the formula is correct **only if $V_1 > V_2$**.
> 6. **Using $V_f$ instead of $V_w$ in the denominator** of $\tan(\alpha)$: This is the most frequent and fatal error. $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{V_f}{V_w}$, **NOT** $\dfrac{V_w}{V_f}$.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Casings and Velocity Triangle"**

- ✅ The **casing** is a stationary chamber that (1) collects fluid from the impeller and (2) converts kinetic energy → pressure energy.
- ✅ Three casings: **Volute, Vortex, and Diffuser (with guide vanes)**.
- ✅ **Volute casing** = spiral shape with progressively increasing cross-section.
- ✅ The **volute tongue** is cut at an angle $\alpha$ for **shockless entry**.
- ✅ Governing angle equation: $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{V_f}{V_w}$ — memorize this verbatim.
- ✅ Absolute velocity at outlet: $V_1 = \sqrt{V_f^2 + V_w^2}$.
- ✅ Pressure rise in casing: $\Delta P = \dfrac{\rho}{2}\left(V_1^2 - V_2^2\right)$.
- ✅ **Design condition:** $V_2 \approx 0 \Rightarrow \Delta P_{max} = \dfrac{\rho V_1^2}{2}$.
- ✅ Pressure rise comes from **Bernoulli's principle** (frictionless, horizontal assumption).
- ✅ Velocity reduction comes from **continuity** ($A \uparrow \Rightarrow V \downarrow$ for constant $Q$).
- ✅ Peripheral velocity: $u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ — used to find $V_w$ from the velocity triangle.
- ✅ Blade angle: $\tan(\beta_2) = \dfrac{V_f}{u_2 - V_w}$ — links impeller geometry to flow.
- ✅ In KTU exams, always **draw the velocity triangle** in Part (a) answers — it's worth 1–2 marks alone.
- ✅ Use $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ for water unless stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Done
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Work Done

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Work Done" in Hydraulic Machines?

In the context of **Fluid Mechanics and Hydraulic Machines (PCAUT302)**, the term **"Work Done"** refers to the mechanical energy transferred between a flowing fluid and the rotating runner/impeller of a **turbomachine** — either a **turbine** (where fluid does work on the runner) or a **pump** (where the runner does work on the fluid).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Work Done by a fluid on the runner of a hydraulic machine per unit weight of fluid striking the runner per second** is defined as the change in the kinetic energy of the fluid (tangential component) as it passes through the runner, expressed per unit weight of fluid. Mathematically, it is given by **Euler's Turbomachine Equation**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are standing at a door and throwing tennis balls at a spinning paddle wheel (like in old water-wheels). Each ball you throw transfers a tiny bit of momentum to the wheel, making it spin. The amount of "push" you give the wheel per ball depends on:
- **How fast** you throw the ball (absolute velocity, $V$)
- **The sideways (tangential) part** of that throw — only this component actually spins the wheel (whirl component, $V_w$)
- **The speed of the wheel** itself (blade velocity, $u$)

> [!NOTE]
> Only the **tangential (whirl) component of the absolute velocity** contributes to actual turning work. A ball thrown straight through the spokes (radially) does **zero work** on the wheel, no matter how fast it moves.

This is the essence of **Euler's Work Done Equation** — the heart of every hydraulic machine problem in KTU exams.

## 1.3 The Two "Work Done" Quantities You MUST Distinguish

| Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
|----------|--------|------|---------|
| Work done per unit weight of fluid | $W$ | m (or Nm/N) | Energy transferred per Newton of fluid weight |
| Work done per second (Power) | $P$ | W (or J/s) | Rate of energy transfer |
| Work done per unit mass per second | $W_{mass}$ | Nm/kg | Energy per kg handled |

The conversion between them is governed by the **discharge (Q)** and the **specific weight (γ = ρg)** of the fluid.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions Used to Derive Work Done

When deriving the work-done equation, the KTU-standard assumptions are:

1. The fluid is **incompressible** and flows **continuously** through the runner.
2. The flow is **steady** — properties at any point do not change with time.
3. The runner has **uniform blade velocity** ($u$) at inlet and outlet (constant rotational speed).
4. The flow is **one-dimensional** at the entry and exit — uniform velocity at inlet (subscript 1) and outlet (subscript 2).
5. There are **no friction losses** inside the runner (for the ideal Euler equation; frictionless analysis is sufficient for KTU Module 4 derivations).
6. **Energy is exchanged only through the tangential (whirl) component** of the absolute velocity.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the **derivation** of Euler's equation. Memorize the assumptions list — it earns free marks in 14-mark questions.

## 2.2 The Velocity Triangle (Quick Recap)

Every problem in this topic requires reading the **velocity triangle** at the **inlet (1)** and **outlet (2)** of the runner.

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $V_1, V_2$ | Absolute velocity of fluid at inlet and outlet |
| $V_{r1}, V_{r2}$ | Relative velocity of fluid w.r.t. blade |
| $V_{w1}, V_{w2}$ | Whirl (tangential) component of absolute velocity |
| $V_{f1}, V_{f2}$ | Flow (radial) component of absolute velocity |
| $u_1, u_2$ | Blade (runner) velocity at inlet and outlet |
| $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ | Guide vane / absolute flow angle (between $V$ and $u$) |
| $\beta_1, \beta_2$ | Blade angle (between $V_r$ and the negative $u$ direction) |

The fundamental resolution from the velocity triangle:
$$V_w = V \cos \alpha = u + V_r \cos \beta$$
$$V_f = V \sin \alpha = V_r \sin \beta$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **most-tested equations** in KTU 2024 ESE for Module 4 "Work Done". Print this and stick it to your wall.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $W = \dfrac{1}{g}(V_1 V_{w1} - V_2 V_{w2})$ | Work done per unit weight of fluid (m) | All turbines & pumps |
| 2 | $P = \rho Q (V_1 V_{w1} - V_2 V_{w2})$ | Work done per second / Power (W) | When $Q$ in m³/s is given |
| 3 | $W = \dfrac{\rho Q}{g}(V_1 V_{w1} - V_2 V_{w2})$ | Power using weight (W) — equivalent to #2 | When $\gamma$ is preferred |
| 4 | $W_m = (V_1 V_{w1} - V_2 V_{w2})$ | Work done per unit mass (Nm/kg) | Direct energy form |
| 5 | $W = \dfrac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} + \dfrac{V_{w1}^2 - V_{w2}^2}{2g} + \dfrac{u_1^2 - u_2^2}{g}$ | Expanded Euler form | Rarely asked |
| 6 | $W = \dfrac{u}{g}(V_{w1} - V_{w2})$ | When $u_1 = u_2 = u$ (most common) | Radial & axial machines |
| 7 | $W = \dfrac{u(V_{w1} + V_{w2})}{g}$ (turbine, opposite swirl) | For radial flow turbines with $V_{w2}$ opposite to $V_{w1}$ | Centrifugal pumps as turbines |
| 8 | $W = \dfrac{u \cdot V_{w1}}{g}$ (whirl at exit = 0) | When $V_{w2} = 0$ — free vortex design | **Pelton, Francis** |
| 9 | $W = \dfrac{u_1 V_{w1} - u_2 V_{w2}}{g}$ | General case (different blade speeds) | **Axial flow machines** |
| 10 | $\eta_h = \dfrac{\text{Work done per kg} \times g}{\text{Head available}}$ | Hydraulic efficiency of a machine | Connects $W$ to head $H$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **The single most important equation** in Module 4 is equation **(1)**:
> $$W = \dfrac{1}{g}(V_1 V_{w1} - V_2 V_{w2})$$
> Every KTU problem you will face in this module is a numerical or conceptual application of this one formula.

## 2.4 Why "Work Done" Matters in Real Engineering

- **Turbine Selection:** The work-done expression dictates whether a turbine is a high-head (Pelton), medium-head (Francis), or low-head (Kaplan) machine.
- **Design of Blade Angles:** To **maximize** work done, designers shape the blades so that the relative velocity enters and leaves without shock (no vane shock).
- **Pump Sizing:** The same equation (with sign reversed) tells an engineer the **brake power** required to drive a pump.
- **Cavitation Prevention:** Understanding the whirl velocity at exit is critical to prevent vortex formation and cavitation in centrifugal pumps.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Euler's Turbomachine Equation (Work Done per Unit Weight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most important derivation** in Module 4. KTU has asked it as a 7-mark and as a 14-mark sub-question repeatedly. Learn it cold.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a **hydraulic machine** (turbine or pump) in which a **steady, continuous flow of fluid** passes through a **rotating runner** (impeller/blade system). The runner rotates with an **angular velocity** $\omega$ rad/s about a fixed axis.

Let:
- $u_1$ = tangential velocity of the blade at the **inlet radius** $r_1$ ⟹ $u_1 = \omega r_1$
- $u_2$ = tangential velocity of the blade at the **outlet radius** $r_2$ ⟹ $u_2 = \omega r_2$
- $V_1$ = absolute velocity of fluid at inlet (m/s)
- $V_2$ = absolute velocity of fluid at outlet (m/s)
- $V_{r1}, V_{r2}$ = relative velocities of fluid w.r.t. blade at inlet and outlet
- $V_{w1}, V_{w2}$ = whirl (tangential) components of $V_1$ and $V_2$ respectively
- $V_{f1}, V_{f2}$ = flow (radial/axial) components

### 3.1.2 The Key Principle — Angular Momentum

The **work done by the fluid on the runner per second** is equal to the **rate of change of angular momentum** of the fluid as it passes through the runner. This is the **Euler's Turbomachine Equation** derived from Newton's second law in rotational form.

$$\boxed{\text{Torque} = \text{rate of change of angular momentum}}$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Mass of fluid handled per second**

Let $Q$ = volume flow rate of fluid (m³/s) and $\rho$ = density of fluid (kg/m³). Then the **mass of fluid per second** is:

$$m = \rho Q$$

**Step 2: Angular momentum of fluid at inlet**

At the inlet (radius $r_1$), the tangential component of absolute velocity is $V_{w1}$. The angular momentum of the fluid per second about the axis of rotation is:

$$(A.M.)_1 = m \cdot V_{w1} \cdot r_1 = \rho Q \cdot V_{w1} \cdot r_1$$

**Step 3: Angular momentum of fluid at outlet**

Similarly, at the outlet (radius $r_2$), the tangential component of absolute velocity is $V_{w2}$:

$$(A.M.)_2 = m \cdot V_{w2} \cdot r_2 = \rho Q \cdot V_{w2} \cdot r_2$$

**Step 4: Rate of change of angular momentum**

Subtracting (since the fluid *enters* with one A.M. and *leaves* with another, and we measure the change imparted to the runner):

$$\text{Torque} \ T = \frac{d(A.M.)}{dt} = (A.M.)_1 - (A.M.)_2$$

$$T = \rho Q (V_{w1} r_1 - V_{w2} r_2)$$

**Step 5: Introduce blade velocities $u_1$ and $u_2$**

By definition, $u_1 = \omega r_1$ and $u_2 = \omega r_2$. Substituting:

$$T = \rho Q \left( V_{w1} \cdot \frac{u_1}{\omega} - V_{w2} \cdot \frac{u_2}{\omega} \right)$$

$$T = \frac{\rho Q}{\omega} (V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2)$$

**Step 6: Work done per second (Power)**

Work done per second is torque times angular velocity:

$$P = T \cdot \omega = \frac{\rho Q}{\omega} (V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2) \cdot \omega$$

$$\boxed{P = \rho Q (V_1 V_{w1} - V_2 V_{w2}) \quad \text{(Watts)}}$$

(General form, where $V_1$ at inlet and $V_2$ at outlet, but the blade-speed versions are $u_1$ and $u_2$.)

**Step 7: Work done per unit weight of fluid**

Work done per second is $P$. Total weight of fluid per second is $\gamma Q = \rho g Q$. Therefore, **work done per unit weight of fluid** is:

$$W = \frac{P}{\gamma Q} = \frac{\rho Q (V_1 V_{w1} - V_2 V_{w2})}{\rho g Q}$$

$$\boxed{W = \frac{V_1 V_{w1} - V_2 V_{w2}}{g} \quad \text{(metres)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The above boxed equation is the **Euler Head Equation** (or Work Done per unit weight). It is sometimes also called the **Euler's Turbomachine Equation**.

### 3.1.4 Special Case — Radial Flow Machines ($u_1 = u_2 = u$)

For **radial flow** machines (centrifugal pumps, Francis turbines), the inlet and outlet blade radii are very different, but the blade velocity is often treated as equal when computing a single rotating reference. The simplified form becomes:

$$W = \frac{u (V_{w1} - V_{w2})}{g} \quad (\text{metres})$$

### 3.1.5 Special Case — Whirl-free Exit ($V_{w2} = 0$)

In a **free-vortex** designed runner (typical of Francis and Kaplan turbines), the whirl at the outlet is intentionally made **zero** so that all kinetic energy is utilized:

$$W = \frac{u_1 V_{w1}}{g} \quad (\text{metres})$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How the Work-Done Formula Changes with Geometry

| Machine Type | Inlet Condition | Outlet Condition | Simplified $W$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pelton Wheel** (Impulse) | Jet strikes buckets tangentially | $V_{w2}$ is reversed (opposite) | $W = \dfrac{u(V_{w1} + V_{w2})}{g}$ |
| **Francis Turbine** (Reaction, radial) | $V_{w1} \neq 0$ | $V_{w2} = 0$ (free vortex) | $W = \dfrac{u_1 V_{w1}}{g}$ |
| **Kaplan / Axial** | $V_{w1} \neq 0$ | $V_{w2} \neq 0$ | $W = \dfrac{u(V_{w1} - V_{w2})}{g}$ |
| **Centrifugal Pump** | $V_{w1} = 0$ (radial entry) | $V_{w2} \neq 0$ | $W = \dfrac{-u_2 V_{w2}}{g}$ (work done *on* fluid) |
| **Mixed Flow Pump** | $V_{w1} = 0$ | $V_{w2} \neq 0$ | Same as centrifugal |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign convention pitfall:** For a **turbine**, $V_1 V_{w1} > V_2 V_{w2}$ ⟹ $W > 0$ (energy extracted from fluid). For a **pump**, the equation gives $W < 0$ if naively used — the **work done ON the fluid** is $\dfrac{u_2 V_{w2}}{g}$ (positive magnitude). KTU examiners LOVE catching students on this sign issue.

## 3.3 Alternative Form — Work Done from Relative Velocity (Derivation Note)

Using the velocity-triangle relations:

$$V_1 \cos \alpha_1 = V_{w1} = u_1 + V_{r1} \cos \beta_1$$
$$V_2 \cos \alpha_2 = V_{w2} = u_2 + V_{r2} \cos \beta_2$$

Substituting back into Euler's equation yields the **second canonical form**:

$$W = \frac{1}{g} \left[ (u_1^2 - u_2^2) + (u_1 V_{r1} \cos \beta_1 - u_2 V_{r2} \cos \beta_2) \right]$$

This form is useful when the **blade angle $\beta$** is given (very common in KTU numericals).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Velocity Triangle at Inlet and Outlet of a Runner

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **standard inlet and outlet velocity triangles** for a generic hydraulic machine, showing all the velocity components and angles that appear in the work-done formula.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["INLET TRIANGLE (1)"] --> A1["V1 = Absolute velocity of fluid at inlet"]
    A1 --> A2["Vw1 = Whirl component = V1 cos alpha1"]
    A1 --> A3["Vf1 = Flow component = V1 sin alpha1"]
    A1 --> A4["Vr1 = Relative velocity at inlet"]
    A4 --> A5["beta1 = Blade angle at inlet"]
    A1 --> A6["u1 = Blade velocity at inlet = omega r1"]
    A1 --> A7["alpha1 = Guide vane / absolute flow angle"]
    B["OUTLET TRIANGLE (2)"] --> B1["V2 = Absolute velocity of fluid at outlet"]
    B1 --> B2["Vw2 = Whirl component = V2 cos alpha2"]
    B1 --> B3["Vf2 = Flow component = V2 sin alpha2"]
    B1 --> B4["Vr2 = Relative velocity at outlet"]
    B4 --> B5["beta2 = Blade angle at outlet"]
    B1 --> B6["u2 = Blade velocity at outlet = omega r2"]
    B1 --> B7["alpha2 = Absolute flow angle at outlet"]
    A1 -->|W = V1 Vw1 - V2 Vw2 over g| B1
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above, the connecting edge between **A1 (V1)** and **B1 (V2)** is symbolically labelled with **Euler's Work-Done Equation** — this is the energy bridge that transfers mechanical power between the fluid and the runner.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Energy Transfer Path

The following diagram shows how **hydraulic energy** of the incoming fluid is converted into **mechanical (rotational) energy** at the runner shaft, and vice versa for a pump.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["FLUID INLET (High Pressure Head)"] --> P2["GUIDE VANE ASSEMBLY\nSets alpha1 and Vw1"]
    P2 --> P3["RUNNER INLET (Radius r1)\nBlade speed u1 = omega r1"]
    P3 --> P4["RUNNER BLADE PASSAGE\nEnergy transfer zone"]
    P4 --> P5["RUNNER OUTLET (Radius r2)\nBlade speed u2 = omega r2"]
    P5 --> P6["DRAFT TUBE / CASING\nRecovers residual kinetic energy"]
    P6 --> P7["FLUID OUTLET (Low Pressure Head)"]
    P4 -->|Torque T on shaft| P8["MECHANICAL SHAFT OUTPUT\nP = T x omega Watts"]
    P8 --> P9["GENERATOR / LOAD"]
    P3 -->|Define inlet velocity triangle| P10["VELOCITY TRIANGLE 1\nV1, Vw1, Vr1, alpha1, beta1"]
    P5 -->|Define outlet velocity triangle| P11["VELOCITY TRIANGLE 2\nV2, Vw2, Vr2, alpha2, beta2"]
    P10 --> P12["EULER'S EQUATION\nW = V1 Vw1 - V2 Vw2 over g"]
    P11 --> P12
    P12 --> P8
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Sign Convention Across Machine Types

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1["TURBINE MODE\nW positive\nFluid loses energy"] -->|fluid turns runner| R["RUNNER / IMPELLER"]
    R -->|runner turns fluid| P1["PUMP MODE\nW negative\nFluid gains energy"]
    R -->|W = V1 Vw1 - V2 Vw2 over g| EQ["EULER FORMULA"]
    EQ -->|Turbine: W = u1 Vw1 over g| FT["Free vortex case Vw2 = 0"]
    EQ -->|Pump: W = u2 Vw2 over g| FP["Radial entry Vw1 = 0"]
```
